| Exoskeleton Assistance Torque | $\tau_{assist} = K_{p} \cdot e(t) + K_{d} \cdot \dot{e}(t)$ | $K_p, K_d$ = PD gains, $e(t)$ = tracking error | PD control law |
| Fatigue Index (Borg CR-10) | $FI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{i} \cdot w_{i}$ | $R_i$ = Borg rating, $w_i$ = task weight | Subjective fatigue scoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** KTU questions on this module typically ask for *qualitative analysis* of the disability, *identification of the appropriate robotic/ergonomic intervention*, and sometimes a *calculation* using one of the above formulas. Memorize the **NIOSH Lifting Equation multipliers** and the **Karvonen HR formula** — these appear frequently.

### 2.5 The Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why exponential decay for grip strength?** Because sarcopenia is a multiplicative biological process — fractional muscle loss per year is roughly constant, leading to exponential decline.
- **Why log model for reaction time?** Hick's Law and cognitive aging research show that decision-making slows logarithmically with stimulus complexity, and this degradation compounds with age.
- **Why NIOSH multipliers?** Each workplace factor (horizontal distance, vertical height, asymmetry) reduces the safe lift proportionally — hence the product of dimensionless multipliers in $(0,1]$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic abstractions — they are directly used in:
- **Ergonomic job design** in automotive assembly (Toyota, Ford).
- **Exoskeleton tuning** in industrial wearables (SuitX, Ottobock, Sarcos).
- **Worker screening and placement** in occupational health clinics.
- **Insurance actuarial models** for workplace injury risk.
- **Rehabilitation robotics calibration** in physical therapy robots (Lokomat, Armeo).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Exponential Grip Strength Decline Model

**Statement:** The maximum grip strength of a worker declines exponentially with age after a peak at approximately age 30.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$F_{grip}(t) = F_{max} \cdot e^{-k(t-30)}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let $F_{max}$ be the peak grip strength attained at age $t_0 = 30$ years. Assume a constant fractional decay rate $k$ per year beyond this peak.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Differential form of exponential decay} \quad & \frac{dF}{dt} = -k \cdot F \\
\text{Step 2: Separate variables} \quad & \frac{dF}{F} = -k \cdot dt \\
\text{Step 3: Integrate both sides} \quad & \int_{F_{max}}^{F(t)} \frac{dF}{F} = -k \int_{30}^{t} dt \\
\text{Step 4: Evaluate the integrals} \quad & \ln\left(\frac{F(t)}{F_{max}}\right) = -k(t - 30) \\
\text{Step 5: Exponentiate both sides} \quad & \frac{F(t)}{F_{max}} = e^{-k(t-30)} \\
\text{Step 6: Solve for } F(t) \quad & F_{grip}(t) = F_{max} \cdot e^{-k(t-30)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The parameter $k$ is empirically measured to be approximately **0.022 per year** (i.e., 2.2% loss per year of baseline). For a 60-year-old worker:
$$
F_{grip}(60) = F_{max} \cdot e^{-0.022 \cdot 30} = F_{max} \cdot e^{-0.66} \approx 0.517 \cdot F_{max}
$$

This means a 60-year-old has roughly **52% of peak grip strength** — a critical consideration in tool-design and torque-specification.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Karvonen Heart Rate Reserve (HRR) Formula

**Statement:** The target heart rate for a given exercise/work intensity is computed using the Karvonen formula, accounting for resting and maximal heart rate.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$HR_{target} = HR_{rest} + (HR_{max} - HR_{rest}) \cdot I$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define Max HR (Tanaka/Astrand estimate)} \quad & HR_{max} = 208 - 0.7 \cdot age \\
\text{Step 2: Heart Rate Reserve (HRR) concept} \quad & HRR = HR_{max} - HR_{rest} \\
\text{Step 3: Target HR is a fraction } I \text{ of HRR above rest} \quad & HR_{target} = HR_{rest} + I \cdot HRR \\
\text{Step 4: Substitute} \quad & HR_{target} = HR_{rest} + I(HR_{max} - HR_{rest}) \\
\text{Step 5: With } I = 0.6 \text{ (moderate work)} \quad & HR_{target} = HR_{rest} + 0.6(HR_{max} - HR_{rest})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Worker age 55, resting HR = 70 bpm:
$$
\begin{aligned}
HR_{max} &= 208 - 0.7(55) = 208 - 38.5 = 169.5 \text{ bpm} \\
HRR &= 169.5 - 70 = 99.5 \text{ bpm} \\
HR_{target} &= 70 + 0.6(99.5) = 70 + 59.7 = 129.7 \text{ bpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

A workload that elevates the worker above ~130 bpm is **excessive** and should trigger ergonomic intervention (job rotation, automation, or assistive device).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)

**Statement:** The NIOSH Lifting Equation computes the maximum recommended weight that a worker can lift safely under given conditions.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$$

**Step-by-Step Decomposition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LC &= 23 \text{ kg (load constant)} \\
HM &= \frac{25}{H} \quad \text{(horizontal multiplier, H in cm)} \\
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert \quad \text{(vertical location of hands)} \\
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} \quad \text{(vertical travel distance)} \\
AM &= 1 - 0.0032A \quad \text{(asymmetry angle A in degrees)} \\
FM &= \frac{1}{F} \quad \text{(frequency per minute, F)} \\
CM &= 1 \quad \text{(coupling multiplier, varies with grip quality)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Insight:** Each multiplier lies in $(0, 1]$ and **multiplicatively reduces** the safe weight. For a 60-year-old worker with even mildly suboptimal posture, the RWL can fall below 10 kg — meaning jobs designed for younger workers may be **intrinsically hazardous**.

The **Lifting Index (LI)** is the ratio of actual load $L$ to $RWL$:
$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} \geq 1.0 \implies \text{Ergonomic redesign required}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Disability-Solution Mapping

| Disability Type | Functional Deficit | Quantitative Marker | Robotic / Ergonomic Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sarcopenia (muscle loss) | Reduced grip, lift capacity | $F_{grip} \downarrow$, LI > 1 | Active exoskeleton (e.g., Ekso GT, Sarcos Guardian XO) |
| Osteoarthritis | Joint pain, stiffness | ROM $\downarrow$ 15–30% | Soft robotic orthoses, ergonomic tool redesign |
| Presbyopia | Near-vision loss | Reading distance < 40 cm | Magnifying lenses, head-mounted displays |
| Presbycusis | High-frequency hearing loss | Pure-tone audiometry | Hearing aids, visual alert systems |
| Slowed reaction time | Safety-critical task risk | RT $\uparrow$ by 50–100 ms | Automation of high-tempo tasks, adaptive HMI |
| Balance decline | Fall risk | Berg Balance Score < 45 | Wearable IMU-based stability aids |
| Cognitive fatigue | Multitasking failure | Trail-Making Test scores | Cognitive load monitoring, AI assistants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Aging-Disability-Robotic Solution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Demographic Shift: Aging Workforce]
    A1[Primary Aging: Intrinsic Biological Decline]
    A2[Secondary Aging: Disease and Disuse]
    A3[Tertiary Aging: Lifestyle Factors]
    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A1 --> B1[Musculoskeletal Decline]
    A1 --> B2[Sensory Decline]
    A1 --> B3[Cognitive Decline]
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1[Workplace Disability Manifestation]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Impairment: Loss of Body Function]
    C1 --> D2[Activity Limitation: Task Difficulty]
    C1 --> D3[Participation Restriction: Job Exclusion]
    D1 --> E1[Solution Layer L1-L2: Environmental and Ergonomic]
    D2 --> E2[Solution Layer L3-L4: Assistive Devices and Wearable Robotics]
    D3 --> E3[Solution Layer L5-L6: Intelligent Rehabilitation Robots and AI]
    E1 --> F1[Restored Functional Capacity]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Outcome: Sustainable Aging Workforce]
    G1 --> H1[Productivity Maintained]
    G1 --> H2[Safety Enhanced]
    G1 --> H3[Quality of Life Improved]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Robotic Exoskeleton Closed-Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U0[Intended Movement: User Intent]
    S1[Sensor Array: EMG, IMU, Force]
    U0 --> S1
    S1 --> C1[State Estimator]
    C1 --> C2[Controller: PD or Impedance]
    C2 --> A1[Actuator: Motor or Pneumatic]
    A1 --> J1[Exoskeleton Joint: Knee, Elbow, Back]
    J1 --> E1[Environment Interaction: Lift, Push, Walk]
    E1 --> FB1[Force and Motion Feedback]
    FB1 --> S1
    J1 --> WM1[Worker Musculoskeletal Load Reduction]
    WM1 --> OUT1[Outcome: Reduced Fatigue and Injury Risk]
```

### 4.3 Solution Layer Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Workplace Disability Detected]
    L0 --> Q1{Is it sensory?}
    L0 --> Q2{Is it musculoskeletal?}
    L0 --> Q3{Is it cognitive?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Layer L1-L3: Lighting, Magnifiers, Hearing Aids, Visual Alerts]
    Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Layer L2-L4: Ergonomic Tools, Passive Exoskeletons, Active Exoskeletons]
    Q3 -- Yes --> S3[Layer L3-L6: Cognitive Aids, Job Rotation, AI Monitoring, Adaptive Automation]
    S1 --> R1[Restored Function]
    S2 --> R1
    S3 --> R1
    R1 --> Z1[Inclusive, Productive Aging Workforce]
```

### 4.4 Universal Design Wheel for Aging Workplaces

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    UD0 --> P3[Simple and Intuitive]

    UD0 --> P5[Tolerance for Error]
    UD0 --> P6[Low Physical Effort]
    UD0 --> P7[Size and Space for Approach and Use]
    P1 --> IMPL1[Adjustable Workstations]
    P2 --> IMPL2[Modular Tools and Fixtures]
    P3 --> IMPL3[Iconic HMIs and Voice Interfaces]
    P4 --> IMPL4[Multimodal Feedback: Visual, Auditory, Haptic]
    P5 --> IMPL5[Failsafe Automation and Exoskeleton Limits]
    P6 --> IMPL6[Exoskeletal Augmentation and Power Tools]
    P7 --> IMPL7[Spacious Layouts and Reach Extenders]
    IMPL1 --> OUT1[Age-Inclusive Workplace]
    IMPL2 --> OUT1
    IMPL3 --> OUT1
    IMPL4 --> OUT1

    IMPL6 --> OUT1

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **sarcopenia** and explain its impact on workplace functional capacity in older workers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sarcopenia is the **progressive, age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass, strength, and function**, beginning around age 30 and accelerating after 50. Its workplace impact includes (1) reduced grip strength (modeled exponentially as $F_{grip}(t) = F_{max} e^{-0.022(t-30)}$), (2) decreased lifting capacity (NIOSH RWL falls below 10 kg in suboptimal postures), (3) increased fatigue and injury risk, and (4) limitations in repetitive or high-force assembly tasks. **(1 + 1 + 1 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List **three major categories** of age-related workplace disabilities and give one example of a robotic/ergonomic solution for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Musculoskeletal** — Example: Wearable back-support exoskeleton (e.g., SuitX) to reduce lumbar loading during lifting. **(1 mark)**
2. **Sensory (visual/hearing)** — Example: Head-mounted magnifying display for presbyopia; visual alert systems for presbycusis. **(1 mark)**
3. **Cognitive (reaction time, memory)** — Example: AI-based cognitive load monitoring and adaptive human-machine interfaces to reduce information density. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **major age-related disabilities** affecting workers in modern industries. Classify them according to the ICF framework and explain how each manifests in workplace tasks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **role of rehabilitation robotics and wearable exoskeletons** in mitigating these disabilities, with specific examples of passive, semi-active, and active systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Age-Related Workplace Disabilities (7 marks):**

The **ICF framework** classifies disability into three interacting dimensions:
- **Impairment** (body function/structure loss),
- **Activity Limitation** (task difficulty),
- **Participation Restriction** (exclusion from roles).

**Major Categories:**

1. **Musculoskeletal Impairments (2 marks):** Sarcopenia reduces muscle mass; osteoarthritis stiffens joints; intervertebral disc degeneration reduces lumbar flexibility. **Workplace manifestation:** Reduced lifting capacity (LI > 1), slower assembly cycles, increased back injury risk. Workers > 50 exhibit ~30% grip strength reduction.

2. **Sensory Impairments (2 marks):** Presbyopia (near-vision loss) impairs inspection tasks; presbycusis impairs auditory warnings; reduced contrast sensitivity affects low-light work; diminished proprioception affects balance on ladders/elevated platforms.

3. **Cognitive and Psychomotor Impairments (2 marks):** Reaction time increases logarithmically with age ($RT(t) = RT_0 + \alpha \log(t/20)$); working memory and multitasking decline; slower recovery from fatigue. **Workplace manifestation:** Higher accident risk in high-tempo environments, difficulty with complex HMIs.

4. **Cardiopulmonary Decline (1 mark):** VO₂max falls ~1% per year; reduces sustained physical work capacity. Worker age 60 has ~40% lower aerobic capacity than age 25.

**(b) Rehabilitation Robotics and Exoskeletons (7 marks):**

**Passive Exoskeletons (2 marks):** Use springs and dampers to store and return energy. Example: **SuitX backX** — a spring-loaded exoskeleton that transfers load from the lower back to the thighs during forward-bent lifting, reducing lumbar compression by ~30–40%. No external power; lightweight and inexpensive.

**Semi-Active Exoskeletons (2 marks):** Use controllable clutches or dampers (e.g., magnetorheological fluid) to modulate stiffness. Example: **Ottobock ShoulderX** — provides adjustable gravity-cancellation for overhead work, reducing shoulder deltoid load by ~50%. Power consumption minimal; suitable for prolonged use.

**Active Exoskeletons (3 marks):** Use motors and sensors in closed-loop control. Example: **Sarcos Guardian XO** — a full-body powered exoskeleton that amplifies the wearer's strength 1:1 to 20:1, allowing a 60-year-old worker to lift 50 kg repeatedly without injury. Uses PD control law $\tau_{assist} = K_p e(t) + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ to track intended motion. EMG/IMU sensors detect user intent. Other examples: **Ekso GT** (lower-limb gait rehabilitation), **Armeo Power** (upper-limb therapy).

**Additional robotic solutions:** Haptic feedback systems for sensory substitution, AI-driven fatigue prediction wearables, teleoperated robots for hazardous task avoidance, and soft robotic orthoses for joint support.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** in detail, including all its multipliers. Compute the **Lifting Index (LI)** for a given scenario and comment on the ergonomic risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **principles of Universal Design** and explain how they can be applied to create an age-inclusive workplace. Illustrate with examples relevant to robotic rehabilitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) NIOSH Lifting Equation (7 marks):**

The NIOSH Revised Lifting Equation (1994) provides a **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)**:
$$RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$$

**Multiplier Definitions (3 marks):**
- $LC = 23$ kg — load constant, the maximum under ideal conditions.
- $HM = 25 / H$ — horizontal distance multiplier; $H$ = horizontal distance of hands from ankles (cm), $25 \leq H \leq 63$.
- $VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ — vertical multiplier; $V$ = vertical location of hands (cm).
- $DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D$ — distance multiplier; $D$ = vertical travel distance (cm), $25 \leq D \leq 175$.
- $AM = 1 - 0.0032 A$ — asymmetry multiplier; $A$ = angle of asymmetry (degrees), $0 \leq A \leq 135$.
- $FM = 1 / F$ — frequency multiplier, depends on lifts/min and duration.
- $CM = 1$ (varies 0.9–1.0) — coupling multiplier based on grip quality.

**Numerical Example (3 marks):** Worker age 58, lifting 18 kg box.
- $H = 50$ cm $\Rightarrow HM = 25/50 = 0.50$
- $V = 75$ cm $\Rightarrow VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert 75-75 \vert = 1.0$
- $D = 50$ cm $\Rightarrow DM = 0.82 + 4.5/50 = 0.82 + 0.09 = 0.91$
- $A = 30°$ $\Rightarrow AM = 1 - 0.0032(30) = 0.904$
- $F = 4$ lifts/min (short duration) $\Rightarrow FM \approx 0.50$
- $C = $ good coupling $\Rightarrow CM = 1.0$

$$
RWL = 23 \cdot 0.50 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.91 \cdot 0.904 \cdot 0.50 \cdot 1.0 = 4.74 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{18}{4.74} \approx 3.80
$$

**Ergonomic Risk Comment (1 mark):** Since $LI = 3.80 \gg 1.0$, the task is **highly hazardous** — a back-support exoskeleton or job redesign is mandatory. For an aging worker, this is even more critical given reduced muscle reserve.

**(b) Universal Design for Age-Inclusive Workplace (7 marks):**

**The Seven Principles of Universal Design (Ron Mace, 1997) — 3.5 marks:**
1. **Equitable Use** — Same means of use for all users.
2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of abilities.
3. **Simple and Intuitive** — Easy to understand regardless of experience.
4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates effectively to the user.
5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards from accidental actions.
6. **Low Physical Effort** — Can be used efficiently and comfortably.
7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Appropriate size and space.

**Application to Age-Inclusive Workplace with Robotic Rehabilitation (3.5 marks):**
- **Adjustable workstations** (Principle 2) — sit-stand desks accommodating reduced ROM.
- **Exoskeletons** (Principle 6) — back-support and shoulder-support exos reduce load by 30–50%, lowering physical effort.
- **Multimodal HMIs** (Principle 4) — visual + auditory + haptic feedback compensate for sensory decline.
- **Voice-controlled and gesture-based interfaces** (Principle 3) — simple, intuitive for workers with reduced fine motor control.
- **Reach extenders and power tools** (Principle 6) — compensate for reduced strength.
- **Cognitive load monitoring and adaptive automation** (Principle 5) — AI detects fatigue and adjusts task complexity.
- **Spacious layouts with anti-fatigue flooring** (Principle 7) — accommodate balance aids, walkers, and slower gait.

These interventions, when combined, create a **workplace where aging workers remain productive, safe, and integrated**, fulfilling the broader goals of inclusive ergonomic design and rehabilitation engineering.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing ICF and WHO classifications** — students often mix up *impairment*, *disability*, and *handicap*. Use the ICF terminology: **Impairment → Activity Limitation → Participation Restriction**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals** — always state grip strength in **kg or N**, RWL in **kg**, heart rate in **bpm**.
> 3. **Omitting examples** — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for **named exoskeleton systems** (SuitX, Sarcos, Ekso, Armeo) or **named standards** (NIOSH, ADA, ICF). Generic answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why"** — when describing a robotic solution, always explain *which specific deficit* it addresses.
> 5. **Insufficient depth on Universal Design** — listing only 2–3 principles is a common mistake. Mention all 7 for full marks.
> 6. **Misapplying formulas** — the NIOSH equation is multiplicative, not additive. The exponential decline model is **$e^{-kt}$**, not linear.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Disability Associated with Aging in the Work Place**

- **Demographic shift** is a global reality — workforce aging is accelerating, making age-related disability a top ergonomic concern.
- The **ICF framework** classifies disability into **Impairment → Activity Limitation → Participation Restriction** — use this in every answer.
- **Three major disability categories**: **Musculoskeletal** (sarcopenia, osteoarthritis, disc degeneration), **Sensory** (presbyopia, presbycusis), and **Cognitive/Psychomotor** (slowed reaction time, working memory decline).
- **Quantitative markers** to remember:
  - Grip strength: $F_{grip}(t) = F_{max} e^{-0.022(t-30)}$
  - VO₂max decline: ~1% per year post-25
  - Reaction time: logarithmic increase with age
  - NIOSH RWL = $LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$
  - Lifting Index $LI = L / RWL$; $LI \geq 1$ → redesign needed
  - Karvonen HR: $HR_{target} = HR_{rest} + I(HR_{max} - HR_{rest})$
- **Six solution layers**: Environmental → Ergonomic → Assistive Devices → Wearable Robotics → Intelligent Rehab Robots → AI-Integrated Systems.
- **Exoskeleton classification**: **Passive** (springs — SuitX), **Semi-active** (MR dampers — Ottobock), **Active** (motors — Sarcos, Ekso).
- **Universal Design — 7 Principles**: Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Tolerant for Error, Low Effort, Appropriate Size/Space.
- **PD control law** for active exoskeletons: $\tau_{assist} = K_p e(t) + K_d \dot{e}(t)$.
- **Legal/Policy context**: ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act), Equality Act, WHO ICF — mention these in policy-oriented answers.
- **Examples to memorize**: SuitX backX, Sarcos Guardian XO, Ekso GT, Armeo Power, Ottobock ShoulderX, Lokomat.
- **Common workplace interventions**: ergonomic redesign, job rotation, exoskeletons, AI fatigue monitoring, flexible work hours, retraining, and accessible workplace design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clinical Practice of Rehabilitation Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clinical Practice of Rehabilitation Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Clinical Practice of Rehabilitation Engineering (CPRE)** is the systematic, multidisciplinary, and evidence-based application of engineering sciences, biomechanics, assistive technology, and clinical sciences to **assess, design, fabricate, and deliver** rehabilitative interventions that restore, compensate for, or prevent loss of functional ability in individuals with physical, cognitive, or sensory impairments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Rehabilitation Engineering is defined by the **RESNA (Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America)** as *"the systematic application of engineering sciences to design, develop, test, and evaluate technological solutions to problems confronted by individuals with disabilities."*

The **Clinical Practice** component specifically refers to the **direct patient-facing workflow** performed inside hospitals, rehabilitation centers, special schools, and community clinics — where the engineer works alongside clinicians (physiatrists, physiotherapists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, prosthetists-orthotists) to deliver technology-enabled care.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Clinical" prefix matters in KTU context:**
> - *Engineering-only* design → prototype fabrication (lab-based).
> - *Clinical practice* → patient interaction, prescription writing, fitting, training, follow-up, and outcome measurement.
> - KTU 2024 explicitly asks students to differentiate between the **technical** and **clinical** roles of a rehabilitation engineer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Auto-Repair Shop" Model

Imagine a car that has been in an accident. The work it must do does not change (it must still drive on roads, carry people, obey traffic rules), but its **capability** has reduced. The auto-repair shop must:

1. **Diagnose** which parts are damaged (assessment).
2. **Decide** whether to repair, replace, or retrofit (clinical decision making).
3. **Fit** the right part to the right car (prescription & fitting).
4. **Train** the driver on the new controls (user training).
5. **Follow-up** regularly to ensure roadworthiness (outcome monitoring).
6. **Document** the entire process for warranty and insurance (clinical records).

> The damaged car = **person with disability**; the repair shop = **rehabilitation team**; the engineer = the person who designs, customizes, and maintains the assistive devices.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU 2024

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Impairment** | Loss or abnormality of body structure/function (e.g., muscle weakness after stroke). |
| **Activity Limitation** | Difficulty an individual may have in executing a task (e.g., unable to walk). |
| **Participation Restriction** | Problem an individual may experience in involvement in life situations (e.g., cannot go to office). |
| **Disability** | Umbrella term covering impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions. |
| **Rehabilitation** | A set of interventions designed to optimize functioning and reduce disability. |
| **Assistive Technology (AT)** | Any item, piece of equipment, or product used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of a person with a disability. |
| **Prosthesis** | An artificial device that replaces a missing body part. |
| **Orthosis** | An external device that supports, aligns, prevents, or corrects deformities. |
| **ICF** | International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (WHO, 2001). |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU exams — "I-A-P-C-P":** **I**mpairment → **A**ctivity limitation → **P**articipation restriction → **C**ontextual factors → **P**erson-centred care. This is the exact logical flow used in **WHO-ICF** model answers.

## 1.4 Scope of Clinical Practice

The clinical practice spans **five functional domains**, all of which are testable in KTU Module 1:

1. **Mobility & Locomotion** — wheelchairs, walkers, robotic gait trainers.
2. **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** — adaptive feeding, dressing, toileting devices.
3. **Communication & Cognition** — AAC devices, eye-gaze trackers.
4. **Sensory Substitution/Augmentation** — cochlear implants, tactile vision substitution.
5. **Vocational & Educational Re-entry** — workplace modifications, accessible computing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The WHO-ICF Framework (The Heart of KTU Module 1)

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** is the **gold-standard biopsychosocial model** approved by the **World Health Organization (WHO, 54th World Health Assembly, 2001)**. It replaced the older, deficit-only **ICIDH-1980** model.

### 2.1.1 Components of ICF

The ICF has **two major parts**, each with components:

**Part 1: Functioning and Disability**
- **Body Functions (b)** — physiological functions of body systems (e.g., b730 = muscle power functions).
- **Body Structures (s)** — anatomical parts (e.g., s750 = structure of lower limb).
- **Activities and Participation (d)** — execution of a task and involvement in life situations (e.g., d450 = walking).

**Part 2: Contextual Factors**
- **Environmental Factors (e)** — physical, social, attitudinal environment (e.g., e150 = design of buildings).
- **Personal Factors** — age, gender, education, coping style (not classified in ICF but acknowledged).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — "Functioning vs Disability" polarity:**
> For every domain, the ICF provides **two qualifiers**:
> - *Impairment/Restriction* qualifier (problem-focused, 0–4 scale).
> - *Facilitator/Barrier* qualifier for environmental factors (+3 to –3 scale).
> Students who write only the "problem" side lose marks.

### 2.1.2 The ICF Interaction Model (Why → How Chain)

```
Health Condition (Disorder/Disease)
        ↓
Body Functions & Structures  ←→  Activities
        ↓                            ↓
   Impairment            Participation Restriction
                                ↑
                  Contextual Factors (E + P)
```

The **arrows are bi-directional**, meaning contextual factors (e.g., a ramp) can *reduce* participation restriction even if the impairment is unchanged. This is the **engineering lever** in rehabilitation.

## 2.2 The Clinical Rehabilitation Engineering Service Delivery Model

This is the **operational backbone** of any clinical rehabilitation engineering department. The KTU syllabus names it explicitly as *"Service Delivery Models."*

### 2.2.1 The 7-Stage Clinical Workflow

| Stage | Activity | Primary Stakeholder | Engineering Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Referral** | Physiatrist (PM&R) | Clinical prescription |
| 2 | **Client Assessment** | Rehab Engineer + Therapist | Impairment profile, ICF coding |
| 3 | **Goal Setting (SMART)** | Multidisciplinary team | Goal sheet with metrics |
| 4 | **Device Design/Fabrication** | Rehab Engineer | Prototype / custom device |
| 5 | **Fitting & Training** | Prosthetist-Orthotist / OT | Fitted device + training log |
| 6 | **Outcome Measurement** | Therapist + Engineer | Pre-post scores (FIM, Barthel) |
| 7 | **Follow-up & Maintenance** | Engineer + Technician | Service log, repair records |

### 2.2.2 SMART Goal Framework (Mandatory for Clinical Records)

> [!TIP]
> **SMART** is the universally accepted goal-writing standard in clinical rehabilitation. KTU expects this acronym in any 14-mark answer.

- **S** — Specific (clearly defined, who/what/where).
- **M** — Measurable (numerical: distance, time, score).
- **A** — Achievable (realistic for the client's current function).
- **D** — Relevant (linked to the client's life role).
- **T** — Time-bound (deadline: 4 weeks, 12 weeks, etc.).

*Example:* "Patient will ambulate **30 meters** with a **standard walker** in the **hospital corridor** within **4 weeks** to enable toilet transfer."

## 2.3 The Rehabilitation Team (Interdisciplinary Model)

The **WHO Rehabilitation 2030 Call for Action (2017)** emphasizes team-based care. The KTU Module 1 explicitly tests this.

| Team Member | Clinical Role | Engineering Interface |
|---|---|---|
| **Physiatrist** | Medical lead, prescription authority | Final sign-off on device |
| **Rehabilitation Engineer** | Design, customize, evaluate | Leads technical R&D |
| **Physiotherapist (PT)** | Gait, strength, balance | Defines mobility-device specs |
| **Occupational Therapist (OT)** | ADL, upper-limb function | Defines ADL device specs |
| **Prosthetist-Orthotist (P&O)** | Custom sockets, braces | Fabricates and aligns |
| **Speech-Language Pathologist (SLP)** | Communication, swallowing | AAC device spec |
| **Rehab Nurse** | 24-hr care, skin integrity | Pressure-relief device design |
| **Clinical Psychologist** | Motivation, cognition | Cognitive AT selection |
| **Social Worker** | Funding, community re-entry | Resource matching |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interdisciplinary vs Multidisciplinary:**
> - *Multidisciplinary* — each member works in parallel, independent notes.
> - *Interdisciplinary* — joint goal setting, shared notes, **rotating case manager**.
> - *Transdisciplinary* — role-blurring; OT teaches PT stretches, etc.
> KTU examiners reward the word **"interdisciplinary team"** with high marks.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Outcome Measurement Tools

These are the **standardized scales** universally accepted in clinical rehabilitation. KTU Module 1 tests their purposes, scoring ranges, and the domains they measure.

| Tool | Full Name | Domain | Score Range | Higher = ? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FIM** | Functional Independence Measure | ADL + mobility + cognition | **18 – 126** | Better function |
| **Barthel Index** | Barthel Index of ADL | Self-care, continence | **0 – 100** | Better function |
| **Fugl-Meyer** | Fugl-Meyer Assessment | Post-stroke motor recovery | **0 – 226** | Better motor |
| **BBS** | Berg Balance Scale | Static/dynamic balance | **0 – 56** | Better balance |
| **GMFM** | Gross Motor Function Measure | Pediatric CP motor | **0 – 100%** | Better motor |
| **MAS** | Modified Ashworth Scale | Spasticity (0–4) | **0 – 4** | Worse tone |
| **TUG** | Timed Up and Go | Functional mobility | **Time in sec** | Worse if slower |
| **6MWT** | 6-Minute Walk Test | Endurance | **Distance in m** | Better endurance |
| **WHODAS 2.0** | WHO Disability Assessment Schedule | Generic functioning | **0 – 100** | Worse function |
| **MBI** | Modified Barthel Index | ADL | **0 – 100** | Better function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this polarity:** FIM, Barthel, Fugl-Meyer, BBS, GMFM → *higher score = better function*. MAS, TUG time, WHODAS → *higher score = worse function*. KTU often asks this as a **3-mark Part A trap question**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

For clinical practice, the "formulas" are mostly **scoring formulas, conversion formulas, and decision thresholds**.

| Formula / Threshold | Expression | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| **FIM Efficiency** | $\eta_{FIM} = \dfrac{\Delta FIM}{LOS}$ | Where $\Delta FIM$ = FIM gain, $LOS$ = Length of Stay (days). Measures rehab productivity. |
| **Rehab Cost per FIM Point** | $C = \dfrac{Total\ Cost}{\Delta FIM}$ | Engineering-economic audit. |
| **Wheelchair Pressure Threshold** | $P_{capillary} \leq 32\ \text{mmHg}$ (≈ 4.3 kPa) | Cushion design limit; above this → ischemic ulcer risk. |
| **Standing Frame Tolerance** | $\theta \leq 60^\circ$ initial tilt, increase by $\approx 5^\circ$/day | Pediatric rehab protocol. |
| **Cane Force Distribution** | $F_{cane} \approx 25\% \cdot BW$ contralateral | Standard elbow crutch loading rule. |
| **Walker Loading** | $F_{walker} \approx 50\% \cdot BW$ | Standard rolling-walker load-share. |
| **CAD/CAM Pressure Mapping Cell** | $N_{cells} = \dfrac{A_{seat}}{9.6\ \text{cm}^2}$ | 4×4 sensor grid minimum. |
| **GMFM % Score** | $G\% = \dfrac{\sum\ scores}{5 \cdot 88} \cdot 100$ | 88-item pediatric tool. |
| **SMART Goal Specificity Index** | $I_{S} = \dfrac{n_{measurable\ terms}}{n_{total\ terms}}$ | Goal quality audit (≥ 0.6 = good). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars in tables:** All magnitudes (e.g., absolute value, interval) are written as $\vert x \vert$ using `\vert` so that markdown table syntax remains unbroken.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real-World Clinical Settings

1. **Wheelchair & Seating Clinics** — engineer measures pressure mapping, customizes cushion, fits wheelchair per ISO 7176 standards.
2. **Gait Labs** — 3D motion capture (Vicon, BTS), force plates (Kistler), EMG-driven robotic exoskeletons.
3. **Prosthetic Clinics** — CAD/CAM socket design, micro-processor knee tuning (C-Leg, Genium), socket-liner interface pressure.
4. **AAC & Computer Access Labs** — eye-tracking, brain-computer interface (BCI), switch scanning.
5. **Pediatric Rehab** — powered mobility training, gait trainers (Lokomat, Ekso GT), standing frames.
6. **Neurorehab Robotics** — upper-limb (Armeo, InMotion ARM), hand (HandyBot), ankle (Anklebot).
7. **Driver Rehab & Smart Home** — vehicle hand-controls, environmental control units (ECU), voice-activated home automation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — FIM Efficiency Calculation (Clinical Engineering KPI)

The **FIM Efficiency** is the most widely cited **clinical-engineering performance metric** in inpatient rehabilitation units (IRFs). It is mandatory for **U.S. CARF-accredited** and equivalent international centres.

### 3.1.1 Setup

A patient with **right hemiplegia post-stroke** is admitted to an IRF.

- **Admission FIM score:** 54 (moderate dependency)
- **Discharge FIM score:** 96 (modified independence in most ADL)
- **Length of stay (LOS):** 32 days

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Compute the FIM gain $\Delta FIM$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta FIM &= FIM_{discharge} - FIM_{admission} \\
\Delta FIM &= 96 - 54 \\
\Delta FIM &= 42\ \text{points}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute the FIM Efficiency $\eta_{FIM}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{FIM} &= \dfrac{\Delta FIM}{LOS} \\
\eta_{FIM} &= \dfrac{42\ \text{points}}{32\ \text{days}} \\
\eta_{FIM} &= 1.31\ \text{points/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Compare with **industry benchmarks** (U.S. national median for stroke IRF = **1.2 – 1.5 points/day**).

> [!NOTE]
> Since $1.31 \in [1.2,\ 1.5]$, this patient's rehabilitation progression is **on par with the U.S. national median**. Such benchmarking is exactly what a clinical rehabilitation engineer would present at a departmental audit.

**Step 4** — Cost per FIM point (engineering-economic dimension).

If the per-day bed cost is ₹ 8,000, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Total\ Cost &= LOS \times Daily\ Cost = 32 \times 8000 \\
Total\ Cost &= 256{,}000\ \text{INR} \\
C_{FIM} &= \dfrac{Total\ Cost}{\Delta FIM} = \dfrac{256{,}000}{42} \\
C_{FIM} &\approx 6{,}095\ \text{INR per FIM point gained}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wheelchair Cushion Pressure Mapping Threshold

### 3.2.1 Setup

A 70 kg spinal-cord injury (SCI) patient with **T10 level** sits in a custom rigid wheelchair with a 36 cm × 40 cm cushion. The engineer places a **Tekscan CONFORMat** pressure mat (sensor cell = 0.965 cm × 0.965 cm) on the cushion.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Total cushion area.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{seat} &= 36\ \text{cm} \times 40\ \text{cm} \\
A_{seat} &= 1440\ \text{cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Number of sensor cells in the mat.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cell} &= 0.965 \times 0.965 \approx 0.931\ \text{cm}^2 \\
N_{cells} &= \dfrac{A_{seat}}{A_{cell}} = \dfrac{1440}{0.931} \\
N_{cells} &\approx 1546\ \text{cells}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Capillary closure pressure threshold.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{capillary} &\leq 32\ \text{mmHg} \\
1\ \text{mmHg} &\approx 0.133\ \text{kPa} \\
P_{capillary} &\leq 32 \times 0.133\ \text{kPa} \\
P_{capillary} &\leq 4.27\ \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Body weight force on the cushion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= 70\ \text{kg} \times 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2 \\
BW &= 686.7\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5** — Average theoretical pressure if load is uniformly distributed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{seat,m^2} &= 1440 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 0.144\ \text{m}^2 \\
P_{avg} &= \dfrac{BW}{A_{seat}} = \dfrac{686.7}{0.144} \\
P_{avg} &\approx 4769\ \text{Pa} \approx 4.77\ \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Although the **average** is above the **4.27 kPa** capillary threshold, real ischial pressure (concentrated over ~250 cm² under the ischial tuberosities) exceeds 200 mmHg in standard foam — hence the engineering mandate for a **contoured, pressure-redistributing cushion** (e.g., Roho, Jay).

**Step 6** — Clinical decision rule.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{peak,ischial} &< P_{capillary} \\
\therefore \text{Cushion material chosen} &: \text{air-foam hybrid with contour depth} \geq 5\ \text{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Setting a SMART Goal from FIM Sub-Domains

### 3.3.1 Setup

Patient: 58-year-old female, **L MCA stroke**, 2 weeks post-event. Current FIM sub-scores:

- **Self-care** = 12 / 42
- **Sphincter control** = 8 / 14
- **Transfers** = 9 / 21
- **Locomotion** = 5 / 14
- **Communication** = 12 / 14
- **Social cognition** = 18 / 21

Total admission FIM = **64 / 126**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Identify the lowest-scoring domains (SMART priority).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{priority} &= \{ Locomotion\ (5/14),\ Transfers\ (9/21),\ Self-care\ (12/42) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Quantify the **measurable target** (assume 3 FIM points gain per sub-domain in 4 weeks is realistic for sub-acute stroke).

$$
\begin{aligned}
FIM_{target} &= 64 + (3 \times 3) = 64 + 9 = 73\ \text{by Week 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Write the **SMART goal** in standard clinical format.

> *"By **Week 4**, patient will independently (FIM ≥ 6) **ambulate 30 m with a single-point cane on level indoor surface** to enable safe toilet transfer, increasing her Locomotion FIM from **5/14 to 8/14**."*

**Step 4** — Specificity Index $I_{S}$ of the goal.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{measurable} &= \{\text{Week 4, 30 m, single-point cane, level, FIM 8}\} = 5 \\
n_{total} &= 5\ (measurable) + 3\ (qualitative) = 8 \\
I_{S} &= \dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I_{S} \geq 0.6$, the goal passes the **clinical quality gate**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Rehabilitation Service Delivery Models

| Feature | Medical Model | Social Model | Biopsychosocial (ICF) Model | Engineering-Integrated Model |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Disease / impairment cure | Societal barriers | Functioning in context | **Functioning + technology** |
| **Goal** | Eliminate pathology | Remove discrimination | Optimize participation | **Optimize participation via AT** |
| **Lead** | Physician | Activist / user | Multidisciplinary team | **Rehab engineer + team** |
| **Intervention** | Drugs / surgery | Policy / advocacy | Therapy + environment | **Device + therapy + environment** |
| **Outcome** | Cure / remission | Rights / accessibility | Activity & participation | **Functional gain per FIM + AT usage** |
| **KTU 2024 emphasis** | Background only | Background only | **Core** | **Applied (Module 1–5)** |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Clinical Pathway Mapping (Stroke Example)

| Phase | Time-window | Primary Goal | Team Lead | Engineering Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acute** | 0 – 7 days | Medical stabilization, prevent complications | Physiatrist + Nurse | Pressure-relief mattress, positioning wedges |
| **Sub-acute** | 1 – 6 weeks | Restore basic mobility & ADL | PT + OT | Wheelchair prescription, simple orthosis |
| **Inpatient Rehab** | 6 – 12 weeks | Advanced gait & self-care | Rehab engineer + PT | Custom AFO, gait-trainer setup, FES |
| **Community Re-entry** | 3 – 6 months | Social/vocational reintegration | OT + Social worker | ECU, AAC, smart-home retrofit |
| **Long-term Follow-up** | 6 months+ | Maintenance, secondary prevention | Rehab engineer | Device servicing, upgrades |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Clinical Rehabilitation Engineering Workflow (Top-Level)

This block diagram maps the **patient journey** through a clinical rehabilitation engineering service — exactly as required by the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REFERRAL ENTRY<br>Physiatrist Prescription] --> B[CLIENT ASSESSMENT<br>ICF Coding + Impairment Profile]
    B --> C[GOAL SETTING<br>SMART Goals + Outcome Measure Selection]
    C --> D[SOLUTION DESIGN<br>Custom vs Off-the-Shelf Device]
    D --> E[FABRICATION AND ASSEMBLY<br>Workshop or 3D Print Lab]
    E --> F[FITTING AND ALIGNMENT<br>Postural and Pressure Check]
    F --> G[USER TRAINING<br>Therapist-led Sessions]
    G --> H[OUTCOME MEASUREMENT<br>Pre Post FIM Barthel BBS]
    H --> I{DISCHARGE OR CONTINUE}
    I -->|Continue| J[REASSESS AND RE GOAL]
    J --> D
    I -->|Discharge| K[FOLLOW UP AT 1 3 6 12 MONTHS]
    K --> L[LONG TERM MAINTENANCE<br>Service Log and Repair]
```

## 4.2 The ICF Interaction Model (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HC[HEALTH CONDITION<br>Disease or Disorder] --> BF[Body Functions and Structures]
    BF -->|Impairment| ACT[Activities]
    ACT -->|Limitation| PART[Participation]
    PART -->|Restriction| OUT[Disability Experience]
    ENV[Environmental Factors] -.->|Facilitator or Barrier| ACT
    ENV -.->|Facilitator or Barrier| PART
    PER[Personal Factors] -.-> ACT
    PER -.-> PART
    ENG[Rehabilitation Engineering Intervention] -.->|Modifies| ENV
    ENG -.->|Adapts| ACT
    ENG -.->|Supports| PART
```

## 4.3 Interdisciplinary Team Communication Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PMR[Physiatrist Medical Lead] --> ENG[Rehabilitation Engineer]
    PMR --> PT[Physiotherapist]
    PMR --> OT[Occupational Therapist]
    PMR --> PO[Prosthetist Orthotist]
    PMR --> SLP[Speech Language Pathologist]
    PMR --> RN[Rehab Nurse]
    PMR --> PSY[Clinical Psychologist]
    PMR --> SW[Social Worker]
    ENG <--> PT
    ENG <--> OT
    ENG <--> PO
    PT <--> OT
    OT <--> SLP
    OT <--> SW
    PO <--> PT
    RN <--> PT
    PSY <--> SW
    ENG <--> SW
```

## 4.4 Decision-Tree for Assistive Device Selection (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Patient with Activity Limitation] --> S1{Type of Limitation}
    S1 -->|Mobility| S2{Ambulant or Non Ambulant}
    S2 -->|Ambulant| S3[Cane or Crutch or Walker]
    S2 -->|Non Ambulant| S4[Manual or Powered Wheelchair]
    S1 -->|Communication| S5{Verbal or Non Verbal}
    S5 -->|Verbal with issues| S6[Speech Amplifier]
    S5 -->|Non Verbal| S7[AAC Low Tech or High Tech]
    S1 -->|ADL| S8[Adaptive Aids Feeding Dressing Toileting]
    S1 -->|Sensory| S9[Hearing Aid or Tactile Vision Sub]
    S3 --> S10[Trial Fitting and Training]
    S4 --> S10
    S6 --> S10
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Outcome Measure Pre Post]
    S11 --> S12{SMART Goal Achieved}
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Discharge with Follow Up]
    S12 -->|No| S14[Re Goal and Re Design]
    S14 --> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Presentation Rule:** Always draw the **ICF model** as a flowchart, not a paragraph. The Mermaid diagrams above can be directly converted to a hand-drawn sketch in the answer booklet. A 14-mark question that has a flowchart + table gains **at least 2–3 extra marks** over a pure-text answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Rehabilitation Engineering. List any **four** members of the interdisciplinary rehabilitation team and their primary role.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Rehabilitation Engineering** is the systematic application of engineering sciences to design, develop, test, and evaluate technological solutions — including assistive devices, accessible environments, and rehabilitation robotics — for individuals with disabilities, in order to restore, substitute, or augment their functional capabilities.

**Four members of the interdisciplinary rehabilitation team:**

1. **Rehabilitation Engineer** — Designs, customizes, and evaluates assistive technology and rehabilitation devices; leads technical R&D and outcome measurement.
2. **Physiatrist (PM&R Physician)** — Medical lead; prescribes devices, signs off on rehabilitation plan, manages spasticity, pain, and complications.
3. **Physiotherapist (PT)** — Focuses on gross motor function, gait, balance, and strength; specifies mobility-device parameters.
4. **Occupational Therapist (OT)** — Focuses on activities of daily living (ADL), upper-limb function, and workplace reintegration; specifies ADL-device parameters.

*(Any 4 of the 9 listed in the team table of Section 2 are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the WHO-ICF model. Why is it preferred over the older ICIDH model in modern rehabilitation practice?**

**Model Answer:**

The **WHO-ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health, 2001)** is a **biopsychosocial framework** that classifies health and disability at three interacting levels:

- **Body Functions & Structures** (impairments)
- **Activities** (limitations)
- **Participation** (restrictions)
- Modified by **Contextual Factors** (Environmental + Personal)

**Why preferred over ICIDH (1980):**

| Aspect | ICIDH (1980) | ICF (2001) |
|---|---|---|
| **Paradigm** | Medical (deficit-only) | Biopsychosocial |
| **Disability view** | Consequence of disease | Interaction of health + context |
| **Contextual factors** | Absent | Explicit (Environmental + Personal) |
| **Neutrality** | Negative only | Includes facilitators (+) |
| **Scope** | Adults only | Universal (all people) |

The ICF allows **rehabilitation engineers** to identify the **engineering lever** — e.g., a ramp (environmental) reduces participation restriction even if impairment is unchanged.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION A

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Adapted]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** framework. With a neat diagram, explain how **contextual factors** modify functioning. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 45-year-old male **trauma-induced paraplegic** (T8 complete) is referred for wheelchair prescription. Discuss the **clinical assessment, prescription criteria (ISO 7176), and pressure-mapping protocol** you would follow as a clinical rehabilitation engineer. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to A(a) — ICF Framework in Detail (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** The ICF, endorsed by the **WHO 54th Assembly (2001)**, is the international standard for describing and measuring health and disability.

**Components (2 marks):**
- *Body Functions & Structures* — physiological and anatomical.
- *Activities & Participation* — task execution and life involvement.
- *Environmental Factors* — physical, social, attitudinal.
- *Personal Factors* — not coded but acknowledged.

**ICF Qualifiers (1 mark):**
- For body functions: 0 (no impairment) → 4 (complete impairment).
- For activities/participation: 0 (no difficulty) → 4 (complete difficulty).
- For environmental factors: +3 (complete facilitator) → –3 (complete barrier).

**How Contextual Factors Modify Functioning (2 marks):**
- *Facilitators* (e.g., wheelchair, ramp, AAC device) **reduce** activity limitation and participation restriction.
- *Barriers* (e.g., stairs, negative attitudes) **increase** them.
- Engineering interventions primarily operate by *engineering the environment* and *engineering the body–device interface* — thereby acting as **environmental facilitators**.

**Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer ICF Interaction Model in Section 4.2.]

---

### Model Solution to A(b) — Wheelchair Prescription in T8 Paraplegia (7 Marks)

**1. Clinical Assessment (2 marks)**
- **History:** Cause (traffic accident), date, comorbidities, social history, home environment.
- **Physical exam:** Muscle strength (ASIA), range of motion, sitting balance, skin integrity, spasticity (MAS), cognition.
- **Functional assessment:** FIM, Barthel Index, Wheelchair Skills Test.
- **ICF coding:** Body function (b730 muscle power = 4), Activity (d465 moving around = 4), Environmental (e150 buildings = barrier).

**2. Prescription Criteria per ISO 7176 (3 marks)**
- **Seat width** = hip width + 2 × 2 cm clearance.
- **Seat depth** = buttock-to-popliteal length – 2.5 cm.
- **Seat height** = lower-leg length + 5 cm for foot clearance.
- **Backrest height** = floor-to-inferior-scapula angle, usually 40–45 cm.
- **Armrest height** = elbow-to-seat + 2.5 cm.
- **Rear-wheel camber** = 0°–3° for manual, 0° for powered.
- **Cushion** = pressure-redistributing (Roho/Jay); peak pressure < 32 mmHg (4.27 kPa).

**3. Pressure-Mapping Protocol (2 marks)**
- Calibrate the **Tekscan CONFORMat** mat per manufacturer.
- Transfer patient onto mat in sitting posture for 2 minutes.
- Capture at ischial tuberosities, sacrum, coccyx, trochanters, thighs.
- **Acceptance criteria:** peak pressure < 32 mmHg, gradient < 1 mmHg/mm.
- Document grid map, repeat at 1-month follow-up.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION B (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**
**(a)** What is a **SMART goal**? Write a SMART goal for a 60-year-old post-stroke patient with right hemiparesis who needs to regain ambulation. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **7-stage clinical service-delivery model** of a rehabilitation engineering department. With examples, state the role of the **rehabilitation engineer** in at least three stages. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to B(a) — SMART Goal Construction (7 Marks)

**SMART Definition (3 marks):**
- **S** — Specific (clearly defined, who/what/where).
- **M** — Measurable (numerical: distance, time, score).
- **A** — Achievable (realistic for the patient).
- **R** — Relevant (linked to the patient's life role).
- **T** — Time-bound (deadline in weeks/months).

**Sample SMART Goal (4 marks):**

> *"By **Week 6** of inpatient rehabilitation, the 60-year-old male post-stroke patient (Mr. X) with right hemiparesis will **independently (FIM ≥ 6)** ambulate **50 meters on a level indoor hospital corridor** using a **single-point cane and right ankle-foot orthosis (AFO)** to enable safe community ambulation for accessing the washroom and physiotherapy gym."*

**Justification of each element (1 mark):**
- *Specific* — 50 m, single-point cane, right AFO, indoor corridor.
- *Measurable* — FIM ≥ 6 and 50 m.
- *Achievable* — 50 m in 6 weeks is realistic for sub-acute stroke (literature: ~10 m/wk gain).
- *Relevant* — enables washroom access → real-life participation.
- *Time-bound* — 6 weeks.

---

### Model Solution to B(b) — 7-Stage Service Delivery Model (7 Marks)

**The 7 stages** (refer to Section 2.2.1 table for detailed stage listing):

1. **Referral** — physiatrist issues a written prescription with diagnosis, ICF codes, and intended outcome.
2. **Client Assessment** — multi-disciplinary: FIM, gait, strength, ROM, cognition, skin, home environment.
3. **Goal Setting** — SMART goals with timeline, baseline scores, target scores.
4. **Device Design / Fabrication** — CAD/CAM, 3D printing, sensor integration, prototype testing.
5. **Fitting & Training** — alignment, pressure mapping, wheelchair skills training, user + caregiver.
6. **Outcome Measurement** — pre-post FIM, BBS, TUG, user satisfaction (QUEST 2.0).
7. **Follow-up & Maintenance** — 1-, 3-, 6-, 12-month reviews; repair log; upgrade trigger.

**Role of Rehabilitation Engineer in three stages (2 marks):**

- **Stage 4 (Design):** Performs CAD modelling, finite-element analysis of the socket, force/torque simulation, and material selection.
- **Stage 5 (Fitting):** Conducts pressure mapping, adjusts alignment using laser levels, validates ISO 7176 compliance.
- **Stage 6 (Outcome):** Analyses sensor data (accelerometry in wheelchairs, EMG in FES), computes FIM efficiency, generates engineering-audit report.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The rehabilitation engineer is the **technical thread** that links the entire clinical pathway, converting clinical needs into engineered, evidence-based assistive solutions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 1 clinical-practice questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Impairment" with "Disability"** — *Impairment* is at body level, *disability* is the umbrella term including activity and participation. 1 mark lost per misuse.
> 2. **Writing "FIM score is 0 to 100"** — FIM is **18 to 126**. This is a **favourite KTU trap question**; full 3 marks can be lost.
> 3. **Confusing Barthel (0–100) with FIM (18–126)** — easy mark-loser.
> 4. **Forgetting to write the polarity of MAS** — MAS increases with *worse* spasticity; students write "higher = better" and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Not mentioning the *interdisciplinary* nature of the team** — saying "multidisciplinary" alone loses 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Omitting the engineer–clinician interface** — KTU specifically tests this; the answer must show how the engineer interacts with PT, OT, P&O, SLP.
> 7. **Not citing ISO 7176 in wheelchair questions** — instant 2-mark loss in the prescription sub-part.
> 8. **Skipping SMART goal components** — each letter (S, M, A, R, T) is worth 0.5 mark; missing one costs 0.5.
> 9. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — 1–2 marks explicitly reserved for "neat labelled diagram."
> 10. **Writing "ICIDH" instead of "ICF"** — outdated; KTU 2024 strictly follows **ICF**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Rehabilitation Engineering** = systematic application of engineering to disability-related problems (RESNA definition).
- **Clinical Practice** of RE = the *patient-facing* delivery of technology-enabled rehabilitation.
- **ICF** (2001) = biopsychosocial model with **4 components** (b, s, d, e) + personal factors.
- **Three levels:** Impairment → Activity Limitation → Participation Restriction.
- **Contextual factors** (environmental + personal) modify functioning bidirectionally.
- **ICF qualifiers:** 0–4 for problems; +3 to –3 for environmental factors.
- **SMART goal** = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- **Interdisciplinary team** = joint goal setting, rotating case manager; **NOT** multidisciplinary (parallel).
- **Team members:** Physiatrist, PT, OT, P&O, SLP, RN, Psychologist, Social Worker, **Rehab Engineer** (key KTU point).
- **FIM:** 18 – 126 (higher = better, 7-level scale).
- **Barthel Index:** 0 – 100 (higher = better).
- **Fugl-Meyer:** 0 – 226 (post-stroke motor).
- **BBS:** 0 – 56 (balance).
- **MAS:** 0 – 4 (higher = *worse* spasticity).
- **TUG:** time in seconds (higher = *worse* mobility).
- **6MWT:** distance in metres (higher = better endurance).
- **GMFM:** 0 – 100 % (pediatric).
- **FIM Efficiency** $\eta_{FIM} = \dfrac{\Delta FIM}{LOS}$ — typical stroke IRF benchmark: **1.2–1.5 points/day**.
- **Capillary closure pressure:** **32 mmHg ≈ 4.27 kPa** — cushion design threshold.
- **ISO 7176** — wheelchair standards; cite in all wheelchair answers.
- **Cane loading** ≈ 25 % body weight; **walker loading** ≈ 50 % body weight.
- **Rehab 2030 (WHO, 2017)** = call for strengthening rehabilitation in health systems.
- **CARF** (Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities) = international accreditation for rehab centres.
- **Engineering roles in clinical stages:** design, fit, outcome measurement, maintenance.
- **Always draw a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers; this is worth 1–2 marks.
- **Use "I-A-P-C-P" mnemonic** to structure ICF answers.
- **Do not confuse ICIDH (1980)** with **ICF (2001)** — KTU 2024 strictly tests the ICF.

> [!TIP]
> **One-page mnemonic for the viva:**
> *"**C**linical **P**ractice = **I**CF + **S**MART + **F**IM + **I**nterdisciplinary **E**ngineer"*
> — **C-P-I-S-F-I-E** — read as **"CP-ISFIE"** (sounds like "specify").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Rehabilitation Engineering Design: Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Rehabilitation Engineering Design: Design Considerations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rehabilitation Engineering Design** is the systematic, interdisciplinary application of engineering sciences, biomechanics, ergonomics, and human-factors principles to develop assistive devices, robotic systems, and therapeutic interventions that restore, substitute, or augment functional capabilities of individuals with physical, sensory, or cognitive impairments. **Design Considerations** refer to the structured set of constraints, performance specifications, user requirements, safety factors, and regulatory standards that govern the conceptualisation, prototyping, and deployment of rehabilitation technology. In the KTU 2024 context, these considerations are evaluated through the lens of the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** framework published by the **World Health Organization (WHO)**. > [!NOTE] > **ICF Framework Triad** — The WHO-ICF model evaluates disability through three interconnected domains: > 1. **Body Functions & Structures** (Impairment level) > 2. **Activities** (Functional limitation level) > 3. **Participation** (Social handicap level) > > Every rehabilitation engineering design must be mapped to at least one of these domains to validate its clinical relevance. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Custom Tailor" Principle Imagine you walk into a bespoke tailor to get a suit stitched. The tailor doesn't just measure your chest and hand you a generic size-40 blazer. They measure your **shoulder slope, arm length, posture, dominant-hand bias, range of motion**, and even your **occupation** (office vs field). A rehabilitation engineer works the same way — they don't just build a "wheelchair" or a "robotic arm"; they measure the **user's residual capability, joint range of motion (ROM), muscle strength, cognitive load tolerance, home environment, caregiver availability, and financial capacity**. If the tailor ignores your posture, the suit pulls at the shoulders. If the rehabilitation engineer ignores the user's biomechanics, the device causes **secondary injuries, abandonment, or even fatality**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams**: The single most-tested concept in Module 1 is the distinction between **Medical Devices** (regulated by CDSCO/FDA, used for diagnosis/treatment) and **Assistive Devices** (focused on functional independence). Rehabilitation robots straddle both categories. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following **bolded** metrics are universally cited in rehabilitation design literature and frequently appear in KTU numerical problems: - **Standard Human Body Mass: 70 kg** (Reference man, ISO 7250) - **Standard Gravitational Acceleration: 9.81 m/s²** - **Maximum Recommended Push Force for Manual Wheelchair: 50 N** (ANSI/RESNA WC standards) - **Acceptable Noise Level for Rehabilitation Equipment: < 60 dB(A)** (WHO recommendation) - **Maximum Safe Skin Contact Pressure: 4.3 kPa (32 mmHg)** for prolonged sitting (NPUAP guidelines) - **Normal Human Walking Cadence: 100–120 steps/min** - **Adult Upper-Limb Reach Envelope: 600–750 mm** (5th–95th percentile) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anthropometric Percentile Rule**: When designing for a population, KTU questions typically expect you to use the **5th percentile female** (smallest user) for reach/access dimensions and **95th percentile male** (largest user) for clearance/strength dimensions. This is the **Universal Design boundary principle**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Rehabilitation Engineering Design Rehabilitation design is governed by seven interdependent pillars. KTU examiners often frame Part A questions around these pillars. #### Pillar 1: User-Centred Design (UCD) - **Definition**: An iterative design philosophy where the end-user (patient, therapist, caregiver) is involved at every stage — concept, prototype, testing, deployment. - **Why**: Devices designed without user input show **abandonment rates of ~30%** within one year (Phillips & Zhao, 1993 — classic study cited in syllabus). - **KTU Hook**: Always mention the four UCD activities: **Specify context of use → Specify requirements → Produce design solutions → Evaluate against requirements**. #### Pillar 2: Universal Design (UD) - **Seven Principles** (Ronald Mace, NC State University): 1. Equitable Use 2. Flexibility in Use 3. Simple and Intuitive 4. Perceptible Information 5. Tolerance for Error 6. Low Physical Effort 7. Size and Space for Approach and Use - **Why**: A truly universal rehabilitation device reduces stigma and increases adoption. #### Pillar 3: Biomechanical Compatibility - The device must move in **kinematic harmony** with the human joint it assists. - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** mismatch causes compensatory injuries. - Example: A shoulder exoskeleton must allow **3 rotational DOF** at the glenohumeral joint plus **scapulothoracic movement**. #### Pillar 4: Safety & Risk Management - **ISO 14971** — Risk management process for medical devices. - **ISO 13482** — Safety requirements for personal care robots (critical for rehabilitation robots). - **Three Risk Classes (FDA/CDSCO)**: Class I (low), Class II (moderate), Class III (high). - Most rehabilitation robots fall into **Class II** (powered exoskeletons may be Class III). #### Pillar 5: Ergonomic & Anthropometric Fit - Design must accommodate the **5th–95th percentile** of the target population. - Use **anthropometric databases**: ANSUR II (US), DINED (Netherlands), or **Indian Anthropometric Database** (Defence Institute of Physiology, DRDO). #### Pillar 6: Functional & Clinical Efficacy - The device must demonstrably improve the **International Classification of Functioning (ICF)** outcome — measured by FIM (Functional Independence Measure), Barthel Index, or Wolf Motor Function Test. - KTU expects you to know at least one outcome measure. #### Pillar 7: Economic & Social Sustainability - **Cost per Quality-Adjusted Life Year (QALY)** must be reasonable. - Devices priced above a typical Indian middle-class annual income (~₹5–10 lakh) face adoption barriers. - Local manufacturing, modular repair, and battery commonality matter. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---| | Body Segment Inertia (Hanavan Model) | $I_i = k_i \cdot m_i \cdot l_i^2$ | $k \approx 0.4$ for thigh, $0.3$ for shank | Dynamic simulation of assistive devices | | Static Joint Torque | $\tau = m \cdot g \cdot r$ | $\tau$ in N·m | Motor sizing for exoskeletons | | Mechanical Power for Gait Assist | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W or J/s | Battery sizing for powered orthoses | | Safety Factor (Sutro Formula) | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{applied}}$ | $SF \geq 2$ for wearable, $\geq 3$ for load-bearing | Material selection for orthotic frames | | Pressure on Skin (Ischial Tuberosity) | $P = \dfrac{W}{A_{contact}}$ | $P < 4.3$ kPa | Wheelchair cushion design | | Range of Motion Compensation | $\theta_{device} = \theta_{human} - \theta_{residual}$ | degrees | Joint angle matching in robot | | Anthropometric Reach | $R_{95} = \mu_R + 1.645 \cdot \sigma_R$ | mm | Workspace design for robotic arm | | Heart Rate Reserve (Therapy) | $HR_{target} = HR_{rest} + 0.6 \cdot (HR_{max} - HR_{rest})$ | bpm | Cardiovascular-safe exercise robot | | Cost per QALY Threshold | $\dfrac{C_{device}}{QALY_{gained}}$ | $< 1 \times \text{GDP/capita}$ | Economic justification in KTU essay | | ISO 13482 Maximum Force | $F_{contact} \leq 50$ N (quasi-static) | N | Robot-human contact safety | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Tip**: When a question asks for a "design calculation", always start by listing **assumptions** (user mass, joint axis, friction coefficient) before substituting into formulas. Examiners award 2 of 7 marks for assumption clarity. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility Rehabilitation engineering design underpins: - **Powered wheelchairs** (Whill, Permobil) - **Lokomat-type gait trainers** (Hocoma) - **Armeo Power** exoskeletons (Hocoma) - **Indegene's CARLO** — upper-limb rehabilitation robot - **Ekso GT** — lower-limb exoskeleton - **Custom 3D-printed prosthetics** (e-NABLE community) In India, the **Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India (ALIMCO)** and **IIT-Madras' TTK Center for Rehabilitation Research and Device Development (R2D2)** lead this field. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Motor Torque Sizing for an Elbow Exoskeleton > **Problem Scenario (Typical KTU 14-mark Part B)**: Design the elbow flexion torque requirement for a wearable rehabilitation exoskeleton assisting a 70 kg adult to lift the forearm against gravity at full extension. **Given Data**: - User forearm mass: $m_f = 1.5$ kg (5th percentile adult female forearm mass, DINED) - User hand + payload mass: $m_h = 2.5$ kg (rehabilitation ball + hand) - Forearm length: $L = 0.27$ m (elbow to centre of mass of forearm) - Distance to payload: $L_h = 0.40$ m - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1: Identify Worst-Case Posture** The worst-case torque occurs at **full horizontal extension** ($\theta = 90°$ from vertical), where the moment arm is maximised. $$ \theta_{worst} = 90°, \quad \cos(\theta - 90°) = 1 $$ **Step 2: Apply the Static Equilibrium Equation** The total gravitational torque about the elbow joint is the sum of torques from the forearm and the hand/payload. $$ \tau_{total} = (m_f \cdot g \cdot L) + (m_h \cdot g \cdot L_h) $$ **Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{total} &= (1.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.27) + (2.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.40) \\ &= 3.973 + 9.810 \\ &= 13.783 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Apply Safety Factor per ISO 13482** For a wearable rehabilitation device in direct human contact, KTU syllabus recommends $SF = 2.0$. $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{design} &= SF \times \tau_{total} \\ &= 2.0 \times 13.783 \\ &= 27.566 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Select the Standard Motor Rating** Choose the next available standard motor size — typically **30 N·m BLDC** motor (common in rehabilitation robotics, e.g., Maxon EC-i 40). $$ \tau_{motor\_selected} = 30 \text{ N·m} \quad (\text{satisfies } \tau_{design} < \tau_{motor}) $$ **Step 6: Verify the Constraint Inequality** $$ \tau_{design} \leq \tau_{motor\_selected} \Rightarrow 27.566 \leq 30.0 \quad \checkmark $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer**: The elbow exoskeleton motor must be rated at a minimum of **27.57 N·m**, justifying the selection of a **30 N·m BLDC motor**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Wheelchair Cushion Pressure Verification **Problem**: Verify whether a foam cushion of dimensions $40 \text{ cm} \times 40 \text{ cm}$ and effective contact area $1200 \text{ cm}^2$ keeps a 70 kg user below the skin-safe pressure limit. **Step 1: Compute User Weight in Newtons** $$ W = m \cdot g = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7 \text{ N} $$ **Step 2: Convert Contact Area to SI Units** $$ A = 1200 \text{ cm}^2 = 1200 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.12 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 3: Apply the Pressure Equation** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= \dfrac{W}{A} \\ &= \dfrac{686.7}{0.12} \\ &= 5722.5 \text{ Pa} \\ &= 5.72 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compare to the NPUAP Limit** $$ P = 5.72 \text{ kPa} > 4.3 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(FAILS)} $$ **Step 5: Iterative Design Correction** The cushion area must be increased: $$ A_{required} = \dfrac{W}{P_{max}} = \dfrac{686.7}{4300} = 0.1597 \text{ m}^2 = 1597 \text{ cm}^2 $$ Required cushion dimension: $\sqrt{1597} \approx 40 \text{ cm} \times 40 \text{ cm}$ minimum; redesign to $45 \text{ cm} \times 45 \text{ cm}$ for a 1.27× safety margin. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Design Considerations to KTU 2024 Outcomes | Design Consideration | KTU Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Marks Weightage (Typical) | |---|---|---|---| | User-Centred Design Process | CO1 — Fundamentals | Understand | 3 marks (Part A) | | Anthropometric Data Application | CO1 | Apply | 7 marks (Part B) | | Biomechanical Compatibility | CO2 — Design Analysis | Analyse | 7 marks (Part B) | | Safety Standards (ISO 13482/14971) | CO3 — Safety & Risk | Remember | 3 marks (Part A) | | ICF Framework Mapping | CO1 | Remember | 3 marks (Part A) | | Motor Sizing / Torque Calculation | CO2 | Apply | 7 marks (Part B) | | Universal Design Principles | CO1 | Understand | 3–7 marks | | Cost-QALY Justification | CO4 — Socio-Economic | Evaluate | 7 marks (Part B) | > [!NOTE] > The matrix above reflects the **KTU 2024 Scheme CO-Weightage Pattern** derived from past university exam papers (2019–2023). Motor sizing and anthropometry are the most frequently appearing 7-mark problems. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The User-Centred Rehabilitation Design Process (UCD Loop) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Clinical Need Identified]) --> stepB[Specify Context of Use: User, Environment, Tasks] stepB --> stepC[Specify User and Organisational Requirements] stepC --> stepD[Produce Design Solutions: Concepts and Prototypes] stepD --> stepE[Evaluate Designs against User Requirements] stepE --> decisionF{Requirements Met?} decisionF -- No --> stepD decisionF -- Yes --> stepG[Final Prototype and Clinical Trial] stepG --> stepH[ISO 14971 Risk Assessment] stepH --> stepI[Regulatory Submission CDSCO or FDA] stepI --> stepJ[Deployment and Post Market Surveillance] stepJ --> stepK([End: Device in Service]) stepJ -.Feedback.-> stepB ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Block Architecture of a Rehabilitation Robot ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph layer1[User Interface Layer] uiA[Patient Intent EMG or Voice] uiB[Therapist Dashboard GUI] end subgraph layer2[Perception and Sensing Layer] psA[IMU and Joint Encoders] psB[Force Torque Sensors] psC[EMG Signal Acquisition] end subgraph layer3[Decision and Control Layer] dcA[Intention Detection Algorithm] dcB[Adaptive Impedance Controller] dcC[Safety Supervisor ISO 13482] end subgraph layer4[Actuation Layer] aaA[BLDC Motors with Harmonic Drives] aaB[Cable Driven Series Elastic Actuators] end subgraph layer5[Mechanical Interface Layer] miA[Exoskeleton or End Effector] miB[Soft Pneumatic Cuff] end uiA --> dcA uiB --> dcB psA --> dcA psB --> dcB psC --> dcA dcA --> dcB dcB --> aaA dcB --> aaB aaA --> miA aaB --> miB dcC -.Safety Cutoff.-> aaA dcC -.Safety Cutoff.-> aaB ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Design Constraint Hierarchy (Biomechanical → Regulatory → Economic) ```mermaid flowchart LR biomech[Biomechanical Constraints
ROM DOF Joint Axes] --> ergo ergo[Ergonomic Constraints
Anthropometry 5th 95th Percentile] --> safety safety[Safety Constraints
ISO 13482 ISO 14971] --> regulatory regulatory[Regulatory Constraints
CDSCO FDA Class II or III] --> clin clin[Clinical Constraints
ICF FIM Barthel Index] --> economic economic[Economic Constraints
Cost per QALY lt GDP per capita] --> deploy([Deployable Design]) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram**: The arrows indicate that earlier-stage constraints **flow into and constrain** later-stage decisions. A KTU Part B question may ask: "If a patient's wrist ROM is reduced by 50%, which design stage is first affected and why?" — The answer is the **Biomechanical Constraints** node, which cascades to all downstream nodes. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Define Rehabilitation Engineering. List any three design considerations specific to assistive robotic devices.** **Model Answer**: Rehabilitation Engineering is the systematic application of engineering principles — biomechanics, ergonomics, robotics, and control theory — to design devices and systems that assist individuals with disabilities in achieving greater functional independence and quality of life. **Three design considerations**: 1. **User-Centred Design**: Device must be tailored to the user's residual capability, lifestyle, and environment. 2. **Biomechanical Compatibility**: Kinematic match between the device and human joints (DOF alignment, ROM accommodation). 3. **Safety Compliance**: Adherence to ISO 13482 and ISO 14971, including force limits (< 50 N quasi-static contact) and emergency stop mechanisms. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall**: Students often write a generic definition of "engineering" and forget to mention **biomechanics and human factors**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing the interdisciplinary nature of the field. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **Q: Explain the WHO-ICF framework and its relevance to rehabilitation engineering design.** **Model Answer**: The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** is a WHO framework that classifies health and disability through three domains: - **Body Functions and Structures** (Impairment — e.g., loss of grip strength). - **Activities** (Functional limitation — e.g., inability to lift a cup). - **Participation** (Restriction — e.g., inability to dine with family). **Relevance to Design**: Every rehabilitation device should be mapped to at least one ICF domain to justify its clinical purpose. A powered exoskeleton primarily targets **Activity** (re-walking) and **Participation** (community ambulation), not just impairment. This mapping also informs clinical trial outcome measures (FIM, Barthel Index, 10-Metre Walk Test). > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall**: Writing "ICF = International Classification of Function" without expanding the abbreviation costs 0.5 mark. Always write the full form once. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **seven principles of Universal Design** as formulated by Ronald Mace. How do they apply to a powered wheelchair for adult users? **Model Answer**: The **seven principles of Universal Design (UD)** were formulated by architect Ronald Mace at North Carolina State University to guide the design of products and environments usable by all people, without the need for adaptation. 1. **Equitable Use**: The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities. *Wheelchair application*: Same model usable by left-handed and right-handed users; control joystick mountable on either side. 2. **Flexibility in Use**: Accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities. *Wheelchair application*: Adjustable seat height, armrest width, and backrest recline. 3. **Simple and Intuitive**: Easy to understand, regardless of user's experience, knowledge, language skills, or concentration level. *Wheelchair application*: Single-button power on/off; large pictographic icons; minimal training required. 4. **Perceptible Information**: Communicates necessary information effectively to the user, regardless of ambient conditions or sensory abilities. *Wheelchair application*: Tactile button differentiation for visually impaired; audible and visual low-battery alerts. 5. **Tolerance for Error**: Minimizes hazards and adverse consequences of accidental or unintended actions. *Wheelchair application*: Anti-tilt sensors; automatic brake on slope release; emergency stop button. 6. **Low Physical Effort**: Can be used efficiently and comfortably with minimum fatigue. *Wheelchair application*: Powered drive reduces push force from ~50 N to a single finger-press on a joystick; ergonomic seat cushion minimises pressure. 7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use**: Provides appropriate size and space for approach, reach, manipulation, and use. *Wheelchair application*: Designed for 5th–95th percentile body dimensions per ANSI/RESNA; accommodates hip breadth up to 550 mm; ground clearance 100 mm. **Conclusion**: A powered wheelchair that rigorously applies all seven UD principles serves a heterogeneous user base — from children with cerebral palsy to elderly stroke survivors — without stigmatising adaptation. This is the essence of **inclusive rehabilitation technology**. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A **shoulder rehabilitation exoskeleton** must support a 4 kg arm (forearm + hand) at full horizontal extension. The arm's centre of mass is 0.32 m from the shoulder joint.
