Calculate the **design torque** required using a **safety factor of 2.5** and **select a suitable BLDC motor** from: 20 N·m, 30 N·m, 40 N·m. **Model Solution**: **Step 1: Identify the worst-case posture** At full horizontal extension, the moment arm equals the full lever length $L = 0.32$ m. **Step 2: Compute the gravitational force on the arm** $$ F = m \cdot g = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24 \text{ N} $$ **Step 3: Compute the raw torque about the shoulder** $$ \tau_{raw} = F \cdot L = 39.24 \times 0.32 = 12.557 \text{ N·m} $$ **Step 4: Apply the safety factor** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{design} &= SF \times \tau_{raw} \\ &= 2.5 \times 12.557 \\ &= 31.39 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Select the motor** $$ \tau_{design} = 31.39 \text{ N·m} \implies \text{Select } \tau_{motor} = 40 \text{ N·m BLDC} $$ The 30 N·m motor fails the inequality $30 < 31.39$. The **40 N·m BLDC motor** (e.g., Maxon EC-i 52) is the appropriate choice. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall**: Students often forget to **include the safety factor** and select 20 N·m. This loses 2 marks. Always state the safety factor explicitly and apply it before motor selection. --- #### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **anthropometric considerations** in designing a rehabilitation robotic arm for a 5th–95th percentile adult Indian population. Use the **DRDO Indian Anthropometric Database** values. **Model Answer**: **Anthropometry** is the study of human body measurements. In rehabilitation design, anthropometric data ensures the device fits the target population without causing discomfort or injury. For an Indian adult population, the **DRDO Anthropometric Database** (Defence Institute of Physiology and Allied Sciences) provides the following key measurements: | Body Dimension | 5th Percentile (Female) | 50th Percentile | 95th Percentile (Male) | Design Implication | |---|---|---|---|---| | Stature (Height) | 1480 mm | 1620 mm | 1780 mm | Vertical reach envelope | | Sitting Height | 780 mm | 850 mm | 930 mm | Workspace vertical limits | | Shoulder Breadth | 330 mm | 395 mm | 470 mm | Mounting frame clearance | | Arm Length (Acromion–Wrist) | 510 mm | 580 mm | 660 mm | Robot link lengths | | Hand Length | 160 mm | 180 mm | 205 mm | End-effector gripper sizing | | Grip Strength | 180 N | 290 N | 420 N | Force threshold for safe interaction | **Design Rules Applied**: 1. **Reach dimensions** use the **5th-percentile female** value (smallest user must reach controls). Arm reach design: $R_{5\%} = 510$ mm → design reach = 500 mm so all users can access. 2. **Clearance dimensions** use the **95th-percentile male** value (largest user must not be cramped). Shoulder breadth design: $B_{95\%} = 470$ mm → frame clearance ≥ 480 mm. 3. **Force thresholds** use the **5th-percentile grip strength** to ensure even weak users can actuate emergency stop (≈ 10 N) — far below the 180 N minimum. 4. **Adjustability** is added to span the 5th–95th range without custom manufacturing — a 1-DOF sliding shoulder mount covers the 140 mm arm-length range. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall**: Students often quote **Western (ANSUR II) data** instead of Indian data. KTU examiners specifically value **Indian context** for the 2024 scheme. Always cite DRDO or national sources. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** For a **wheelchair cushion** designed for a 70 kg user, the maximum tolerable skin contact pressure is **4.3 kPa** (NPUAP guidelines). If the contact area is $0.12 \text{ m}^2$, verify whether the cushion meets the safety criterion. If not, calculate the **required contact area**. **Model Solution**: **Step 1: Compute the user's weight** $$ W = m \cdot g = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7 \text{ N} $$ **Step 2: Compute the existing contact pressure** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{existing} &= \dfrac{W}{A} \\ &= \dfrac{686.7}{0.12} \\ &= 5722.5 \text{ Pa} \\ &= 5.72 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compare to NPUAP limit** $$ P_{existing} = 5.72 \text{ kPa} > P_{max} = 4.3 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(DESIGN FAILS)} $$ **Step 4: Compute the required area** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{required} &= \dfrac{W}{P_{max}} \\ &= \dfrac{686.7}{4300} \\ &= 0.1597 \text{ m}^2 \\ &= 1597 \text{ cm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Propose a standard cushion size** A standard square cushion of $45 \text{ cm} \times 45 \text{ cm}$ provides $A = 2025 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.2025 \text{ m}^2$, giving a **margin of safety**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{final} &= \dfrac{686.7}{0.2025} \\ &= 3391 \text{ Pa} \\ &= 3.39 \text{ kPa} < 4.3 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer**: The existing $0.12 \text{ m}^2$ cushion is **unsafe**; redesign to at least **$0.1597 \text{ m}^2$** (recommended: $45 \text{ cm} \times 45 \text{ cm}$). > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall**: Forgetting the **Pa to kPa unit conversion** ($5722.5 \text{ Pa} = 5.72 \text{ kPa}$, not $5722.5 \text{ kPa}$) is the most common error. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE** - **Rehabilitation Engineering** = interdisciplinary (biomechanics + ergonomics + robotics + clinical science). - **ICF Triad**: Body Functions → Activities → Participation. Map every device to one domain. - **User-Centred Design (UCD)** has four iterative activities: context, requirements, solutions, evaluation. - **Universal Design** has **seven principles** (Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Tolerant, Low Effort, Sized for All). - **Anthropometric Rule**: 5th-percentile female for **reach**, 95th-percentile male for **clearance**. - **Standard metrics to memorise**: - Reference body mass = **70 kg** - Max push force for manual wheelchair = **50 N** - Max skin pressure = **4.3 kPa** - Walking cadence = **100–120 steps/min** - ISO 13482 max contact force = **50 N quasi-static** - **Motor sizing formula**: $\tau_{design} = SF \cdot m \cdot g \cdot L$ (always apply $SF \geq 2$). - **Safety standards**: ISO 13482 (robots), ISO 14971 (risk), IEC 60601 (medical electrical). - **Pressure formula**: $P = W / A$, with unit conversion $1 \text{ kPa} = 1000 \text{ Pa}$. - **Indian context**: Use **DRDO Anthropometric Database**, not ANSUR II. - **Outcome measures**: FIM, Barthel Index, Wolf Motor Function Test, 10-Metre Walk Test. - **Economic justification**: Cost per QALY < 1× GDP per capita. - **Three FDA/CDSCO classes**: I (low), II (moderate — most rehabilitation robots), III (high — e.g., implantable). - **Common KTU trick**: They may give you a Western dataset and expect you to **comment on Indian applicability** — always add this dimension. - **Figure units**: When the problem gives area in cm², convert to m² **before** computing pressure. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit slips. - **Remember to state assumptions** (user mass, posture, joint axis) before any derivation — earns easy 2 marks. - **Hierarchy in design**: Biomechanical → Ergonomic → Safety → Regulatory → Clinical → Economic constraints. > [!WARNING] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Warning**: Questions on this topic frequently combine **definition + numerical application**. A 14-mark question may begin with "Define UCD (3 marks)" and then ask for an "anthropometric workspace calculation (11 marks)". Read the entire question before answering and partition your time accordingly.

topics.title: Total Quality Management in Rehabilitation Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management in Rehabilitation Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a *comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy* that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, processes, and services by involving all members of the organization — from top management to frontline engineers and clinicians — to meet or exceed **customer (patient) expectations** and **regulatory standards**.

In the context of **Rehabilitation Engineering**, TQM is defined as the systematic application of quality principles, statistical methods, and process improvement tools to the **design, development, manufacturing, clinical delivery, and post-deployment lifecycle** of assistive and rehabilitative devices (e.g., robotic exoskeletons, prosthetics, orthoses, FES systems, and assistive technologies) so that the final user — the **person with disability** — receives a safe, effective, reliable, and dignified intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):** *TQM in Rehabilitation Engineering is the integration of quality planning, quality control, quality assurance, and quality improvement across the entire rehabilitation technology lifecycle, with the person-with-disability (PWD) as the central stakeholder and clinical outcome validation as the ultimate measure of quality.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you run a small restaurant.

- **Without TQM:** The chef cooks whatever he likes, the waiter serves it however he wants, the owner checks the cash register only at month-end. Some customers love the food, some get food poisoning, and nobody knows why.
- **With TQM:** The owner sets clear standards (recipes), the chef is trained and empowered to improve the dish daily, the waiter collects feedback after every meal, the kitchen tracks food-safety temperature logs, and every bad review triggers a "why did this happen?" investigation.

The restaurant doesn't just *try* to be good — it **engineers goodness into every step**.

**Rehabilitation Engineering TQM is the same idea, but the "customer" is a person with a disability, and the "food" is a robotic gait trainer, a prosthetic limb, or a home-assistive robot.** Quality means:

1. The device **does not harm** the patient (Safety).
2. The device **achieves its clinical goal** (Efficacy).
3. The device is **usable by the patient** in real life (Usability/Accessibility).
4. The service is **delivered on time, every time** (Reliability).
5. The patient **feels dignified and empowered** (Human-Centered Design).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a regular factory, the "customer" is the buyer. In Rehabilitation Engineering, the **end-user is a vulnerable person with functional limitations** — so the tolerance for "low quality" is essentially **zero**. A failing batch of phones hurts a wallet; a failing batch of exoskeletons can hurt a spine.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical/Performance Constants

| Metric | Symbol / Tool | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | DPMO | Six Sigma Benchmark |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ | ≥ 1.33 (industry), ≥ 1.67 (medical devices) |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | MTBF | Hours / Cycles |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | MTTR | Hours |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | OEE ≥ 85% | World-class benchmark |
| **Customer (Patient) Satisfaction Score** | CSAT / NPS | % satisfaction |
| **Functional Independence Measure** | FIM | 18–126 scale |
| **Barthel Index** | BI | 0–100 scale |
| **ISO 13485** | Medical Device QMS | Global regulatory standard |
| **ISO 9001** | Generic QMS | Foundation standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For rehabilitation devices, the relevant QMS standard is **ISO 13485:2016** (Medical Devices — Quality Management Systems), layered on top of the generic **ISO 9001** framework. The U.S. FDA enforces **21 CFR Part 820** (Quality System Regulation), and the EU enforces the **Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745)**.

---

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of TQM in Rehabilitation

```
        ┌──────────────────────┐
        │   PERSON WITH        │
        │   DISABILITY (PWD)   │  ← Central Stakeholder
        └──────────┬───────────┘
                   │
       ┌───────────┼───────────┐
       │           │           │
   ┌───▼───┐  ┌────▼────┐  ┌───▼───┐
   │PRODUCT│  │ PROCESS │  │PEOPLE │
   │QUALITY│  │QUALITY  │  │QUALITY│
   └───────┘  └─────────┘  └───────┘
```

- **Product Quality** → Device safety, durability, accuracy of actuation.
- **Process Quality** → Manufacturing, fitting, training, follow-up.
- **People Quality** → Clinician competence, patient empowerment, caregiver training.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Evolution of Quality Thinking (Historical Context)

TQM did not appear overnight. It evolved through **five distinct eras**, each adding a new layer of understanding:

1. **Inspection Era (Pre-1920s):** Quality = detect bad products and throw them away.
2. **Quality Control Era (1920s–1950s):** Statistical sampling (Shewhart control charts) introduced.
3. **Quality Assurance Era (1950s–1980s):** Build quality *into* the system, not just inspect the output.
4. **Strategic Quality Management (1980s–1990s):** Quality becomes a competitive weapon (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum).
5. **Total Quality Management (1990s–Today):** Organization-wide, customer-driven, continuous improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Rehabilitation Engineering, we are firmly in Era 5**, but the field adds a unique constraint: the "customer" is also the **clinical subject** — so quality must satisfy *both* the engineering and the medical communities simultaneously.

---

### 2.2 The Core Principles of TQM (Deming + Juran + ISO Framework)

TQM rests on **8 universally accepted principles**, each directly applicable to rehabilitation engineering:

| # | Principle | Definition | Rehabilitation Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | The PWD (and clinician) defines quality. | Therapy robots must match patient impairment level and goals. |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction. | Hospital rehab dept. head drives adoption of evidence-based robotics. |
| 3 | **Involvement of People** | People at all levels are the essence of an organization. | Train physiotherapists, technicians, caregivers in device use. |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | A desired result is achieved more efficiently when activities are managed as a process. | Map the full patient journey: prescription → fitting → training → follow-up. |
| 5 | **System Approach to Management** | Identifying, understanding, and managing interrelated processes. | Integrate engineering, clinical, and regulatory silos. |
| 6 | **Continual Improvement** | Should be a permanent objective. | Each device generation must improve torque control, comfort, AI adaptability. |
| 7 | **Factual Approach to Decision Making** | Effective decisions are based on data and information. | Use FIM scores, gait analysis, EMG data — not anecdotal feedback. |
| 8 | **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** | Enhances the ability to create value. | Sensor suppliers, actuator vendors, software partners must be ISO 13485 certified. |

---

### 2.3 The TQM Toolset (The "Seven Basic Tools" of Quality)

These are the **mandatory problem-solving tools** a KTU student must know. Every rehabilitation engineering project will use at least 3 of them.

| # | Tool | Purpose | Rehab Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone)** | Identify root causes of a problem. | Why do patients abandon exoskeletons? (Cost, weight, cosmesis, training gap) |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Structured data collection. | Log daily device failures, patient complaints. |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | Monitor process stability over time. | Track torque-accuracy drift in a robotic joint. |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Show frequency distribution. | Distribution of patient recovery times post-stroke. |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Prioritize the vital few vs. trivial many. | 80% of complaints come from 20% of device features. |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Show correlation between two variables. | Therapy-session count vs. FIM score improvement. |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Map a process visually. | Patient onboarding → assessment → device fitting → training → discharge. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 80/20 Rule (Pareto Principle):** Roughly **80% of problems come from 20% of causes**. In a rehab clinic, 80% of robotic-therapy dropouts may come from 20% of causes (e.g., poor fitting, lack of caregiver support).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Quantitative Tools)

These are the equations a KTU 2024 student must be able to *write from memory* and *apply* in Part B questions.

#### (a) Six Sigma Process Capability

$$DPMO = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities} \times \text{Number of Units}} \times 10^6$$

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

> **Units:** dimensionless. **Benchmarks:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ = capable, $\geq 1.67$ = medical-grade.

#### (b) Reliability & Availability

$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$$

$$\text{Availability} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

#### (c) Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

$$OEE = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

> **Units:** percentage. **World-class:** $\geq 85\%$.

#### (d) Cost of Quality (COQ)

$$COQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}$$

> **Key TQM insight:** Investing in prevention ($C_{prevention}$) is **always cheaper** than paying for external failures (recalls, lawsuits, patient injury).

#### (e) Patient Outcome Improvement (Therapy Effectiveness)

$$\Delta FIM = FIM_{discharge} - FIM_{admission}$$

$$\%\,Improvement = \frac{\Delta FIM}{FIM_{max} - FIM_{admission}} \times 100\%$$

$$NRS_{eff} = \frac{\text{Number of Patients with Clinically Meaningful Improvement}}{\text{Total Number of Patients}} \times 100\%$$

#### (f) Process Sigma Level

$$\sigma\text{ level} = \frac{(USL - LSL)}{2 \times \text{StDev of process}}$$

> **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = near-perfect process.**

---

### 2.5 The TQM Trilogy (Juran's Framework) — Applied to Rehabilitation

Joseph Juran defined quality management as a **three-part process**:

| Juran's Component | Definition | Rehab Engineering Translation |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Planning** | Identify customers & their needs, design a process to meet them. | Identify PWD functional goals; design device specs; plan clinical trial. |
| **Quality Control** | Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; act on the difference. | Monitor patient outcomes weekly; compare to expected FIM gain. |
| **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement. | Iteratively redesign device based on patient feedback and outcome data. |

---

### 2.6 TQM Methodologies Relevant to Rehabilitation

| Methodology | Origin | Application in Rehab |
|---|---|---|
| **PDCA / Deming Cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) | Deming | Iterative device refinement & clinical workflow optimization. |
| **Six Sigma (DMAIC)** | Motorola | Reducing device defect rates, improving therapy consistency. |
| **Lean** | Toyota | Eliminating waste in clinical pathways (wait times, redundant steps). |
| **Kaizen** | Japanese | Continuous, incremental, employee-driven improvement. |
| **FMEA** (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis) | Aerospace / Medical | Pre-emptively identify device failure risks. |
| **Poka-Yoke** (Mistake-Proofing) | Toyota | Design devices that physically prevent user errors. |
| **BPMN** (Business Process Modeling) | Software | Map and optimize the patient-rehab journey. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Students are expected to know the **PDCA cycle by heart** and be able to draw it for any rehab engineering process (e.g., "fitting a robotic knee orthosis to a post-polio patient").

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

TQM in Rehabilitation Engineering is **not optional** — it is **legally mandated** in most jurisdictions:

- **ISO 13485** certification is required to sell medical/rehab devices in the EU, Canada, Japan, Australia.
- **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** governs the U.S. market.
- **MDR 2017/745** (EU) requires **post-market surveillance**, **clinical evaluation**, and **risk management (ISO 14971)** for every rehab device.
- **Clinical outcome validation** is the ultimate TQM metric — a device that is technically excellent but clinically useless is, by TQM definition, **low quality**.

> A device that is "perfect on paper" but is **abandoned by 60% of patients** has a TQM failure rate of 60%, regardless of its internal engineering.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Process Capability ($C_{pk}$) of a Robotic Knee Joint Torque Sensor

**Scenario:** A rehabilitation robot's knee joint must deliver torque within the specification **$15\text{ Nm} \pm 2\text{ Nm}$** (i.e., $\text{USL} = 17\text{ Nm}$, $\text{LSL} = 13\text{ Nm}$). A quality engineer collects $n = 50$ measurements of actual delivered torque.

**Given (assumed sample data summary):**
- Sample mean: $\mu = 14.8\text{ Nm}$
- Sample standard deviation: $\sigma = 0.6\text{ Nm}$

**Step 1 — State the goal.**
We must determine whether the manufacturing process is **capable** of meeting the specification. The relevant index is $C_{pk}$ (because the process mean is *not* perfectly centered).

**Step 2 — Write the formula.**

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the upper-side index.**

$$\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{17 - 14.8}{3 \times 0.6} = \frac{2.2}{1.8} = 1.2222$$

**Step 4 — Compute the lower-side index.**

$$\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{14.8 - 13}{3 \times 0.6} = \frac{1.8}{1.8} = 1.0000$$

**Step 5 — Take the minimum.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.2222,\ 1.0000) = 1.0000$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $< 1.00$ | Process is **NOT capable** (defects likely) |
| $1.00$ to $1.33$ | Marginally capable (needs monitoring) |
| $1.33$ to $1.67$ | Capable (typical industrial benchmark) |
| $\geq 1.67$ | Highly capable (medical-device benchmark) |

**Conclusion:** With $C_{pk} = 1.00$, the process is **borderline**. For a *rehabilitation* device (where under-torque can cause patient falls and over-torque can cause injury), the process must be improved to **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$**.

**Step 7 — Recommendation.** Reduce process variation ($\sigma$) by half → $\sigma' = 0.3\text{ Nm}$:

$$C_{pk}^{new} = \min\!\left(\frac{2.2}{0.9},\ \frac{1.8}{0.9}\right) = \min(2.44,\ 2.00) = 2.00$$

Now the process is **excellent** and safe for clinical deployment.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Availability of a Robotic Gait Trainer in a Hospital

**Scenario:** A clinic operates a **Lokomat-style robotic gait trainer** for 12 hours/day. Over 90 days, the device failed 6 times, with total repair time of 18 hours.

**Step 1 — Compute MTBF.**

$$MTBF = \frac{12 \times 90}{6} = \frac{1080}{6} = 180\ \text{hours/failure}$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTTR.**

$$MTTR = \frac{18}{6} = 3\ \text{hours/repair}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Availability.**

$$\text{Availability} = \frac{180}{180 + 3} = \frac{180}{183} = 0.9836 = 98.36\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

For a clinical rehab device, target availability is **$\geq 99\%$**. The current 98.36% means the device is unavailable about **22 hours/month** — significant for a high-demand clinic.

**Step 5 — Action (TQM Continual Improvement).**
- Add preventive maintenance every 50 hours → reduces failures.
- Stock critical spares → reduces MTTR.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Pareto Analysis of Patient Dropouts in a Robotic Therapy Program

A clinic tracks reasons why 100 stroke patients stopped robotic therapy. Tally:

| Reason | Count |
|---|---|
| Device too heavy / bulky | 32 |
| Cost of therapy | 18 |
| Lack of caregiver support | 14 |
| Discomfort / skin irritation | 10 |
| Travel difficulty | 8 |
| No perceived benefit | 6 |
| Other | 12 |

**Step 1 — Compute cumulative percentages.**

| Reason | Count | % | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Device too heavy / bulky | 32 | 32% | 32% |
| Cost of therapy | 18 | 18% | 50% |
| Lack of caregiver support | 14 | 14% | 64% |
| Discomfort / skin irritation | 10 | 10% | 74% |
| Travel difficulty | 8 | 8% | 82% |
| No perceived benefit | 6 | 6% | 88% |
| Other | 12 | 12% | 100% |

**Step 2 — Apply the 80/20 rule.**
The first **3 reasons (Device weight, Cost, Caregiver support)** account for **64%** of all dropouts. Extending to the first 4 reaches **74%**; first 5 reaches **82%**.

**Step 3 — TQM Action.**
Focus the next design cycle on **reducing device weight** (e.g., use carbon-fiber composites), **securing insurance coverage**, and **training caregivers** — these 2–3 actions will eliminate the majority of dropouts.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TQM Tools ↔ Rehabilitation Scenarios

| TQM Tool | Typical Question Answered | Rehab Scenario | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fishbone (6M)** | Why is device return rate high? | Man: clinician training gap; Machine: actuator failure; Material: cable wear; Method: poor fitting; Measurement: no outcome tracking; Environment: home humidity. | Root cause map. |
| **Pareto** | Which complaints to fix first? | 80% of complaints from 20% of causes. | Prioritized action list. |
| **Control Chart** | Is our process stable? | Monitor FIM score gain per week. | Stability verdict. |
| **FMEA** | What could fail in an exoskeleton? | RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection. | Risk-prioritized redesign list. |
| **PDCA** | How to iteratively improve? | Plan new protocol → Do trial → Check outcomes → Act (adopt/revise/abandon). | Continuous improvement loop. |
| **Histogram** | Is my data normal? | Distribution of patient recovery times. | Visual frequency check. |
| **Scatter** | Is there correlation? | Therapy hours vs. FIM gain. | Correlation coefficient. |

---

### 3.5 FMEA Numerical Example (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis) — Knee Orthosis

For a robotic knee orthosis, three failure modes are scored 1–10:

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Actuator over-torque | 9 | 4 | 3 | 108 |
| Sensor calibration drift | 7 | 5 | 5 | 175 |
| Battery sudden failure | 10 | 2 | 4 | 80 |

**RPN ranking:** Sensor drift (175) > Actuator over-torque (108) > Battery failure (80).

**TQM action:** Prioritize redesigning the calibration routine and adding self-test diagnostics to the sensor, since they have the highest RPN.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Deming PDCA Cycle Adapted to a Rehabilitation Robotics Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN: Define patient needs and device specs"] --> B["DO: Prototype device and run pilot trial"]
    B --> C["CHECK: Measure FIM scores, safety events, patient feedback"]
    C --> D{"Did outcomes meet targets?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["ACT: Standardize and deploy at scale"]
    D -- No --> F["Identify root cause via Fishbone or FMEA"]
    F --> A
    E --> G["Continuous improvement: new PDCA cycle for next iteration"]
    G --> A
```

**Reading guide:**
- Start at **PLAN** (top-left).
- The **CHECK** step decides whether to **ACT** (success path) or to loop back to **PLAN** with a new hypothesis.
- The bottom loop **"Continuous improvement: new PDCA cycle"** is the heart of TQM — *there is no end-state*.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: TQM System Architecture for a Rehabilitation Device Manufacturer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Leadership["LEADERSHIP & POLICY"]
        L1["Quality Policy aligned with ISO 13485"]
        L2["Quality Objectives e.g. Cpk greater than 1.67"]
    end

    subgraph Planning["QUALITY PLANNING - Juran P"]
        P1["Identify PWD needs and clinical goals"]
        P2["Design V&V protocol"]
        P3["Risk Management per ISO 14971"]
    end

    subgraph Control["QUALITY CONTROL - Juran C"]
        C1["Incoming material inspection"]
        C2["In-process control charts on torque"]
        C3["Final device acceptance test"]
        C4["Post-market surveillance"]
    end

    subgraph Improvement["QUALITY IMPROVEMENT - Juran I"]
        I1["PDCA cycles on field complaints"]
        I2["FMEA updates every 12 months"]
        I3["Kaizen workshops with clinicians"]
    end

    subgraph People["PEOPLE & TRAINING"]
        T1["Engineer competence matrix"]
        T2["Clinician certification"]
        T3["Patient and caregiver education"]
    end

    Leadership --> Planning
    Leadership --> Control
    Leadership --> Improvement
    Planning --> Control
    Control --> Improvement
    Improvement --> Planning
    People -.-> Planning
    People -.-> Control
    People -.-> Improvement
```

**Reading guide:**
- The three blue subgraphs (**Planning / Control / Improvement**) form Juran's trilogy.
- **People & Training** is shown as a *lateral* influence — it touches all three.
- The arrows form a **closed feedback loop**: improvement feeds back into planning.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Ishikawa (Fishbone) — Why Do Patients Abandon Robotic Therapy?

```mermaid
fishbone
    Problem["High patient dropout rate from robotic therapy"]
    Measurement["MEASUREMENT"] --> M1["No outcome tracking"]
    Measurement --> M2["Subjective clinician notes only"]
    Measurement --> M3["Patient self-report not captured"]
    Method["METHOD"] --> P1["No structured onboarding"]
    Method --> P2["Inconsistent therapy schedules"]
    Method --> P3["Lack of caregiver involvement"]
    Material["MATERIAL / DEVICE"] --> D1["Exoskeleton too heavy"]
    Material --> D2["Battery life too short"]
    Material --> D3["Skin irritation from cuff"]
    Machine["MACHINE / TECH"] --> T1["Frequent calibration drift"]
    Machine --> T2["Software crashes during session"]
    Machine --> T3["Sensor noise on EMG"]
    Manpower["MANPOWER"] --> H1["Therapist training gap"]
    Manpower --> H2["Insufficient technical staff"]
    Manpower --> H3["Language barrier with PWD"]
    Environment["ENVIRONMENT"] --> E1["No accessible transport"]
    Environment --> E2["Hot and humid clinic"]
    Environment --> E3["Home space not adapted"]
    Measurement --> Problem
    Method --> Problem
    Material --> Problem
    Machine --> Problem
    Manpower --> Problem
    Environment --> Problem
```

**Reading guide:** This is a complete 6M Ishikawa diagram. Each branch is a *category* of causes; each sub-leaf is a *specific* potential cause. The team would now vote on which causes to investigate first using **Pareto analysis**.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: The Quality Loop for a Robotic Exoskeleton Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Concept and clinical need"] --> B["Design input specifications"]
    B --> C["Prototype build"]
    C --> D["Verification and Validation testing"]
    D --> E{"Pass safety and efficacy?"}
    E -- No --> F["Design changes"]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G["Regulatory submission FDA CE"]
    G --> H["Clinical deployment"]
    H --> I["Post-market surveillance"]
    I --> J["Field data and complaint analysis"]
    J --> K{"Quality issues detected?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["CAPA Corrective and Preventive Action"]
    L --> B
    K -- No --> M["Continuous improvement ideas"]
    M --> B
```

**Reading guide:** This shows the **closed-loop quality cycle** mandated by ISO 13485. The **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action) subsystem is the legal mechanism that turns field complaints into design improvements.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Total Quality Management (TQM) as applied to Rehabilitation Engineering. Mention any two ISO standards relevant to it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TQM in Rehabilitation Engineering is an organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, processes, and services to meet or exceed patient (PWD) and regulatory expectations across the entire lifecycle of a rehabilitative or assistive device (1 mark). It integrates quality planning, quality control, quality assurance, and continuous improvement (1 mark).
Relevant ISO standards: **ISO 13485:2016** (Medical Device QMS) and **ISO 14971** (Application of risk management to medical devices) — 1 mark for both.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**List and briefly explain any three of the "Seven Basic Tools of Quality" with one rehabilitation-engineering example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per tool):**
1. **Pareto Chart** — 80/20 prioritization. *Example:* 80% of exoskeleton dropouts come from 20% of causes (e.g., weight, cost, comfort).
2. **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** — Root-cause analysis across 6Ms. *Example:* Identifying why a robotic knee orthosis drifts out of calibration.
3. **Control Chart** — Monitors process stability. *Example:* Tracking weekly mean torque delivery of a rehab robot.
*(Or Check Sheet, Histogram, Scatter, Flowchart — any three.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **core principles of Total Quality Management** with specific reference to Rehabilitation Engineering. Discuss how each principle influences the design and delivery of a **robotic gait-training device** for post-stroke patients. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **PDCA (Deming) cycle** in detail. For a clinic introducing a **robotic upper-limb therapy system**, design a complete PDCA cycle covering one full iteration of process improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-A (a)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** TQM rests on 8 universally accepted principles codified in ISO 9000/13485. In Rehabilitation Engineering these principles directly affect patient safety, clinical efficacy, and device usability.

**Principle-by-principle (5 marks — short paragraphs):**
1. **Customer Focus:** The "customer" in rehab is multi-layered — the patient (PWD), the prescribing clinician, the payer, and the caregiver. For a gait trainer, this means the device must match the patient's height, weight, and motor capacity, not just meet engineering specs.
2. **Leadership:** The hospital's rehabilitation director must champion evidence-based robotic therapy and ensure proper resource allocation.
3. **Involvement of People:** Engineers, physiotherapists, and patients must collaborate. Technicians must be trained to recalibrate the device; patients must be trained to use it confidently.
4. **Process Approach:** The patient journey (assessment → prescription → fitting → therapy → reassessment → discharge) must be mapped and standardized.
5. **System Approach:** The device is one node in a larger system of clinical workflow, maintenance, and data analytics.
6. **Continual Improvement:** Each new device generation must improve adaptability, comfort, and AI-driven personalization.
7. **Factual Decision-Making:** Use FIM scores, gait symmetry indices, and EMG data — not impressions.
8. **Supplier Relationships:** Sensor and actuator vendors must be ISO 13485 certified.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** TQM transforms a gait-training robot from a *machine* into a *clinical service*, ensuring that technology translates into measurable patient benefit.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-A (b)**

**Introduction to PDCA (2 marks):** The PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle, popularized by W. Edwards Deming, is a four-stage iterative loop for continuous improvement. It is mandated by ISO 13485 §8.1 and §8.2.

**Application to Robotic Upper-Limb Therapy (5 marks):**

| Stage | Action |
|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Define objective: *increase patient adherence to the robotic upper-limb program from 60% to 85% over 3 months*. Identify current process: patient referral → therapist screening → device fitting → 20 sessions → discharge. Hypothesize root causes via Fishbone: training gap, device discomfort, scheduling conflict. |
| **DO** | Pilot the new process on 10 patients: structured 30-min pre-fitting orientation, redesigned ergonomic cuff, and a flexible evening session slot. Document deviations. |
| **CHECK** | After 3 months, measure: (i) adherence rate, (ii) FIM upper-body sub-score gain, (iii) patient satisfaction CSAT, (iv) number of device-related complaints. Compare against targets using **control charts**. |
| **ACT** | If targets met (e.g., adherence = 87%, FIM gain = +12, CSAT = 90%): **standardize** the new process across the department. If not met: revise the plan and re-enter the PDCA cycle. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The PDCA cycle institutionalizes **evidence-based iterative improvement** — the central tenet of TQM in clinical rehabilitation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**? Construct a complete FMEA table for a **robotic exoskeleton for spinal-cord-injury patients**, identifying at least **five failure modes** and computing the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **ISO 13485** and **post-market surveillance** in ensuring the long-term quality of rehabilitation devices. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-B (a)**

**Definition (1 mark):** FMEA is a structured, systematic method for identifying potential failure modes of a product/process, evaluating their effects, and prioritizing corrective actions using the **RPN = S × O × D** index (S = Severity, O = Occurrence, D = Detection; each scored 1–10).

**FMEA Table for Robotic Exoskeleton (5 marks):**

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Actuator over-torque | Patient joint injury | 9 | 3 | 4 | **108** |
| 2 | Battery sudden cutoff | Patient fall | 10 | 2 | 5 | **100** |
| 3 | EMG sensor noise | Wrong assistance level | 6 | 5 | 6 | **180** |
| 4 | Hip-strap breakage | Patient drop | 9 | 2 | 3 | **54** |
| 5 | Software crash mid-session | Therapy interruption | 7 | 4 | 4 | **112** |
| 6 | Calibration drift | Inaccurate gait pattern | 7 | 6 | 5 | **210** |

**Prioritization (1 mark):** Highest RPN = Calibration drift (210) → redesign the self-calibration routine and add automated diagnostic alerts. Next: EMG sensor noise (180).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** FMEA is a *proactive* TQM tool — it identifies risks **before** they reach the patient, satisfying ISO 14971 risk-management requirements.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q-B (b)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** ISO 13485:2016 is the internationally recognized QMS standard for medical-device manufacturers, including rehabilitation robotics. Post-market surveillance (PMS) is the regulatory mechanism for monitoring device performance *after* clinical release.

**Role of ISO 13485 (3 marks):**
- Mandates a **documented QMS** covering design, production, installation, and servicing.
- Requires **risk management** (linked to ISO 14971) and **clinical evaluation** (linked to ISO 14155).
- Enforces **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action) and **management review** processes.
- Required for CE marking under EU MDR 2017/745 and for FDA 510(k) submissions.

**Role of Post-Market Surveillance (3 marks):**
- Collects real-world data on device performance, adverse events, and patient outcomes.
- Triggers **field safety corrective actions (FSCAs)** and recalls when needed.
- Feeds data back into the **design history file (DHF)** for the next-generation device.
- Examples: tracking exoskeleton battery degradation, monitoring skin-pressure incidents, capturing patient-reported outcomes (PROs).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** ISO 13485 + PMS closes the **quality loop** — from initial design through years of clinical use — ensuring that rehabilitation devices remain *safe, effective, and trusted* throughout their lifecycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse ISO 9001 (generic QMS) with ISO 13485 (medical-device QMS). KTU examiners *always* deduct marks for this.
> 2. **Do NOT** write "TQM = ISO certification." TQM is a *philosophy*; ISO 13485 is *one* framework that supports it.
> 3. **Always** mention the **person with disability (PWD)** as the central stakeholder — generic factory examples lose marks.
> 4. **Always** include **clinical outcome validation** (FIM, Barthel Index, gait symmetry) in any answer about "quality" of a rehab device.
> 5. In FMEA, **never forget** the formula: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, with each on a 1–10 scale.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **TQM Definition** = organization-wide, customer-driven, continuous improvement → applied to **rehab device lifecycle** with PWD as the customer.
- **Juran's Trilogy** = **P**lanning, **C**ontrol, **I**mprovement — must be in every long answer.
- **Deming's PDCA** = Plan → Do → Check → Act — *iterative*, *never-ending*.
- **8 TQM Principles** = customer, leadership, people, process, system, continual improvement, facts, suppliers.
- **7 Basic Quality Tools** = Cause-and-effect, Check sheet, Control chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart.
- **FMEA** = RPN = S × O × D; **prioritize highest RPN**.
- **Six Sigma** = 3.4 DPMO; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for medical devices.
- **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) → target ≥ 99% for clinical rehab devices.
- **OEE** = A × P × Q → world-class ≥ 85%.
- **Pareto 80/20** → focus on the "vital few" causes.
- **ISO 13485** = medical-device QMS (NOT ISO 9001 alone).
- **ISO 14971** = risk management for medical devices.
- **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** + **EU MDR 2017/745** = regulatory anchors.
- **Clinical metrics** = FIM (18–126), Barthel Index (0–100), gait symmetry, EMG-based outcomes.
- **Cost of Quality (COQ)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- **PWD is the central stakeholder** — never write a TQM answer without referencing the patient.
- **Six Sigma tools in rehab** → DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) for therapy-process optimization.
- **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** → design rehab devices that *physically* prevent dangerous misuse.
- **Closed-loop quality** = design → V&V → deployment → PMS → CAPA → redesign.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel as a Structural Material
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steel as a Structural Material

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Steel as a Structural Material

**Steel** is defined (per **IS 2062:2011** and **KTU 2024 syllabus** terminology) as an **alloy of iron (Fe) and carbon (C)** in which the carbon content typically lies between **0.05% and 2.0%** by weight, with manganese (Mn) usually limited to **≤ 1.5%**. When used as a *structural material*, it refers to a category of **medium- and high-strength rolled sections, plates, and hollow sections** specifically manufactured to bear design loads in engineered frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"Steel as a structural material is a ferrous alloy with controlled carbon content (≤ 0.25% for structural grades), possessing a unique combination of high tensile strength, ductility, weldability, and predictable elastic–plastic response, making it the primary load-bearing material in modern skeletal frameworks."*

## 1.2 Key Quantitative Identity of Structural Steel

| Parameter | Standard Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Density | **7850 kg/m³** | ρ |
| Young's Modulus | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)** | E |
| Shear Modulus | **0.769 × 10⁵ N/mm²** | G |
| Poisson's Ratio | **0.30** | ν |
| Thermal Expansion | **12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | α |
| Yield Stress (Fe 250) | **250 MPa** | fy |
| Yield Stress (Fe 410) | **410 MPa** | fy |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Why Steel is the "Skeleton"

Think of a building as a **human body** and steel as the **skeletal bones**:
- Just as **bones** (calcium + collagen) provide rigidity *and* slight flexibility, steel provides stiffness *and* ductility.
- Just as the **vertebral column** absorbs shocks, steel absorbs seismic/impact energy through its **plastic deformation zone**.
- In **rehabilitation robotics**, the **aluminium/steel frame of an exoskeleton** acts exactly like a bone — it transmits actuator force to the patient's limb while remaining light enough to wear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world connection to Robotic Rehabilitation:**
> The **rigid links** of a gait-training exoskeleton (e.g., LOKOMAT, ReWalk) are typically built from **high-grade structural steel or titanium-steel composites** to ensure repeatable kinematics under cyclic loading of ~2,000 N.

## 1.4 Why Steel? — The Three Pillars

1. **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio** → Lighter than concrete for same load capacity.
2. **Ductility** → Warns before failure (visible deflection).
3. **Homogeneity & Isotropy** → Predictable behaviour in all directions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Composition of Structural Steel

Structural steel is **not pure iron**. Its behaviour is governed by its precise chemistry:

- **Iron (Fe):** ~98–99% — base metal.
- **Carbon (C):** 0.05% – 0.25% (for structural grade) — increases hardness and strength but reduces ductility.
- **Manganese (Mn):** 0.5% – 1.5% — improves strength and deoxidises.
- **Silicon (Si):** ≤ 0.4% — deoxidiser.
- **Sulphur (S) & Phosphorus (P):** ≤ 0.05% each (impurities) — kept low to prevent brittleness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why low carbon?**
> Low carbon gives the **upper yield point** in mild steel, which is essential for elastic design. High carbon → brittle → unsuitable for seismic/cyclic applications like rehabilitation actuators.

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties — The "Five Pillars of Steel Behaviour"

### Pillar 1: Elasticity
Steel obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit:
- **Stress (σ)** is directly proportional to **Strain (ε)**.
- Modulus of Elasticity **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** is constant for all structural steels regardless of grade.

### Pillar 2: Ductility
Ability to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture.
- Measured by **percentage elongation** (typically **23–25%** for Fe 410 on a 50 mm gauge).
- Essential for **earthquake-resistant design** and **patient-safety compliance** in rehab robots (no brittle failure).

### Pillar 3: Strength
Three strength limits are critical:
- **Yield Stress (fy)** → onset of plastic deformation.
- **Ultimate Tensile Stress (fu)** → maximum load capacity.
- **Breaking Stress** → stress at fracture (≈ 1.2 fu for mild steel).

### Pillar 4: Toughness
Ability to absorb energy up to fracture (area under stress-strain curve).
- Critical for **impact loads** in rehabilitation (e.g., patient stumbling while wearing exoskeleton).

### Pillar 5: Weldability
Carbon Equivalent (CE) ≤ 0.55% → good weldability.
- Critical for **fabrication of custom orthotic frames** and exoskeleton linkages.

## 2.3 Stress-Strain Behaviour — The Signature Curve

The mild-steel stress-strain curve is a **four-zone phenomenon**:

1. **Zone I — Elastic Zone (OA):** Linear, obeys Hooke's Law. Slope = E.
2. **Zone II — Yield Plateau (AB):** Sudden drop, then horizontal flow. *Upper yield point* (A) and *lower yield point* (B).
3. **Zone III — Strain-Hardening (BC):** Curve rises due to dislocation entanglement. Reaches **ultimate stress (fu)** at C.
4. **Zone IV — Necking & Fracture (CD):** Cross-section reduces, stress drops, fracture at D.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-tensile steel (HYSD bars, Fe 415, Fe 500)** does *not* show a distinct yield point. It has a **0.2% proof stress** — the stress at which 0.2% permanent strain occurs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Direct stress | N/mm² |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Longitudinal strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus | N/mm² |
| 4 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear Modulus relation | N/mm² |
| 5 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$ | Poisson's Ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_{ult} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile stress | N/mm² |
| 7 | $\% \text{elongation} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure | % |
| 8 | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent (weldability) | % |
| 9 | $\Delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Elongation under axial load | mm |
| 10 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$ | Euler's buckling load | N |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Symbol Rule (KTU Format):** All absolute value/division separators in this sheet are written using `\dfrac` (display fraction) — never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside table cells as it breaks the markdown structure.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Steel Property |
|---|---|
| **Rehabilitation Robotics** | High strength/weight ratio in exoskeleton links |
| **Civil Construction** | Skyscraper frames, bridges (ductility → seismic safety) |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Surgical-grade 316L stainless steel (corrosion resistance) |
| **Industrial Machinery** | Crane jibs, robotic arms (fatigue resistance) |
| **Rehab Equipment** | Parallel bars, walkers, treadmill frames (weldability) |

## 2.6 Advantages of Steel as a Structural Material

1. **High tensile strength** — supports heavy rehabilitation patients.
2. **Ductility** — visible warning before collapse.
3. **Homogeneous & isotropic** — uniform behaviour simplifies FEA.
4. **Recyclable** — sustainable for medical waste management.
5. **Prefabrication friendly** — modular rehab devices.
6. **Speed of construction** — bolted/welded joints.
7. **Additively manufacturable** — modern 3D-printed steel components.

## 2.7 Limitations of Steel

1. **Corrosion** — must be galvanised or painted (passivation for implants).
2. **Fire susceptibility** — loses 50% strength at **550 °C** — needs fireproofing.
3. **Buckling** — slender compression members fail prematurely.
4. **Fatigue** — cyclic loading of actuators weakens links over time.
5. **High initial cost** — compared to timber/concrete for low-rise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hooke's Law from First Principles

**Statement to prove:** *Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.*

### Step 1 — Consider a uniform steel bar
Let:
- Original length = $L$
- Cross-sectional area = $A$
- Applied axial tensile load = $P$

### Step 2 — Define stress
By definition, the internal resisting force per unit area is called **stress**.

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$$

This equation gives the magnitude of stress generated in the bar due to the applied tensile load $P$.

### Step 3 — Define strain
The deformation per unit original length is called **strain**.

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Here $\Delta L$ is the change (elongation) of the bar under load $P$.

### Step 4 — Experimental observation
For mild steel loaded within the **elastic limit**, careful experiment shows a **linear relationship** between $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon$. This is the basis of Hooke's Law.

$$\sigma \propto \varepsilon$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the constant of proportionality
The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Elasticity (E)**.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Step 6 — Combine with definitions
Substituting Step 2 and Step 3 into Step 5:

$$E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{P/A}{\Delta L / L} = \dfrac{PL}{A \, \Delta L}$$

### Step 7 — Final elongation formula
Rearranging to solve for $\Delta L$:

$$\Delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Always state *“within the elastic limit”* when writing Hooke's Law. Marks are cut if this qualifier is missing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship between E, G, and ν

**Statement to prove:** *The elastic constants E, G, and ν are not independent — they are linked by a single equation.*

### Step 1 — Set up the pure shear scenario
Consider a small steel cube subjected to a pure shear stress $\tau$ on its faces. The shear strain produced is $\gamma$.

By definition of Shear Modulus:

$$G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

### Step 2 — Use complementary shear
A pure shear state is equivalent to a **principal stress state** with equal tension and compression at 45°.
- Principal tensile stress: $\sigma_1 = \tau$
- Principal compressive stress: $\sigma_2 = -\tau$

### Step 3 — Compute strains using Hooke's Law (2D)
For a 2D stress state, the longitudinal and lateral strains are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y)$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu \sigma_x)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute principal stresses
With $\sigma_x = \tau$ and $\sigma_y = -\tau$:

$$\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}(\tau - \nu(-\tau)) = \dfrac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{1}{E}(-\tau - \nu \tau) = -\dfrac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

### Step 5 — Compute total shear strain
The engineering shear strain is the **sum of the absolute angular distortions**:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y = \dfrac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu) - \left(-\dfrac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu)\right) = \dfrac{2\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

### Step 6 — Equate with shear modulus definition
Since $G = \tau / \gamma$:

$$G = \dfrac{\tau}{\dfrac{2\tau}{E}(1+\nu)} = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

### Step 7 — Final relationship
Rearranging to the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\,E = 2G(1+\nu)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification for structural steel:**
> $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$
> $G = \dfrac{200}{2(1+0.3)} = \dfrac{200}{2.6} \approx 76.92 \text{ GPa}$ ✓ (matches the standard $0.769 \times 10^5$ N/mm²).

## 3.3 Worked Example — Axial Deformation of a Rehab Robot Link

**Problem (KTU Typical):**
A steel link in a rehabilitation exoskeleton is **500 mm long**, has a **rectangular cross-section of 20 mm × 10 mm**, and carries an axial tensile load of **20 kN**.
- Modulus of Elasticity: $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm².
- Poisson's Ratio: $\nu = 0.3$.

**Find:** (a) Stress, (b) Strain, (c) Elongation, (d) Lateral contraction.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $L = 500$ mm
- $A = 20 \times 10 = 200$ mm²
- $P = 20{,}000$ N
- $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $\nu = 0.3$

#### Part (a) — Stress
Apply the direct stress formula.

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{200} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \; (100 \text{ MPa})$$

**Check:** 100 MPa < fy = 250 MPa (Fe 250) ✓ — the link is within the elastic limit.

#### Part (b) — Strain
Using Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{100}{2.0 \times 10^5} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

This is a **dimensionless quantity** (mm/mm).

#### Part (c) — Elongation
Apply the elongation formula.

$$\Delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 500}{200 \times 2.0 \times 10^5} = \dfrac{10^7}{4 \times 10^7} = 0.25 \text{ mm}$$

#### Part (d) — Lateral Contraction
First compute the lateral strain using Poisson's Ratio.

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.3 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = -1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

For a width of 20 mm and thickness of 10 mm:

$$\Delta b = \varepsilon_{lat} \times b = -1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -0.003 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta t = \varepsilon_{lat} \times t = -1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 = -0.0015 \text{ mm}$$

**Negative sign indicates contraction**, which is the expected physical behaviour under tension.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mild Steel vs. High-Tensile Steel

| Property | Mild Steel (Fe 250) | HYSD Bar (Fe 415) | HYSD Bar (Fe 500) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Content | 0.10 – 0.20% | 0.20 – 0.30% | 0.25 – 0.35% |
| Yield Stress fy | 250 MPa | 415 MPa | 500 MPa |
| Ultimate Stress fu | 410 MPa | 485 MPa | 545 MPa |
| Elongation | 23% | 14.5% | 12% |
| Yield Point | Distinct | Not distinct | Not distinct |
| Ductility | High | Moderate | Low |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good | Fair |
| Use Case | Rehab walker frames | RCC structures | High-rise columns |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Structural Steel

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STRUCTURAL STEEL]:::root
    A --> B[CARBON STEEL]
    A --> C[ALLOY STEEL]

    B --> B2[Medium Carbon Steel C 0.25 to 0.60 percent]
    B --> B3[High Carbon Steel C 0.60 to 2.0 percent]

    C --> C1[High Strength Low Alloy HSLA]
    C --> C2[Quenched and Tempered Q and T]
    C --> C3[Weathering Steel]

    D --> D1[Austenitic 316L for implants]
    D --> D2[Ferritic 430 for casings]
    D --> D3[Martensitic 410 for surgical tools]

    B1 --> B1A[Fe 250 General Fabrication]
    B1 --> B1B[Fe 410 Universal Use]

    C1 --> C1A[Rehabilitation Robot Links]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress-Strain Behaviour of Mild Steel

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Applied Load P]:::in --> A[Stage 1 Elastic Zone OA]:::el
    A --> B[Stage 2 Yield Plateau AB]:::yi
    B --> C[Stage 3 Strain Hardening BC]:::sh
    C --> D[Stage 4 Necking and Fracture CD]:::fr
    D --> R[Material Failure]:::out

    A -->|Slope equals E| M1[Modulus of Elasticity 200 GPa]:::prop
    B -->|Stress constant| M2[Yield Stress fy]:::prop
    C -->|Stress peaks at C| M3[Ultimate Stress fu]:::prop
    D -->|Area under curve| M4[Toughness Energy Absorbed]:::prop

    classDef in fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    classDef el fill:#bbdefb,color:#000
    classDef yi fill:#fff59d,color:#000
    classDef sh fill:#ffcc80,color:#000
    classDef fr fill:#ef9a9a,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#b71c1c,color:#fff

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture: Steel Link in a Rehab Robot

```mermaid

        M[Brushless DC Motor 200 W]:::m
        G[Gear Reducer Ratio 1 to 50]:::g
    end

    subgraph STRUCTURAL[STRUCTURAL COMPONENT]
        SL[Steel Link Fe 410 Grade Cross Section 20x10 mm]:::sl
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT SIDE]
        JO[Joint Pivot]:::jo
        PA[Patient Attachment Cuff]:::pa
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK[CONTROL FEEDBACK]
        EN[Encoder 4096 PPR]:::en
        TS[Torque Sensor 0 to 50 Nm]:::ts
    end

    M -->|Torque Output| G
    G -->|Reduced Torque Higher RPM| SL
    SL -->|Tensile or Compressive Load| JO
    JO -->|Rotation| PA

    SL -.->|Strain Gauge Reading| TS
    JO -.->|Angular Position| EN

    classDef m fill:#42a5f5,color:#fff
    classDef g fill:#26a69a,color:#fff
    classDef sl fill:#ff7043,color:#fff
