## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define composite materials used in assistive technology. List any two matrix and reinforcement materials commonly employed in prosthetic and orthotic design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
A **composite material** is a heterogeneous, engineered material consisting of two or more distinct phases—a **matrix** (binder) and a **reinforcement**—combined macroscopically to deliver properties unattainable by any single constituent. In assistive technology, composites are used to fabricate lightweight, high-strength, and patient-customized rehabilitation devices.

Two matrix materials:
1. **Epoxy resin** — thermoset matrix with low shrinkage, used in CF sockets and orthotic shells.
2. **PEEK (Polyetheretherketone)** — high-performance thermoplastic for robotic end-effectors and implantable prosthetics.

Two reinforcement materials:
1. **Carbon Fiber (CF)** — high specific stiffness for prosthetic pylons, AFOs, and energy-storing feet.
2. **E-Glass Fiber** — economical, used in wheelchair frames and pediatric orthoses.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Rule of Mixtures. With a neat expression, explain how the longitudinal modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite is determined.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
The **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** is a property-estimation principle for composites that assumes the contribution of each phase is weighted by its volume fraction.

For a unidirectional composite loaded along the fiber direction (isostrain condition, $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$):

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

where $E_f$ and $E_m$ are the elastic moduli of the fiber and matrix respectively, and $V_f$ and $V_m$ are their volume fractions. Since the load is shared in parallel (Voigt model), the stiffer phase (typically the fiber) carries the larger fraction of the applied load.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **Rule of Mixtures** for the longitudinal elastic modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite, clearly stating the isostrain assumption. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A prosthetic transtibial socket is fabricated from a unidirectional **carbon-fiber/epoxy** composite. Given:
- $E_f = 230 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$
- $\rho_f = 1.80 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $\sigma_{f,UTS} = 4000 \text{ MPa}$, $V_f = 0.55$

Calculate the **composite longitudinal modulus**, **density**, **specific modulus**, and compare the specific modulus with that of **Ti-6Al-4V** ($E = 114 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho = 4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3$). Discuss why this material choice is preferred for lower-limb prostheses. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a)**:
- State the **isostrain assumption**: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$.
- Force equilibrium: $P_c = P_f + P_m \Rightarrow \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$.
- Divide by $A_c$: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$.
- Apply Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$.
- Cancel common strain: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- Conclude with the physical interpretation (Voigt/parallel model).

**Model Solution for (b)**:

**Step 1: Composite Longitudinal Modulus**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

$$E_L = (230)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)$$

$$E_L = 126.5 + 1.575 \approx 128.08 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2: Composite Density**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$

$$\rho_c = (1.80)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45)$$

$$\rho_c = 0.99 + 0.54 = 1.53 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Specific Modulus of Composite**

$$SM_c = \frac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{128.08}{1.53} \approx 83.7 \text{ GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 4: Specific Modulus of Ti-6Al-4V**

$$SM_{Ti} = \frac{114}{4.43} \approx 25.7 \text{ GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 5: Ratio**

$$\frac{SM_c}{SM_{Ti}} = \frac{83.7}{25.7} \approx 3.26$$

**Conclusion**: The CF/epoxy composite has a specific modulus **~3.26× greater** than titanium. For a transtibial prosthesis, this translates to a **socket-pylon assembly weighing roughly 40-50% less** than a titanium equivalent, dramatically reducing the metabolic cost of walking (every 100 g saved reduces energy expenditure by 3-5%). The composite also allows **anisotropic tailoring** (angled-ply layups) to match the natural stiffness gradient of the residual limb, providing **comfort where flex is needed and rigidity where load is high**—impossible with monolithic titanium.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **classification of composites** used in assistive technology, listing one example application of each class in rehabilitation engineering. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **critical fiber length** $l_c$ in a fiber-reinforced composite. A unidirectional glass-fiber/epoxy composite has fiber tensile strength $\sigma_f = 2500 \text{ MPa}$, fiber diameter $d = 10 \text{ μm}$, and interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 35 \text{ MPa}$. Compute $l_c$ and state its engineering significance in AT design. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a)**:
Classify into PMC, MMC, CMC, and NFC, with AT application for each:

- **PMC** → CF/Epoxy → prosthetic sockets, AFOs.
- **MMC** → Al/SiC → prosthetic knee joint components.
- **CMC** → Zirconia/Alumina → bio-implants, dental.
- **NFC** → Flax/Epoxy → pediatric low-cost orthoses.

**Model Solution for (b)**:

**Step 1**: Equate fiber tensile force to interfacial shear force:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

**Step 2**: Solve for $l_c$:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}$$

**Step 3**: Numerical substitution:

$$l_c = \frac{2500 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 35} = \frac{25}{70} \approx 0.357 \text{ mm}$$

**Significance**: 
- If actual fiber length $l \geq 15 l_c$, fibers behave as **continuous** and develop full strength.
- If $l < l_c$, the **fiber pulls out** of the matrix before breaking → energy-absorbing failure (desirable for **impact-tolerant orthoses** like helmets and protective braces for cerebral palsy patients).
- For static-load prosthetic pylons, $l \gg l_c$ is preferred to maximize stiffness.
- In AT design, controlling fiber length is a **design parameter for tuning failure mode** (brittle vs. ductile).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Volume fraction confusion**: Many students write $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ but forget the constraint $V_f + V_m = 1$ — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Isostrain vs. Isostress**: Mixing up longitudinal (parallel) and transverse (series) formulas is the most common error in 14-mark questions. Use the mnemonic: **"Long**gitudinal = **Lo**ad-sharing = parallel springs."
> 3. **Units in specific modulus**: Always express specific modulus as $E / \rho$ with consistent units, e.g., $\text{GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$, not mixed SI-CGS.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption statement**: A derivation without explicitly stating the isostrain/isostress condition will lose 2 of 7 marks in Part (a).
> 5. **Forgetting the 15$l_c$ rule**: Many students calculate $l_c$ correctly but fail to state its engineering significance, costing 2 marks in Part (b).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: A composite combines two or more distinct phases — a **matrix** (binder) and a **reinforcement** (fiber/particulate) — to achieve properties superior to individual components.
- **Why in AT**: Lightweight, high specific strength/modulus, anisotropic tailoring, energy storage & return, corrosion resistance, biocompatibility.
- **Four Major Classes**: PMC (most common), MMC (high-load joints), CMC (implants), NFC (sustainable/low-cost).
- **Common Reinforcements**: Carbon Fiber (high stiffness), Glass Fiber (low cost), Aramid (impact), Natural Fiber (sustainable).
- **Common Matrices**: Epoxy (thermoset), PEEK (thermoplastic), Bio-resins (eco-friendly).
- **Rule of Mixtures — Longitudinal (Isostrain)**: $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — **parallel spring** model.
- **Rule of Mixtures — Transverse (Isostress)**: $\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ — **series spring** model.
- **Density Rule of Mixtures**: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- **Volume Fraction Constraint**: $V_f + V_m = 1$ — always true and must be checked.
- **Critical Fiber Length**: $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ — minimum length for full load transfer.
- **15$l_c$ Rule**: Fibers with $l \geq 15 l_c$ behave as **continuous**; shorter fibers allow pull-out (energy-absorbing failure).
- **Specific Modulus** is the **golden metric** for AT material selection — CF/Epoxy offers ~3.3× that of titanium.
- **Failure Modes**: Fiber breakage, matrix cracking, fiber-matrix debonding, delamination.
- **Manufacturing Routes**: Hand layup, vacuum bagging, resin transfer molding (RTM), pre-preg autoclave, 3D printing of continuous-fiber composites.
- **Key Trade-off**: Carbon Fiber is stiffer and lighter but **more brittle** and expensive than glass fiber — choice depends on the AT application's load, impact, and cost profile.
- **KTU Hot-Question Pattern**: Expect 14-mark derivations of $E_L$ or $l_c$ with a worked numerical problem based on CF/Epoxy or GF/Epoxy prosthetic data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Rehabilitation Robotics** is a specialized branch of biomedical engineering that integrates principles of **mechatronics, robotics, control systems, and neuroscience** to design, develop, and deploy robotic devices that **assist, augment, or restore** sensorimotor function in individuals with physical impairments resulting from neurological trauma, musculoskeletal disorders, or age-related degeneration. In the context of **KTU OEBRT722 – Module 2**, the "Introduction" establishes the **engineering vocabulary, taxonomy, and architectural framework** required to understand robotic systems applied in physical and neurological rehabilitation. It serves as the foundation upon which subsequent lectures (sensors, actuators, control, HRI, exoskeletons) are constructed. ### 1.2 Scope of the Module Module 2 broadly encompasses the following technical pillars: | Pillar | Engineering Domain | Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Sensing** | Signal Processing, Embedded Systems | Capturing patient intent (EMG, EEG, IMU) | | **Actuation** | Mechanical & Electrical Engineering | Delivering forces/motions safely | | **Control** | Control Theory, AI/ML | Ensuring stable, intent-driven assistance | | **HMI/HRI** | Ergonomics, Cognitive Science | Seamless human-machine cooperation | | **Biomechanics** | Musculoskeletal Modeling | Synchronizing robot with human limbs | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intelligent Physiotherapist" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a physiotherapist who *never gets tired*, *never loses patience*, *measures every joint angle to the nearest 0.1°*, and can *repeat the exact same movement pattern ten thousand times* with perfect precision. A **rehabilitation robot** is essentially this intelligent, tireless, and measurable physiotherapist, embedded inside a mechanical structure that interfaces with a patient's limb. Just as a human therapist uses: - **Eyes & hands (senses)** → Robot uses **sensors** (encoders, force/torque, EMG, IMUs) - **Muscles (force generation)** → Robot uses **actuators** (motors, pneumatics, Series Elastic Actuators) - **Brain (decision making)** → Robot uses a **controller** (PID, impedance, admittance, AI-based) - **Verbal feedback** → Robot uses **haptic/audio/visual displays** ### 1.4 Why Robotics in Rehabilitation? — The Driving Motivation > [!NOTE] > Three core clinical realities create the demand for robotic rehabilitation: > 1. **Therapist Shortage:** Globally, the ratio of patients needing neuro-rehabilitation to qualified therapists is approximately **> 50:1**. > 2. **Dosage Dependency:** Neuroplasticity research (Kleim & Jones, 2008) proves recovery requires **high-intensity, repetitive, task-specific training** — typically **> 300 repetitions/session**, impractical manually. > 3. **Quantifiable Progress:** Robots provide **objective, repeatable measurements** of motor recovery (smoothness, accuracy, power) that human observation cannot match. Standard clinical metrics introduced at this stage: - **Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA):** max score **226** - **Barthel Index (BI):** max score **100** - **Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS):** spasticity grading **0–4** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Historical Evolution — A Brief Timeline ``` 1960s │ First orthotic manipulators (Case Institute, Rancho "Golden Arm") 1970s │ Microprocessor-controlled prosthetics 1980s │ MIT-MANUS inception (Hogan, 1985) — first therapeutic arm robot 1990s │ Lokomat (Hocoma), ARM-in, MIME — commercial gait & arm systems 2000s │ InMotion ARM, BONES, wearable exoskeletons (BLEEX) 2010s │ Soft robotics, EMG-driven control, cloud-connected rehab 2020s+ │ AI-cognitive rehab, digital twins, socially assistive robots (SAR) ``` ### 2.2 Taxonomy of Rehabilitation Robots > [!IMPORTANT] > Rehabilitation robots are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: *Function*, *Anatomical Region*, and *Interaction Modality*. #### Classification by Function 1. **Therapeutic (Rehabilitative) Robots** — temporary use during recovery (e.g., MIT-MANUS, Lokomat). 2. **Assistive Robots** — permanent aids for daily living (e.g., wheelchair-mounted arms like JACO). 3. **Prosthetic Robots** — replace a missing limb (e.g., DEKA LUKE arm, bebionic hand). 4. **Orthotic / Exoskeleton Robots** — worn *on* the limb to augment or support it (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT). #### Classification by Anatomical Region - **Upper-limb:** MIT-MANUS, ARMEO Power, InMotion ARM - **Lower-limb:** Lokomat, ALEX, ReWalk, Ekso - **Hand:** AMADEO, Hand of Hope - **Trunk/Spine:** Tethered pelvic orthoses #### Classification by Interaction Type - **Endo-effectors:** Robot contacts patient at a single distal point (e.g., handle/glove). - **Exoskeleton:** Robot's links align collinearly with patient's anatomical segments. - **Hybrid:** Combines both paradigms (e.g., LOKOMAT — driven gait orthosis with body-weight support). ### 2.3 Core Architectural Block Diagram (Conceptual) A rehabilitation robot, irrespective of its type, consists of **four functional layers**: | Layer | Function | Typical Components | |---|---|---| | **L1 — Mechanical** | Physical structure, links, joints | Lightweight alloys, carbon fiber, ergonomic cuffs | | **L2 — Actuation** | Force/torque generation | BLDC motors, pneumatics, SEA, HASEL actuators | | **L3 — Sensing** | State & intent measurement | Encoders, F/T sensors, IMU, EMG, EEG, vision | | **L4 — Control & Cognition** | Decision-making | PID, impedance, admittance, MPC, ML classifiers | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Although "Introduction" is largely conceptual, the following governing equations underpin the *entire* module. Mastering them now is **mandatory** for subsequent topics. #### 2.4.1 Generalized Robot Dynamics (Newton–Euler / Lagrangian) $$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{robot}} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{human}} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{ext}} $$ where: - $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ — mass/inertia matrix (configuration-dependent) - $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ — Coriolis & centrifugal matrix - $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — gravitational torque vector - $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{human}}$ — torque contributed by the patient (the *key unknown* in rehab) - $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{ext}}$ — environmental/contact torques #### 2.4.2 Impedance Control Law (Hogan, 1985) The single most important control equation in rehabilitation robotics: $$ \mathbf{M}_d(\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_r - \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d) + \mathbf{B}_d(\dot{\mathbf{x}}_r - \dot{\mathbf{x}}_d) + \mathbf{K}_d(\mathbf{x}_r - \mathbf{x}_d) = \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}} $$ - $\mathbf{M}_d, \mathbf{B}_d, \mathbf{K}_d$ — desired inertia, damping, stiffness (the *impedance* the robot *presents* to the patient) - $\mathbf{x}_r$ — robot position, $\mathbf{x}_d$ — desired trajectory - $\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ — force exerted *by* the patient *on* the robot > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in rehab:** Rather than rigidly commanding motion (position control), impedance control lets the patient *lead* and the robot *comply*, much like a therapist's compliant hand. **Stability** requires the system to be **passive**, i.e., $\mathbf{M}_d, \mathbf{B}_d, \mathbf{K}_d \succ 0$ (positive definite). #### 2.4.3 Admittance Control (Dual of Impedance) $$ \mathbf{M}_d \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{B}_d \dot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d (\mathbf{x}_d - \mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}} $$ Here, measured force is *mapped* to a velocity/position command. Suited for **stiff, position-controlled backdrivable robots**. #### 2.4.4 Haptic Safety Bound Maximum safe end-effector velocity for human interaction is bounded by: $$ v_{\max} \leq \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{\text{lim}}}{m_{\text{eff}}}} $$ where $E_{\text{lim}}$ is the biomechanical impact-energy limit (ISO/TS 15066 for collaborative robots sets $\mathbf{E_{\text{lim}} \le 10\,J}$ for transient contact). #### 2.4.5 Quantitative Recovery Metric (Therapy Outcome) A commonly used smoothness/coordination index: $$ \text{NK} = \frac{\bar{v}_{\text{peak}}}{\bar{v}_{\text{mean}}} $$ - Higher **Number of Peaks (NK)** → more **fractionated/healthy** motion (post-stroke healthy ≈ 1.1–1.5). #### 2.4.6 Compact Cheat-Sheet Table | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | Robot dynamics | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{q}+\mathbf{C}\dot{q}+\mathbf{G}=\tau$ | $\tau$: N·m | High | | Impedance | $\mathbf{M}_d,\mathbf{B}_d,\mathbf{K}_d$ | kg, N·s/m, N/m | Very High | | Admittance | Dual of impedance | Same | High | | Safety velocity | $v_{\max}$ | m/s | Medium | | NK smoothness | $\bar{v}_p / \bar{v}_m$ | dimensionless | Medium | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** These equations are not academic — they govern FDA-cleared devices used in stroke, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, and Parkinson's rehabilitation worldwide. Recruiters in **Hocoma, Ekso Bionics, Fourier Intelligence, Tyromotion** routinely test candidates on these. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Impedance Relationship from Energy/Passivity The impedance control law is *not* postulated arbitrarily — it emerges from the **port-Hamiltonian framework**. The derivation below shows how a spring–damper–mass triplet emerges naturally as a safe, passive, human-compatible controller. **Step 1 — Start with a virtual mass–spring–damper at the robot–patient port.** A 1-DOF human–robot interaction can be modeled as the robot presenting a mechanical *impedance* to the patient. In the Laplace domain, force-to-velocity ratio (mechanical impedance) is: $$ Z(s) = \frac{F(s)}{V(s)} $$ **Step 2 — Choose a second-order impedance profile (Hogan's choice).** We require a *critically tunable* second-order response: $$ Z(s) = M_d s + B_d + \frac{K_d}{s} $$ **Step 3 — Cross-multiply and convert to time domain.** Multiplying both sides by $V(s) = sX(s)$: $$ F(s) = M_d s^2 X(s) + B_d s X(s) + K_d X(s) $$ Replace $s^2 X(s) \leftrightarrow \ddot{x}(t)$, $sX(s) \leftrightarrow \dot{x}(t)$, $X(s) \leftrightarrow x(t)$: $$ F_{\text{ext}}(t) = M_d \ddot{x}_r(t) + B_d \dot{x}_r(t) + K_d x_r(t) $$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of *deviation* from desired trajectory.** Define the position error $e(t) = x_r(t) - x_d(t)$. The control force that the robot applies to *track* the desired trajectory while *admitting* external force is: $$ F_{\text{ctrl}} = M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e - F_{\text{ext}} $$ Rearranging, the final **impedance-control law** is: $$ M_d (\ddot{x}_r - \ddot{x}_d) + B_d (\dot{x}_r - \dot{x}_d) + K_d (x_r - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}} $$ **Step 5 — Verify passivity for stability.** The total energy stored in the virtual mass–spring–damper is: $$ E = \tfrac{1}{2} M_d \dot{e}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} K_d e^2 \;\; \ge 0 $$ Its time derivative (power dissipated/added): $$ \dot{E} = M_d \dot{e}\ddot{e} + K_d e\dot{e} = \dot{e}\bigl(M_d\ddot{e} + K_d e\bigr) = \dot{e}\bigl(F_{\text{ext}} - B_d \dot{e}\bigr) $$ Therefore: $$ \dot{E} = F_{\text{ext}}\dot{e} - B_d \dot{e}^2 $$ For **passivity** we require $\dot{E} \le F_{\text{ext}}\dot{e}$, which is satisfied **if and only if** $B_d \ge 0$. Hence the **damping term is mandatory for human safety**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway:** *Never* deploy an impedance controller on a patient with $B_d = 0$. The robot would store and *release* the patient's energy, leading to unstable oscillations. ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Impedance vs. Admittance Control > [!NOTE] > A frequent KTU Part-B question asks students to *justify* the choice of controller. The matrix below is a high-yield exam-ready answer. | Property | Impedance Control | Admittance Control | |---|---|---| | **Input measured** | Position/velocity | Force/torque | | **Output commanded** | Force/torque | Position/velocity | | **Best suited robot** | Compliant (torque-controlled) | Stiff (position-controlled) | | **Patient feels** | A *virtual spring–damper* | A *mass with friction* | | **Stability in stiff contact** | Stable inherently | Sensitive to contact stiffness | | **Stability in free space** | Stable (no contact) | Can drift if $F_{\text{ext}}=0$ | | **Computational cost** | Moderate (Jacobians + dynamics) | Low (1st/2nd order filter) | | **Examples in rehab** | MIT-MANUS, DLR LWR-based | Lokomat (gait), InMotion ARM | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Haptic Safety Bound > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A 6-DOF upper-limb rehabilitation robot has an effective end-effector mass $m_{\text{eff}} = 2.5\,\text{kg}$. Compute the maximum permissible Cartesian velocity such that the impact energy does not exceed the ISO/TS 15066 transient-contact limit of $E_{\text{lim}} = 10\,\text{J}$. **Step 1 — Write the safety formula:** $$ v_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2E_{\text{lim}}}{m_{\text{eff}}}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:** $$ v_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10\,\text{J}}{2.5\,\text{kg}}} $$ $$ v_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{2.5}}\,\text{m/s} = \sqrt{8}\,\text{m/s} $$ $$ v_{\max} = 2.828\,\text{m/s} \approx 2.83\,\text{m/s} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret:** The end-effector velocity must be capped at $\le 2.83\,\text{m/s}$. In practice, a **safety factor of 2–3×** is applied, giving an operational ceiling of $\sim 1.0\,\text{m/s}$ for direct-contact rehab robots. ### 3.4 Penetration-Depth Trigger Formula (Soft-Tissue Injury Model) For skin contact, the minimum injury threshold is reached when the product of *force* and *penetration* exceeds a critical energy density $E_{\text{skin}}$: $$ E_{\text{skin}} = \int_0^{\delta_{\max}} F(\delta)\,d\delta $$ For a linearized soft-tissue model $F = k_s \delta$ (with $k_s \approx 50\,\text{N/mm}$): $$ E_{\text{skin}} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} k_s \delta_{\max}^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \delta_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2E_{\text{skin}}}{k_s}} $$ A typical allowed penetration is $\delta_{\max} \le 5\,\text{mm}$, giving $E_{\text{skin}} \le 0.625\,\text{J}$ — a stringent constraint for **cobot rehabilitators**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rehabilitation Robot Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Intent Layer
EMG / EEG / IMU / Voice] --> B[Intention Decoder
Classifier or Threshold] B --> C[High-Level Controller
Trajectory + Impedance Setpoints] C --> D[Low-Level Controller
PID / Torque Loop] D --> E[Actuator Bank
BLDC / SEA / Pneumatic] E --> F[Mechanical Structure
Exo or End-effector] F --> G[Patient Limb
Physical Coupling] G --> H[Force Torque Sensor
and Encoders] H --> D H --> C C --> I[Safety Supervisor
ISO 13482 / TS 15066] I --> E C --> J[Therapist Dashboard
Progress Metrics] J --> C ``` > [!NOTE] > The double feedback loop (H → D for inner torque control, H → C for outer impedance/intent control) is the *hallmark* of rehab robots. The **Safety Supervisor** is a hardware-redundant module that can cut actuator power within **< 5 ms** in case of anomalous force or runaway motion. ### 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Types of Rehabilitation Robots ```mermaid flowchart TD RR[Rehabilitation Robots] RR --> TH[Therapeutic] RR --> AS[Assistive] RR --> PR[Prosthetic] RR --> OR[Orthotic / Exoskeleton] TH --> TH1[Upper Limb: MIT MANUS, ARMEO] TH --> TH2[Lower Limb: Lokomat, ALEX] TH --> TH3[Hand: AMADEO] AS --> AS1[Wheelchair Mounted: JACO, iARM] AS --> AS2[Mobile Platforms: Care O Bot] PR --> PR1[Upper Limb: LUKE Arm, Michelangelo] PR --> PR2[Lower Limb: Power Knee, BiOM] OR --> OR1[Stationary: Lokohelp] OR --> OR2[Overground: ReWalk, Ekso, Indego] OR --> OR3[Soft: Soft Exosuit Harvard] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid State Machine — Patient–Robot Interaction Modes ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Passive Passive --> ActiveAssisted : Patient volitional effort detected ActiveAssisted --> ActiveConstrained : Therapist demands accuracy ActiveConstrained --> Resistive : Strength training mode Resistive --> Bimanual : Mirror therapy engaged Bimanual --> Passive : Fatigue timer elapsed Passive --> [*] : Session end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Examiner-Insight:** The four classic therapy modes are: > 1. **Passive** — robot moves the limb fully (zero patient effort). > 2. **Active-Assisted** — robot completes motion patient initiates. > 3. **Active-Constrained** — patient moves inside a virtual channel. > 4. **Resistive** — robot opposes motion (strengthening). > Memorize these — they appear in **almost every KTU Part-B question** on control strategies. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define a rehabilitation robot. Differentiate between a therapeutic robot and an assistive robot with one example each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Rehabilitation robot (1 mark):** A mechatronic device designed to assist, augment, or restore sensorimotor function in individuals with physical disabilities through programmable, sensor-guided motion. - **Therapeutic robot (1 mark):** Used *temporarily* during recovery to deliver high-intensity, repetitive, task-specific training (e.g., **MIT-MANUS** for upper-limb post-stroke therapy). - **Assistive robot (1 mark):** Used *permanently* to compensate for lost function in daily living (e.g., **JACO arm** mounted on a wheelchair for self-feeding). > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** List any three categories of sensors used in upper-limb rehabilitation robots and state the physical quantity each measures. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Encoder / Resolver** — measures **joint angle/position** (units: rad or deg). 2. **Force/Torque (F/T) sensor** — measures **contact force at end-effector** (units: N, N·m). 3. **Surface Electromyography (sEMG) sensor** — measures **muscle electrical activity** (units: µV or mV). *(Acceptable alternates: IMU for orientation, vision for kinematic tracking.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Conflating impedance and admittance control** — examiners deduct **2–3 marks** if you swap input/output definitions. > 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers → lose **1 mark** per quantity. > 3. **Skipping the "Why"** behind control choice → at least **2 marks** reserved for clinical justification. > 4. **Omitting safety / ISO standards** → examiners in biomedical papers award a dedicated **1–2 mark** line for "safety considerations". --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Impedance Control in Rehab Robotics* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model | CO2, K3 Apply, K4 Analyze]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the impedance control law for a 1-DOF rehabilitation robot interacting with a human limb. Show that the system is passive if and only if the desired damping $B_d \ge 0$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1-DOF robot is desired to behave as a virtual spring of stiffness $K_d = 200\,\text{N/m}$ and a damper of $B_d = 20\,\text{N·s/m}$ in front of the patient. If the patient pushes with a steady force of $F_{\text{ext}} = 10\,\text{N}$, compute the steady-state deviation of the end-effector from the desired trajectory. What is the time constant of the response? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:** 1. **Define impedance as a virtual mass–spring–damper at the port** (1 mark). $$ Z(s) = M_d s + B_d + \frac{K_d}{s} $$ 2. **Cross-multiply with $V(s)$ to obtain force–position relation** (1 mark). 3. **Convert to time domain** yielding the impedance law (2 marks): $$ M_d(\ddot{x}_r-\ddot{x}_d) + B_d(\dot{x}_r-\dot{x}_d) + K_d(x_r-x_d) = F_{\text{ext}} $$ 4. **State total energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}M_d\dot{e}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}K_d e^2$** (1 mark). 5. **Differentiate and obtain $\dot{E} = F_{\text{ext}}\dot{e} - B_d\dot{e}^2$** (1 mark). 6. **Conclude passivity requires $B_d \ge 0$** (1 mark). **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:** - Step 1 — Steady state implies $\ddot{x}_r = \dot{x}_r = 0$, so impedance equation reduces to **(1 mark)**: $$ K_d (x_r - x_d) = F_{\text{ext}} $$ - Step 2 — Solve for steady-state deviation $e_{\text{ss}}$ **(1 mark)**: $$ e_{\text{ss}} = \frac{F_{\text{ext}}}{K_d} = \frac{10\,\text{N}}{200\,\text{N/m}} = 0.05\,\text{m} = 5\,\text{cm} $$ - Step 3 — Time constant of the 1st-order equivalent (ignoring inertia) **(1 mark)**: $$ \tau = \frac{B_d}{K_d} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.1\,\text{s} $$ - Step 4 — Interpretation: a 5 cm deflection is large; in practice stiffness is raised to 500–2000 N/m to keep deviations < 1 cm **(2 marks)**. - Step 5 — Note that increasing $B_d$ for safety *slows* the response — a classic **stability vs. responsiveness trade-off** in impedance tuning **(1 mark)**. - Step 6 — Final clinical comment: this is why therapist-tuning of "game difficulty" in devices like ArmeoPower is essentially tuning $(K_d, B_d)$ **(1 mark)**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Classification & Components of Rehabilitation Robots* **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model | CO1, K2 Understand, K3 Apply]** **(a)** Classify rehabilitation robots based on *function* and *anatomical region*. Give one commercial example for each category and explain why an end-effector design differs mechanically from an exoskeleton design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List and explain the four functional layers of a rehabilitation robot. For a lower-limb exoskeleton, identify the most suitable actuator type for the knee joint, justifying your choice with reference to torque, back-drivability, and safety. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:** 1. **Function-based classification (2 marks):** Therapeutic (e.g., **Lokomat**), Assistive (e.g., **JACO arm**), Prosthetic (e.g., **LUKE arm**), Orthotic/Exoskeleton (e.g., **ReWalk**). 2. **Anatomical classification (2 marks):** Upper limb (InMotion ARM), Lower limb (Lokomat), Hand (AMADEO), Trunk (Tethered pelvic orthosis). 3. **End-effector vs. Exoskeleton distinction (3 marks):** - *End-effector* applies force at a single distal point — simpler mechanics, fewer alignment issues, but limited control over individual joints. - *Exoskeleton* aligns joints collinearly with the human — enables **joint-level torque control**, more physiologically accurate, but heavier and harder to align. **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:** 1. **Four layers listed (2 marks):** Mechanical, Actuation, Sensing, Control & Cognition. 2. **Brief explanation of each (2 marks):** as per the table in Section 2.3. 3. **Actuator choice for knee (2 marks):** **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** — provides inherent compliance, accurate torque sensing via spring deflection, back-drivable (safe in unintended contact), and produces knee-scale torques (~30–60 N·m peak). 4. **Justification linkage (1 mark):** SEA satisfies the **impedance-controller requirement** for force fidelity while meeting ISO safety bounds; rigid gear motors are *rejected* due to poor back-drivability and high reflected inertia. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Many students answer "BLDC motor" without the elastic element. **Mark deduction of 1** because a rigid BLDC *alone* is unsafe for a knee orthosis — the elastic stage is essential. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):** - ☐ **Rehabilitation robot** = mechatronic system that *assists, augments, or restores* sensorimotor function. - ☐ Four **clinical metrics**: FMA (226 max), Barthel Index (100), MAS (0–4), NK smoothness (≈1 for healthy). - ☐ Four **robot categories**: Therapeutic, Assistive, Prosthetic, Orthotic/Exoskeleton. - ☐ Four **therapy modes**: Passive, Active-Assisted, Active-Constrained, Resistive (and Bimanual). - ☐ Four **functional layers**: Mechanical, Actuation, Sensing, Control & Cognition. - ☐ **Impedance control** = measure position, command force — needs torque-controlled robot. - ☐ **Admittance control** = measure force, command position — needs stiff position-controlled robot. - ☐ **Passivity** of impedance controller ⇔ $B_d \ge 0$ (and $M_d, K_d > 0$). - ☐ **Safety ceiling**: $v_{\max} = \sqrt{2E_{\text{lim}}/m_{\text{eff}}} \le 2.83\,\text{m/s}$ for $E_{\text{lim}} = 10\,\text{J}$, $m = 2.5\,\text{kg}$; in practice limit to $\sim 1\,\text{m/s}$. - ☐ **Lokomat, MIT-MANUS, ARMEO, ReWalk, Ekso, LUKE arm, JACO** are commercial must-knows. - ☐ **SEA** is the gold-standard actuator for compliant rehab joints; **soft actuators** (HASEL, pneumatics) are emerging. - ☐ **ISO/TS 15066** = cobot safety; **ISO 13482** = personal-care robot safety; mention in any safety-related answer. - ☐ **EMG, EEG, IMU, F/T sensor, encoder** are the five must-know sensors. - ☐ **Time-constant formula** for an impedance-controlled system: $\tau = B_d/K_d$. - ☐ **Steady-state deviation**: $e_{\text{ss}} = F_{\text{ext}}/K_d$. > **Closing Note for the Student:** Module 2's Introduction lays the *taxonomy and mathematical vocabulary* of rehab robotics. Every subsequent topic — sensors, actuators, controllers, HRI, exoskeletons — will re-use these equations. If this introduction is unclear, re-read Sections 2.2, 2.4, and 3.1 before moving forward.

topics.title: Selecting a Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selecting a Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, **"Selecting a Vehicle"** refers to the *systematic, evidence-based clinical and engineering process* of prescribing the most appropriate **Powered Mobility Device (PMD)** — typically a manual wheelchair, power wheelchair, scooter, or **robotic/smart wheelchair** — for a person with mobility impairment. The selection is matched to the user's **physical, cognitive, sensory, and functional profile**, the **environmental context** (home, workplace, community), and the **task demands** of daily living, as defined under the *Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model* and the *Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model*.

The process integrates **clinical assessment**, **engineering specifications**, **ergonomic fit**, and **technology acceptance frameworks** to ensure that the prescribed device maximizes **independence, safety, participation, and quality of life (QOL)** as mandated by the *World Health Organization's International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEBRT722 – Module 2):**
> Vehicle selection in robotic rehabilitation is **NOT** a commercial choice — it is a **prescription-based clinical decision** that requires matching a *human* with a *machine* through a *context-aware* engineering design process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a vehicle for a family member who uses a wheelchair:

- A **child with cerebral palsy** needs a *lightweight, tilt-in-space, custom-molded seating system* → like fitting a child into a car with a properly sized child seat and parental controls.
- A **spinal cord injury (SCI) patient** with strong upper limbs → a *manual ultra-light rigid-frame wheelchair* is like a *sports car* — efficient, fast, low energy.
- An **elderly stroke survivor** with hemiparesis → a *power wheelchair with one-arm drive or joystick control* is like an *automatic car with power steering*.
- A **high-level quadriplegic** with no upper-limb control → a *smart wheelchair driven by sip-and-puff, head-array, or eye-tracking* is like a *self-driving car* — autonomous, sensor-fused, collision-aware.

Just as a car salesman asks "Where will you drive? Who will ride? What is your budget?", a **rehabilitation engineer** asks:
- What are the **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** goals?
- What is the **Anthropometric profile** of the user?
- What is the **Physical environment** (door widths, ramps, kerbs)?
- What is the **Cognitive load** the user can tolerate?

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Design Principle:** The right vehicle is the one that *disappears into the user's life* — it should feel less like a piece of medical equipment and more like a *natural extension of the body*. In KTU parlance, this is the **Human–Machine Symbiosis Goal**.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Selection

| Metric | Standard Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Maximum safe slope | **8° (1:7 grade)** for indoor PMDs |
| Kerb climbing height | **50 mm – 100 mm** for power chairs |
| Doorway clearance (ADA) | **815 mm** (32 inches) minimum |
| Battery range (full charge) | **25 km – 40 km** |
| Maximum user weight (standard) | **113 kg (250 lbs)**; bariatric up to **250 kg** |
| Indoor turning radius | **≤ 600 mm** for tight spaces |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Tri-Phase Selection Framework

Vehicle selection in robotic rehabilitation is governed by a structured **Tri-Phase Model**:

#### Phase 1 — User Profiling (Clinical Assessment)
- **Physical capacity:** muscle strength (MRC scale), range of motion (ROM), postural control, sitting balance (trunk control).
- **Sensory profile:** vision, hearing, proprioception, tactile discrimination.
- **Cognitive & behavioural load:** ability to learn, remember sequences, anticipate hazards, problem-solve.
- **Functional goals:** transfers, indoor mobility, community ambulation, vocation.
- **Anthropometry:** weight, height, thigh length, hip width, popliteal angle.

#### Phase 2 — Context & Environment Analysis
- **Micro-environment:** home layout — door widths, floor surface, thresholds, tight turning zones, presence of lifts.
- **Meso-environment:** workplace, school — workstation access, transport between buildings.
- **Macro-environment:** community — kerbs, ramps, terrain variability, weather exposure, public transport compatibility.
- **Transportation:** vehicle lift capacity, foldability, weight for car-boot loading.

#### Phase 3 — Device Specification & Matching
Match the device taxonomy:

| Vehicle Class | Drive Type | Best For | Robotic Integration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manual wheelchair (MWC) | Self-propelled, attendant | Strong upper limbs, transient users | Minimal |
| Power-assisted MWC | Hub motors assist push-rim | Variable strength, endurance issues | Sensor-augmented rim |
| Power wheelchair (PWC) — standard | Joystick, rear-wheel drive | Indoor-outdoor balanced | Programmable controller |
| PWC — complex rehab | Custom seating, alternate controls | High-dependency users | CAN-bus controlled |
| Smart / robotic wheelchair | Sensor + AI navigation | Cognitive/visual impairment | **Obstacle avoidance, SLAM** |
| Mobility scooter | Tiller steering | Outdoor community use | Limited (some GPS models) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **HAAT Model (Cook & Hussey, 1995)** frames selection as the interaction of *Human × Activity × Assistive Technology × Context*. A device fails when any one of these four pillars is mis-matched.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-relevant** for derivations, numerical problems, and design-based questions in OEBRT722.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Mechanical power required to propel user | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $F = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force on flat ground | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | $P_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta$ | Power needed to climb a slope of angle $\theta$ | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{roll}} + P_{\text{grade}} + P_{\text{accel}}$ | Total power demand | Watts (W) |
| 5 | $E = P \cdot t$ | Energy consumed over time $t$ | Joules / Wh |
| 6 | $R_{\text{range}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$ | Vehicle range from battery | km |
| 7 | $\tau_{\text{motor}} = F \cdot r_{\text{wheel}}$ | Required motor torque | N·m |
| 8 | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r_{\text{wheel}}}$ | Wheel angular velocity | rad/s |
| 9 | $n = \dfrac{\omega_{\text{motor}}}{\omega_{\text{wheel}}}$ | Gear reduction ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{\text{turn}} = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$ | Approx. turning radius (Ackermann model) | metres |
| 11 | $S_{\text{score}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_i$ | Weighted matching score (MPT) | points |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{drivetrain}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Drivetrain efficiency | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**
> - Coefficient of rolling friction $\mu$ on rubber tyre on linoleum: **0.015 – 0.025**
> - Standard grade: **8° (≈ 1 in 7)**
> - Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **NEVER** use the vertical pipe `|` in equation cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This theoretical framework is used directly in:

- **Hospital Rehabilitation Engineering Departments** for clinical prescription.
- **Smart wheelchair R&D labs** (e.g., MIT WhizWheel, TetraNose project) for autonomous navigation integration.
- **Standards bodies** (ISO 7176 series, RESNA, FDA Class I/II medical device classification).
- **Insurance / public funding bodies** (e.g., NDIS, Medicare) for evidence-based reimbursement.
- **Disability-rights advocacy** for ensuring user-centred outcomes over profit-driven sales.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Total Power Demand of a Power Wheelchair

Let the user mass be $m_u$ and the wheelchair mass be $m_c$. Total mass:

$$
m = m_u + m_c
$$

**Step 1: Rolling resistance force on a flat surface**

The rolling resistance on a flat, hard surface is governed by the coefficient of rolling friction $\mu$:

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g
$$

**Step 2: Power to overcome rolling resistance at constant velocity $v$**

$$
P_{\text{roll}} = F_{\text{roll}} \cdot v = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v
$$

**Step 3: Grade-climbing force component**

For an incline of angle $\theta$, the gravitational component parallel to the slope is $m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$:

$$
F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 4: Power required to climb the grade at constant velocity $v$**

$$
P_{\text{grade}} = F_{\text{grade}} \cdot v = m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 5: Power required to accelerate mass $m$ at acceleration $a$**

$$
P_{\text{accel}} = m \cdot a \cdot v
$$

**Step 6: Total instantaneous mechanical power demand**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{roll}} + P_{\text{grade}} + P_{\text{accel}}
$$

Substituting all three components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v + m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a \cdot v \\
&= m \cdot v \left( \mu \cdot g + g \cdot \sin\theta + a \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Steady-state simplification (constant velocity, no acceleration, flat ground)**

For a typical indoor cruise, $a = 0$ and $\theta = 0$:

$$
P_{\text{ss}} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v
$$

> **Engineering insight:** This explains why *lightweight aluminium power chairs* (low $m_c$) drastically extend **battery range** for the same user.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Range from Battery Capacity

Battery capacity $C_{\text{batt}}$ is specified in **Watt-hours (Wh)**. If the average power draw during a typical journey is $P_{\text{avg}}$:

**Step 1: Total energy available**

$$
E_{\text{avail}} = C_{\text{batt}} \text{ [Wh]}
$$

**Step 2: Time of operation**

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{avail}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} \text{ [hours]}
$$

**Step 3: Range in kilometres**

$$
R_{\text{range}} = v_{\text{avg}} \cdot t_{\text{op}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{avg}} \cdot C_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}
$$

**Step 4: Accounting for drivetrain efficiency $\eta$**

The motors, gearbox, and electronics dissipate energy. Only a fraction $\eta$ reaches the wheels:

$$
P_{\text{wheel}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{batt}}
$$

Hence the **practical** range is:

$$
R_{\text{practical}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{avg}} \cdot \eta \cdot C_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{wheel}}}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A power wheelchair of total mass $m = 95$ kg travels on a flat indoor surface ($\mu = 0.020$, $\theta = 0$) at constant speed $v = 1.5$ m/s. The battery is rated $2 \times 12$ V, $50$ Ah, with drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Calculate the **(a)** rolling power, **(b)** battery energy, and **(c)** practical range.

**Solution:**

**(a) Rolling power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{roll}} &= \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v \\
&= 0.020 \times 95 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \\
&= 27.96 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Battery energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{batt}} &= V \times \text{Ah} = 2 \times 12 \times 50 = 1200 \text{ Wh} \\
E_{\text{avail}} &= 1200 \text{ Wh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Practical range**

Power at the wheels must be $P_{\text{roll}}$. Drawing from battery needs:

$$
P_{\text{batt}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{roll}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{27.96}{0.80} = 34.95 \text{ W}
$$

Time of operation:

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \dfrac{1200}{34.95} = 34.34 \text{ hours}
$$

Range:

$$
R_{\text{practical}} = 1.5 \times 34.34 = 51.51 \text{ km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Real-world power chairs quote **25–40 km** because the average power draw also includes *acceleration, slope, controller electronics standby (≈ 5 W), lighting, and actuators for tilt/recline* — not just steady-state rolling. The simple formula gives an **upper bound**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matching Matrix (Quantitative Scoring)

The **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Tool** assigns a weighted score to each prescription. Let the device be scored on $n$ criteria with weights $w_i$ and ratings $r_i$ on a 1–5 Likert scale:

$$
S_{\text{score}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_i
$$

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Rating $r_i$ (Manual) | Rating $r_i$ (Power) | Rating $r_i$ (Smart) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Independent indoor mobility | 0.25 | 4 | 5 | 5 |
| Community / outdoor use | 0.20 | 2 | 4 | 5 |
| Cognitive demand on user | 0.15 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Physical exertion required | 0.15 | 2 | 5 | 5 |
| Cost-effectiveness | 0.10 | 5 | 3 | 1 |
| Maintenance burden | 0.10 | 4 | 3 | 2 |
| Safety (collision avoidance) | 0.05 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| **Weighted Total** | **1.00** | **3.50** | **4.10** | **3.85** |

In this illustrative case, the **Power Wheelchair** has the highest aggregate score and would be the recommended vehicle — a typical KTU reasoning problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Smart Wheelchair Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Inputs: Joystick, Sip-and-Puff, Head Array, Eye Tracker] --> B[Central Controller Microcontroller and CAN Bus]
    B --> C[Drive Module: Two BLDC Motors with Gearbox]
    B --> D[Sensor Array: Ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, IMU, Encoders]
    D --> E[Sensor Fusion Processor: SLAM and Obstacle Detection]
    E --> B
    B --> F[Power Management: Battery Pack BMS and DC DC Converters]
    B --> G[Seating and Postural System: Tilt, Recline, Elevate Actuators]
    B --> H[Human Machine Interface: LCD Display and Buzzer]
    B --> I[Wireless Telemetry: Bluetooth and WiFi for Clinician Monitoring]
    C --> J[Wheels and Casters]
    F --> J
    G --> K[User Body Interface]
    H --> L[Caregiver and Clinician Feedback]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flowchart — Vehicle Selection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Patient Referral] --> S1{User has good upper limb function}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Environment: indoor only and short trips}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Prescribe Manual Ultra Light Wheelchair]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Prescribe Power Assisted Manual Wheelchair]
    S1 -- No --> S5{User can use hand joystick}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Prescribe Standard Power Wheelchair]
    S5 -- No --> S7{User has reliable head or sip puff control}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Prescribe Power Wheelchair with Alternate Control]
    S7 -- No --> S9{User has cognitive capacity for safe navigation}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Prescribe Smart Wheelchair with Sensor Collision Avoidance]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Prescribe Attendant Controlled Power Wheelchair with Tilt Recline]
    S3 --> END[Device Trial and Fitting]
    S4 --> END
    S6 --> END
    S8 --> END
    S10 --> END
    S11 --> END
```

### 4.3 Mermaid HAAT Model Interaction Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Human: User Profile] --> B1[HAAT Matching Engine]
    A2[Activity: ADL and IADL Goals] --> B1
    A3[Assistive Technology: Vehicle Specs] --> B1
    A4[Context: Environment and Social] --> B1
    B1 --> O1[Prescription and Outcome]
    O1 --> F1[Follow up and Reassessment Loop]
    F1 --> B1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*List and briefly explain any three clinical assessment parameters that govern the selection of a powered mobility device for a rehabilitation patient.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**
1. **Physical capacity** — Muscle strength, ROM, and sitting balance determine whether the user can self-propel a manual chair or needs powered drive.
2. **Cognitive ability** — The user must be able to learn control sequences, anticipate hazards, and follow traffic rules for safe operation; severe cognitive impairment mandates attendant control or sensor-assist.
3. **Functional goals & environment** — Indoor-only versus community use, kerb heights, doorway widths, and transport logistics decide the class of vehicle (manual, power, scooter, or smart).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest Pattern]**
*Differentiate between a power wheelchair and a smart wheelchair. State one clinical indication for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **power wheelchair (PWC)** uses an electric motor and a programmable joystick controller for direct user control. It is indicated for patients with intact cognition but limited upper-limb strength (e.g., lower thoracic SCI).

A **smart wheelchair** augments a PWC with sensors (ultrasonic, LiDAR, IMU) and a control algorithm for **obstacle detection, collision avoidance, and semi-autonomous navigation**. It is indicated for users with *cognitive impairment, visual field deficits, or motor-learning difficulties* (e.g., advanced MS, stroke with hemianopia), who would otherwise be unsafe with a conventional PWC.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for OEBRT722, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two complete alternate choices are given below — answer **either** the full OR the full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose EITHER A OR B*

**(a) [7 Marks] — Design & Calculation**
A power wheelchair with total mass $m = 110$ kg is to be used by a user on a tiled indoor floor ($\mu = 0.018$). The maximum cruising speed is $v = 1.8$ m/s, and the drivetrain efficiency is $\eta = 0.78$. The battery is rated at $24$ V, $75$ Ah. Calculate:
(i) the rolling resistance power, and
(ii) the practical range of the wheelchair at this cruising speed.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Conceptual / Descriptive**
With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a smart robotic wheelchair** suitable for a high-level quadriplegic user. Identify the role of at least four sensor modalities and the human–machine interface.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

**(a) [7 Marks — Step-by-step solution]**

**Given:**
$m = 110$ kg, $\mu = 0.018$, $v = 1.8$ m/s, $\eta = 0.78$, $V = 24$ V, $\text{Ah} = 75$.

**(i) Rolling resistance power (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{roll}} &= \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v \\
&= 0.018 \times 110 \times 9.81 \times 1.8 \\
&= 34.95 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Battery energy (1 mark)**

$$
C_{\text{batt}} = 24 \times 75 = 1800 \text{ Wh}
$$

**(ii) Practical range (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{batt}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{roll}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{34.95}{0.78} = 44.81 \text{ W} \\
t_{\text{op}} &= \dfrac{1800}{44.81} = 40.17 \text{ hours} \\
R_{\text{practical}} &= v \cdot t_{\text{op}} = 1.8 \times 40.17 = 72.31 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The 72 km figure is the *theoretical* upper bound at *flat, constant-velocity* cruise. Real-world range (25–40 km) is lower because of acceleration events, slopes, controller standby draw, and battery depth-of-discharge limits.

**(b) [7 Marks — Block Diagram + Description]**

The **smart wheelchair architecture** integrates four functional layers (also see Section 4.1):

1. **Human–Machine Interface (HMI) — 2 marks**
   The user issues commands through accessible controllers: **sip-and-puff** (pneumatic pressure for users with no hand function), **head-array** (proximity sensors in headrest), **chin joystick**, or **eye-tracker**. The HMI translates these into standard digital drive commands.

2. **Sensor Array — 2 marks**
   - **Ultrasonic sensors:** short-range (≤ 2 m) obstacle detection at wheel level.
   - **IR / LiDAR:** mid-range environmental mapping; LiDAR gives 2-D or 3-D scans for SLAM.
   - **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** detects tilt, slope, and sudden accelerations for fall/stability control.
   - **Wheel encoders:** provide odometry for dead-reckoning and speed feedback.

3. **Central Controller (1.5 marks)**
   A microcontroller or embedded PC runs a **sensor-fusion algorithm** (e.g., Extended Kalman Filter) combining LiDAR + odometry + IMU to build a real-time occupancy grid. An **obstacle-avoidance planner** (e.g., Dynamic Window Approach) modulates the user's joystick command, overriding it when a collision is imminent.

4. **Drive & Power Layer (1 mark)**
   Two **BLDC motors with planetary gearboxes** deliver the torque. A **CAN-bus** links the controller, BMS, actuators, and HMI. The **battery pack** feeds through a **DC–DC converter** for stable 5 V / 12 V rails to the electronics.

5. **Tele-monitoring (0.5 mark)**
   Bluetooth / Wi-Fi link allows the **clinician to review usage logs, battery health, and collision events** for outcome assessment and prescription refinement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Conceptual**
Explain the **HAAT (Human–Activity–Assistive Technology) Model** and the **MPT (Matching Person and Technology) Process**. Illustrate with one example of how a mismatched vehicle prescription can lead to device abandonment.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Analytical**
A rehabilitation engineer must select between two wheelchairs for a 75 kg, 1.70 m tall, post-polio patient who lives in a 1-BHK apartment with 760 mm doorways and uses community transport thrice weekly:

- **Device X:** Manual folding wheelchair, mass 12 kg, 18-inch seat width.
- **Device Y:** Power wheelchair, mass 65 kg, 18-inch seat width, foldable back, 24 V / 40 Ah battery.

Using the **Tri-Phase selection model** and a **weighted scoring matrix** with five criteria, justify the recommendation. *(Student is expected to construct the matrix and reach a defensible conclusion.)*

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

The **HAAT Model** (Cook & Hussey) describes the dynamic interaction of four elements that determine whether a person with a disability can perform an activity:
- **Human** (physical, cognitive, sensory profile),
- **Activity** (ADL, IADL, leisure, work),
- **Assistive Technology** (the vehicle), and
- **Context** (physical, social, cultural, institutional environment).

All four must be in **harmony**. A breakdown in any one element cascades into device failure, even if the technology itself is state-of-the-art.

The **MPT Process** (Scherer) is a *person-centred* assessment tool that matches the user's characteristics, needs, and preferences to the technology, while predicting **psychosocial fit and abandonment risk**.

**Example of mismatch / abandonment:** A young, active, paraplegic construction supervisor is prescribed a *heavy, rear-wheel-drive power wheelchair with a captain's seat* for community use. The chair cannot navigate the construction-site gravel, has poor range, and the captain's seat forces an undignified, passive posture. Result: the user *abandons* the device within 3 months and buys a second-hand manual chair. The failure lies in **Activity–Context mismatch**, not in the technology itself.

**(b) [7 Marks — Analytical]**

**Step 1: User Profile (1 mark)**
75 kg, 1.70 m, post-polio with **moderate lower-limb weakness** but **preserved upper limbs**. Apartment: 760 mm doorways (just at ADA minimum). Transport: community bus thrice weekly.

**Step 2: Context (1 mark)**
Indoor — tight turns, 760 mm doors. Outdoor — uneven kerbs, ramps, bus lifts. Requires frequent folding or vehicle lift compatibility.

**Step 3: Construct the weighted matrix (3 marks)**

| Criterion | Weight | Device X (Manual) | Device Y (Power) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor manoeuvrability | 0.20 | 5 | 3 |
| Upper-limb preservation | 0.20 | 5 | 5 |
| Community access | 0.20 | 4 | 4 |
| Transport compatibility (fold/lift) | 0.25 | 5 | 2 |
| Cost & maintenance | 0.15 | 5 | 3 |
| **Weighted Score** | 1.00 | **4.80** | **3.20** |

**Step 4: Conclusion (2 marks)**
The **manual folding wheelchair (Device X)** scores higher because:
- The user has **preserved upper-limb strength** (post-polio typically spares arms).
- The 760 mm doors and frequent bus use **penalise** the heavier, non-foldable power chair.
- Power chairs are clinically appropriate when upper limbs are weak — here, they are not.

**Recommendation: Device X, with optional power-assist add-on wheels** as a hybrid compromise for the community trips. This preserves the user's functional independence, dignity, and social participation — the *core goals* of robotic rehabilitation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units mismatch:** Students often forget to convert **mm → m** in kerb-climbing or doorway-width problems. Always work in SI units.
> 2. **Sign convention in slope:** Use $+$ for *uphill* power and $-$ for *regenerative downhill* braking. Examiner penalises confusion between $\sin\theta$ and $\tan\theta$.
> 3. **Cognitive domain is non-optional:** Many students focus only on *physical* parameters and forget the **MPT psychosocial check**. Marks are reserved for cognitive, environmental, and social fit — not just torque and speed.
> 4. **No `|` in tables:** Using `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table will *break the table rendering* — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX cells.
> 5. **Single blank line** is required before and after every `$$` block. Failing this causes **equation-rendering errors** in digital answer scripts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Selecting a Vehicle** is a *prescription*, not a sale — driven by the **HAAT** and **MPT** models.
- The **Tri-Phase model** (User → Context → Device) is the standard clinical-engineering workflow.
- The four key vehicle classes are **Manual**, **Power-Assisted Manual**, **Power Wheelchair**, and **Smart/Robotic Wheelchair**.
- **Smart wheelchairs** add *sensor fusion, SLAM, and collision avoidance* on top of a PWC for cognitively or visually impaired users.
- The **total power demand** is $P_{\text{total}} = m \cdot v \left( \mu \cdot g + g \cdot \sin\theta + a \right)$.
- **Range** $R = \dfrac{v \cdot \eta \cdot C_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{wheel}}}$ — always report *practical* (efficiency-adjusted) values.
- **Standard boundaries:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², ADA door = 815 mm, indoor slope ≤ 8°.
- **Smart wheelchair sensors:** Ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, IMU, encoders — each with a distinct role.
- **Alternative controls:** sip-and-puff, head-array, chin-joystick, eye-tracker — graded by user capability.
- **ICF framework:** selection outcome is measured by *Activity* and *Participation*, not just by device specs.
- **Validation:** every prescription must end with a *device trial, fitting, and follow-up* — abandonment is a metric of failure.
- **Ethical reminder:** *Nothing about us, without us* — user preference and dignity outweigh technical capability.
- **Standards to know for viva:** ISO 7176 (wheelchair test methods), RESNA WC standards, FDA Class I/II device classification.
- **Quick mental check:** *Does the device disappear into the user's life?* If yes → correct selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lift Mechanisms, Wheelchair Securement Systems, Passenger Restraint Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lift Mechanisms, Wheelchair Securement Systems, Passenger Restraint Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: LIFT MECHANISMS, WHEELCHAIR SECUREMENT & PASSENGER RESTRAINT SYSTEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Lift Mechanisms (Rehabilitation Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> A **Lift Mechanism** in robotic rehabilitation is a mechatronic or robotic actuation system designed to vertically or obliquely transfer a patient, a wheelchair-bound user, or assistive equipment between different elevation levels. It integrates principles of mechanical engineering (linkages, hydraulics, screw drives), electrical engineering (motors, controllers, sensors), and rehabilitation science (biomechanics, ergonomics) to enable safe and dignified mobility for individuals with locomotor disabilities.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a mechanical "elevator" for people who cannot climb stairs.**
> Imagine you are carrying a heavy suitcase up the stairs — exhausting, right? A lift mechanism does this heavy lifting for you. In rehabilitation, a person with a spinal cord injury cannot climb stairs on their own. A **lift mechanism** acts as a robotic assistant that physically moves the person (and often their wheelchair) from the ground floor to a higher floor, or into a vehicle. The "robot" part comes from the sensors and motors that ensure the lift moves smoothly, stops gently, and never tilts dangerously — much like a self-balancing Segway, but designed for human transport.

**Types of Lift Mechanisms in Rehabilitation:**

| Type | Primary Use | Key Metric |
|------|------------|------------|
| **Vertical Platform Lift (VPL)** | Building entry for wheelchair users | Lift capacity: **250–500 kg** |
| **Stair Lift (Perched/Seated)** | Residential multi-floor movement | Speed: **0.10–0.15 m/s** |
| **Hydraulic Vehicle Lift** | Loading wheelchair into van/bus | Lifting force: **1500–4000 N** |
| **Electric Scissor Lift** | Transfer to elevated surfaces | Stroke: **300–1500 mm** |
| **Patient Hoist (Ceiling-Mounted)** | Bed-to-chair transfer | Travel: **up to 6 m** |

---

### 1.2 Wheelchair Securement Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Wheelchair Securement System (WSS)** is an engineered restraint assembly that mechanically anchors a wheelchair and its occupant to the floor of a motor vehicle (bus, van, train) such that, during normal driving and in the event of a collision, no relative motion occurs between the wheelchair, the occupant, and the vehicle chassis. The system conforms to international standards such as **SAE J2249**, **ISO 10542**, and **WC19 (ANSI/RESNA)**.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a "seatbelt for the entire wheelchair."**
> When you sit in a car, the seatbelt holds *you*. But a wheelchair user sits *inside* a wheelchair, so simply holding the person is not enough — we must also hold the wheelchair itself, otherwise the chair becomes a heavy projectile in a crash. A Wheelchair Securement System uses **strong straps with metal hooks** (like the tie-downs used to secure cargo in a truck) to lock the wheelchair's frame to the vehicle's floor. It is essentially "strapping the chair down" the same way you would strap a piece of luggage onto a roof rack — but engineered to survive a 30 mph (≈48 km/h) crash.

**Key Standards & Load Capacities:**

- **SAE J2249 (WTORS – Wheelchair Tie-down and Occupant Restraint Systems):** Defines **4-point tie-down** with a minimum strength of **22.2 kN (5000 lbf)** per strap.
- **ISO 10542:** International equivalent; specifies **3-point** and **4-point** configurations.
- **WC19 Wheelchair Standard:** Crash-tested wheelchair frame; **passes 20g frontal impact test**.

---

### 1.3 Passenger Restraint Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Passenger Restraint System (PRS)** in rehabilitation is a personal safety device — typically a multi-point belt, harness, or postural support — that retains the wheelchair-seated occupant against the forces of deceleration, lateral acceleration, and vibration. It operates in conjunction with the Wheelchair Securement System to protect the user from secondary injury (e.g., submarining, head excursion) during transit.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a "5-point harness for a child in a race car."**
> In a Formula 1 car, drivers wear a 5-point harness that holds their shoulders, hips, and between the legs. Similarly, passengers with disabilities often need more than a simple car seatbelt because they have poor trunk control (e.g., cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy). A Passenger Restraint System uses **padded shoulder straps, lap belts, and crotch straps** to keep the user's torso upright and prevent them from sliding forward ("submarining") or sideways during a sudden stop. It is the "human seatbelt" that works *together* with the wheelchair securement straps to give complete crash protection.

**Standard 3-Point + 2-Point Configuration:**

$$\text{Total Anchor Points} = 3_{\text{(shoulder + lap)}} + 2_{\text{(pelvic + crotch)}} = 5 \text{ points}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse **Wheelchair Securement** with **Passenger Restraint**. Securement ties the *chair* to the floor. Restraint holds the *person* to the chair. Both are required for safe transport.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lift Mechanism — Engineering Analysis

### 2.1.1 Operating Principles (Bulleted Logic)

A rehabilitation lift mechanism is governed by four functional blocks:

1. **Actuator Block** — Converts stored energy into mechanical motion.
   - *Hydraulic cylinder*: Uses incompressible fluid (oil) pressurized by an electric pump.
   - *Linear actuator (ball screw)*: A rotating servo motor drives a nut along a threaded shaft.
   - *Pneumatic cylinder*: Uses compressed air; lightweight but less precise.

2. **Transmission Block** — Multiplies force and modifies motion.
   - *Scissor linkage*: A series of crossed arms that convert vertical piston motion into amplified vertical platform lift.
   - *Cable-and-pulley*: Used in ceiling hoists for horizontal traverse.
   - *Rack-and-pinion*: Converts rotational motor motion to linear lift.

3. **Control Block** — Governs speed, position, and safety.
   - *Sensors*: Limit switches, load cells, inclinometers, encoders.
   - *Controller*: Microcontroller / PLC implementing PID control.
   - *HMI*: Handheld pendant, push-button panel, or joystick.

4. **Safety Block** — Redundant failure protection.
   - *Mechanical locking pin*, *manual override valve*, *emergency stop*, *anti-crush sensor*.

### 2.1.2 Why and How — Accessible Explanation

- **Why a scissor mechanism?** A single hydraulic cylinder can only lift as much force as its bore area × pressure allows. A scissor linkage *geometrically multiplies* this force. If the cylinder pushes the scissor to a 45° angle, the platform rises at a slower rate but with significantly more lifting capacity. This is the principle of **mechanical advantage**.

- **How is patient safety ensured?** Load cells on the platform continuously measure weight. If the weight exceeds a safe threshold (e.g., the lift's rated capacity of 300 kg), the controller cuts power to the motor and triggers a brake. An *inclinometer* ensures the platform is level (typically within ±2° of horizontal) before motion begins.

### 2.1.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Lift Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas (Table Format)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Hydraulic cylinder force | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ = lift force, $P$ = pressure, $A$ = piston area | $N$, $Pa$, $m^2$ |
| 2 | Scissor mechanical advantage | $MA = \dfrac{L_{arm}}{2 \cdot h \cdot \tan(\theta)}$ | $L_{arm}$ = arm length, $h$ = vertical lift, $\theta$ = arm angle | dimensionless |
| 3 | Power required | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta}$ | $v$ = lift speed, $\eta$ = efficiency (0.7–0.9) | $W$ (watts) |
| 4 | Hydraulic pressure | $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot h_{head}}{1000}$ | $\rho$ = fluid density, $g$ = gravity, $h_{head}$ = head | $kPa$ |
| 5 | Torque on screw drive | $\tau = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{2\pi\eta}$ | $p$ = screw pitch, $F$ = axial load | $N \cdot m$ |
| 6 | Patient weight transfer force | $F_{transfer} = m_{patient} \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = tilt angle from vertical | $N$ |
| 7 | Lift cycle time | $t_{cycle} = \dfrac{h_{lift}}{v_{avg}}$ | $h_{lift}$ = lift height | $s$ |
| 8 | Energy per cycle | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h_{lift}$ | $m$ = total lifted mass | $J$ (joules) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Table Symbols:** Per KTU formatting protocol, vertical bars such as $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

---

## 2.2 Wheelchair Securement System — Engineering Analysis

### 2.2.1 Operating Principles (Bulleted Logic)

1. **Anchor Point Block (Vehicle Floor):**
   - Floor-track rails (continuous) or individual floor-insert anchors.
   - Rated to **22.2 kN** tensile load per ISO 10542-1.

2. **Tie-down Strap Block (Wheelchair Frame):**
   - 4-point system: Two front straps + Two rear straps.
   - Hook ends attach to reinforced wheelchair frame (WC19-compliant).
   - Ratchet tensioner applies **22–45 N·m** of torque.

3. **Docking Lock Block (Alternative to straps):**
   - A "lock-and-pin" device mounted under the wheelchair automatically engages a floor receptacle. No manual strapping required.

4. **Crash Performance Block:**
   - System must withstand a **20g (196 m/s²) deceleration** in the longitudinal axis.
   - Upper anchorage must restrain shoulder-belt load up to **10 kN**.

### 2.2.2 Why and How — Accessible Explanation

- **Why 4 points and not 2?** Two points would allow the wheelchair to rotate around the front-rear axis like a see-saw. Four points (front-left, front-right, rear-left, rear-right) prevent rotation in all three axes: pitch, roll, and yaw.

- **How is crash safety tested?** The wheelchair and dummy occupant are mounted on a sled and accelerated to 30–48 km/h, then decelerated to 0 in less than 100 ms. High-speed cameras record whether the dummy's head, chest, and pelvis exceed injury thresholds (HIC < 1000, chest deflection < 75 mm).

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Wheelchair Securement

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Deceleration force on wheelchair | $F_{decel} = m_{sys} \cdot a_{peak}$ | $m_{sys}$ = total system mass, $a_{peak}$ = peak decel | $N$, $kg$, $m/s^2$ |
| 2 | Required strap tensile strength | $F_{strap} = \dfrac{F_{decel}}{n \cdot \cos(\phi)}$ | $n$ = number of straps, $\phi$ = strap angle from horizontal | $N$ |
| 3 | Stopping distance from energy | $d = \dfrac{v^2}{2a}$ | $v$ = impact velocity, $a$ = deceleration | $m$ |
| 4 | Dynamic load factor | $DLF = \dfrac{a_{peak}}{g}$ | Ratio of peak decel to gravity | dimensionless |
| 5 | Sled test velocity | $v_{test} = 48 \, km/h = 13.33 \, m/s$ | Standard transit crash velocity | $m/s$ |
| 6 | Wheelchair + user total mass | $m_{sys} = m_{chair} + m_{user} + m_{restraint}$ | Sum of masses | $kg$ |
| 7 | Strap elongation under load | $\Delta L = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A \cdot E}$ | $L_0$ = strap length, $A$ = cross-section, $E$ = Young's modulus | $m$ |
| 8 | Hook disengagement force | $F_{release} = F_{strap} \cdot SF$ | $SF$ = safety factor (typically 1.5–2.0) | $N$ |

---

## 2.3 Passenger Restraint System — Engineering Analysis

### 2.3.1 Operating Principles (Bulleted Logic)

1. **Pelvic Restraint Block:**
   - Lap belt anchored at 30°–75° from horizontal.
   - Restrains pelvis to prevent forward sliding (*submarining*).

2. **Upper-Torso Restraint Block:**
   - Shoulder belt anchored above and behind the shoulder.
   - Anchored to vehicle wall, NOT to wheelchair.

3. **Crotch/Anti-Submarine Strap Block:**
   - Optional 5th point anchored between the thighs.
   - Prevents the pelvis from sliding under the lap belt.

4. **Postural Support Block (Optional):**
   - Lateral trunk supports, headrests, abductors.
   - For users with poor trunk control (cerebral palsy, ALS).

### 2.3.2 Why and How — Accessible Explanation

- **Why is the upper anchor attached to the vehicle wall and not the chair?** If the shoulder belt were attached to the wheelchair, in a frontal crash the chair would deform and the belt would not transfer the load to the strong vehicle structure. Anchoring to the *vehicle* ensures the load path goes directly into the chassis.

- **How is "submarining" prevented?** When a vehicle stops suddenly, the lower body tends to slide forward under the lap belt (this is called submarining — the pelvis dives under the belt like a submarine). A **crotch strap** anchored to the floor between the legs physically blocks this motion by pulling the pelvis backward.

### 2.3.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Passenger Restraint

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Belt tension in crash | $T = m_{upper} \cdot a_{decel}$ | $m_{upper}$ = mass of upper body (~65% body) | $N$ |
| 2 | Lap belt angle optimum | $\theta_{lap} = 30° \text{ to } 75°$ from horizontal | Standard range per ISO 10542 | degrees |
| 3 | Shoulder belt angle optimum | $\theta_{sh} = 45° \pm 10°$ from shoulder | Anchored above shoulder | degrees |
| 4 | HIC (Head Injury Criterion) | $HIC = \left[\dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a \, dt\right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | $a$ = head accel in g | dimensionless |
| 5 | Chest deflection limit | $\delta_{chest} \leq 75 \, mm$ | Thorax compression | $mm$ |
| 6 | Submarine prevention force | $F_{sub} = m_{pelvis} \cdot a_{decel} \cdot \sin(\beta)$ | $\beta$ = belt angle from seat plane | $N$ |
| 7 | Restraint load path | $F_{anchor} = \dfrac{F_{belt}}{\cos(\alpha)}$ | $\alpha$ = belt angle to anchor | $N$ |
| 8 | Webbing energy absorption | $E_{web} = \int F \cdot dL$ | Area under force-elongation curve | $J$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these systems are used in industry:**
> - **Public Transit Buses:** City buses in Europe, Japan, USA mandate WC19-compliant securement.
> - **Accessible Vans:** Conversion companies (BraunAbility, VMI) integrate hydraulic lifts + 4-point tie-downs.
> - **Air Travel:** Airlines use the ACAA (Air Carrier Access Act) compliant tie-downs in lavatories and cabin floors.
> - **Hospital Patient Transfer:** Overhead ceiling hoists reduce nurse back injuries by 80% (per OSHA studies).
> - **Rehabilitation Robotics Research:** Exoskeleton lifts (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT) use miniature scissor actuators to assist sit-to-stand transitions.
