| ISO 7176 Part | Title | What It Tests | Typical Setup |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Part 1** | Determination of static stability | Tipping angle (rearward, forward, sideways) on a tilt platform | Inclinable test plane + ATD (Anthropomorphic Test Device) |
| **Part 2** | Determination of dynamic stability of electric wheelchairs | Stability during straight running, turning, slope traversal | Motor-driven treadmill / slope rig |
| **Part 3** | Determination of effectiveness of brakes | Braking distance / deceleration on level and 7° slope | Force-brake rig, slope test rig |
| **Part 4** | Energy consumption of electric wheelchairs | Distance travelled per charge at defined load | Treadmill dynamometer |
| **Part 5** | Determination of dimensions, mass, turning space | Footprint, mass, minimum turning radius | Tape, weighing scale, marked floor |
| **Part 6** | Determination of maximum speed | Top speed under load and no load | Speed-measurement rollers |
| **Part 7** | Measurement of seating and wheel dimensions | Seat depth, width, wheel diameter | Calipers, gauges |
| **Part 8** | Static, impact and fatigue strength tests | Frame robustness under repeated loading | Hydraulic actuators, drop rig |
| **Part 9** | Climatic tests for electric wheelchairs | Behaviour under humidity, temperature | Environmental chamber |
| **Part 10** | Obstacle-climbing ability | Kerb / threshold climbing | Test kerb of defined height |
| **Part 11** | Test dummies | ATD specifications for load simulation | Standardised ATD |
| **Part 13** | Determination of coefficient of friction of test surfaces | Surface friction specification | Tribometer |
| **Part 14** | Power and control systems for electric wheelchairs | Electrical safety, controller logic | Bench electrical test rig |
| **Part 15** | Requirements for information disclosure | User manual content, labelling | Document review |
| **Part 16** | Resistance to ignition | Flammability of upholstery | Flame test chamber |
| **Part 19** | Wheeled mobility devices for use as seats in motor vehicles | Crashworthiness in transit | Sled test rig |
| **Part 22** | Set-up procedures | Pre-test preparation protocol | Documented procedure |

> [!NOTE]
> **For the KTU Board Exam, treat the ISO 7176 series as the canonical reference.** Always cite the part number and the year of the standard (e.g., ISO 7176-8:2014) in descriptive answers.

## 2.2 Static Stability (ISO 7176-1) — The Most Asked Concept

**Static stability** quantifies the wheel chair's resistance to tipping over when the user is stationary. The test is performed by:

1. Loading the wheelchair with the **standard test dummy (ATD)** of defined mass.
2. Placing the wheelchair on a **gradually inclinable plane**.
3. Tilting the plane in the **rearward, forward, and lateral** directions until the chair reaches the *tipping point* (the angle at which the wheel-chair's centre of gravity passes outside the support polygon).
4. Recording the **tilt angle at which tipping occurs** as the *stability angle*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A wheelchair is **stable** at a tilt angle if it returns to its original upright position when the plane is levelled. It is **unstable (tipped)** if it falls over during tilting.

## 2.3 Brake Effectiveness (ISO 7176-3)

The brake test is performed in two configurations:

- **Level test:** Wheelchair on flat ground, propelled to a defined test speed, brakes applied, **stopping distance** measured.
- **Slope test:** Wheelchair parked on a **7° inclined plane** (approx. 1:8 gradient, representative of a standard ramp). The parking brake is engaged, and the chair must not roll for a minimum dwell time (typically **5 minutes**).

## 2.4 Static, Impact and Fatigue Strength (ISO 7176-8)

This is the **most engineering-intensive** test and uses hydraulic actuators to load:

- **Seat** with vertical load (simulating user weight).
- **Backrest** with horizontal load (simulating leaning back).
- **Armrests** with vertical and lateral loads.
- **Footrests** with vertical loads.

The chair is subjected to **200,000 cycles** (or as specified) of repeated loading without permanent deformation or fracture.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the magic number:** Fatigue test is generally executed over **≥ 200,000 loading cycles** — this is a frequently quoted figure in KTU questions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Wheelchair Testing (commit to memory):**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description of Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tipping (tip-over) angle for static stability | $\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{h}\right)$ | $d$ = horizontal distance from CoG to tipping edge, $h$ = height of Centre of Gravity | degrees (°) |
| 2 | Stability factor (anti-tip margin) | $SF = \dfrac{\theta_{tip}}{\theta_{slope}}$ | $\theta_{tip}$ = tip-over angle, $\theta_{slope}$ = actual slope of travel | dimensionless |
| 3 | Braking deceleration | $a = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2s}$ | $v$ = velocity at brake application, $s$ = stopping distance | $\text{m/s}^{2}$ |
| 4 | Brake effectiveness (level) | $BE = \dfrac{s_{test}}{s_{ref}} \times 100$ | $s_{test}$ = measured stopping distance, $s_{ref}$ = reference distance | % |
| 5 | Braking force on slope | $F_{brake} \geq m g \sin(\alpha) + \mu m g \cos(\alpha)$ | $m$ = total mass, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\alpha$ = slope angle, $\mu$ = tyre–surface friction coeff. | N (Newton) |
| 6 | Energy consumption (powered chair) | $E = \dfrac{C \cdot V}{d}$ | $C$ = battery capacity (Ah), $V$ = nominal voltage, $d$ = distance travelled | Wh/km |
| 7 | Range of powered chair | $R = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{\overline{P}_{draw}}$ | $E_{batt}$ = usable battery energy, $\overline{P}_{draw}$ = mean power draw | km |
| 8 | Maximum theoretical slope (no slip) | $\alpha_{max} = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between tyre and surface | degrees (°) |
| 9 | Reynolds / fatigue cycle life (Wöhler) | $N = k \cdot \sigma^{-m}$ | $N$ = cycles to failure, $\sigma$ = applied stress, $k, m$ = material constants | dimensionless |
| 10 | Centre of gravity height (bilinear) | $h_{CG} = \dfrac{m_{u} h_{u} + m_{c} h_{c}}{m_{u} + m_{c}}$ | $m_{u}, h_{u}$ = user mass and CoG height, $m_{c}, h_{c}$ = chair mass and CoG height | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - **Reference test slope** for brake tests: **7° (1:8 gradient)**
> - **Reference ATD mass** for ISO 7176: configurable, commonly **75 kg, 100 kg, 150 kg, 200 kg** test loads
> - **Reference temperature** for climatic tests: **23 °C ± 2 °C**
> - **Minimum door-passing dimension** per ISO 7176-5: chair must fit through an **800 mm** doorway

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Static Tipping Angle (Forward Stability)

Consider a side-view free body diagram of a wheelchair on a tilting plane. The **centre of gravity (G)** of the combined user-chair system is at a height $h$ above the ground. The **support polygon** is bounded by the front and rear wheel contact points. As the plane is tilted, the vertical from G shifts horizontally. The chair becomes **unstable** at the instant this vertical line crosses the **forward wheel contact point (or the rear, depending on direction of tilt)**.

### Step 1 — Define the geometry

Let the horizontal distance from the centre of gravity G to the tipping edge (say, the front wheel contact) be $d$ when the plane is level. The height of G above the tipping edge is $h$.

### Step 2 — Express the tipping condition geometrically

As the plane tilts by an angle $\theta$, the gravity vector (vertical) effectively rotates by $\theta$ with respect to the chair frame. The chair tips when the vertical line through G passes through the tipping edge. From the geometry of a right triangle:

$$
\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \dfrac{d}{h}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the tip angle

$$
\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{h}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> A **larger $d$** (CoG farther forward from the rear axle) means a **larger tipping angle** (more stable). A **higher $h$** (taller user, or load placed high on the chair) means a **smaller tipping angle** (less stable).

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A wheelchair user has a combined centre of gravity located 0.45 m behind the front wheel contact point and at a height of 0.90 m above the ground. Calculate the forward static tip-over angle.

**Solution:**

$$
\tan(\theta_{tip}) = \dfrac{d}{h} = \dfrac{0.45}{0.90} = 0.5
$$

$$
\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57^{\circ}
$$

The wheelchair will **tip forward** if the plane is tilted beyond **26.57°**. The test engineer records this value as the *forward static stability angle*.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Braking Force Required on an Incline

A wheelchair of total mass $m$ is parked on a slope inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. The parking brake must prevent rolling. The forces acting along the slope are:

- **Gravity component along the slope (downhill):** $F_{g} = m g \sin(\alpha)$
- **Maximum static friction force opposing motion (uphill, from tyres on surface):** $F_{f} = \mu m g \cos(\alpha)$

### Step 1 — Write the force balance at the threshold of rolling

For the chair to remain stationary, the brake must counteract the net downhill force. The worst case is when friction acts **downhill** (i.e., chair is on the verge of rolling, friction opposing the impending motion of the rear wheels is in the downhill direction). The required brake force is:

$$
F_{brake} \geq m g \sin(\alpha) + \mu m g \cos(\alpha)
$$

### Step 2 — Factor the expression

$$
F_{brake} \geq m g \left[ \sin(\alpha) + \mu \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate for the ISO 7176-3 reference slope

For the standard test slope of $\alpha = 7^{\circ}$ and assuming a typical rubber-tyre-on-steel friction coefficient $\mu = 0.7$ and a total system mass of $m = 100$ kg:

$$
F_{brake} \geq 100 \times 9.81 \times \left[ \sin(7^{\circ}) + 0.7 \cos(7^{\circ}) \right]
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq 981 \times \left[ 0.1219 + 0.7 \times 0.9925 \right]
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq 981 \times \left[ 0.1219 + 0.6948 \right] = 981 \times 0.8167
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq 801.2 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

The parking brake must be capable of holding the chair with at least **801 N of braking force** under these test conditions. If the brake slips before this force is sustained for 5 minutes, the chair **fails** ISO 7176-3.

## 3.3 Derivation of Braking Deceleration and Stopping Distance

### Step 1 — Kinematic relation

Using the constant-deceleration kinematic identity (third equation of motion):

$$
v_{f}^{2} = v_{i}^{2} - 2 a s
$$

At the moment the chair stops, $v_{f} = 0$:

$$
0 = v_{i}^{2} - 2 a s
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for stopping distance

$$
s = \dfrac{v_{i}^{2}}{2 a}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for deceleration

$$
a = \dfrac{v_{i}^{2}}{2 s}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A powered wheelchair is travelling at $v_{i} = 2.5$ m/s (9 km/h). The brake effectiveness test records a stopping distance of $s = 0.8$ m. Calculate the deceleration.

$$
a = \dfrac{(2.5)^{2}}{2 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{6.25}{1.6} = 3.906 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

In units of g:

$$
a = \dfrac{3.906}{9.81} = 0.398 \, g
$$

The brake effectiveness is then compared with the ISO 7176-3 reference deceleration. The chair passes if the measured deceleration is **less than or equal to** the regulatory maximum (typically 0.6 g) to ensure occupant safety.

## 3.4 Derivation of Energy Consumption per Kilometre

For a powered wheelchair:

### Step 1 — Total energy stored in battery

$$
E_{batt} = V \times C
$$

where $V$ is the nominal battery voltage (V) and $C$ is the rated capacity in ampere-hours (Ah). Note: $1 \text{ V·Ah} = 1 \text{ Wh}$.

### Step 2 — Energy per kilometre (specific consumption)

$$
e_{km} = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{d_{range}}
$$

where $d_{range}$ is the measured test distance (km) covered in ISO 7176-4 protocol.

### Step 3 — Worked Example

> A powered chair uses two 12 V, 35 Ah batteries. Test range = 30 km. Calculate specific energy consumption.

$$
E_{batt} = 24 \text{ V} \times 35 \text{ Ah} = 840 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
e_{km} = \dfrac{840}{30} = 28 \text{ Wh/km}
$$

This is a reasonable value for a class-A powered wheelchair per ISO 7176-4.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ISO 7176 Test Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the relationship between the various ISO 7176 test categories. Use this as a mental "menu" when answering exam questions on standard tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA[Wheelchair Standard Test Suite - ISO 7176]
    startA --> groupA[Stability Tests]
    startA --> groupB[Strength and Durability Tests]
    startA --> groupC[Performance Tests]
    startA --> groupD[Environmental and Safety Tests]
    startA --> groupE[Documentation and Labelling Tests]

    groupA --> testA1[Static Stability - ISO 7176-1]
    groupA --> testA2[Dynamic Stability - ISO 7176-2]
    groupA --> testA3[Test Dummies - ISO 7176-11]

    groupB --> testB1[Static Impact and Fatigue Strength - ISO 7176-8]
    groupB --> testB2[Resistance to Ignition - ISO 7176-16]
    groupB --> testB3[Crashworthiness in Vehicles - ISO 7176-19]

    groupC --> testC1[Brake Effectiveness - ISO 7176-3]
    groupC --> testC2[Energy Consumption - ISO 7176-4]
    groupC --> testC3[Overall Dimensions - ISO 7176-5]
    groupC --> testC4[Maximum Speed - ISO 7176-6]
    groupC --> testC5[Obstacle Climbing - ISO 7176-10]
    groupC --> testC6[Power and Control Systems - ISO 7176-14]

    groupD --> testD1[Climatic Tests - ISO 7176-9]
    groupD --> testD2[Surface Friction Coefficient - ISO 7176-13]

    groupE --> testE1[Information Disclosure - ISO 7176-15]
    testE1 --> testE2[User Manual Review]
    testE1 --> testE3[Label and Marking Inspection]

    testA1 --> reqA1[Uses Inclinable Test Plane]
    testA1 --> reqA2[Loads with Standard ATD]
    testB1 --> reqB1[Uses Hydraulic Actuator]
    testB1 --> reqB2[200000 Loading Cycles]
    testC1 --> reqC3[Reference Slope 7 degrees]
    testC5 --> reqC4[Test Kerb of Defined Height]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Test-Sequence Flow — Static Stability Test (ISO 7176-1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 - Prepare ATD and Configure Mass] --> stepB[Step 2 - Seat ATD in Wheelchair]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 - Place Wheelchair on Inclinable Plane]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 - Lock All Wheels and Brakes]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 - Tilt Plane Slowly in Target Direction]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 - Record Angle at Tipping Event]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 - Reset and Repeat for All 4 Directions]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 - Document Forward Rearward Lateral Stability Angles]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9 - Compare with ISO 7176-1 Acceptance Criteria]
    stepI --> dec1{Pass or Fail}
    dec1 -->|Pass| out1[Wheelchair Certified]
    dec1 -->|Fail| out2[Wheelchair Returned to Manufacturer]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Brake Effectiveness Test Rig (ISO 7176-3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rigA[Wheelchair Brake Test Rig]
    rigA --> rigA1[Horizontal Level Surface]
    rigA --> rigA2[7 Degree Inclined Ramp]
    rigA --> rigA3[Force Sensor in Brake Cable]
    rigA --> rigA4[Speed Measurement Roller]
    rigA --> rigA5[Data Acquisition System]

    rigA1 --> testA1[Propel to Test Speed]
    testA1 --> testA2[Apply Brake]
    testA2 --> testA3[Measure Stopping Distance]

    rigA2 --> testB1[Park Chair on Ramp]
    testB1 --> testB2[Engage Parking Brake]
    testB2 --> testB3[Hold for 5 Minutes]
    testB3 --> testB4[Verify No Roll Occurred]

    testA3 --> result[Pass or Fail Decision]
    testB4 --> result
    result --> critA[Stopping Distance must be within Specified Limit]
    result --> critB[No Rolling during 5 Minute Dwell Time]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: List any three tests prescribed by the ISO 7176 standard for evaluating wheelchair safety. State the purpose of each test in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **ISO 7176-1 — Static Stability Test:** Determines the maximum tilt angle of an inclinable plane beyond which the wheelchair tips over (forward, rearward, or sideways) when loaded with the standard test dummy.

2. **ISO 7176-3 — Brake Effectiveness Test:** Verifies that the wheelchair's parking and service brakes are capable of stopping the chair within the prescribed stopping distance on level ground and of holding the chair stationary on a **7° inclined slope** for at least **5 minutes** without rolling.

3. **ISO 7176-8 — Static, Impact and Fatigue Strength Test:** Evaluates the structural integrity of the wheelchair frame, seat, backrest, armrests, and footrests under repeated cyclic loading (typically **200,000 cycles**) and under impact loading, to ensure the chair does not suffer permanent deformation or fracture.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark-split strategy (3 marks):** 1 mark per test name + purpose sentence. Use the standard part number and remember the magic numbers (7° and 200,000 cycles).

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: What is an Anthropomorphic Test Dummy (ATD) in the context of wheelchair testing? Why is it used instead of a human subject?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Anthropomorphic Test Dummy (ATD)** is a **standardised, instrumented mannequin** with defined mass distribution, joint mobility, and centre of gravity that simulates the seated human body during wheelchair tests. It is used in place of a human subject for the following reasons:

1. **Safety:** Repeated drop, impact, and tipping tests could injure a real person; the ATD can be subjected to destructive loading without harm.
2. **Repeatability and Standardisation:** The ATD has a precisely defined mass, geometry, and CoG location (per **ISO 7176-11**), ensuring that test results are reproducible across laboratories and product batches.
3. **Instrumentation:** The ATD is fitted with load cells, accelerometers, and strain gauges to quantitatively measure the forces and accelerations experienced during the test, providing objective pass/fail data.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark-split strategy (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the "why not human" justification (cover safety, repeatability, and instrumentation in 2-3 lines).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Convention:** For each Part B question, two alternatives (Question A and Question B) are provided. The student answers **one** of the two. Each alternative is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: (a) Describe in detail the ISO 7176-1 static stability test procedure for wheelchairs. What are the various stability angles measured, and what is the minimum acceptable value for clinical certification? (7 Marks)**

**(b) A powered wheelchair of total mass 120 kg (chair + user) is parked on a 7° ramp. The tyre–surface coefficient of static friction is 0.65. Calculate the minimum parking brake force required to prevent the chair from rolling. State two consequences if this brake force is insufficient. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) ISO 7176-1 Static Stability Test Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Pre-Test Setup (1 mark):** The wheelchair is configured per ISO 7176-22 setup procedure. The **standard test dummy (ATD)** of defined mass (commonly 75 kg or 100 kg, per test requirement) is placed in the wheelchair in the standard seated posture. Tyre pressures are set to manufacturer-specified values.

2. **Test Platform (1 mark):** The wheelchair is positioned on a **calibrated, inclinable test plane** equipped with a **graduated protractor** to measure tilt angle. All brakes are engaged, and the castors are positioned in the least stable configuration.

3. **Tilt Sequence (2 marks):** The plane is tilted **slowly and steadily** in four directions:
   - **Rearward** (backward tip test)
   - **Forward** (forward tip test)
   - **Lateral — left** (left-side tip test)
   - **Lateral — right** (right-side tip test)

4. **Recording (1 mark):** The **maximum tilt angle at which the wheelchair remains stable** (returns to upright when the plane is levelled) is recorded for each direction. The angle at which the chair *actually tips over* is also noted.

5. **Acceptance Criteria (2 marks):** Per ISO 7176-1 and most clinical certification bodies, a wheelchair should achieve:
   - **Forward static stability angle ≥ 10°**
   - **Rearward static stability angle ≥ 10°** (with anti-tip devices fitted if needed)
   - **Lateral static stability angle ≥ 15°** (left and right)

These minimum thresholds ensure that the user can traverse typical indoor ramps and uneven surfaces without tipping.

**(b) Brake Force Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Total mass $m = 120$ kg
- Slope angle $\alpha = 7°$
- Coefficient of static friction $\mu = 0.65$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Identify forces along the slope (2 marks):**

- Gravity component along slope (downhill): $F_{g} = m g \sin(\alpha)$
- Frictional resistance (worst case, downhill): $F_{f} = \mu m g \cos(\alpha)$

**Step 2 — Force balance for stationary condition (2 marks):**

$$
F_{brake} \geq m g \sin(\alpha) + \mu m g \cos(\alpha)
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq m g \left[ \sin(\alpha) + \mu \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**

$$
F_{brake} \geq 120 \times 9.81 \times \left[ \sin(7^{\circ}) + 0.65 \cos(7^{\circ}) \right]
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq 1177.2 \times \left[ 0.1219 + 0.65 \times 0.9925 \right]
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq 1177.2 \times \left[ 0.1219 + 0.6451 \right]
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq 1177.2 \times 0.7670
$$

$$
F_{brake} \geq 902.9 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Consequences of insufficient brake force (1 mark):**
- **Consequence 1:** The wheelchair will **roll down the ramp**, causing the user to fall or collide with obstacles, resulting in injury.
- **Consequence 2:** The chair will **fail the ISO 7176-3 brake test** and cannot be legally marketed or prescribed, leading to commercial loss and potential liability for the manufacturer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: (a) Explain the ISO 7176-8 static, impact and fatigue strength test for wheelchairs. Why is the 200,000-cycle benchmark used, and what components of the chair are tested? (7 Marks)**

**(b) A powered wheelchair is fitted with two 24 V, 40 Ah batteries. In an ISO 7176-4 energy consumption test, the chair covers a range of 35 km on a single charge at a constant test speed of 6 km/h. Calculate the specific energy consumption in Wh/km and the mean power draw during the test. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) ISO 7176-8 Static, Impact and Fatigue Strength Test (7 Marks):**

1. **Purpose (1 mark):** This test verifies that the structural components of a wheelchair can withstand the **repeated cyclic loads** experienced during daily use over several years of service, without fracture, permanent deformation, or loosening of fasteners.

2. **Components Tested (2 marks):**
   - **Seat** (vertical load)
   - **Backrest** (horizontal and vertical load)
   - **Armrests** (vertical and lateral load)
   - **Footrests / Legrests** (vertical load)
   - **Push handles** (lateral and vertical load)
   - **Frame and cross-braces** (overall structural integrity)

3. **Test Method (2 marks):** The wheelchair is mounted on a **servo-hydraulic test rig**. Each component is subjected to a defined force corresponding to the maximum user mass (e.g., 100 kg or 150 kg). The loading is cycled **200,000 times** (representing approximately 5 years of normal use). The chair is then inspected for cracks, permanent deformation, and fastener loosening.

4. **Rationale for 200,000 Cycles (1 mark):** The benchmark represents the **cumulative load cycles** that a typical wheelchair endures during a 5-year service life. Statistical fatigue analysis (Wöhler / S-N curve) shows that below the endurance limit, most aluminium and steel wheelchair alloys survive this cycle count without failure.

5. **Additional Impact Test (1 mark):** Separate from the fatigue test, a **single-impact drop test** is performed where the chair is dropped from a defined height onto a hard surface. The chair must remain functional and free of sharp protrusions after the impact.

**(b) Energy Consumption and Power Draw (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Two batteries of 24 V, 40 Ah each
- Range $d = 35$ km
- Test speed $v = 6$ km/h
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (not directly required here)

**Step 1 — Total stored energy (2 marks):**

$$
E_{batt} = V \times C = 24 \text{ V} \times 40 \text{ Ah} = 960 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 2 — Specific energy consumption (2 marks):**

$$
e_{km} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{d} = \dfrac{960 \text{ Wh}}{35 \text{ km}} = 27.43 \text{ Wh/km}
$$

**Step 3 — Test duration (1 mark):**

$$
t_{test} = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{35 \text{ km}}{6 \text{ km/h}} = 5.833 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean power draw (2 marks):**

$$
\overline{P}_{draw} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{t_{test}} = \dfrac{960 \text{ Wh}}{5.833 \text{ h}} = 164.6 \text{ W}
$$

> **Result:** The wheelchair consumes **27.43 Wh/km** at a mean power draw of **164.6 W**. These values are within the typical range (20–40 Wh/km) for class-A powered wheelchairs tested per ISO 7176-4.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Standard Part Number:** Students often write "the ISO 7176 test" without specifying the part. Always quote **ISO 7176-1, -3, -8**, etc. (Penalty: **−1 to −2 marks**)
>
> 2. **Mixing up the 7° slope with the 8° slope:** The ISO 7176-3 reference slope is **7° (1:8 gradient)**, not 8°. Writing 8° will lose a mark.
>
> 3. **Incorrect units on energy consumption:** Energy per distance is **Wh/km**, **not** W/km. Power is in **W (or Wh/h)**.
>
> 4. **Sign error in the brake-force equation:** Forgetting to add friction OR forgetting to multiply by mass OR using $\cos$ instead of $\sin$ for the slope component.
>
> 5. **Confusing $\mu$ (friction coefficient) in the brake equation:** The friction force can act in either direction depending on the verge-of-motion sense; always state the **worst case** (friction aiding the gravity component).
>
> 6. **Skipping units in intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award **partial marks** for correct intermediate values; missing units is a −0.5 mark penalty per occurrence.
>
> 7. **Not drawing / describing a free body diagram** in derivation questions: For 7-mark derivations, a brief FBD is **expected** by the examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Wheelchair Safety, Standards and Testing**

- [x] **Primary Standards:** ISO 7176 (global), ANSI/RESNA WC (US), IS 14452 (India), EN 12183/12184 (EU).
- [x] **ISO 7176 Series:** 26 parts — focus on Parts **1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11, 14, 15, 16, 19**.
- [x] **Static Stability (Part 1):** Tipping angle measured on inclinable plane; minimum clinical threshold is ~10° forward/rearward and ~15° lateral.
- [x] **Dynamic Stability (Part 2):** Stability during motion; only required for **powered** wheelchairs.
- [x] **Brake Effectiveness (Part 3):** Stopping distance on level + parking on **7° slope for 5 minutes**.
- [x] **Energy Consumption (Part 4):** Wh/km benchmark for powered wheelchairs; typical range **20–40 Wh/km**.
- [x] **Overall Dimensions (Part 5):** Must fit through an **800 mm** doorway.
- [x] **Static, Impact, Fatigue Strength (Part 8):** **200,000 cycle** benchmark over ~5 years of service.
- [x] **Climatic Test (Part 9):** Behaviour under humidity and temperature extremes (powered chairs only).
- [x] **Obstacle Climbing (Part 10):** Standard test kerb of defined height.
- [x] **Test Dummies (Part 11):** ATD specifications — replaces humans for safety and repeatability.
- [x] **Power and Control Systems (Part 14):** Electrical safety of controllers and battery systems.
- [x] **Information Disclosure (Part 15):** User manual, labelling, weight-limit declarations.
- [x] **Resistance to Ignition (Part 16):** Flammability of upholstery — must self-extinguish.
- [x] **Crashworthiness in Vehicles (Part 19):** Wheelchair-as-seat in motor vehicles.
- [x] **Tipping Angle Formula:** $\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}(d/h)$ — **must be memorised**.
- [x] **Brake Force Formula:** $F_{brake} \geq m g [\sin(\alpha) + \mu \cos(\alpha)]$ — **must be memorised**.
- [x] **Deceleration Formula:** $a = v^{2}/(2s)$ — **must be memorised**.
- [x] **Energy Consumption Formula:** $e_{km} = V \cdot C / d$ — **must be memorised**.
- [x] **Boundary Conditions:** 7° slope, 200,000 cycles, 800 mm doorway, ATD mass 75–200 kg.
- [x] **Always cite the Part Number and Year** (e.g., ISO 7176-8:2014) for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** If the question is *"Describe the safety tests for wheelchairs,"* structure your answer under **three** headings: **(i) Stability Tests (ISO 7176-1, -2)**, **(ii) Durability Tests (ISO 7176-8, -16)**, and **(iii) Performance Tests (ISO 7176-3, -4, -10)**. This shows the examiner that you understand the **categorical structure** of the standard and almost always secures full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normative Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Normative Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Normative Values** in the context of robotic rehabilitation are statistically derived, population-based reference data sets that quantify the *typical* or *healthy* range of human biomechanical, physiological, and functional parameters. These values are obtained from large samples of able-bodied individuals (stratified by age, sex, anthropometry, and laterality) and serve as the quantitative benchmark against which a patient's post-injury or post-surgery performance is compared.

In KTU 2024 terminology, normative values form the **reference dataset layer** of any clinical decision-support algorithm embedded inside a rehabilitation robot. They enable the robot to:

1. **Quantify Impairment** — Express the patient's deviation from healthy baselines as a percentage deficit.
2. **Personalize Therapy** — Adapt task difficulty, ROM targets, and assistance levels to the individual.
3. **Track Recovery Trajectory** — Plot progress curves against population norms.
4. **Validate Device Performance** — Confirm that a robot's output matches human-equivalent movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Normative values are the *bridge* between raw sensor data (kinematics, kinetics, EMG) and clinically meaningful rehabilitation outcomes. Without them, a rehabilitation robot is a motion-capture system — not a therapy device.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a driving instructor. Before teaching a recovering stroke patient to walk again, you must first know how a *healthy* person walks — how far each step should be, how fast, how symmetrically. These "average walking numbers" of healthy people are exactly what **normative values** represent.

Think of it like a school report card:

- The **class average** (normative value) tells you where a student *should* be.
- The **individual score** (patient's measured value) tells you where they *actually* are.
- The **gap** (deficit) tells the rehabilitation robot how much assistance, repetition, and resistance to deliver.

Just as a thermometer has fixed reference points (0°C = water freezing, 100°C = boiling), a rehabilitation robot needs fixed reference points (normative ROM, normative strength, normative gait speed) to interpret what a patient's body is doing.

### 1.3 Categories of Normative Data

| Category | What It Measures | Example Parameter |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Kinematic** | Motion (displacement, velocity) | Joint angles, stride length |
| **Kinetic** | Forces and moments | Joint torques, ground reaction forces |
| **Electromyographic (EMG)** | Muscle activation | RMS amplitude, median frequency |
| **Functional** | Task-level performance | Timed Up-and-Go (TUG), Berg Balance Score |
| **Anthropometric** | Body structure | Segment mass, limb length, COM location |
| **Strength** | Muscle force capacity | Isokinetic peak torque |
| **Cardiorespiratory** | Endurance metrics | VO₂ max, 6-min walk distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Range of Motion (ROM):** The maximum angular displacement achievable at a joint, measured in **degrees (°)**. Healthy adult values are well-tabulated and form the backbone of upper-limb and lower-limb robotic therapy protocols.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Normative Values Matter in Robotic Rehabilitation

A rehabilitation robot operates in a **closed perception–decision–action loop**. Normative values feed into the *perception* and *decision* stages:

1. **Patient Assessment Phase**
   - The robot measures the patient's current ROM, strength, and coordination.
   - These measurements are normalized: $z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$
   - The z-score tells the clinician *how many standard deviations* the patient lies from the healthy population mean.

2. **Goal-Setting Phase**
   - Target trajectories are generated as fractions of normative data (e.g., 70% of normal shoulder flexion).
   - This prevents under-therapy and over-therapy.

3. **Adaptive Control Phase**
   - Assist-as-needed (AAN) controllers use the gap between actual and normative values to modulate robotic force fields.
   - The smaller the gap, the less assistance; the larger the gap, the more guidance.

4. **Outcome Evaluation Phase**
   - Post-therapy, the patient's scores are re-normalized and compared to published minimal clinically important difference (MCID) thresholds.

### 2.2 Sources of Normative Data

Reliable normative data is generated through:

- **Laboratory goniometry** — manual and digital ROM measurement
- **3D Motion Capture Systems** — Vicon, OptiTrack, Qualisys
- **Force Plates** — Kistler, AMTI
- **Dynamometers** — Biodex, Cybex for isokinetic strength
- **EMG Systems** — Delsys, Noraxon
- **Wearable IMUs** — Xsens, APDM
- **Large-scale epidemiological studies** — e.g., NHANES, Framingham studies

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$ | Z-score normalization of patient value $x_i$ against population mean $\mu_i$ and SD $\sigma_i$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\% \text{Deficit} = \frac{\mu_i - x_i}{\mu_i} \times 100$ | Percent deviation of patient from healthy norm | % |
| 3 | $\text{Normalized ROM} = \frac{\text{Measured ROM}}{\text{Normative ROM}_{\max}}$ | Scales patient's motion to a [0, 1] workspace for robot control | unitless |
| 4 | $\text{Assist Level} = K_p \cdot (\theta_{norm} - \theta_{patient})$ | AAN proportional controller output torque | N·m |
| 5 | $\text{MCID} = \Delta x_{min}$ | Minimum change that constitutes a clinically meaningful improvement | varies |
| 6 | $v_{norm} = \frac{L_{stride}}{t_{stride}}$ | Normal gait speed from stride length and time | m/s |
| 7 | $\text{Cadence}_{norm} \approx 110 \text{–} 120$ steps/min | Typical healthy adult cadence | steps/min |
| 8 | $\text{Stride Length}_{norm} \approx 1.41 \cdot \text{height}$ | Approximate proportionality to stature | m |
| 9 | $\omega = \frac{\theta}{t}$ | Joint angular velocity used in ROM testing | rad/s or °/s |
| 10 | $\tau = I \alpha$ | Joint torque from moment of inertia and angular acceleration (kinetic norm) | N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the *mathematical DNA* of every assist-as-needed controller, impedance controller, and progress-tracking dashboard used in clinical rehab robotics. Memorize the z-score, percent-deficit, and normalized ROM equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical question.

### 2.4 Typical Normative ROM Values (Adult, Healthy)

| Joint | Movement | Normative ROM (°) |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| Shoulder | Flexion | 0 – 180 |
| Shoulder | Abduction | 0 – 180 |
| Shoulder | External Rotation | 0 – 90 |
| Elbow | Flexion | 0 – 150 |
| Forearm | Pronation/Supination | 0 – 80 / 0 – 80 |
| Wrist | Flexion / Extension | 0 – 80 / 0 – 70 |
| Hip | Flexion | 0 – 120 |
| Hip | Abduction | 0 – 45 |
| Knee | Flexion | 0 – 135 |
| Ankle | Dorsiflexion / Plantarflexion | 0 – 20 / 0 – 50 |

> [!NOTE]
> Values are **age-dependent** and **gender-dependent**. Pediatric and geriatric norms differ significantly. Always cite the reference population when using these in a KTU answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Z-Score Normalization for Robotic Assessment

The z-score is the *canonical* normalization method in rehabilitation robotics. Here is the exhaustive derivation of how a patient's measured value is converted into a comparable, dimensionless index.

**Step 1 — State the Clinical Measurement**
Let $x_i$ be the patient's measured value for a given parameter (e.g., knee flexion ROM in degrees).

**Step 2 — State the Population Reference**
Let the healthy reference population be characterized by a mean $\mu_i$ and a standard deviation $\sigma_i$ (both obtained from a normative database).

**Step 3 — Express the Deviation from the Mean**
The raw deviation of the patient from the healthy mean is:

$$
\Delta x_i = x_i - \mu_i
$$

This quantity has the same units as $x_i$ (e.g., degrees) and is therefore *not directly comparable* across parameters with different units.

**Step 4 — Normalize by the Standard Deviation**
To obtain a unit-less, comparable metric, divide the deviation by the population standard deviation:

$$
z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Z-Score**

- $z_i = 0$ → patient is exactly at the population mean (healthy).
- $z_i = -1$ → patient lies 1 SD below the mean (mild deficit).
- $z_i = -2$ → patient lies 2 SD below the mean (moderate deficit).
- $z_i \leq -3$ → severe impairment (clinical alarm threshold).

**Step 6 — Convert Z-Score to Percent Deficit (Engineering Form)**

$$
\% \text{Deficit} = \frac{\mu_i - x_i}{\mu_i} \times 100
$$

This is more intuitive for clinicians and forms the basis of robotic therapy progress charts.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 55-year-old male post-stroke patient achieves a shoulder flexion ROM of 95°. The age-matched healthy normative mean is $\mu = 165°$ with $\sigma = 12°$. Compute the z-score and the percent deficit. Comment on the severity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values**

$$
x_i = 95°, \quad \mu_i = 165°, \quad \sigma_i = 12°
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Z-Score**

$$
z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i} = \frac{95 - 165}{12} = \frac{-70}{12}
$$

$$
\boxed{z_i \approx -5.83}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Percent Deficit**

$$
\% \text{Deficit} = \frac{\mu_i - x_i}{\mu_i} \times 100 = \frac{165 - 95}{165} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{70}{165} \times 100 \approx 42.42\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The patient lies **5.83 standard deviations below** the healthy mean, with a **42.42% deficit** in shoulder flexion. This represents *severe* impairment. A robotic therapy protocol should:

- Set the initial ROM target at 30–40% of normative (≈ 50–65°).
- Use a high assist-as-needed gain $K_p$.
- Schedule frequent reassessments (e.g., weekly z-score updates).

### 3.3 Derivation: Normalized Gait Speed

**Step 1 — Define Stride Length $L_s$** as the distance between two successive heel strikes of the same foot.

**Step 2 — Define Stride Time $T_s$** as the duration of one complete stride cycle.

**Step 3 — Gait Speed is their ratio:**

$$
v = \frac{L_s}{T_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Healthy Norms.** For adults: $L_s \approx 1.41 \cdot h$ (where $h$ is body height) and $T_s \approx 1.0$ s. Thus:

$$
v_{norm} \approx 1.41 \cdot h \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

For $h = 1.70$ m:

$$
v_{norm} \approx 1.41 \times 1.70 \approx 2.40 \text{ m/s} \quad (\approx 8.6 \text{ km/h})
$$

**Step 5 — Express Patient's Deviation:**

$$
z_{v} = \frac{v_{patient} - v_{norm}}{\sigma_v}
$$

A patient post-stroke typically walks at 0.3–0.6 m/s, yielding $z_v \approx -3$ to $-5$, again indicating severe gait pathology.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Normative Data Flow in a Rehabilitation Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Healthy Population Database] --> B[Compute Mean mu and SD sigma]
    B --> C[Normative Reference Vector N]
    D[Patient Sensor Array] --> E[IMU Goniometer EMG Force]
    E --> F[Patient Measurement Vector P]
    F --> G[Normalization Module]
    C --> G
    G --> H[Z-score and Percent Deficit]
    H --> I[Clinical Decision Module]
    I --> J[Assist-as-Needed Controller]
    J --> K[Robot Actuator Output Torque tau]
    K --> L[Patient performs therapy task]
    L --> M[Re-measure and update P]
    M --> F
    H --> N[Progress Dashboard for Clinician]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Normative-Based Rehab System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PERCEPTION
    P1[Goniometers]
    P2[IMU Sensors]
    P3[EMG Electrodes]
    P4[Force Plates]
    end

    subgraph NORMATIVE_ENGINE
    N1[Population Database]
    N2[Statistical Processor mu sigma]
    N3[Deficit Calculator]
    N4[Trajectory Generator]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL
    C1[Assist-as-Needed AAN]
    C2[Impedance Controller]
    C3[Safety Limiter]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION
    A1[Robot Joint 1]
    A2[Robot Joint 2]
    A3[Robot Joint N]
    end

    P1 --> N3
    P2 --> N3
    P3 --> N3
    P4 --> N3
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> C1
    N4 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> A1
    C3 --> A2
    C3 --> A3
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: From Norm to Therapy Intensity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normative Value mu] --> B[Patient Value x]
    B --> C{Gap greater than 2 SD?}
    C -->|Yes| D[High Assistance Mode]
    C -->|No| E[Moderate Assistance Mode]
    D --> F[Robot delivers high Kp gain]
    E --> G[Robot delivers low Kp gain]
    F --> H[Reassess Weekly]
    G --> H
    H --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define normative values. Why are they essential in robotic rehabilitation?*

**Model Answer:**
Normative values are statistically derived reference data obtained from healthy populations, representing the typical range of biomechanical, physiological, and functional parameters such as joint ROM, muscle strength, gait speed, and EMG activity. They are essential in robotic rehabilitation because they:

- Provide a quantitative benchmark to assess the severity of a patient's impairment (via z-score and percent deficit).
- Enable personalized goal-setting for therapy.
- Drive adaptive control strategies such as assist-as-needed (AAN) algorithms.
- Allow objective tracking of patient recovery progress.
- Validate the clinical efficacy of the rehabilitation robot.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing/justifying importance)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any six categories of normative data used in rehabilitation robotics and give one example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | Kinematic | Shoulder flexion ROM (norm 0–180°) |
| 2 | Kinetic | Knee extension peak torque (norm ≈ 1.5–2.5 N·m/kg) |
| 3 | EMG | Biceps RMS amplitude during elbow flexion |
| 4 | Functional | Timed Up-and-Go (TUG) test (norm < 10 s for healthy adults) |
| 5 | Anthropometric | Body segment mass ratios (e.g., thigh ≈ 10% body mass) |
| 6 | Gait | Stride length (norm ≈ 1.41 × height) |

(3 marks: 0.5 mark per correct category-example pair)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]*

**(a)** Explain with neat block diagrams the role of normative data in the closed-loop architecture of a robotic rehabilitation system. Describe how patient measurements are normalized and fed back to the controller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A post-stroke patient has a measured knee flexion ROM of 65°. The age-matched normative mean is 130° with a standard deviation of 10°. Compute the z-score and percent deficit. Based on your result, recommend an initial therapy strategy for an AAN-controlled knee rehabilitation robot. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The closed-loop architecture of a normative-based rehabilitation robot consists of four interlinked stages:

**Stage 1 — Perception Layer (Sensors):**
Goniometers, IMUs, EMG electrodes, and force plates capture the patient's joint angles, velocities, muscle activity, and interaction forces in real time.

**Stage 2 — Normative Engine (Reference Comparison):**
The measured values are compared against a normative database. The mean $\mu_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$ for the relevant parameter (matched for age, sex, and anthropometry) are retrieved. The z-score is computed as:

$$
z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}
$$

The percent deficit is computed as:

$$
\% \text{Deficit} = \frac{\mu_i - x_i}{\mu_i} \times 100
$$

**Stage 3 — Decision Layer (Clinical Logic):**
Based on the magnitude of the deficit, the system selects a therapy intensity. Severe deficits ($\vert z_i \vert \geq 2$) trigger high-assistance modes; mild deficits allow resistance or perturbational training.

**Stage 4 — Action Layer (Robot Actuators):**
The controller (typically AAN or impedance-based) computes the required assistive torque and commands the actuators. The interaction force is fed back, closing the loop.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2):**
The functional flow is:

> Sensors → Patient Vector → Normalization Engine → Normative Engine → Decision Module → Controller → Actuators → Patient → Sensors.

(7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 3 for explanation, 2 for normalization formulas)

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**

$$
x_i = 65°, \quad \mu_i = 130°, \quad \sigma_i = 10°
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Z-Score:**

$$
z_i = \frac{65 - 130}{10} = \frac{-65}{10} = -6.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Percent Deficit:**

$$
\% \text{Deficit} = \frac{130 - 65}{130} \times 100 = \frac{65}{130} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The patient lies **6.5 standard deviations below** the healthy mean, with a **50% deficit** in knee flexion ROM. This indicates *severe* functional impairment.

**Step 4 — Therapy Recommendations for AAN-Controlled Robot:**

- Set the initial ROM target at **40% of normative** (i.e., ≈ 52°).
- Use a **high proportional gain** $K_p$ to provide substantial guidance.
- Limit peak robotic torque to a **safety ceiling** (e.g., 20 N·m).
- Schedule **weekly z-score reassessments** to track progress.
- Gradually reduce $K_p$ as the patient's z-score approaches $-1$.

(7 marks: 2 for z-score, 2 for percent deficit, 3 for therapy recommendations)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the major categories of normative data used in robotic rehabilitation. Provide at least three examples of normative values for upper-limb and lower-limb joints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the z-score normalization formula and explain how the resulting index is used to grade impairment severity in clinical decision-making. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline for Q.B:**

- Part (a): Enumerate kinematic, kinetic, EMG, functional, anthropometric, and strength categories. Provide normative ROM tables for shoulder, elbow, hip, knee, and ankle (as in Section 2.4).

- Part (b): Full algebraic derivation as in Section 3.1, plus interpretation table:

| Z-Score Range | Severity |
|---------------|----------|
| $-1 \leq z \leq +1$ | Within normal limits |
| $-2 \leq z < -1$ | Mild deficit |
| $-3 \leq z < -2$ | Moderate deficit |
| $z < -3$ | Severe deficit |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do not** confuse the z-score sign convention. A *negative* z-score always means *below* the healthy mean, which in rehab almost always indicates impairment.
> - **Do not** forget units in percent deficit calculation — write the answer as a percentage.
> - **Do not** state normative values without specifying the **reference population** (age, sex, side).
> - **Do not** use bare pipe `|` for absolute value in markdown tables — use `\vert` (e.g., write $\vert z_i \vert \geq 2$).
> - In 14-mark answers, always include a **block diagram**; missing it costs at least 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Normative values** are healthy-population reference data used as benchmarks in rehab robotics.
- The **z-score** $z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$ is the universal normalization metric.
- **Percent deficit** $= \frac{\mu_i - x_i}{\mu_i} \times 100$ expresses impairment intuitively.
- **Six major categories**: Kinematic, Kinetic, EMG, Functional, Anthropometric, Strength.
- **Healthy adult ROM values** must be memorized: shoulder 0–180°, elbow 0–150°, hip 0–120°, knee 0–135°, ankle dorsiflexion 0–20° / plantarflexion 0–50°.
- **Gait norms**: speed ≈ 1.41 × height, cadence ≈ 110–120 steps/min.
- **AAN controllers** use the normative-vs-patient gap to modulate robotic assistance via $\tau = K_p (\theta_{norm} - \theta_{patient})$.
- **Z-score interpretation**: $|z| < 1$ = normal, $1 \leq |z| < 2$ = mild, $2 \leq |z| < 3$ = moderate, $|z| \geq 3$ = severe.
- Always **age-match, sex-match, and side-match** the normative reference to the patient.
- The **closed perception–decision–action loop** is incomplete without the normative engine layer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Static Stability** in the context of robotic rehabilitation and legged locomotion is formally defined as the ability of a robotic system (or a bipedal/humanoid assistive device such as an exoskeleton, prosthetic limb, or smart walker) to maintain its equilibrium while in a *quasi-static* state — i.e., standing still, or moving with negligible accelerations such that inertial effects can be ignored in the force/moment balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** *A robotic system is statically stable if, and only if, the vertical projection of its overall Center of Mass (CoM) on the ground plane lies strictly within the convex hull of its support polygon (the contact area between the robot and the ground).*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Projection of CoM on Ground} \in \text{Support Polygon}$$

When this condition is violated — i.e., the CoM projection falls *outside* the support polygon — the system generates an unbalanced gravitational moment about the edge of the support, and a **tipping-over instability** occurs.

## 1.2 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol / Unit | Meaning |
|------|--------------|---------|
| Center of Mass | CoM (m) | Point where the total body mass is effectively concentrated. |
| Center of Gravity | CoG (m) | Point where gravitational force effectively acts (coincident with CoM in uniform gravity). |
| Support Polygon | $P_s$ (m²) | Convex hull of all ground-contact points/areas. |
| Zero Moment Point | ZMP (m) | Point on the ground where the net moment of active forces = 0. |
| Stability Margin | $d_{sm}$ (m) | Shortest distance from CoM projection to the support polygon edge. |
| Energy Stability Margin | $E_{sm}$ (J) | Minimum potential energy required to topple the system. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sumo Stance":**
> Imagine a Sumo wrestler standing in his wide, crouched starting stance. Why doesn't he fall over? Because his **belly-button (Center of Mass)** is positioned directly over a wide patch of ground between his two feet — a large *support polygon*. Now imagine the same wrestler trying to balance on a single foot on a tightrope. His CoM projection is no longer safely over the support — he will tip and fall. The **wider the support** and the **lower the CoM**, the *more statically stable* the system is. **Rehabilitation robots use exactly this principle** to decide whether a paraplegic patient using an exoskeleton can safely stand or take a step.

**Why this matters in rehabilitation robotics:**
- **Exoskeletons (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT):** Must guarantee the patient doesn't fall during standing or slow walking.
- **Powered Prosthetic Legs:** Must compute safe gait patterns where the CoM stays over the remaining foot.
- **Smart Walkers / Rollators:** Assess patient balance in real-time.
- **Gait Trainers (Lokomat, etc.):** Provide partial body-weight support with controlled stability margins.

## 1.4 Why "Static" and not "Dynamic"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static vs. Dynamic Stability (KTU Distinction):**
> - **Static Stability** applies when the robot is *stationary or moving so slowly* that inertial forces ($ma$, $I\alpha$) are negligible compared to gravitational force ($mg$). It is the **conservative** stability criterion.
> - **Dynamic Stability** is required during fast motion (running, recovering from a push), where inertial and Coriolis effects dominate and the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** must equal a target point within the support polygon.

For Module 2 (Static Stability), we treat all motion as quasi-static, so the analysis reduces to a pure **geometric CoM-projection problem**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Static Stability

Static stability of a robotic system is governed by **two simultaneously satisfied conditions**:

### Pillar 1 — Geometric (Projection) Condition
The vertical projection of the CoM onto the ground plane must fall **inside** the convex hull of all contact points. This is the *necessary* condition for static equilibrium.

### Pillar 2 — Force/Moment (ZMP) Condition
The resultant ground reaction force must pass through a point within the support polygon, and the net moment about that point must be zero. This is the *sufficient* kinematic-dynamic condition.

## 2.2 The Support Polygon — Detailed Construction

The **support polygon** $P_s$ is the smallest convex polygon (in 2-D ground plane) enclosing all contact regions between the robot and the ground.

**Step-by-step construction logic:**
- Step 1: Identify every foot/contact point in contact with the ground.
- Step 2: For each rectangular/circular foot, take the 2-D convex hull of its contact patch.
- Step 3: Compute the **union** of all such contact regions.
- Step 4: Compute the **convex hull** of the union → this is $P_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** For a biped in *Double Support Phase (DSP)* (both feet on ground), $P_s$ is the convex hull joining the outermost edges of both feet — typically a quadrilateral. For *Single Support Phase (SSP)* (one foot lifted), $P_s$ degenerates to the contact patch of the single stance foot.

## 2.3 Stability Margin (Geometric)

The **Shortest Distance Stability Margin** $d_{sm}$ is the minimum perpendicular distance from the CoM's vertical projection $\mathbf{c} = (c_x, c_y)$ to any edge of the support polygon $P_s$.

For an $n$-sided convex polygon with edges described by $\mathbf{a}_i \cdot \mathbf{p} = b_i$ (where $\mathbf{a}_i$ is the outward unit normal), the margin is:

$$d_{sm} = \min_{i=1,\dots,n} \left( b_i - \mathbf{a}_i \cdot \mathbf{c} \right)$$

A system is statically stable if, and only if, $d_{sm} > 0$.

## 2.4 Energy Stability Margin (ESM)

Proposed by **Messuri and Klein (1985)**, the ESM measures the *minimum potential energy change* required to rotate the robot about the tipping edge until it reaches an unstable equilibrium (where CoM projection lies on the edge).

If the robot has mass $M$, CoM height $h$ from the ground, and $d_{sm}$ is the shortest distance to a tipping edge, then:

$$E_{sm} = M \cdot g \cdot h \cdot d_{sm}$$

where $g \approx \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ is the gravitational acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ESM is preferred over pure geometric margin in rehabilitation robotics:** The geometric margin ignores the CoM *height* — a tall, narrow robot can have the same $d_{sm}$ as a short, wide one, but is *much* easier to topple because of the longer moment arm. ESM naturally incorporates both height and mass distribution.

## 2.5 Force-Angle Stability Margin

A combined metric proposed by **Yoshikawa (1990)**, often used in force-controlled manipulation and rehabilitation:

$$\text{FA} = \min_i \left( \frac{\vert f_i \vert}{\vert f \vert} \cdot \frac{\theta_i}{\pi/2} \right)$$

where $f_i$ is the contact force on the $i$-th edge and $\theta_i$ is the angle between the resultant force and the surface normal.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for this topic. Memorize the boxed entries with a ⚠ marker.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | CoM of Multi-body System ⚠ | $\mathbf{c} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | m | $\mathbf{r}_i$ = position of $i$-th link CoM |
| 2 | Static Stability Condition ⚠ | $\mathbf{c}_{xy} \in P_s$ | — | Projection of CoM inside support polygon |
| 3 | Stability Margin ⚠ | $d_{sm} = \min_i (b_i - \mathbf{a}_i \cdot \mathbf{c})$ | m | Shortest distance to polygon edge |
| 4 | Energy Stability Margin ⚠ | $E_{sm} = M g h \, d_{sm}$ | J (Joules) | Includes height effect |
| 5 | ZMP Coordinates ⚠ | $x_{zmp} = \dfrac{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - \sum_i m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i}{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$ | m | Static case: $\ddot{x}_i = \ddot{z}_i = 0 \Rightarrow x_{zmp} = x_{com}$ |
| 6 | Static ZMP Simplification ⚠ | $x_{zmp} = x_{com}, \quad y_{zmp} = y_{com}$ | m | In static case, ZMP = CoM projection |
| 7 | Support Polygon Area | $A_s = \frac{1}{2} \vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \vert$ | m² | Shoelace formula |
| 8 | Tipping Moment | $M_{tip} = M g \, d_{sm}$ | N·m | About the tipping edge |
| 9 | Restoring Moment | $M_{res} = M g \, d_{foot}$ | N·m | $d_{foot}$ = distance from CoM to foot center |
| 10 | Stability Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{d_{sm}}{h}$ | dimensionless | Height-normalized margin |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU examiners love this topic:** Static stability is the *gatekeeper* of safe operation in:
> 1. **Rehabilitation Exoskeletons** — fall prevention algorithms.
> 2. **Bipedal Assistive Robots** — deciding when to lift the swing foot.
> 3. **Wheelchair-to-Standing Transfers** — ensuring CoM stays over the wheelchair base.
> 4. **Lower-limb Prosthetics** — designing socket geometry for amputee balance.
> 5. **Pediatric Gait Trainers** — maintaining balance in cerebral palsy patients.
> 6. **Fall-Detection Wearables** — IMU-based CoM estimation for elderly monitoring.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Center of Mass of a Multi-Link Rehabilitation Robot

Consider a rehabilitation exoskeleton (or humanoid assistive robot) with $N$ rigid links. Link $i$ has mass $m_i$, and its individual CoM is located at $\mathbf{r}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in the world frame.

**Step 1 — Definition of the system's overall CoM.**
The CoM of the entire system is the *mass-weighted average* of all link positions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \, \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i} \\
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \, (x_i \hat{\imath} + y_i \hat{\jmath} + z_i \hat{k})}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M = \sum m_i$ is the total mass of the system.

**Step 2 — Component-wise expansion.**
For the $x$-component (and analogously for $y$ and $z$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_x &= \frac{m_1 x_1 + m_2 x_2 + \cdots + m_N x_N}{M} \\
C_y &= \frac{m_1 y_1 + m_2 y_2 + \cdots + m_N y_N}{M} \\
C_z &= \frac{m_1 z_1 + m_2 z_2 + \cdots + m_N z_N}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CoM projection on the ground plane.**
For static stability, we need the projection on the ground ($z = 0$ plane):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{c}_{xy} &= (C_x, C_y) \\
\text{where } \quad C_x &= \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{M}, \quad C_y = \frac{\sum m_i y_i}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

This projected point $\mathbf{c}_{xy}$ is the *only* geometric quantity that determines static stability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stability Margin for a Rectangular Foot Support

**Setup:** A bipedal robot in Single Support Phase (SSP) with one rectangular foot of width $w$ (in the $x$-direction) and depth $d$ (in the $y$-direction), centered at the origin. The support polygon is therefore the rectangle with vertices $(\pm w/2, \pm d/2)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the four supporting edges.**
The four edges of the rectangle have outward unit normals:
- $\mathbf{a}_1 = (1, 0)$, constraint $b_1 = w/2$ (right edge)
- $\mathbf{a}_2 = (-1, 0)$, constraint $b_2 = w/2$ (left edge)
- $\mathbf{a}_3 = (0, 1)$, constraint $b_3 = d/2$ (front edge)
- $\mathbf{a}_4 = (0, -1)$, constraint $b_4 = d/2$ (back edge)

**Step 2 — Express the signed distance to each edge.**
For a CoM projection $\mathbf{c} = (c_x, c_y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= b_1 - \mathbf{a}_1 \cdot \mathbf{c} = \tfrac{w}{2} - c_x \\
d_2 &= b_2 - \mathbf{a}_2 \cdot \mathbf{c} = \tfrac{w}{2} + c_x \\
d_3 &= b_3 - \mathbf{a}_3 \cdot \mathbf{c} = \tfrac{d}{2} - c_y \\
d_4 &= b_4 - \mathbf{a}_4 \cdot \mathbf{c} = \tfrac{d}{2} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the minimum.**
The stability margin is the minimum of these four values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{sm} &= \min(d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4) \\
       &= \min\left(\tfrac{w}{2} - c_x,\; \tfrac{w}{2} + c_x,\; \tfrac{d}{2} - c_y,\; \tfrac{d}{2} + c_y\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Stability condition.**
The robot is statically stable if and only if $d_{sm} > 0$, which is equivalent to all four partial distances being positive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-c_x &< \tfrac{w}{2} < c_x \quad \text{(in the x-direction)}\\
-c_y &< \tfrac{d}{2} < c_y \quad \text{(in the y-direction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 2-DOF lower-limb rehabilitation robot stands on a single foot with $w = 0.20$ m, $d = 0.28$ m. If the CoM projection is at $\mathbf{c} = (0.04, 0.06)$ m, the stability margin is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 0.10 - 0.04 = 0.06 \text{ m} \\
d_2 &= 0.10 + 0.04 = 0.14 \text{ m} \\
d_3 &= 0.14 - 0.06 = 0.08 \text{ m} \\
d_4 &= 0.14 + 0.06 = 0.20 \text{ m} \\
\therefore \; d_{sm} &= \min(0.06, 0.14, 0.08, 0.20) = \mathbf{0.06 \; m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $d_{sm} > 0$, the robot is **statically stable**, and the *next tipping event* will occur about the **right edge** of the foot.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Stability Margin for a Humanoid Standing Robot

**Setup:** A humanoid rehabilitation robot of total mass $M = 65$ kg, CoM height $h = 0.95$ m above the ground, with CoM projection located at horizontal distance $d_{sm} = 0.04$ m from the nearest support edge.

**Step 1 — Identify the potential energy at the current state.**
The gravitational potential energy of the CoM is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{current}} &= M g h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the potential energy at the tipping threshold.**
The system becomes unstable when the CoM projection crosses the support edge. At this moment, the CoM is still at height $h$, but the rotation about the edge begins. For a *pure rotation* about the edge by a small angle $\theta$, the CoM rises to a new height:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(\theta) &= \sqrt{h^2 + d_{sm}^2} \cdot \cos\left(\theta - \arctan\!\frac{d_{sm}}{h}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum height of CoM during the rotation.**
Setting $\dfrac{dh}{d\theta} = 0$ gives the maximum CoM height, which is $\sqrt{h^2 + d_{sm}^2}$. This occurs when the CoM is directly above the tipping edge.

**Step 4 — The Energy Stability Margin.**
The minimum energy needed to push the robot over is the difference in potential energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sm} &= U_{\text{max}} - U_{\text{current}} \\
       &= M g \left(\sqrt{h^2 + d_{sm}^2} - h\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Small-angle approximation (the form used in KTU problems).**
When $d_{sm} \ll h$, we use $\sqrt{h^2 + d_{sm}^2} \approx h + \dfrac{d_{sm}^2}{2h}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sm} &\approx M g \left(h + \dfrac{d_{sm}^2}{2h} - h\right) \\
       &= \boxed{\dfrac{1}{2} \, M g \, \dfrac{d_{sm}^2}{h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The simplified $E_{sm} = M g h \, d_{sm}$ form is the *first-order* form. The form derived above, $E_{sm} = \frac{1}{2} M g d_{sm}^2 / h$, is the *more accurate* form. Both are acceptable in exams; use the one whose assumptions match the problem statement.

**Numerical Example:** For $M = 65$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h = 0.95$ m, $d_{sm} = 0.04$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sm} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 65 \times 9.81 \times \frac{(0.04)^2}{0.95} \\
       &= \frac{1}{2} \times 65 \times 9.81 \times 0.001684 \\
       &\approx \mathbf{0.537 \; J}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **minimum energy** that a disturbance (push, stumble, or motor error) must deliver to topple the robot — a key safety threshold in rehabilitation exoskeleton design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Static ZMP Equals CoM Projection (Important KTU Result)

**Step 1 — General ZMP definition.**
The Zero Moment Point $(x_{zmp}, y_{zmp})$ is defined as the point on the ground where the net moment of all active forces (gravity + inertial) about the horizontal axes is zero.

The full dynamic form (Vukobratović, 1972) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{zmp} &= \frac{\sum_i m_i \left[ (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - \ddot{x}_i z_i \right]}{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)} \\
y_{zmp} &= \frac{\sum_i m_i \left[ (\ddot{z}_i + g) y_i - \ddot{y}_i z_i \right]}{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the static assumption.**
In the *static* (quasi-static) case, all linear accelerations vanish: $\ddot{x}_i = \ddot{y}_i = \ddot{z}_i = 0$.

The numerator simplifies drastically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{zmp} &= \frac{\sum_i m_i \, g \, x_i}{\sum_i m_i \, g} = \frac{\sum_i m_i x_i}{M} = C_x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final result.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{x_{zmp} = C_x, \qquad y_{zmp} = C_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam-Ready Statement:** *"In the static (quasi-static) case, the Zero Moment Point coincides with the vertical projection of the Center of Mass onto the ground plane. The static stability condition is therefore equivalently stated as: the ZMP must lie within the support polygon."*

This is one of the most important takeaways of the entire module and is a frequent KTU 14-marker.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Limb Stability Synthesis Table

The following table consolidates the analytical results for quick revision:

| Configuration | Support Polygon Type | Typical $d_{sm}$ Range | Key Risk Edge | ESM Dominant Term |
|---------------|----------------------|-------------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Biped standing (DSP) | Quadrilateral | 0.04–0.08 m | Outer foot edge | $M g h \, d_{sm}$ |
| Biped walking (SSP) | Single rectangle | 0.02–0.05 m | Front or back toe | $M g h \, d_{sm}$ |
| Quadruped standing | Wide rectangle | 0.08–0.15 m | Any of 4 outer edges | $M g h \, d_{sm}$ |
| Exoskeleton standing | Parallelogram (both feet) | 0.05–0.10 m | Lateral outer edges | $M g h \, d_{sm}$ |
| Wheelchair transfer | Triangular (3 wheels) | 0.10–0.20 m | Rear caster edge | $M g h \, d_{sm}$ |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Static Stability Evaluation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the full data-flow pipeline for evaluating static stability in a real-time rehabilitation robot controller. The architecture is the **industry-standard flow** used in exoskeletons such as ReWalk, Ekso, and BLEEX.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IMU Sensors on Trunk and Limbs] --> B[Sensor Fusion and Filtering]
    C[Joint Encoders and Force Torque Sensors at Feet] --> B
    B --> D[Forward Kinematics Engine]
    D --> E[Compute Multi Link CoM Position]
    E --> F[Project CoM onto Ground Plane]
    F --> G{Query Foot Contact States}
    G --> H[Construct Convex Support Polygon]
    H --> I[Point in Polygon Test]
    I --> J{ZMP lies inside Polygon}
    J --> K[Stable: Continue Operation]
    J --> L[Unstable: Trigger Safety Protocol]
    L --> M[Engage Fall Arrest and Alarm]
    I --> N[Compute Shortest Distance Stability Margin]
    N --> O[Compute Energy Stability Margin]
    O --> P[Adjust Gait Parameters or Pause Motion]
    K --> Q[Update Controller Loop and Log Telemetry]
    M --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Support Polygon Construction Subsystem

This diagram shows the *interior* data flow inside the support polygon generator:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Left Foot Contact Patch] --> U[Union of All Contact Regions]
    F2[Right Foot Contact Patch] --> U
    F3[Additional Contacts e.g. Crutches] --> U
    U --> CH[Convex Hull Algorithm]
    CH --> SP[Support Polygon Vertices and Edges]
    SP --> SM[Stability Margin Module]
    SP --> SA[Polygon Area Calculator]
    SM --> OUT[Stability Report]
    SA --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Static Stability Knowledge Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SS[Static Stability] --> COM[Center of Mass Computation]
    SS --> SP[Support Polygon Definition]
    SS --> ZMP[Zero Moment Point Coincidence]
    COM --> CI[CoM Projection on Ground]
    SP --> POLY[Convex Hull of Contact Points]
    CI --> COND{Projection inside Polygon}
    POLY --> COND
    COND --> YES[Statically Stable]
    COND --> NO[Toppling Instability]
    ZMP --> COND
    YES --> SMARGIN[Stability Margin]
    YES --> ESM[Energy Stability Margin]
    YES --> FA[Force Angle Margin]
    SMARGIN --> SAFEDESIGN[Safety Threshold for Rehab Robot Controller]
    ESM --> SAFEDESIGN
    FA --> SAFEDESIGN
```

## 4.4 Geometric Schematic — CoM Projection vs. Support Polygon

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic (ASCII Representation for Notebook Reproduction):**
> ```
>                CoM (in 3D space)
>                  ●
>                  │
>                  │ vertical line
>                  │
>                  ▼ projection
>                  ●  ← CoM projection
>              ╔═══════════╗
>             ║   P_s      ║  ← Support Polygon
>              ╚═══════════╝
>                       ↑
>                  Tipping Edge
> ```
> The arrow $\leftarrow d_{sm} \rightarrow$ is the *Shortest Distance Stability Margin*. The robot tips when this distance reaches zero.


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define static stability of a legged/walking robot. State the necessary and sufficient condition. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Static stability is the property of a robotic system to maintain its equilibrium while in a stationary or quasi-static state, i.e., when inertial forces are negligible.

- **Necessary Condition:** The vertical projection of the Center of Mass (CoM) on the ground plane must lie within the support polygon.
- **Sufficient Condition:** The Zero Moment Point (ZMP) computed from the force/moment balance must lie within the support polygon.

In the static case, the ZMP coincides with the CoM projection, so both criteria are equivalent.

---

### Q2. What is the Energy Stability Margin? Why is it preferred over the geometric stability margin in rehabilitation robotics? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Energy Stability Margin (ESM)** is the minimum potential energy that must be supplied to the system to cause it to topple about the nearest support edge.

$$
E_{sm} \approx M g h \, d_{sm}
$$

It is preferred over the geometric margin $d_{sm}$ because it accounts for **both** the *horizontal* distance to the tipping edge and the *vertical* height of the CoM. A tall robot with the same $d_{sm}$ is more easily toppled, and ESM captures this directly, making it ideal for fall-risk assessment in exoskeletons and prosthetic devices.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B Module-2 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each choice has sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both sub-parts must be answered for full credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 2]`**

**A (a) [7 marks]** Define the Center of Mass and Support Polygon for a multi-link walking robot. Derive the equations for the CoM of an $N$-link system and explain how the static stability condition is obtained from these.

**A (b) [7 marks]** A bipedal rehabilitation robot has the following link parameters (in the world frame):

| Link | Mass (kg) | CoM Position (m) |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| Trunk | 30 | (0.00, 0.00, 0.95) |
| Left Thigh | 8 | (0.00, 0.05, 0.55) |
| Right Thigh | 8 | (0.00, −0.05, 0.55) |
| Left Shank | 4 | (0.00, 0.05, 0.20) |
| Right Shank | 4 | (0.00, −0.05, 0.20) |

The robot stands on a single left foot whose contact patch is the rectangle $x \in [-0.10, +0.10]$ m, $y \in [-0.05, +0.15]$ m. Compute (i) the overall CoM, (ii) its ground projection, and (iii) the geometric stability margin. State whether the robot is statically stable.

#### Model Solution

**A (a) — Model Answer [7 marks]:**

**Definition of CoM (1 mark):**
The Center of Mass of a multi-link system is the point where the total mass of the system can be assumed to be concentrated for translational dynamics.

**Definition of Support Polygon (1 mark):**
The support polygon is the convex hull of all ground-contact points of the robot in the ground plane.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
For an $N$-link robot, the CoM is defined as the mass-weighted average of the individual link CoMs. Let link $i$ have mass $m_i$ and CoM position $\mathbf{r}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i} = \frac{\sum m_i x_i \hat{\imath} + \sum m_i y_i \hat{\jmath} + \sum m_i z_i \hat{k}}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M = \sum m_i$.

The static stability condition is obtained by projecting $\mathbf{C}$ vertically onto the ground ($z = 0$ plane), giving $\mathbf{c}_{xy} = (C_x, C_y)$. The robot is statically stable if and only if $\mathbf{c}_{xy}$ lies inside the support polygon $P_s$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This purely geometric condition ensures that the gravitational moment about every support edge is restoring, not tipping.

---

**A (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Total mass (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 30 + 8 + 8 + 4 + 4 = \mathbf{54 \; kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CoM components (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_x &= \frac{30(0) + 8(0) + 8(0) + 4(0) + 4(0)}{54} = \mathbf{0 \; m} \\
C_y &= \frac{30(0) + 8(0.05) + 8(-0.05) + 4(0.05) + 4(-0.05)}{54} \\
    &= \frac{0 + 0.40 - 0.40 + 0.20 - 0.20}{54} = \mathbf{0 \; m} \\
C_z &= \frac{30(0.95) + 8(0.55) + 8(0.55) + 4(0.20) + 4(0.20)}{54} \\
    &= \frac{28.5 + 4.4 + 4.4 + 0.8 + 0.8}{54} = \frac{38.9}{54} \approx \mathbf{0.720 \; m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ground projection (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{c}_{xy} &= (C_x, C_y) = \mathbf{(0, 0) \; m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Stability margin (1.5 marks):**
The support polygon is the rectangle with edges at $x = \pm 0.10$, $y = \pm 0.05$ and $+0.15$ (asymmetric in $y$). Since the CoM is at $(0, 0)$, the distances to each edge are:

- To $x = +0.10$: $0.10$ m
- To $x = -0.10$: $0.10$ m
- To $y = +0.15$: $0.15$ m
- To $y = -0.05$: $0.05$ m ← **minimum**

$$
\boxed{d_{sm} = 0.05 \; \text{m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (0.5 marks):** Since $d_{sm} = 0.05$ m $> 0$, the robot is **statically stable**, and the critical tipping edge is the **back edge of the left foot** (at $y = -0.05$ m).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 2 Alt Choice]`**

**B (a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of the Zero Moment Point (ZMP). Starting from the general dynamic ZMP equation, prove that in the static (quasi-static) case the ZMP coincides with the vertical projection of the Center of Mass. State the static stability criterion in terms of ZMP.

**B (b) [7 marks]** Compute the Energy Stability Margin (ESM) for the robot in Question A(b). Assume a patient mass is added to the trunk of $M_p = 60$ kg at the same height as the trunk CoM. Use the more accurate form $E_{sm} = \frac{1}{2} M g d_{sm}^2 / h$. State its engineering significance in fall-prevention controller design.

#### Model Solution

**B (a) — Model Answer [7 marks]:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is defined (Vukobratović, 1972) as the point on the ground (or any contact surface) where the net moment of the active forces (gravity + inertial) about the horizontal axes is zero. It is the point where the resultant ground reaction force effectively acts.

**General equation (2 marks):** For an $N$-body system, the ZMP coordinates are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{zmp} &= \frac{\sum_i m_i \left[(\ddot{z}_i + g)\, x_i - \ddot{x}_i \, z_i\right]}{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)} \\
y_{zmp} &= \frac{\sum_i m_i \left[(\ddot{z}_i + g)\, y_i - \ddot{y}_i \, z_i\right]}{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Static-case simplification (2.5 marks):** In the static (quasi-static) case, all linear accelerations vanish: $\ddot{x}_i = \ddot{y}_i = \ddot{z}_i = 0$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{zmp} &= \frac{\sum_i m_i \, g \, x_i}{\sum_i m_i \, g} = \frac{\sum_i m_i x_i}{M} = C_x \\
y_{zmp} &= \frac{\sum_i m_i \, g \, y_i}{\sum_i m_i \, g} = \frac{\sum_i m_i y_i}{M} = C_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (1 mark):** Therefore, in the static case:

$$
\boxed{(x_{zmp}, y_{zmp}) = (C_x, C_y) = \text{CoM projection on the ground.}}
$$

**Stability criterion:** The robot is statically stable if and only if the ZMP (equivalently, the CoM projection) lies strictly inside the support polygon $P_s$.

---

**B (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Updated total mass and CoM height (2 marks):**
With the added patient mass, the new total mass and CoM position are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{new}} &= 54 + 60 = \mathbf{114 \; kg} \\
C_{z,\text{new}} &= \frac{38.9 + 60(0.95)}{114} = \frac{38.9 + 57.0}{114} = \frac{95.9}{114} \approx \mathbf{0.841 \; m}
