\end{aligned}
$$

(We keep the horizontal CoM at the origin, since the patient mass is added directly above the trunk CoM.)

**Step 2 — Identify the stability margin (0.5 marks):** From Question A(b), the geometric stability margin to the *nearest* (back) edge of the left foot is:

$$
d_{sm} = 0.05 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Energy Stability Margin (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sm} &= \frac{1}{2} \, M_{\text{new}} \, g \, \frac{d_{sm}^2}{C_{z,\text{new}}} \\
       &= \frac{1}{2} \times 114 \times 9.81 \times \frac{(0.05)^2}{0.841} \\
       &= \frac{1}{2} \times 114 \times 9.81 \times \frac{0.0025}{0.841} \\
       &= \frac{1}{2} \times 114 \times 9.81 \times 0.002973 \\
       &= \frac{1}{2} \times 3.328 \\
       &\approx \mathbf{1.664 \; J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering significance (1.5 marks):**
The ESM of **≈ 1.66 J** is the minimum energy disturbance (a push, stumble, or actuator transient) that can cause the loaded robot to topple. In a real-time fall-prevention controller, this value is compared with the estimated kinetic energy of the disturbance (computed from IMU accelerometer data). If the disturbance energy exceeds the ESM, the controller must immediately (i) widen the support by repositioning the foot, (ii) reduce CoM height by knee flexion, or (iii) trigger a protective crouch. This is the *energy-based safety threshold* used in modern rehabilitation exoskeleton controllers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Static Stability Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "vertical projection"** — many students write "the CoM must lie inside the support polygon." This is *wrong*. The CoM is in 3-D space; the *projection* of the CoM on the ground plane must lie inside the polygon. (-1 mark per KTU pattern.)
> 2. **Confusing the ZMP with the CoP (Center of Pressure)** — the CoP is always *inside* the contact patch by definition; the ZMP may lie *outside* (in dynamic cases). In the static case they coincide, but in general they are different. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. (-1 to -2 marks.)
> 3. **Using the wrong formula for ESM** — the simple form $E_{sm} = Mgh\,d_{sm}$ is a *first-order approximation* valid only when $d_{sm} \ll h$. The more accurate form $E_{sm} = M g \left(\sqrt{h^2 + d_{sm}^2} - h\right)$ should be used when $d_{sm}$ is comparable to $h$. Always state your assumption. (-1 mark.)
> 4. **Neglecting the convex hull** — for multi-foot support, students sometimes join foot centers with a straight line and call that the support polygon. The correct construction requires the *convex hull* of the contact patches. (-1 mark.)
> 5. **Mixing up static and dynamic ZMP** — when asked for the static ZMP, do NOT carry the inertial terms ($\ddot{x}_i, \ddot{z}_i$). The static case is defined by setting all accelerations to zero. (-2 marks for a fully wrong derivation.)
> 6. **Forgetting units** — every quantity must carry units. Write "m" for distances, "kg" for mass, "J" for energy, "N·m" for moments. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in formula sheets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Static Stability:**

- ☐ **Definition:** A robot is statically stable if the *vertical projection* of its CoM lies inside the support polygon.
- ☐ **Support Polygon** = Convex hull of all ground-contact patches in the $xy$-plane.
- ☐ **Stability Margin** $d_{sm}$ = minimum perpendicular distance from CoM projection to any support edge.
- ☐ **Static stability condition:** $d_{sm} > 0$ (strict inequality — equality means critical tipping).
- ☐ **ZMP = CoM projection** in the static case (this is THE key KTU result of this module).
- ☐ **Energy Stability Margin:** $E_{sm} \approx M g h \, d_{sm}$ (first-order) or $E_{sm} = \frac{1}{2} M g d_{sm}^2 / h$ (small-angle, more accurate).
- ☐ **ESM units:** Joules (J); preferred over geometric margin in rehabilitation because it accounts for CoM height.
- ☐ **CoM formula:** $\mathbf{C} = \frac{\sum m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$ — mass-weighted average position.
- ☐ **Stability Ratio** $SR = d_{sm}/h$ — height-normalized dimensionless stability metric.
- ☐ **Application to rehab robotics:** Exoskeletons, prosthetics, smart walkers, gait trainers all use this criterion in real-time fall-prevention loops.
- ☐ **Single Support Phase (SSP):** Support polygon = single foot contact patch (smallest margin).
- ☐ **Double Support Phase (DSP):** Support polygon = convex hull of both feet (largest margin).
- ☐ **Tipping edge** = the support polygon edge closest to the CoM projection (this is where toppling will occur).
- ☐ **Restoring Moment** $M_{res} = M g d_{foot}$ vs. **Tipping Moment** $M_{tip} = M g d_{sm}$ — stability requires $M_{res} > M_{tip}$.
- ☐ **Distinction to remember:** ZMP ≡ CoM projection (static) ≠ CoP in general (CoP is always inside contact, ZMP may not be in dynamic cases).


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Approach to Static Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geometric Approach to Static Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Static stability** in robotic rehabilitation devices and legged robots refers to the ability of a system to remain in equilibrium under gravitational loading **without** any acceleration of its center of mass. The **Geometric Approach to Static Stability** is a deterministic, kinematic-level analysis method that evaluates the equilibrium of a standing/standing-like robot (e.g., a lower-limb exoskeleton, a prosthetic leg, a parallel walker, or a humanoid balance trainer) by purely inspecting the spatial relationship between the **Center of Mass (CoM)** projection and the **Support Polygon** formed by the contact points/areas between the robot and the ground.

The fundamental geometric stability condition is:

> A statically stable robotic system must have its CoM ground projection contained strictly within the convex hull of its support contacts.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the geometric approach is also termed the **Support Polygon / CoM-Projection Method** and forms the foundation for the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** formulation in quasi-static and dynamic walking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OEBRT722 Module 2):**
> The geometric approach is treated as a *prerequisite* to dynamic stability (ZMP-based dynamic walking). For rehabilitation robotics — where the patient/robot contact is slow, deliberate, and often quasi-static — the geometric approach provides a clinically safe, computationally cheap, and verifiable stability criterion.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a four-legged wooden stool placed on a flat floor. If you place a heavy book on the seat, the stool will not tip over **as long as** the combined center-of-mass of (stool + book) projected vertically downward falls **inside the rectangle** traced by the four leg tips. This imaginary rectangle is the **support polygon**, and the shadow of the CoM is the **CoM projection**.

- If the shadow falls **inside** the rectangle → **STABLE** (the stool stands).
- If the shadow falls **exactly on the edge** → **MARGINALLY STABLE** (a tiny push tips it over).
- If the shadow falls **outside** the rectangle → **UNSTABLE** (the stool tips immediately).

The same logic applies to:
- A **patient standing in a robotic standing frame** → the support polygon is the convex hull of both feet.
- A **four-wheeled rehabilitation mobile robot** → the support polygon is the quadrilateral formed by the four wheel contact patches.
- A **lower-limb exoskeleton** attached to a therapist and patient → the support polygon spans both stances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> The geometric approach ignores inertia, joint torques, and time derivatives. It treats the robot as a *frozen sculpture* and asks only one question: *"Is the gravity vector passing through the support base?"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | $\mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ |
| $m$ | Total mass of the robot-patient system | $\mathbf{kg}$ |
| $P_{CoM}$ | 2-D ground projection of the CoM | $\mathbf{(x_{CoM}, y_{CoM})}$ in meters |
| $S$ | Support polygon (convex hull) | $\mathbf{m^2}$ |
| $d_{min}$ | Minimum distance from $P_{CoM}$ to $\partial S$ | meters |
| $h_{CoM}$ | Height of CoM above ground | meters |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks

The geometric approach to static stability is constructed from **four conceptual building blocks**. Each is introduced below in the logical order required for a KTU board answer.

### Building Block 1 — The Center of Mass (CoM)

For a multi-body system composed of $n$ rigid links of masses $m_i$ with CoM positions $\mathbf{r}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$, the system CoM is given by the mass-weighted average:

$$\mathbf{r}_{CoM} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}$$

> **Why this matters:** In a rehabilitation robot, the CoM of the *combined* robot + patient system is what determines tipping. Mis-estimating patient mass distribution is the most common source of instability in clinical deployments.

### Building Block 2 — The Support Polygon (Convex Hull)

The **support polygon** $S$ is the **2-D convex hull** of all ground-contact points (or areas) of the robot. For a bipedal stance, $S$ is the convex hull of the contact polygons of the left and right feet. The boundary $\partial S$ consists of straight-line edges connecting consecutive contact vertices in counter-clockwise order.

> **Why this matters:** When the feet are close together (e.g., during a single-leg stance training exercise), $S$ shrinks dramatically, and the static stability margin collapses. This is the geometric reason why such exercises are *harder* and *more rehabilitative*.

### Building Block 3 — CoM Projection onto the Ground Plane

The vertical projection of the 3-D CoM onto the ground plane ($z=0$) is:

$$P_{CoM} \;=\; (x_{CoM}, \; y_{CoM}, \; 0) \;=\; \left( \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}, \; \frac{\sum m_i y_i}{\sum m_i}, \; 0 \right)$$

The system is **statically stable** if and only if $P_{CoM} \in S$ (strictly inside, or on the boundary for marginal stability).

### Building Block 4 — The Zero Moment Point (ZMP) in the Static Limit

The ZMP is the point on the ground about which the **net moment of the active forces (gravity + inertial forces) is zero**. In the *static* (zero-acceleration) limit:

$$x_{ZMP} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, x_i \, g}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, g} \;=\; x_{CoM}$$

$$y_{ZMP} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, y_i \, g}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, g} \;=\; y_{CoM}$$

Therefore, **under static conditions, the ZMP coincides with the CoM ground projection**. This is the single most important fact for the KTU Module-2 exam.

## 2.2 Stability Margins — The Quantitative Geometric Measures

The *binary* condition $P_{CoM} \in S$ is not enough for clinical/engineering use. We need a **scalar measure** of *how stable* the configuration is. Three classical geometric margins are prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### 2.2.1 Static Stability Margin (SSM)

The **SSM** is the shortest Euclidean distance from the CoM projection to the boundary $\partial S$ of the support polygon:

$$SSM \;=\; \min_{Q \in \partial S} \;\Vert P_{CoM} - Q \Vert_2$$

Larger SSM ⇒ more stable.

### 2.2.2 Longitudinal and Lateral Stability Margins

When the support polygon is axis-aligned (e.g., a rectangular stance), the SSM decomposes into:
- **Longitudinal SSM**: distance to the front/rear edge.
- **Lateral SSM**: distance to the left/right edge.

### 2.2.3 Force-Angle Stability Margin (FASM)

A combined measure (Yoneda & Hirose, 1996) used for *rough terrain* multi-legged robots, which is also relevant for rehabilitation walkers traversing uneven floors:

$$FASM \;=\; \min_{j} \left( \alpha_j \cdot f_j \right)$$

where $\alpha_j$ is the angle between the gravity vector and the line from the $j$-th support edge to the CoM projection, and $f_j$ is a force ratio involving the resultant ground reaction force.

### 2.2.4 Energy Stability Margin (ESM)

The **ESM** is the minimum gravitational potential energy that must be supplied to tip the robot over a support edge:

$$ESM \;=\; m \, g \, h_{CoM} \cdot \min_{j} \left( \cos \theta_j \right)$$

where $\theta_j$ is the tipping angle associated with the $j$-th edge.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **KTU board-exam ready cheat sheet**. Memorize the formulas and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Mass-weighted centroid of the system | $\mathbf{m}$ |
| 2 | $x_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) x_i - \sum m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i}{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}$ | General ZMP x-coordinate | $\mathbf{m}$ |
| 3 | $x_{ZMP}^{static} = x_{CoM}$ | Static limit: ZMP = CoM projection | $\mathbf{m}$ |
| 4 | $SSM = \min_{Q \in \partial S} \Vert P_{CoM} - Q \Vert$ | Shortest distance to support edge | $\mathbf{m}$ |
| 5 | $ESM = m g h_{CoM} \min_j \cos \theta_j$ | Energy to tip over the most critical edge | $\mathbf{J}$ |
| 6 | Stability condition: $P_{CoM} \in S$ | CoM projection must lie inside the support polygon | dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_{min} = m g$ | Minimum ground reaction force for equilibrium | $\mathbf{N}$ |
| 8 | $h_{max} = \dfrac{SSM}{\sqrt{1 - (SSM/R)^2}}$ approx. for small margins | Max CoM height for given SSM | $\mathbf{m}$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** In a robotic exoskeleton for stroke rehabilitation, the geometric approach is used in *real-time* by the balance controller. The onboard IMU estimates the patient's CoM, the foot pressure sensors estimate $S$, and the SSM is fed back to a torque controller that modulates the exoskeleton's hip/ankle stiffness to *prevent* $P_{CoM}$ from crossing $\partial S$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — CoM of a Multi-Body Robotic System

### Statement
For a rehabilitation robot composed of $n$ rigid links, derive the closed-form expression for the 3-D Center of Mass.

### Step 1 — Definition of the Mass-Weighted Centroid
The center of mass is defined as the unique point at which the *first moment of mass* about any axis through that point vanishes. Mathematically:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_{CoM}) \;=\; \mathbf{0}$$

> **Logic:** This is the direct application of the moment-balance principle. The vector sum of all mass-weighted displacements from the CoM must be zero.

### Step 2 — Expand and Rearrange
Distributing the sum:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{r}_i \;-\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\right) \mathbf{r}_{CoM} \;=\; \mathbf{0}$$

### Step 3 — Isolate $\mathbf{r}_{CoM}$
Moving the second term to the right-hand side:

$$\mathbf{r}_{CoM} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}$$

This is the **mass-weighted average position** of all link CoMs. $\blacksquare$

> **Real-world check:** For a humanoid rehabilitation robot with a torso, two arms, two legs, and a head, $n = 6$ to $12$ links. The denominator $\sum m_i$ is simply the total mass $M$ of the robot.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — ZMP Equation from the Moment-Balance Principle

### Statement
Starting from Newton's second law for a multi-body system, derive the position of the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) on the ground plane $z = 0$.

### Step 1 — Active Forces on the $i$-th Link
The active (non-contact) force on link $i$ is gravity + inertial force:

$$\mathbf{F}_i^{act} \;=\; m_i \mathbf{g} \;-\; m_i \mathbf{a}_i$$

where $\mathbf{a}_i = (\ddot{x}_i, \ddot{y}_i, \ddot{z}_i)$ is the linear acceleration of the $i$-th link's CoM.

### Step 2 — Active Moment about an Arbitrary Ground Point $P = (p_x, p_y, 0)$
The moment of the active forces about $P$ is:

$$\mathbf{M}_P^{act} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{P}) \times \mathbf{F}_i^{act}$$

### Step 3 — The ZMP Definition
The ZMP is the unique point $P^\ast = (p_x^\ast, p_y^\ast, 0)$ on the ground where the **horizontal components** of the active moment vanish (the ground reaction force supplies the missing vertical moment). Setting the $x$-component of the moment to zero:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (y_i - p_y^\ast) F_{i,z}^{act} - (z_i - 0) F_{i,y}^{act} \right] \;=\; 0$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Active Forces
With $F_{i,z}^{act} = m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)$ and $F_{i,y}^{act} = -m_i \ddot{y}_i$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (y_i - p_y^\ast) \, m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) + z_i \, m_i \ddot{y}_i \right] \;=\; 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $p_y^\ast$
Distributing and isolating $p_y^\ast$:

$$p_y^\ast \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \, m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) + z_i \, m_i \ddot{y}_i \right]$$

$$\boxed{\, p_y^\ast \;=\; \frac{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) y_i \;+\; \sum m_i z_i \ddot{y}_i}{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)} \,}$$

> **Logic:** Note the sign convention — a positive $z_i \ddot{y}_i$ term *adds* to the numerator because the moment arm is along the $z$-axis.

### Step 6 — The Static Limit ($\ddot{x}_i = \ddot{y}_i = \ddot{z}_i = 0$)
Setting all accelerations to zero:

$$p_y^\ast \big|_{static} \;=\; \frac{\sum m_i \, g \, y_i}{\sum m_i \, g} \;=\; \frac{\sum m_i y_i}{\sum m_i} \;=\; y_{CoM}$$

By symmetric derivation along the $x$-axis:

$$p_x^\ast \big|_{static} \;=\; x_{CoM}$$

Therefore, **the ZMP coincides with the CoM ground projection under static conditions**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Static Stability Margin (SSM) for a Rectangular Stance

### Statement
A two-legged rehabilitation robot stands with feet placed at $(\pm a, \pm b, 0)$ — i.e., the support polygon is a rectangle of width $2a$ and depth $2b$. The CoM projection is at $(x_c, y_c)$. Derive the SSM.

### Step 1 — Identify the Support Polygon Edges
The four edges of the rectangle are at $x = \pm a$ and $y = \pm b$.

### Step 2 — Compute Distances to Each Edge
- Distance to right edge: $d_R = a - x_c$
- Distance to left edge: $d_L = x_c + a$
- Distance to front edge: $d_F = b - y_c$
- Distance to rear edge: $d_{Re} = y_c + b$

### Step 3 — Stability Margins Along the Two Axes

$$SSM_x \;=\; \min(a - x_c, \; x_c + a) \;=\; a - \vert x_c \vert$$

$$SSM_y \;=\; \min(b - y_c, \; y_c + b) \;=\; b - \vert y_c \vert$$

### Step 4 — Overall SSM
The overall SSM is the **shortest** of the four distances (the first edge the CoM will cross if pushed):

$$\boxed{\, SSM \;=\; \min(a - \vert x_c \vert, \; b - \vert y_c \vert) \,}$$

### Step 5 — Special Case: Centered Stance
If the CoM is exactly centered ($x_c = 0, \; y_c = 0$):

$$SSM \;=\; \min(a, b)$$

i.e., the SSM equals the *shorter* half-width of the support rectangle. For a long, narrow stance (typical of a treadmill-based gait trainer), $b \ll a$, so $SSM = b$ — the lateral margin dominates and limits stability. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Geometric Stability Measures

| Criterion | Formula | Strength | Weakness | Typical Use in Rehab Robotics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CoM in Polygon | $P_{CoM} \in S$ | Simple, binary | No magnitude info | Real-time safety check |
| SSM | $d_{min}$ to $\partial S$ | Geometric, intuitive | Ignores CoM height | Standing balance trainers |
| FASM | $\min_j \alpha_j f_j$ | Terrain-aware | Needs reaction forces | Outdoor exoskeletons |
| ESM | $m g h_{CoM} \min \cos \theta_j$ | Energy-based, conservative | Ignores CoM height? No — includes $h_{CoM}$ | Powered prosthetics |
| ZMP (static) | $= P_{CoM}$ | Standard formulation | Coincides with CoM in static | Bipedal gait trainers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — The Geometric Approach Workflow

The following block diagram captures the **data flow** of a static-stability computation in a rehabilitation robot controller. Each block represents a computational or sensing stage, and the arrows show the dependency of the final stability verdict on the inputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Link Kinematics: Joint Angles q1..qn] --> B[Forward Kinematics Solver]
    B --> C[Link CoM Positions r1..rn]
    D[Mass Properties Database: m1..mn] --> E[Mass-Weighted Averaging]
    C --> E
    E --> F[System CoM r_CoM in 3D]
    F --> G[Vertical Projection: P_CoM = x_CoM, y_CoM, 0]
    H[Foot Pressure Sensor Array OR Foot Contact Map] --> I[Support Polygon Construction: Convex Hull S]
    I --> J[Polygon Boundary Extraction: edges of S]
    G --> K[SSM Computation: min distance to edges of S]
    J --> K
    G --> L[ZMP Computation: Static Limit equals P_CoM]
    K --> M{Is SSM greater than Threshold epsilon?}
    L --> N{CoM Projection inside S?}
    M -->|Yes| O[STABLE: Green Indicator on Therapist GUI]
    M -->|No| P[UNSTABLE: Trigger Safety Catch or Increase Stance Width]
    N -->|Yes| O
    N -->|No| P
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Tipping Geometry Around a Support Edge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Body with CoM at height h_CoM] --> B[CoM Projection P_CoM on Ground]
    C[Support Polygon Edge j] --> D[Tipping Pivot Line]
    B --> E[Distance d_j from P_CoM to Tipping Pivot]
    D --> E
    A --> F[Tipping Angle theta_j = arctan d_j / h_CoM]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Energy Stability Margin ESM = m g h_CoM cos theta_j]
    G --> H{Is ESM greater than Disturbance Energy?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Safe Against Tipping]
    H -->|No| J[Robot Tips Over Edge j]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Typical 2-Foot Bipedal Support Polygon

> A schematic representation of how the support polygon is constructed from the foot contact points of a bipedal rehabilitation robot.
>
> - Left foot contact vertices: $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ (rectangular foot, projected to ground).
> - Right foot contact vertices: $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$.
> - Support polygon $S$ = convex hull of $\{L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4, R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4\}$.
> - For two parallel feet, the hull degenerates into a hexagon (8 vertices if the feet are spaced apart, or a rectangle if the feet touch).
> - CoM projection $P_{CoM}$ must lie inside this hexagon.
> - The shortest distance from $P_{CoM}$ to any of the six edges is the SSM.

```
                Y (lateral)
                ^
                |
       L1 ----- L2           R1 ----- R2
       |        |             |       |
       |        |             |       |
       L4 ----- L3           R4 ----- R3
                |
   <------------+--------------+-----------------> X (forward)
                |
                |        * P_CoM  (CoM Projection)
                |
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the figure above, the convex hull $S$ is the polygon whose vertices are (in CCW order) $L_1, L_2, R_1, R_2, R_3, L_3, L_4, R_4$ (assuming the feet are laterally separated). The asterisk marks the CoM projection. If the asterisk moves outside the convex hull, the robot will tip.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEBRT722]`
**Define the geometric approach to static stability. What is the support polygon, and what is the role of the CoM projection in determining stability?**

**Model Answer:**

The **geometric approach to static stability** is a kinematic, force-independent method of evaluating whether a robotic system is in static equilibrium. It reduces the problem to a single geometric condition involving two 2-D entities on the ground plane:

1. The **support polygon** $S$, defined as the convex hull of all ground-contact points (or areas) of the robot.
2. The **Center of Mass (CoM) projection** $P_{CoM}$, which is the vertical projection of the 3-D system CoM onto the ground plane.

The system is **statically stable if and only if $P_{CoM}$ lies strictly inside $S$**. If $P_{CoM}$ lies on the boundary of $S$, the system is *marginally stable*; if it lies outside, the system will tip over.

**Role of the CoM projection:** It represents the point through which the net gravitational force vector of the entire system acts. For the system to be in equilibrium, this line of action must fall within the base of support.

**(3 marks — Definition 1.5 m, Support polygon 0.75 m, CoM projection role 0.75 m)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEBRT722]`
**Explain the relationship between the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) and the Center of Mass (CoM) under static conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is defined as the point on the ground about which the net moment of all active forces (gravity and inertial forces) is zero. Mathematically:

$$x_{ZMP} = \frac{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) x_i - \sum m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i}{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}$$

Under **static conditions**, all link accelerations vanish ($\ddot{x}_i = \ddot{y}_i = \ddot{z}_i = 0$). Substituting these into the ZMP equation gives:

$$x_{ZMP}^{static} = \frac{\sum m_i g x_i}{\sum m_i g} = \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i} = x_{CoM}$$

Similarly, $y_{ZMP}^{static} = y_{CoM}$.

**Conclusion:** Under static conditions, the ZMP **coincides exactly with the vertical projection of the CoM** onto the ground plane. This equivalence is the foundation of the geometric approach to static stability.

**(3 marks — ZMP definition 1 m, Static limit derivation 1.5 m, Conclusion 0.5 m)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Module-2 questions on geometric stability, students commonly lose marks in three places:
> 1. **Confusing ZMP with CoG** — remember ZMP is a *ground* point; CoG is in 3-D space.
> 2. **Forgetting the static limit** — the full ZMP formula with $\ddot{x}$ terms is for *dynamic* walking; the static exam answer must reduce to $x_{CoM}$.
> 3. **Drawing a "picture" without a labeled convex hull** — examiners want the *geometric* construction explicit, not a sketch.
> 4. **Not specifying "strictly inside" vs. "on the boundary"** — strict inequality is the stable condition; equality is marginal.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEBRT722]`

**Q. (a) Starting from the moment-balance principle, derive the expression for the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) of a multi-link robotic system. Show explicitly that under static conditions, the ZMP coincides with the CoM ground projection.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Active Forces:** The active (non-contact) forces on link $i$ are gravity $m_i \mathbf{g}$ and inertial force $-m_i \mathbf{a}_i$:

$$\mathbf{F}_i^{act} = m_i \mathbf{g} - m_i \mathbf{a}_i$$

**Step 2 — Define Moment about Ground Point $P$:**

$$\mathbf{M}_P^{act} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{P}) \times \mathbf{F}_i^{act}$$

**Step 3 — Set Horizontal Moment Components to Zero (ZMP Definition):**
For the ZMP at $P^\ast = (p_x^\ast, p_y^\ast, 0)$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (y_i - p_y^\ast) m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) + z_i m_i \ddot{y}_i \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p_y^\ast$:**

$$p_y^\ast = \frac{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) y_i + \sum m_i z_i \ddot{y}_i}{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}$$

By symmetry (replacing $y \to x$ and $\ddot{y} \to \ddot{x}$):

$$p_x^\ast = \frac{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) x_i - \sum m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i}{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Static Limit:** Set $\ddot{x}_i = \ddot{y}_i = \ddot{z}_i = 0$:

$$p_x^\ast \big|_{static} = \frac{\sum m_i g x_i}{\sum m_i g} = \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i} = x_{CoM}$$

$$p_y^\ast \big|_{static} = \frac{\sum m_i g y_i}{\sum m_i g} = y_{CoM}$$

**Conclusion:** In static equilibrium, $\boxed{P_{ZMP} = P_{CoM}}$. **(7 marks — full derivation with steps 1–5)**

---

**Q. (b) With a neat sketch, explain the concept of the Static Stability Margin (SSM) for a bipedal rehabilitation robot. How does the SSM change as the feet are brought closer together during a balance-training exercise?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** is the shortest Euclidean distance from the CoM ground projection $P_{CoM}$ to the boundary $\partial S$ of the support polygon:

$$SSM = \min_{Q \in \partial S} \Vert P_{CoM} - Q \Vert$$

It quantifies *how far* the robot is from tipping over, providing a continuous measure of stability rather than a binary yes/no.

**Sketch (2 marks):**

```
        Y (lateral)
         ^
   L1 -- L2          R1 -- R2
   |     |            |     |
   L4 -- L3          R4 -- R3
         |
   <-----+-----------* P_CoM --+----> X (forward)
         |     d_min  |
         |  <-------->|
         |   SSM     |
         |
```

The diagram shows the support polygon (convex hull of the eight foot vertices), the CoM projection $P_{CoM}$, and the SSM as the perpendicular distance from $P_{CoM}$ to the nearest edge.

**Effect of Bringing Feet Closer (3 marks):**
As the feet are brought closer together laterally (e.g., during a narrow-stance balance drill):
- The support polygon's lateral width $2a$ shrinks.
- The CoM projection $P_{CoM}$ remains approximately at the body's mid-line (assuming symmetric posture).
- Therefore, the **lateral SSM**, $SSM_{lat} = a - \vert x_c \vert$, **decreases linearly** with the stance width.
- Eventually, $SSM \to 0$ when the feet are aligned under the CoM, and the patient must rely on active ankle/hip control (ankle strategy, hip strategy) to maintain balance.

**Clinical Significance:** Narrowing the stance is a standard rehabilitation protocol to challenge and improve the patient's postural control. The geometric approach quantifies this challenge in real-time.

**(7 marks — Definition 2 + Sketch 2 + Effect analysis 3)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEBRT722 — Alternative Choice]`

**Q. (a) Explain in detail the construction of the support polygon for a multi-legged rehabilitation robot. How is the support polygon used to determine static stability?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction Algorithm (3 marks):**
The support polygon is constructed by the following steps:
1. **Identify all ground-contact points** (or contact patch centroids) of the robot. For a 2-legged system these are the vertices of the two feet; for a 4-legged walker they are the four wheel contact patches; for a hexapod gait trainer they are the six foot tips.
2. **Project all contact points onto the ground plane** $z = 0$ (this is already the case for contact points).
3. **Compute the 2-D convex hull** of the projected points (e.g., using Graham scan, QuickHull, or Andrew's monotone chain algorithm). The convex hull is the **support polygon** $S$.
4. **Extract the boundary** $\partial S$ as an ordered sequence of edges (counter-clockwise for consistency).

**Stability Determination (4 marks):**
- **Point-in-Polygon Test:** The CoM projection $P_{CoM} = (x_{CoM}, y_{CoM})$ is tested for containment in $S$ using the ray-casting algorithm or the half-plane intersection test:

$$P_{CoM} \in S \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \bigwedge_{j=1}^{k} \; \mathbf{n}_j \cdot (P_{CoM} - \mathbf{V}_j) \;\geq\; 0$$

where $\mathbf{n}_j$ is the outward normal of the $j$-th edge and $\mathbf{V}_j$ is a vertex of that edge. This must hold for *all* $k$ edges.

- **Binary Stability Verdict:** If the test returns TRUE for all edges, the robot is statically stable; otherwise it is unstable.
- **Quantitative Margin:** Once containment is verified, the SSM is computed as the minimum perpendicular distance to any edge — a single scalar that quantifies stability.

**(7 marks — Construction 3 + Point-in-polygon test 2 + Stability logic 2)**

---

**Q. (b) Define the Energy Stability Margin (ESM) for a legged robot. Show that the ESM is proportional to the CoM height and the cosine of the tipping angle. Why is the ESM considered a more conservative stability measure than the SSM?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Tipping (2 marks):**
Consider a robot with CoM at height $h_{CoM}$ above the ground, and CoM projection at perpendicular distance $d_j$ from the $j$-th support edge. If the robot is tilted about that edge by an angle $\theta$, the geometry gives:

$$\tan \theta_j \;=\; \frac{d_j}{h_{CoM}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d_j \;=\; h_{CoM} \tan \theta_j$$

The robot begins to tip irreversibly when its CoM passes over the pivot edge, i.e., when $\theta_j \geq \theta_{critical}$.

**ESM Derivation (3 marks):**
The work done by gravity as the robot tilts by $\theta_j$ about edge $j$ is:

$$W_j \;=\; m \, g \, h_{CoM} \, (1 - \cos \theta_j)$$

The **Energy Stability Margin (ESM)** is defined as the *minimum* such work, evaluated over all support edges, required to tip the robot:

$$ESM \;=\; \min_{j} \; m \, g \, h_{CoM} \, (1 - \cos \theta_j) \;\approx\; m \, g \, h_{CoM} \, \min_j \cos \theta_j$$

This is the **gravitational potential energy** that an external disturbance must supply to make the robot tip.

**Why ESM is More Conservative (2 marks):**
- The ESM accounts for the **height of the CoM**, not just its 2-D projection. A tall robot is more top-heavy and has a *smaller* ESM even if its SSM is large.
- The ESM uses $\cos \theta_j$ (a *non-linear* function) rather than a linear distance, so it penalizes configurations where the CoM is close to the *middle* of a long edge (low $\theta_j$, low ESM contribution) more accurately.
- The SSM can be misleadingly large when the CoM is high (the robot can still tip easily due to the large $h_{CoM}$), but the ESM correctly accounts for this.

**Conclusion:** The ESM is the **gold standard** for conservative stability analysis in rehabilitation robotics, where patient safety is paramount.

**(7 marks — Tipping definition 2 + Derivation 3 + Conservatism 2)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Geometric Approach to Static Stability**

- ✅ **Static stability** is a *geometric*, force-free analysis. The system is a *frozen sculpture*; accelerations are zero.
- ✅ The **support polygon** $S$ is the 2-D *convex hull* of all ground-contact points.
- ✅ The **CoM projection** $P_{CoM}$ is the vertical drop of the 3-D CoM onto the ground plane.
- ✅ **Stability condition:** $P_{CoM} \in S$ (strictly inside for full stability, on boundary for marginal).
- ✅ **SSM (Static Stability Margin):** shortest distance from $P_{CoM}$ to $\partial S$. Geometric, intuitive, but height-blind.
- ✅ **ZMP in the static limit:** coincides *exactly* with $P_{CoM}$. This is the most-tested KTU fact.
- ✅ **ESM (Energy Stability Margin):** $ESM = m g h_{CoM} \min_j \cos \theta_j$. Includes CoM height. More conservative than SSM.
- ✅ **FASM (Force-Angle Stability Margin):** combines tipping angle and ground-reaction-force ratio; used on rough terrain.
- ✅ For a **bipedal stance with rectangular feet** at $(\pm a, \pm b)$, the SSM is $\min(a - \vert x_c \vert, \; b - \vert y_c \vert)$.
- ✅ **Narrowing the stance shrinks the support polygon** and linearly reduces the lateral SSM — the geometric basis of progressive balance training.
- ✅ The geometric approach is the **clinical standard** for slow, deliberate rehabilitation movements (e.g., sit-to-stand, weight-shift, single-leg stance drills).
- ✅ **Common exam pitfalls:** confusing ZMP with CoG, forgetting the static limit, not specifying strict inequality, omitting the convex hull in diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability with Road Crown and Inclination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Stability with Road Crown and Inclination # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of rehabilitation engineering and assistive mobility devices (such as powered wheelchairs, robotic exoskeletons, smart crutches, and lower-limb prosthetics), **Stability with Road Crown and Inclination** refers to the analysis of the static and dynamic equilibrium of a wheeled or legged mobility platform on a road surface that is simultaneously sloped in two directions — longitudinally (along the direction of travel) and transversely (across the direction of travel). The **Road Crown** is the cross-slope provided on a road surface, typically rising from the edges toward the centerline, to facilitate drainage of surface water. It is usually expressed as a ratio (e.g., **1 in 40** or **1 in 50**), corresponding to a transverse angle $\beta$ such that $\tan\beta = 1/40 \approx 2.5\%$. The **Inclination (Longitudinal Gradient)** is the rise of the road along its centerline, expressed as a ratio (e.g., **1 in 30**) or a percentage, corresponding to a longitudinal angle $\alpha$ such that $\tan\alpha = 1/30 \approx 3.33\%$. A mobility device is said to be **stable** on such a composite slope if it satisfies two simultaneous conditions: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Condition 1 — No Overturning:** The line of action of the resultant weight (including any inertial effects) must fall within the **support polygon** defined by the wheel-ground contact points. > > **Condition 2 — No Skidding:** The magnitude of the tangential reaction force (component of weight along the road plane) must not exceed the maximum available frictional resistance, $\mu R$, where $R$ is the normal reaction and $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction (typically **$\mu = 0.6$ to $0.8$** for rubber tire on dry asphalt). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a hospital wheelchair across a real-world urban road — the road is not perfectly flat. As you go up a small hill, you tilt forward. But the road also slopes sideways to drain rainwater, so the chair leans slightly to one side as well. The chair is now balanced on a surface that is tilted in **two directions at once**, like a book resting on a tilted book stand that is itself tipped sideways. The question the engineer must answer is: *"Will the wheelchair tip over sideways? Will it slide down the hill? Will its wheels still grip the road?"* In rehabilitation robotics, this same question applies to: - **Powered wheelchairs** navigating ramps and crowned pavements - **Lower-limb exoskeletons** worn by paraplegic patients on inclined hospital corridors - **Smart prosthetics** during stair-climbing or sloped-walk training - **Gait-training treadmills** that simulate crowned outdoor terrain The road surface is mathematically a **doubly-tilted plane**, and stability analysis reduces to projecting the gravity vector onto this plane and checking if the projection lies within the wheelbase. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** The two slopes (crown and inclination) do **not** simply add linearly. They combine **vectorially** to produce a single "effective steepest slope" along a diagonal direction. This is the direction along which skidding will first occur, and along which the overturning tendency is maximum. ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Constants and Metrics The following values are typical in KTU-level rehabilitation and transportation engineering problems: | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Coefficient of friction (rubber on dry road) | $\mu$ | **0.6 – 0.8** | | Coefficient of friction (rubber on wet road) | $\mu$ | **0.3 – 0.5** | | Road crown slope | $1/n$ | **1 in 40** to **1 in 50** | | Maximum longitudinal gradient (ramps for disabled access) | — | **1 in 12** (≈ 8.33%) | | Comfortable pedestrian gradient | — | **1 in 20** to **1 in 30** | | Standard acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometry of the Doubly-Tilted Road Surface Consider a road surface that is inclined at: - Angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal in the **longitudinal** direction (direction of travel, $x$-axis) - Angle $\beta$ to the horizontal in the **transverse** direction (across the road, $y$-axis) The **unit outward normal vector** $\hat{n}$ to this doubly-tilted plane can be expressed as: $$ \hat{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\alpha + \tan^2\beta}}\left(\tan\alpha\,\hat{i} \;+\; \tan\beta\,\hat{j} \;+\; \hat{k}\right) $$ The **unit vector along the steepest descent** (the direction in which a ball would roll) is perpendicular to $\hat{n}$ within the plane, and its slope angle $\phi$ relative to the true horizontal satisfies: $$ \tan\phi = \sqrt{\tan^2\alpha + \tan^2\beta} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule:** When checking skidding stability, you **must** use the resultant slope angle $\phi$, not $\alpha$ or $\beta$ individually. This is a very common KTU exam trap. ## 2.2 Force Analysis on a Mobility Device A mobility device (e.g., wheelchair) of weight $W$ rests on this surface. Three forces act on it: - **Weight** $W$ acting vertically downward - **Normal reaction** $R$ acting along $\hat{n}$ - **Frictional force** $F$ acting in the plane of the road, opposing motion Resolving $W$ along and perpendicular to the road plane: $$ R = W\cos\phi $$ $$ F_{\text{required}} = W\sin\phi $$ ## 2.3 Skidding Condition The device will **not skid** if: $$ W\sin\phi \leq \mu R = \mu W\cos\phi $$ $$ \boxed{\tan\phi \leq \mu} $$ ## 2.4 Overturning Condition Consider a two-wheeled (or two-track) device with: - Track width $= 2b$ (so each wheel is at distance $b$ from the centerline) - Height of center of gravity above the road surface $= h$ The moment about the **downhill wheel** is: $$ M_{\text{overturning}} = W \cdot \sin\phi \cdot h $$ The restoring moment (provided by the weight component acting through the wheel contact) is: $$ M_{\text{restoring}} = W \cdot \cos\phi \cdot b $$ The device will **not overturn** if: $$ W\cos\phi \cdot b \geq W\sin\phi \cdot h $$ $$ \boxed{\tan\phi \leq \frac{b}{h}} $$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Condition for Stability | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Effective slope angle | $\tan\phi = \sqrt{\tan^2\alpha + \tan^2\beta}$ | Always defined | | 2 | Normal reaction | $R = W\cos\phi$ | $R > 0$ | | 3 | Tangential (sliding) force | $F_t = W\sin\phi$ | — | | 4 | Skidding limit | $\tan\phi \leq \mu$ | $\mu \geq \tan\phi$ | | 5 | Overturning limit | $\tan\phi \leq b/h$ | $b/h \geq \tan\phi$ | | 6 | Crown angle | $\tan\beta = 1/n$ | $n$ in $1$ | | 7 | Longitudinal gradient | $\tan\alpha = 1/m$ | $m$ in $1$ | | 8 | Combined slope expression | $\tan\phi = \sqrt{(1/m)^2 + (1/n)^2}$ | Vector sum | | 9 | Critical friction coefficient | $\mu_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{(1/m)^2 + (1/n)^2}$ | Threshold value | | 10 | Height of CG for no-overturn | $h \leq b/\tan\phi$ | Geometric limit | > [!NOTE] > The **critical friction coefficient** $\mu_{\text{crit}}$ is the value of $\mu$ below which skidding occurs and above which the device is stable. KTU problems frequently ask students to compute this. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In **rehabilitation engineering**, this analysis directly determines: - The **maximum safe gradient** for an accessible ramp (ADA standards and KTU 2024 scheme both reference $\leq 1$ in $12$ for wheelchair ramps) - The **anti-tip wheel design** on powered wheelchairs - The **tilt-table angle** for vestibular and balance rehabilitation - The **gait-training surface specification** for sloped-ground therapy In **road engineering**, the same analysis governs **stopping sight distance** on crowned curves, **side friction factor** on superelevated roads, and the **minimum radius of curvature** at design speeds. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Slope Angle $\phi$ We begin by establishing the equation of the doubly-inclined road plane. **Step 1 — Define the plane equation.** Let the road rise by $1$ unit for every $m$ units in the $x$-direction (longitudinal) and for every $n$ units in the $y$-direction (transverse). The elevation $z$ at any point is: $$ z = \frac{x}{m} + \frac{y}{n} $$ Rewriting in the standard plane form: $$ \frac{x}{m} + \frac{y}{n} - z = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the unit normal vector.** For a plane $Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$, the unit normal is $\hat{n} = \dfrac{(A, B, C)}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2}}$. Applying this: $$ \hat{n} = \frac{\left(\tfrac{1}{m},\; \tfrac{1}{n},\; -1\right)}{\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{m^2} + \tfrac{1}{n^2} + 1}} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $mn$ for clarity: $$ \hat{n} = \frac{(n,\; m,\; -mn)}{\sqrt{n^2 + m^2 + m^2 n^2}} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the slope of the plane.** The angle $\phi$ that the plane makes with the horizontal satisfies $\cos\phi = \hat{n} \cdot \hat{k}$, where $\hat{k}$ is the vertical unit vector. $$ \cos\phi = \frac{mn}{\sqrt{n^2 + m^2 + m^2 n^2}} $$ Taking the reciprocal squared: $$ \sec^2\phi = 1 + \tan^2\phi = \frac{n^2 + m^2 + m^2 n^2}{m^2 n^2} $$ $$ \tan^2\phi = \frac{n^2 + m^2 + m^2 n^2 - m^2 n^2}{m^2 n^2} = \frac{1}{m^2} + \frac{1}{n^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\tan\phi = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m^2} + \frac{1}{n^2}}} $$ **Step 4 — Physical interpretation.** This derivation confirms that the effective slope combines the two component slopes in a **Pythagorean** (vector) manner. If $m = n$ (equal slopes in both directions), then $\tan\phi = \sqrt{2}/m$, not $2/m$. ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Overturning Condition Consider a wheelchair (or two-tracked rehabilitation robot) of weight $W$, with center of gravity $G$ located at height $h$ above the road surface and at the geometric center between two wheels separated by distance $2b$. **Step 1 — Identify the pivot wheel.** When the device is on a slope, gravity acts vertically downward. The **downhill wheel** acts as the potential pivot for overturning. Let us take moments about this wheel. **Step 2 — Resolve weight into road-aligned components.** The weight $W$ can be decomposed into: - A component $W\cos\phi$ acting **normal to the road surface** (pressing the wheels into the ground) - A component $W\sin\phi$ acting **along the steepest slope**, directed downhill **Step 3 — Compute the overturning moment.** The downhill component $W\sin\phi$ acts at the center of gravity, which is at height $h$ above the road. The moment arm about the downhill wheel contact is $h$ (perpendicular distance to the line of action of $W\sin\phi$). $$ M_{\text{overturning}} = W\sin\phi \cdot h $$ **Step 4 — Compute the restoring moment.** The normal component $W\cos\phi$ acts through the center of gravity, which is offset horizontally by $b$ from the downhill wheel. This creates a restoring moment. $$ M_{\text{restoring}} = W\cos\phi \cdot b $$ **Step 5 — Equate for the limiting case.** For the **just-about-to-overturn** condition: $$ W\cos\phi \cdot b = W\sin\phi \cdot h $$ Cancelling $W$ on both sides: $$ \cos\phi \cdot b = \sin\phi \cdot h $$ Dividing by $h\cos\phi$: $$ \boxed{\tan\phi_{\text{crit, overturn}} = \frac{b}{h}} $$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** If $\tan\phi \leq b/h$, the device is safe against overturning. Notice that **low center of gravity** (small $h$) and **wide wheelbase** (large $b$) both improve stability. This is why powered wheelchairs place their heavy batteries low and between the wheels, and why racing wheelchairs have wide cambered wheels. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Skidding Condition **Step 1 — Force balance along the road plane.** The component of weight along the steepest slope is $W\sin\phi$. The maximum available static friction is $\mu R = \mu W\cos\phi$. **Step 2 — Apply the no-skid condition.** For no skidding, friction must be sufficient to balance the downhill pull: $$ W\sin\phi \leq \mu W\cos\phi $$ $$ \boxed{\tan\phi_{\text{crit, skid}} = \mu} $$ **Step 3 — Combined stability.** The device is **fully stable** on the doubly-tilted road only if **both** conditions are satisfied: $$ \boxed{\tan\phi \leq \min\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{b}{h}\right)} $$ The lower of the two limits governs. KTU problems typically ask: *"Will the device skid first, or overturn first?"* — and the answer depends on which limit is smaller. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix) **Problem:** A robotic rehabilitation walker has $b = 0.25$ m (half-track), $h = 0.80$ m (CG height), and rubber feet with $\mu = 0.6$ on the road. The road has a longitudinal gradient of $1$ in $30$ and a crown of $1$ in $40$. Determine whether the walker will skid, overturn, or remain stable. **Step 1 — Compute component slope angles.** $$ \tan\alpha = \frac{1}{30} \approx 0.0333 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \alpha \approx 1.91^\circ $$ $$ \tan\beta = \frac{1}{40} = 0.0250 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \beta \approx 1.43^\circ $$ **Step 2 — Compute effective slope angle.** $$ \tan\phi = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{30}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{40}\right)^2} = \sqrt{0.001111 + 0.000625} = \sqrt{0.001736} $$ $$ \tan\phi \approx 0.04166 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi \approx 2.386^\circ $$ **Step 3 — Check skidding.** $$ \mu = 0.6 \gg 0.04166 $$ Skidding condition satisfied by a large margin — the walker will **not skid**. **Step 4 — Check overturning.** $$ \frac{b}{h} = \frac{0.25}{0.80} = 0.3125 $$ Since $0.3125 \gg 0.04166$, the overturning condition is also satisfied. **Step 5 — Conclusion.** The walker is **stable**. In fact, it can tolerate a much steeper effective slope — up to $\tan\phi = 0.3125$ ($\phi \approx 17.35^\circ$) before overturning becomes a concern. ## 3.5 Reverse Problem: Critical Gradient for Instability A more useful design question: *"Given $\mu$ and $b/h$, what is the maximum allowable road crown and inclination?"* Setting $\tan\phi = \min(\mu, b/h)$: $$ \sqrt{\frac{1}{m^2} + \frac{1}{n^2}} = \min\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{b}{h}\right) $$ If the longitudinal gradient is $1/m$, the maximum allowable crown is $1/n_{\max}$: $$ \frac{1}{n_{\max}} = \sqrt{\min\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{b}{h}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{m^2}} $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{1}{n_{\max}} = \sqrt{\min\!\left(\mu^2,\; \left(\frac{b}{h}\right)^2\right) - \frac{1}{m^2}}} $$ This is the **design equation** used by accessibility engineers to specify the maximum safe road geometry for a given mobility device. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of Mobility Device on Doubly-Tilted Road The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the architecture of forces, geometry, and decision logic governing stability analysis. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Mobility Device
Weight W, CG height h, half-track b"] A --> B["Road Surface
Longitudinal slope 1/m
Transverse crown 1/n"] B --> C1["Compute tan alpha = 1/m"] B --> C2["Compute tan beta = 1/n"] C1 --> D["Compute effective slope:
tan phi = sqrt tan-sq-alpha + tan-sq-beta"] C2 --> D D --> E["Resolve Weight W into:
Normal: W cos phi
Tangential: W sin phi"] E --> F1["Skidding Check:
tan phi vs mu"] E --> F2["Overturning Check:
tan phi vs b by h"] F1 --> G1["If tan phi is less than mu: NO SKID"] F1 --> G2["If tan phi greater than mu: SKID FAILS"] F2 --> H1["If tan phi is less than b/h: NO OVERTURN"] F2 --> H2["If tan phi greater than b/h: OVERTURN FAILS"] G1 --> I["DEVICE STABLE
Design is safe"] G2 --> J["DEVICE UNSAFE
Increase mu or reduce slope"] H1 --> I H2 --> J I --> K["Rehab Application:
Wheelchair, Exoskeleton, Prosthetic"] J --> L["Re-design Required:
Lower CG, Wider Base, Higher mu"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Stability System The following diagram shows how a stability monitoring system inside a robotic rehabilitation device would architecturally process the same physics in real-time. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENSORS S1["IMU Sensor
Roll phi, Pitch theta"] S2["Wheel Encoder
Velocity v"] S3["Force Sensor
Normal Reaction R"] end SENSORS --> CTRL["Stability Controller
Microcontroller Unit"] CTRL --> COMPUTE["Compute Module
tan phi eff = sqrt tan-sq-alpha + tan-sq-beta"] COMPUTE --> COMPARE["Compare with limits:
mu and b/h"] COMPARE --> DECISION{"Both limits
satisfied?"} DECISION -- Yes --> SAFE["GREEN STATUS
Continue Operation"] DECISION -- No --> ALERT["RED ALERT
Trigger Anti-tip Motor
Apply Brakes
Warn User"] SAFE --> ACTUATORS["Drive Motors"] ALERT --> ACTUATORS ``` ## 4.3 Vector Force Resolution Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD W["Weight W
acts vertically downward"] W --> WN["Component W cos phi
Normal to road surface
Acts at CG"] W --> WT["Component W sin phi
Tangential along steepest slope
Downhill direction"] WN --> R["Normal Reaction R
at wheel contact
R equals W cos phi"] WT --> F["Friction Force F
at wheel contact
F opposes motion"] R --> EQ1["Equilibrium:
R balances W cos phi"] F --> EQ2["Equilibrium:
F balances W sin phi
if F is less than mu R: STABLE"] EQ2 --> LIM1["Friction limit:
F max equals mu R equals mu W cos phi"] LIM1 --> CRIT1["Critical slope:
tan phi crit equals mu"] WN --> MOM["Restoring Moment about downhill wheel:
M r equals W cos phi times b"] WT --> MOM2["Overturning Moment:
M o equals W sin phi times h"] MOM --> EQM["Equilibrium condition:
M r greater than or equal to M o"] MOM2 --> EQM EQM --> CRIT2["Critical slope:
tan phi crit equals b/h"] CRIT1 --> FINAL["Stability Limit:
tan phi less than min of mu and b/h"] CRIT2 --> FINAL ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In a typical KTU problem, the examiner will provide a numerical value for the slope and the device parameters. Your task is to compute $\tan\phi$ using the Pythagorean combination, then compare with **both** $\mu$ and $b/h$. The **smaller** of the two governs the failure mode. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2] **Q:** Define the term **road crown** and state its typical value in highway engineering. Why is it provided? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Road crown is the transverse slope provided on a road surface, rising from the edges toward the centerline. It is expressed as a ratio, typically **1 in 40 to 1 in 50** (≈ 2% to 2.5%). It is provided to drain off surface water quickly, preventing waterlogging, skidding, and pavement damage. In rehabilitation contexts, it influences the lateral stability of wheelchairs, walkers, and exoskeletons traversing standard urban roads. **(3 marks)** ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2] **Q:** A wheelchair crosses a road with longitudinal gradient **1 in 30** and crown **1 in 40**. Write the expression for the effective slope angle $\phi$ and compute its value. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The effective slope angle is given by: $$ \tan\phi = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{m}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{30}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{40}\right)^2} $$ $$ \tan\phi = \sqrt{0.001111 + 0.000625} = \sqrt{0.001736} \approx 0.0417 $$ $$ \phi \approx 2.39^\circ $$ **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **The biggest mark-loss trap** in this topic is treating the two slopes as additive. Students often write $\tan\phi = \tan\alpha + \tan\beta$, which is **wrong** and will cost full marks. Always use the **Pythagorean sum**: $\tan\phi = \sqrt{\tan^2\alpha + \tan^2\beta}$. > 2. Many students forget to compare against **both** $\mu$ and $b/h$. You must check **both** failure modes and identify the governing one. > 3. Always state the units and physical interpretation of your final answer. > 4. When asked "will it skid or overturn?", state which is **more critical** (lower limiting value) — this earns the design-recommendation marks. ### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2] **A rehabilitation robotic walker has the following specifications:** - Weight $W = 80$ kg (including patient support frame) - Center of gravity height $h = 0.9$ m above the road - Half-track $b = 0.30$ m - Wheel-ground friction coefficient $\mu = 0.5$ (rubber on wet asphalt) - Road longitudinal gradient: **1 in 25** - Road crown: **1 in 35** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** Determine whether the walker will skid, overturn, or remain stable. Identify the governing failure mode. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** If the road crown is increased (steeper) to **1 in 20**, recompute the effective slope and state what safety measures the rehabilitation engineer should implement. --- **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Compute slope components.** $$ \tan\alpha = \frac{1}{25} = 0.0400, \quad \alpha \approx 2.29^\circ $$ $$ \tan\beta = \frac{1}{35} \approx 0.0286, \quad \beta \approx 1.64^\circ $$ **Step 2 — Effective slope.** $$ \tan\phi = \sqrt{(0.0400)^2 + (0.0286)^2} = \sqrt{0.001600 + 0.000818} = \sqrt{0.002418} $$ $$ \tan\phi \approx 0.04917 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi \approx 2.816^\circ $$ **Step 3 — Skidding check.** We require $\tan\phi \leq \mu$: $$ 0.04917 \leq 0.5 \quad\checkmark \text{ (Satisfied by a wide margin)} $$ **Step 4 — Overturning check.** We require $\tan\phi \leq b/h$: $$ \frac{b}{h} = \frac{0.30}{0.90} = 0.3333 $$ $$ 0.04917 \leq 0.3333 \quad\checkmark \text{ (Satisfied)} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion.** The walker is **fully stable**. The governing limit (smaller of the two) is the **skidding limit** at $\mu = 0.5$, but the actual slope is far below it. **No failure mode is triggered.** **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Recompute with new crown.** New crown: $1$ in $20$, so $\tan\beta_{\text{new}} = 0.05$. $$ \tan\phi_{\text{new}} = \sqrt{(0.0400)^2 + (0.05)^2} = \sqrt{0.001600 + 0.002500} = \sqrt{0.004100} $$ $$ \tan\phi_{\text{new}} \approx 0.06403 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_{\text{new}} \approx 3.665^\circ $$ **Step 2 — Re-check both conditions.** Skidding: $0.06403 \leq 0.5$ — still safe. Overturning: $0.06403 \leq 0.3333$ — still safe. **Step 3 — Numerical interpretation.** The walker is still stable, but the **safety margin has shrunk**. The ratio of margin to limit: $$ \text{Skidding margin} = \frac{0.5 - 0.06403}{0.5} \times 100\% \approx 87.2\% $$ **Step 4 — Recommended safety measures for the rehabilitation engineer:** 1. **Lower the center of gravity** by relocating batteries and heavy actuators closer to the wheel axle. 2. **Widen the wheelbase** (increase $b$) by repositioning the wheels. 3. **Use high-friction wheels** (replace smooth rubber with treaded or polyurethane composite) to raise $\mu$. 4. **Install an IMU-based anti-tip system** that triggers automatic braking if $\tan\phi$ approaches the critical value. 5. **Display a real-time stability indicator** on the device's user interface, in line with **KTU 2024 scheme accessibility standards**. 6. **Restrict use on roads steeper than the critical combined slope** — define this in the device manual. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2] **A lower-limb robotic exoskeleton is tested on an outdoor test track that has a longitudinal gradient of 1 in 40 and a cross-fall (crown) of 1 in 50. The exoskeleton-plus-patient system has a total weight of 700 N, a center-of-gravity height of 1.0 m, and a foot support width (effective half-track) of 0.15 m. The coefficient of friction between the exoskeleton foot and the test surface is 0.7.** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** Determine whether the exoskeleton will skid or overturn first. State the critical value of either the friction coefficient or the effective slope. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** If the patient adopts a posture that lowers the CG by 0.2 m (while keeping the foot support width constant), by what factor does the safety margin against overturning improve? Recommend whether this posture should be adopted for outdoor sloped walking. --- **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Component slopes.** $$ \tan\alpha = \frac{1}{40} = 0.025, \quad \tan\beta = \frac{1}{50} = 0.020 $$ **Step 2 — Effective slope.** $$ \tan\phi = \sqrt{(0.025)^2 + (0.020)^2} = \sqrt{0.000625 + 0.000400} = \sqrt{0.001025} $$ $$ \tan\phi \approx 0.03202 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi \approx 1.834^\circ $$ **Step 3 — Skidding limit.** $$ \mu = 0.7 $$ Skidding requires $\tan\phi \geq 0.7$ — far from satisfied. **No skidding.** **Step 4 — Overturning limit.** $$ \frac{b}{h} = \frac{0.15}{1.0} = 0.15 $$ Overturning requires $\tan\phi \geq 0.15$ — also far from satisfied. **No overturning.** **Step 5 — Identify the governing mode.** Both limits are well above the actual slope. The **governing (more critical) limit** is the overturning limit at $\tan\phi = 0.15$ (which is smaller than $\mu = 0.7$). Therefore, **overturning is the more critical failure mode** if the slope were to increase. **Step 6 — Critical slope for failure.** $$ \tan\phi_{\text{crit, overturn}} = 0.15 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_{\text{crit}} \approx 8.53^\circ $$ The exoskeleton is **stable** under the given conditions. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — New CG height.** Lowered CG: $h_{\text{new}} = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8$ m. **Step 2 — New overturning limit.** $$ \left(\frac{b}{h}\right)_{\text{new}} = \frac{0.15}{0.8} = 0.1875 $$ **Step 3 — Factor of improvement.** $$ \text{Improvement factor} = \frac{(b/h)_{\text{new}}}{(b/h)_{\text{old}}} = \frac{0.1875}{0.15} = 1.25 $$ The safety margin against overturning improves by a factor of **1.25** (a 25% increase). **Step 4 — Numerical verification using margins.** Old margin: $(0.15 - 0.03202) / 0.15 = 78.65\%$ New margin: $(0.1875 - 0.03202) / 0.1875 = 82.92\%$ Improvement: $(82.92 - 78.65) = +4.27$ percentage points, consistent with the factor of 1.25. **Step 5 — Recommendation.** The lowered-CG posture is **strongly recommended** for outdoor sloped walking, especially in **post-stroke and elderly rehabilitation** patients, where balance reflexes are compromised. This is the same principle used by mountaineers, race-car drivers, and dynamic walkers: **a low center of gravity dramatically improves stability on uneven ground**. The 0.2 m reduction in CG height also reduces the moment arm for any external disturbance (e.g., a push or stumble), further enhancing safety. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Stability with Road Crown and Inclination** - **Road crown** is the **transverse** slope (1 in 40 to 1 in 50 typical), used for drainage. - **Inclination** is the **longitudinal** slope (e.g., 1 in 30 for roads, ≤ 1 in 12 for accessible ramps). - The two slopes combine **vectorially (Pythagorean)**, **not additively**: $$ \tan\phi = \sqrt{\tan^2\alpha + \tan^2\beta} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m^2} + \frac{1}{n^2}} $$ - **Two failure modes** must always be checked: - **Skidding:** $\tan\phi \leq \mu$ - **Overturning:** $\tan\phi \leq b/h$ - The **smaller** of $\mu$ and $b/h$ **governs** the failure mode. - **Lowering CG** ($h \downarrow$) and **widening track** ($b \uparrow$) both improve overturning stability linearly. - **Higher friction** ($\mu \uparrow$) improves skidding stability. - In **rehabilitation contexts**, this analysis governs wheelchair ramp design, exoskeleton anti-tip control, and gait-training surface specification. - The **critical friction coefficient** for a given slope pair is $\mu_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{(1/m)^2 + (1/n)^2}$. - The **steepest descent direction** is **diagonal** to the longitudinal and transverse axes — not along either axis. - For very gentle slopes ($\alpha, \beta < 5^\circ$), the small-angle approximation $\phi \approx \sqrt{\alpha^2 + \beta^2}$ (in radians) is acceptable, but the exact $\tan\phi$ form is preferred for KTU answers. - **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² (for any dynamic extension involving inertia). - **Always** state which failure mode governs, and always state both numerical results in your final answer.

topics.title: Impact Strength Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impact Strength Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Impact Strength Tests** are standardized high-strain-rate mechanical testing procedures used to determine the amount of energy absorbed by a material when subjected to a sudden, dynamic, and rapid loading condition (impact). In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation Engineering**, impact tests quantify the toughness, fracture resistance, and shock-absorption capacity of materials used in:

- Exoskeleton structural frames
- Prosthetic socket liners
- Orthotic braces and splints
- End-effector contact surfaces
- Crash-tested mobility aids (wheelchairs, walkers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> Impact strength is defined as the *capacity of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform without fracturing* when subjected to a sudden shock load. It is measured in **Joules (J)** per unit cross-sectional area at the notch.

### 1.2 The Two Principal Standardized Tests

| Test Type | Specimen Notch Position | Loading Geometry | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Izod Impact Test** | Notch on the *same side* as the striking pendulum | Cantilever (vertical) | Polymer/plastic selection for rehabilitation orthotics |
| **Charpy Impact Test** | Notch on the *opposite side* of the striker (V-notch) | Simply supported beam (horizontal) | Metallic implants, exoskeleton alloys, wheelchair frames |

> [!NOTE]
> Both tests are governed by **ASTM E23** (Standard Test Methods for Notched Bar Impact Testing of Metallic Materials) and **ISO 179/180** for polymers.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Imagery)

Imagine you drop a **ceramic coffee mug** and a **rubber shoe sole** from the same height onto a hard floor:

- The **ceramic mug** shatters instantly → **Brittle Material** → **Low Impact Strength** (absorbs almost no energy before fracture).
- The **rubber sole** bounces and deforms → **Tough Material** → **High Impact Strength** (absorbs the kinetic energy by deforming).

> **Rehabilitation Engineering Analogy:** A **transfemoral prosthetic knee joint** must survive an accidental stumble or fall. If its housing is made of a low-impact-strength polymer, it will crack catastrophically. Impact strength testing is the laboratory method to **numerically certify** that the material can absorb the sudden kinetic energy of a fall (typically **50–150 J** for adult body impact) without failure.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

- **Standard Hammer Velocity at Impact:** $v = 3.46 \text{ m/s}$ (falling height $h = 0.61 \text{ m}$)
- **Standard Test Temperature:** $23 \pm 2 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ (per ASTM E23)
- **Standard Specimen Dimensions:** $55 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$
- **Notch Depth:** $2 \text{ mm}$ (V-notch, $45^\circ$ angle, $0.25 \text{ mm}$ root radius)
- **Impact Energy Range (Commercial Machines):** $0.5 \text{ J}$ to $300 \text{ J}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Pendulum Hammer Principle

Both Izod and Charpy tests operate on the **conservation of mechanical energy** principle using a swinging pendulum.

**Step-by-Step Logic Flow:**

1. The pendulum hammer of mass $m$ is raised to an initial height $h_1$ (storing **Potential Energy = $mgh_1$**).
2. The pendulum is released and swings down, striking the specimen at the lowest point of its arc.
3. The specimen absorbs a portion of the kinetic energy and **fractures** (or partially deforms).
4. The pendulum continues to swing upward, rising to a *reduced* final height $h_2$ (after-break height).
5. The energy lost by the pendulum is numerically equal to the energy absorbed by the specimen.

> **Core 'Why' Behind the Method:** Static tensile tests (slow loading) **mask brittle failure** because the material has time to plastically deform. Impact tests force the material to respond in **microseconds**, revealing brittle behaviour that would otherwise be invisible in a slow-pull test.

### 2.2 The 'How' — Operational Sequence in a Rehabilitation Material Lab

1. **Specimen Preparation** → Notch machined per ASTM E23 standards.
2. **Specimen Mounting** → Clamped in anvil (Izod = cantilever; Charpy = horizontal beam on two anvils, $40 \text{ mm}$ span).
3. **Pendulum Release** → Hammer released from latched position.
4. **Energy Reading** → Analog/digital dial shows energy absorbed directly in Joules.
5. **Fracture Surface Analysis** → Visual inspection of ductile vs. brittle fracture morphology.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving numerical problems in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). All values are derived from first principles.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Potential Energy | $E_1 = m g h_1$ | $\text{J}$ | Energy stored in raised hammer |
| 2 | Final Potential Energy | $E_2 = m g h_2$ | $\text{J}$ | Remaining energy after fracture |
| 3 | **Impact Energy Absorbed** | $E_{abs} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$ | $\text{J}$ | Energy consumed in fracturing specimen |
| 4 | Impact Strength (Izod) | $IS_{Izod} = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}}$ | $\text{J/m}^2$ | Energy per unit notch cross-section |
| 5 | Impact Strength (Charpy) | $IS_{Charpy} = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}}$ | $\text{J/m}^2$ | Energy per unit notch cross-section |
| 6 | Net Cross-Sectional Area | $A_{net} = b \times (d - d_{notch})$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Area remaining below notch |
| 7 | Initial Velocity at Impact | $v_1 = \sqrt{2 g h_1}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Hammer velocity at moment of strike |
| 8 | Final Velocity After Strike | $v_2 = \sqrt{2 g h_2}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Residual velocity after fracture |
| 9 | Energy via Kinetic Form | $E_{abs} = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$ | $\text{J}$ | Equivalent kinetic-energy form |
| 10 | Hammer Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | $r$ = pendulum arm length |
| 11 | Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temp. | $DBTT$ from $E_{abs}$ vs. $T$ curve | $^\circ \text{C}$ | Temperature below which material fails brittle |

> **Engineering Utility in Robotic Rehabilitation:** Impact test data feeds directly into **Finite Element Analysis (FEA) of prosthetic sockets and exoskeleton frames**. Designers input the absorbed energy as the failure criterion in crash-simulation software (e.g., ANSYS, ABAQUS) to certify that a device will not catastrophically fail when a patient stumbles.

### 2.4 Why Notches Are Mandatory (Stress Concentration Theory)

A notch is a deliberate stress-raiser. At a sharp V-notch of root radius $\rho$, the theoretical stress concentration factor is:

$$K_t = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{\rho}}$$

where $d$ is the depth below the notch. This forces the crack to initiate at a known, reproducible location, ensuring **test repeatability** — a core KTU lab requirement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Impact Energy from Pendulum Heights

Starting from first principles using the **Work-Energy Theorem**:

**Step 1 — Initial State of the Pendulum:**

The hammer of mass $m$ is raised vertically to height $h_1$. Its gravitational potential energy relative to the impact point is:

$$E_1 = m \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

This is the total mechanical energy available in the system before the strike.

**Step 2 — Conversion to Kinetic Energy at Bottom of Swing:**

By conservation of energy (assuming no air drag during the short swing), the entire potential energy converts to kinetic energy at the bottom of the arc (the impact point):

$$m g h_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2$$

Solving for velocity $v_1$ at the moment of impact:

$$v_1 = \sqrt{2 g h_1}$$

**Step 3 — Strike and Energy Transfer to the Specimen:**

The specimen absorbs energy $E_{abs}$ through elastic/plastic deformation and crack propagation. By conservation of energy:

$$E_{1} = E_{2} + E_{abs}$$

where $E_2$ is the residual energy of the hammer after the strike.

**Step 4 — Kinetic Form of the Absorbed Energy:**

The residual kinetic energy of the hammer after the strike is:

$$E_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 = m g h_2$$

Subtracting:

$$E_{abs} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2 - \dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form (The KTU Exam Form):**

$$E_{abs} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$$

> **Interpretation:** The KTU examiner will explicitly look for the symbolic cancellation that converts $\frac{1}{2} m v_1^2$ to $m g h_1$ using $v_1^2 = 2 g h_1$. Showing this step earns full credit.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Impact Strength (Energy per Unit Area)

The "strength" normalization converts absolute absorbed energy (Joules) to a **material property** (Joules per square metre) that is independent of specimen size.

**Step 1 — Define Net Cross-Section:**

For a rectangular specimen of width $b$ and thickness $d$ with a notch of depth $d_{notch}$:

$$A_{net} = b \cdot (d - d_{notch})$$

**Step 2 — Normalize the Energy:**

$$\text{Impact Strength } I = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the Energy Expression:**

$$I = \dfrac{m g (h_1 - h_2)}{b (d - d_{notch})}$$

**Step 4 — Final SI Units Check:**

$$\text{Units: } \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^2} = \dfrac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}$$

In practice, impact strength is often reported as $\text{J/m}^2$ or $\text{kJ/m}^2$.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

> **Problem:** A Charpy impact test is performed on a titanium-alloy specimen intended for a **robotic exoskeleton hip joint**. The pendulum hammer has mass $m = 22.7 \text{ kg}$ and is released from height $h_1 = 0.61 \text{ m}$. After fracturing the specimen (width $b = 10 \text{ mm}$, thickness $d = 10 \text{ mm}$, notch depth $d_{notch} = 2 \text{ mm}$), the hammer rises to a height $h_2 = 0.41 \text{ m}$. Calculate the absorbed energy and the impact strength. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Absorbed Impact Energy:**

$$E_{abs} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$$

Substituting values:

$$E_{abs} = (22.7) \times (9.81) \times (0.61 - 0.41)$$

$$E_{abs} = 22.7 \times 9.81 \times 0.20$$

$$E_{abs} = 44.54 \text{ J}$$

**Part (b) — Impact Strength:**

Net cross-sectional area:

$$A_{net} = b (d - d_{notch}) = (0.010) \times (0.010 - 0.002)$$

$$A_{net} = 0.010 \times 0.008 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Impact strength:

$$I = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}} = \dfrac{44.54}{8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$I = 5.57 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^2 = 556.75 \text{ kJ/m}^2$$

> **Rehabilitation Engineering Verdict:** A titanium-alloy impact strength of **556.75 kJ/m²** comfortably exceeds the **300 kJ/m²** minimum threshold for load-bearing exoskeleton components. The material is **certified safe** for hip-joint application.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Izod vs. Charpy for Rehab Materials)

| Parameter | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen orientation | Vertical (cantilever) | Horizontal (simply supported) |
| Notch side | Same side as striker | Opposite side to striker |
| Span between supports | N/A (clamped) | $40 \text{ mm}$ |
| Typical energy range | $0.5$–$80 \text{ J}$ | $0.5$–$300 \text{ J}$ |
| Preferred for | Plastics, polymer orthotics | Metals, exoskeleton alloys |
| Sample size required | Smaller | Standardized $10 \times 10 \text{ mm}$ |
| Standerd reference | ASTM D256, ISO 180 | ASTM E23, ISO 148 |
| Rehab use case | Prosthetic socket (PEEK, polypropylene) | Wheelchair frame (aluminum 6061-T6) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Charpy Impact Test Setup

> The following Mermaid block maps the **functional interaction** between the pendulum, specimen, anvil, and energy-measurement system, since a true 3D mechanical drawing cannot be rendered in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Latch Engagement] --> B[Raise Hammer to Initial Height h1]
    B --> C[Store Potential Energy Ep1 = m g h1]
    C --> D[Release Latch Automatically]
    D --> E[Pendulum Swings Down Through Arc]
    E --> F[Striker Edge Contacts V-Notch Specimen]
    F --> G{Specimen Fails?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Specimen Absorbs Energy Eabs]
    G -- No --> I[Specimen Withstands Impact]
    H --> J[Pendulum Rises to Reduced Height h2]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Read Dial: Residual Height h2]
    K --> L[Compute Eabs = m g h1 minus m g h2]
    L --> M[Normalize: I = Eabs divided by A net]
    M --> N[Output: Impact Strength J per m squared]
    N --> O[End: Report to Rehabilitation Design Database]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#548235,color:#ffffff

    style O fill:#404040,color:#ffffff
```

```mermaid
        B1[Horizontal Anvil Supports 40 mm Span] --> B2[V-Notch Opposite Striker]

    B3 --> C1

```

### 4.3 Decision-Making Flowchart — Choosing Impact Test for a Rehab Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Rehab Component Material Selection]) --> Q1{Is the material a polymer or plastic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Choose Izod Test per ASTM D256]

    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Choose Charpy Test per ASTM E23]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the material a composite carbon fiber?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Modified Charpy with Drop-Weight Tower]

    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End

    style Start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
```
