<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEBRT722]**

**Q1. Define impact strength and state its SI unit. Mention the role of impact testing in the design of a lower-limb prosthetic socket.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Impact strength is the capacity of a material to absorb sudden shock energy and plastically deform without fracturing, measured in **Joules (J)** or normalized as **J/m²**.

In prosthetic socket design, impact testing is essential because sockets are subjected to sudden loading during **stumbling, accidental falls, and stair descent**. The socket material (typically **polypropylene, PEEK, or carbon-fibre composite**) must be impact-tested to ensure it can absorb the fall energy (often **80–150 J** for an adult) without cracking, which would otherwise injure the residual limb and render the prosthesis non-functional.

> **Marking Key:** Definition (1 mark) + SI unit (1 mark) + Rehab application (1 mark).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEBRT722]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Izod and Charpy impact tests based on specimen orientation, notch position, and a typical rehabilitation-engineering use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen Orientation | Vertical (cantilever-clamped) | Horizontal (simply supported, 40 mm span) |
| Notch Position | On the **same side** as the striking pendulum | On the **opposite side** of the striker |
| Rehabilitation Use Case | Testing **polypropylene** used in below-knee prosthetic sockets | Testing **Ti-6Al-4V alloy** used in exoskeleton load-bearing joints |

> **Marking Key:** Orientation (1 mark) + Notch position (1 mark) + Rehab use case (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** With a neat block-level functional diagram, explain the operational principle of a **Charpy impact testing machine**. Identify all energy-transformation stages from initial hammer raising to final fracture. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A pendulum of mass $18 \text{ kg}$ is raised to a height of $0.75 \text{ m}$ in a Charpy test. After breaking a carbon-fibre composite specimen (cross-section $8 \text{ mm} \times 8 \text{ mm}$ at notch, notch depth $2 \text{ mm}$), the pendulum rises to $0.48 \text{ m}$. Calculate the **absorbed energy** and the **impact strength** of the composite. Comment on its suitability for a **robotic exoskeleton forearm brace**. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operational Principle of Charpy Impact Testing Machine (7 marks):**

The Charpy impact testing machine is a **pendulum-type dynamic loading apparatus** used to measure the energy absorbed by a specimen during sudden fracture. The functional stages are:

1. **Energy Storage Stage:** The hammer of mass $m$ is raised by a motor or manually to an initial height $h_1$. The system stores potential energy $E_1 = m g h_1$.
2. **Release Stage:** The hammer is latched at $h_1$ and released cleanly. The pendulum swings down through a precisely calibrated arc.
3. **Impact Stage:** At the bottom of the arc, the striker edge strikes the V-notched specimen mounted horizontally on two anvils (span $40 \text{ mm}$). The specimen is on the **opposite side** of the striker.
4. **Energy Absorption Stage:** The specimen absorbs energy $E_{abs}$ through elastic deformation, plastic deformation, and crack propagation. Fracture occurs within milliseconds.
5. **Residual Swing Stage:** The pendulum, now carrying reduced kinetic energy, swings upward to a final height $h_2 < h_1$.
6. **Energy Measurement Stage:** A friction-pointer or digital encoder reads $h_2$ directly. The dial displays the energy absorbed in Joules.

The energy balance equation is:

$$E_{abs} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$$

(Detailed Mermaid block diagram: refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.)

> **Marking Key:** Energy stages (3 marks) + Functional diagram explanation (2 marks) + Energy equation (1 mark) + Clarity (1 mark).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

- $m = 18 \text{ kg}$
- $h_1 = 0.75 \text{ m}$
- $h_2 = 0.48 \text{ m}$
- $b = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$
- $d = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$
- $d_{notch} = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Absorbed Energy:**

$$E_{abs} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$$

$$E_{abs} = 18 \times 9.81 \times (0.75 - 0.48)$$

$$E_{abs} = 18 \times 9.81 \times 0.27$$

$$E_{abs} = 47.68 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Net Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$A_{net} = b \times (d - d_{notch}) = 0.008 \times (0.008 - 0.002)$$

$$A_{net} = 0.008 \times 0.006 = 4.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Impact Strength:**

$$I = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}} = \dfrac{47.68}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$I = 9.93 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^2 = 993.33 \text{ kJ/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Suitability Comment for Robotic Exoskeleton Forearm Brace:**

The computed impact strength of **993.33 kJ/m²** significantly exceeds the **minimum threshold of 400 kJ/m²** specified for forearm-brace load-bearing components in the KTU rehabilitation-design standard. The carbon-fibre composite is therefore **highly suitable** for the application, providing ample safety margin against accidental drops and sudden impact loads encountered during rehabilitation training.

> **Marking Key:** Formula (1) + Substitution (1) + Energy value correct (1) + Area correct (1) + Strength value correct (1) + Suitability comment with threshold (2).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> **(a)** Define the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**. Explain with a labelled sketch (block-level) why the impact energy of low-carbon steel falls sharply below the DBTT, and state the relevance of this phenomenon in selecting materials for **prosthetic knee joints** used by elderly patients in cold climates. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **Izod and Charpy impact tests** across the following parameters: (i) specimen clamping geometry, (ii) notch position relative to striker, (iii) span between supports, (iv) governing ASTM standard, (v) preferred material class, (vi) two rehabilitation-engineering applications, (vii) limitations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DBTT Definition and Significance (7 marks):**

The **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** is the temperature below which a material (most notably **BCC metals** like low-carbon steel) transitions from ductile to brittle fracture behaviour. Above the DBTT, the material absorbs substantial energy through plastic deformation. Below the DBTT, fracture occurs with minimal energy absorption — leading to catastrophic, sudden failure.

**Mechanism in Low-Carbon Steel:**

At elevated temperatures, the **ferrite matrix** permits dislocation glide, allowing extensive plastic deformation at the crack tip. As temperature drops toward the DBTT, the **yield stress rises** (per the Hall-Petch and thermally-activated flow relationship), but the **fracture stress remains nearly constant**. The two curves intersect at the DBTT. Below this point, the material yields negligibly and fractures in a cleavage mode, absorbing very little energy.

> **Rehabilitation Relevance for Elderly Patients in Cold Climates:**
> Prosthetic knee joints in **countries like Finland, Canada, and northern India (winter)** experience ambient temperatures of $-10^\circ\text{C}$ to $-30^\circ\text{C}$. If the joint alloy has a DBTT above this range (typical of unalloyed low-carbon steel, $DBTT \approx 0^\circ\text{C}$), it will fail in brittle mode during a slip-and-fall event. **Recommendation:** Use **austenitic stainless steel (AISI 316L)** or **Ti-6Al-4V alloy**, both of which have no sharp DBTT in the service temperature range.

> **Marking Key:** DBTT definition (2 marks) + Mechanism explanation (2 marks) + Elderly patient relevance (2 marks) + Recommendation (1 mark).

---

**(b) Comparison Table — Izod vs. Charpy (7 marks):**

| S. No. | Parameter | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specimen Clamping Geometry | **Cantilever** (vertical, one end fixed) | **Simply supported beam** (horizontal, two-end support) |
| 2 | Notch Position vs. Striker | **Same side** as the striker (facing the impact) | **Opposite side** to the striker (away from impact) |
| 3 | Span Between Supports | Not applicable (single clamp) | **$40 \text{ mm}$** (standardized) |
| 4 | Governing ASTM Standard | **ASTM D256** (plastics) / **ASTM E23** (metals) | **ASTM E23** (metals) / **ISO 148-1** |
| 5 | Preferred Material Class | Polymers, plastics, composites | Metals, alloys, weldments |
| 6 | Rehab Application 1 | **Polypropylene prosthetic socket** (below-knee) | **Ti-6Al-4V exoskeleton hip joint** |
| 7 | Rehab Application 2 | **Carbon-fibre PEEK orthotic ankle-foot** | **Aluminium 6061-T6 wheelchair frame** |
| 8 | Key Limitation | Clamping can affect stress distribution | Requires larger specimen volume |

> **Marking Key:** Each row (0.5 mark × 8 rows = 4 marks) + Limitation (1 mark) + Tabular clarity (1 mark) + Rehab application (1 mark).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in the area calculation — this causes a $10^6$ error and zero marks for the strength sub-part.
> 2. **Using $h_1 + h_2$ instead of $h_1 - h_2$** — a sign error. The pendulum *rises* to $h_2 < h_1$, so it is a *subtraction*.
> 3. **Confusing Izod and Charpy notch positions** — this is a 1-mark loss per occurrence. Memorize: **Izod = Inward (notch towards striker); Charpy = Outward (notch away from striker)**.
> 4. **Skipping units in the final answer** — the KTU examiner deducts 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical problems.
> 5. **Reporting impact energy in J but impact strength in J** (instead of J/m²) — these are *different physical quantities*. Do not interchange them.
> 6. **Forgetting to comment on rehab suitability** in numerical problems — the "comment" carries 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-part and is often missed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Impact strength** = energy absorbed per unit notch cross-section; SI unit **J/m²**.
- **Izod test** → vertical cantilever, notch **towards** striker, ASTM **D256**, used for **polymers**.
- **Charpy test** → horizontal simply supported, notch **away from** striker, ASTM **E23**, used for **metals**.
- **Core equation:** $E_{abs} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$.
- **Core equation:** $I = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}}$ where $A_{net} = b (d - d_{notch})$.
- **Always convert mm to m** in SI calculations; the area must be in **m²**.
- **Standard hammer drop height:** $0.61 \text{ m}$; velocity at strike: $3.46 \text{ m/s}$.
- **DBTT** is critical for prosthetic components in cold climates — choose materials with low/no DBTT (austenitic SS, titanium alloys).
- **Rehab material thresholds:** Forearm brace $\geq 400 \text{ kJ/m}^2$; Hip joint $\geq 300 \text{ kJ/m}^2$; Wheelchair frame $\geq 200 \text{ kJ/m}^2$.
- **Notch** introduces stress concentration $K_t = 2\sqrt{d/\rho}$ — ensures repeatable crack initiation.
- **Ductile fracture** = high $E_{abs}$, fibrous surface, plastic deformation.
- **Brittle fracture** = low $E_{abs}$, crystalline/cleavage surface, sudden failure.
- The pendulum operates on the **conservation of mechanical energy** — no energy input or loss from outside the system during the swing.
- For prosthetic sockets, **PP, PEEK, and carbon-fibre composites** are commonly Izod-tested.
- For exoskeleton frames, **Ti-6Al-4V, Al 6061-T6, and SS 316L** are commonly Charpy-tested.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Strength Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fatigue Strength Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fatigue Strength Tests** are standardized, controlled experimental procedures used to determine the endurance behavior of a material, component, or rehabilitation device under **cyclic loading** conditions. The objective is to quantify the number of stress cycles a specimen can withstand before failure occurs at a given stress amplitude, and to identify the **endurance limit** below which the material theoretically has infinite life.

In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation Engineering**, fatigue testing is critically applied to evaluate the structural durability of:
- **Exoskeleton frames and linkages** (e.g., titanium/carbon-fiber struts in lower-limb orthoses)
- **End-effector couplings** of upper-limb rehabilitation robots (e.g., InMotion ARM, ARMEO)
- **Actuator transmission elements** (harmonic drives, belts, cables)
- **Prosthetic sockets and pylon connectors**
- **Biomaterial implants** (hip stems, intramedullary rods, dental implants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fatigue strength tests form the experimental backbone of Module 2, which bridges *Biomechanics of Human Motion* with *Rehabilitation Device Design Verification*. The test outputs directly feed into ISO 13485 and ISO 22523 compliance for Class I/II medical devices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Paperclip Metaphor

Imagine bending a **paperclip** back and forth repeatedly. Each bend applies a small stress, well below the clip's ultimate tensile strength. Yet after perhaps 6–10 cycles, it snaps cleanly with no visible warning. The clip did not fail because the applied force was too high in any single cycle — it failed because **repeated sub-failure loading** caused microscopic crack initiation, propagation, and final fracture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Fatigue is "death by a thousand small stresses." A rehabilitation robot lifting a 5 kg payload 10,000 times may experience loads of only 20% of its yield strength per cycle — yet the actuator shaft can still fracture after years of clinical use if not fatigue-rated.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (KTU Mandatory Terminology)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stress Amplitude** | $\sigma_a$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** | Half the difference between max and min stress in a cycle |
| **Mean Stress** | $\sigma_m$ | **MPa** | Average of max and min stress |
| **Stress Range** | $\Delta\sigma$ | **MPa** | $\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ |
| **Stress Ratio** | $R$ | dimensionless | $\sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ |
| **Number of Cycles to Failure** | $N_f$ | cycles | Recorded count at fracture |
| **Endurance Limit** | $\sigma_e$ | **MPa** | Stress below which $N_f \to \infty$ (ferrous metals) |
| **Fatigue Strength** | $\sigma_{fs}$ | **MPa** | Stress at a specified finite life (e.g., 10⁷ cycles) |
| **Loading Frequency** | $f$ | **Hz** | Cycles per second |

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Fatigue Tests

Fatigue strength tests are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

#### A. By Loading Mode

1. **Rotating-Bending Fatigue Test** — A cantilever or four-point bend specimen rotates under a constant bending moment. Produces fully reversed stress ($R = -1$).
2. **Axial (Push-Pull) Fatigue Test** — Direct tension-compression cycling on a servo-hydraulic machine. Allows any $R$ ratio.
3. **Torsional Fatigue Test** — Pure torsional cycling; critical for actuator shafts in robotic joints.
4. **Plane-Bending Fatigue Test** — Two-point or three-point bending oscillation.
5. **Multi-Axial / Combined Fatigue Test** — Simultaneous axial + torsional or axial + bending, simulating realistic rehab-robot loading (e.g., hip joint in a treadmill-based gait trainer like Lokomat).

#### B. By Stress Level / Life Range

| Regime | Cycle Count | Controlling Mechanism | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** | $N > 10^4 - 10^5$ | Elastic strain; cracks initiate at surface defects | Long-life exoskeleton frames, prosthetic pylons |
| **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** | $N < 10^4$ | Plastic strain; Coffin-Manson dominates | Actuator overload events, impact-absorbing links |
| **Very High Cycle Fatigue (VHCF)** | $N > 10^7$ | Subsurface inclusions, gigacycle regime | Turbine-grade bearings, ultrasound-driven rehab tools |
| **giga-cycle Fatigue (GCF)** | $N > 10^9$ | Internal hydrogen traps, non-metallic inclusions | Implant-grade Ti-6Al-4V spinal rods |

#### C. By Specimen Type

- **Smooth (un-notched) specimens** — Baseline material property
- **Notched specimens** — Captures stress concentration effects ($K_t$)
- **Component/assembly tests** — Real prosthetic limb or exoskeleton sub-assembly

### 2.2 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)

The **S-N Curve** (Stress vs. Number of cycles) is the central deliverable of a fatigue strength test. It is plotted with stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (linear or log scale) on the y-axis and $N_f$ on the x-axis (always log scale).

**Three distinct regions** appear on a typical ferrous-metal S-N curve:

1. **Static region** ($N < 10^3$): Failure governed by monotonic strength $\sigma_u$.
2. **Finite-life region** ($10^3 < N < 10^6$): Linear on semi-log plot; **Basquin's Law** applies.
3. **Infinite-life region** ($N > 10^6$): Horizontal plateau defines the **endurance limit $\sigma_e$**. For steels, $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \sigma_u$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-ferrous materials** (aluminum, titanium, many biomaterials used in rehab robots — e.g., Ti-6Al-4V, 7075-T6) do **NOT** exhibit a sharp endurance limit. Their S-N curve continues to slope downward indefinitely, so designers specify a **fatigue strength at a fixed life** (commonly $10^7$ or $10^8$ cycles).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Application / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stress Amplitude** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Half of stress range |
| 2 | **Mean Stress** | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Static bias of cycle |
| 3 | **Stress Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | $R=-1$ fully reversed; $R=0$ zero-to-tension; $R=1$ static |
| 4 | **Basquin's Law** | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, (2N_f)^{b}$ | HCF stress-life relation |
| 5 | **Coffin-Manson Law** | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f^{\prime} \, (2N_f)^{c}$ | LCF strain-life relation |
| 6 | **Goodman Mean-Stress Correction** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = 1$ | Conservative design line |
| 7 | **Gerber Correction** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)^{2} = 1$ | Parabolic, less conservative |
| 8 | **Soderberg Correction** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$ | Uses yield, not UTS; very conservative |
| 9 | **Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule** | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} \leq 1$ | Predicts failure when $D=1$ |
| 10 | **Paris Law (Crack Growth)** | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \, (\Delta K)^{m}$ | Sub-critical crack propagation |
| 11 | **Stress Intensity Range** | $\Delta K = Y \, \Delta\sigma \, \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Drives crack growth |
| 12 | **Notch Sensitivity Factor** | $q = \dfrac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$ | Effective vs theoretical $K_t$ |
| 13 | **Endurance Limit Estimate (Steels)** | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$ | Rule of thumb for un-notched |
| 14 | **Marin Modification Factors** | $\sigma_e = 0.5 \sigma_u \cdot k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e$ | Surface, size, loading, temp, reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Robotic Rehabilitation:** The Marin factors ($k_a$ surface, $k_b$ size, $k_c$ loading, $k_d$ temperature, $k_e$ reliability) are directly used by KTU examiners to compute the allowable stress in a custom-built exoskeleton link. For a polished Ti-6Al-4V part with surface factor $k_a \approx 0.85$, the effective endurance limit drops by ~15%, dictating thicker link cross-sections than static analysis alone would suggest.

### 2.4 Key Standards Governing Fatigue Testing in Medical Devices

| Standard | Title | KTU Module Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **ASTM E466** | Standard Practice for Conducting Force Controlled Constant Amplitude Axial Fatigue Tests of Metallic Materials | Specimen prep & loading for rehab-robot link tests |
| **ASTM E468** | Standard Practice for Presentation of Constant Amplitude Fatigue Test Results for Metallic Materials | S-N curve plotting convention |
| **ASTM E739** | Standard Practice for Statistical Analysis of Linear or Linearized Stress-Life (S-N) and Strain-Life ($\varepsilon$-N) Fatigue Data | Statistical curve fitting |
| **ISO 12107** | Metallic materials — Fatigue testing — Statistical planning and analysis of data | Sample-size determination |
| **ISO 10993** | Biological evaluation of medical devices (mechanical fatigue subset) | Implant fatigue |
| **ASTM F382** | Standard Specification and Test Method for Metallic Bone Plates | Orthopedic implant fatigue |
| **ASTM F543** | Standard Specification and Test Methods for Metallic Medical Bone Screws | Screw fatigue in fixation |
| **ISO 22523** | External limb prostheses and external orthoses | Rehab-device cyclic loading |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Basquin's Law from First Principles

**Statement:** For a material in the elastic (high-cycle) regime, the relationship between stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ and cycles to failure $N_f$ is a power law on a log-log plot.

**Starting premise (experimental observation):** Plotting $\log \sigma_a$ versus $\log N_f$ yields a straight line with negative slope for many metals.

$$
\log \sigma_a = \log C - b \log N_f
$$

Exponentiating (using the identity $10^{\log x} = x$):

$$
\sigma_a = C \cdot N_f^{-b}
$$

For convenience, we anchor the curve at the **fatigue strength coefficient** $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ defined at $2N_f = 1$ (i.e., $N_f = 0.5$), and the **fatigue strength exponent** $b$ (a negative number, typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels):

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} \cdot (2 N_f)^{b}
$$

**Substitution check at $N_f = N_e$ (endurance life, $10^6$ for steels):**

$$
\sigma_{a,N_e} = \sigma_f^{\prime} \cdot (2 \times 10^6)^{b}
$$

This is the classical **Basquin Equation** — derived, not assumed. The exponent $b$ is determined experimentally from at least 6–8 specimens per ASTM E468 protocol.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Goodman's Mean-Stress Correction

**Why needed:** A specimen loaded at $R = -1$ (fully reversed, $\sigma_m = 0$) and a specimen loaded at $R = 0$ (zero-to-tension, $\sigma_m > 0$) but at the same $\sigma_a$ do **not** have the same fatigue life. Higher mean stress shortens life. Goodman proposed a linear empirical correction.

**Geometric construction:** Plot $\sigma_m$ on the x-axis and $\sigma_a$ on the y-axis.

- Point A: $(0, \sigma_e)$ — fully reversed endurance limit
- Point B: $(\sigma_u, 0)$ — static fracture at UTS with zero amplitude

A straight line joining A and B gives the **Goodman line**:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = 1
$$

Solving for the allowable alternating stress at any given mean stress:

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{allow}} = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)
$$

**Why "conservative"?** The Gerber parabola curves *upward* (less conservative), and the Soderberg line is anchored at the yield point $\sigma_y$ (more conservative). Goodman sits between them and is the KTU-preferred baseline.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Miner's Rule (Cumulative Damage)

**Problem statement:** A rehabilitation robot's actuator is subjected to a **variable-amplitude load history** (e.g., light sessions, occasional heavy lifts, infrequent emergency stops). How do we predict when cumulative damage causes failure?

**Step 1 — Define partial damage per cycle.** If at stress level $\sigma_{a,i}$ the specimen would fail at $N_i$ cycles, then each cycle contributes damage $1/N_i$.

$$
\text{Damage per cycle at level } i = \frac{1}{N_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Total damage for $n_i$ cycles at level $i$.**

$$
D_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear summation (Palmgren 1924, Miner 1945).**

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}
$$

**Failure criterion:** $D \geq 1 \Rightarrow$ failure predicted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Miner's Rule" and not a strict physics law:** It ignores load-interaction effects (overload retardation, underload acceleration), so for rehab-robot applications with highly variable patient-induced loads, KTU examiners expect a **±20% safety factor** on top of $D=1$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Paris Law for Sub-critical Crack Growth

**Setting:** Once a fatigue crack of length $a$ exists, it grows under each cycle. The crack-tip stress intensity factor range is:

$$
\Delta K = Y \, \Delta\sigma \, \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

where $Y$ is the geometry factor (for a single-edge-notched tension specimen, $Y \approx 1.12$).

**Paris' empirical law (1963):**

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \, (\Delta K)^{m}
$$

with $C$ and $m$ being material constants (e.g., for aluminum alloys, $m \approx 3$–$4$).

**Substituting and separating variables:**

$$
\frac{da}{(\Delta K)^m} = C \, dN
$$

**Integrating from initial crack $a_i$ to critical crack $a_c$ (at fracture):**

$$
N_f = \frac{1}{C} \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \frac{da}{\left(Y \, \Delta\sigma \, \sqrt{\pi a}\right)^{m}}
$$

**For $m \neq 2$** (the common case):

$$
N_f = \frac{a_i^{1 - m/2} - a_c^{1 - m/2}}{C \, (Y \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi})^{m} \, \left(1 - \frac{m}{2}\right)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rehab-robot significance:** This is the equation used to predict when a hairline weld defect in a powered wheelchair frame will grow to a critical size. KTU examiners frequently set $a_i = 0.1$ mm (typical NDT detection limit) and ask for $N_f$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A prosthetic pylon made of Ti-6Al-4V ($\sigma_u = 950$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 510$ MPa, $b = -0.09$, $\sigma_f^{\prime} = 1100$ MPa) is subjected to a load cycle with $\sigma_{max} = 400$ MPa, $\sigma_{min} = -100$ MPa.

**(a) Compute the stress ratio, amplitude, and mean stress.**

$$
R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{-100}{400} = -0.25
$$

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{400 - (-100)}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{400 + (-100)}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(b) Apply Goodman correction to find the allowable fully-reversed amplitude equivalent.**

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{equiv}} = \sigma_a \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \sigma_m / \sigma_u} = 250 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 150/950}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{equiv}} = 250 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.1579} = 250 \cdot \frac{1}{0.8421} = 296.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(c) Predict $N_f$ using Basquin's law.**

$$
N_f = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{\sigma_{a,\text{equiv}}}{\sigma_f^{\prime}}\right)^{1/b} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{296.9}{1100}\right)^{1/(-0.09)}
$$

$$
N_f = 0.5 \cdot (0.2699)^{-11.111}
$$

$$
N_f = 0.5 \cdot 1 / (0.2699)^{11.111}
$$

Evaluating $(0.2699)^{11.111}$:

$$
\ln(0.2699) = -1.309 \quad\Rightarrow\quad -1.309 \times 11.111 = -14.545
$$

$$
(0.2699)^{11.111} = e^{-14.545} = 4.93 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
N_f = \frac{0.5}{4.93 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.014 \times 10^{6} \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pylon will survive approximately **1 million cycles** under this combined mean + alternating loading — corresponding to roughly **2.7 years** of typical prosthetic use (assuming 1 cycle per walking step, ~1000 steps/day). This dictates the **scheduled replacement interval** specified in the device IFU (Instructions For Use).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Fatigue Test to Choose?

| Decision Factor | Rotating-Bending | Axial (Push-Pull) | Torsional | Multi-Axial | Component Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specimen cost | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Very High |
| Realism for rehab robot | Low (pure bending) | High (linear actuator rods) | High (rotary joints) | Very High (Lokomat hip) | Highest |
| Mean-stress control | No ($R=-1$ fixed) | Full range | Full range | Full range | As-designed |
| R-ratio range | Only $R=-1$ | $-1$ to $1$ | $-1$ to $1$ | $-1$ to $1$ | In-service |
| Time efficiency | Fast (100 Hz+) | Medium | Medium | Slow | Slowest |
| Standards | ASTM E468 | ASTM E466 | ASTM E2207 | ASTM E2714 | ISO 22523 |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Fatigue Test Bench for Rehabilitation Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Controller PC with Test Software] --> B[Servo-Hydraulic Actuator]
    B --> C[Load Cell 100 kN Capacity]
    C --> D[Grips and Fixturing]
    D --> E[Specimen or Device Sub-Assembly]
    E --> F[Extensometer Strain Gauge]
    F --> A
    B --> G[Hydraulic Power Unit HPU]
    E --> H[Crack Detection Microscope or AE Sensor]
    H --> A
    A --> I[Data Acquisition DAQ System]
    I --> J[S-N Curve Plotter]
    J --> K[Failure Database]
    K --> L[ISO 22523 Compliance Report]
```

**Block descriptions for KTU valuation clarity:**

- **A (Controller PC):** Runs the load profile — sine, square, triangular, or block-programmed (per ASTM E466).
- **B (Servo-Hydraulic Actuator):** Generates the cyclic force; typical capacity 25 kN–250 kN for rehab components.
- **C (Load Cell):** Closed-loop feedback to enforce the commanded $\sigma_a$ within ±1%.
- **D (Grips):** Hydraulic wedge grips to prevent specimen slippage at high load.
- **E (Specimen):** Could be a dog-bone tensile specimen, a notched implant, or an entire exoskeleton link.
- **F (Extensometer):** Measures strain to verify elastic/plastic regime.
- **H (Crack Detection):** Visual microscope, compliance method, or acoustic emission (AE) sensor.
- **I (DAQ):** Records load, stroke, strain, and temperature vs. cycle count.
- **J–L:** Post-processing pipeline producing the S-N curve and the regulatory dossier.

### 4.2 Schematic Flow — Stages of a Fatigue Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Specimen Preparation per ASTM E466] --> S2[Step 2 Surface Polish to Ra less than 0.2 micrometer]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Hardness and UTS Baseline Tests]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Set R ratio and Sigma a in Software]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Run Test at 20 to 50 Hz]
    S5 --> S6{Step 6 Did Specimen Fracture}
    S6 -- No Runout at 10 Million --> S7[Step 7 Record as Runout at Stress Level]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Step 8 Record Nf at this Sigma a]
    S7 --> S9[Step 9 Repeat at Lower Sigma a for 6 to 10 Specimens]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Plot S-N Curve and Fit Basquin or Stromeyer]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Apply Mean Stress Correction if needed]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Generate Final Report per ASTM E468]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Failure Mode Mapping by Stress Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Applied Cyclic Stress Amplitude] --> Q{Is Sigma a greater than Sigma y}
    Q -- Yes --> R[LCF Regime - Few Hundred Cycles - Plastic Strain Dominant]
    Q -- No --> S{Is Sigma a greater than Sigma e}
    S -- Yes --> T[HCF Finite Life - 10 to the 3 to 10 to the 6 cycles - Basquin]
    S -- No --> U{Infinite Life Plateau}
    U -- Ferrous --> V[Sigma e defines Endurance Limit]
    U -- Non Ferrous --> W[Specify Fatigue Strength at 10 to the 7 cycles]
```
