topics.title: Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering, Telecommunications, and Web Accessibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering, Telecommunications, and Web Accessibility ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering **Formal Definition:** Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering refer to the systematic integration of hardware, software, and computational methodologies to assess, monitor, train, and assist individuals with physical, cognitive, sensory, or communication impairments. The objective is to restore, substitute, or augment functional capabilities using digital systems. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology — KTU 2024 Syllabus** > - **Rehabilitation Engineering (RE):** The branch of biomedical engineering that applies engineering principles to design assistive devices and therapeutic systems for persons with disabilities. > - **Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADL):** Computer-controlled systems that enable independent control of household appliances through switches, voice, or biosignals. > - **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC):** A clinical sub-domain using computer-based systems (text-to-speech, picture boards, eye-tracking) to supplement or replace natural speech. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scope of Computer Applications in RE (KTU Module 2):** > 1. **Patient Assessment Software** — Quantitative measurement of range of motion (ROM), gait, balance, and cognitive load. > 2. **Therapy Software** — Computer-aided biofeedback, virtual reality exposure, and gamified rehabilitation. > 3. **Electronic Aids** — AAC devices, environmental control units (ECUs), and powered wheelchair controllers. > 4. **Clinical Records** — Electronic Medical Records (EMR) and Rehabilitation Information Systems. **Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Clinic":** Imagine a hospital where every patient is connected like an IoT device. A stroke survivor wears motion sensors → the data is fed to a computer that runs AI algorithms to detect deviations from healthy movement → a virtual avatar on the screen gives real-time biofeedback → progress is uploaded to a cloud dashboard that the therapist monitors from home. This is exactly what **Computer Applications in Rehabilitation Engineering** achieves — replacing subjective paper-based therapy with measurable, repeatable, data-driven rehabilitation. --- ### 1.2 Telecommunications in Rehabilitation (Telerehabilitation) **Formal Definition:** Telerehabilitation (TR) is the delivery of rehabilitation services over telecommunication networks, enabling remote assessment, intervention, supervision, and education between clinicians and patients separated by distance. > [!NOTE] > **Telehealth vs. Telemedicine vs. Telerehabilitation** > - **Telehealth:** An umbrella term covering all remote healthcare services (clinical + non-clinical). > - **Telemedicine:** Remote *clinical* services by a physician (diagnosis, prescription). > - **Telerehabilitation:** A *sub-discipline* of telehealth, focused on therapy delivery (physical, occupational, speech). **Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor in Your Phone":** Just as banking moved from physical branches to mobile apps, healthcare is migrating from clinics to cloud platforms. A patient in a rural Kerala village with post-stroke paralysis can now receive guided physiotherapy sessions over a 4G/5G video link, with sensors transmitting joint angles in real-time to a therapist in Thiruvananthapuram. **Telecommunications is the digital highway** that makes this possible. **Two Operational Modes of Telerehabilitation:** | Mode | Description | Example | Latency Requirement | |------|-------------|---------|---------------------| | Synchronous | Real-time interaction between patient and therapist | Live video physiotherapy session | Low (\textless 150 ms) | | Asynchronous ("Store-and-Forward") | Data recorded, sent, reviewed later | Patient uploads gait video, therapist replies next day | High latency tolerable | | Hybrid | Combination of both | Live session + recorded home exercise review | Variable | --- ### 1.3 Web Accessibility **Formal Definition:** Web Accessibility means designing and developing websites, applications, and digital content so that **people with disabilities can perceive, understand, navigate, and interact** with them effectively — and so that the content is equally accessible to all users, including those using assistive technologies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Governing Standard:** The **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1**, published by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)**, is the international reference standard. WCAG 2.2 (released October 2023) is the most current iteration. > **Legal Backing in India:** The **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act)** and the **GIGW (Guidelines for Indian Government Websites)** mandate accessibility compliance for public digital services. **Conceptual Analogy — "The Curb Cut Principle":** A *curb cut* in a sidewalk was originally built for wheelchair users. But it also helps parents with strollers, delivery workers with trolleys, and travelers with luggage. **Web accessibility follows the same logic:** When we design for disability (vision, hearing, motor, cognitive), we create a better experience for *everyone* — including elderly users, those on slow networks, and people using smartwatches. This is known as the **Curb Cut Effect** or **Universal Design**. **The Four POUR Principles of WCAG:** > [!NOTE] > **POUR — The Heart of WCAG 2.1 / 2.2** > - **P — Perceivable:** Information must be presentable in ways users can perceive (alt-text for images, captions for videos, sufficient color contrast). > - **O — Operable:** Interface components and navigation must be operable (keyboard accessibility, no seizure-inducing flashes, sufficient time limits). > - **U — Understandable:** Information and UI operation must be understandable (readable text, predictable behavior, input assistance). > - **R — Robust:** Content must be robust enough to be interpreted reliably by a wide range of user agents, including assistive technologies (valid markup, ARIA labels). **Conformance Levels:** | Level | Meaning | Typical Use Case | |-------|---------|------------------| | A | Minimum essential — removes major barriers | Basic legal compliance | | AA | Standard legal requirement (recommended global target) | Government, education, healthcare sites | | AAA | Gold standard — enhanced accessibility | Specialized applications | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Computer Applications in Rehabilitation **A. Computer-Based Assessment Systems** The workflow consists of five functional blocks: 1. **Data Acquisition:** Sensors (encoders, IMUs, EMG electrodes, pressure mats) capture kinematic, kinetic, and physiological signals. 2. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering (band-pass $0.5 - 500$ Hz for EMG), and analog-to-digital conversion (sampling rate $\geq 2 \times f_{max}$ per **Nyquist criterion**). 3. **Feature Extraction:** Algorithms compute clinically meaningful parameters — e.g., RMS amplitude, mean frequency, joint angles, step length. 4. **Decision Support:** Rule-based or AI-based classifiers interpret data and flag abnormalities. 5. **Reporting:** Visual dashboards (graphs, ROM charts) for the clinician. **B. Computer-Based Training Systems (Virtual Reality Rehabilitation)** VR systems create **immersive, gamified, task-oriented** environments that promote **neuroplasticity** through repetition, feedback, and motivation. Architecture: Head-Mounted Display (HMD) + Motion Tracker + Haptic Glove + Rendering Engine (Unity/Unreal). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why VR Works in Rehabilitation (Neuroscience Basis):** > Motor learning follows the principle of **task-specificity** and **feedback-driven adaptation**. VR provides augmented feedback (visual, auditory, haptic) at a frequency and intensity impossible in conventional therapy, accelerating cortical reorganization after stroke or spinal cord injury. **C. Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) Devices** | AAC Type | Method | Example | |----------|--------|---------| | No-Tech | Gestures, picture boards | Communication book | | Low-Tech | Paper-based, alphabet boards | Eye-gaze board | | Mid-Tech | Simple speech-output devices | Big Mack switch | | High-Tech | Computer/screen-based | Tobii Dynavox, iPad with Proloquo2Go | **High-tech AAC components:** Input device (touchscreen, switch, eye-tracker) → Communication software (symbol-based or text-based) → Output device (synthesized voice, display). --- ### 2.2 Telecommunications Theory for Telerehabilitation **A. Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Channel Capacity)** The maximum data rate achievable over a noisy communication channel: $$ C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) $$ Where: - $C$ = Channel capacity in bits per second (bps) - $B$ = Bandwidth in Hertz (Hz) - $S$ = Signal power (Watts) - $N$ = Noise power (Watts) - $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), often expressed in decibels as $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ **B. Telerehabilitation Network Requirements** | Parameter | Typical Value | Reason | |-----------|---------------|--------| | Bandwidth | $\geq 1$ Mbps (SD video) | Smooth video for synchronous TR | | Latency | $\textless 150$ ms | Real-time biofeedback requires minimal delay | | Jitter | $\textless 30$ ms | Avoids choppy video/audio | | Packet Loss | $\textless 1\%$ | Maintains clinical fidelity | | Security | End-to-end encryption (AES-256) | HIPAA/RPwD Act patient data protection | **C. Bit Error Rate (BER)** $$ \text{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total number of bits transmitted}} $$ For telerehabilitation, target $\text{BER} \leq 10^{-5}$ for motion control signals; $\leq 10^{-3}$ acceptable for video. **D. Wearable Sensor Data Rate (Nyquist Sampling)** $$ f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} $$ For EMG (max frequency $\approx 500$ Hz), minimum $f_s = 1000$ Hz. For ECG (max frequency $\approx 150$ Hz), minimum $f_s = 300$ Hz. --- ### 2.3 Web Accessibility — Mathematical Foundations **A. Relative Luminance (Per WCAG 2.x)** For an sRGB color component $C$ (normalized to $[0,1]$): $$ C_{\text{linear}} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{C_{\text{sRGB}}}{12.92} & \text{if } C_{\text{sRGB}} \leq 0.03928 \\ \left(\dfrac{C_{\text{sRGB}} + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} & \text{if } C_{\text{sRGB}} > 0.03928 \end{cases} $$ Then total relative luminance: $$ L = 0.2126 \cdot R_{\text{linear}} + 0.7152 \cdot G_{\text{linear}} + 0.0722 \cdot B_{\text{linear}} $$ **B. Contrast Ratio** $$ \text{CR} = \dfrac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05} $$ Where $L_1$ is the lighter luminance and $L_2$ is the darker luminance ($\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ chosen such that $\text{CR} \geq 1$). **WCAG Contrast Requirements:** | Level | Normal Text | Large Text (≥18 pt or 14 pt bold) | |-------|-------------|----------------------------------| | AA | $\text{CR} \geq 4.5 : 1$ | $\text{CR} \geq 3 : 1$ | | AAA | $\text{CR} \geq 7 : 1$ | $\text{CR} \geq 4.5 : 1$ | **C. Accessible Color Contrast — Quick Reference Table** > [!NOTE] > **Compliance Examples (Contrast Ratios):** > - Black on White: **21 : 1** (AAA) — Perfect > - #777777 on White: **4.48 : 1** — Fails AA for normal text > - #595959 on White: **7.01 : 1** — Passes AAA --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference) | Formula / Concept | Expression | Domain | Application in RE | |-------------------|------------|--------|------------------| | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Telecommunications | Max TR video bitrate | | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Signal Acquisition | EMG/ECG sensor sampling | | Bit Error Rate | $\text{BER} = N_e / N_t$ | Communications | Telemetry reliability | | Decibel Conversion | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | Telecommunications | Signal quality metric | | Relative Luminance | $L = 0.2126 R + 0.7152 G + 0.0722 B$ | Web Accessibility | WCAG contrast calc | | Contrast Ratio | $\text{CR} = (L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05)$ | Web Accessibility | Text legibility check | | Fitts's Law (Pointer Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ | HCI / Web | Accessible target sizing | | Stroke Volume Index | $\text{SVI} = \text{SV} / \text{BSA}$ | Cardiopulmonary RE | Exercise tolerance | | Range of Motion (ROM) | $\text{ROM} = \theta_{max} - \theta_{min}$ | Physical RE | Joint flexibility | | Heart Rate Reserve | $\text{HRR} = \text{HR}_{max} - \text{HR}_{rest}$ | Cardiac RE | Exercise prescription | **Fitts's Law** is particularly relevant for designing accessible buttons and touch targets. WCAG 2.5.5 (AAA) specifies a minimum target size of $44 \times 44$ CSS pixels, derived empirically from Fitts's Law usability studies. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hospitals:** Computerized patient records integrated with wearable sensors reduce documentation time by ~30%. - **Rural India:** Telerehabilitation via 4G/5G reaches patients in low-resource settings; ASHA workers operate mobile rehab units. - **Education:** Accessible KTU e-learning platforms (KTU Global) must comply with GIGW and WCAG 2.1 AA. - **Smart Cities:** Public kiosks and digital signage require accessibility for inclusive citizen services. - **Assistive Tech Industry:** Indian assistive tech market is projected to grow significantly, with strong demand for accessible digital infrastructure. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Channel Capacity for a Telerehabilitation Video Link **Problem:** A telerehabilitation synchronous video link uses a 4G LTE channel of bandwidth $B = 5$ MHz. The received signal power is $S = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ W, and the noise power is $N = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ W. Compute the maximum data rate (channel capacity) and the SNR in dB. **Step 1 — Identify parameters:** $B = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Hz $S = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ W $N = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ W **Step 2 — Compute SNR (linear):** $$ \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-7}} = 40 $$ **Step 3 — Convert SNR to decibels:** $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(40) $$ Computing the logarithm: $\log_{10}(40) = \log_{10}(4 \times 10) = \log_{10}(4) + \log_{10}(10) = 0.602 + 1 = 1.602$ $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 1.602 = 16.02 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem:** $$ C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) = 5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 40) = 5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(41) $$ **Step 5 — Convert $\log_2(41)$ to base 10 for computation:** $$ \log_2(41) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(41)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{1.6128}{0.3010} \approx 5.358 $$ **Step 6 — Compute channel capacity:** $$ C = 5 \times 10^{6} \times 5.358 = 26.79 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \approx 26.79 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Result:** The maximum theoretical data rate is **$C \approx 26.79$ Mbps**, more than sufficient for a synchronous SD video TR session (1–2 Mbps needed) with headroom for EMG/audio streams. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: WCAG Contrast Ratio Calculation **Problem:** Verify whether white text ($L_1$) on a green background (hex `#007A3D`, $L_2$) passes WCAG 2.1 AA for normal text. Required: $\text{CR} \geq 4.5 : 1$. **Step 1 — Convert white to sRGB linear values:** White: $R = G = B = 1.0$ (in sRGB, normalized). Since $> 0.03928$: $$ R_{\text{linear}} = \left(\dfrac{1.0 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = (1.0)^{2.4} = 1.0 $$ Similarly, $G_{\text{linear}} = B_{\text{linear}} = 1.0$. **Step 2 — Compute white luminance:** $$ L_1 = 0.2126(1.0) + 0.7152(1.0) + 0.0722(1.0) = 1.0 $$ **Step 3 — Convert `#007A3D` to sRGB normalized values:** - $R = 0/255 = 0.0$ - $G = 122/255 = 0.4784$ - $B = 61/255 = 0.2392$ **Step 4 — Apply gamma decoding for each channel:** - For $R = 0.0 \leq 0.03928$: $R_{\text{linear}} = 0.0 / 12.92 = 0.0$ - For $G = 0.4784 > 0.03928$: $$ G_{\text{linear}} = \left(\dfrac{0.4784 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = \left(\dfrac{0.5334}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = (0.5056)^{2.4} $$ Computing: $0.5056^{2.4}$. Take natural log: $2.4 \ln(0.5056) = 2.4 \times (-0.6820) = -1.6368$. Then $e^{-1.6368} = 0.1943$. $$ G_{\text{linear}} \approx 0.1943 $$ - For $B = 0.2392 > 0.03928$: $$ B_{\text{linear}} = \left(\dfrac{0.2392 + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = \left(\dfrac{0.2942}{1.055}\right)^{2.4} = (0.2789)^{2.4} $$ Computing: $2.4 \ln(0.2789) = 2.4 \times (-1.277) = -3.0648$. Then $e^{-3.0648} = 0.0466$. $$ B_{\text{linear}} \approx 0.0466 $$ **Step 5 — Compute luminance of green:** $$ L_2 = 0.2126(0) + 0.7152(0.1943) + 0.0722(0.0466) $$ $$ L_2 = 0 + 0.13896 + 0.003365 = 0.1423 $$ **Step 6 — Compute Contrast Ratio:** $$ \text{CR} = \dfrac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05} = \dfrac{1.0 + 0.05}{0.1423 + 0.05} = \dfrac{1.05}{0.1923} = 5.46 $$ **Result:** $\text{CR} \approx 5.46 : 1 \geq 4.5 : 1$ → **PASSES WCAG 2.1 AA** for normal text. ✅ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sampling Rate for EMG in a Computer-Based Rehab System **Problem:** An EMG signal in a computer-aided rehabilitation system has a maximum meaningful frequency component of $f_{max} = 450$ Hz. Determine the minimum sampling rate $f_s$ required to avoid aliasing. **Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:** $$ f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$ f_s \geq 2 \times 450 = 900 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 3 — Engineering practice (oversampling factor of 2.5 to 5×):** A safety margin is used to account for anti-aliasing filter roll-off. Choosing $f_s = 2000$ Hz (oversampling factor = $2000/900 \approx 2.22\times$) is common. **Result:** Minimum theoretical $f_s = 900$ Hz; practical choice **$f_s = 1000 - 2000$ Hz** for EMG in rehab engineering applications. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Computer Applications in RE Use Cases | Clinical Population | Computer Application | Specific Technology | Functional Outcome | |---------------------|----------------------|---------------------|--------------------| | Stroke survivors | Motor recovery VR | Nintendo Wii, Saebo VR, MindMaze | Upper-limb dexterity | | Spinal cord injury | FES + biofeedback | Parastep, RT300 | Gait restoration | | Cerebral palsy | AAC + powered mobility | Tobii eye-tracker, Permobil wheelchair | Communication + mobility | | Autism | Social skills training | Software apps (proloquo), VR exposure | Social interaction | | Amputees | Prosthetic control | Myoelectric pattern recognition | Functional grasp | | Elderly with dementia | Cognitive training | BrainHQ, constant therapy | Memory/cognition maintenance | | Hearing impaired | Telecommunications | Video relay service (VRS), captioned telephony | Real-time communication | | Visually impaired | Web accessibility | NVDA, JAWS, screen magnifier | Digital independence | --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Accessibility Features vs. POUR Principles | WCAG Principle | Functional Need | Web Feature | Assistive Tech Used | |----------------|-----------------|-------------|---------------------| | **Perceivable** | Cannot see image | `alt` attribute on `` | Screen reader (JAWS, NVDA) | | **Perceivable** | Cannot hear audio | Captions / transcripts | Real-time captioning | | **Perceivable** | Low vision | High-contrast mode, scalable text | ZoomText, browser zoom | | **Operable** | Cannot use mouse | Keyboard navigation, focus indicators | Switch input, head-tracking | | **Operable** | Motor tremor | Large touch targets (44×44 px) | Stylus, mouth-stick | | **Operable** | Photosensitive epilepsy | No flashing \textgreater 3 Hz | n/a (prevention) | | **Understandable** | Cognitive disability | Plain language, consistent layout | Reading assistants | | **Robust** | Inconsistent markup | Semantic HTML, ARIA roles | All assistive tech | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Integrated Rehabilitation Engineering System Architecture The block diagram below shows how Computer Applications, Telecommunications, and Web Accessibility converge into a single, accessible, connected rehabilitation ecosystem. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PATIENT["PATIENT SIDE"] PA1[Wearable IMU and EMG Sensors] PA2[Mobile Phone or Tablet] PA3[Patient AAC Device] PA4[Wheelchair ECU] end subgraph NET["TELECOMMUNICATION LAYER"] NET1[WiFi or 4G or 5G Wireless Link] NET2[Edge Computing Node] NET3[Cloud Server with AES Encryption] end subgraph COMP["COMPUTER APPLICATION LAYER"] CA1[Rehab Assessment Software] CA2[Virtual Reality Therapy Module] CA3[AI Based Progress Tracker] CA4[Biofeedback Processor] end subgraph WEB["WEB ACCESSIBILITY LAYER"] WA1[WCAG 2.1 Compliant Portal] WA2[Screen Reader Compatible] WA3[Keyboard and Switch Navigation] WA4[Multilingual Captions] end subgraph CLIN["CLINICIAN SIDE"] CL1[Therapist Dashboard] CL2[Teleconsultation Interface] CL3[Electronic Medical Records] end PA1 --> NET1 PA2 --> NET1 PA3 --> NET1 PA4 --> NET1 NET1 --> NET2 NET2 --> NET3 NET3 --> CA1 NET3 --> CA2 NET3 --> CA3 NET3 --> CA4 CA1 --> CL1 CA2 --> CL1 CA3 --> CL1 CA4 --> CL1 CA1 --> WEB CA2 --> WEB CA3 --> WEB CA4 --> WEB WA1 --> CL1 WA2 --> CL2 WA3 --> CL2 WA4 --> CL3 CL1 --> CL3 CL2 --> CL3 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The **Patient Side** captures data via sensors, mobile apps, AAC devices, and ECU. - The **Telecommunication Layer** securely transmits data to the cloud. - The **Computer Application Layer** processes signals, runs VR therapy, and tracks progress. - The **Web Accessibility Layer** ensures outputs are usable by clinicians and patients with diverse abilities. - The **Clinician Side** receives accessible, real-time information for decision-making. --- ### 4.2 Web Accessibility Compliance Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR A[New Website or App] --> B{Content Uses Images?} B -- Yes --> C[Add alt text] B -- No --> D{Contains Video?} C --> D D -- Yes --> E[Add Captions and Transcripts] D -- No --> F{Keyboard Navigable?} E --> F F -- No --> G[Add Tab Order and Focus Indicators] F -- Yes --> H{Color Contrast Check} G --> H H -- Fails --> I[Adjust Colors to CR greater than 4.5 to 1] H -- Passes --> J{Valid Semantic HTML} I --> J J -- No --> K[Use Proper Heading and ARIA Roles] J -- Yes --> L[WCAG 2.1 AA Compliant] K --> L ``` **Reading the Flowchart:** Each node represents a checkpoint. A site passes the WCAG 2.1 AA audit only when it traverses all four POUR checks (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust). --- ### 4.3 Telerehabilitation System — Mode Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SYNC["SYNCHRONOUS MODE"] S1[Patient Performs Exercise] S2[Real Time Sensor Data] S3[Live Video Stream] S4[Therapist Gives Instant Feedback] end subgraph ASYNC["ASYNCHRONOUS MODE"] A1[Patient Records Session Locally] A2[Data Encrypted and Uploaded] A3[Therapist Reviews Later] A4[Feedback Returned by Email or Portal] end S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 ``` **Reading the Block Diagram:** Synchronous mode is latency-critical (requires broadband); Asynchronous mode tolerates delays and is suitable for low-bandwidth rural areas. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) and explain the four POUR principles. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines)** is a set of recommendations published by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** that defines how to make web content more accessible to people with disabilities. The current version is **WCAG 2.1** (with WCAG 2.2 released in 2023). The four core principles are: > > 1. **Perceivable:** Information and UI components must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive. Example: providing text alternatives for images (`alt` attribute) and captions for video. > 2. **Operable:** Interface components and navigation must be operable. Example: all functionality must be available via keyboard for users who cannot use a mouse. > 3. **Understandable:** Information and UI operation must be understandable. Example: text must be readable, and web pages must operate in predictable ways. > 4. **Robust:** Content must be interpretable by a wide variety of user agents, including current and future assistive technologies. Example: using valid, semantic HTML and ARIA roles. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Differentiate between **synchronous** and **asynchronous** telerehabilitation with one example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > | Parameter | Synchronous TR | Asynchronous TR | > |-----------|----------------|------------------| > | Interaction | Real-time, live | Delayed, store-and-forward | > | Technology | Video conferencing, live sensors | Recorded video, uploaded data | > | Latency | Low (\textless 150 ms) required | High latency tolerated | > | Example | Live video physiotherapy session for stroke | Patient records walking video at home; therapist reviews next day | > | Bandwidth Need | High (≥1 Mbps) | Low (text/email acceptable) | > > **Key Difference:** Synchronous TR mimics an in-clinic session in real time, while asynchronous TR decouples the patient and therapist in time, making it more flexible for rural or low-bandwidth regions. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **Q.A (a) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 7 marks]** > Explain the role of computer applications in rehabilitation engineering. Describe **any three** categories with suitable examples. **Model Solution (7 marks):** Computer applications play a pivotal role in modern rehabilitation engineering across assessment, therapy, and patient empowerment. **1. Computer-Based Assessment (2 marks):** Software tools digitize clinical evaluation. For example, **goniometer software** uses digital encoders to compute joint Range of Motion (ROM) with $\pm 1°$ accuracy. **Posturography systems** use force platforms to compute center-of-pressure (CoP) sway, helping diagnose balance disorders. **2. Computer-Aided Therapy / Biofeedback (2 marks):** VR-based systems (e.g., **Saebo VR**, **MindMaze**) use head-tracked displays to deliver goal-oriented exercises. The patient sees an avatar of their arm on screen; reaching targets reinforces neural pathways through **neuroplasticity**. Real-time EMG biofeedback helps patients re-learn muscle activation. **3. Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) (1.5 marks):** For patients with speech impairments (e.g., cerebral palsy, ALS), high-tech AAC devices like the **Tobii Dynavox** use eye-tracking to type text and produce synthesized speech, enabling full communication independence. **4. Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADL) (1.5 marks):** Environmental Control Units (ECUs) allow patients to operate lights, fans, TVs, and phones via a single switch, voice command, or even brain-computer interface. **ECU + Internet-of-Things (IoT)** integration is a current research frontier. --- > **Q.A (b) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 7 marks]** > With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a **telerehabilitation system**. Mention the network requirements for synchronous operation. **Model Solution (7 marks):** A telerehabilitation system consists of four functional layers (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram): **1. Patient Layer (1.5 marks):** Comprises wearable sensors (IMU, EMG), camera, microphone, and home-based hub (laptop/tablet). **2. Communication Layer (2 marks):** Data travels over a **secure telecommunications network** (WiFi/4G/5G). Encryption (AES-256) and HIPAA-equivalent (India: **DISHA Act, 2018 — Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act**) compliance ensure patient privacy. **3. Server/Cloud Layer (1.5 marks):** Stores Electronic Medical Records (EMR), runs AI analytics, and hosts the therapy software. **4. Clinician Layer (1 mark):** Therapist accesses the data through a secure, accessible web portal. **Network Requirements for Synchronous Telerehabilitation:** - **Bandwidth:** $\geq 1$ Mbps (SD), $\geq 4$ Mbps (HD) per session. - **Latency:** \textless 150 ms round-trip. - **Jitter:** \textless 30 ms. - **Packet Loss:** \textless 1%. - **BER:** $\leq 10^{-5}$ for motion data. - **Security:** End-to-end encryption, two-factor authentication. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q.A(b):** > Many students draw the block diagram but **forget to list the numerical network requirements** (bandwidth, latency, BER). This is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**. Always quantify the network specs. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **Q.B (a) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 7 marks]** > Explain the **WCAG 2.1 guidelines** in detail with reference to the **POUR principles** and their corresponding success criteria. **Model Solution (7 marks):** WCAG 2.1 organizes accessibility into **4 principles, 13 guidelines, and 78 testable success criteria**. The conformance levels are A, AA, and AAA. **1. Perceivable (1.75 marks):** - Guideline 1.1: Provide text alternatives (`alt` text, `longdesc`). - Guideline 1.2: Provide captions and other alternatives for time-based media (video, audio). - Guideline 1.3: Create content that can be presented in different ways (e.g., responsive layout). - Guideline 1.4: Make it easier for users to see and hear content (color contrast $\text{CR} \geq 4.5 : 1$, resize text up to 200%, no audio-only content, etc.). **2. Operable (1.75 marks):** - Guideline 2.1: Keyboard accessible (no mouse-only functions). - Guideline 2.2: Provide users enough time to read and use content. - Guideline 2.3: Do not design content in a way that is known to cause seizures (no flashing more than 3 times per second). - Guideline 2.4: Navigable — provide skip links, descriptive page titles, focus order. - Guideline 2.5: Input modalities (target size, pointer cancellation). **3. Understandable (1.75 marks):** - Guideline 3.1: Readable — specify the language of the page and of any unusual words. - Guideline 3.2: Predictable — consistent navigation, no unexpected context changes. - Guideline 3.3: Input assistance — error identification, labels, suggestions. **4. Robust (1.75 marks):** - Guideline 4.1: Compatible — maximize compatibility with current and future user agents (valid HTML, ARIA roles, status messages). **Indian Context (bonus point):** The **GIGW (Guidelines for Indian Government Websites) v3.0** mandates WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for all government digital services, and the **RPwD Act 2016** provides legal backing. --- > **Q.B (b) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 7 marks]** > Describe the various **assistive technologies** that interact with web content. How do screen readers and AAC devices rely on accessibility-compliant websites? **Model Solution (7 marks):** **1. Screen Readers (2 marks):** Screen readers (e.g., **JAWS**, **NVDA**, **VoiceOver**, **TalkBack**) convert on-screen text and interface elements into synthesized speech or braille output. They depend on: - Semantic HTML (`

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction # 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Rehabilitation Robotics** is a specialised branch of biomedical and assistive engineering that integrates robotic systems, mechatronic actuators, sensor networks, and intelligent control algorithms to assist, augment, or restore the sensorimotor and functional capabilities of individuals suffering from physical impairments, neurological disorders, musculoskeletal injuries, or age-related degenerative conditions. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: OEBRT722) definition framework: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rehabilitation Robot**: An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multi-functional manipulator or mechatronic system, purpose-designed to interact with a human patient for the explicit goal of assessment, training, substitution, or recovery of physical function. The discipline formally sits at the convergence of: - **Robotics & Mechatronics** (actuation, kinematics, control) - **Neuroscience & Motor Learning** (neuroplasticity, recovery mechanisms) - **Biomedical Engineering** (human anatomy, biomechanics) - **Artificial Intelligence** (intent recognition, adaptive therapy) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Physical Therapy Gym Partner"** > > Imagine a patient recovering from a stroke who needs to repeatedly practise lifting their arm. A human therapist is skilled but tires after 50 repetitions. A **rehabilitation robot** acts as an *inexhaustible, infinitely patient, sensor-precise training partner* — it never tires, can measure the exact joint angle, speed, and force of every single movement, gently corrects the patient's motion when it strays, and records progress across thousands of trials that no human could monitor. Just as a flight simulator lets a pilot practise a dangerous manoeuvre thousands of times safely, a rehabilitation robot lets a patient practise lost movements thousands of times in a safe, measurable environment. ## 1.3 Scope of the Topic The "Introduction" module in Robotic Rehabilitation generally covers the following foundational areas: 1. **Definition and Classification** of rehabilitation robots. 2. **Historical Evolution** from simple mechanical aids to AI-driven exoskeletons. 3. **Core Subsystem Architecture** (actuators, sensors, controllers, end-effectors, HMI). 4. **Therapeutic Paradigms** (assistive, therapeutic, augmentative, substitutive). 5. **Anatomical Categorisation** (upper-limb, lower-limb, ankle, hand, gait, trunk). 6. **Engineering & Clinical Significance** (objective measurement, intensity, repeatability). ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard units and metrics are essential in rehabilitation robotics: - **Force/Torque Output**: $0$ to $\mathbf{N \cdot m}$ (Newton-metre), typically $5$–$\mathbf{80\ N \cdot m}$ for therapeutic devices. - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: $1$ to $7+$ for upper-limb devices; $4$ to $12$ for lower-limb. - **Bandwidth of Human Neuromuscular System**: $0$ to $\mathbf{10\ Hz}$ for voluntary motion; $0$ to $\mathbf{20\ Hz}$ for tremor. - **Typical Therapy Session Duration**: $30$ to $\mathbf{90\ minutes}$. - **Repetitions per Session**: $100$ to $\mathbf{1{,}000+}$ (a key advantage over manual therapy). - **Mean Human Upper-Limb Mass**: $\approx \mathbf{5\%}$ of body mass (approx. $3.5$–$\mathbf{4.5\ kg}$). - **Gait Cycle Duration**: $\approx \mathbf{1.0}$ to $\mathbf{1.2\ seconds}$ (cadence $50$–$\mathbf{100\ steps/min}$). # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Five-Stage Rehabilitation Loop A rehabilitation robot does not merely "move" a limb. It operates through a closed-loop functional pipeline: ### Step 1 — **Patient–Robot Coupling** The patient's limb is physically connected to the robot's end-effector via an **orthosis, cuff, exoskeleton link, or handle**. The mechanical interface must be: - **Compliant** (to avoid injury during spasm or volitional error), - **Adjustable** (to fit anthropometric variation), - **Rigid enough** to transmit therapeutic force accurately. ### Step 2 — **Sensing & State Estimation** The robot continuously measures: - **Joint angles** ($\theta_i$) using optical encoders, Hall-effect sensors, or IMUs. - **End-effector position** ($x, y, z$) using potentiometers or resolvers. - **Interaction force/torque** ($F, \tau$) using load cells or current-based estimation. - **Electromyographic (EMG) signals** from residual muscle activity. - **Electroencephalographic (EEG) signals** for BCI-driven systems. ### Step 3 — **Control Strategy Execution** The controller computes an assistive/resistive/reference action based on one of the following paradigms: | Control Strategy | Functional Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Impedance Control** | Robot behaves as a programmable spring-damper ($M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{interaction}$) | Post-stroke arm therapy | | **Admittance Control** | Robot measures force and outputs motion | Treadmill-based gait training | | **Assist-As-Needed (AAN)** | Robot supplies force *only* when patient performance falls below a threshold | Upper-limb reaching tasks | | **Constraint-Induced (CI)** Therapy | Robot restricts compensatory movement, forcing use of impaired limb | Chronic stroke rehabilitation | | **EMG/EEG-Triggered** | Robot actuates only when patient *initiates* volitional neural signal | Severe paralysis therapy | ### Step 4 — **Therapeutic Interaction** The robot delivers a controlled mechanical stimulus to the patient's limb. The stimulus can be: - **Active-assistive** (robot helps complete the motion), - **Active-resistive** (robot opposes the motion for strength training), - **Passive** (robot moves the limb fully without patient effort — used in early stages), - **Active** (patient moves fully — robot only measures). ### Step 5 — **Performance Quantification & Adaptive Feedback** The robot logs every trial and computes **clinical outcome metrics**: - **Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA)** — a $0$–$\mathbf{66}$ (upper) or $0$–$\mathbf{34}$ (lower) scale. - **Wolf Motor Function Test (WMFT)** — time + functional ability score. - **Range of Motion (ROM)** in degrees. - **Smoothness metrics** (jerk-based, spectral arc length). - **Motor performance index** — success rate, trajectory error. ## 2.2 Classification of Rehabilitation Robots Rehabilitation robots are classified along four principal axes: ### A. By Therapeutic Goal 1. **Therapeutic Robots** — designed for temporary use during recovery (e.g., MIT-Manus, Armeo Power). 2. **Assistive Robots** — designed for permanent functional substitution (e.g., wheelchair-mounted manipulators). 3. **Augmentative / Wearable Robots** — exoskeletons that *enhance* normal function (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT). 4. **Prosthetic Robots** — replace a missing limb (e.g., modular prosthetic limbs). ### B. By Anatomical Region | Region | Example Device | DOF Range | |---|---|---| | Upper-limb (shoulder/elbow) | MIT-Manus, Armeo Spring | 2–7 | | Hand / Fingers | Hand of Hope, Fesia Grasp | 5–17 | | Lower-limb (hip/knee) | Lokomat, ReWalk | 4–8 | | Ankle | Anklebot | 1–2 | | Trunk / Spine | T-WREX (Trunk) | 2–3 | ### C. By Mechanical Architecture - **End-effector type**: applies force at the distal segment only (e.g., InMotion ARM). - **Exoskeleton type**: aligned with the anatomical joint axes (e.g., Armeo Power, Lokomat). ### D. By Degree of Autonomy - **Manual / Therapist-controlled**, - **Patient-cooperative (AAN-based)**, - **Fully autonomous / AI-driven** adaptive therapy. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Formula Sheet — Module 3: Introduction** | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Meaning of Each Term | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Generalised Impedance Model** | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$ | $M$ = virtual mass, $B$ = damping, $K$ = stiffness, $F_{ext}$ = external (patient) force | $M$: kg, $B$: N·s/m, $K$: N/m | | 2 | **Degree of Freedom (Grübler)** | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $n$ = links, $j_i$ = joints with $i$ DOF | Dimensionless | | 3 | **Required Joint Torque** | $\tau_i = \sum_{j} M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k} C_{ijk}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + G_i$ | $M_{ij}$ = inertia matrix, $C_{ijk}$ = Coriolis terms, $G_i$ = gravity | N·m | | 4 | **Bandwidth of Intent Detection** | $f_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_{delay}}$ | $\tau_{delay}$ = controller update delay | Hz | | 5 | **Therapy Intensity Index** | $I = N_{reps} \times t_{session}$ | $N_{reps}$ = number of repetitions, $t_{session}$ = session time | reps·min | | 6 | **Smoothness Metric (Jerk-based)** | $J = \int_{0}^{T} \vert \dddot{x}(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | $\dddot{x}$ = third derivative of position (jerk) | $\mathrm{m^{2}/s^{5}}$ | | 7 | **Workspace Reach (Spherical)** | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ | $r$ = maximum reach radius | $\mathrm{m^{3}}$ | | 8 | **Patient Body Segment Inertia (approx.)** | $I_{seg} \approx m_{seg} r_{g}^{2}$ | $m_{seg}$ = segment mass, $r_g$ = radius of gyration | $\mathrm{kg \cdot m^{2}}$ | | 9 | **Safety-Critical Stop Distance** | $d_{stop} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2a_{max}}$ | $v$ = max end-effector speed, $a_{max}$ = max deceleration | m | | 10 | **Stiffness Ratio (Transparency)** | $\eta = \dfrac{K_{robot}}{K_{human}}$ | Ratio of robot stiffness to human limb stiffness | Dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Formula 1 (Impedance) is the bedrock of modern rehabilitation control — it allows the robot to behave as a *virtual coach* that is "soft" when the patient struggles and "stiff" when the patient strays. Formula 5 explains *why robots are clinically valuable* — they can deliver 10–100× the repetition intensity of a human therapist. ## 2.4 Why This Topic Matters in Robotic Rehabilitation The introduction establishes the **lexicon and architecture** required to analyse every subsequent module. A KTU examiner will assume mastery of: - The classification terminology (assistive vs therapeutic vs augmentative). - The five-stage rehabilitation loop. - The major anatomical categorisations. - The fundamental impedance model and AAN paradigm. These concepts are repeatedly tested in both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** university questions. # 🔍 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Grübler's Equation for DOF of a Rehabilitation Exoskeleton **Problem Statement:** Determine the degrees of freedom of a 3-DOF planar upper-limb rehabilitation exoskeleton comprising 4 links connected by 3 revolute joints. **Step 1 — State the Generalised Grübler Equation:** $$ \text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} (6 - f_{i}) $$ where: - $n$ = total number of rigid links (including ground), - $k$ = number of joints, - $f_i$ = DOF of the $i^{\text{th}}$ joint. **Step 2 — For a planar system in 2D**, the mobility reduces to 3 DOF per link in the plane, so the planar Grübler equation is: $$ \text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 $$ where $j_1$ is a 1-DOF joint (revolute) and $j_2$ is a 2-DOF joint (planar — not used here). **Step 3 — Substitute Known Values:** - $n = 4$ (ground link + 3 moving links: upper arm, forearm, hand) - $j_1 = 3$ (three revolute joints at shoulder, elbow, wrist) $$ \text{DOF} = 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 0 $$ **Step 4 — Simplify:** $$ \text{DOF} = 3 \times 3 - 6 = 9 - 6 = 3 $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** The exoskeleton has **3 independent rotational degrees of freedom**, corresponding to shoulder flexion/extension, elbow flexion/extension, and wrist rotation. This is sufficient to cover most activities of daily living (ADL) training for the upper limb. > **Conversion Logic:** Each moving link in 2D has 3 independent motions (2 translations + 1 rotation). Subtracting joint constraints yields the net mobility. The result (3) matches the clinical requirement of 3 active joint axes for planar reaching. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Impedance Model of a Rehabilitation Robot **Problem Statement:** Derive the dynamic equation of a 1-DOF rehabilitation robot interacting with a human arm in Cartesian space under impedance control. **Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for the Coupled System:** $$ M\ddot{x} = F_{robot} - F_{human} - F_{friction} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Programmable Impedance Terms:** The robot's force is designed to emulate a virtual spring-damper around a reference trajectory $x_{ref}$: $$ F_{robot} = M\ddot{x}_{ref} + B(\dot{x}_{ref} - \dot{x}) + K(x_{ref} - x) $$ **Step 3 — Combine (assuming friction is negligible for clarity):** $$ M\ddot{x} = M\ddot{x}_{ref} + B(\dot{x}_{ref} - \dot{x}) + K(x_{ref} - x) - F_{human} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to the Standard Impedance Form:** $$ M(\ddot{x} - \ddot{x}_{ref}) + B(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_{ref}) + K(x - x_{ref}) = -F_{human} $$ **Step 5 — Define Position Error $e = x - x_{ref}$:** $$ M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_{human} $$ This is a **second-order linear differential equation** with a forcing function equal to the negative of the patient's applied force. **Step 6 — Physical Interpretation:** - If the patient applies no force ($F_{human} = 0$): the error decays exponentially and the limb is "pulled" to $x_{ref}$. - If the patient pushes back, the error grows — the robot "yields" by an amount proportional to the patient's force. - The **stiffness $K$** controls how strictly the robot enforces the reference path. > **Conversion Logic:** This derivation shows *why impedance control is safe* — there are no rigid constraints; the robot can always be overpowered by the patient, ensuring inherent safety. This property is the foundation of the safety standards (ISO 13482, ISO/TS 15066) governing rehabilitation robots. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Therapy Intensity Quantification **Problem Statement:** A stroke patient performs therapy for $45$ minutes per day. A human therapist administers $30$ repetitions per session. A rehabilitation robot administers $400$ repetitions per session of the same duration. Compute the therapy intensity ratio. **Step 1 — Define Therapy Intensity:** $$ I = N_{reps} \times t_{session} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Human Therapy Intensity:** $$ I_{human} = 30 \times 45 = 1{,}350 \text{ reps·min} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate Robot Therapy Intensity:** $$ I_{robot} = 400 \times 45 = 18{,}000 \text{ reps·min} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the Ratio:** $$ R = \dfrac{I_{robot}}{I_{human}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{1{,}350} \approx 13.33 $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** The robot delivers approximately **13× more therapy intensity** than a human therapist in the same session time. This is one of the most clinically cited justifications for robotic rehabilitation. > **Conversion Logic:** Repetition intensity is directly correlated with neuroplastic recovery (Kleim & Jones, 2008 principle: "use it and improve it"). The numerical derivation makes this abstract principle concrete and testable. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Robot Types | Feature | Therapeutic (e.g., MIT-Manus) | Assistive (e.g., wheelchair arm) | Augmentative (e.g., ReWalk) | Prosthetic (e.g., LUKE arm) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Usage Duration** | Temporary (weeks–months) | Permanent | Permanent | Permanent | | **Patient Effort** | Required | Optional | Required | Required | | **Primary Goal** | Recovery | Functional substitution | Functional enhancement | Limb replacement | | **Power Source** | External (mains) | Battery | Battery | Battery | | **Clinical Stage** | Sub-acute / chronic | Chronic | Chronic | Post-amputation | | **Cost Range (USD)** | 50K – 200K | 20K – 100K | 70K – 150K | 50K – 100K | | **Example CO Linkage** | CO1, CO2 | CO1, CO3 | CO1, CO3, CO4 | CO1, CO4 | # 🗂️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rehabilitation Robot The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete functional pipeline of a generic rehabilitation robotic system, illustrating the closed-loop interaction between sensing, control, actuation, and clinical assessment modules. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient with Impairment] --> B[Mechanical Coupling Interface
Orthosis or Cuff] B --> C[Sensor Array Layer
Encoders, Load Cells, EMG, IMU] C --> D[Signal Conditioning
and A-D Conversion] D --> E[State Estimation Module
Position, Velocity, Force] E --> F{Controller Core
Impedance or AAN} F --> G[Actuator Command
Generation Layer] G --> H[Power Amplifier
and Motor Drivers] H --> I[Mechanical Actuator
Servo or Pneumatic] I --> J[End Effector Motion
Applied to Patient Limb] J --> A F --> K[Performance Logger
FMA, WMFT, ROM] K --> L[Adaptive Therapy
Parameter Update] L --> F M[Therapist Workstation
HMI and Override] --> F F --> M ``` > **Reading Guide:** The diagram shows two coupled loops — the **inner loop** (A→B→C→D→E→F→G→H→I→J→A) is the real-time control loop operating at $\mathbf{100}$–$\mathbf{1000\ Hz}$. The **outer loop** (F→K→L→F) is the clinical adaptation loop running at $\mathbf{1\ Hz}$ or slower, where therapy parameters are updated based on logged outcomes. ## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Rehabilitation Robots] --> T[Therapeutic Robots] R --> A[Assistive Robots] R --> G[Augmentative Exoskeletons] R --> P[Prosthetic Robots] T --> T1[Upper Limb Therapy
MIT Manus and Armeo] T --> T2[Lower Limb Therapy
Lokomat and Gait Trainer] T --> T3[Hand Therapy
Hand of Hope and Amadeo] A --> A1[Wheelchair Mounted Arms
Manus ARM and JACO] A --> A2[Feeding and ADL Robots
My Spoon and Handy 1] G --> G1[Lower Limb Exoskeletons
ReWalk and Ekso and HAL] G --> G2[Upper Limb Exoskeletons
MyoPro and ABLE Exoskeleton] P --> P1[Hand Prosthetics
iLimb and Bebionic] P --> P2[Full Arm Prosthetics
LUKE Arm and Modular Prosthetic Limb] ``` ## 4.3 The Five-Stage Rehabilitation Interaction Loop (Time-Frequency View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Coupling] S1[Orthosis and Mechanical Link] end subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Sensing] S2[Position, Force, EMG, EEG] end subgraph Stage3[Stage 3 Control] S3[Impedance, AAN, Admittance, CI] end subgraph Stage4[Stage 4 Interaction] S4[Passive, Active Assistive, Active Resistive, Active] end subgraph Stage5[Stage 5 Quantification] S5[FMA, WMFT, ROM, Smoothness, Motor Learning Index] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S3 ``` > **Reading Note:** The arrow from S5 back to S3 illustrates the **adaptive feedback** mechanism — clinical outcomes from Stage 5 are used to retune the controller in Stage 3 for the next trial. This is the engineering basis of *personalised robotic rehabilitation*. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEBRT722]` **Q: Define the term "rehabilitation robot" and distinguish between therapeutic, assistive, and augmentative rehabilitation robots with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > A **rehabilitation robot** is a reprogrammable, mechatronic device designed to assist, augment, or restore physical function in individuals with sensorimotor impairments. **Distinctions:** | Type | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | **Therapeutic** | Used temporarily during recovery to promote motor relearning | **MIT-Manus** (upper-limb stroke therapy) | | **Assistive** | Used permanently to substitute for lost function | **JACO arm** (wheelchair-mounted manipulator) | | **Augmentative** | Worn to *enhance* existing function, not substitute for loss | **ReWalk exoskeleton** (enables ambulation in paraplegics) | > [!NOTE] > **Key distinguishing feature:** Therapeutic robots are *training tools*; assistive robots are *functional replacements*; augmentative robots are *performance boosters*. A 3-mark answer must include all three definitions + one example each. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEBRT722]` **Q: Explain the concept of "Assist-As-Needed" (AAN) control in rehabilitation robotics. Why is it clinically preferred over fully passive control?** **Model Answer:** **Assist-As-Needed (AAN)** is a control paradigm in which the rehabilitation robot supplies assistive force **only when** the patient's voluntary effort falls below a pre-defined performance threshold. Mathematically, the robot's force $F_{robot}$ is given by: $$ F_{robot} = \max\left(0,\ F_{threshold} - F_{patient}\right) $$ **Why AAN is preferred:** 1. **Promotes Neuroplasticity** — the patient must *initiate* volitional effort, reinforcing cortical reorganisation. 2. **Prevents Slacking** — fully passive control allows the patient to remain inactive, leading to learned non-use. 3. **Patient-Customised Challenge** — the robot's effort adapts to the patient's current capability, maintaining an optimal difficulty level. 4. **Safety** — AAN inherently limits the robot's force output, reducing risk of injury. > **Examination Tip:** For full 3 marks, mention *both* the mathematical/computational principle and the clinical rationale (neuroplasticity + learned non-use). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, OEBRT722]` **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a generic rehabilitation robotic system. Explain the role of each subsystem. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the classification of rehabilitation robots based on (i) therapeutic goal, (ii) anatomical region, and (iii) mechanical architecture, with suitable examples for each category. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** The functional architecture comprises **five interconnected subsystems** (refer Section 4.1 diagram): 1. **Mechanical Coupling Interface (1 mark):** Orthosis, cuff, or end-effector that physically links the patient's limb to the robot. Must be safe, comfortable, and adjustable. 2. **Sensing Subsystem (1 mark):** Encoders for joint angles, load cells for interaction force, EMG/EEG for neural intent. Signal conditioning and A/D conversion included. 3. **Controller Subsystem (2 marks):** Implements the chosen control law — impedance, admittance, or AAN. Computes desired actuator commands at $\mathbf{100}$–$\mathbf{1000\ Hz}$ update rate. Includes a safety layer with limit checking and emergency stop. 4. **Actuation Subsystem (1 mark):** Power amplifiers, servo motors (or pneumatic / hydraulic actuators), and gear trains. Delivers the required torque at each joint. 5. **Clinical/Outcome Subsystem (2 marks):** Logs trial-by-trial data, computes FMA, ROM, smoothness metrics, and updates therapy parameters adaptively. Includes the therapist's HMI for manual override. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** (i) **Therapeutic Goal (3 marks):** Therapeutic, Assistive, Augmentative, Prosthetic (as defined in Section 2.4). Example mapping: MIT-Manus (therapeutic), JACO (assistive), ReWalk (augmentative), LUKE arm (prosthetic). (ii) **Anatomical Region (2 marks):** Upper-limb, lower-limb, hand, ankle, trunk. Examples: Armeo Power (upper), Lokomat (lower), Hand of Hope (hand), Anklebot (ankle). (iii) **Mechanical Architecture (2 marks):** End-effector type (force applied at distal segment — e.g., InMotion ARM) vs Exoskeleton type (joint-aligned — e.g., Lokomat). --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, OEBRT722]` **Q: (a)** Derive the impedance control equation for a 1-DOF rehabilitation robot interacting with a patient's limb. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A stroke patient undergoes $50$ minutes of robotic therapy per day. The robot delivers $480$ repetitions per session, whereas conventional therapy delivers $35$ repetitions per session. Calculate: (i) therapy intensity for each modality, and (ii) the intensity gain ratio. Comment on the clinical significance of the result. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** Starting from Newton's second law: $$ M\ddot{x} = F_{robot} - F_{human} $$ Substituting the virtual spring-damper model: $$ F_{robot} = M\ddot{x}_{ref} + B(\dot{x}_{ref} - \dot{x}) + K(x_{ref} - x) $$ Combining: $$ M\ddot{x} = M\ddot{x}_{ref} + B(\dot{x}_{ref} - \dot{x}) + K(x_{ref} - x) - F_{human} $$ Rearranging: $$ M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = M\ddot{x}_{ref} + B\dot{x}_{ref} + Kx_{ref} - F_{human} $$ Defining error $e = x - x_{ref}$: $$ M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_{human} $$ (1 mark — setup; 2 marks — substitution; 2 marks — final impedance form; 2 marks — physical interpretation: stiffness $K$ tunes compliance, damping $B$ tunes smoothness, mass $M$ tunes inertia emulation.) **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** (i) Therapy intensity: - Conventional: $I_c = 35 \times 50 = 1{,}750$ reps·min - Robotic: $I_r = 480 \times 50 = 24{,}000$ reps·min (ii) Intensity gain ratio: $$ R = \dfrac{I_r}{I_c} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{1{,}750} \approx 13.71 $$ (3 marks — calculations; 2 marks — clinical comment.) **Clinical Comment (2 marks):** The robot delivers approximately **14× more therapy intensity** than conventional therapy. This is clinically significant because: - Repetition intensity is a key driver of neuroplastic recovery. - Robotic systems enable high-intensity, dose-controlled, measurable therapy not feasible with human therapists alone. - The result supports robotic rehabilitation as a *complement* (not replacement) to conventional therapy. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. Many students confuse "DOF" with "number of joints" — they are *not* the same. A 3-joint exoskeleton may have DOF = 3 (if all revolute) or DOF = 5 (if one is a spherical joint). Always apply Grübler's equation. > 2. In AAN-based questions, students often write only the clinical rationale and forget the mathematical formulation. You will lose **1 to 1.5 marks** if the formula is absent. > 3. In impedance control derivations, **do not skip the step of substituting $F_{robot}$** — examiners specifically look for that transition (worth ~2 marks). > 4. The intensity-ratio question is a high-frequency KTU favourite. Practice the calculation **twice** so you don't make unit or arithmetic errors in the exam. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🧠 Module 3 — Introduction: Final Revision Sheet** - [x] **Rehabilitation Robot** = reprogrammable mechatronic system for assessment/training/substitution of physical function. - [x] **Three main classes**: *Therapeutic* (temporary, MIT-Manus), *Assistive* (permanent, JACO), *Augmentative* (performance-enhancing, ReWalk). - [x] **Five-stage loop**: Coupling → Sensing → Control → Interaction → Quantification. - [x] **Impedance model**: $M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke = -F_{human}$ — the foundation of safe rehabilitation control. - [x] **Assist-As-Needed (AAN)**: robot helps *only* when patient effort falls below threshold — promotes neuroplasticity. - [x] **Grübler's equation (planar)**: $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — used to determine exoskeleton mobility. - [x] **DOF ranges**: Upper-limb = 2–7; Lower-limb = 4–8; Hand = 5–17. - [x] **Therapy intensity gain**: Robots typically deliver $10$–$15\times$ more repetitions per session than human therapists. - [x] **Two mechanical architectures**: *End-effector* (force at distal segment) vs *Exoskeleton* (joint-aligned). - [x] **Key clinical metrics**: FMA, WMFT, ROM, jerk-based smoothness, motor performance index. - [x] **Sensors used**: encoders, load cells, IMUs, EMG electrodes, EEG electrodes. - [x] **Safety standard**: Impedance control ensures no rigid constraint — patient can always overpower the robot. - [x] **Typical session**: $30$–$90$ min, $100$–$1000+$ repetitions. - [x] **Key formula to memorise**: $I = N_{reps} \times t_{session}$ (therapy intensity). - [x] **Critical "Why"**: Rehabilitation robotics is justified by *objective measurement + intensity + repeatability* — three things a human therapist cannot provide simultaneously at scale. > 🎯 **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark question in this module, structure your answer as **Definition → Classification → Block Diagram → Control Equation → Clinical Metric → Example**. Examiners reward structured, diagram-supported answers with full marks.

topics.title: Classes of Manual Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classes of Manual Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **manual wheelchair** is a manually propelled, occupant-operated mobility device designed to provide wheeled mobility to individuals with ambulatory disabilities. The **classification of manual wheelchairs** is a hierarchical taxonomy used in rehabilitation engineering to categorize wheelchairs based on structural configuration, mass distribution, adjustability, frame geometry, propulsion biomechanics, and intended clinical use-case.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBRT722 – Robotic Rehabilitation)** and aligned with **ISO 7176 / RESNA wheelchair standards**, manual wheelchairs are classified into distinct classes that govern prescription, clinical fitting, and reimbursement protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Manual wheelchairs are NOT a single homogenous category. Engineers MUST know the class to design robotic retrofit modules, sensors, actuators, and control algorithms in robotic rehabilitation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **shoe rack** in a sports store: you don't buy "running shoes" generically — you choose between **marathon shoes, sprinting spikes, trail shoes, or casual sneakers**. Each is engineered for a specific biomechanical demand. Similarly, manual wheelchairs are engineered for **specific user profiles** — a stroke survivor, an active paraplegic athlete, a bariatric patient, or a child with cerebral palsy all require fundamentally different wheelchair architectures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Wheelchair class = the *engineering blueprint* that matches the *user's clinical and functional profile*. In robotic rehabilitation, the class determines the placement of motor controllers, joystick modules, drive-assist sensors, and battery housing.

### 1.3 Primary Classification Axes (KTU High-Yield)

Manual wheelchairs are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Description |
|---|---|
| **Mass (Weight Class)** | Standard, Lightweight, Ultra-lightweight |
| **Frame Geometry** | Folding (cross-brace) vs Rigid |
| **Clinical Function** | Self-propelled, Attendant-propelled, Tilt/Recline |
| **User Population** | Adult, Pediatric, Bariatric, Active-user, Sports |

### 1.4 The Six Core Classes (Master List)

The KTU syllabus recognizes the following six principal classes:

1. **Standard (Conventional) Manual Wheelchair**
2. **Lightweight Manual Wheelchair**
3. **Ultra-lightweight (Active) Manual Wheelchair**
4. **Hemi-Height Manual Wheelchair**
5. **Tilt-in-Space / Reclining Manual Wheelchair**
6. **Rigid Frame (Custom-configured) Manual Wheelchair**

Specialized sub-classes include **Sports Wheelchairs, Bariatric Wheelchairs, Pediatric Wheelchairs, and One-Arm-Drive Wheelchairs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Threshold Metrics (per RESNA / ISO 7176):**
> - **Standard:** $\geq 15 \text{ kg}$
> - **Lightweight:** $12 - 15 \text{ kg}$
> - **Ultra-lightweight:** $\leq 12 \text{ kg}$ (often $7 - 10 \text{ kg}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Class 1 — Standard (Conventional) Manual Wheelchair

**Definition:** A basic, mass-produced wheelchair typically manufactured from **steel tubing**, intended for short-term or institutional use.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Mass:** $\geq 15 \text{ kg}$ (often $18 - 25 \text{ kg}$)
- **Frame:** Folding cross-brace (X-frame) construction
- **Materials:** Chrome-plated steel, basic aluminium
- **Adjustability:** Limited or none — fixed axle plate, fixed backrest angle
- **Seat Width:** Standard $16", 18", 20"$
- **Drive Wheels:** Composite or pneumatic, 24-inch rear
- **Push Rims:** Standard, single-loop
- **Use Case:** Hospitals, post-acute care, temporary loaner chairs
- **Expected Service Life:** $1 - 3$ years

**Biomechanical Implication:** High rolling resistance, inefficient propulsion, high risk of **repetitive strain injury (RSI)** in upper limbs for long-term users.

### 2.2 Class 2 — Lightweight Manual Wheelchair

**Definition:** A reduced-mass wheelchair using **aluminium alloy frames**, designed for moderate-term community use.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Mass:** $12 - 15 \text{ kg}$
- **Frame:** Folding or rigid hybrid
- **Materials:** Aluminium 6061-T6, some composite components
- **Adjustability:** Adjustable axle plate, swing-away footrests
- **Use Case:** Long-term users with moderate activity levels, outpatient rehabilitation
- **Expected Service Life:** $3 - 5$ years

### 2.3 Class 3 — Ultra-Lightweight (Active) Manual Wheelchair

**Definition:** A high-performance, custom-configured wheelchair engineered for **active full-time users** with permanent mobility impairment.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Mass:** $\leq 12 \text{ kg}$ (high-end models: $6 - 9 \text{ kg}$)
- **Frame:** Rigid (preferred) or folding; custom-machined
- **Materials:** Aluminium 7075-T6, titanium (Ti-6Al-4V), carbon fibre composite
- **Adjustability:** Fully customizable — camber angle, CoG, seat-to-floor height, axle position
- **Camber Angle:** Adjustable $0° - 8°$ (typical: $2° - 4°$)
- **Use Case:** Active paraplegic/quadriplegic users, daily long-distance propulsion
- **Expected Service Life:** $5 - 10$ years

### 2.4 Class 4 — Hemi-Height (Hemi) Manual Wheelchair

**Definition:** A low-seat-height wheelchair designed for users who can propel using **one or both feet**, typically post-stroke hemiplegia.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Seat-to-Floor Height:** $\leq 17.5$ inches (lowered for foot-propulsion)
- **Drive:** Foot-propulsion or hand-propulsion
- **Use Case:** Stroke recovery, amputee transition, short stature
- **Frame:** Folding, lightweight aluminium

### 2.5 Class 5 — Tilt-in-Space / Reclining Manual Wheelchair

**Definition:** A wheelchair with a mechanism to **rotate the entire seat assembly** about a fixed axis (tilt) or to recline the backrest, redistributing pressure for users who cannot shift weight independently.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Tilt Range:** $0° - 45°$ (some models up to $60°$)
- **Recline Range:** $0° - 180°$ (full recline)
- **Use Case:** Pediatric CP, MS, ALS, severe postural instability, pressure-sore prevention
- **Mass:** $15 - 25 \text{ kg}$ (heaviest class)
- **Drive:** Attendant-propelled primarily

### 2.6 Class 6 — Rigid Frame (Custom-Configured) Manual Wheelchair

**Definition:** A non-folding, open-frame wheelchair built to **user-specific anthropometric and biomechanical measurements**.

**Engineering Characteristics:**
- **Frame:** Single-tube or double-tube rigid (no folding cross-brace)
- **Mass Distribution:** Optimized to user body weight
- **Propulsion Efficiency:** Highest among all classes
- **Use Case:** Active daily users, long-term performance

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and metrics are commonly tested in KTU examinations for manual wheelchair classes.

**Table 1: KTU High-Yield Engineering Metrics & Formulas**

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheelchair Mass Class Threshold** | $m_{std} \geq 15$, $m_{lw} \in [12, 15)$, $m_{ult} < 12$ | $\text{kg}$ |
| **Rolling Resistance Coefficient** | $\mu_r = \dfrac{F_{roll}}{N}$ | dimensionless, $\approx 0.005 - 0.015$ (pneumatic tyre) |
| **Propulsion Force Required** | $F_p = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g + m \cdot a$ | $\text{N}$ |
| **Camber Angle Effect on Lateral Stability** | $W_{eff} = W \cdot \cos\theta + 2 r \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\text{mm}$ |
| **Energy Cost of Transport (ECOT)** | $ECOT = \dfrac{E_{prop}}{m_{user} \cdot d}$ | $\text{J \cdot kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^{-1}$ |
| **Mechanical Efficiency (Push-Rim)** | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{useful}}{W_{applied}} \times 100\%$ | $5\% - 12\%$ (typical) |
| **Static Tip-Over Angle (Forward)** | $\phi_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{d_{rear}}{h_{cg}}\right)$ | degrees |
| **Seat-to-Floor Height (Hemi)** | $H_{hemi} \leq 17.5$ | $\text{inches}$ |
| **Standard Tilt Range (Tilt-in-Space)** | $\theta_{tilt} \in [0°, 45°]$ | degrees |
| **Pressure Relief Interval** | $T_{relief} = \dfrac{P_{cap}}{\Delta P \cdot \dot{P}}$ | $\text{minutes}$ |

**Boundary Conditions for Rolling Resistance:**

$$\mu_r = \begin{cases} 0.005 \text{ to } 0.015 & \text{(pneumatic tyre, smooth surface)} \\ 0.02 \text{ to } 0.05 & \text{(solid tyre, rough surface)} \end{cases}$$

**Engineering Real-World Utility:** These metrics directly inform **robotic drive-assist retrofit design** in OEBRT722. For instance, **ECOT** values guide the sizing of battery packs for powered add-on units; **camber angle** determines the mounting geometry of motor encoders; and **propulsion force** defines the torque capacity required for servo-controlled push-rim activation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Propulsion Force for a Manual Wheelchair on a Flat Surface

We derive the total propulsion force $F_p$ required for a manual wheelchair user (mass $M$ including wheelchair and user) moving with acceleration $a$ on a flat, level surface.

**Step 1 — Identify the resistive forces on a wheelchair in steady motion on a flat surface:**

The dominant resistive forces are:
- **Rolling friction:** $F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot N$ where $N = M \cdot g$ is the normal force
- **Inertial force (during acceleration):** $F_{inertia} = M \cdot a$
- **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ (typically negligible at manual speeds $< 2 \text{ m/s}$)

**Step 2 — Write the force balance equation (Newton's 2nd Law in the horizontal direction):**

The sum of propulsive force equals sum of resistive forces:

$$F_p = F_{roll} + F_{inertia} + F_{drag}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute expressions for each term:**

$$F_p = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g + M \cdot a + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the manual-wheelchair low-speed approximation** ($v < 2 \text{ m/s}$):

At low speeds, the aerodynamic drag term is less than $1\%$ of rolling friction and is therefore neglected:

$$F_p \approx \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g + M \cdot a$$

**Step 5 — Factor $M$ to obtain the canonical KTU formula:**

$$F_p = M \left( \mu_r \cdot g + a \right)$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check (KTU exam favourite):**

For a standard wheelchair ($M = 90 \text{ kg}$, user + chair combined), $\mu_r = 0.01$ (pneumatic tyre on tile), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $a = 0$ (constant velocity):

$$\begin{aligned} F_p &= 90 \times (0.01 \times 9.81 + 0) \\ &= 90 \times 0.0981 \\ &= 8.83 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

This matches empirical measurements of push-rim force during steady-state manual propulsion ($8 - 12 \text{ N}$ range), confirming the model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The result $F_p \approx 8.83 \text{ N}$ represents the force per drive wheel for a two-wheel chair. The total user-applied force on both push rims must overcome this value. Because most adults can apply $50 - 150 \text{ N}$ per stroke, manual propulsion is comfortably feasible on flat surfaces — but rapidly becomes infeasible on inclines (which add $M \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$).

### 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Camber Angle on Effective Wheelbase

The camber angle $\theta$ is the inclination of the drive wheels from vertical, measured in the frontal plane. It affects both **lateral stability** and **propulsion biomechanics**.

**Step 1 — Define the geometric setup:**

Let $W$ be the wheel-to-wheel distance measured at the ground contact points (zero camber baseline). The wheel radius is $r$. With camber $\theta$, the wheels tilt inward at the top.

**Step 2 — Project the wheel hub to compute effective wheelbase:**

The hub moves both **outward** (lateral component) and **upward** (vertical component). The horizontal component of the hub displacement is $r \cdot \sin\theta$.

**Step 3 — Write the effective wheelbase:**

$$W_{eff} = W \cdot \cos\theta + 2r \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU exam favourite):**

For a typical ultra-lightweight active wheelchair: $W = 380 \text{ mm}$ (zero camber), $r = 305 \text{ mm}$ (24-inch wheel), $\theta = 4°$ (typical active chair):

$$\begin{aligned} W_{eff} &= 380 \cdot \cos 4° + 2 \cdot 305 \cdot \sin 4° \\ &= 380 \cdot 0.9976 + 610 \cdot 0.0698 \\ &= 379.08 + 42.58 \\ &= 421.66 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** A camber of just $4°$ increases the effective wheelbase by approximately $42 \text{ mm}$, substantially improving lateral stability during sharp turns and ramp descents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight for Robotic Rehabilitation:** The camber angle determines the **mounting plane** of motor encoders in retrofitted powered wheels. Robotic drive-assist systems must accommodate non-vertical wheel planes, requiring either gimbaled encoder mounts or software-based geometric compensation in the control algorithm.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Manual Wheelchair Classes

The following matrix is a KTU high-yield comparative summary suitable for both Part A short-answer and Part B long-answer questions.

**Table 2: Manual Wheelchair Class Comparison Matrix**

| Property | Standard | Lightweight | Ultra-Lightweight | Hemi | Tilt-in-Space | Rigid Custom |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass (kg)** | $15 - 25$ | $12 - 15$ | $6 - 12$ | $13 - 16$ | $15 - 25$ | $5 - 12$ |
| **Frame Material** | Steel | Aluminium 6061 | Al 7075 / Ti / Carbon | Aluminium | Steel / Al | Al 7075 / Ti |
| **Folding** | Yes | Yes | Optional | Yes | Yes | No (rigid) |
| **Adjustability** | None | Limited | Full | Limited | Tilt angle | Full custom |
| **Propulsion Efficiency** | Low | Moderate | High | Moderate | N/A (attendant) | Highest |
| **ECOT ($\text{J/kg/m}$)** | $3.5 - 5.0$ | $2.5 - 3.5$ | $1.0 - 2.0$ | $2.0 - 3.0$ | N/A | $0.8 - 1.5$ |
| **Camber Range** | $0°$ | $0°$ | $0° - 8°$ | $0°$ | $0° - 3°$ | $0° - 12°$ |
| **Service Life (years)** | $1 - 3$ | $3 - 5$ | $5 - 10$ | $3 - 5$ | $3 - 5$ | $5 - 10$ |
| **Primary User** | Institutional | Moderate user | Active user | Hemiplegic | Pressure relief | Active user |
| **Drive Type** | Self / Attendant | Self | Self | Self / Foot | Attendant | Self |
| **Cost (INR, approx.)** | $5\text{k} - 10\text{k}$ | $10\text{k} - 25\text{k}$ | $30\text{k} - 2\text{L}$ | $12\text{k} - 20\text{k}$ | $25\text{k} - 60\text{k}$ | $40\text{k} - 3\text{L}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** ECOT (Energy Cost of Transport) decreases as the wheelchair class advances. Ultra-lightweight and rigid custom chairs reduce upper-limb metabolic cost by up to $60\%$ compared to standard chairs, which is why long-term active users MUST be prescribed higher classes despite the cost differential.

### 3.4 Derivation: Mechanical Efficiency of Push-Rim Propulsion

The mechanical efficiency $\eta$ of a manual wheelchair push-rim system is defined as the ratio of useful work done to move the user forward to the metabolic work expended by the user.

**Step 1 — Define useful work per stroke:**

During one push stroke of arc length $s$ (along the push-rim circumference), the user applies a tangential force $F_t$:

$$W_{useful} = F_t \cdot s \cdot \cos\alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between the force vector and the rim tangent (ideally $\alpha = 0°$).

**Step 2 — Define applied (metabolic) work:**

The user's metabolic energy expenditure per stroke is $E_{metabolic}$, related to oxygen consumption $\dot{V}O_2$ and the caloric equivalent of oxygen ($20.1 \text{ J/mL O}_2$):

$$W_{applied} = E_{metabolic} = 20.1 \times \Delta \dot{V}O_2 \times t_{stroke}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mechanical efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{W_{useful}}{W_{applied}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Typical KTU value range:**

$$\eta_{typical} \in [5\%, 12\%]$$

**Interpretation:** The low efficiency (5 - 12%) reflects the **biomechanical inefficiency** of upper-limb propulsion. This is the **core engineering motivation** for robotic drive-assist modules studied in OEBRT722 — supplementing the user's push-rim force with motorized assistance can effectively raise the system-level efficiency to $40\% - 60\%$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Manual Wheelchairs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Manual Wheelchair] --> B[Mass-Based Class]
    A --> C[Frame Geometry]
    A --> D[Clinical Function]
    A --> E[User Population]

    B --> B1[Standard GE 15 kg]
    B --> B2[Lightweight 12 to 15 kg]
    B --> B3[Ultra-lightweight LT 12 kg]

    C --> C1[Folding X-brace]
    C --> C2[Rigid Open-frame]
    C --> C3[Tilt-in-Space]

    D --> D1[Self-propelled]
    D --> D2[Attendant-propelled]
    D --> D3[Foot-propelled Hemi]

    E --> E1[Adult Active]
    E --> E2[Pediatric]
    E --> E3[Bariatric GE 100 kg user]
    E --> E4[Sports Athletic]
    E --> E5[Geriatric]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Robotic Retrofit Compatibility Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    U1[Standard WC] --> R1[Compatible with Attendant-Assist Only]
    U2[Lightweight WC] --> R2[Compatible with Light Power-Assist]
    U3[Ultra-lightweight WC] --> R3[Compatible with Full Power-Assist and Smart Sensors]
    U4[Hemi WC] --> R4[Compatible with Foot-Plate Encoders]
    U5[Tilt-in-Space WC] --> R5[Compatible with Tilt-Sensor Modules]
    U6[Rigid Custom WC] --> R6[Compatible with High-Performance Drive Retrofit]

    R3 --> S1[Encoder Mount on Push-Rim]
    R3 --> S2[Torque Sensor in Hub]
    R3 --> S3[IMU on Frame for Slope Detection]
    R3 --> S4[Joystick or EMG Interface]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of Push-Rim Activated Assist (PRAA)

```mermaid
graph TD
    INPUT[User Push-Rim Force] --> SENSE[Force Torque Sensor]
    SENSE --> MCU[Microcontroller Unit]
    MCU --> CTRL[Control Algorithm PID]
    CTRL --> DRIVER[Motor Driver]
    DRIVER --> MOTOR[Hub Motor]
    MOTOR --> WHEEL[Drive Wheel]
    WHEEL --> FEEDBACK[Encoder Feedback]
    FEEDBACK --> MCU

    IMU[Inertial Measurement Unit] --> MCU
    BAT[Lithium Battery Pack] --> DRIVER
    UI[User Interface Joystick EMG] --> MCU
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Clinical Decision Flow for Wheelchair Class Prescription

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Patient Assessment] --> Q1{Can User Self-Propel?}
    Q1 -->|No| ATT[Attendant-Propelled]
    ATT --> T1{Postural Control OK?}
    T1 -->|Yes| S1[Standard WC for Institutional Use]
    T1 -->|No| S2[Tilt-in-Space or Reclining WC]

    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Daily Usage Hours?}
    Q2 -->|LT 2 hours| S3[Standard or Lightweight WC]
    Q2 -->|GE 2 hours| Q3{User Weight and Build?}
    Q3 -->|GE 100 kg| S4[Bariatric Reinforced WC]
    Q3 -->|LT 100 kg| Q4{Activity Level?}
    Q4 -->|Low| S5[Lightweight WC]
    Q4 -->|Moderate| S6[Ultra-lightweight Rigid WC]
    Q4 -->|High Sport| S7[Sport-Specific Custom WC]

    Q1 -->|Foot-propulsion Only| HEMI[Hemi-Height WC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The clinical decision tree maps the **patient assessment** workflow to the correct wheelchair class. In robotic rehabilitation, this same flow governs the selection of appropriate **retrofit kits** (drive-assist motors, sensors, controllers) for upgrading existing wheelchairs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "ultra-lightweight manual wheelchair" and list THREE of its key engineering characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **ultra-lightweight manual wheelchair** is a high-performance, custom-configured mobility device with a frame mass **less than 12 kg** (typically $7 - 10 \text{ kg}$), designed for active full-time users with permanent mobility impairment.

Three key engineering characteristics:
