1. **Material:** Aluminium 7075-T6, titanium (Ti-6Al-4V), or carbon fibre composite — chosen for high strength-to-mass ratio.
2. **Frame:** Primarily **rigid open-frame** construction (no folding cross-brace) to eliminate energy loss in folding joints.
3. **Adjustability:** Fully adjustable camber angle ($0° - 8°$), rear axle position (for CoG tuning), and seat-to-floor height.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "lightweight" with "ultra-lightweight." The 12 kg threshold is the hard line. Mentioning the specific material (e.g., Al 7075 or Ti-6Al-4V) earns the third mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**What is a Hemi-height manual wheelchair? In which clinical condition is it primarily prescribed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Hemi-height manual wheelchair** is a manual wheelchair with a **reduced seat-to-floor height (typically $\leq 17.5$ inches)**, allowing the user to **propel the chair using one or both feet** in addition to, or instead of, hand propulsion on the push-rims.

**Primary clinical indication:** It is prescribed for patients with **hemiplegia following stroke**, where one side of the body has reduced motor function. Foot-propulsion on the unaffected side allows independent mobility without requiring a powered chair.

Secondary indications include: lower-limb amputee transition training, short-stature users, and patients with limited upper-limb strength.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not just write "for short people." State the specific seat-to-floor height ($\leq 17.5$ inches) and the stroke/hemiplegia context for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Classify manual wheelchairs into **six principal classes** based on the KTU 2024 syllabus. For each class, state the **mass range, frame material, and primary clinical use-case.** (7 Marks)

**(b)** With the help of a **labelled sketch or block diagram**, describe the **functional architecture of a Push-Rim Activated Assist (PRAA) system** used as a robotic retrofit for a manual wheelchair. Explain the role of the **force/torque sensor, microcontroller, motor driver, and encoder feedback** in the closed-loop control. (7 Marks)

### **Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Classification Table (7 Marks):**

| Class | Mass Range (kg) | Frame Material | Primary Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard | $\geq 15$ | Chrome-plated steel | Institutional / temporary loaner |
| Lightweight | $12 - 15$ | Aluminium 6061 | Moderate community use |
| Ultra-lightweight | $\leq 12$ | Al 7075 / Ti / Carbon | Active full-time user |
| Hemi-height | $13 - 16$ | Aluminium | Hemiplegia, foot-propulsion |
| Tilt-in-Space | $15 - 25$ | Steel / Aluminium | Pressure relief, postural support |
| Rigid Custom | $5 - 12$ | Al 7075 / Titanium | High-performance active user |

*(1 mark for correct enumeration of six classes; 6 marks distributed: 0.5 mark per correct mass range + 0.5 mark per correct material + 0.5 mark per correct use-case for 6 classes = 6 marks.)*

**Part (b) — PRAA Functional Architecture (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    PR[Push-Rim] --> FTS[Force Torque Sensor]
    FTS --> MCU[Microcontroller]
    MCU --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID --> MD[Motor Driver]
    MD --> M[Hub BLDC Motor]
    M --> WH[Drive Wheel]
    WH --> ENC[Encoder]
    ENC --> MCU
    IMU[IMU Module] --> MCU
    BAT[Li-ion Battery] --> MD
```

**Explanation of Each Block (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Force/Torque Sensor:** Mounted concentric to the push-rim hub, it measures the **tangential force** applied by the user's hand. This signal is the primary user intent input.
2. **Microcontroller (MCU):** Samples the FTS signal at $\geq 1 \text{ kHz}$, runs the closed-loop control algorithm, and computes the **assistive torque command** proportional to the user's applied force.
3. **Motor Driver:** Receives low-power PWM commands from the MCU and delivers high-current three-phase power to the **brushless DC hub motor** integrated into the drive wheel.
4. **Encoder Feedback:** Mounted on the motor shaft, it provides **real-time wheel angular velocity** to the MCU, closing the velocity/torque loop and ensuring the motor output matches the commanded assist level.

The system thus implements a **transparent** (intention-aware) power-assist: motor torque is scaled to the user's input, so the user perceives no added mass or lag.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Many students draw the diagram but forget to **explicitly close the feedback loop** back to the MCU. A sensor-only or open-loop diagram loses 2 marks. The **encoder feedback arrow returning to the MCU** is mandatory.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the **engineering significance of the camber angle** in an ultra-lightweight rigid manual wheelchair. Derive the relationship between camber angle $\theta$ and the **effective wheelbase** $W_{eff}$ for a 24-inch wheel ($r = 305 \text{ mm}$) with zero-camber wheelbase $W = 380 \text{ mm}$, at $\theta = 6°$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Define **Energy Cost of Transport (ECOT)** for a manual wheelchair user. Why is ECOT **significantly higher** for a standard wheelchair compared to an ultra-lightweight chair? List **TWO engineering modifications** that can reduce ECOT. (7 Marks)

### **Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Camber Angle and Effective Wheelbase (7 Marks):**

**Significance (3 marks):**
- **Lateral Stability:** Positive camber increases the effective wheelbase, raising the wheel's resistance to side-tip during sharp turns or ramp descents.
- **Biomechanical Efficiency:** Cambered wheels bring the push-rim closer to the user's shoulder, allowing a more natural arc of motion and reducing shoulder abduction.
- **Protection:** Camber protects the user's hands from doorframes and obstacles.
- **Robotic Mounting:** Camber determines the encoder-mount plane in retrofitted powered wheels.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

When the drive wheels are cambered by angle $\theta$ from vertical, each wheel hub displaces horizontally by $r \cdot \sin\theta$ outward. The total effective wheelbase becomes:

$$W_{eff} = W \cdot \cos\theta + 2r \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned} W_{eff} &= 380 \cdot \cos 6° + 2 \cdot 305 \cdot \sin 6° \\ &= 380 \cdot 0.9945 + 610 \cdot 0.1045 \\ &= 377.91 + 63.75 \\ &= 441.66 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The effective wheelbase increases from $380 \text{ mm}$ to $\approx 441.66 \text{ mm}$, an increase of $61.66 \text{ mm}$ ($\approx 16\%$ improvement) — directly improving lateral stability.

**Part (b) — ECOT Definition and Reduction (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Energy Cost of Transport (ECOT) is the metabolic energy expended by a user per unit body mass per unit distance travelled:

$$ECOT = \frac{E_{metabolic}}{m_{user} \cdot d}$$

Typical units: $\text{J \cdot kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^{-1}$.

**Why Standard > Ultra-lightweight (3 marks):**

Standard wheelchairs have:
- **Higher mass** ($18 - 25 \text{ kg}$ vs. $7 - 10 \text{ kg}$) → more rolling resistance, more inertia
- **Inefficient folding frame** → energy loss in cross-brace joints
- **No camber** → biomechanically inefficient arm stroke
- **Higher rolling resistance tyres** → more frictional dissipation
- These factors increase $E_{metabolic}$ for the same $d$, yielding ECOT values of $3.5 - 5.0 \text{ J/kg/m}$ vs. $0.8 - 1.5 \text{ J/kg/m}$ for ultra-lightweight.

**Two Engineering Modifications to Reduce ECOT (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Reduce frame mass** via aluminium 7075-T6 or titanium substitution, lowering rolling and inertial loads.
2. **Add a robotic power-assist drive** (e.g., PRAA system) to supplement user push-rim force, reducing metabolic demand per unit distance by $40\% - 60\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** A common error is forgetting the factor of $2$ in the second term of the $W_{eff}$ formula. Both wheels contribute; missing it loses 1 mark. Also, the units ($r$ in mm, $W$ in mm) MUST be consistent — mixing cm and mm is a frequent KTU mistake.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Pitfalls for "Classes of Manual Wheelchairs" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Lightweight" with "Ultra-lightweight"** — Always state the 12 kg threshold explicitly.
> 2. **Omitting the clinical use-case** — Each class MUST be tied to a patient population (e.g., hemiplegia, active paraplegic, bariatric).
> 3. **Not drawing the closed feedback loop** in robotic retrofit diagrams — the encoder-to-MCU arrow is mandatory.
> 4. **Forgetting to factor the 2 in camber formula** — both wheels contribute.
> 5. **Mixing units (cm vs. mm, kg vs. N)** — Be consistent throughout any numerical derivation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Six principal classes:** Standard, Lightweight, Ultra-lightweight, Hemi, Tilt-in-Space, Rigid Custom.
- [x] **Mass thresholds:** $\geq 15$ (Standard), $12 - 15$ (Lightweight), $\leq 12$ kg (Ultra-lightweight).
- [x] **Hemi-height** is defined by seat-to-floor height $\leq 17.5$ inches, used for foot-propulsion in hemiplegia.
- [x] **Tilt-in-Space** tilts the entire seat $0° - 45°$ to relieve pressure; reclining models add backrest recline.
- [x] **Ultra-lightweight** chairs use **Al 7075, Ti-6Al-4V, or carbon fibre** — not plain aluminium.
- [x] **Camber angle** $\theta$ increases effective wheelbase: $W_{eff} = W \cos\theta + 2r \sin\theta$.
- [x] **Propulsion force** on flat ground: $F_p = M(\mu_r g + a)$.
- [x] **ECOT** for standard chairs is $3.5 - 5.0 \text{ J/kg/m}$; for ultra-lightweight is $0.8 - 1.5 \text{ J/kg/m}$.
- [x] **Push-rim mechanical efficiency** is only $5\% - 12\%$, motivating robotic drive-assist retrofits.
- [x] **RESNA / ISO 7176** are the governing standards for wheelchair classification.
- [x] For **robotic rehabilitation retrofit design**, the wheelchair class determines the mounting plane of encoders, motor torque sizing, and controller architecture.
- [x] **Standard Tilt Range:** $0° - 45°$; **Hemi Seat-to-Floor Height:** $\leq 17.5$ inches.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frame Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frame Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, **Frame Design** refers to the systematic engineering process of conceptualizing, dimensioning, materializing, and validating the **mechanical skeleton (structure/chassis)** of a rehabilitation robot. The frame is the load-bearing architectural element that physically links the actuators, sensors, transmission systems, and the **end-effector (patient interface)** to the ground or to a mounting platform, while satisfying constraints of **rigidity, compliance, payload capacity, safety, ergonomics, and kinematic fidelity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** The frame is *not* the actuators, nor the controller — it is the **passive, structural backbone** whose geometry dictates the reachable workspace and whose stiffness dictates the positioning accuracy at the patient's limb.

Mathematically, the frame design problem is a constrained multi-objective optimization:

$$\min_{\{L_i, A_i, \rho_i\}} \; \{ m(\mathbf{x}), \; \delta(\mathbf{x}), \; C(\mathbf{x}) \}$$
$$\text{subject to:} \;\; \sigma_i \le \sigma_{allow}, \;\; \delta \le \delta_{allow}, \;\; DOF = DOF_{req}$$

Where:
- $L_i, A_i, \rho_i$ are the **length, cross-sectional area, and material density** of the $i^{th}$ link.
- $m$ is the total mass, $\delta$ is the deflection, $C$ is the cost.
- $\sigma_{allow}$ and $\delta_{allow}$ are the **maximum allowable stress and deflection** (derived from safety factors).
- $DOF_{req}$ is the required number of **Degrees of Freedom** for the therapeutic task.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Skeleton of the Robot"

Imagine a human body. The bones form a rigid **frame**; the muscles are the **actuators**; the tendons are the **transmissions**; the brain is the **controller**. A rehabilitation robot's frame plays exactly the same role as the human skeleton:

- **Bones (Frame):** Hold everything in the right place, resist gravity and external forces, define body proportions.
- **Joints (Kinematic Pairs):** Allow controlled motion at specific points.
- **Muscles (Actuators):** Generate forces — but they need a sturdy skeleton to *push against*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy for students:** Think of a **wheelchair-mounted robotic arm** used for stroke rehabilitation. The "frame" of this arm is the metallic linkage you see — the silver/aluminum beams connecting the motor housings to the patient's hand-grip. If that frame bends even 1 mm under the patient's spastic jerk, the robot cannot deliver accurate therapy. Hence, the frame must be **stiff**, **light**, and **safe**.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded for Memorization)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Rehab Robots) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Factor of Safety** | $FoS$ | **2.0 to 4.0** (medical devices) | Ratio of failure load to working load |
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | **70 GPa (Al)**, **200 GPa (Steel)**, **110 GPa (Ti)** | Stiffness of the frame material |
| **Specific Stiffness** | $E/\rho$ | **Al: 25.9 GPa·cm³/g**, **Ti: 26.2 GPa·cm³/g** | Stiffness per unit weight — critical for wearable rehab robots |
| **Static Deflection Limit** | $\delta_{allow}$ | **≤ 0.1 mm** at end-effector | Maximum permissible positional error |
| **Patient Payload** | $m_p$ | **5–20 kg** (limb + spastic force) | Effective load the frame must carry |
| **Natural Frequency** | $f_n$ | **≥ 20 Hz** (avoid human tremor band) | Frame should not resonate with physiological tremor (3–12 Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the natural frequency ≥ 20 Hz so important?** Human physiological tremor lies in the 3–12 Hz band. If the robot frame resonates in this band, the controller will be fighting the structural vibration, leading to **unstable therapy, patient discomfort, and potential injury**. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 1.4 Classification of Frames in Rehabilitation Robotics (Overview)

| Type | Description | Example Rehab Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Serial Frame** | Open kinematic chain — links connected end-to-end | MIT-Manus (shoulder/elbow planar module) |
| **Parallel Frame** | Multiple limbs connect ground to end-effector (Stewart-Gough type) | CarriR (lower-limb gait trainer) |
| **Cable-Suspended Frame** | Frame is a fixed gantry, motion delivered by cables | LOKOMAT (gait rehab) — gantry frame |
| **Exoskeleton Frame** | Anthropomorphic frame worn by patient | ReWalk, Ekso GT, BLEEX |
| **End-effector Frame** | Robot acts on the distal limb; proximal frame is grounded | InMotion ARM |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Frame Design Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

The design of a rehabilitation robot frame follows a structured, iterative engineering workflow. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to **list, justify, and apply** each step:

1. **Task Analysis:** Identify the therapeutic goal — gait training, upper-limb reaching, ankle mobilization, etc. This determines the **required Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, workspace volume, and force range.
2. **Kinematic Synthesis:** Choose the **kinematic architecture** (serial, parallel, hybrid) and apply **Gruebler's Equation** to verify the DOF count.
3. **Static Load Estimation:** Compute the **worst-case forces and moments** the frame will experience — including patient weight, spastic hypertonia (involuntary muscle contraction, force ≈ 80–200 N), and gravity of moving links.
4. **Material Selection:** Use **Ashby charts** to choose a material that optimizes **specific stiffness ($E/\rho$)** and **specific strength ($\sigma_y/\rho$)** while being biocompatible (no toxic ion release in wearables).
5. **Cross-Sectional Sizing:** Solve the **bending stress** and **deflection equations** for the dominant load case to size the cross-section (typically hollow circular/rectangular for $J$ and $I$ maximization at minimum mass).
6. **Dynamic Analysis:** Compute the **first natural frequency** $f_n$ and ensure $f_n \ge 20$ Hz (or at least 3× the controller bandwidth).
7. **Safety Factor Verification:** Confirm $FoS = \sigma_{yield} / \sigma_{working} \ge 2.5$ for medical applications per **ISO 13482** and **ISO 14971** (risk management).
8. **Prototyping & Validation:** Manufacture, instrument with **strain gauges**, and perform **modal analysis** (impact hammer or shaker test) to validate the analytical model.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why start with task analysis?** Because a frame designed for upper-limb reaching (workspace ≈ 0.5 m sphere) is useless for gait training (workspace ≈ 1.5 m linear path). The frame's geometry is dictated by the therapy, not by the engineer's preference.
- **Why optimize $E/\rho$ specifically?** In wearable exoskeletons, the frame mass is carried by the patient. A 1 kg heavier frame can cause shoulder fatigue, skin pressure sores, and rejection of the device.
- **Why hollow cross-sections?** For a given mass of material, a **hollow circular tube** gives the highest **second moment of area ($I$)**, which directly reduces bending deflection $\delta \propto 1/I$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Vertical pipe symbols have been replaced with `\vert` to keep the markdown tables intact.

### A. Kinematic / Structural Formulas

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom (Gruebler) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| Bending Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | $M$ = bending moment, $c$ = distance to outer fiber, $I$ = 2nd moment of area |
| Axial Stress | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $F$ = axial load, $A$ = cross-sectional area |
| Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | $T$ = torque, $r$ = outer radius, $J$ = polar moment |
| Combined (von Mises) Stress | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | For ductile materials under combined loading |
| Factor of Safety | $FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | $FoS \ge 2.5$ for medical rehab devices |

### B. Deflection Formulas (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory)

| Loading Case | Maximum Deflection $\delta_{max}$ | Slope at Free End $\theta_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cantilever, point load $P$ at tip | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^3}{3 E I}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{P L^2}{2 E I}$ |
| Cantilever, UDL $w$ over span $L$ | $\delta = \dfrac{w L^4}{8 E I}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{w L^3}{6 E I}$ |
| Simply supported, point load $P$ at center | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 E I}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{P L^2}{16 E I}$ |
| Simply supported, UDL $w$ | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{w L^3}{24 E I}$ |

### C. Section Properties (2nd Moment of Area)

| Cross-Section | $I$ (about neutral axis) | $J$ (polar) | Area $A$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solid circle, radius $R$ | $I = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$ | $J = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2}$ | $\pi R^2$ |
| Hollow circle, $R_o, R_i$ | $I = \dfrac{\pi (R_o^4 - R_i^4)}{4}$ | $J = \dfrac{\pi (R_o^4 - R_i^4)}{2}$ | $\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ |
| Solid rectangle, $b \times h$ | $I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ | $J \approx \beta b h^3$ | $b h$ |
| Hollow rectangle | $I = \dfrac{B H^3 - b h^3}{12}$ | — | $BH - bh$ |

### D. Dynamic / Vibration Formulas

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Frequency (SDOF) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = stiffness, $m$ = effective mass |
| Effective stiffness, cantilever | $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3}$ | From $F = k \delta$ |
| First Natural Freq. of uniform beam (cantilever) | $f_1 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E I}{\rho A L^4}}$ | $\beta_1 L = 1.875$ for cantilever |
| Rayleigh's quotient (approx.) | $f_1 \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{static}}}$ | Useful sanity check: $f_1 \approx 0.5/\sqrt{\delta_{static}[\text{m}]}$ Hz |

### E. Material Indices (Ashby Method)

| Objective | Material Index to Maximize |
|---|---|
| Lightweight **stiff** frame (bending-dominated) | $M_1 = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ |
| Lightweight **strong** frame (strength-dominated) | $M_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$ |
| Minimize deflection at minimum weight | $M_3 = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are the *exact tools* a robotic rehabilitation frame designer uses in **SolidWorks Simulation, ANSYS, or COMSOL**. KTU Part-B 14-mark questions frequently ask students to size a link using these formulas and verify a factor of safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gruebler's Equation for DOF of a Planar Rehabilitation Frame

Gruebler's equation gives the **mobility (M)** of a planar mechanism. We derive it from first principles for a planar frame with $n$ links and $j$ lower-pair (1-DOF) joints.

**Step 1 — Start with the DOF of each rigid body in the plane.** A single rigid link in 2D space has 3 degrees of freedom (2 translations + 1 rotation).

**Step 2 — Total DOF of an unconnected system of $n$ links.** If the $n$ links float freely (not connected), they have $3n$ DOF total.

**Step 3 — DOF removed by each joint.** Each 1-DOF lower-pair joint (revolute R or prismatic P) constrains the relative motion by removing **2** degrees of freedom. Each 2-DOF joint (cam, gear pair) removes **1** degree of freedom.

**Step 4 — Account for the ground link.** The ground is conventionally counted as 1 of the $n$ links.

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total DOF available} &= 3n \\
\text{DOF removed by joints} &= 2 j_1 + 1 j_2 \\
\text{Net mobility } M &= 3n - 2 j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Adjust convention.** Since one of the $n$ links is the (fixed) ground, replace $n$ with $(n-1)$ moving links:

$$\boxed{\;M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2\;}$$

**Step 7 — Worked Example: 2-DOF planar ankle rehabilitation robot.** Suppose we have a 4-bar mechanism with: $n = 4$ links (including ground), $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints, $j_2 = 0$.

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

A 4-bar has only 1 DOF. To get 2 DOF (e.g., dorsiflexion + plantarflexion), we must add **one more link and two more revolute joints** to form a 5-bar or 6-bar mechanism:

$$M = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 0 = 12 - 10 = 2 \; \text{DOF} \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cantilever Beam Deflection (Rehab Robot Link Under Patient Force)

The most common load case in a rehabilitation frame: a **horizontal link of length $L$** is driven by a joint at one end (the proximal end, treated as a "fixed" cantilever root) and bears a transverse force $P$ at the **distal end** (the patient's hand or foot). We need the tip deflection $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Set up the bending moment equation.** Take the origin at the fixed end. At a distance $x$ from the origin, the bending moment (from the load $P$ at the tip) is:

$$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation.**

$$E I \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate once to get the slope.**

$$E I \frac{d y}{dx} = -P \left( Lx - \frac{x^2}{2} \right) + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary condition at the fixed end ($x = 0$): slope is zero.**

$$0 = -P(0) + C_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_1 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Integrate a second time to get the deflection.**

$$E I y(x) = -P \left( \frac{L x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6} \right) + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply boundary condition at the fixed end ($x = 0$): deflection is zero.**

$$0 = 0 + C_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_2 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate deflection at the free end ($x = L$).**

$$E I \, y(L) = -P \left( \frac{L \cdot L^2}{2} - \frac{L^3}{6} \right) = -P L^3 \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{6} \right) = -P L^3 \cdot \frac{1}{3}$$

Taking magnitudes (deflection is downward, so $|y(L)| = \delta$):

$$\boxed{\;\delta_{max} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sizing a Hollow Aluminum Link — Full Numerical Walk-Through

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark style):** A wrist rehabilitation robot has a horizontal link of length $L = 300$ mm that must withstand a transverse patient force $P = 50$ N at its tip. The link is a hollow circular tube of **Aluminum 6061-T6** ($E = 70$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa). Allowable deflection is $\delta_{allow} = 0.1$ mm and the design must have $FoS \ge 2.5$. Find the inner and outer radii of the tube.

**Step 1 — Set up the deflection constraint.**

$$\delta = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I} \le \delta_{allow}$$

Solve for the required second moment of area:

$$I_{req} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E \, \delta_{allow}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numbers.** Convert all units to SI: $L = 0.3$ m, $P = 50$ N, $E = 70 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\delta_{allow} = 0.1 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{req} &= \frac{50 \times (0.3)^3}{3 \times (70 \times 10^9) \times (0.1 \times 10^{-3})} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 0.027}{3 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{1.35}{21 \times 10^6} \\
&= 6.43 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Choose a wall-thickness ratio.** Let $k = R_i / R_o = 0.9$ (a common lightweight choice — thin wall). Then:

$$I = \frac{\pi R_o^4}{4} (1 - k^4) = \frac{\pi R_o^4}{4} (1 - 0.9^4) = \frac{\pi R_o^4}{4} (0.3439)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_o$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_o^4 &= \frac{4 I_{req}}{\pi (1 - k^4)} = \frac{4 \times 6.43 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 0.3439} \\
&= \frac{2.572 \times 10^{-7}}{1.0805} = 2.380 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 \\
R_o &= (2.380 \times 10^{-7})^{1/4} = 2.21 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 22.1 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Inner radius.**

$$R_i = 0.9 \times 22.1 = 19.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Check the strength (stress) constraint.** Maximum bending moment at the fixed root:

$$M_{max} = P \cdot L = 50 \times 0.3 = 15 \text{ N·m}$$

Bending stress at the outer fiber ($c = R_o$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{M_{max} \cdot R_o}{I} = \frac{15 \times 0.0221}{6.43 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= 5.16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 5.16 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Factor of Safety.**

$$FoS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{240}{5.16} = 46.5$$

This is *much* higher than the required 2.5 — meaning the link is **over-designed for strength** but **right-sized for stiffness**. This is a classic insight in lightweight rehab robot design: **stiffness, not strength, is usually the governing constraint**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The frame dimension is governed by the **stiffness requirement** ($\delta \le 0.1$ mm), not the material strength. This is a very common KTU conceptual question: *"Why do rehabilitation robot frames use stiffness-driven design rather than strength-driven design?"*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Frame Design

| Design Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|
| Is the robot **wearable** (carried by patient)? | Maximize $E/\rho$ (Ti, CF composites); minimize mass | Mass is less critical; consider cost |
| Is the workspace > 1 m? | Use **parallel or gantry** frame (better stiffness-to-weight) | Serial frame acceptable |
| Does patient exert spastic forces > 100 N? | Add **compliant element** + FoS ≥ 3.0 | FoS ≥ 2.0 is sufficient |
| Must the frame be MRI-compatible? | Use **non-ferrous** materials (Al, polymer, Ti) | Standard steel acceptable |
| Is the therapy **gait training**? | Frame must handle **2000+ N** ground reaction force | Upper-limb forces are < 50 N |
| Is hygiene/disinfection important? | Use **anodized Al, medical-grade polymers**; avoid crevices | Surface finish less critical |

## 3.5 Quick-Reference Numerical Examples (Practice Values)

> [!NOTE]
> These values appear frequently in KTU problem sets.

| Parameter | Wrist Rehab Robot | Gait Trainer (LOKOMAT-class) |
|---|---|---|
| Link length $L$ | 0.3 m | 1.0 m |
| Payload force $P$ | 50 N | 2000 N |
| Material | Al 6061-T6 | Steel AISI 304 |
| $E$ | 70 GPa | 200 GPa |
| Allowable $\delta$ | 0.1 mm | 1.0 mm |
| Required $I$ | $6.43 \times 10^{-8}$ m$^4$ | $3.33 \times 10^{-6}$ m$^4$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Rehabilitation Robot Frame (Block Diagram)

This block diagram maps the **physical and functional interactions** between the frame and the other subsystems of a rehabilitation robot. This is the standard Mermaid block-level representation used in KTU answers when a physical engineering drawing would be too complex.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Limb / End-Effector Interface] -->|Force F, Moment M| B[Frame Linkage Network]
    B -->|Carries & Positions| C[Actuator Modules BLDC Motors + Harmonic Drives]
    B -->|Supports| D[Sensor Suite Encoders, F/T Sensors, IMUs]
    B -->|Defines Workspace| E[Kinematic Architecture Serial, Parallel, or Hybrid]
    E -->|Determines| F[Degrees of Freedom via Gruebler Equation]
    F -->|Must Match| G[Therapeutic Task DOF]
    B -->|Resists| H[External Loads Gravity, Spasticity, Inertia]
    H -->|Generates| I[Internal Stresses sigma, tau]
    I -->|Must Satisfy| J[Strength Constraint sigma less than sigma_allow]
    B -->|Undergoes| K[Deflection delta]
    K -->|Must Satisfy| L[Stiffness Constraint delta less than delta_allow]
    B -->|Exhibits| M[Natural Frequency f_n]
    M -->|Must Satisfy| N[Dynamics Constraint f_n greater than 20 Hz]
    J --> S[Verified Frame Design]
    L --> S
    N --> S
    S -->|Feeds into| P[ISO 13482 Compliance Check]
    P -->|Outputs| Q[Certified Rehabilitation Robot]
```

## 4.2 Frame Design Workflow (Process Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Task Analysis] --> S2[Step 2: Define DOF and Workspace]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Choose Kinematic Type]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Gruebler Equation]
    S4 --> S5{DOF Matches Task?}
    S5 -- No --> S3
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 5: Estimate Loads P, M, T]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Select Material via Ashby Chart]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Compute Required I and A]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Choose Cross Section]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Compute Deflection and Stress]
    S10 --> S11{Both FoS and delta OK?}
    S11 -- No --> S9
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Step 10: Check Natural Frequency f_n]
    S12 --> S13{f_n greater than 20 Hz?}
    S13 -- No --> S9
    S13 -- Yes --> S14[Step 11: CAD and FEA Validation]
    S14 --> S15[Step 12: Prototype and Modal Test]
    S15 --> S16[Step 13: ISO Certification]
    S16 --> S17[Step 14: Clinical Trials]
```

## 4.3 Load-Transfer Pathway in an Exoskeleton Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Ground Reaction Force GRF at Footplate] -->|Transmitted through| L1[Shank Link]
    L1 -->|Through| J1[Knee Joint Pivot] --> L2[Thigh Link]
    L2 -->|Through| J2[Hip Joint Pivot] --> L3[Pelvic Link]
    L3 -->|Through| J3[Back Link] --> L4[Shoulder Link]
    L4 -->|Through| J4[Upper Arm Link] --> L5[Forearm Link]
    L5 -->|Applied to| P2[Patient Hand / End Effector]
    P2 -.Spastic Reaction Force.-> L5
    L5 -.Bending Moment M.-> L4
    L4 -.Bending Moment M.-> L3
    L3 -.Bending Moment M.-> L2
    L2 -.Bending Moment M.-> L1
    L1 -.Reaction to GRF.-> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how the bending moment grows as we move up the exoskeleton. The **thigh link** sees the cumulative moment from shank + foot + payload, while the **forearm link** only sees the hand load. Hence in a real exoskeleton, links are **progressively larger** from distal (hand) to proximal (hip) — this is a direct visualization of the **load-path principle** in frame design.

## 4.4 Stress Distribution Across a Frame Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Beam Cross Section] --> R[Top Fiber: Max Compression sigma_c]
    Q --> M[Neutral Axis: sigma equals 0]
    Q --> N[Bottom Fiber: Max Tension sigma_t]
    R --> O[Linear Stress Distribution: sigma equals M y divided by I]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Material Yield: sigma_y]
    P --> Q1{Working Stress less than sigma_y divided by FoS?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Safe Design]
    Q1 -- No --> S2[Increase I or Change Material]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reminder (as per University Exam pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark questions, ~50–80 words, no choice.
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions, internal choice between Q-A and Q-B, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> - Bloom's levels tested: Apply, Analyze, Design (per Revised Taxonomy 2001).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the term "Frame" in the context of a rehabilitation robot. List any four design constraints that govern its design.**

**Model Answer:**

A **frame** in a rehabilitation robot is the **passive mechanical structure** (links, joints, housings, mounting plates) that physically supports and geometrically defines the relative motion of the actuators, sensors, and the patient interface. It is the **load-bearing skeleton** of the robot, analogous to the human skeletal system.

The four governing design constraints are:

1. **Stiffness:** Deflection at the end-effector must remain below a therapeutic threshold (typically ≤ 0.1 mm for upper-limb rehab).
2. **Strength:** The factor of safety $FoS = \sigma_y / \sigma_{working}$ must be ≥ 2.5 per ISO 13482.
3. **Mass:** For wearable exoskeletons, mass must be minimized via high specific stiffness materials ($E/\rho$).
4. **Natural Frequency:** The first bending mode $f_1$ must lie **above 20 Hz** to avoid resonance with physiological tremor (3–12 Hz).

*(Word count: 110 — concise and within the KTU 3-mark limit.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]

**Why is the hollow circular cross-section preferred over a solid circular cross-section for links in a rehabilitation robot frame? Justify with a relevant formula.**

**Model Answer:**

A hollow circular cross-section is preferred because, **for the same mass of material**, it provides a **larger second moment of area ($I$)** and therefore a **smaller bending deflection**.

**Justification via formula:**

For a solid circle of radius $R$:

$$I_{solid} = \frac{\pi R^4}{4}, \quad A_{solid} = \pi R^2$$

For a hollow circle with outer radius $R_o$ and inner radius $R_i$ (with $k = R_i/R_o < 1$):

$$I_{hollow} = \frac{\pi R_o^4 (1 - k^4)}{4}, \quad A_{hollow} = \pi R_o^2 (1 - k^2)$$

The deflection of a cantilever tip is $\delta = PL^3 / (3EI)$. Because $\delta$ is inversely proportional to $I$, a **larger $I$ gives a stiffer frame** for the same load $P$ and length $L$. A hollow tube with $k = 0.9$ retains about 34% of the area but only loses ~1% of the moment of inertia is *retained* (since $1 - 0.9^4 = 0.344$), so the structure becomes very efficient. This is the **stiffness-to-weight** principle that is critical in wearable exoskeletons.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice format (per KTU 2024):** Students answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Stiffness-Driven Frame Design

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> A 2-DOF planar wrist rehabilitation robot has a forearm link modeled as a **horizontal cantilever** of length $L = 250$ mm. A patient exerts a transverse force $P = 40$ N at the **distal end** (the handgrip). The link is to be fabricated from a **hollow circular tube of Aluminum 6061-T6** ($E = 70$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa, density $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³).
>
> **(a)** Compute the **required second moment of area** $I_{req}$ if the maximum allowable tip deflection is $\delta_{allow} = 0.05$ mm. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** If the wall-thickness ratio is $k = R_i / R_o = 0.92$, find the **outer and inner radii** of the tube. Also verify that the resulting **factor of safety** in bending is at least **2.5**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $L = 0.25$ m, $P = 40$ N, $E = 70 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\delta_{allow} = 0.05 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

The cantilever tip deflection formula is:

$$\delta = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I}$$

Solve for $I$:

$$I_{req} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E \, \delta_{allow}}$$

Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{req} &= \frac{40 \times (0.25)^3}{3 \times (70 \times 10^9) \times (0.05 \times 10^{-3})} \\
&= \frac{40 \times 0.015625}{3 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{0.625}{1.05 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= 5.952 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{I_{req} \approx 5.95 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

For a hollow circular tube:

$$I = \frac{\pi R_o^4}{4} (1 - k^4), \quad \text{where } k = 0.92$$

Compute $(1 - k^4)$:

$$k^4 = 0.92^4 = 0.7164 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 - k^4 = 0.2836$$

Solve for $R_o^4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_o^4 &= \frac{4 I_{req}}{\pi (1 - k^4)} = \frac{4 \times 5.95 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 0.2836} \\
&= \frac{2.381 \times 10^{-7}}{0.8910} = 2.673 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$R_o = (2.673 \times 10^{-7})^{1/4} = 2.273 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 22.7 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_i = 0.92 \times 22.7 = 20.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Check factor of safety.** Maximum bending moment at the root:

$$M_{max} = P \cdot L = 40 \times 0.25 = 10 \text{ N·m}$$

Bending stress at the outer fiber:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot R_o}{I_{req}} = \frac{10 \times 0.0227}{5.95 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.815 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 3.82 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of Safety:

$$FoS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{240}{3.82} \approx 62.8$$

Since $FoS = 62.8 \gg 2.5$, the design is **massively over-safe in strength**, confirming that **stiffness, not strength, governs the design of rehabilitation robot frames**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — DOF Verification and Material Selection

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

> A designer proposes a **5-bar planar parallel mechanism** for a 2-DOF ankle rehabilitation robot. The mechanism has **5 moving links** (including the ground) and **6 revolute joints** (each with 1 DOF).
>
> **(a)** Use **Gruebler's equation** to verify that the mechanism has the required **2 degrees of freedom** for combined dorsiflexion/plantarflexion and inversion/eversion motion. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Justify the selection of **Aluminum 6061-T6 over mild steel AISI 1020** for the frame links using the **Ashby material index** for a stiffness-driven, mass-critical wearable application. The relevant material data is given below. (7 marks)
>
> | Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Al 6061-T6 | 70 | 2700 | 240 |
> | AISI 1020 Steel | 200 | 7850 | 350 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Gruebler's equation for planar mechanisms:**

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$$

Given: $n = 5$ (links including ground), $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints, $j_2 = 0$ (no 2-DOF joints).

Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(6) - 0 \\
&= 3 \times 4 - 12 \\
&= 12 - 12 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — this gives $M = 0$, meaning the mechanism is **a structure (locked)**, not a mechanism. The proposed design is **kinematically invalid**. To obtain $M = 2$, we must **add one link and one joint** (5-bar should actually be 6-bar for 2 DOF in plane), or use a different configuration.

**Corrected 6-bar configuration** for 2 DOF: $n = 6$ (links including ground), $j_1 = 7$:

$$M = 3(6 - 1) - 2(7) - 0 = 15 - 14 = 1$$

That gives only 1 DOF. We need 2 DOF, so add another R joint: $j_1 = 8$:

$$M = 3(6 - 1) - 2(8) - 0 = 15 - 16 = -1 \; \text{(over-constrained)}$$

A better-known 2-DOF planar parallel structure is the **5-bar with $j_1 = 5$**:

$$M = 3(5 - 1) - 2(5) = 12 - 10 = 2 \; \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The correct configuration is a 5-bar mechanism with **5 revolute joints** (not 6). The original proposal with 6 joints is a **statically determinate structure** with mobility zero, not a 2-DOF manipulator. The designer must **remove one redundant revolute joint** to achieve the required 2 DOF.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

For a **stiffness-driven, mass-critical** wearable application, the relevant **Ashby material index** is:

$$M_1 = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$$

(The exponent $1/2$ comes from the fact that deflection $\delta \propto L^3 / (EI)$, and for fixed $\delta$ and minimum mass, $m = \rho A L \propto \rho \cdot (I / c^2)^{1/2} \cdot L$, which yields the index $E^{1/2}/\rho$ when $c$ is fixed.)

**Compute for Aluminum 6061-T6:**

$$M_1^{Al} = \frac{(70 \times 10^9)^{1/2}}{2700} = \frac{2.646 \times 10^{4}}{2700} = 9.80 \; (\text{SI units})$$

**Compute for AISI 1020 Steel:**

$$M_1^{Steel} = \frac{(200 \times 10^9)^{1/2}}{7850} = \frac{4.472 \times 10^{4}}{7850} = 5.70 \; (\text{SI units})$$

**Comparison:**

$$\frac{M_1^{Al}}{M_1^{Steel}} = \frac{9.80}{5.70} = 1.72$$

**Conclusion:** Aluminum 6061-T6 has a material index **1.72 times higher** than mild steel for stiffness-driven, mass-critical design. Hence, for a **wearable rehabilitation robot frame** (where the patient carries the frame mass), **Aluminum 6061-T6 is the preferred material**.

Although steel is **stronger in absolute terms** ($\sigma_y = 350$ MPa vs. 240 MPa for Al), strength is **not the limiting factor** — stiffness is. Steel would produce a 2.9× heavier frame ($\rho_{steel}/\rho_{Al} = 2.91$) for the same stiffness, leading to patient fatigue and rejection of the device. Additionally, Al 6061-T6 offers:
- **Corrosion resistance** (no rusting, important for sweat/cleaning fluids in rehab clinics).
- **Biocompatibility** when anodized.
- **MRI compatibility** (non-ferrous).
- **Ease of CNC machining** for complex frame geometries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (14-Mark Question B):**
> Students frequently make three costly errors in this question:
> 1. **Forgetting to count the ground as one of the $n$ links** — this gives the wrong DOF by exactly 3.
> 2. **Using the wrong Ashby index** ($E/\rho$ for axial loading vs. $E^{1/2}/\rho$ for bending). The correct index for **bending-dominated** rehab frames is $E^{1/2}/\rho$, not $E/\rho$. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the unit of $E$** — using GPa instead of Pa in the index calculation gives a numerically wrong but dimensionally still "small" answer. Always convert to **base SI units (Pa, kg/m³)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This recap distills the entire Module 3 Frame Design topic into exam-ready bullets. Memorize the items tagged **[MUST]**.

- **[MUST]** **Frame Definition:** A frame is the **passive, load-bearing skeleton** of a rehab robot — not the actuator, not the controller. It carries loads, defines workspace, and positions the end-effector.
- **[MUST]** **Gruebler's Equation (Planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$. Always count **ground as one of the $n$ links**.
- **[MUST]** **Cantilever Deflection (most common load case):** $\delta = P L^3 / (3 E I)$. Tip slope: $\theta = P L^2 / (2 E I)$.
- **[MUST]** **Bending Stress:** $\sigma = M c / I$. Torsional Shear: $\tau = T r / J$.
- **[MUST]** **Factor of Safety for medical rehab robots:** $FoS \ge 2.5$ (some sources 3.0 for wearable exoskeletons). Formula: $FoS = \sigma_y / \sigma_{working}$.
- **[MUST]** **Natural Frequency Rule:** $f_1 \ge 20$ Hz to avoid resonance with human physiological tremor (3–12 Hz).
- **[MUST]** **Governing Constraint:** Stiffness, not strength, usually governs rehab frame sizing (because typical operating stresses are 5–10% of yield).
- **[MUST]** **Hollow > Solid:** Hollow cross-sections maximize $I/A$ ratio — same stiffness at lower mass.
- **[MUST]** **Material Index for bending, mass-critical:** $M_1 = E^{1/2} / \rho$. Top candidates: CFRP, Al 6061-T6, Ti-6Al-4V.
- **[MUST]** **Stiffness formula for cantilever beam:** $k = 3 E I / L^3$. Combined with $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m_{eff}}$.
- **[MUST]** **Load Path:** In an exoskeleton, bending moment accumulates from distal (small) to proximal (large) — link sizes scale accordingly.
- **[MUST]** **Spastic Force:** Patient hypertonia can generate **80–200 N** involuntary forces — must be included in worst-case load.
- **[MUST]** **Design Standards:** **ISO 13482** (Personal care robots), **ISO 14971** (Risk management), **ISO 13849** (Safety-related parts of control systems).
- **[KEY INSIGHT]** **Rayleigh's Quick Check:** For a SDOF approximation, $f_1 \approx 0.5 / \sqrt{\delta_{static}[\text{m}]}$ Hz. If $\delta_{static} = 0.1$ mm $= 10^{-4}$ m, then $f_1 \approx 50$ Hz — well above 20 Hz. Use this as a sanity check.
- **[KEY INSIGHT]** **Wearable vs. Stationary:** Wearable frames → minimize mass; stationary frames → minimize cost.
- **[COMMON MISTAKE]** Using $E$ in GPa without conversion in formulas — always convert to **Pa** before substituting.
- **[COMMON MISTAKE]** Confusing **second moment of area $I$** (units m⁴) with **polar moment $J$** (units m⁴) — $I$ is for bending, $J$ is for torsion.
- **[COMMON MISTAKE]** Forgetting that **stiffness-driven design is the norm** — students keep trying to maximize strength, but in rehab robotics, **mass + stiffness is the real optimization target**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MATERIALS IN ROBOTIC REHABILITATION

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 — OEBRT722)**
> This module studies the engineering substrates that make physical human–robot interaction (pHRI) in rehabilitation devices safe, durable, and biocompatible. The topic "Materials" covers the classification, mechanical behavior, and selection criteria of materials used in **exoskeletons, prosthetics, orthoses, soft robotic actuators, and end-effector rehabilitation devices**.

---

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the context of robotic rehabilitation, **Materials** refer to the structured engineering substances—metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, and smart/functional materials—that form the structural, actuation, sensing, and skin-contact layers of rehabilitation robots. The KTU 2024 syllabus defines material selection in rehabilitation engineering as a *multi-objective constrained optimization* problem where the chosen material must simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Biocompatibility** (ISO 10993) — non-toxic, non-allergenic skin/ tissue contact.
2. **Mechanical Compliance** — tunable stiffness to match human joints (muscle stiffness ≈ **10–100 N/m**, bone ≈ **10–20 GPa** modulus).
3. **Fatigue Resistance** — withstand >**10⁶ cycles** for long-term wearable use.
4. **Processability** — 3D-printable, sterilizable, lightweight (density < **2000 kg/m³** ideal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> "Rehabilitation materials are functionally graded engineering substances engineered to operate in direct or indirect contact with the human body, providing structural support, actuation, sensing, or a combination thereof, while meeting the dual requirements of biomechanical compatibility and long-term physiological safety."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a rehabilitation robot like a second skin + a powered skeleton fused together:**
> - The **outer layer** (touching the skin) is like a **soft cotton glove** — soft polymers, silicone, fabric (cares for comfort).
> - The **middle layer** is like the **bones and ligaments** — composites and light alloys (carries load).
> - The **active layer** is like **muscle fibers** — smart materials that contract and sense (provides motion and feedback).
>
> Just as you cannot build a human body out of a single material (bone ≠ muscle ≠ skin), a rehabilitation robot needs a **hierarchy of materials**, each picked for a specific job.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus of cortical bone | **15–20 GPa** | Upper stiffness limit for orthoses |
| Young's Modulus of muscle | **0.01–0.1 MPa** | Lower stiffness limit for soft actuators |
| Density of Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | **4430 kg/m³** | Benchmark lightweight alloy |
| Density of PDMS (silicone) | **970 kg/m³** | Benchmark soft skin material |
| Body temperature | **36.5 ± 0.5 °C** | Operating temp for shape-memory alloys |
| Biocompatibility standard | **ISO 10993-1** | Mandatory regulatory test |
| Cycle life (rehabilitation) | **> 10⁶ cycles** | 3–5 years of daily use |

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Rehabilitation materials are broadly classified into **four families**:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Structural Materials** — Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, Composites.
> 2. **Smart / Functional Materials** — Shape Memory Alloys (SMA), Shape Memory Polymers (SMP), Piezoelectric, Magneto-rheological fluids, Electroactive polymers.
> 3. **Biomaterials** — Tissue-engineered scaffolds, hydrogels, biocompatible coatings.
> 4. **Soft Materials** — Silicones, elastomers, textiles for wearable interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Material Choice Governs Rehabilitation Robot Performance

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that material choice is **not** a downstream engineering decision — it is the **primary design driver**. Three governing principles apply:

1. **Impedance Matching Principle** — The robot's mechanical impedance at the human–robot interface must be tuned to the human limb's impedance to prevent skeletal loading and skin trauma. This requires materials with **tunable stiffness** spanning **3 decades** (kPa to GPa).
2. **Weight-to-Strength Ratio** — Wearable rehabilitation devices must weigh **< 5 kg** total for upper-limb and **< 15 kg** for lower-limb, mandating high specific strength (σ/ρ).
3. **Biocompatibility Hierarchy** — Materials are tiered: **Implant-grade > Skin-contact > External-shell**.

## 2.2 Family-wise Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Structural Metals
- **Titanium Alloys (Ti-6Al-4V):** High specific strength, excellent fatigue life, biocompatible (forms passive TiO₂ layer), non-magnetic (MRI-safe).
- **Aluminum Alloys (7075, 6061):** Lightweight (ρ ≈ **2700 kg/m³**), used in exoskeleton frames; less fatigue-resistant.
- **Stainless Steel (316L):** High wear resistance, used in joints, pins, and load-bearing pivots; heavier.
- **Magnesium Alloys (AZ91D):** Ultra-light (ρ ≈ **1800 kg/m³**); emerging for wearables; corrosion-sensitive.

### 2.2.2 Polymers
- **Thermoplastics (PLA, ABS, Nylon, PEEK):** 3D-printable, PEEK is biocompatible and sterilizable.
- **Elastomers (Silicone, TPU, rubber):** Used in cuffs, soft pads; high compliance.
- **Hydrogels:** Water-rich polymer networks used in tissue interfacing and drug-eluting scaffolds.

### 2.2.3 Composites
- **Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP):** Specific stiffness > **100 GPa/(g/cm³)**, the gold standard for exoskeleton links.
- **Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP):** Cheaper alternative for orthotic shells.
- **Natural fiber composites (flax, jute):** Emerging sustainable option.

### 2.2.4 Smart Materials (KTU High-Priority)
- **Shape Memory Alloys (Nitinol):** Recovers shape upon heating; used in actuators and stents.
- **Piezoelectric (PZT, PVDF):** Converts mechanical↔electrical energy; used in sensors and micro-actuators.
- **Electroactive Polymers (EAP):** Large strain (> 100%) under voltage; soft actuators.
- **Magneto-rheological (MR) fluids:** Viscosity changes in magnetic field; used in **Variable Stiffness Actuators (VSAs)**.
- **Shape Memory Polymers (SMP):** Lightweight, programmable stiffness; emerging in soft orthoses.

### 2.2.5 Biomaterials and Coatings
- **Hydroxyapatite coatings** on titanium for osseointegration in bone-anchored prostheses.
- **PEG-based hydrogels** for drug delivery and anti-fouling.
- **Silver nanoparticle coatings** for antibacterial surfaces.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations for any KTU numerical/derivation on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Engineering stress | $F$ = force [N], $A$ = area [m²] |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus (Hooke's law) | $E$ [Pa] |
| 4 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress in orthosis beam | $M$ [N·m], $c$ [m], $I$ [m⁴] |
| 5 | $k = \dfrac{E A}{L}$ | Axial stiffness of link | $k$ [N/m] |
| 6 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | Elastic strain energy stored | $U$ [J] |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ | Specific strength (material index) | [Pa·m³/kg] |
| 8 | $W_{shape} = E \cdot A \cdot \varepsilon_{max}$ | Shape memory work density (Nitinol) | [J/m³] |

> **Auxiliary Non-Equations to Memorize:**
> - Biocompatibility ranking: **Titanium > Ti-6Al-4V > 316L SS > Co-Cr > Ni-Ti (Nitinol)**.
> - Fatigue limit for steels: ≈ **0.5 × σ_uts** (for ferrous alloys only).
> - Densities (kg/m³): **Ti = 4430, Al = 2700, Steel = 7850, CFRP = 1600, PDMS = 970**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lokomat (Hocoma)** uses **aluminum + steel** exoskeleton links with **cuffs made of soft polymer + fabric** to interface with stroke patients.
- **ReWalk exoskeleton** uses **carbon fiber struts** for lightweight lower-limb support.
- **Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs)** are commonly **3D-printed in polypropylene or PA12 nylon**.
- **Soft pneumatic rehabilitation gloves** (e.g., Bioservo) use **silicone elastomer (PDMS)** bladders actuated by air.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bending Stress in a Rehabilitation Orthosis Beam

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A polymer orthosis link of rectangular cross-section supports a bending moment $M$. Derive the maximum bending stress and identify the governing material index.

### Step 1 — Define Geometry
Consider a beam of length $L$, rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ (width × thickness). The second moment of area is:

$$
I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Euler–Bernoulli Bending Equation
The bending stress at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$
\sigma(y) = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Maximum Stress Location
Maximum stress occurs at the extreme fiber, where $y = c = h/2$:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot (h/2)}{I} = \dfrac{M \cdot (h/2)}{b h^{3}/12}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify
Multiplying numerator and denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \dfrac{M \cdot 6}{b h^{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{6M}{b h^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Material Selection Criterion
For safe design, $\sigma_{max} \le \sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{n}$, where $n$ is the safety factor (typically $n = 2$ to $3$ for rehabilitation devices, KTU 2024 convention).

$$
\dfrac{6M}{b h^{2}} \le \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{n}
$$

Rearranging for the minimum required yield strength:

$$
\sigma_{y} \ge \dfrac{6 M n}{b h^{2}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The material index for a bending-dominated orthosis link is $\eta_{bend} = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{1/3}}{\rho}$, favoring **CFRP or Ti-6Al-4V** over stainless steel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Stored in a Compliant Rehabilitation Spring

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A series-elastic actuator (SEA) used in rehabilitation has a linear spring of stiffness $k$. Show that the maximum elastic energy it can store equals $\tfrac{1}{2} k x_{max}^{2}$, and compute the work density for Nitinol.

### Step 1 — Hooke's Law at Displacement $x$
$$
F(x) = k \cdot x
$$

### Step 2 — Energy is the Integral of Force Over Displacement
$$
U = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} F(x)\, dx = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} k x\, dx
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral
$$
U = k \left[ \dfrac{x^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{x_{max}} = \dfrac{1}{2} k x_{max}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Stress and Strain
Since $k = \dfrac{E A}{L}$ and $x_{max} = \varepsilon_{max} L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{E A}{L} \cdot (\varepsilon_{max} L)^{2} \\
  &= \dfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon_{max}^{2} \cdot A \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Work Density (Energy per Unit Volume)
$$
w = \dfrac{U}{A L} = \dfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon_{max}^{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation for Nitinol
For Nitinol, $E \approx 30 \text{ GPa} = 30 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, recoverable strain $\varepsilon_{max} \approx 0.08$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 30 \times 10^{9} \times (0.08)^{2} \\
  &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 30 \times 10^{9} \times 0.0064 \\
  &= 9.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ J/m}^{3} \\
  &= 96 \text{ MJ/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comparison:** Steel spring has $w \approx 1$ MJ/m³, Aluminum $w \approx 0.4$ MJ/m³. **Nitinol stores ~100× more elastic energy per unit volume** — a key reason for its use in compact rehabilitation actuators.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Strength Comparison for Exoskeleton Links

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare three candidate materials for an exoskeleton femoral link on a 70 kg wearer who weighs the device. Required axial load $F = 700$ N, length $L = 0.4$ m, safety factor $n = 3$.**

| Material | $\sigma_{uts}$ [MPa] | $\rho$ [kg/m³] | $\eta = \sigma_{uts}/\rho$ [kN·m/kg] |
|---|---|---|---|
| Al 7075 | 572 | 2810 | 203.6 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 950 | 4430 | 214.4 |
| CFRP | 1500 | 1600 | **937.5** |

### Required Cross-Sectional Area
For each material:

$$
A_{min} = \dfrac{F \cdot n}{\sigma_{uts}}
$$

- Al 7075: $A_{min} = \dfrac{700 \times 3}{572 \times 10^{6}} = 3.67 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2} = 3.67 \text{ mm}^{2}$
- Ti-6Al-4V: $A_{min} = \dfrac{700 \times 3}{950 \times 10^{6}} = 2.21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2} = 2.21 \text{ mm}^{2}$
- CFRP: $A_{min} = \dfrac{700 \times 3}{1500 \times 10^{6}} = 1.40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2} = 1.40 \text{ mm}^{2}$

### Link Mass $m = \rho A L$
- Al 7075: $m = 2810 \times 3.67\times10^{-6} \times 0.4 = 4.12$ g
- Ti-6Al-4V: $m = 4430 \times 2.21\times10^{-6} \times 0.4 = 3.92$ g
- CFRP: $m = 1600 \times 1.40\times10^{-6} \times 0.4 = \mathbf{0.90}$ g

> **Conclusion:** CFRP is the optimal exoskeleton link material — **4.5× lighter** than aluminum for the same load. This derivation appears frequently in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Material Selection for Rehab Devices)

| Application | Primary Load | Critical Property | Recommended Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exoskeleton frame | Bending + axial | Specific stiffness | CFRP, Ti-6Al-4V |
| Skin-contact cuff | Compression | Softness + biocompatibility | Silicone (PDMS), TPU |
| Soft actuator bladder | Inflation strain | High strain, no delamination | Silicone elastomer, TPU |
| Bone-anchored implant | Static load | Osseointegration | Ti + Hydroxyapatite coating |
| Sensor layer | Mechanical → electrical | Piezoelectric coefficient | PVDF, PZT |
| Variable stiffness joint | Shear | MR fluid yield stress | MR fluid + coil |
| 3D-printed orthotic shell | Bending | Printability + strength | PLA, Nylon (PA12), PETG |
| Cable-driven glove | Tension | Flex fatigue | UHMWPE (Dyneema) fibers |

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define biocompatibility as applied to rehabilitation materials. Name any two materials certified as biocompatible for skin-contact applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1+1+1):**
Biocompatibility is the ability of a material to perform its desired function in contact with living tissue without producing any adverse local or systemic effects, as defined under **ISO 10993-1**.
Two biocompatible materials for skin-contact use in rehabilitation:
1. **Medical-grade silicone (PDMS)** — used in soft cuffs and pneumatic actuators.
2. **Titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4V** — used in external orthotic frames and bone-anchored implants.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the ISO standard citation. Always mention the standard number.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) and Shape Memory Polymer (SMP) with one application of each in rehabilitation robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 + 1.5):**

| Property | SMA (e.g., Nitinol) | SMP (e.g., Polyurethane) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Heat (thermal) | Heat, light, or moisture |
| Recoverable strain | ~8% | ~100–400% |
| Stiffness recovery | High (metallic) | Lower (polymeric) |
| Density | ~6450 kg/m³ | ~1000–1300 kg/m³ |
| App in rehab | Compact joint actuator | Soft orthosis with tunable stiffness |

> **Application in rehab:** SMA — actuator in wearable hand exoskeleton; SMP — adaptive ankle-foot orthosis that stiffens during stance phase.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Part B requires **balanced coverage of all sub-parts**. Do not over-write part (a) and leave part (b) under-developed. Also, when asked to "compare" or "discuss," examiners expect a **table or numerical comparison**, not just prose. Skipping numerical examples loses **3–4 marks** immediately.

---

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify the materials used in robotic rehabilitation devices. With neat sketches, explain the role of each category and provide at least one engineering example for each.

**Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks):**

The four main categories of materials used in robotic rehabilitation are:

**1. Structural Metals (2 marks):**
Provide high stiffness, strength, and fatigue resistance. Examples:
- **Ti-6Al-4V** — exoskeleton joint pivots, bone-anchored fixtures.
- **Al 7075** — lightweight upper-limb orthotic shells.
- **316L Stainless Steel** — pins, bearings, high-wear surfaces.

**2. Polymers and Elastomers (1.5 marks):**
Provide skin comfort, flexibility, and processability. Examples:
- **PDMS (silicone)** — soft actuator bladders and cuffs.
- **TPU** — wearable strap interfaces.
- **PEEK** — sterilizable, MRI-compatible implant-grade polymer.

**3. Smart/Functional Materials (2 marks):**
Materials that respond to external stimuli to provide actuation or sensing.
- **Nitinol (SMA)** — thermally triggered actuators in hand exoskeletons.
- **PVDF (piezoelectric)** — sensitive strain sensors on glove fingertips.
- **MR fluids** — variable stiffness joints for safe pHRI.
- **EAPs** — soft artificial muscles for assistive suits.

**4. Biomaterials & Composites (1.5 marks):**
- **CFRP** — high specific stiffness exoskeleton links.
- **Hydroxyapatite-coated titanium** — promotes bone growth at implant interface.
- **Hydrogels (PEG, PVA)** — tissue-mimicking scaffolds and cushioning.

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** for the layered architecture of these materials in a typical rehabilitation device.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A polymer orthotic link is subjected to a bending moment of **M = 12 N·m**. The cross-section is rectangular with width **b = 20 mm** and height **h = 8 mm**. Using a safety factor of **n = 3**, determine:
(i) the maximum bending stress in the link,
(ii) the minimum required yield strength of the material,
(iii) suggest two suitable materials and justify with the specific strength criterion.

**Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Stress (2 marks)**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 M}{b h^{2}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \dfrac{6 \times 12}{0.020 \times (0.008)^{2}} \\
            &= \dfrac{72}{0.020 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}} \\
            &= \dfrac{72}{1.28 \times 10^{-6}} \\
            &= 5.625 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
            &= 56.25 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Minimum Required Yield Strength (2 marks)**

$$
\sigma_{y,min} = n \cdot \sigma_{max} = 3 \times 56.25 = 168.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Material Suggestion with Specific Strength Justification (3 marks)**

Specific strength $\eta = \sigma_y / \rho$:

| Material | $\sigma_y$ [MPa] | $\rho$ [kg/m³] | $\eta$ [kN·m/kg] |
|---|---|---|---|
| Al 6061-T6 | 276 | 2700 | 102.2 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 880 | 4430 | 198.6 |
| CFRP | 1500 | 1600 | **937.5** |
| PA12 Nylon (3D printed) | 50 | 1010 | 49.5 |

> **Selected Materials:** **CFRP** and **Ti-6Al-4V**.
> **Justification:** Both satisfy the yield requirement (≥ 168.75 MPa) and offer the highest specific strengths, making them ideal for weight-sensitive orthotic applications. CFRP gives the lightest link; titanium gives the best damage tolerance and biocompatibility.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a stress-strain diagram, explain the mechanical behavior of Nitinol as a Shape Memory Alloy. Discuss its use in rehabilitation actuators and quote two key limitations.

**Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Mechanical Behavior (3 marks):**
Nitinol (Nickel-Titanium alloy) exhibits a unique stress-strain curve featuring four critical temperatures:
- **Austenite start (A_s)** and **Austenite finish (A_f)** — high-temperature phase (stiff, ~30 GPa).
- **Martensite start (M_s)** and **Martensite finish (M_f)** — low-temperature phase (soft, detwinnable).

**Pseudoelastic (superelastic) loading path:**
At temperatures above A_f, loading produces a large plateau at constant stress (~500 MPa) where austenite transforms to detwinned martensite. Unloading reverses the transformation, recovering the strain (~8%).

**Shape Memory Effect (SME) loading path:**
At temperatures below A_f, the material is plastically deformed in martensite; upon heating above A_f, it reverts to its original austenite shape.

**Application in Rehabilitation (2 marks):**
- **Compact wearable actuators** in hand/finger exoskeletons (e.g., the **Nitinol-powered soft glove** for stroke rehabilitation).
- **Active ankle-foot orthoses** that contract during swing phase.
- **Stents and bone anchors** in prosthetic limbs.

**Two Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Hysteresis losses** (10–15% energy dissipated per cycle) → low efficiency, heating issues.
2. **Slow response** (cooling-limited, typically 1–2 Hz) — not suitable for high-bandwidth tasks.
3. **Non-linear control complexity** — phase transition is temperature-dependent and hysteretic.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for work density stored in a linear elastic spring. Compute the work density for a Nitinol spring of Young's modulus **E = 30 GPa** and maximum recoverable strain **ε_max = 0.08**, and compare it with a steel spring (E = 200 GPa, ε_max = 0.01).

**Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Derivation of Work Density (3 marks)**

$$
U = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} F\, dx = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} k x\, dx = \dfrac{1}{2} k x_{max}^{2}
$$

Substituting $k = \dfrac{EA}{L}$ and $x_{max} = \varepsilon_{max} L$:

$$
U = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{EA}{L} \cdot (\varepsilon_{max} L)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon_{max}^{2} \, A L
$$

Dividing by volume $V = AL$:

$$
w = \dfrac{U}{V} = \dfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon_{max}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Numerical Computation for Nitinol (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{NiTi} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 30 \times 10^{9} \times (0.08)^{2} \\
         &= 9.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ J/m}^{3} = 96 \text{ MJ/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Computation for Steel (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{steel} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times (0.01)^{2} \\
          &= 1.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ J/m}^{3} = 10 \text{ MJ/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison and Conclusion (1 mark)**

$$
\dfrac{w_{NiTi}}{w_{steel}} = \dfrac{96}{10} = 9.6
$$

> **Conclusion:** Nitinol stores approximately **9.6 times** more elastic energy per unit volume than steel. This makes Nitinol the preferred actuator material for **compact, lightweight rehabilitation devices** such as wearable exoskeletons and prosthetic hands where space and mass are at a premium.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Materials in Robotic Rehabilitation (KTU 2024 Module 3)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Rehabilitation materials = structurally engineered, biocompatible substances for pHRI devices; must satisfy ISO 10993.
- ☐ **Four Material Families:** Structural Metals · Polymers/Elastomers · Smart Materials · Biomaterials/Composites.
- ☐ **Key Smart Materials:** Nitinol (SMA), PVDF/PZT (piezoelectric), MR fluids, EAPs, SMPs.
- ☐ **Biocompatibility Ranking (Skin Contact):** Ti > Ti-6Al-4V > 316L SS > Co-Cr > Nitinol.
- ☐ **Specific Strength Champion:** **CFRP** (η ≈ 937 kN·m/kg) — lightest exoskeleton link.
- ☐ **Stiffness Range to Cover:** from PDMS (~0.5 MPa) to cortical bone (~15 GPa) — 4–5 decades.
- ☐ **Master Equations:** $\sigma = F/A$, $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$, $\sigma_{max} = 6M/(bh^2)$, $w = \tfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon_{max}^{2}$, $k = EA/L$.
- ☐ **Nitinol Magic Number:** Work density ≈ **96 MJ/m³** — ~10× steel.
- ☐ **Standard Fatigue Requirement:** > **10⁶ cycles** for daily-use rehabilitation devices.
- ☐ **Processability:** 3D-printable polymers (PLA, Nylon, TPU) dominate orthotic shells.
- ☐ **KTU Pitfall:** Always quote the **safety factor (n ≥ 2)** and the **biocompatibility standard (ISO 10993)** in design answers.
- ☐ **Real-World Examples to Quote:** Lokomat (steel + Al), ReWalk (CFRP), soft pneumatic gloves (PDMS).

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **wheelchair–rider system** is a coupled man–machine dynamic assembly in which the human occupant (rider) and the mobility platform (wheelchair frame, wheels, motors, controllers, and sensors) are treated as a single integrated unit for the purpose of design, analysis, and control. In the context of **robotic rehabilitation**, the wheelchair is no longer a passive mechanical chair — it becomes a **robotic mobility platform** that adapts to the rider's posture, intent, environment, and residual physical capacity.

The rider contributes **anthropometric mass, posture, muscular effort, and physiological intent**, while the wheelchair contributes **structural support, propulsion, braking, suspension, sensing, and intelligent navigation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):** *"The wheelchair–rider system is the human–machine interface in assistive mobility, where biomechanical, ergonomic, and mechatronic parameters must be jointly optimized to achieve stable, energy-efficient, and safe locomotion for individuals with motor impairments."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are wearing a heavy backpack while walking. You don't think of the backpack as separate from yourself — you instinctively lean forward when climbing stairs, shift your weight when turning, and brace when stopping suddenly. The backpack becomes part of **your dynamic system**.

A wheelchair is exactly that — but in reverse. The rider **sits on** the wheelchair, and the combined center of gravity (CG) determines whether the chair tips, accelerates, or stops. Just as your brain predicts and corrects the backpack's effect, a **robotic wheelchair** uses sensors and controllers to predict and correct the rider's motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A wheelchair–rider is like a **motorcyclist with a sidecar**. The sidecar (wheelchair) adds mass, shifts the combined CG, and affects cornering — both the rider and the sidecar must be designed **together** for stability.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standardized parameters govern every wheelchair–rider design calculation:

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average adult body mass | $m_r$ | **75 kg** | ISO 7176-5 |
| Wheelchair mass (powered) | $m_w$ | **50–120 kg** | ISO 7176 |
| Combined CG height | $h_{cg}$ | **0.45–0.65 m** | RESNA Standards |
| Wheelbase | $L$ | **0.45–0.60 m** | ISO 7176 |
| Track width | $W$ | **0.55–0.70 m** | ISO 7176 |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance | $\mu_r$ | **0.005–0.015** (rubber on tile) | KTU Reference |
| Static friction coefficient (drive wheel) | $\mu_s$ | **0.6–0.9** | Engineering Handbook |
| Maximum safe slope | $\theta_{max}$ | **8°–10° (1:12 ADA ramp)** | ADA Standards |
| Pushrim mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{pr}$ | **0.08–0.16** | Brubaker & McLaurin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act)** standard of **1:12 ramp slope (≈ 4.76°)** is the international benchmark. A wheelchair–rider system should climb this slope **safely without rear-tipping**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Wheelchair–Rider System

The system can be decomposed into **four interacting subsystems**:

1. **Rider subsystem** — mass, posture, joint stiffness, voluntary control signals (EMG, joystick, sip-and-puff).
2. **Structural subsystem** — frame, seat, backrest, armrests, footrests; defines the **support polygon**.
3. **Propulsion subsystem** — manual pushrims OR motorized drive wheels (DC/BLDC), gears, batteries.
4. **Sensing & control subsystem** — encoders, IMU, force sensors, ultrasonic/LiDAR, microcontroller.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Decision

- **Why lower the CG?** A lower combined CG height $h_{cg}$ increases the **static stability margin** against forward and rear tipping.
- **Why track width $W$ > wheelbase $L$?** A wider track resists **lateral (sideways) tipping** during cornering.
- **Why rear-wheel drive (RWD)?** Placing drive wheels behind the CG allows the front castors to swivel freely for **maneuverability** while the rear wheels provide **traction for climbing**.
- **Why mid-wheel drive (MWD)?** MWD places the drive wheels **directly under the rider's CG**, giving the smallest turning radius and best indoor maneuverability — but is the most **tip-sensitive**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Notice:** All absolute values and norms below use $\vert \cdot \vert$ instead of the vertical pipe $\vert$ so as not to break markdown table syntax. This is per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Combined system mass** | $M = m_r + m_w$ | $m_r$ rider, $m_w$ chair | kg |
| 2 | **Weight (downward force)** | $W_t = M \cdot g$ | $g = 9.81$ | N |
| 3 | **Rolling resistance force** | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g$ | $\mu_r$ rolling coeff. | N |
| 4 | **Grade-climbing force** | $F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ slope angle | N |
| 5 | **Total tractive force** | $F_t = M \cdot g (\mu_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ | combined | N |
| 6 | **Static tip-over threshold (rear)** | $\tan\theta_{tip} = \dfrac{L_r}{h_{cg}}$ | $L_r$ CG-to-rear-axle | — |
| 7 | **Static tip-over threshold (forward)** | $\tan\theta_{tip,f} = \dfrac{L_f}{h_{cg}}$ | $L_f$ CG-to-front-axle | — |
| 8 | **Lateral (sideways) tip threshold** | $\tan\phi_{tip} = \dfrac{W/2}{h_{cg}}$ | $W$ track width | — |
| 9 | **Power required (level ground)** | $P = F_r \cdot v = \mu_r M g v$ | $v$ velocity | W |
| 10 | **Power required (slope)** | $P = M g v (\mu_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ | combined | W |
| 11 | **Kinetic energy of system** | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} M v^2$ | $v$ linear velocity | J |
| 12 | **Stopping distance (kinetic)** | $d_s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$ | $a_{max}$ decel | m |
| 13 | **Traction (no slip) limit** | $F_{traction,max} = \mu_s N$ | $N$ normal force | N |
| 14 | **Battery energy required** | $E_{bat} = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{\eta_{motor} \eta_{drive}}$ | $\eta$ efficiencies | Wh |
| 15 | **Dynamic stability margin** | $DSM = \dfrac{L}{2 h_{cg} \tan(\phi)}$ | $\phi$ turning angle | m |
| 16 | **CG location from rear axle** | $L_r = \dfrac{m_r x_r + m_w x_w}{M}$ | $x_r, x_w$ positions | m |
| 17 | **Pushrim mechanical advantage** | $MA = \dfrac{r_{wheel}}{r_{pushrim}}$ | $r$ radii | — |
| 18 | **Shoulder torque on pushrim** | $\tau_{sh} = F_{push} \cdot r_{pushrim}$ | $F_{push}$ push force | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units reminder for KTU exams:** Always express angles in **radians** in trigonometric functions inside a calculator, but **degrees** in written answers. Always show the **SI unit** in the final answer — examiners allocate **½ mark** specifically for correct units.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in the Real World

- **Clinical prescription:** Therapists use the **tip-over thresholds** to decide whether a patient can safely use a particular chair on community ramps.
- **Robotic wheelchair design:** The **Dynamic Stability Margin (DSM)** is fed back in real time to a **model-predictive controller (MPC)** that auto-slows the chair when cornering too fast.
- **Insurance & ADA compliance:** The **1:12 slope** corresponds to $\theta = \arctan(1/12) \approx 4.76°$. Any chair that rear-tips on this slope fails ADA.
- **Rehabilitation robotics:** The **pushrim mechanical advantage** determines whether a partial-paralysis patient can self-propel a manual chair — if $MA < 1.5$, self-propulsion is often impossible.
- **Smart wheelchair R&D:** Companies like **WHILL, Permobil, and DEKA** use the **battery energy equation** to advertise real-world range (typically 15–25 km per charge for a 50 Ah Li-ion pack).
