topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are "Components" in Robotic Rehabilitation?

In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, "Components" refers to the integrated set of **electromechanical, sensory, computational, and biomechanical subsystems** that collectively enable a rehabilitation robot to perceive the patient's intent, interact safely with the human body, and deliver controlled therapeutic motion or assistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Robotic rehabilitation components are the modular functional building blocks — including sensors, actuators, mechanical transmission elements, controllers, power systems, and human-machine interfaces — that together form a closed-loop, patient-cooperative system designed to restore, substitute, or augment impaired sensorimotor function.*

The components can be broadly grouped into **five functional pillars**:

| Pillar | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Perceive patient state and environment | Force sensors, EMG, encoders |
| **Actuation** | Produce controlled motion/torque | DC motors, pneumatic muscles |
| **Mechanical Structure** | Transmit and guide motion | Exoskeleton, cables, linkages |
| **Control & Computation** | Process data, generate commands | Microcontroller, PID, admittance |
| **Power & Safety** | Supply energy, protect patient | Batteries, E-stops, fuses |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Robot as a Human Therapist's Toolkit"**
> Imagine a physiotherapist treating a stroke patient. The therapist's **eyes and hands** (sensors) feel muscle tension, the **arms and grip** (actuators + mechanical structure) move the patient's limb, the **brain** (controller) decides how much force to apply, and the **energy from food** (power system) keeps everything running. Robotic rehabilitation components are the engineered equivalents of these human abilities — but with the precision, repeatability, and endurance that a human therapist cannot sustain over thousands of repetitive movements.

## 1.3 Why These Components Matter in Rehabilitation

A standard industrial robot is designed for *speed, accuracy, and stiffness*. A **rehabilitation robot** is designed for *safety, compliance, and patient-cooperativity*. This fundamental shift in design philosophy dictates the choice of every component:

- **Compliance over stiffness** → Series Elastic Actuators (SEA) instead of rigid gearboxes.
- **Safety over speed** → Redundant force/torque sensing at the human interface.
- **Intent detection over position tracking** → Bioelectric sensors (EMG/EEG) and intent decoders.
- **Wearable over fixed-base** → Lightweight exoskeleton materials (aluminium, carbon fibre) and compact battery power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The choice of components in rehabilitation robots is governed by three overriding constraints: **(1) Patient Safety (ISO 13482, IEC 60601)**, **(2) Biomechanical Compatibility (anthropometric fit, joint ranges)**, and **(3) Therapeutic Efficacy (repeatability, adaptability to recovery stages)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Component 1 — Sensors

Sensors convert physical quantities into electrical signals readable by the controller. In rehabilitation robotics, sensors fall into two classes:

### 2.1.1 Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors
Measure the **robot's own state** (position, velocity, current).

- **Optical Encoders:** Output discrete digital pulses per unit rotation. Resolution defined as pulses per revolution (PPR). Common values: **500–10,000 PPR** (industry standard), with sub-arc-second precision after quadrature decoding (×4).
- **Resolvers / Synchros:** Rugged analog rotary transducers using mutual induction. Used in MRI-compatible rehab robots where magnetic interference rules out encoders.
- **Potentiometers:** Simple voltage-divider output. Linear relationship between wiper position and output voltage.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs):** Combine 3-axis accelerometer, 3-axis gyroscope, and 3-axis magnetometer. Output: roll, pitch, yaw via sensor fusion (Madgwick / Mahony / Kalman filter).

### 2.1.2 External (Exteroceptive) Sensors
Measure the **patient's state and intent**.

- **Force/Torque Sensors (F/T):** Strain-gauge based, mounted between end-effector and patient interface. Typical ranges: **±50 N to ±500 N** for upper-limb rehab, **±2000 N** for lower-limb gait assistance. The 6-axis F/T sensor (e.g., ATI Mini45) measures three forces and three torques simultaneously.
- **Electromyography (EMG) Sensors:** Detect electrical potential of muscle contraction. Surface EMG (sEMG) amplitude is **proportional to muscle activation level** (Henneman's size principle).
- **Electroencephalography (EEG) Sensors:** Capture cortical activity for Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) driven rehabilitation. Used in motor imagery-based stroke therapy.
- **Tactile / Pressure Sensor Arrays:** E-skin and FSR (Force-Sensitive Resistor) arrays for grip force feedback and prevention of tissue damage.

## 2.2 Component 2 — Actuators

Actuators convert electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic energy into mechanical motion. Rehabilitation robots demand **back-drivability and inherent compliance** — properties missing in traditional stiff industrial actuators.

### 2.2.1 Electric Motors
- **DC Servo Motors:** Closed-loop with encoder feedback. Torque constant $K_t$ (Nm/A) and back-EMF constant $K_e$ (V·s/rad), with $K_t = K_e$ in SI units.
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors:** Higher power density, longer life, less electrical noise (important for EMG/EEG environments).
- **Harmonic Drive (HD) Geared Motors:** High reduction ratio (30:1 to 320:1), zero backlash — used in high-precision rehab devices like the **InMotion ARM**.
- **Direct-Drive Motors:** No gearbox → high back-drivability. Used in research platforms like the **MIT Manus**.

### 2.2.2 Pneumatic Artificial Muscles (PAMs)
- **McKibben Muscles:** A bladder encased in a braided mesh. Contraction upon pressurization produces axial force.
- Characteristics: **High power-to-weight ratio**, inherent compliance, low cost. Used in devices like the **RUPERT** (Robotic Upper Extremity Repetitive Trainer).

### 2.2.3 Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)
A spring is intentionally placed in series between the motor and the load. The spring deflection $\delta$ is measured, and force $F = k \cdot \delta$ is computed, where $k$ is the spring stiffness.
- **Advantage:** Tolerance to shock loads, stable force control, intrinsic safety — **critical for human-robot interaction**.
- **Example:** Used in the **LOPES** gait trainer and **MIT Anklebot**.

### 2.2.4 Variable Stiffness Actuators (VSA)
Mechanisms (antagonistic PAMs, leaf springs) that allow **real-time adjustment of joint stiffness**. This matches the variable impedance of human muscles and is a frontier research area.

## 2.3 Component 3 — Mechanical Transmission

- **Cables and Tendons (Tendon-Driven Systems):** Lightweight, remote actuation, suitable for wearable exoskeletons. Example: **Cable-Driven Arm Rehabilitation (CAREX)**.
- **Planar Linkages:** Five-bar, parallelogram, and Pantograph mechanisms in devices like the **MIME** (Mirror-Image Motion Enabler).
- **Gimbals and Spherical Wrists:** Provide multiple degrees of freedom in compact form factors for wrist/ankle rehabilitation.
- **Bowden Cables:** Transfer force over a distance, allowing actuators to be mounted on the waist/back of an exoskeleton, reducing distal mass.

## 2.4 Component 4 — Controllers and Computation

- **PID Controller:** The workhorse of rehab control. Output $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$.
- **Impedance Control:** Models the robot as a virtual mass-spring-damper. Desired dynamic relation: $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$.
- **Admittance Control:** Inverse of impedance. Used when robot is highly back-drivable.
- **Adaptive Controllers (MRAC, Sliding Mode):** Adjust gains in real-time to accommodate patient recovery progression.
- **Embedded Platforms:** Arduino, STM32, Raspberry Pi, and dedicated DSP boards (e.g., dsPIC) for real-time control loops.

## 2.5 Component 5 — Power and Safety

- **Lithium-ion / Li-Po battery packs:** Nominal voltage **3.7 V/cell**, typical rehab exoskeletons use **24 V or 48 V** bus.
- **Fuses, E-Stops, Watchdog Timers:** Hardware and software safety layers.
- **Compliance Standards:** ISO 13482 (personal care robots), IEC 60601-1 (medical electrical equipment safety), ISO 13849 (safety-related parts of control systems).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF equation | V, V·s/rad, rad/s | Motor speed estimation |
| $T = K_t \cdot I$ | Motor torque equation | Nm, Nm/A, A | Current-torque control |
| $F = k \cdot \delta$ | SEA force from spring deflection | N, N/m, m | Force control in SEA |
| $\theta = \frac{2\pi \cdot n}{PPR}$ | Encoder angle per pulse | rad, count, PPR | Position sensing |
| $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_{total}}$ | Potentiometer output | V, Ω | Linear position sensing |
| $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | PID control law | varies | Closed-loop control |
| $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$ | Impedance equation | N, kg, Ns/m, N/m | Compliant interaction |
| $P = \frac{V^2}{R}$ | Power dissipation in muscle | W, V, Ω | PAM electrical model |
| $E_{spring} = \frac{1}{2}k\delta^2$ | Energy stored in SEA spring | J, N/m, m | Energy safety analysis |
| $\tau = J \ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta}$ | Motor mechanical equation | Nm, kg·m², Nms/rad | Motor dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Recall for KTU:**
> - Normal human grip force: **20–300 N**
> - Upper limb therapeutic motion range: **0°–180°** shoulder flexion
> - Stroke patient gait cycle cadence: **~1 Hz**
> - Safety current limit (patient-coupled): **< 10 µA (AC)** per IEC 60601
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: DC Motor Transfer Function (for PID Tuning in Rehab Robots)

The transfer function of a separately excited DC motor forms the foundation of all rehabilitation actuator analysis. We derive it step by step, including the load (robot link + patient limb).

### Step 1 — Electrical Equation (Armature Circuit)
Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit. The applied voltage $V_a$ is opposed by the inductive drop, resistive drop, and the back-EMF.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let: } & V_a = \text{applied armature voltage (V)} \\
& R_a = \text{armature resistance (}\Omega\text{)} \\
& L_a = \text{armature inductance (H)} \\
& I_a = \text{armature current (A)} \\
& E_b = \text{back-EMF (V)} \\
\text{KVL: } & V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b
\end{aligned}
$$

The back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back into the KVL equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + K_e \omega_m \quad \text{...(1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Mechanical Equation (Newton's 2nd Law for Rotation)
The motor produces electromagnetic torque $T_m = K_t I_a$, which accelerates the rotor (inertia $J_m$, damping $b_m$) and the connected load (inertia $J_L$, referred to motor shaft as $J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{N^2}$ where $N$ is the gear ratio).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m - T_L = J_{eq} \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + b_m \omega_m
\end{aligned}
$$

Replacing $T_m = K_t I_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_t I_a - T_L = J_{eq} \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + b_m \omega_m \quad \text{...(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine in Laplace Domain
Taking the Laplace transform of (1) and (2) with zero initial conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a(s) &= (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_e \omega_m(s) \quad \text{...(3)} \\
K_t I_a(s) &= (J_{eq} s + b_m) \omega_m(s) + T_L(s) \quad \text{...(4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\frac{\omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)}$ (with $T_L = 0$)
From (4), solve for $I_a(s)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a(s) = \frac{(J_{eq} s + b_m) \omega_m(s)}{K_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute into (3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a(s) = \left[\frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J_{eq} s + b_m)}{K_t} + K_e \right] \omega_m(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J_{eq} s + b_m) + K_t K_e}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> This second-order transfer function is the **plant** that the PID controller regulates. In a rehabilitation robot, the controller typically wraps a position loop around this velocity plant, forming a third-order position-control system. The PID gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are tuned (often via Ziegler-Nichols or LQR) to give a critically damped response that follows patient motion without oscillation.

## 3.2 Derivation: SEA Force Control Law

The Series Elastic Actuator is fundamental in rehabilitation because it allows direct force control — essential for safe human-robot interaction.

### Step 1 — Geometry
A linear spring of stiffness $k$ is placed between motor position $\theta_m$ and load position $\theta_L$. Spring deflection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta = \theta_m - \theta_L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Force from Deflection
By Hooke's law, the force transmitted to the patient is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{out} = k \cdot \delta = k(\theta_m - \theta_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Desired vs Measured Force
A force sensor measures $F_{meas}$ across the spring. The controller computes the error:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_F = F_{des} - F_{meas}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Motor Position Update (Force Feedback)
A simple proportional controller on force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{m,new} = \theta_{m,old} + K_{pF} \cdot e_F
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SEA Matters:**
> If the patient suddenly spasms or the robot contacts an obstacle, the spring absorbs the impact. The motor's inertia is decoupled from the patient by the spring. This **mechanical safety** is unmatched by rigid actuators and is the **single most important component design choice** in modern rehabilitation robotics.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Actuator Selection

| Criterion | DC + HD Gearbox | Direct-Drive BLDC | Pneumatic Muscle (PAM) | Series Elastic (SEA) | VSA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Back-drivability** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Force Control Bandwidth** | High | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Power Density** | High | High | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| **Inherent Safety** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Complexity** | Low | Low | High (compressor) | Medium | Very High |
| **Cost** | Medium | High | Low | High | Very High |
| **Example Device** | InMotion ARM | MIT Manus | RUPERT | LOPES, Anklebot | DLR Hand-Arm |

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Sensor Selection

| Sensor | Measures | Range (Typical) | Bandwidth | Noise Figure | Rehab Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Optical Encoder | Angular position | 0–2π rad | > 1 kHz | Very Low | Joint position feedback |
| F/T Sensor (6-axis) | Force + Torque | ±500 N, ±20 Nm | 1 kHz | Low | Patient interaction force |
| sEMG | Muscle activation | 0–5 mV | 20–500 Hz | Moderate | Intent detection |
| IMU (9-axis) | Orientation, accel. | ±2000°/s, ±16 g | 1 kHz | Low | Gait phase detection |
| EEG | Cortical potential | ±100 µV | 0.5–100 Hz | High | BCI-driven therapy |
| FSR Array | Pressure distribution | 0–100 N/cm² | 100 Hz | Moderate | Grip pressure feedback |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Rehabilitation Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Intent - EMG or Movement] --> S1[Sensor Array - sEMG, IMU, Encoder]
    S1 --> P1[Signal Conditioning - Amplify, Filter, ADC]
    P1 --> C1[Controller Core - STM32 / DSP]
    C2[Therapist Input - GUI / Tablet] --> C1
    C1 --> A1[Actuator Driver - PWM / DAC]
    A1 --> A2[Actuator - DC Motor / PAM / SEA]
    A2 --> M1[Mechanical Transmission - Cable / Linkage]
    M1 --> H1[Human Interface - Orthosis / End-Effector]
    H1 -->|Force feedback| F1[Force/Torque Sensor]
    F1 --> C1
    PWR[Power System - Battery 24V/48V] -.powers.-> A2
    PWR -.powers.-> C1
    PWR -.powers.-> S1
    SAF[Safety Layer - E-Stop, Watchdog, Fuse] -.monitors.-> C1
    SAF -.monitors.-> A1
```

## 4.2 Component Hierarchy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Rehab Robot System] --> SENS[Sensors]
    R --> ACT[Actuators]
    R --> MECH[Mechanical Structure]
    R --> CTRL[Control and Computation]
    R --> POW[Power and Safety]
    SENS --> S1[Proprioceptive - Encoder, Resolver, IMU]
    SENS --> S2[Exteroceptive - F-T, EMG, EEG, Tactile]
    ACT --> A1[Electric - DC, BLDC, Stepper]
    ACT --> A2[Pneumatic - PAM, McKibben]
    ACT --> A3[Compliant - SEA, VSA]
    MECH --> M1[Linkages - 5-bar, Pantograph]
    MECH --> M2[Tendon - Cable-driven, Bowden]
    MECH --> M3[Frame - Exoskeleton, End-effector]
    CTRL --> C1[Algorithms - PID, Impedance, Admittance]
    CTRL --> C2[Hardware - MCU, DSP, PC]
    POW --> P1[Source - Li-ion, SMPS]
    POW --> P2[Protection - Fuse, E-Stop, Watchdog]
```

## 4.3 SEA Block-Level Flow (Component-Level Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[Desired Force Fdes] --> SUM[Error Calculator]
    FFB[Measured Force Fmeas] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[Force Controller - PI]
    CTRL --> MTR[Motor Driver]
    MTR --> M[DC Motor]
    M --> SPR[Series Spring - k N/m]
    SPR --> OUT[Output to Patient]
    POS[Position Sensor on Output] --> FFB
```

## 4.4 Control Loop for Compliant Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PD[Patient Desired Motion] -->|Intent| I[Intent Decoder - sEMG or Position]
    I --> REF[Reference Generator]
    REF --> EC[Error Computation]
    POS[Joint Position Sensor] --> EC
    EC --> CC[Compliance Controller - Impedance or Admittance]
    CC --> D[Driver]
    D --> AC[Actuator - SEA or PAM]
    AC --> OR[Orthosis or End-Effector]
    OR --> PT[Patient Limb]
    FT[Force-Torque Sensor] --> OR
    FT --> CC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain any three types of sensors used in robotic rehabilitation systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per sensor):**

1. **Force/Torque (F/T) Sensor (1 mark):** Mounted at the human-robot interface, it measures the interaction forces and torques. It uses strain gauges arranged in a Wheatstone bridge configuration. Outputs three force components ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) and three torque components ($\tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z$) for closed-loop force control and patient safety monitoring.

2. **Surface Electromyography (sEMG) Sensor (1 mark):** Detects the electrical activity of muscles through skin-surface electrodes. The signal amplitude is proportional to muscle activation, allowing the robot to anticipate and assist the patient's intended movement — used in **prosthetic control** and **stroke rehabilitation**.

3. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) (1 mark):** Combines a 3-axis accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer to estimate the orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) of body segments. Used in gait-phase detection for lower-limb exoskeletons and balance assessment in fall-risk patients.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a Series Elastic Actuator? Why is it preferred in rehabilitation robots?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** is an actuator in which an elastic element (spring) is intentionally placed in series between the motor and the load. The transmitted force is computed from the spring's deflection: $F = k \cdot \delta$ **(2 marks)**.

**Why preferred in rehabilitation (1 mark):**
- Provides **mechanical compliance**, decoupling motor inertia from the patient.
- Tolerates sudden impact (e.g., patient spasm) without injury.
- Enables **accurate, stable force control** essential for therapeutic interaction.
- Stores transient energy in the spring, improving shock tolerance.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

#### (a) [7 marks]
**With neat diagrams, classify the actuators used in robotic rehabilitation systems. Compare their characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

Rehabilitation robot actuators can be classified into three categories **(1 mark for classification, 1 mark for diagram, rest for comparison)**:

**1. Electric Actuators**
- DC servo, BLDC, stepper motors
- Often coupled with **harmonic drive** gearboxes for high torque
- High bandwidth, easy control
- *Limitation:* Stiff, poor back-drivability

**2. Pneumatic Actuators (PAMs)**
- McKibben artificial muscles
- Inherently compliant, high power-to-weight ratio
- *Limitation:* Requires compressor, nonlinear dynamics, low bandwidth

**3. Compliant Actuators**
- **SEA (Series Elastic Actuator):** Motor + spring in series
- **VSA (Variable Stiffness Actuator):** Allows on-line stiffness tuning
- Best suited for safe human-robot interaction

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Electric (HD) | Pneumatic | SEA / VSA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Back-drivability | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Safety | Low | High | Very High |
| Bandwidth | High | Low | Medium |
| Cost | Medium | Low | High |

**Real-world example (1 mark):** LOPES gait trainer uses SEA; RUPERT uses PAMs; InMotion ARM uses geared DC motors.

#### (b) [7 marks]
**Explain the working principle of a Series Elastic Actuator with a block diagram. Derive the relationship between the spring deflection and the output force.**

**Model Answer:**

A Series Elastic Actuator consists of a motor, a transmission, and a **compliant spring** placed in series between the transmission output and the load (patient interface). The motor applies a force that compresses/tensions the spring, and the load reacts against the spring. **(2 marks for description, 1 mark for diagram)**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Reference Force] → [Force Controller] → [Motor Driver] → [Motor] → [Spring] → [Output to Patient]
                                                  ↑                                       ↓
                                          [Position Sensor on Spring] ←————————————— [F/T Sensor]
```

**Derivation (2 marks):**

By Hooke's Law, the spring force is proportional to its deflection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta = \theta_m - \theta_L \\
F_{out} = k \cdot \delta = k(\theta_m - \theta_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ is the spring stiffness (N/m), $\theta_m$ is the motor-side position, and $\theta_L$ is the load-side position. The force sensor measures this $F_{out}$, and the controller adjusts motor position to minimise the error $F_{des} - F_{meas}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

#### (a) [7 marks]
**Describe the various sensors used in rehabilitation robotics. Mention their working principles and applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Optical Encoders (1.5 marks):**
- *Working:* A disc with alternating transparent/opaque slots rotates between a light source and photodetector, producing N pulses per revolution. Quadrature decoding gives 4× resolution.
- *Application:* Joint angle feedback in exoskeletons.

**2. Force/Torque Sensors (1.5 marks):**
- *Working:* Strain gauges bonded to a flexure deform under load; resistance changes are measured in a Wheatstone bridge. Six gauges yield 6-axis force/torque.
- *Application:* Closed-loop force control during reaching tasks; safety monitoring.

**3. sEMG Sensors (1.5 marks):**
- *Working:* Surface electrodes detect motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) summated across many muscle fibres. Bandpass filtered (20–500 Hz), rectified, and smoothed (RMS envelope).
- *Application:* Intent detection for prosthetic and orthotic control.

**4. IMU (1.5 marks):**
- *Working:* MEMS accelerometer (mass-spring-capacitor), gyroscope (Coriolis force on vibrating mass), and magnetometer (Hall/anisotropic magnetoresistive) are fused via Kalman/Madgwick filter to give orientation.
- *Application:* Gait phase detection, balance assessment.

**5. Tactile/Pressure Sensors (1 mark):**
- *Working:* FSRs (Force-Sensitive Resistors) change resistance under pressure; e-skin arrays use capacitive or piezoelectric elements.
- *Application:* Grip control, pressure injury prevention in bed-bound rehab.

#### (b) [7 marks]
**Explain the impedance and admittance control schemes used in rehabilitation robots. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

**Impedance Control (3.5 marks):**

Models the robot as a **virtual mass-spring-damper** coupled to the environment. The desired dynamic relationship between position error and contact force is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_d \ddot{x} + B_d \dot{x} + K_d x = F_{ext}
\end{aligned}
$$

The controller measures $F_{ext}$ and generates a velocity/position correction. Best suited for robots with **high back-drivability** (e.g., direct-drive, SEA) where the environment imposes a position. A patient pushing the robot results in a controlled deflection.

**Admittance Control (3.5 marks):**

The inverse formulation. The robot measures the **position** of interaction (via encoder) and outputs a force. Used when the robot is **highly geared and stiff** — a small patient force would otherwise cause no motion. The admittance law is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F = M_d \ddot{x} + B_d \dot{x} + K_d (x - x_{des})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Selection Rule (1 mark implicit):**
- **Low back-drivability robot (geared) → Admittance control** (force output based on position error).
- **High back-drivability robot (SEA, direct-drive) → Impedance control** (motion output based on force error).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always quote SI units (N, Nm, rad/s, m/s, A, V). Many students lose 0.5–1 mark per question by writing "force" without units.
> 2. **Mixing Impedance and Admittance:** These are **inverses** but not interchangeable. Impedance = Force → Motion; Admittance = Motion → Force. Confusing the direction of causality is a common error.
> 3. **Skipping Diagrams:** KTU awards 1–2 marks for a well-labelled block diagram. A textual-only answer is incomplete.
> 4. **Missing Real-World Examples:** Mentioning a device (LOPES, MIT Manus, RUPERT, InMotion ARM) strengthens the answer and shows depth.
> 5. **SEA vs Pneumatic Confusion:** SEA is an *electric* actuator with a spring; PAM is a *pneumatic* actuator. They are not the same.
> 6. **Controller Algorithm Equations:** When asked for the control law, always write the **full expression** (e.g., $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$), not just "PID is used."

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Components of Rehabilitation Robots**

- ✅ **Five functional pillars:** Sensors, Actuators, Mechanical Structure, Controllers, Power & Safety.
- ✅ **Sensors (Proprioceptive):** Encoders (position), IMU (orientation), resolvers (MRI-safe).
- ✅ **Sensors (Exteroceptive):** F/T (interaction), sEMG (muscle intent), EEG (cortical intent), tactile (grip).
- ✅ **Actuator hierarchy:** Electric (DC, BLDC) → Pneumatic (PAM, McKibben) → Compliant (SEA, VSA).
- ✅ **SEA formula:** $F = k \cdot \delta$ — spring in series decouples motor inertia from patient.
- ✅ **Motor equations:** $V = IR + L\frac{dI}{dt} + K_e\omega$ and $T = K_t I$.
- ✅ **PID control law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ — must write all three terms.
- ✅ **Impedance equation:** $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$.
- ✅ **Admittance equation:** $F = M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_{des})$.
- ✅ **Back-drivability** is the gold standard for safe human-robot interaction.
- ✅ **Rehab-specific standards:** ISO 13482, IEC 60601-1, ISO 13849.
- ✅ **Real-world devices to remember:**
    - **InMotion ARM** (MIT) — DC + HD gearbox
    - **LOPES** (Twente) — SEA-based gait trainer
    - **RUPERT** (Arizona State) — Pneumatic muscle
    - **MIT Manus** — Direct-drive, high back-drivability
    - **Lokomat** (Hocoma) — Cabled exoskeleton
    - **CAREX** — Cable-driven upper-limb rehab
- ✅ **Safety current limit:** < 10 µA AC for patient-coupled circuits (IEC 60601).
- ✅ **Typical rehab battery bus:** 24 V or 48 V (Li-ion/Li-Po).
- ✅ **EMG signal band:** 20–500 Hz, surface amplitude typically 0–5 mV.
- ✅ **F/T sensor mounting point:** Between the end-effector and the patient interface, not on the motor side.
- ✅ **Choice rationale:** Compliance > Stiffness; Safety > Speed; Intent > Position tracking.
- ✅ **KTU favourite exam topics:** SEA derivation, PID law, Impedance vs Admittance, sensor classification with examples.
- ✅ **Diagram must include:** Closed-loop arrows, labelled blocks, sensor feedback path.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Do not call SEA "pneumatic" — it is fundamentally an electric actuator with mechanical compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Factors Design Considerations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Human Factors Design Considerations

# Module 3: Human Factors Design Considerations in Robotic Rehabilitation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Human Factors Design (Ergonomics)** in the context of robotic rehabilitation is the interdisciplinary scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans, robotic assistive devices, and the broader rehabilitation environment. It applies **theories, principles, data, and design methods** to optimize both human well-being and overall system performance during therapeutic intervention.

Mathematically, human factors design for rehabilitation robotics can be expressed as a multi-objective optimization problem:

$$\mathcal{F}_{design} = \max_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \left[ w_1 \cdot S(x) + w_2 \cdot C(x) + w_3 \cdot A(x) + w_4 \cdot U(x) + w_5 \cdot E(x) \right]$$

Where:
- $S(x)$ = Safety score function
- $C(x)$ = Comfort and ergonomics score
- $A(x)$ = Accessibility and adaptability
- $U(x)$ = Usability and user acceptance
- $E(x)$ = Efficacy of therapeutic delivery
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5$ = Weighted importance coefficients ($\sum w_i = 1$)
- $\mathcal{X}$ = Design parameter space

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Human factors is the bridge between the *mechatronic hardware* (Module 1 & 2) and the *clinical/patient-centered outcomes* of rehabilitation. Without proper ergonomic integration, even the most sophisticated robot will fail in clinical adoption.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Custom Tailor" Analogy:**
Imagine buying an off-the-rack suit versus a bespoke tailored suit. The off-the-rack suit is built for the *average person* using generic measurements — it may roughly fit, but it pinches here, pulls there, and feels uncomfortable during prolonged wear. A tailored suit, on the other hand, is crafted from precise body measurements, posture, and movement patterns of *one specific person*.

**Robotic rehabilitation devices work the same way:**
- A **poorly designed** exoskeleton (off-the-rack) uses generic dimensions, generic force limits, and generic control — it causes pressure sores, restricts natural movement, and the patient refuses to wear it.
- A **human-factor-optimized** exoskeleton (tailored) considers the *actual* anthropometry of the patient, the *biomechanical limits* of their joints, the *cognitive load* they can tolerate, and the *specific disability* being addressed — leading to compliance, comfort, and clinical effectiveness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Human factors is NOT a "soft" topic. It is **quantitative engineering** involving measurable metrics: pressure distribution (kPa), range of motion (degrees), acceptable noise levels (dB), cognitive load (NASA-TLX score), and many more.

### 1.3 Why Human Factors Matter in Rehabilitation Robotics

| Standard Metric | KTU-Reported Typical Value | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Patient abandonment rate of assistive devices | **~30%** (WHO estimate) | Most devices unused within 1 year |
| Pressure injury threshold (prolonged skin contact) | **< 4.3 kPa** (NPUAP guideline) | Prevents bedsores/decubitus ulcers |
| Acceptable joint range redundancy | **±10°–15°** beyond anatomical ROM | Avoids hyper-extension injuries |
| Maximum safe end-effector force on limb | **< 50 N** (ISO 13482 for medical robots) | Prevents bruising, fractures |
| Maximum continuous noise exposure | **< 65 dB** (ISO 9241) | Avoids auditory/cognitive fatigue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** ISO 13482:2014 and ISO 9241-210 are the **governing international standards** for safety requirements and ergonomics of personal care robots, including rehabilitation devices. KTU 2024 expects awareness of these standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Human Factors Design

The discipline decomposes into five interconnected pillars, each requiring distinct engineering analysis:

#### Pillar 1: **Anthropometry** (Body Measurement Science)
Anthropometry provides the *geometric envelope* within which the robot must operate. It is a statistical discipline because humans are not uniformly sized.

For any limb segment, the design must accommodate the **5th–95th percentile** of the target population:

$$L_{design} = L_{50} \pm 1.645 \cdot \sigma_L$$

Where:
- $L_{design}$ = Design length to accommodate population
- $L_{50}$ = 50th percentile (median) dimension
- $\sigma_L$ = Standard deviation of the dimension
- **1.645** = Z-score for 90% population coverage (5th to 95th percentile)

For a *universal* rehabilitation robot (across age, gender, disability level), the design must accommodate:

$$L_{design,universal} = L_{50} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma_L$$

where **1.96** is the Z-score for 95% population coverage (2.5th to 97.5th percentile).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1.645 vs 1.96?** The multiplier depends on the confidence interval required. For a hospital-specific deployment (homogeneous population), 1.645 suffices. For mass-produced devices, 1.96 is mandatory.

#### Pillar 2: **Biomechanics Compatibility**
The robot's actuators must respect the *biomechanical envelope* of the human body. Critical parameters:

**Maximum voluntary joint torque** scales with body segment length and muscle cross-sectional area:

$$\tau_{max} \approx \sigma_{muscle} \cdot A_{CSA} \cdot r_{moment\_arm}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{max}$ = Maximum voluntary joint torque (N·m)
- $\sigma_{muscle}$ = Muscle stress capacity (~**30–100 N/cm²**)
- $A_{CSA}$ = Physiological cross-sectional area of muscle (cm²)
- $r_{moment\_arm}$ = Moment arm of the muscle (m)

**Robot force safety limit** (derived from tissue injury thresholds):

$$F_{robot,max} = \frac{F_{tissue,failure}}{SF \cdot n_{actuators}}$$

Where:
- $F_{tissue,failure}$ = Force causing tissue damage (N)
- $SF$ = Safety factor (typically **2–5** for medical devices)
- $n_{actuators}$ = Number of contact points

#### Pillar 3: **Workspace & Reach**
The robot's kinematic workspace must envelop the **functional reach envelope** of the human arm/leg during therapy.

**Functional reach volume** (3D, hemispherical model):

$$V_{reach} = \frac{2}{3} \pi R_{reach}^3$$

Where $R_{reach}$ is the maximum functional reach distance (~**65–80 cm** for adults).

The robot's **dexterous workspace** must be at least:

$$W_{robot} \supseteq W_{human,functional}$$

This is a **set inclusion constraint**, meaning the robot workspace must *contain* the human functional workspace.

#### Pillar 4: **Cognitive Load & User Interface**
Human information processing follows **Hick's Law**:

$$T_{decision} = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$$

Where:
- $T_{decision}$ = Decision time (seconds)
- $n$ = Number of equally probable choices
- $a, b$ = Empirically derived constants (a ≈ 0.1–0.2 s, b ≈ 0.1–0.3 s)

For rehabilitation interfaces, this means **minimizing the number of choices** presented to the patient (typically n ≤ 4) to keep decision time under ~0.5 s.

**Cognitive load measurement (NASA-TLX scale):**

$$NASA\_TLX = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{i=1}^{6} L_i$$

Where $L_i$ are the 6 subscale ratings (Mental Demand, Physical Demand, Temporal Demand, Performance, Effort, Frustration), each rated 0–100.

#### Pillar 5: **Sensory & Multi-Modal Feedback**
Effective human-robot interaction in rehabilitation requires the **redundancy of feedback channels** aligned with human sensory capabilities:

| Sensory Channel | Capacity (Weber's Law) | KTU-Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Visual | ~10⁷ bits/s (highest) | Primary feedback display |
| Auditory | ~10⁴ bits/s | Alarms, encouragement cues |
| Haptic/Tactile | ~10² bits/s | Force feedback, vibration cues |
| Vestibular | ~10² bits/s | Balance/posture feedback |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule:** If one channel fails (e.g., visually impaired patient), the robot must fall back to auditory and haptic channels. This is the principle of **redundant multi-modal feedback**.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Below is the consolidated cheat-sheet for the KTU ESE. Memorize the formulas, but more importantly understand the engineering reasoning behind each.**

| # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Units | Boundary/Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{design} = L_{50} \pm z \cdot \sigma_L$ | Anthropometric design length | m | z = 1.645 (90%), 1.96 (95%) |
| 2 | $\tau_{max} = \sigma \cdot A_{CSA} \cdot r$ | Max voluntary joint torque | N·m | $\sigma_{muscle}$ ≈ 30–100 N/cm² |
| 3 | $F_{robot,max} = F_{failure}/(SF \cdot n)$ | Safe robot force | N | SF ≥ 2 (medical) |
| 4 | $V_{reach} = \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3$ | Functional reach volume | m³ | $R_{reach}$ ≈ 0.65–0.80 m |
| 5 | $T_{decision} = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ | Hick's Law decision time | s | n ≤ 4 for rehab UI |
| 6 | $NASA\_TLX = \frac{1}{6}\sum L_i$ | Cognitive workload index | 0–100 | Acceptable < 50 |
| 7 | $S_{system} = 1 - P_{failure}$ | System safety probability | dimensionless | S > 0.999999 (medical) |
| 8 | $W_{robot} \supseteq W_{human}$ | Workspace inclusion | set theory | Mandatory constraint |
| 9 | $P_{interface} \leq P_{capillary,closure}$ | Max safe contact pressure | kPa | < 4.3 kPa (skin) |
| 10 | $Z_{comfort} = f(F,T,\theta_{joint})$ | Comfort index function | varies | Multi-parameter model |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic exercises — they drive real engineering decisions:

- **Formula 1** determines the **adjustment range** of a wheelchair-mounted robotic arm.
- **Formula 2** sets the **motor torque ratings** for an exoskeleton.
- **Formula 3** defines the **emergency stop thresholds** in software.
- **Formula 4** dictates the **link lengths and joint ranges** during mechanism synthesis.
- **Formula 5** determines **button count and menu hierarchy** in the patient interface tablet.
- **Formula 6** is used in **clinical trials** to validate a device's cognitive acceptability.
- **Formula 7** drives the **redundancy and fail-safe design** (e.g., dual encoders, mechanical stops).
- **Formula 8** is checked during **CAD workspace simulation**.
- **Formula 9** determines **cushion material selection and contact patch geometry**.
- **Formula 10** is integrated into **finite element comfort simulations** of orthoses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Questions often ask: *"Given a target population, compute the design range of a robotic link."* This is a direct application of Formula 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Anthropometric Design Length with Worked Example

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU 2-Mark Sub-Question):**
A lower-limb rehabilitation exoskeleton is to be designed for adult Indian patients. The femur length $L_{femur}$ has a measured mean $\mu = 46$ cm and standard deviation $\sigma = 3.2$ cm. Determine the design length range to accommodate 95% of the population.

**Step 1: Identify the statistical requirement.**
We need to cover 95% of the population, which corresponds to the range from the 2.5th percentile to the 97.5th percentile. The Z-score for a 95% two-tailed confidence is **z = 1.96**.

**Step 2: Apply the anthropometric design formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{design} &= L_{50} \pm z \cdot \sigma_L \\
L_{design} &= 46 \pm 1.96 \times 3.2 \quad \text{(substitute values in cm)} \\
L_{design} &= 46 \pm 6.272 \quad \text{(perform multiplication)} \\
L_{design} &\in [39.728, \; 52.272] \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply engineering rounding.**
Designs must be implemented with practical tolerance. Round the design range to a 0.5 cm grid:

$$L_{design} \in [40.0, \; 52.0] \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the adjustment range required.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{adjust} &= L_{max} - L_{min} \\
\Delta L_{adjust} &= 52.0 - 40.0 \\
\Delta L_{adjust} &= 12.0 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Engineering interpretation.**
The exoskeleton's thigh-length link must be **continuously adjustable over a 12.0 cm range**, or alternatively, provide at least **5 discrete size settings** spaced ~2.5 cm apart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "design" a parameter, always end with an *engineering statement* of what the number means physically (e.g., number of discrete settings, range of motion required, etc.).

### 3.2 Derivation: Safe Robot Force Using Tissue Failure Threshold

**Problem Setup:**
An upper-limb rehabilitation robot applies assistive force to a patient's wrist during reach therapy. Clinical data shows the soft tissue failure threshold is $F_{failure} = 250$ N. The robot uses 4 contact points and a required safety factor of $SF = 3.5$ for medical-grade applications.

**Step 1: State the safety formula.**

$$F_{robot,max} = \frac{F_{tissue,failure}}{SF \cdot n_{actuators}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute known values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{robot,max} &= \frac{250}{3.5 \times 4} \quad \text{(N substitution)} \\
F_{robot,max} &= \frac{250}{14.0} \quad \text{(denominator evaluation)} \\
F_{robot,max} &= 17.86 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply engineering safety margin.**
For real deployment, additional 20% margin is typical:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{operational,max} &= 0.8 \times F_{robot,max} \\
F_{operational,max} &= 0.8 \times 17.86 \\
F_{operational,max} &= 14.29 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Verify against ISO 13482.**
The ISO 13482 standard specifies that **maximum allowable force** in collaborative medical robots is < **50 N** for the upper limb. Our value of 14.29 N is comfortably within the limit, confirming the design is **safe and standards-compliant**.

**Step 5: Engineering action.**
The robot's force controller must be programmed with a hard limit of **14 N** at the end-effector, with a software interlock to trigger emergency stop at **17 N**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Functional Reach Workspace Volume

**Problem Setup:**
Compute the minimum dexterous workspace volume of a robotic arm for shoulder rehabilitation therapy, given the average adult functional reach $R = 0.72$ m.

**Step 1: Apply the hemispherical reach volume formula.**

$$V_{reach} = \frac{2}{3} \pi R_{reach}^3$$

**Step 2: Substitute and compute cube of R.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{reach}^3 &= (0.72)^3 \\
R_{reach}^3 &= 0.72 \times 0.72 \times 0.72 \\
R_{reach}^3 &= 0.5184 \times 0.72 \\
R_{reach}^3 &= 0.3732 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Multiply by the constant.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{reach} &= \frac{2}{3} \times \pi \times 0.3732 \\
V_{reach} &= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.3732}{3} \\
V_{reach} &= \frac{2.3456}{3} \\
V_{reach} &= 0.7819 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation.**
The robot's end-effector must be able to reach any point within a **0.78 m³ hemispherical volume** centered on the patient's shoulder. This dictates the link lengths in the kinematic chain.

### 3.4 Derivation: Interface Decision Time (Hick's Law)

**Problem Setup:**
A patient interface has either 2 buttons (Simple mode) or 8 buttons (Advanced mode). Using Hick's Law with constants a = 0.15 s, b = 0.20 s, determine the additional cognitive time imposed by Advanced mode.

**Step 1: Compute decision time for Simple mode (n = 2).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{simple} &= a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) \\
T_{simple} &= 0.15 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2(3) \\
T_{simple} &= 0.15 + 0.20 \times 1.585 \\
T_{simple} &= 0.15 + 0.317 \\
T_{simple} &= 0.467 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute decision time for Advanced mode (n = 8).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{advanced} &= 0.15 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2(9) \\
T_{advanced} &= 0.15 + 0.20 \times 3.170 \\
T_{advanced} &= 0.15 + 0.634 \\
T_{advanced} &= 0.784 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute additional time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{advanced} - T_{simple} \\
\Delta T &= 0.784 - 0.467 \\
\Delta T &= 0.317 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Engineering decision.**
The 317 ms difference is clinically significant for stroke patients with motor deficits. The KTU-aligned design recommendation is to **default to simple mode (n ≤ 4)** for all patients and unlock advanced features only after competency is demonstrated.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Design Trade-offs

The following table summarizes how competing human-factor constraints force engineering compromises:

| Design Goal | Increases ↑ | But Decreases ↓ | Engineering Compromise |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adjustable length range | User fit, adaptability | Structural mass, complexity | Telescoping links with detents |
| Higher peak force | Therapeutic efficacy | Tissue safety risk | Active force limiting + e-stop |
| More sensory feedback | User awareness | Cognitive overload | Adaptive feedback (only when needed) |
| Smaller form factor | Portability | Motor power, workspace | Use of high-torque-density motors |
| Higher DOF exoskeleton | Natural movement | Control complexity, cost | Series-elastic actuators + impedance control |
| Lighter materials | User comfort | Cost, durability | Carbon fiber reinforced polymers |
| Wireless operation | Freedom of movement | Battery life, EMI | Hybrid tethers for high-power phases |
| Larger workspace | Therapy flexibility | Safety envelope, size | Region-based speed/force limits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** "Discuss the trade-offs in designing a portable upper-limb exoskeleton" — this is a classic 7-mark question where the analytical matrix above provides a structured answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** Define "Human Factors Engineering" in the context of rehabilitation robotics. List any four human factors that influence the design of a lower-limb exoskeleton. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

Human Factors Engineering, also called Ergonomics, is the discipline of designing rehabilitation robots to fit the physical, cognitive, and sensory characteristics of the human user, rather than forcing the user to adapt to the machine.

**Four human factors influencing lower-limb exoskeleton design:**

1. **Anthropometry** — Variation in thigh, shank, and foot dimensions across patients requires adjustable link lengths.
2. **Joint Range of Motion (ROM)** — The exoskeleton must respect and follow the anatomical hip, knee, and ankle ROM, not impose mechanical limits.
3. **Muscle Strength Capacity** — Actuator torque must be matched to the patient's residual voluntary strength; over-assistance leads to learned non-use.
4. **Skin Pressure Tolerance** — Cuff and strap pressure must remain below the **4.3 kPa** capillary closure threshold to prevent pressure injuries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Full 3 marks require the definition (1 mark) + 4 listed factors with brief relevance (2 marks).

---

#### **Question 2:** What is "Hick's Law" and how is it applied in the design of a patient interface for a rehabilitation robot? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

**Hick's Law** states that the time taken to make a decision is a logarithmic function of the number of available choices:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$$

**Application in rehabilitation interface design:**

- The number of on-screen buttons (n) should be limited to **≤ 4** for cognitively impaired or motor-impaired patients.
- Therapists can unlock advanced menus only after patient competency is verified.
- Voice-command redundancy should be limited to **3–4 distinct commands** to avoid decision paralysis.
- This minimizes cognitive load and reaction time, especially critical for stroke patients.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Instructions as per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Module 3 Question]`

**(a)** With the help of suitable equations, explain how anthropometric data is used to determine the design length of a robotic link in a rehabilitation device. A shoulder rehabilitation robot is to be designed for adult patients. The upper arm length follows a normal distribution with mean **$\mu = 32$ cm** and standard deviation **$\sigma = 2.1$ cm**. Calculate the design length range to cover **90% of the population**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the biomechanical safety considerations in the design of a robotic exoskeleton. Include in your answer: (i) tissue pressure thresholds, (ii) maximum voluntary force, and (iii) the role of safety factors. **[7 Marks]**

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Anthropometric Design Length**

**Conceptual Explanation (4 marks):**
Anthropometric design uses statistical data of human body dimensions to ensure the device fits a target population. The design length is determined by:

$$L_{design} = L_{50} \pm z \cdot \sigma_L$$

For 90% population coverage (5th to 95th percentile), the Z-score is **z = 1.645**.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{design} &= 32 \pm 1.645 \times 2.1 \\
L_{design} &= 32 \pm 3.454 \\
L_{design} &\in [28.55, \; 35.45] \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

Rounded to 0.5 cm grid: $L_{design} \in [28.5, \; 35.5]$ cm. The upper-arm link must be **continuously adjustable** over a 7.0 cm range, or provide **4 discrete size settings**.

**Part (b) — Biomechanical Safety Considerations**

**(i) Tissue Pressure Thresholds (2 marks):**
Skin and subcutaneous tissue begin to suffer ischemic damage when contact pressure exceeds capillary perfusion pressure. The NPUAP (National Pressure Ulcer Advisory Panel) threshold is **P < 4.3 kPa** for prolonged contact. Design must distribute loads using contoured cuffs, gel padding, and maximize contact area to reduce pressure (P = F/A).

**(ii) Maximum Voluntary Force (2.5 marks):**
The robot's assistive force must not exceed the patient's physiological capability, otherwise it may cause soft tissue injury. The safe force per contact point follows:

$$F_{safe} = \frac{F_{tissue,failure}}{SF \cdot n}$$

Typical upper-limb tissue failure is **F ≈ 250 N** with safety factor **SF = 2–5** and multiple contact points (n ≥ 4), yielding **F < 15 N** per contact.

**(iii) Role of Safety Factors (2.5 marks):**
Safety factors account for:
- Variability in patient anatomy and pathology
- Unpredictable muscle spasms or reflexes (e.g., hyperreflexia in stroke)
- Sensor noise and actuator inaccuracies
- Cumulative fatigue over many therapy sessions

Medical-grade exoskeletons use **SF = 2–5** layered with hardware (mechanical stops) and software (force limiters) for defense-in-depth.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Module 3 Question]`

**(a)** Define **ergonomics** and explain its role in rehabilitation robotics. Describe the **NASA-TLX** method for evaluating cognitive load in patients using a rehabilitation robot. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **user-centered design process** for a rehabilitation robot. Explain why iterative prototyping and clinical validation are essential. **[7 Marks]**

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Ergonomics and NASA-TLX**

**Definition and Role (3 marks):**
Ergonomics is the scientific discipline concerned with designing systems, products, and environments to optimize human well-being and system performance. In rehabilitation robotics, ergonomics ensures devices are safe, comfortable, intuitive, and clinically effective, addressing physical fit (anthropometry), biomechanical compatibility, sensory feedback adequacy, and cognitive demands.

**NASA-TLX Method (4 marks):**
The NASA Task Load Index (TLX) is a multi-dimensional subjective workload assessment tool. It evaluates workload across **six subscales**:

1. Mental Demand
2. Physical Demand
3. Temporal Demand
4. Performance (self-rated)
5. Effort
6. Frustration

Each subscale is rated from **0 to 100**. The overall TLX score is the average:

$$NASA\_TLX = \frac{1}{6}\sum_{i=1}^{6} L_i$$

**Interpretation:**
- **TLX < 20** = Low workload (ideal for routine therapy)
- **20–50** = Moderate workload (typical for active rehabilitation)
- **> 50** = High workload (system may be too complex for patient)

A paired-comparison weighting procedure can also be applied to weight subscales before averaging.

**Part (b) — User-Centered Design Process**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram for the visual block diagram)*

The process follows iterative steps:
1. Specify the **user context** (patient demographics, clinical setting, therapist workflow).
2. Identify **user requirements** (mobility, strength, cognitive ability, accessibility).
3. Produce **design solutions** (CAD, prototypes).
4. **Evaluate** the design against requirements via usability testing.
5. **Iterate** if requirements are not met.
6. Conduct **clinical validation** with real patients (pilot trials).
7. **Deploy** and monitor in the field.
8. Collect **post-market feedback** to inform the next cycle.

**Why Iteration and Validation are Essential (3 marks):**
- **Patient variability:** Stroke, spinal cord injury, and cerebral palsy patients have vastly different impairments; what works for one cohort may fail for another.
- **Unexpected interactions:** Real-world use surfaces issues (e.g., donning/doffing difficulty, sensor slippage) invisible in lab prototypes.
- **Regulatory compliance:** ISO 13482 and medical device regulations (CDSCO in India) require documented clinical validation before market release.
- **Safety:** Untested devices risk patient injury; iterative testing allows incremental safety assurance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In Part (a) Question A:** Students often forget to *apply Z = 1.645 for 90%* and incorrectly use 1.96. This costs 1 mark. Always match the Z-score to the **exact confidence level stated** in the problem.
> 2. **In Part (a) Question A:** Do not skip the *engineering interpretation* — naming the final numerical range as "design length" without stating what it implies mechanically (e.g., adjustment range, discrete size count) will lose 1 mark.
> 3. **In Part (b) Question A:** Students often list NASA-TLX subscales without explaining the **interpretation thresholds** or the **averaging formula**. Both are required for full marks.
> 4. **In Part (b) Question B:** Drawing the block diagram without explaining **why iteration is essential** will cost 2–3 marks. The "why" is as important as the "what".

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Human Factors = Ergonomics** — the bridge between hardware and clinical outcomes.
- ✅ **Anthropometric formula:** $L_{design} = L_{50} \pm z \cdot \sigma_L$ — use **z = 1.645** for 90%, **z = 1.96** for 95%.
- ✅ **Biomechanical limit:** $F_{robot,max} = F_{failure}/(SF \cdot n)$ with **SF ≥ 2** for medical devices.
- ✅ **Pressure threshold:** Skin contact must stay **below 4.3 kPa** to prevent pressure injuries.
- ✅ **Workspace inclusion:** $W_{robot} \supseteq W_{human,functional}$ — set-theoretic constraint.
- ✅ **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ — limit interface choices to **n ≤ 4** for cognitively impaired patients.
- ✅ **NASA-TLX:** 6 subscales (Mental, Physical, Temporal, Performance, Effort, Frustration), each 0–100, averaged for total score. Acceptable < 50.
- ✅ **Sensory hierarchy:** Visual > Auditory > Haptic > Vestibular (in information capacity).
- ✅ **Standards to know by name:** ISO 13482 (medical robot safety), ISO 9241-210 (human-centered design), NPUAP (pressure ulcer guidelines).
- ✅ **Defense-in-depth safety:** Four layers — Mechanical, Hardware, Software, Operational.
- ✅ **User-centered design is iterative** — never a linear one-shot process.
- ✅ **Patient abandonment rate** for poorly designed assistive devices is ~**30%** (WHO) — human factors directly determines adoption.
- ✅ **Reasonable workspace reach:** $R_{reach} \approx$ **0.65–0.80 m** for adults.
- ✅ **Default to simple mode** in patient interfaces; advanced features are unlocked by competency.
- ✅ **Engineering compromise** is the *heart* of human factors — every benefit has a cost, and the engineer must balance them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Preparation Tip:** When you see a 7-mark question, *always* structure your answer in **three layers**: (1) the principle/concept, (2) the formula or framework, (3) the engineering decision or trade-off. This matches the KTU valuation key exactly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Wheelchair Design, Classes of Power Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Power Wheelchair Design, Classes of Power Wheelchairs ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Power Wheelchair (PWC)** is a motorized, wheeled mobility device whose propulsion and direction are controlled electronically by the user through an input device (typically a joystick, switch array, or alternative controller), rather than by manual human physical exertion. It integrates a power source, electronic control module, motor-drive system, seating platform, and frame into a single assistive technology system classified under the broader domain of **Rehabilitation Robotics and Mobility Engineering**. From a biomechatronic design perspective, a power wheelchair is a **wheeled inverted pendulum-class dynamic system** that must satisfy three coupled engineering constraints simultaneously: 1. **Static & dynamic stability** against tip-over on slopes and uneven terrain. 2. **Energy autonomy** (range, battery life) within payload (user + device) constraints. 3. **Maneuverability** (turning radius, controllability) within human-environment dimensional limits. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim intent):** A power wheelchair is a mobility assistance robot designed for users with limited upper-limb strength, coordination, or endurance. Its design integrates anthropometric data, biomechanics, drive-train engineering, electronic control, and human-factors ergonomics. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Car That Fits in a Room":** > Think of a power wheelchair as a *miniaturized, single-seater electric car* — but engineered to navigate a *kitchen, bathroom, and office corridor* rather than a highway. Like a car, it has a battery, motors, wheels, a steering "input," brakes, and suspension. Unlike a car, its steering system is often a single **joystick** that blends forward/backward (throttle) and left/right (steering) into one 2-axis signal, and its entire frame must keep the user's **center of gravity (CoG)** low and centered between the wheels to prevent tipping on ramps and curbs. ### 1.3 Key Engineering Terms and Standard Metrics | Term | Standard Definition | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | **Wheelbase** | Distance between front and rear wheel contact patches | 0.40 m – 0.60 m | | **Track width** | Lateral distance between left & right wheels | 0.55 m – 0.70 m | | **Ground clearance** | Minimum frame-to-ground vertical distance | **50 mm** (minimum) | | **Static stability angle** | Maximum tip-over angle on a slope | ≥ **10°** (ISO 7176-1) | | **Dynamic stability angle** | Maximum slope during motion | ≥ **6°** (ISO 7176-2) | | **Range** | Distance traveled on a single battery charge | 15 km – 40 km | | **Maximum speed** | Programmable top velocity | 6 km/h – 10 km/h | | **Payload capacity** | User + accessories mass | 100 kg – 180 kg standard, up to 250 kg bariatric | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Engineering Insight:** A power wheelchair is *not* a powered version of a manual wheelchair. Manual wheelchairs rely on the user's arms as the propulsion/steering actuator; power wheelchairs replace this entirely with a **closed-loop mechatronic system** (controller → motor driver → motor → wheel), making the user the *supervisor* rather than the *actuator* of the system. ### 1.4 Why Power Wheelchairs Matter in Rehabilitation Engineering A power wheelchair is often the **first and most-used robotic rehabilitation device** a person encounters post-injury (spinal cord injury, ALS, cerebral palsy, multiple sclerosis, amputation, stroke). It is the gateway to independent mobility, and therefore a foundational case study in: - Human–robot interaction (HRI) - Assistive technology ergonomics - Wheeled mobile robotics - Medical device regulatory standards (**ISO 7176** series, **RESNA WC-1**, **FDA 21 CFR §890.3860**, **EU MDR 2017/745**) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Subsystems of a Power Wheelchair Every power wheelchair, regardless of class, decomposes into **six coupled subsystems**. Understanding this decomposition is the single most high-yield concept for KTU essay questions. #### 1. Power Subsystem (Energy Source) - **Battery pack** (sealed lead-acid, AGM, gel, or increasingly Li-ion / LiFePO₄). - Typical voltage rails: **24 V DC** (most common) or **36 V / 48 V DC** (high-performance). - Capacity rated in **ampere-hours (Ah)**. - Charger: off-board or on-board, typically **C/10 to C/20 charge rate**. #### 2. Drive-Train Subsystem - **DC permanent-magnet motors** (geared or gearless) — one per drive wheel. - Optional **electromagnetic parking brake** on each motor (fail-safe, spring-applied, electrically released). - Gear reduction ratios typically 10:1 to 30:1. - Some modern PWCs use **brushless DC (BLDC) hub motors** integrated into the wheel. #### 3. Control Subsystem - **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** — microcontroller or DSP. - Receives analog/digital command from input device. - Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) drives the motors. - Often includes **proportional-integral (PI) speed feedback** from encoders. #### 4. Input / Human–Machine Interface (HMI) - Standard: **joystick** (2-axis proportional). - Alternative: sip-and-puff, head-array, chin control, tongue drive, EMG switch, eye-gaze. #### 5. Seating & Postural Support Subsystem - Sling seat vs. captain's seat vs. custom-molded seating. - **Powered seating functions**: tilt-in-space, recline, elevating leg rests, seat elevator. - Backrest and lateral supports for postural control. #### 6. Frame & Suspension Subsystem - Rigid steel or aluminum frame. - Spring-damper suspension on outdoor-class chairs. - Drive-wheel placement determines **drive configuration** (front/mid/rear). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** The following equations are the highest-frequency formulas for power-wheelchair design problems. Memorize the variables and the units — board examiners love to test unit consistency. **Battery & Range Equations** $$ E_{\text{stored}} \;=\; V_{\text{batt}} \times C_{\text{Ah}} \times 3600 \;\;[\text{Joules}] $$ $$ t_{\text{range}} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{stored}} \times \eta_{\text{sys}}}{P_{\text{motor}}} \;\;[\text{seconds}] $$ $$ d_{\text{range}} \;=\; v_{\text{avg}} \times t_{\text{range}} \;\;[\text{meters}] $$ **Motor Torque and Mechanical Power** $$ \tau_{\text{wheel}} \;=\; F_{\text{resist}} \times r_{\text{wheel}} \;\;[\text{N·m}] $$ $$ P_{\text{mech}} \;=\; \tau_{\text{wheel}} \times \omega_{\text{wheel}} \;\;[\text{W}] $$ $$ \omega_{\text{wheel}} \;=\; \frac{v}{r_{\text{wheel}}} \;\;[\text{rad/s}] $$ **Resistive Forces on a Wheelchair** $$ F_{\text{roll}} \;=\; \mu_{r} \times m_{\text{total}} \times g \cos\theta $$ $$ F_{\text{grade}} \;=\; m_{\text{total}} \times g \sin\theta $$ $$ F_{\text{total}} \;=\; F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{acc}} + F_{\text{air}} $$ **Stability (Tip-Over) Condition** $$ \tan\phi_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{w/2}{h_{\text{cog}}} $$ **Turning Radius (Differential-Drive Kinematics)** $$ R_{\text{turn}} \;=\; \frac{L}{2} \left( \frac{v_{R} + v_{L}}{v_{R} - v_{L}} \right) \;\;[\text{m}] $$ | Symbol | Quantity | Unit | |---|---|---| | $V_{\text{batt}}$ | Battery nominal voltage | V | | $C_{\text{Ah}}$ | Battery capacity | A·h | | $E_{\text{stored}}$ | Stored energy | J | | $\eta_{\text{sys}}$ | System efficiency (battery→wheel) | dimensionless (0.5 – 0.75) | | $\tau_{\text{wheel}}$ | Wheel torque required | N·m | | $r_{\text{wheel}}$ | Wheel radius | m | | $\mu_{r}$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | 0.015 – 0.04 (PU tire on hard floor) | | $m_{\text{total}}$ | User + chair mass | kg | | $\theta$ | Slope angle | rad | | $h_{\text{cog}}$ | Height of combined CoG above ground | m | | $w$ | Track width (lateral wheel spacing) | m | | $L$ | Wheelbase | m | | $v_{R}, v_{L}$ | Right/left wheel linear velocities | m/s | | $\phi_{\text{tip}}$ | Static tip-over angle | rad or deg | ### 2.3 Drive Configurations — Comparative Analysis | Parameter | **Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD)** | **Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD)** | **Front-Wheel Drive (FWD)** | |---|---|---|---| | Drive wheels | Rear | Center (under user) | Front | | Caster wheels | Front (2 or 4) | Front + Rear | Rear | | Turning center | Aft of seat (intuitive for ex-car drivers) | Almost under seat (smallest radius) | Forward of seat | | Stability on slopes | Excellent (drive wheels bear weight on uphill) | Good; sensitive to user weight shift | Moderate | | Maneuverability in tight spaces | Poor (large rear sweep) | **Best** (tightest turning radius) | Good | | High-speed handling | Best (no caster flutter) | Good | Susceptible to "fish-tailing" | | Best for | Outdoor, high-speed | Indoor, tight spaces, multi-environment | Outdoor, climbing obstacles (curbs) | | Representative | Quickie P-222 SE | Permobil M3, Quantum Q6 Edge | Invacare TDX SP, Pride Jazzy | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked to "compare" drive configurations in a 14-mark question, **always structure your answer using a table** with parameters: stability, turning radius, indoor vs outdoor suitability, and one example. The board examiner marks for the comparison matrix, not just the prose. ### 2.4 Classification Systems for Power Wheelchairs Power wheelchairs are classified along **three independent axes**: #### Axis 1 — By RESNA / ISO Mobility Class (Environmental Use) | Class | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Class A** | Compact, indoor only, short range | Apartments, hospitals | | **Class B** | Indoor + modest outdoor, moderate range | Mixed environment | | **Class C** | Outdoor-capable, long range, larger wheels | Community, rough terrain | > Note: This A/B/C system is distinct from the older RESNA wheelchair classification and is often replaced by K-codes (below) in modern clinical practice. #### Axis 2 — By Medicare HCPCS K-Code (Clinical / Functional Classification) | Code | Class | Description | |---|---|---| | **K0014** | Group 1 Standard | Basic, single-power-option capable | | **K0015** | Group 2 Standard | Multiple power options | | **K0017** | Group 2 Heavy-Duty | Bariatric (≥ 113 kg) | | **K0020** | Group 3 Standard | Rehab-grade, programmable electronics | | **K0040** | Group 3 Heavy-Duty | Heavy-duty rehab | | **K0050** | Group 4 Standard | High-strength rehab (tilt, recline, etc.) | | **K0052** | Group 4 Heavy-Duty | Heavy-duty rehab + custom seating | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Groups 1–2** are basic/standard; **Groups 3–4** are complex rehabilitation — they support power seating, alternative controls, and programmable performance curves. KTU questions on "rehab-grade vs consumer-grade" almost always use this scheme. #### Axis 3 — By Drive Configuration (covered in §2.3 above) ### 2.5 The Design Problem — Coupled Constraints A power wheelchair designer must solve a coupled optimization problem. The principal trade-offs are: $$ \boxed{\text{Maximize } \{ \text{Range}, \text{Stability}, \text{Maneuverability} \} \;\; \text{subject to } \{ \text{Payload} \le P_{\max}, \text{Volume} \le V_{\max}, \text{Cost} \le C_{\max} \}} $$ - **Range** ↑ by ↑ battery capacity → **mass and volume penalty** → ↓ maneuverability and ↑ tip-over risk. - **Stability** ↑ by ↓ $h_{\text{cog}}$ and ↑ $w$ → **vehicle footprint penalty** → ↓ indoor maneuverability. - **Maneuverability** ↑ by ↓ wheelbase and ↓ caster trail → **stability penalty** on slopes. This trade-off is the **engineering heart** of wheelchair design and the most common essay question in KTU 2024 Module 3. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems ### 3.1 Derivation: Static Stability Angle of a Power Wheelchair **Problem setup:** A power wheelchair (combined user + chair mass $m$ = 120 kg) has a track width $w$ = 0.62 m and a combined center-of-gravity height $h_{\text{cog}}$ = 0.55 m above the ground. Find the maximum slope angle on which the chair can rest in static equilibrium without tipping about the downhill wheel contact. **Step 1 — Free Body Setup.** Place the wheelchair on a slope of angle $\theta$. The CoG lies above the midpoint of the wheelbase in the lateral direction. When the slope angle increases, the gravity vector $m\vec{g}$ rotates with respect to the support polygon. **Step 2 — Identify the Tip-Over Pivot.** Tipping occurs when the line of action of $\vec{g}$ passes outside the support base. The support base is bounded laterally by the wheels; the relevant pivot is the **downhill wheel contact edge**. The lateral half-distance is $w/2$. **Step 3 — Geometric Condition.** At the verge of tipping, the gravity vector is vertical while the chair frame is inclined at $\theta$ to the horizontal. The horizontal distance from the CoG to the pivot is: $$ d_{x} = \frac{w}{2} - h_{\text{cog}} \sin\theta $$ The vertical distance from the CoG to the pivot is: $$ d_{y} = h_{\text{cog}} \cos\theta $$ **Step 4 — Tipping Condition (Moment Balance).** The moment of gravity about the downhill pivot is: $$ M_{\text{tip}} = m g \left( \frac{w}{2} \cos\theta - h_{\text{cog}} \sin\theta \right) $$ Setting $M_{\text{tip}} = 0$ for the verge of tipping: $$ \frac{w}{2} \cos\theta \;=\; h_{\text{cog}} \sin\theta $$ **Step 5 — Solve for the Critical Angle.** $$ \tan\theta_{\text{crit}} \;=\; \frac{w/2}{h_{\text{cog}}} $$ $$ \theta_{\text{crit}} \;=\; \arctan\!\left( \frac{w/2}{h_{\text{cog}}} \right) \;=\; \arctan\!\left( \frac{0.31}{0.55} \right) $$ $$ \theta_{\text{crit}} \;\approx\; \arctan(0.5636) \;\approx\; 29.4^{\circ} $$ **Step 6 — Apply Safety Factor.** ISO 7176-1 requires the **rated static stability angle** to be measured on a test plane. With a typical safety factor of 1.5 applied to the design: $$ \theta_{\text{safe}} = \frac{\theta_{\text{crit}}}{\text{SF}} \approx \frac{29.4^{\circ}}{1.5} \approx 19.6^{\circ} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** This $19.6^{\circ}$ is the maximum slope on which the chair is statically stable. The **rated (advertised) static stability** is intentionally less than the calculated $\theta_{\text{crit}}$ to account for: (i) user shift of CoG during reach, (ii) braking on slopes (dynamic overshoot), (iii) tire deformation, and (iv) manufacturing tolerances. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Required Motor Torque to Climb a Slope **Given:** Power wheelchair mass $m$ = 120 kg (user + chair). Slope $\theta$ = 8°. Wheel radius $r$ = 0.16 m. Coefficient of rolling resistance $\mu_r$ = 0.025. Two-wheel drive (one motor per drive wheel). Find the **minimum total motor torque** needed to maintain constant climbing speed (neglect acceleration and aerodynamic drag). **Step 1 — Identify Resistive Forces.** The total resistive force is the sum of rolling resistance and gravitational (grade) resistance: $$ F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_r \, m \, g \cos\theta $$ $$ F_{\text{grade}} = m \, g \sin\theta $$ **Step 2 — Numerical Substitution.** $$ F_{\text{roll}} = 0.025 \times 120 \times 9.81 \times \cos(8^{\circ}) \;\approx\; 0.025 \times 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.9903 $$ $$ F_{\text{roll}} \;\approx\; 29.15 \; \text{N} $$ $$ F_{\text{grade}} = 120 \times 9.81 \times \sin(8^{\circ}) \;\approx\; 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.1392 $$ $$ F_{\text{grade}} \;\approx\; 163.83 \; \text{N} $$ **Step 3 — Total Resistive Force.** $$ F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{grade}} \;\approx\; 29.15 + 163.83 \;\approx\; 192.98 \; \text{N} $$ **Step 4 — Required Wheel Torque (sum of both drive wheels).** $$ \tau_{\text{wheel,total}} = F_{\text{total}} \times r_{\text{wheel}} = 192.98 \times 0.16 \;\approx\; 30.88 \; \text{N·m} $$ **Step 5 — Per-Motor Torque (Two-Wheel Drive).** $$ \tau_{\text{per motor}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{wheel,total}}}{2} \;\approx\; 15.44 \; \text{N·m} $$ **Step 6 — Output Torque at Motor Shaft (with Gear Reduction).** Assume a gear reduction ratio $N$ = 20:1. Then motor-shaft torque: $$ \tau_{\text{motor}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{per motor}}}{N \times \eta_{\text{gear}}} = \frac{15.44}{20 \times 0.85} \;\approx\; 0.908 \; \text{N·m} $$ where $\eta_{\text{gear}}$ = 0.85 is the gear-train efficiency. > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** A small DC motor with a 20:1 gear reduction can readily produce 0.9 N·m. Therefore a 30 W motor is sufficient for slope climbing — this is why modern power wheelchairs use small, efficient BLDC gearmotors. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Battery Range Estimation **Given:** Battery $V_{\text{batt}} = 24$ V, capacity $C = 75$ Ah. Average power draw $P_{\text{avg}} = 180$ W. System efficiency $\eta_{\text{sys}} = 0.65$. Average speed $v_{\text{avg}} = 6$ km/h. Estimate the driving range. **Step 1 — Usable Stored Energy.** $$ E_{\text{usable}} = V_{\text{batt}} \times C \times 3600 \times \eta_{\text{sys}} $$ $$ E_{\text{usable}} = 24 \times 75 \times 3600 \times 0.65 $$ $$ E_{\text{usable}} = 4\,212\,000 \; \text{J} \;\approx\; 4.21 \; \text{MJ} $$ **Step 2 — Endurance Time at Constant Power.** $$ t = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{4.21 \times 10^{6}}{180} \;\approx\; 23\,389 \; \text{s} \;\approx\; 6.5 \; \text{h} $$ **Step 3 — Driving Range.** $$ d_{\text{range}} = v_{\text{avg}} \times t = 6 \; \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times 6.5 \; \text{h} \;\approx\; 39 \; \text{km} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Note:** Real-world range is typically 60–80% of this theoretical value because of (i) Peukert losses at high discharge currents, (ii) voltage drop at the end of discharge, and (iii) terrain variability. KTU examiners often reduce the theoretical answer by a derating factor of 0.7 in their mark scheme. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Differential-Drive Turning Radius **Given:** Wheelchair wheelbase $L = 0.50$ m. Right wheel velocity $v_R = 0.6$ m/s; left wheel velocity $v_L = 0.2$ m/s (forward). Find the turning radius. **Step 1 — Apply the Differential-Drive Kinematic Equation.** $$ R_{\text{turn}} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$ R_{\text{turn}} = \frac{0.50}{2} \cdot \frac{0.6 + 0.2}{0.6 - 0.2} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.4} = 0.25 \times 2.0 $$ $$ R_{\text{turn}} = 0.50 \; \text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Special Cases — Sanity Check.** - If $v_R = v_L$ (straight line): $R_{\text{turn}} \to \infty$. ✓ - If $v_L = -v_R$ (in-place rotation): $R_{\text{turn}} = 0$. ✓ - If $v_L = 0$ (pivot turn about left wheel): $R_{\text{turn}} = L/2 = 0.25$ m. ✓ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Pattern:** A 7-mark sub-question often asks you to *derive* this formula. The full derivation comes from the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** construction on a differential-drive robot. Be ready to draw it. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Power Wheelchair Mechatronic Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Intent

Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** Define a power wheelchair. List its six main subsystems. **Model Answer:** A power wheelchair is a motorized mobility device in which propulsion and steering are controlled electronically by the user through an input device. Its six main subsystems are: 1. Power source (battery pack) 2. Drive-train (motors, gearboxes, brakes) 3. Electronic control unit (ECU) 4. Human–machine interface (joystick / alternative input) 5. Seating and postural support 6. Frame and suspension *(Self-explanatory, 3 marks)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Distinguish between a power wheelchair and a mobility scooter. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Power Wheelchair | Mobility Scooter | |---|---|---| | Steering input | Joystick (integrated) | Tiller (handlebar) | | Seating | Often rehab-style, custom molded | Standard seat | | Programmability | Yes (controller curves) | Minimal | | Drive placement | Direct to wheels | Usually front | | Use case | Severe disability / rehab | Elderly with limited mobility | | Indoor maneuverability | High (mid-wheel) | Lower | *(2 marks for distinction; 1 mark for example)* --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **Module 3 — Internal Choice Question Paper Format** (as per KTU 2024 ESE regulation: any one full 14-mark question from the module, with sub-parts). --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the design considerations for a power wheelchair, including anthropometric, biomechanical, and stability constraints. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With neat diagrams, compare rear-wheel, mid-wheel, and front-wheel drive configurations in terms of maneuverability, stability, and indoor/outdoor suitability. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution A(a) — Design Considerations (7 marks)** 1. **Anthropometric Considerations (2 marks):** - Wheelchair dimensions must accommodate 5th–95th percentile adult body dimensions. - Seat depth ≈ 95th-percentile buttock-to-popliteal length. - Seat width ≈ 95th-percentile hip breadth + clothing clearance. - Armrest height and joystick position must allow shoulder neutral posture. - Footrest must support the 5th-percentile leg length without thigh compression. 2. **Biomechanical / Postural Considerations (2 marks):** -
