Pressure redistribution to prevent pressure ulcers (sittingsurface should not exceed interface pressure > 32 mmHg at ischial tuberosities). - Seat-to-back angle 90°–105° depending on postural control. - Tilt-in-space for users unable to shift weight independently. - Recline for catheterization, dressing, and pressure relief. 3. **Stability Constraints (2 marks):** - Static stability: $\theta_{\text{crit}} = \arctan(w/2 \cdot h_{\text{cog}}^{-1})$ must exceed rated slope with safety factor. - Dynamic stability on deceleration: must not pitch forward — CoG must stay within front wheelbase during emergency braking. - Lateral stability on side-slopes: weight transfer to lower wheels must not exceed tire friction. 4. **Power & Energy Constraints (1 mark):** - Battery sizing for daily-distance requirement. - Motor sizing for slope climbing (typically 8° minimum rated slope). --- #### **Model Solution A(b) — Drive Configurations (7 marks)** **1. Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD):** (≈ 2 marks) - Drive wheels at the rear; front casters. - The pivot for turning is *behind* the user, giving an intuitive car-like feel. - **Best high-speed stability** because drive wheels bear the user's weight on uphill slopes. - Disadvantage: large rear "sweep" radius → poor indoor maneuverability. - Example: Quickie P-222 SE. **2. Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD):** (≈ 2.5 marks) - Drive wheels directly under the user, with casters front and rear. - The pivot for turning is *under the user* → tightest possible turning radius (often equal to the chair length). - Excellent indoor maneuverability in tight spaces (bathrooms, kitchens). - Slightly reduced dynamic stability compared to RWD on uneven terrain. - Example: Permobil M3 Corpus, Quantum Q6 Edge 3. **3. Front-Wheel Drive (FWD):** (≈ 2.5 marks) - Drive wheels at the front; rear casters. - Excellent curb-climbing ability because front wheels pull the chair up. - Disadvantage: at high speed, the chair can "fish-tail" (oversteer) because rear casters are light and can lift. - Best for active outdoor users. - Example: Invacare TDX SP2, Pride Jazzy 1450. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Key:** Award 1 mark for a labeled diagram/sketch of each drive configuration. Award 1 mark for stating a representative product. Award 2 marks for comparative commentary (maneuverability, stability, suitability). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Derive the equation for the static tip-over angle of a power wheelchair on a lateral slope, given track width $w$ and center-of-gravity height $h_{\text{cog}}$. A wheelchair with $w = 0.60$ m and $h_{\text{cog}} = 0.50$ m is rated for a 6° slope. Verify whether this design meets the safety criterion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Classify power wheelchairs using the (i) RESNA/ISO mobility-class system and (ii) Medicare HCPCS K-code system. Compare both classification schemes. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution B(a) — Derivation + Numerical Check (7 marks)** **Derivation (4 marks):** The wheelchair rests on a slope inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. Let $h_{\text{cog}}$ be the height of the combined center of gravity above the ground, and $w$ be the track width. Tipping is impending when the moment of the weight about the downhill wheel contact equals zero: $$ \sum M_{\text{pivot}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; m g \left( \frac{w}{2}\cos\theta - h_{\text{cog}}\sin\theta \right) = 0 $$ $$ \tan\theta_{\text{crit}} = \frac{w/2}{h_{\text{cog}}} $$ **Numerical substitution (2 marks):** $$ \tan\theta_{\text{crit}} = \frac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60 $$ $$ \theta_{\text{crit}} = \arctan(0.60) \approx 30.96^{\circ} $$ **Safety check (1 mark):** Applying ISO 7176-1 safety factor SF = 1.5: $$ \theta_{\text{rated,safe}} = \frac{30.96^{\circ}}{1.5} \approx 20.6^{\circ} $$ Since the rated slope of 6° is well below $20.6^{\circ}$, the design **comfortably satisfies** the safety criterion with a large safety margin (factor of ~3.4). ✓ --- #### **Model Solution B(b) — Classification Systems (7 marks)** **(i) RESNA/ISO Mobility Class (3 marks):** - **Class A** — Compact, indoor use only. Short range, small wheels. Examples: hospital ward chairs, indoor transport chairs. - **Class B** — Combined indoor/outdoor moderate use. Larger battery, modest suspension. Examples: mid-range consumer PWCs. - **Class C** — Outdoor-capable, long range, robust suspension, larger wheels. Examples: all-terrain rehab PWCs. **(ii) HCPCS K-Code (Medicare) Classification (3 marks):** - **Group 1 (K0014)** — Standard PWC, basic electronics, single power option. - **Group 2 (K0015, K0017)** — Standard PWC, multiple power options. - **Group 3 (K0020, K0040)** — Rehab-grade, programmable electronics, alternative controls. - **Group 4 (K0050, K0052)** — High-strength rehab, custom seating, advanced power functions. **(iii) Comparison (1 mark):** The RESNA/ISO A/B/C system classifies by *environmental use* and *capability*; the HCPCS K-code system classifies by *clinical/functional complexity* and *reimbursement tier*. They are complementary, not interchangeable. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 3 Specific):** > 1. **Confusing "Power Wheelchair" with "Powered Wheelchair Add-On"** — A powered add-on (e.g., SmartDrive) is a clip-on motor that propels a manual wheelchair; it is **not** a power wheelchair. Many students lose marks by treating them as the same. > 2. **Forgetting units in the tip-over derivation** — Always include "m" in $w$ and $h_{\text{cog}}$ and write the final angle in degrees with a "°" symbol. A dimensionless answer gets partial marks only. > 3. **Mixing up MWD and FWD turning behavior** — A common error is to claim MWD has the largest turning radius; in fact, MWD has the *smallest*. Memorize the pivot location. > 4. **Battery equation units** — Use $C_{\text{Ah}} \times 3600$ to convert A·h to A·s (= Coulombs) before multiplying by voltage. Many students write $E = V \times C$ in Wh and forget that Watts = Joules/second. > 5. **Citing ISO standards without numbers** — Always cite **ISO 7176-1** (static stability) and **ISO 7176-2** (dynamic stability) by number, not just "the ISO standard." This signals board-level command of the subject. --- ### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024):** - ☐ **Power Wheelchair = Mechatronic system**, not just an "electric wheelchair." Six subsystems: Power, Drive, Control, Input, Seating, Frame. - ☐ **Three drive configurations** — RWD (stable, large sweep), MWD (tightest turn, under-seat pivot), FWD (curb climbing, fish-tailing). - ☐ **Static tip-over angle** is given by $\tan\theta_{\text{crit}} = (w/2)/h_{\text{cog}}$ — always derive, never quote without proof. - ☐ **Differential-drive turning radius** — $R = (L/2)(v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$. - ☐ **Range equation** — $E = V \cdot C \cdot 3600 \cdot \eta_{\text{sys}}$; divide by average power for time, then multiply by speed for distance. - ☐ **Motor torque** on a slope — sum of $F_{\text{roll}}$ and $F_{\text{grade}}$ forces, multiplied by wheel radius. - ☐ **Classification axes** — (i) RESNA/ISO A-B-C environmental class; (ii) Medicare HCPCS K0014–K0052 group; (iii) Drive configuration; (iv) Power seating functions. - ☐ **Key standards** — **ISO 7176-1** (static stability ≥ 10°), **ISO 7176-2** (dynamic ≥ 6°), **ISO 7176-15** (information documentation), **RESNA WC-1**, **FDA 21 CFR §890.3860** (powered wheelchair classification as Class I medical device). - ☐ **Alternative input devices** for users who cannot use a joystick — sip-and-puff, head array, chin control, tongue drive, EMG, eye-gaze. - ☐ **Design trade-off triangle** — Range ↔ Stability ↔ Maneuverability; improving one degrades another. This is the **exam-winning insight**. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Power Wheelchair Design, Classes of Power Wheelchairs**

topics.title: Motor Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motor Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motor Selection** in the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation** is defined as the systematic engineering methodology employed to identify the most appropriate electromechanical actuator (DC, BLDC, Servo, or Stepper) for a given assistive or therapeutic robotic system by matching the device's mechanical power, torque, speed, precision, safety, and human–robot interaction (HRI) requirements to the motor's electromechanical performance envelope.

In rehabilitation robotics, the actuator is not merely a "power source" — it is the **physical link between the therapy algorithm and the patient's limb**. A poorly chosen motor can lead to unsafe forces, sluggish response during gait assistance, oversized end-effectors, or excessive heat near the patient's skin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rehabilitation-Grade Motor Selection** = Mechanical Sizing (Torque, Speed, Power) + Control Sizing (Bandwidth, Resolution) + Human-Safety Sizing (Backdrivability, Compliant Response, Thermal Limits).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are prescribing a **pair of shoes** for a patient recovering from a stroke:

- A **powerlifter's motor** = high torque, very low speed, heavy, stiff (like steel-toed boots — wrong for walking).
- A **sprinting motor** = very high speed, low torque, lightweight (like racing flats — no ankle support).
- A **rehabilitation motor** = must behave like a **lightweight hiking shoe** — *moderate torque* (to lift the limb against gravity), *moderate speed* (to match human joint velocities), *backdrivable* (so the patient can push it voluntarily), *quiet* (no mental load), and *thermally safe* (no burning the skin).

This "balanced personality" is the essence of rehab motor selection.

## 1.3 Common Motor Families in Rehabilitation Robotics

| Family | Typical Use in Rehab | Key Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Brushed DC Motor** | Educational / bench-top exoskeletons | Simple control, low cost |
| **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** | Wearable exoskeletons (Ekso, ReWalk) | High power density, long life |
| **DC Servo Motor + Encoder** | End-effector robots (InMotion ARM) | Closed-loop precision |
| **Stepper Motor** | Low-cost orthoses, CPM machines | Open-loop position holding |
| **Pneumatic Muscle Actuator (PMA) / McKibben** | Soft exosuits (Harvard Soft Exosuit) | Intrinsic compliance, high power-to-weight |
| **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | MIT Anklebot, Agility Robotics Cassie | Force control, safe HRI |
| **Harmonic Drive Geared BLDC** | Upper-limb exoskeleton (ARTHuR) | Zero backlash, high torque |

## 1.4 Critical Performance Metrics (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four **fundamental motor selection metrics** for KTU examination purposes are:
> 1. **Continuous Stall Torque ($T_s$)** — in **N·m**
> 2. **No-Load Speed ($\omega_{nl}$)** — in **rad/s**
> 3. **Torque Constant ($K_t$)** — in **N·m/A**
> 4. **Back-EMF Constant ($K_e$ or $K_b$)** — in **V/(rad/s)**
>
> Plus the **safety-critical** rehab-specific metrics:
> 5. **Backdrivability** (qualitative — ratio of output torque to input torque when externally driven)
> 6. **Mechanical Time Constant ($\tau_m$)** — in **s**
> 7. **Thermal Time Constant ($\tau_{th}$)** — in **s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Motor Selection Methodology (KTU Board Pattern)

### Phase 1 — Load Specification (Dynamic Analysis)
The load is the patient's limb + orthosis link. Its dynamics follow the standard rigid-body rotational equation:

$$J_{total} \cdot \ddot{\theta} + B \cdot \dot{\theta} + T_{gravity}(\theta) + T_{friction} = T_{motor}$$

where:
- $J_{total} = J_{limb} + J_{orthosis}$ is the **total reflected inertia** at the joint axis (kg·m²).
- $B$ is the viscous damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad).
- $T_{gravity}(\theta)$ is the gravitational torque due to the limb segment hanging at angle $\theta$:

$$T_{gravity}(\theta) = m \cdot g \cdot r_{cm} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

- $T_{friction} = T_{c} \cdot \text{sgn}(\dot{\theta}) + B \cdot \dot{\theta}$ is the **Coulomb + viscous friction** model.

### Phase 2 — Peak vs Continuous Torque Identification
- **Peak Torque Demand ($T_{peak}$)** occurs during the maximum-acceleration interval (e.g., toe-off in gait).
- **Continuous (RMS) Torque ($T_{rms}$)** is computed over the entire duty cycle:

$$T_{rms} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} \int_{0}^{T_{cycle}} [T(t)]^2 \, dt }$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A motor is selected such that:
> - $T_{cont} \geq T_{rms} \times SF$ (with Safety Factor $SF \in [1.5, 2.0]$ for rehab)
> - $T_{peak} \leq T_{stall}$ (stall torque of motor)

### Phase 3 — Speed Determination & Gear Ratio
- Human joint angular velocities: shoulder $\approx 6$ rad/s, knee $\approx 8$ rad/s, ankle $\approx 5$ rad/s.
- The required **motor speed** is:

$$\omega_{motor} = N \cdot \omega_{joint}$$

where $N$ is the **gear reduction ratio** (output/input). For a harmonic-drive exoskeleton, $N$ is typically 50–160.

### Phase 4 — Power Computation
Mechanical power delivered to the joint:

$$P_{joint} = T_{joint} \cdot \omega_{joint}$$

Electrical power drawn from the supply:

$$P_{elec} = \frac{P_{joint}}{\eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$$

where $\eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{motor} \approx 0.5 - 0.8$ for geared BLDC systems.

### Phase 5 — Thermal & Safety Verification
The motor's thermal limit is governed by:

$$P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{phase}$$

The temperature rise $\Delta T$ must remain below the insulation class limit (typically **130 °C** for Class B, **155 °C** for Class F):

$$\Delta T = R_{th} \cdot P_{loss}$$

where $R_{th}$ is the thermal resistance (°C/W).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = J \cdot \alpha$ | Torque = Inertia × Angular acceleration | N·m |
| 2 | $T_{grav} = m g r \cos\theta$ | Gravitational torque on limb | N·m |
| 3 | $T_{rms} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\int T^2 dt}$ | RMS torque over duty cycle | N·m |
| 4 | $\omega_m = N \cdot \omega_{joint}$ | Motor speed vs joint speed | rad/s |
| 5 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W |
| 6 | $V = K_e \cdot \omega + I \cdot R$ | Motor voltage equation | V |
| 7 | $T = K_t \cdot I$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 8 | $K_e = K_t$ (in SI units) | Back-EMF = Torque constant | V·s/rad = N·m/A |
| 9 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{J \cdot R}{K_t \cdot K_e}$ | Mechanical time constant | s |
| 10 | $J_{ref} = J_{motor} + \dfrac{J_{load}}{N^2}$ | Reflected inertia to motor shaft | kg·m² |
| 11 | $\Delta T = R_{th} \cdot P_{loss}$ | Steady-state temperature rise | °C |
| 12 | $F_{safe} = SF \cdot T_{peak} \leq T_{stall}$ | Safety-limited stall torque | N·m |
| 13 | $T_{backdrive} = \dfrac{T_{friction}}{N \cdot \eta_{gear}}$ | Backdriving friction torque | N·m |
| 14 | $P_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{total}}$ | Electrical input power | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU trick**: In SI units, **$K_e$ (in V/(rad/s)) is numerically equal to $K_t$ (in N·m/A)**. Examiners frequently test whether students know this.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The motor selection procedure is the **backbone of prescription in rehab device design**:
- A **lower-limb exoskeleton** (ReWalk, Ekso GT) uses a **BLDC + harmonic drive** combination — high torque for stance, backdrivable for swing.
- A **soft exosuit** (Harvard Biodesign Lab) uses **pneumatic McKibben muscles** — the "motor" is selected by tube diameter and braid angle, but the selection *mathematics* (force vs pressure, contraction vs pressure) is identical in structure to $T$ vs $I$.
- A **CPM (Continuous Passive Motion) machine** uses a **stepper motor** — low cost, open-loop, no backdrivability needed (the patient is passive).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Joint Dynamics to Motor Torque

**Problem Setup (KTU Pattern):** A wearable elbow orthosis must assist a 2 kg forearm whose center of mass lies 15 cm from the elbow. The orthosis link adds 0.5 kg at 10 cm. The desired motion is a smooth 0 → π/2 rad in 0.5 s, with the patient providing 30% of the required torque.

**Step 1 — Compute the total inertia at the elbow joint.**

For a uniform slender rod rotating about one end, the moment of inertia is $\frac{1}{3} m L^2$. We approximate the limb + link as point masses at their respective $r_{cm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{forearm} &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot m_{arm} \cdot L^2 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 2 \cdot (0.30)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \cdot 2 \cdot 0.09 = 0.060 \;\; \text{kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{orthosis} &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot m_{orth} \cdot L_{orth}^2 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot (0.20)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.04 \approx 0.00667 \;\; \text{kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
J_{total} = J_{forearm} + J_{orthosis} = 0.060 + 0.00667 = 0.0667 \;\; \text{kg·m}^2
$$

*Logic:* Inertia is additive when both bodies share the same rotation axis (the elbow).

**Step 2 — Required angular acceleration.**

The motion is a constant-acceleration ramp from 0 to $\pi/2$ in 0.5 s, so $\omega_f = \alpha \cdot t$, and $\theta = \frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$.

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 \theta}{t^2} = \frac{2 \cdot (\pi/2)}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{\pi}{0.25} = 4\pi \approx 12.57 \;\; \text{rad/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Inertial torque demand.**

$$
T_{inertia} = J_{total} \cdot \alpha = 0.0667 \cdot 12.57 \approx 0.838 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case gravitational torque.**

Maximum $T_{grav}$ occurs when the arm is **horizontal** ($\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$). Take $r_{cm,eff}$ as a weighted average: $r_{eff} = (2 \cdot 0.15 + 0.5 \cdot 0.10) / 2.5 = 0.14$ m.

$$
T_{grav,max} = m_{total} \cdot g \cdot r_{eff} = 2.5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.14 \approx 3.43 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Total instantaneous torque at worst posture.**

$$
T_{peak,total} = T_{inertia} + T_{grav,max} = 0.838 + 3.43 \approx 4.27 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Motor contribution (patient supplies 30%).**

$$
T_{motor,peak} = 0.70 \cdot 4.27 \approx 2.99 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply safety factor (SF = 1.5).**

$$
T_{stall,req} = 1.5 \cdot 2.99 \approx 4.49 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Motor must have } T_{stall} \geq 4.5 \;\; \text{N·m}}
$$

**Step 8 — Determine required motor speed.**

Maximum joint speed $\omega_{joint,max} = \alpha \cdot t = 12.57 \cdot 0.5 = 6.28$ rad/s. For a harmonic drive with $N = 100$:

$$
\omega_{motor,max} = 100 \cdot 6.28 = 628 \;\; \text{rad/s} \approx 6000 \;\; \text{RPM}
$$

**Step 9 — Required motor mechanical power.**

$$
P_{mech} = T_{rms} \cdot \omega_{joint,avg}
$$

Assuming $T_{rms} \approx T_{peak,motor} / \sqrt{2} = 2.99 / 1.414 \approx 2.11$ N·m and $\omega_{avg} = 3.14$ rad/s:

$$
P_{mech} \approx 2.11 \cdot 3.14 \approx 6.6 \;\; \text{W}
$$

At the motor shaft (after gear, $T_{motor,rms} = T_{joint,rms} / (N \cdot \eta)$):

$$
P_{motor} = 6.6 / 0.7 \approx 9.5 \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 24 V BLDC servo with **$T_{stall} \geq 4.5$ N·m, $\omega_{nl} \geq 6000$ RPM, $P_{cont} \geq 10$ W**, geared at 100:1, satisfies the specification.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Mechanical Time Constant (Why it Matters in Rehab)

The mechanical time constant defines **how fast the motor can change speed** — a direct measure of *responsiveness* during patient-triggered movement assistance.

**Step 1 — Write the motor's electrical equation.**

$$
V = I \cdot R + K_e \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 2 — Write the motor's mechanical equation.**

$$
J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_{em} - T_{load} = K_t \cdot I - T_{load}
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate current $I$ in the no-load case ($T_{load}=0$).**

From electrical equation: $I = (V - K_e \omega)/R$. Substitute:

$$
J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = K_t \cdot \frac{V - K_e \omega}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into first-order form.**

$$
\frac{J R}{K_t K_e} \frac{d\omega}{dt} + \omega = \frac{V}{K_e}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the time constant.**

$$
\tau_m = \frac{J R}{K_t K_e}
$$

**Step 6 — KTU insight — why this is critical for rehabilitation.**

> [!NOTE]
> A *fast* rehab controller (e.g., impedance controller at 100 Hz updating force) requires $\tau_m \ll 10$ ms. Otherwise, the motor lags the patient's volitional movement, producing a sensation of "fighting the robot." Most rehab-grade BLDCs satisfy $\tau_m \in [2, 15]$ ms.

**Step 7 — Numerical example.**

Given: $J = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ kg·m², $R = 1.2 \;\Omega$, $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad.

$$
\tau_m = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 1.2}{0.05 \cdot 0.05} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.024 \;\; \text{s} = 24 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

This motor is borderline acceptable — a *lower $J$ rotor* (e.g., Maxon EC-45 flat) would reduce $\tau_m$ to ~10 ms, which is preferable for a hand-wrist orthosis.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Direct-Recall)

| Selection Parameter | BLDC | Brushed DC | Stepper | Servo (BLDC+Encoder+Harmonic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Density (W/kg)** | High (3–5) | Low–Medium (1–2) | Medium (1.5–3) | High (3–5) |
| **Efficiency (%)** | 85–95 | 60–75 | 70–85 | 80–90 |
| **Backdrivability** | Medium (gear dependent) | High (no gear) | Poor (detent torque) | Low (high gear ratio) |
| **Control Complexity** | High (needs ESC) | Low | Low (open-loop OK) | High (closed-loop) |
| **Cost (INR)** | Medium–High | Low | Low | High |
| **Typical Rehab Use** | Lower-limb exoskeletons | Educational kits | CPM machines | Upper-limb end-effectors |
| **Thermal Risk to Patient** | Medium (driver heat) | Low | Low | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Motor Selection Workflow for Rehabilitation Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Clinical Task] --> B[Quantify Patient Limb Inertia and Mass]
    B --> C[Compute Joint Torque Demand]
    C --> D[Apply Safety Factor SF 1.5 to 2]
    D --> E[Select Gear Reduction Ratio N]
    E --> F[Calculate Required Motor Speed]
    F --> G[Compute Continuous and Peak Power]
    G --> H[Choose Motor Family: BLDC vs Servo vs Stepper]
    H --> I[Verify Thermal Limits Delta T below 130C]
    I --> J[Verify Backdrivability for Patient Safety]
    J --> K[Verify Mechanical Time Constant below 20 ms]
    K --> L{Motor Acceptable?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Final Motor Selected]
    L -- No --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Motor Sizing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Limb] --> B[Reflected Inertia Jtotal]
    C[Gravity m g r] --> B
    D[Friction B omega] --> B
    B --> E[Joint Torque Tjoint]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply Gear Ratio N]
    F --> G[Motor Torque Tmotor]
    F --> H[Motor Speed omega motor]
    G --> I[Power PMech]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Electrical Power PElec]
    J --> K[Motor Selection Decision]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rehab-Specific Compliance Considerations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Motor Output Torque] --> B[Gearbox]
    B --> C[Series Elastic Element if SEA]
    C --> D[Patient Limb]
    D --> E{Voluntary Motion Detected}
    E -- Yes --> F[Controller Reduces Assistance]
    E -- No --> G[Continue Therapy Assistance]
    F --> H[Impedance Controller Updates at 100 Hz]
    G --> H
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams map the **interactions** in motor selection — for full physical drawings (e.g., exploded view of a geared BLDC assembly), students are expected to draw them freehand in the KTU answer script as per the syllabus instruction in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define motor selection for rehabilitation robots. List any four parameters used in selecting a motor for a wearable exoskeleton.**

**Model Answer:**

Motor selection in rehabilitation robotics is the engineering process of choosing the most suitable electromechanical actuator for a therapeutic or assistive robotic system such that it can safely deliver the required torque, speed, and bandwidth to the patient's limb while maintaining human–robot interaction safety.

The four key parameters are:
1. **Continuous Torque ($T_{cont}$)** — must exceed the RMS joint torque multiplied by a safety factor.
2. **No-Load Speed ($\omega_{nl}$)** — must exceed the maximum motor speed required after gear reduction.
3. **Mechanical Time Constant ($\tau_m$)** — must be small enough to respond to patient volitional movement within 10–20 ms.
4. **Backdrivability** — the motor-gear combination must allow the patient to move the limb freely when the controller is idle (essential for safe HRI).

*(Any four valid parameters accepted. Full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the significance of the mechanical time constant of a DC motor in the design of a rehabilitation orthosis.**

**Model Answer:**

The mechanical time constant $\tau_m = \dfrac{J R}{K_t K_e}$ represents the time required for the motor to reach approximately **63.2%** of its no-load speed when a step voltage is applied. In a rehabilitation orthosis:

- It governs how *quickly* the actuator can change its assistance level in response to patient motion or controller updates.
- A low $\tau_m$ (typically 5–20 ms) is required to avoid the patient feeling "resisted" by the device.
- For therapy applications such as gait or upper-limb recovery, controllers typically operate at 100 Hz, so $\tau_m$ must be less than one-tenth of the controller period.
- A motor with a high $\tau_m$ leads to sluggish response, patient discomfort, and potential instability in impedance/admittance control loops.

*(Full 3 marks.)*

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Choice 1

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the working principle of a **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** and justify why it is preferred over a brushed DC motor for a lower-limb exoskeleton. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hip exoskeleton assists a 5 kg thigh whose center of mass is 18 cm from the hip. The orthosis link adds 1 kg at 12 cm. The leg must swing from 0° to 60° in 0.4 s with constant acceleration. Assuming the patient supplies 20% of the required torque, compute:
- (i) the total inertia at the hip joint,
- (ii) the peak inertial torque,
- (iii) the peak gravitational torque,
- (iv) the required motor stall torque after applying a safety factor of 1.5. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BLDC Working Principle & Justification:**

A BLDC motor is a synchronous motor in which the rotor is a permanent magnet and the stator carries three-phase windings. Electronic commutation (using Hall sensors or back-EMF sensing) switches current between the phases to produce a rotating magnetic field, which drags the rotor along at a synchronous speed given by:

$$
\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi f}{p/2}
$$

where $f$ is the electrical frequency and $p$ is the number of poles.

**Justification for lower-limb exoskeleton:**
- **Higher efficiency (85–95%)** — less battery heat, longer operation.
- **Higher power density** — smaller and lighter for wearable design.
- **No brush wear** — long operational life, low maintenance (essential for clinical devices).
- **Lower electromagnetic interference** — safer for sensitive patient-monitoring sensors.
- **Better thermal management** — heat is dissipated from the stator housing, away from the patient's skin.

*(7 marks: 3 for principle, 2 for diagram description, 2 for justification.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total Inertia:**

Approximating thigh + orthosis as point masses at $r_{cm}$:

$$
J_{thigh} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 5 \cdot (0.36)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 5 \cdot 0.1296 = 0.216 \;\; \text{kg·m}^2
$$

$$
J_{orth} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 1 \cdot (0.24)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 1 \cdot 0.0576 = 0.0192 \;\; \text{kg·m}^2
$$

$$
J_{total} = 0.216 + 0.0192 = 0.2352 \;\; \text{kg·m}^2
$$

**(ii) Peak Inertial Torque:**

$\theta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad, $t = 0.4$ s.

$$
\alpha = \frac{2\theta}{t^2} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi/3}{0.16} = \frac{2.094}{0.16} \approx 13.09 \;\; \text{rad/s}^2
$$

$$
T_{inertia} = J_{total} \cdot \alpha = 0.2352 \cdot 13.09 \approx 3.08 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**(iii) Peak Gravitational Torque:**

Worst case is at $\theta = 0°$ (horizontal, $\cos\theta = 1$). Effective mass radius:

$$
r_{eff} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.18 + 1 \cdot 0.12}{6} = \frac{0.90 + 0.12}{6} = 0.17 \;\; \text{m}
$$

$$
T_{grav,max} = m_{total} \cdot g \cdot r_{eff} = 6 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.17 \approx 10.01 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**(iv) Required Motor Stall Torque:**

$$
T_{peak,total} = T_{inertia} + T_{grav,max} = 3.08 + 10.01 = 13.09 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

Motor contribution (patient supplies 20%):

$$
T_{motor,peak} = 0.80 \cdot 13.09 \approx 10.47 \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

Apply safety factor 1.5:

$$
\boxed{T_{stall,req} = 1.5 \cdot 10.47 \approx 15.71 \;\; \text{N·m}}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii)+(iii), 3 for (iv) with safety factor application.)*

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Choice 2

**(a)** Compare the use of **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** and **Rigid Geared BLDC actuators** in rehabilitation robotics, with reference to backdrivability, force control bandwidth, and patient safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BLDC servo motor has the following specifications: $K_t = 0.08$ N·m/A, $K_e = 0.08$ V·s/rad, $R = 1.0 \;\Omega$, rotor inertia $J = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ kg·m². The motor is geared (N = 80, $\eta = 0.75$) to drive a wrist joint with reflected load inertia $J_L = 0.15$ kg·m².
- (i) Calculate the **mechanical time constant** at the motor shaft.
- (ii) Calculate the **electrical power required** to deliver a continuous joint torque of 4 N·m at a joint speed of 2 rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SEA vs Rigid Geared BLDC Comparison:**

| Property | Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) | Rigid Geared BLDC |
|---|---|---|
| **Backdrivability** | Excellent (spring decouples motor inertia) | Poor at high gear ratios |
| **Force Control Bandwidth** | Limited to ~5–30 Hz (resonance of spring) | High (kHz) |
| **Position Stiffness** | Low–Medium (adjustable) | Very high |
| **Patient Safety on Impact** | Excellent (spring absorbs shock) | Poor (rigid coupling) |
| **Energy Storage for Assistance** | Yes (spring can release) | No (energy must flow through motor) |
| **Complexity** | High (extra spring + deflection sensor) | Moderate |
| **Example Device** | MIT Anklebot | ReWalk lower-limb exoskeleton |

**Justification:** SEAs are preferred in **patient-interactive** joints (ankle, knee during stance) where impact safety is critical. Rigid BLDCs are preferred in **high-precision trajectory** applications (CPM machine, gait end-effector) where stiffness is acceptable.

*(7 marks: 4 for comparison table, 3 for justification.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Mechanical Time Constant:**

$$
\tau_m = \frac{J R}{K_t K_e} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 1.0}{0.08 \cdot 0.08} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-5}}{6.4 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.00938 \;\; \text{s} \approx 9.4 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

This is well within the 20 ms requirement for a wrist orthosis controller.

**(ii) Electrical Power:**

Mechanical power at the joint:

$$
P_{mech} = T_{joint} \cdot \omega_{joint} = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \;\; \text{W}
$$

Electrical power at the motor input (accounting for gear + motor efficiency):

$$
P_{elec} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{motor}} = \frac{8}{0.75 \cdot 0.9} \approx 11.85 \;\; \text{W}
$$

(Assuming motor efficiency $\eta_{motor} \approx 0.9$ for a well-sized BLDC.)

$$
\boxed{P_{elec} \approx 12 \;\; \text{W}}
$$

**Sanity check (using current):** Motor torque needed: $T_{motor} = T_{joint} / (N \cdot \eta_{gear}) = 4 / (80 \cdot 0.75) = 0.0667$ N·m. Current: $I = T_{motor} / K_t = 0.0667 / 0.08 = 0.833$ A. Back-EMF: $E = K_e \cdot \omega_{motor} = 0.08 \cdot (80 \cdot 2) = 12.8$ V. Applied voltage: $V = E + IR = 12.8 + 0.833 \cdot 1.0 = 13.63$ V. Power input: $V \cdot I = 13.63 \cdot 0.833 \approx 11.35$ W — matches the earlier estimate within rounding. ✓

*(7 marks: 3 for (i), 4 for (ii) with full efficiency chain shown.)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Motor Selection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Safety Factor (SF = 1.5–2.0)** — Examiners explicitly test whether students multiply $T_{peak}$ by SF before selecting the motor. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up RMS and Peak Torque** — Motor nameplate uses $T_{cont}$, but torque demand is given as $T_{peak}$. Students often size to $T_{peak}$ alone, violating thermal limits.
> 3. **Incorrect unit conversion** — RPM must be converted to **rad/s** ($\omega = \text{RPM} \cdot \pi/30$) before plugging into $P = T \cdot \omega$. The board commonly tests this.
> 4. **Ignoring gear efficiency** — A harmonic drive with $N=100$ has $\eta \approx 0.6$–0.8. Forgetting this in back-calculations gives a falsely low power requirement.
> 5. **Not stating $K_e = K_t$ (SI units)** — In SI, these are numerically equal, and the KTU answer key expects students to *explicitly state* this equivalence when deriving both.
> 6. **Using point-mass instead of rod inertia for the limb** — The human limb is more accurately modeled as a rod ($\tfrac{1}{3}mL^2$). Examiners may deduct if you use $mr^2$ for a full limb.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Motor selection = matching torque, speed, power, bandwidth, and backdrivability to the rehab device's clinical requirements.
- **Top 4 motor families** for rehab: Brushed DC (education), BLDC (exoskeletons), Stepper (CPM), Servo (precision end-effectors).
- **Equation of motion at the joint:** $J_{total} \ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + T_{grav}(\theta) = T_{motor}$.
- **RMS torque** is the correct metric for continuous operation; **Peak torque** governs motor selection when multiplied by SF.
- **In SI units, $K_e$ (V·s/rad) = $K_t$ (N·m/A)** — a frequently tested KTU equivalence.
- **Mechanical time constant** $\tau_m = JR/(K_t K_e)$ dictates responsiveness; aim for <20 ms in rehab.
- **Safety Factor (1.5–2.0)** is mandatory when computing $T_{stall,req}$.
- **BLDC** is the workhorse for modern lower-limb exoskeletons (ReWalk, Ekso) — high efficiency, long life, high power density.
- **SEAs** are preferred for safe human–robot interaction in ankle/knee applications.
- **Backdrivability** is non-negotiable in active orthoses — the patient must always be able to override the controller.
- **Thermal limit** $\Delta T = R_{th} P_{loss} \leq$ insulation class temperature (130–155 °C).
- **Power conversion chain:** $P_{elec} = P_{joint} / (\eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{motor})$.
- **Reflected inertia** to motor shaft: $J_{ref} = J_{motor} + J_{load}/N^2$.
- **Exam mantra:** *Always convert RPM to rad/s, always apply SF, always state assumptions about the limb model.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servo Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Servo Amplifiers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Servo Amplifier** (also referred to as a **Servo Drive** or **Servo Controller**) is a specialized electronic power amplifier designed to deliver precisely controlled current, voltage, and frequency to a **servo motor** in a closed-loop feedback configuration. In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, the servo amplifier acts as the high-power actuation interface between the low-level command signals issued by the rehabilitation controller (e.g., trajectory planner, EMG classifier, or therapist console) and the physical servo motor that drives the orthosis, end-effector, or exoskeleton joint. The fundamental purpose of a servo amplifier is threefold: 1. **Signal Amplification**: Convert low-power analog/digital control signals (typically 0–10 V, ±10 V, or PWM/fieldbus commands) into high-power drive signals (tens to hundreds of volts and amperes) capable of producing useful mechanical torque. 2. **Closed-Loop Regulation**: Implement high-bandwidth inner control loops — typically the **current (torque) loop**, **velocity loop**, and **position loop** — using feedback from encoders, resolvers, or Hall-effect sensors to enforce the desired motion profile. 3. **Safety and Protection**: Enforce current limiting, thermal shutdown, over-voltage protection, and fault isolation — features that are *non-negotiable* in human-coupled rehabilitation systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEBRT722 Module 3):** Servo amplifiers are categorized under the *actuation subsystem* of rehabilitation robots. Their bandwidth, resolution, and current-loop dynamics directly determine the **impedance rendering fidelity** and **back-drivability** of an exoskeleton — two of the most heavily examined concepts in modern rehabilitation robotics. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscle and Motor Neuron" of the Robot Imagine the human **motor neuron–muscle system**: - The **brain** (cerebral cortex) generates a small electrical intention signal of a few millivolts. - The **motor neuron** acts as a biological amplifier: it boosts this small signal and routes it to the correct muscle. - The **muscle** then produces a large, precisely graded mechanical force. - **Muscle spindles and Golgi tendon organs** provide continuous feedback about force and length back to the spinal cord for fine regulation. A **servo amplifier is the robotic equivalent of this entire motor neuron–muscle assembly**: - The **rehabilitation controller** = the brain (issues small command signals). - The **servo amplifier** = the motor neuron (amplifies and shapes the command). - The **servo motor** = the muscle (produces the actual force/torque). - The **encoder/tachometer** = the spindle/Golgi organ (provides feedback). > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Insight:** Just as a weak or sluggish muscle limits athletic performance, a slow or low-bandwidth servo amplifier limits the **transparency** and **responsiveness** of a rehabilitation robot. If the amplifier cannot track the commanded current at > 1 kHz bandwidth, the patient will feel the robot as "sluggish" or "sticky" — defeating the goal of therapy. ## 1.3 Standard Specifications & Performance Metrics The following performance metrics are standard across the rehabilitation robotics industry and form the basis of KTU numerical questions: | Metric | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Current Loop Bandwidth** | **1–5 kHz** | Determines torque fidelity and transparency | | **PWM Switching Frequency** | **8–32 kHz** | Above audible range for quiet operation | | **Resolution** | **12–20 bit** | Affects smoothness of low-speed motion | | **Bus Voltage** | **24 V – 700 V DC** | Matched to motor rating | | **Continuous Current** | **3 A – 50 A** | Determines sustained torque | | **Communication Protocols** | EtherCAT, CAN, RS-485, Analog | Real-time control feasibility | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Servo Amplifier A modern servo amplifier consists of four cascaded functional blocks, each critical to its operation: ### Block A — Power Stage (Output Stage) The power stage is the high-current/voltage section that physically drives the motor windings. It is implemented using **power semiconductor switches** (IGBTs, MOSFETs, or GaN transistors) arranged in specific topologies. The dominant topology is the **H-Bridge** (full-bridge) for DC brushless and brushed servo motors, and the **three-phase inverter** (6-switch bridge) for three-phase AC brushless servo motors. The H-Bridge uses **4 switches** (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an "H" around the motor: - S1 & S4 ON → current flows left-to-right → forward rotation. - S2 & S3 ON → current flows right-to-left → reverse rotation. - PWM chopping on one half controls **speed/direction of current**, hence **torque**. ### Block B — Modulator (PWM Generator) The modulator converts the analog control command into a **Pulse Width Modulated** switching pattern. The two principal techniques are: 1. **Sine-Triangle PWM (Sinusoidal PWM, SPWM):** The reference sine wave is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier to produce switching pulses. Used primarily for AC/BLDC drives. 2. **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM):** A more sophisticated technique that uses the α-β reference frame to compute switching vectors, yielding **15% higher DC bus utilization** than SPWM. Standard in high-performance rehabilitation drives. ### Block C — Control Loops (Outer → Inner) Servo amplifiers typically implement **three nested control loops** (cascaded control): - **Innermost — Current (Torque) Loop:** Highest bandwidth (~1–5 kHz). Uses shunt-resistor or Hall-effect current sensors. Directly controls motor torque, since $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$. - **Middle — Velocity Loop:** Bandwidth ~100–500 Hz. Uses encoder-derived $\omega$ as feedback. Outputs current command. - **Outermost — Position Loop:** Bandwidth ~10–100 Hz. Uses cumulative encoder counts as feedback. Outputs velocity command. > [!NOTE] > **Cascaded Loop Principle:** The inner loop must be at least **3–5× faster** than the outer loop to ensure stability and decoupling. This is why current loops are always the fastest. ### Block D — Feedback Signal Conditioning Raw sensor signals (encoder quadrature, Hall transitions, current shunt voltage) are filtered, digitized, and decoded before being fed to the control loops. Optical or magnetic isolation is used to protect low-voltage logic from high-voltage switching noise. ## 2.2 Classification of Servo Amplifiers Servo amplifiers are classified along several axes relevant to KTU Module 3: **A. By Output Stage Topology** - **Linear (Class A/B/AB) Amplifiers:** Continuous conduction; low switching noise; inefficient (heat dissipation). Used only in low-power precision applications (e.g., haptic finger devices). - **Switching (Class D) Amplifiers:** Use PWM at high frequency; highly efficient (>95%); standard for all rehabilitation exoskeletons. **B. By Motor Type** - Brushed DC servo amplifiers - Brushless DC (BLDC) servo amplifiers - AC synchronous (PMSM) servo amplifiers - Voice-coil / direct-drive amplifiers **C. By Control Variable** - **Torque-controlled (current-mode) amplifiers** — preferred in rehabilitation for *impedance control* and *back-drivability*. - **Velocity-controlled amplifiers** — common in treadmill-based gait trainers. - **Position-controlled amplifiers** — used in end-effector robots like InMotion ARM. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table consolidates all equations the student must memorize for Module 3 examination. | Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | |---|---|---| | **Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_{sw}}$ | $t_{on}$ = ON time, $T_{sw}$ = switching period | | **Average Output Voltage** | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{dc}$ | $V_{dc}$ = DC bus voltage | | **PWM Frequency** | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{sw}}$ | $f_{sw}$ typically **8–32 kHz** | | **Armature Voltage Equation** | $V_a = R_a \cdot I_a + L_a \cdot \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ = back-EMF constant, $\omega$ = angular velocity | | **Motor Torque Equation** | $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | $K_t$ = torque constant **(Nm/A)** | | **Back-EMF** | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ **(V·s/rad)** | | **KTU Critical Relation** | $K_t = K_e$ (in SI units) | Dimensional equivalence — **always state this** | | **Mechanical Load Equation** | $J \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = T_m - T_L - B \cdot \omega$ | $J$ = inertia, $B$ = damping, $T_L$ = load torque | | **Current Loop Transfer Function** | $\dfrac{I_a(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{1}{L_a s + R_a}$ | First-order plant | | **PI Controller Output** | $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \displaystyle\int e(t)\,dt$ | $e$ = current error | | **Closed-Loop Current BW** | $f_{BW} \approx \dfrac{K_p \cdot K_i}{2\pi \cdot K_p} = \dfrac{K_i}{2\pi}$ | For first-order system with PI control | | **Power Dissipated (Linear Amp)** | $P_{diss} = (V_{cc} - V_{out}) \cdot I_a$ | Justifies switching topology choice | | **Switching Amp Efficiency** | $\eta > 95\%$ | Independent of load (ideal) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In rehabilitation robots such as the **Lokomat** (gait trainer) or **Armeo Power** (arm exoskeleton), the servo amplifier's **current-loop bandwidth directly governs the closed-loop mechanical impedance rendered to the patient.** A bandwidth below ~500 Hz makes the robot feel "stiff" or "robotic," destroying the desired *transparent interaction* essential for neuro-recovery. This is the single most important reason **torque-controlled Class-D switching amplifiers** dominate modern rehabilitation hardware. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Output Voltage of a PWM-Driven Servo Amplifier This derivation is a **direct KTU board-exam favorite** (frequently worth 3–4 marks). **Problem Setup:** A Class-D servo amplifier switches the DC bus voltage $V_{dc}$ across a motor winding using PWM with switching period $T_{sw}$. The switch is ON for duration $t_{on}$ and OFF for duration $T_{sw} - t_{on}$. Derive the average voltage applied to the motor. **Step 1 — Define the switching waveform.** The instantaneous voltage across the motor $V_m(t)$ is a rectangular pulse train: $$V_m(t) = \begin{cases} V_{dc}, & 0 \le t < t_{on} \\ 0, & t_{on} \le t < T_{sw} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the definition of the average (mean) value over one period.** The average voltage is the integral of the instantaneous voltage over one full period, divided by the period: $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{sw}} \int_{0}^{T_{sw}} V_m(t)\, dt$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the piecewise waveform and split the integral.** $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{sw}} \left[ \int_{0}^{t_{on}} V_{dc}\, dt + \int_{t_{on}}^{T_{sw}} 0\, dt \right]$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate each integral.** The first integral gives $V_{dc} \cdot t_{on}$; the second integral is zero. Therefore: $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{sw}} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot t_{on}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the duty cycle definition $D = t_{on}/T_{sw}$.** $$V_{avg} = V_{dc} \cdot D = V_{dc} \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{T_{sw}}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{dc}}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** By varying $D$ from 0 to 1, the amplifier can produce any average voltage between 0 V and $V_{dc}$. For a bipolar H-bridge (with $\pm V_{dc}$), $D$ can produce $-V_{dc}$ to $+V_{dc}$, enabling full four-quadrant operation (forward/reward motoring and regenerative braking). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Armature Current of a DC Servo Motor This derivation links amplifier output voltage to motor torque — the central relationship in torque-controlled rehabilitation drives. **Given:** Motor is running at constant angular velocity $\omega$ in steady state, with constant load torque $T_L$. **Step 1 — Write the armature voltage equation.** $$V_a = R_a \cdot I_a + L_a \cdot \frac{dI_a}{dt} + K_e \cdot \omega$$ **Step 2 — Apply the steady-state condition $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$.** The inductive term vanishes because current is no longer changing: $$V_a = R_a \cdot I_a + K_e \cdot \omega$$ **Step 3 — Solve algebraically for the armature current $I_a$.** $$I_a = \frac{V_a - K_e \cdot \omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the torque-current relationship $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$.** $$T_m = K_t \cdot \frac{V_a - K_e \cdot \omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 5 — Apply mechanical equilibrium (constant velocity, no acceleration).** In steady state, $J \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$, hence: $$T_m = T_L + B \cdot \omega$$ **Step 6 — Equate and solve for $\omega$ (no-load speed derivation).** Setting $T_L = 0$ and $B = 0$ (ideal no-load): $$K_t \cdot \frac{V_a - K_e \cdot \omega_0}{R_a} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K_e}$$ **Step 7 — Solve for stall current (starting current with $\omega = 0$).** $$I_{stall} = \frac{V_a}{R_a}$$ **Consolidated Result:** $$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_e} - \frac{R_a \cdot T_L}{K_t \cdot K_e}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Insight:** This linear $\omega$–$T_L$ relationship is the *mechanical load line* of the motor. The servo amplifier's job is to manipulate $V_a$ in real time so that the operating point traces any desired trajectory on this load line — which is the essence of **velocity/torque control** in rehabilitation robotics. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Current-Loop Bandwidth of a PI-Controlled Servo Amplifier This derivation shows why **high-gain current loops** are critical for safe human–robot interaction. **Plant Model:** From the armature equation, the transfer function from applied voltage to armature current (ignoring back-EMF disturbance, which the inner loop rejects) is: $$G_p(s) = \frac{I_a(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{1}{L_a s + R_a}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute $s = 0$ to find the DC gain.** $$G_p(0) = \frac{1}{R_a}$$ **Step 2 — Find the natural time constant.** $$\tau_a = \frac{L_a}{R_a}$$ So $G_p(s)$ is first-order with time constant $\tau_a$ — typically 1–10 ms for a small servo motor. **Step 3 — Apply a Proportional-Integral (PI) controller.** $$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p \cdot s + K_i}{s}$$ **Step 4 — Write the open-loop transfer function.** $$L(s) = C(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot s + K_i}{s(L_a s + R_a)}$$ **Step 5 — Apply the closed-loop formula $T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)}$.** After algebraic expansion (assume $L_a \cdot K_p \gg R_a \cdot \tau$ so that the closed-loop system remains first-order dominated): $$T(s) \approx \frac{K_i / L_a}{s + K_i / L_a}$$ **Step 6 — Identify the closed-loop bandwidth.** For a first-order system of form $T(s) = \frac{\omega_c}{s + \omega_c}$, the −3 dB bandwidth $\omega_{BW} = \omega_c$. Therefore: $$\omega_{BW} = \frac{K_i}{L_a}$$ Converting to Hz: $$\boxed{f_{BW} = \frac{K_i}{2\pi \cdot L_a}}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For a servo motor with $L_a = 5$ mH, if we choose $K_i = 100$ V/A·s, then: $$f_{BW} = \frac{100}{2\pi \cdot 0.005} = \frac{100}{0.0314} \approx 3183 \text{ Hz}$$ This is well within the **1–5 kHz** target range for rehabilitation-grade amplifiers. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Servo Amplifier for Which Rehabilitation Application? | Application | Joint Type | Required BW | Amplifier Choice | Reason | |---|---|---|---|---| | Finger exoskeleton (haptic) | Small, fast | > 3 kHz | **Linear Class-AB** | Low noise, smooth current at low power | | Wrist/ankle orthosis | Medium | 1–2 kHz | **PWM Class-D, current mode** | Efficiency + torque fidelity | | Elbow/knee exoskeleton (Armeo, Lokomat) | Large, strong | 500 Hz – 1 kHz | **3-phase SVPWM, torque mode** | High torque, regenerative braking | | Gait trainer treadmill | Velocity, not position | 50–200 Hz | **Velocity-mode DC amplifier** | Position not critical, only speed | | Prosthetic limb socket | Fine torque | > 2 kHz | **Torque-controlled BLDC drive** | Direct-drive transparency | | Pediatric rehab robot | Safe, low-force | 500 Hz | **Low-voltage current-limited amp** | Patient safety paramount | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Servo Amplifier in a Rehabilitation Robot The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete signal and power flow** from the rehabilitation controller through the servo amplifier to the motor, and the feedback paths closing the loops. Node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed, and labels are clean uppercase text per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safety rules. ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[REHAB CONTROLLER
PC OR DSP OR MCU] --> A2[TRAJECTORY PLANNER
DESIRED POSITION] A2 --> A3[POSITION LOOP PI
OUTER LOOP] A3 --> A4[VELOCITY LOOP PI
MIDDLE LOOP] A4 --> A5[CURRENT LOOP PI
INNERMOST LOOP] A5 --> A6[PWM MODULATOR
SINE TRIANGLE OR SVPWM] A6 --> A7[POWER STAGE
H BRIDGE OR 3PH INVERTER] A7 --> A8[DC BUS
24 TO 700 V] A7 --> A9[SERVO MOTOR
JOINT ACTUATOR] A9 --> A10[MECHANICAL LOAD
EXOSKELETON OR ORTHOSIS] A9 --> A11[ENCODER
POSITION FEEDBACK] A9 --> A12[TACHOMETER OR DERIVED
VELOCITY FEEDBACK] A9 --> A13[CURRENT SENSOR
SHUNT OR HALL] A11 --> A3 A12 --> A4 A13 --> A5 A7 --> A14[FAULT MONITOR
OCP OVP OTP] A14 --> A15[SAFETY SHUTDOWN
PATIENT PROTECTION] ``` **Reading the diagram (left → right and bottom feedback):** 1. The **Rehab Controller** issues a high-level command (e.g., "move elbow to 45° at 30°/s with 5 Nm assist torque"). 2. The **Trajectory Planner** smooths this into a time-stamped reference signal. 3. The **outer Position Loop** compares the encoder reading with the reference and outputs a velocity command. 4. The **middle Velocity Loop** compares the tachometer/derived speed with the velocity command and outputs a current (torque) command. 5. The **inner Current Loop** compares the actual motor current with the command and outputs a voltage reference for the modulator. 6. The **PWM Modulator** generates switching pulses; the **Power Stage** amplifies these to bus-level voltages driving the motor. 7. Three sensor feedback paths close the three loops. 8. A **Fault Monitor** continuously watches the power stage for over-current, over-voltage, and over-temperature, executing a hardware safety shutdown if any threshold is breached. ## 4.2 Signal-Cascade Block Diagram Showing the Three Cascaded Loops ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[REF POSITION
THETA REF] --> B2[SUMMING JUNCTION 1
POS ERROR] B3[ENCODER
THETA MEAS] --> B2 B2 --> B4[PI POS
KP POS KI POS] B4 --> B5[REF VELOCITY
OMEGA REF] B5 --> B6[SUMMING JUNCTION 2
VEL ERROR] B7[TACHOMETER
OMEGA MEAS] --> B6 B6 --> B8[PI VEL
KP VEL KI VEL] B8 --> B9[REF CURRENT
I REF] B9 --> B10[SUMMING JUNCTION 3
CURR ERROR] B11[SHUNT SENSOR
I MEAS] --> B10 B10 --> B12[PI CURR
KP I KI I] B12 --> B13[PWM AND
POWER STAGE] B13 --> B14[MOTOR AND
JOINT LOAD] B14 --> B3 B14 --> B7 B14 --> B11 ``` This diagram is the **single most important visual aid for the 14-mark Part B question**. Examiners expect students to be able to draw (or describe) this cascaded structure and identify which loop has the highest bandwidth. ## 4.3 Power Stage Topology — H-Bridge Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB C1[V DC POSITIVE
BUS PLUS] --> C2[SWITCH S1
HIGH SIDE LEFT] C2 --> C3[MOTOR TERMINAL A
PHASE A] C3 --> C4[SWITCH S2
LOW SIDE LEFT] C4 --> C5[V DC NEGATIVE
BUS GROUND] C1 --> C6[SWITCH S3
HIGH SIDE RIGHT] C6 --> C7[MOTOR TERMINAL B
PHASE B] C7 --> C8[SWITCH S4
LOW SIDE RIGHT] C8 --> C5 C3 <--> C7 ``` **Operating Modes:** - **S1 + S4 ON, S2 + S3 OFF:** Current flows A→B; motor turns forward. - **S3 + S2 ON, S1 + S4 OFF:** Current flows B→A; motor turns reverse. - **S1 + S2 ON (or S3 + S4 ON):** Both ends of motor at $V_{dc}$ (or ground) — **braking/short-circuit mode** for regenerative dissipation. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar Pattern] **Define a servo amplifier. List any two advantages of PWM-based switching amplifiers over linear amplifiers in rehabilitation robotic applications.** **Model Answer:** A **servo amplifier** is an electronic power amplifier that delivers precisely regulated current, voltage, or frequency to a servo motor, in closed-loop coordination with feedback signals from position, velocity, and current sensors. It is the actuation interface between the low-level rehabilitation controller and the high-power motor. **Two advantages of PWM switching amplifiers over linear amplifiers:** 1. **High efficiency (> 95%):** Switching amplifiers dissipate negligible power in the ON or OFF states, whereas linear amplifiers continuously dissipate $P_{diss} = (V_{cc} - V_{out}) \cdot I_a$ as heat. This is critical for wearable exoskeletons where battery life and thermal management against the patient's skin are constrained. 2. **Compact and lightweight:** Lower heat dissipation permits smaller heat sinks, allowing the amplifier to be integrated *into the orthosis itself* (a feature impossible with linear Class-AB amplifiers above ~50 W). *(A third advantage often accepted: ability to regenerate braking energy back to the bus capacitor, important for gait trainers during swing phase.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern] **With a neat block diagram, explain the three cascaded control loops implemented inside a typical torque-controlled servo amplifier.** **Model Answer:** A torque-controlled servo amplifier implements three **cascaded (nested) closed loops**, with the innermost loop having the highest bandwidth: 1. **Current (Torque) Loop — innermost, ~1–5 kHz:** Compares measured armature current (from shunt or Hall sensor) with the commanded current. Output is the voltage reference sent to the PWM modulator. 2. **Velocity Loop — middle, ~100–500 Hz:** Compares measured angular velocity (from encoder differentiation or tachometer) with the velocity command. Output is the current command sent to the inner loop. 3. **Position Loop — outermost, ~10–100 Hz:** Compares measured joint angle (from encoder counts) with the trajectory reference. Output is the velocity command sent to the middle loop. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR D1[THETA REF] --> D2[PI POS] --> D3[OMEGA REF] --> D4[PI VEL] --> D5[I REF] --> D6[PI CURR] --> D7[PWM STAGE] --> D8[MOTOR] D8 --> D9[ENCODER] --> D2 D8 --> D10[TACHO] --> D4 D8 --> D11[I SENSE] --> D6 ``` **Key Rule:** The bandwidth of each inner loop must be at least **3–5× higher** than the outer loop to guarantee loop decoupling and stability. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern] (14 Marks) **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete functional architecture of a PWM-based servo amplifier used in a rehabilitation exoskeleton. Identify and justify the role of each block. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive an expression for the **average output voltage** of a PWM switching amplifier in terms of duty cycle and DC bus voltage. A Class-D servo amplifier operates from a 48 V DC bus with a switching frequency of 20 kHz. If the desired average motor voltage is 18 V, calculate the required ON-time of the switch. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution to (a): The PWM-based servo amplifier for a rehabilitation exoskeleton consists of the following cascaded blocks (described from input to output, with feedback shown): 1. **Command Input Interface** — accepts low-level signals from the controller (analog ±10 V, PWM, or fieldbus like EtherCAT/CAN). Provides electrical isolation. 2. **Reference Shaping Block** — applies a rate-limiter and feed-forward term to ensure the trajectory commanded to the motor does not violate joint limits or patient-safety constraints. 3. **Cascaded Control Loops** (Position → Velocity → Current PI controllers), as described in Section 5.1 Q2. 4. **PWM Modulator** — converts the analog voltage command from the current loop into a duty-cycle-modulated switching signal using sine-triangle comparison or space-vector PWM. 5. **Gate Driver** — boosts the logic-level PWM signal to the high-voltage/high-current levels needed to drive the gates of IGBTs or MOSFETs; provides isolation between control and power grounds. 6. **H-Bridge / 3-Phase Inverter Power Stage** — the high-current switches that physically connect the DC bus to the motor phases in the commanded polarity and duration. 7. **DC Bus Capacitor Bank** — provides instantaneous current during PWM transitions; absorbs regenerative energy from the motor during deceleration. 8. **Current Sensing Stage** — shunt resistors (in the low side) or Hall-effect LEM modules sense instantaneous phase current; signals are conditioned and fed to the current loop. 9. **Encoder/Resolver Decoder** — converts quadrature or sine/cosine feedback into a digital position word. 10. **Protection & Fault Logic** — hardware comparators that trip the gate driver and disconnect the motor within microseconds of an over-current, over-voltage, or over-temperature event. **Justification of Block Roles:** - The **control loops** enforce the *tracking performance*. - The **PWM modulator + power stage** provides the *energy conversion*. - The **sensors + protection logic** provide the *patient safety* that is mandatory in human-coupled rehabilitation systems. #### Model Solution to (b): **Derivation** (from Section 3.1): $$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{dc} = \frac{t_{on}}{T_{sw}} \cdot V_{dc}$$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given: - $V_{dc} = 48$ V - $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz, hence $T_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{20{,}000} = 50\ \mu s$ - $V_{avg} = 18$ V Compute the required duty cycle: $$D = \frac{V_{avg}}{V_{dc}} = \frac{18}{48} = 0.375 \quad (37.5\%)$$ Compute the required ON-time: $$t_{on} = D \cdot T_{sw} = 0.375 \times 50\ \mu s = 18.75\ \mu s$$ **Result:** $\boxed{t_{on} = 18.75\ \mu s}$ --- ### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern] (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the difference between **linear** and **switching (PWM)** servo amplifiers. Why are switching amplifiers universally preferred in robotic rehabilitation applications? List any two disadvantages of switching amplifiers. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A DC servo motor used in a knee exoskeleton has the following parameters: $R_a = 1.2\ \Omega$, $L_a = 3$ mH, $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $K_t = 0.05$ Nm/A. It is driven by a current-controlled servo amplifier from a 36 V DC bus. The amplifier implements a PI current controller with $K_p = 4$ V/A and $K_i = 60$ V/A·s. Calculate: (i) the no-load speed $\omega_0$ in rad/s, (ii) the steady-state armature current when the motor delivers 2 Nm of torque at 50 rad/s, and (iii) the closed-loop current-loop bandwidth in Hz. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution to (a): | Aspect | Linear Amplifier | Switching (PWM) Amplifier | |---|---|---| | Conduction mode | Continuous (linear region) | Switched (saturation/cutoff) | | Efficiency | 30–60% | > 95% | | Heat dissipation | High → bulky heatsink | Low → compact | | Bandwidth | High, low distortion | High, with switching ripple | | Cost (low power) | Low | Higher | | Cost (high power) | Very high | Moderate | **Why switching is preferred in rehabilitation:** - Battery-powered wearable exoskeletons demand high efficiency to extend operating time and reduce thermal load on patient-contact surfaces. - Regenerative braking (returning energy to the bus during swing phase) is naturally possible. - Compactness enables joint-integrated electronics. **Two disadvantages of switching amplifiers:** 1. **Switching ripple** in the output current causes torque ripple and audible noise (mitigated by raising $f_{sw}$ above 20 kHz). 2. **EMI generation** from high $dV/dt$ edges can interfere with biosignal sensors (EMG/EEG) used alongside the robot — requires careful shielding and filtering. #### Model Solution to (b): **(i) No-load speed $\omega_0$:** Using the formula derived in Section 3.2, step 6: $$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K_e} = \frac{36}{0.05} = 720 \text{ rad/s}$$ **(ii) Steady-state armature current at $T_L = 2$ Nm, $\omega = 50$ rad/s:** From the torque equation: $$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{2}{0.05} = 40 \text{ A}$$ Verification: The back-EMF at $\omega = 50$ rad/s is $E_b = 0.05 \times 50 = 2.5$ V. Voltage drop across the armature is $R_a \cdot I_a = 1.2 \times 40 = 48$ V. Note that $V_a = E_b + R_a I_a$ gives $36 = 2.5 + 48 = 50.5$ V, which exceeds the 36 V bus — meaning this operating point is **not physically realizable** with the given bus, illustrating why **bus voltage sizing** is a critical design step in rehabilitation robots. (In practice the bus must be elevated or the speed reduced.) **(iii) Current-loop bandwidth:** From Section 3.3, step 6: $$f_{BW} = \frac{K_i}{2\pi \cdot L_a} = \frac{60}{2\pi \times 0.003} = \frac{60}{0.01885} \approx 3183 \text{ Hz} \approx 3.18 \text{ kHz}$$ **Results:** - $\omega_0 = 720$ rad/s - $I_a = 40$ A (note the bus limitation) - $f_{BW} \approx 3.18$ kHz > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert mH to H** in the bandwidth calculation (writing $2\pi \times 3$ instead of $2\pi \times 0.003$). This single error can cost **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Stating "$K_t = K_e$ in SI units"** without explicitly showing the dimensional equivalence. Examiners require the explicit citation of this principle. > 3. **Confusing the three loop bandwidths.** The current loop is *not* 100 Hz; it is typically 1–5 kHz. Many students swap the loop order. > 4. **Drawing the H-bridge with only two switches.** A full H-bridge requires **four** switches to provide bidirectional current. > 5. **Omitting the dead-time interval** when describing PWM modulation of an H-bridge — without it, shoot-through current destroys the switches. A topper-level answer mentions 1–2 µs dead time. > 6. **Neglecting to mention patient-safety features** (current limiting, hardware E-stop, watchdog timer). In a rehabilitation course, omitting safety costs marks even if the technical content is correct. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Servo Amplifiers (KTU Module 3)** > > - **Definition:** Servo amplifier = high-power, closed-loop electronic interface between a low-level controller and a servo motor. > - **Primary job:** Convert small command signals into precisely regulated motor current (and hence torque). > - **Three nested loops (outer → inner):** Position (slowest) → Velocity (medium) → Current (fastest). Bandwidth rule: each inner loop ≥ 3–5× faster than the next outer loop. > - **Current-loop bandwidth target for rehabilitation:** **1–5 kHz** (non-negotiable for transparent interaction). > - **Class-D switching amplifiers dominate** rehabilitation hardware due to > 95% efficiency, compactness, and battery compatibility. > - **PWM formula:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{dc}$, where $D = t_{on} / T_{sw}$. > - **Motor equations you must memorize:** $V_a = R_a I_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + K_e \omega$, and $T_m = K_t I_a$. > - **SI Equivalence:** $K_t$ (Nm/A) = $K_e$ (V·s/rad) — a question-favorite to state explicitly. > - **Topologies:** H-bridge (brushed or single-phase BLDC) and 3-phase inverter (PMSM/BLDC). H-bridge has **4 switches**, three-phase inverter has **6 switches**. > - **Feedback sensors:** Encoder (position), tachometer or differentiated encoder (velocity), shunt resistor or Hall LEM (current). > - **Safety features (always mention in answers):** over-current protection (OCP), over-voltage protection (OVP), over-temperature protection (OTP), hardware emergency stop, watchdog timer, dead-time insertion in PWM. > - **Implication for rehab:** The amplifier's bandwidth and resolution determine the rendered mechanical impedance, hence the *feel* and *therapeutic quality* of the robot. > - **KTU numerical pattern:** Expect 1 derivation (5–7 marks) + 1 application/system question (7 marks) in Part B. Average voltage calculation and current-loop bandwidth calculation appear almost every exam cycle.

topics.title: Microprocessor Control, Shared Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microprocessor Control, Shared Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Microprocessor Control in Rehabilitation Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Microprocessor Control* in rehabilitation robotics refers to the use of an embedded processing unit (MCU/MPU) as the central decision-making node that acquires multi-modal sensor data, executes real-time control algorithms (PID, impedance, admittance), and generates low-level actuation commands for motors, pneumatic artificial muscles, or series elastic actuators, all within deterministic time-bounded loops (typically **1 ms to 10 ms**).

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of a microprocessor in a rehabilitation robot exactly like the **brain of a waiter in a busy restaurant**:
- The **waiter (microprocessor)** stands in the **kitchen (the robot's body)**.
- He constantly **listens to the customer** (reads sensor data — position, force, EMG).
- He looks at the **recipe book** (runs the control algorithm — PID, impedance).
- He **gives instructions to the chef** (sends PWM / voltage / current commands to motors).
- He does this **continuously, in a tight loop**, so the food (movement) is delivered correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why microprocessors and not plain analog circuits?**
> Because rehabilitation robots must adapt to *each individual patient* in real time. Only a programmable processor can reconfigure its gains, switch control modes, and apply safety limits on-the-fly, whereas analog circuits are "frozen" once built.

---

## 1.2 Shared Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Shared Control* is a bilateral control architecture in which **two intelligent agents** — the human (patient/therapist) **and** the autonomous controller (robot) — **simultaneously contribute** to the motion/force command of a rehabilitation device, governed by a defined **arbitration law** that determines the relative weight of each agent's input at every time instant.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine teaching a child to ride a bicycle using **stabilizer wheels**:
- The **child provides the steering and pedaling intent** (human command).
- The **stabilizer wheels prevent the bike from falling over** (robot assistance).
- The bike never takes over completely, but it **fills the gap** of what the child cannot yet do.
- As the child improves, stabilizers are gradually removed → this is **gain adaptation** in shared control.

> [!NOTE]
> In rehabilitation, Shared Control bridges the spectrum between **100 % manual** (the patient does everything) and **100 % autonomous** (the robot does everything). The robot's role is to **augment** the patient's residual ability, not to replace it.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally used in KTU 2024 scheme evaluations for this module:

- **Control loop period ($T_s$):** typically **1 ms – 10 ms** (hard real-time)
- **EMG sampling rate:** **1 kHz – 2 kHz**
- **PWM frequency for DC motor drivers:** **20 kHz** (above audible range)
- **Impedance stiffness ($K$) range in admittance control:** **50 N/m – 2000 N/m**
- **Patient-to-robot torque weighting factor ($\alpha$):** **0 (full robot) to 1 (full patient)**

---

## 1.4 Relation to KTU Course Outcomes

| Course Outcome | How this topic satisfies it |
|---|---|
| **CO3** | Apply microprocessor-based control architectures to rehabilitation devices |
| **CO4** | Evaluate shared-control strategies for patient-cooperative robots |
| **CO5** | Design safe human-robot interaction loops with arbitration logic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microprocessor Control — Layered Architecture

A modern rehabilitation robot is built in **four functional layers**, and the microprocessor sits at the heart of layers 2 and 3.

### Layer 1 — Sensor / Transducer Layer (Analog Domain)
- **Position sensors:** encoders, Hall-effect sensors, IMUs
- **Force/torque sensors:** strain gauges, FSRs
- **Bioelectric sensors:** surface EMG, EEG
- **Safety sensors:** limit switches, e-stops

### Layer 2 — Signal Conditioning & ADC Layer
- Amplification (INA128, AD620)
- Anti-aliasing filtering (Butterworth, cutoff $\le f_s/2.5$)
- **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** with 12-bit or 16-bit resolution

> **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist):**
> $$f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$$
> where $f_{max}$ is the highest meaningful frequency in the biosignal.

### Layer 3 — Microprocessor / Real-Time Control Layer
- **MCU families used in rehab robotics:** STM32F4, TI C2000, Arduino Due, ESP32
- **RTOS options:** FreeRTOS, RTX, Zephyr
- Runs the **control algorithm** at fixed frequency using timer interrupts
- Implements **PID / State-Feedback / Impedance / Admittance** control laws

### Layer 4 — Actuator & Power Layer
- DC motors, BLDC motors, pneumatic muscles
- Driven via H-bridge drivers (e.g., DRV8301, BTS7960)
- **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** commands generated by the MCU's timer peripherals
- Current sensing for torque control (FOC — Field Oriented Control)

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Microprocessor Control Loop (Why & How)

| Step | Action | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trigger** by timer interrupt every $T_s$ | Guarantees deterministic timing |
| 2 | **Read encoders / ADC** | Get actual position $\theta_{actual}$ and force $F_{actual}$ |
| 3 | **Compute error** $e = \theta_{desired} - \theta_{actual}$ | Forms the basis of feedback |
| 4 | **Apply control law** $u(t)$ | PID: $u=K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ |
| 5 | **Output PWM duty cycle** | Converts digital command to motor input |
| 6 | **Log / transmit** to host PC (UART/USB/Ethernet) | For visualization and tuning |
| 7 | **Check safety bounds** | Hard limit on torque, velocity, temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Watchdog Timer:** A hardware safety mechanism that resets the MCU if the main loop "freezes". KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

---

## 2.3 Shared Control — Mathematical Foundation

The defining equation of shared control is the **linear convex combination** of the patient's command and the robot's autonomous command:

$$\tau_{applied}(t) = \alpha(t)\,\tau_{patient}(t) + \big(1-\alpha(t)\big)\,\tau_{robot}(t)$$

where:
- $\tau_{applied}(t)$ = torque actually sent to the actuator
- $\tau_{patient}(t)$ = torque/intent estimated from the patient (via EMG or residual force)
- $\tau_{robot}(t)$ = torque computed by the autonomous controller
- $\alpha(t) \in [0, 1]$ = **arbitration gain** (also called *assistance level* or *patient authority*)

### 2.3.1 Special Cases of $\alpha(t)$

| Value of $\alpha$ | Mode | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha = 1$ | **Full manual** | Patient alone drives the limb; robot is passive (transparent) |
| $\alpha = 0$ | **Full autonomous** | Robot executes a pre-programmed trajectory (e.g., for completely paralyzed patients) |
| $0 < \alpha < 1$ | **Genuine shared control** | Both agents contribute; arbitration rules decide the mix |

### 2.3.2 Adaptive $\alpha(t)$ — *Assist-as-Needed (AAN)*

In modern rehab, $\alpha$ is **not constant**; it adapts to the patient's instantaneous performance.

A canonical *Error-Based AAN* rule is:

$$\alpha(t) = \alpha_{min} + (\alpha_{max} - \alpha_{min}) \cdot \frac{\vert e(t) \vert}{e_{max}}$$

subject to clamping: $0 \le \alpha \le 1$

- When the patient is **performing well** (small error $e$), $\alpha$ is **high** → less robot help.
- When the patient **struggles** (large error $e$), $\alpha$ drops → robot helps more.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the *core* of **"Assist-as-Needed (AAN)"** algorithms — a guaranteed KTU exam favourite.

---

## 2.4 Impedance & Admittance Control — The Substrate of Shared Control

Rehabilitation robots are **fundamentally compliant** because they touch a human. Compliance is mathematically expressed as a virtual mechanical impedance:

$$M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$$

Switching the input/output roles gives the **admittance** formulation:

$$F_{applied} = M_d\ddot{x}_d + B_d\dot{x}_d + K_d(x_d - x_{actual})$$

where $M_d, B_d, K_d$ are the desired virtual mass, damping, and stiffness.

> **Engineering Utility:** A therapist can *reprogram* the feel of a rehab robot (stiff, springy, free-floating) just by changing software parameters — no mechanical redesign needed. This is the practical reason impedance/admittance is the **de-facto** control paradigm for rehab devices like the **MIT-Manus**, **Lokomat**, and **ARMEO Power**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units / Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e + K_d \dot{e}$ | PID control law | $u$ in volts or PWM % |
| 3 | $\tau_{app} = \alpha \tau_p + (1-\alpha)\tau_r$ | Shared-control blending | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ |
| 4 | $\alpha(t) = \alpha_{min} + (\alpha_{max}-\alpha_{min})\frac{\vert e \vert}{e_{max}}$ | Error-based AAN | Clamped to $[0,1]$ |
| 5 | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$ | Mechanical impedance | $K$ in N/m, $B$ in Ns/m |
| 6 | $F_{app} = M_d\ddot{x}_d + B_d\dot{x}_d + K_d(x_d-x)$ | Admittance control | $M_d,B_d,K_d$ tunable |
| 7 | $G_{cl}(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s+K_p}$ | First-order closed-loop | Stable for $K_p>0$ |
| 8 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ | Natural frequency of impedance | rad/s |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}$ | Damping ratio | $\zeta = 1$ critically damped |
| 10 | $T_s \le 10$ ms | Hard real-time loop bound | rehab safety standard |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From PID to Discrete-Time Microprocessor Implementation

### 3.1.1 Continuous-Time PID

The textbook PID law is:

$$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Why each term exists:**
- $K_p e$ → proportional "push" proportional to current error.
- $K_i \int e$ → eliminates *steady-state* error (a surgeon aligning a tool: any remaining drift gets corrected over time).
- $K_d \dot{e}$ → predicts future error, damps oscillations.

### 3.1.2 Discretization for the Microprocessor

A microprocessor cannot integrate continuously. We replace the integral with a **backward-sum** and the derivative with a **backward difference**, at sampling period $T_s$.

**Step 1 — Replace the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k\,T_s$$

The recurrence for the integral term is:

$$I_n = I_{n-1} + e_n\,T_s$$

**Step 2 — Replace the derivative:**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the continuous law:**

$$u_n = K_p\,e_n + K_i\,T_s\sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + K_d\,\frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Express as a recursive difference equation (velocity form, with anti-windup):**

$$\Delta u_n = K_p(e_n - e_{n-1}) + K_i\,T_s\,e_n + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})$$

$$\boxed{\,u_n = u_{n-1} + \Delta u_n\,}$$

**Why velocity form?** It prevents *integral wind-up* — a phenomenon where $I_n$ keeps growing during saturation and causes a dangerous overshoot on the patient. This is a critical safety consideration in rehabilitation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shared Control Blending from Impedance Perspective

We start with a *desired* trajectory $x_d(t)$ and an *actual* patient motion $x_p(t)$.

**Step 1 — Define tracking error:**

$$e(t) = x_d(t) - x_p(t)$$

**Step 2 — The robot alone (impedance mode) would apply:**

$$\tau_{robot} = M_d\ddot{x}_d + B_d\dot{x}_d + K_d\,e$$

**Step 3 — The patient's voluntary torque** (measured via residual force sensor or estimated from EMG):

$$\tau_{patient} = \tau_{EMG \rightarrow torque}(EMG_{RMS})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the linear arbitration law:**

$$\tau_{app} = \alpha\,\tau_{patient} + (1-\alpha)\,\tau_{robot}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\tau_{app} = \alpha\,\tau_{patient} + (1-\alpha)\big[M_d\ddot{x}_d + B_d\dot{x}_d + K_d(x_d - x_p)\big]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the effective stiffness the patient "feels":**

$$K_{eff} = (1-\alpha)\,K_d$$
