This shows a beautiful physical meaning: **as $\alpha$ increases, the robot's virtual stiffness vanishes**, and the patient feels a softer, more transparent device. At $\alpha = 1$, $K_{eff} = 0$ — the robot is fully transparent.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most intuitive way to convince an examiner that you understand *why* shared control works — the robot literally "dials down" its own mechanical stiffness as the patient takes charge.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Control Architectures

| Property | Direct Control (manual) | Shared Control | Supervisory / Autonomous |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patient authority $\alpha$ | 1 | 0 < $\alpha$ < 1 | 0 |
| Robot authority | 0 | $1-\alpha$ | 1 |
| Sensor richness | Low | High (force + EMG) | High (vision + sensors) |
| Computational load | Low | Medium-High | High |
| Safety complexity | Low (transparent) | High (arbitration) | Very high (full autonomy) |
| Typical patient | High-functioning | Mid-recovery | Severe impairment |
| KTU exam weight | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem (Model):** A microprocessor-based elbow exoskeleton is operated in shared-control mode. The patient's voluntary torque is $\tau_p = 5$ Nm, the robot's autonomous torque is $\tau_r = 12$ Nm, and the error-based AAN controller computes $\alpha = 0.4$.

**Solution:**

Apply the shared-control law:

$$\tau_{app} = \alpha\,\tau_p + (1-\alpha)\,\tau_r$$

Substitute:

$$\tau_{app} = 0.4 \times 5 + (1-0.4) \times 12$$

$$\tau_{app} = 2.0 + 0.6 \times 12 = 2.0 + 7.2$$

$$\boxed{\,\tau_{app} = 9.2 \text{ Nm}\,}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The patient contributed 40 % of 5 Nm, the robot 60 % of 12 Nm, producing a *blended* assistance of 9.2 Nm. The patient is *not* passive — 2.0 Nm of his own effort was honoured by the system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microprocessor Control — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Intent] -->|EMG / Force| B[Sensor Array]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D[ADC]
    D --> E[Microprocessor MCU]
    E -->|Control Law PID/Impedance| F[PWM Generator]
    F --> G[Motor Driver H-Bridge]
    G --> H[Actuator DC BLDC Pneumatic]
    H --> I[Exoskeleton Joint]
    I -->|Force interaction| A
    E --> J[Safety Watchdog]
    J -->|Reset if frozen| E
    E --> K[Host PC Telemetry]
```

**Reading the loop:** Data flows **clockwise** from patient → sensor → processor → actuator → joint → back to patient. This is the canonical *closed-loop* rehabilitation architecture.

---

## 4.2 Shared Control — Arbitration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Patient Command] --> M1[Patient Model Estimator]
    M1 --> A1[Arbitration Module alpha]
    R1[Robot Autonomous Command] --> M2[Trajectory / Impedance Controller]
    M2 --> A1
    A1 -->|alpha . tau_p| SUM[Combiner Sum]
    A1 -->|1-alpha . tau_r| SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Torque Output to Actuator]
    PERF[Performance Monitor Error e] --> A1
    PERF --> LOG[Therapy Log]
```

**Key insight:** The **Arbitration Module** is the *single most important* block. It receives both the patient's command and the robot's command, computes $\alpha$ in real time, and emits the blended signal.

---

## 4.3 Control-Mode State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Manual
    Manual --> Shared : Patient unable to complete task
    Shared --> Autonomous : Severe fatigue detected
    Autonomous --> Shared : Patient regains strength
    Shared --> Manual : Patient near full recovery
    Manual --> EmergencyStop : Safety violation
    Shared --> EmergencyStop : Safety violation
    Autonomous --> EmergencyStop : Safety violation
    EmergencyStop --> [*]
```

**Why a state machine?** Because real rehab sessions transition between modes dozens of times per minute. The microprocessor's RTOS scheduler executes this state machine inside every control tick.

---

## 4.4 Sampling & Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title  Microprocessor Control Loop Timing (T_s = 1 ms)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section Sampling
    ADC Read        :a1, 0, 100
    section Compute
    PID / Impedance :a2, 100, 300
    section Output
    PWM Update      :a3, 400, 200
    section Safety
    Watchdog Reset  :a4, 600, 50
    section Communication
    Telemetry TX    :a5, 700, 300
```

**Note:** Total budget must fit within $T_s = 1$ ms. If it overruns, the watchdog will reset the MCU — hence "hard real-time".

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** *Define Shared Control in the context of rehabilitation robotics. Write its governing blending equation.*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Shared control is an architecture in which the patient and the autonomous controller **simultaneously contribute** to the actuation of a rehabilitation device. The two commands are blended via a weighting coefficient $\alpha \in [0,1]$:

> $$\tau_{applied} = \alpha\,\tau_{patient} + (1-\alpha)\,\tau_{robot}$$

> When $\alpha = 1$ the system is fully manual; when $\alpha = 0$ it is fully autonomous; intermediate values implement *Assist-as-Needed* behaviour. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for equation with limits.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** *List the four functional layers of a microprocessor-based rehabilitation robot and state the function of any one.*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Sensor / Transducer Layer** — acquires position, force, EMG, IMU signals.
> 2. **Signal Conditioning & ADC Layer** — filters and digitises the analog signals.
> 3. **Microprocessor / Real-Time Control Layer** — executes PID, impedance or admittance laws.
> 4. **Actuator & Power Layer** — delivers energy to motors or pneumatic muscles via PWM.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for each correct layer up to 3.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern]*
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a microprocessor-based rehabilitation robot. Discuss the role of the ADC, the PID law, and the watchdog timer.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A patient performs a reaching task with an exoskeleton. The patient torque is $\tau_p = 4$ Nm, the robot torque is $\tau_r = 15$ Nm, and the controller uses an error-based AAN rule with $\alpha_{min} = 0.2$, $\alpha_{max} = 0.9$, $|e| = 0.3$ m, $e_{max} = 1$ m. Compute the applied torque and comment on the patient's contribution.

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Block diagram and explanation:**

- Draw the **block diagram of SECTION 4.1** (1 mark).
- **ADC role:** converts analog sensor voltages to digital numbers the MCU can process; 12-bit ADC gives 4096 levels over 0–3.3 V ⇒ resolution ≈ 0.8 mV (1.5 marks).
- **PID law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e + K_d \dot{e}$; explain physical meaning of each term and why velocity form is used on a processor (anti-windup) (2.5 marks).
- **Watchdog timer:** independent hardware counter that resets the MCU if the main loop exceeds its budget. Mandatory for patient safety (2 marks).

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \alpha_{min} + (\alpha_{max}-\alpha_{min})\frac{\vert e \vert}{e_{max}}$$

$$\alpha = 0.2 + (0.9-0.2)\cdot \frac{0.3}{1.0} = 0.2 + 0.21 = 0.41$$

**Step 2 — Apply the shared-control law:**

$$\tau_{app} = 0.41 \times 4 + (1-0.41)\times 15$$

$$\tau_{app} = 1.64 + 0.59 \times 15 = 1.64 + 8.85 = 10.49 \text{ Nm}$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**

The patient contributed 1.64 Nm (~15.6 %) and the robot 8.85 Nm (~84.4 %). The relatively low $\alpha = 0.41$ indicates the patient is *struggling* (since the error is 30 % of the maximum), so the robot is providing most of the torque — this is the desired *Assist-as-Needed* behaviour.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for $\alpha$ formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for final numeric answer, 1 for engineering comment.)*

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Alternative Pattern]*
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the velocity-form discrete PID law suitable for microprocessor implementation, starting from the continuous-time PID equation. Explain why integral wind-up is dangerous in a rehabilitation application.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare impedance and admittance control. Show mathematically that in shared control, the effective stiffness felt by the patient is $K_{eff} = (1-\alpha)K_d$.

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Derivation:**

1. Start with $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e + K_d \dot{e}$ (1 mark).
2. Discretise: $\int e \to T_s \sum e_k$ and $\dot e \to (e_n-e_{n-1})/T_s$ (2 marks).
3. Form $\Delta u_n$ and $u_n = u_{n-1} + \Delta u_n$ (2 marks).
4. *Integral wind-up explanation:* if the actuator saturates (e.g., current limit reached), the integrator keeps accumulating, so when the error finally reverses, the actuator overshoots dangerously — in a rehab robot this could injure a patient's joint. Anti-windup clamps $I_n$ to a saturation limit (2 marks).

**(b) Impedance vs Admittance and $K_{eff}$ derivation:**

- **Impedance control** treats the robot as accepting a force input and outputting motion: $F \to x$. Used when the environment is *rigid* (2 marks).
- **Admittance control** treats the robot as accepting a motion input and outputting force: $x \to F$. Used when the environment is *light/inertial*, e.g., a free human limb (2 marks).

- **Derivation of $K_{eff}$:** start from $\tau_{app} = \alpha \tau_p + (1-\alpha)\tau_r$ with $\tau_r = K_d(x_d - x_p)$. Substituting the patient's voluntary torque $\tau_p$ and rearranging gives $K_{eff} = (1-\alpha)K_d$ (3 marks). Physical meaning: as $\alpha \to 1$, $K_{eff} \to 0$ and the device is *transparent* to the patient.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bound** $\alpha \in [0,1]$ — examiners specifically test that you clamp the arbitration gain. Lose 1 mark if you do not state it explicitly.
> 2. **Conflating impedance and admittance control** — these are *not* interchangeable. Memorise which one takes force as input and which one takes position as input.
> 3. **Skipping the discretization step in PID derivations** — writing the continuous law and stopping there earns at most 2/7. The examiner expects the difference equation form $u_n = u_{n-1} + \Delta u_n$.
> 4. **Not mentioning the watchdog** in microprocessor architectures — a single line earns you 1 mark and signals real-world awareness.
> 5. **Mixing up $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$** in the discrete equation with the wrong sign or wrong units — always re-derive on paper before writing the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Microprocessor control is a **real-time, deterministic** closed loop with $T_s \le 10$ ms.
- ✅ The **four-layer architecture**: Sensors → Conditioning/ADC → MCU/Algorithm → Actuators.
- ✅ **PID in discrete form** is what actually runs on the MCU — never the continuous form.
- ✅ **Velocity-form PID** with **anti-windup** is mandatory for patient safety.
- ✅ **Watchdog timer** is a non-negotiable safety primitive.
- ✅ **Shared control** = linear convex blending of patient and robot torques.
- ✅ **Arbitration gain $\alpha \in [0,1]$** is the *only* knob that decides "who is in charge".
- ✅ **Assist-as-Needed (AAN)** uses error-based, performance-based, or EMG-based rules to adapt $\alpha$ online.
- ✅ **Impedance** = force in, motion out; **Admittance** = motion in, force out. Both are needed for rehab.
- ✅ **Effective patient-felt stiffness** in shared admittance control = $(1-\alpha)K_d$.
- ✅ Sampling theorem $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ is the foundation of every ADC stage.
- ✅ Always include a **safety envelope** (torque, velocity, temperature limits) inside the control loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault-Tolerant Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault-Tolerant Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fault-Tolerant Control (FTC)** is a control engineering discipline that designs control systems capable of maintaining stability, performance, and acceptable operational behavior of a plant even in the presence of **component faults, sensor failures, actuator malfunctions, or unforeseen disturbances**, without requiring immediate human intervention or system shutdown.

In the specific context of **Robotic Rehabilitation (OEBRT722)**, FTC refers to the design philosophy and control architecture applied to therapeutic robots (e.g., exoskeletons, end-effector-based gait trainers, upper-limb rehabilitation devices) so that if an actuator stalls, a sensor drifts, or a gearbox fails during a therapy session on a neurologically impaired patient, the robot does NOT injure the patient and continues to deliver safe, partial, or adapted assistance.

Mathematically, a controlled plant with faults is represented as:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t) + E\,d(t) + F\,f(t)
$$

$$
y(t) = C\,x(t) + D\,u(t) + F_s\,f_s(t)
$$

where $f(t)$ and $f_s(t)$ are the **process fault** and **sensor fault** vectors, and $F$, $F_s$ are their respective fault distribution (signature) matrices. **$E\,d(t)$** captures the **disturbance** term, while **$F\,f(t)$** captures the **fault** term. The distinction between the two is critical: a disturbance is an *external* unknown input, whereas a fault is an *internal* malfunction of the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy: The Airplane with a Damaged Engine**
> Imagine you are flying a commercial twin-engine aircraft. Suddenly, one engine catches fire and shuts down. A *conventional* control system would either crash the plane or dump control to the pilot in panic. A **Fault-Tolerant Control (FTC) system** automatically detects the loss of thrust, redistributes thrust demand to the remaining engine, adjusts control surfaces, and re-balances the autopilot to keep the aircraft gliding safely to the nearest airport.
>
> **In a rehabilitation robot**, the "airplane" is a powered exoskeleton strapped to a stroke patient. The "engine" could be a knee actuator, an encoder, or a force sensor. If it fails, the FTC system must detect the failure, limit torque, switch to a safe impedance profile, and *continue therapy* — never harm the patient.

### 1.3 Two Principal Classes of FTC

| Class | Full Name | Philosophy |
|---|---|---|
| **PFTC** | **Passive Fault-Tolerant Control** | Uses a *single fixed robust controller* designed a-priori to tolerate a *predefined* set of faults. No on-line reconfiguration. |
| **AFTC** | **Active Fault-Tolerant Control** | Uses an *on-line* **Fault Detection and Isolation (FDI)** module + a **controller reconfiguration** block. Adapts in real-time to *unknown* faults. |

> [!TIP]
> For a 14-mark KTU answer, always **explicitly name** the class you are discussing and justify the choice. Examiners in Kerala consistently deduct marks for vague "FTC" answers that do not distinguish PFTC vs AFTC.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics in FTC

> [!NOTE]
> Three metrics KTU examiners repeatedly test:
>
> 1. **Fault Detectability** — the minimum fault magnitude $|f_{min}|$ that produces a statistically significant residual.
> 2. **Fault Isolation Time $T_{iso}$** — time between fault occurrence and correct classification, typically **< 50 ms** for safe rehab robots.
> 3. **Graceful Degradation Index $\eta$** — ratio of post-fault performance $J_{pf}$ to nominal performance $J_{nom}$, where $0 < \eta \leq 1$.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of an FTC System

A complete FTC system for a rehabilitation robot operates across **three logical layers**:

- **Layer 1 — Physical Layer**: Sensors (encoders, IMU, EMG, force/torque), actuators (series-elastic actuators, pneumatic muscles, DC motors), and the patient–robot interaction port.
- **Layer 2 — Diagnostic Layer (FDI)**: Runs in real time and produces a **residual signal** $r(t)$ that is nominally zero in fault-free conditions and deviates when a fault occurs. Includes *residual generation* and *decision logic* (threshold, hypothesis testing, or learning-based classifier).
- **Layer 3 — Reconfiguration / Accommodation Layer**: Receives the fault estimate $\hat{f}(t)$ and updates controller gains, switches to a back-up controller, or modifies reference trajectories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's heuristic:** A complete FTC answer must always describe all three layers and *link* them with arrows showing information flow. Marks are awarded for architecture, not just equations.

## 2.2 Passive FTC (PFTC) — Theory

A passive FTC controller is a **fixed, robust controller** $K$ designed such that closed-loop stability is preserved for *all* admissible faults $\mathcal{F}$. The design typically uses **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$** or **$\mu$-synthesis** to bound the worst-case $\mathcal{L}_2$ gain from disturbances *and* faults to regulated outputs.

The **robust performance condition** is:

$$
\sup_{\omega} \, \sigma_{\max}\!\left[T_{z\leftarrow w}(j\omega)\right] < \gamma, \quad \gamma > 0
$$

where $T_{z\leftarrow w}$ is the closed-loop transfer function from exogenous inputs (disturbances **+ faults**) $w = [d^{\top}, f^{\top}]^{\top}$ to performance outputs $z$. The parameter **$\gamma$** is called the **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$ attenuation level** — a smaller $\gamma$ means the system rejects faults better, but the controller becomes more conservative.

### 2.3 Active FTC (AFTC) — Theory

Active FTC has two sub-architectures:

- **AFTC with Controller Reconfiguration (AFTC-CR):** The FDI module identifies the fault, and a *new* controller $K_{\text{new}}$ is *swapped in* from a precomputed bank.
- **AFTC with Controller Accommodation (AFTC-CA):** The controller parameters are *continuously adapted* based on $\hat{f}(t)$, e.g., via an adaptive law using the fault estimate.

> [!TIP]
> In AFTC-CA, the **fault estimation error** $\tilde{f} = f - \hat{f}$ drives an adaptation law such as:
>
> $$\dot{\hat{f}}(t) = \Gamma \, \Phi^{\top}(x,u) \, r(t)$$
>
> where $\Gamma > 0$ is the adaptation gain matrix and $\Phi(x,u)$ is the regressor relating fault magnitude to the residual.

## 2.4 Fault Detection & Isolation (FDI) Block

FDI is the *eyes* of any AFTC system. Two principal mathematical tools dominate:

- **Observer-based FDI (e.g., Luenberger, Unknown Input Observer, Kalman Filter):** Generates residual $r = y - C\hat{x}$. A fault is declared when $\vert r_i \vert > \text{threshold}_i$.
- **Parity-space FDI:** Uses analytical redundancy relations $p(t) = V^{\top} y(t) - W^{\top} u(t)$ where $V$ and $W$ are derived such that $p \equiv 0$ in the fault-free case.

For a **Kalman Filter–based FDI**, the innovation sequence $e_k = y_k - C\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}$ is monitored; under the no-fault hypothesis, $e_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_e)$. A fault is flagged when:

$$
e_k^{\top} \Sigma_e^{-1} e_k > \chi^2_{n_y, \alpha}
$$

where $\chi^2_{n_y, \alpha}$ is the chi-square critical value with $n_y$ degrees of freedom at significance level $\alpha$ (commonly $\alpha = 0.01$).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for a 14-mark FTC answer. Memorize the units and conditions.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + Ed + Ff$ | Faulty state-space model | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $f \in \mathbb{R}^q$ |
| 2 | $r(t) = y(t) - C\hat{x}(t)$ | Residual signal | $r \in \mathbb{R}^{n_y}$ |
| 3 | $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$ | Observer dynamics | $L$ = observer gain |
| 4 | $T_{iso}$ | Fault isolation time | seconds; aim < 0.05 s |
| 5 | $\eta = J_{pf} / J_{nom}$ | Graceful degradation index | dimensionless, $0 < \eta \leq 1$ |
| 6 | $\sup_\omega \sigma_{\max}[T_{z\leftarrow w}(j\omega)] < \gamma$ | $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ PFTC condition | $\gamma > 0$ |
| 7 | $e_k^{\top} \Sigma_e^{-1} e_k > \chi^2_{n_y, \alpha}$ | $\chi^2$ FDI decision rule | $\alpha \in (0,1)$ |
| 8 | $\dot{\hat{f}} = \Gamma \Phi^{\top} r$ | Adaptive fault estimator | $\Gamma = \Gamma^{\top} > 0$ |
| 9 | $V(x,\tilde{f}) = x^{\top} P x + \text{tr}(\tilde{f}^{\top} \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{f})$ | Lyapunov candidate for AFTC stability proof | $P = P^{\top} > 0$ |
| 10 | $u = -K_1 \hat{x} - K_2 \hat{f}$ | Reconfigured control law | $K_1, K_2$ are gain matrices |
| 11 | $W_{fd} = \int_0^{T_w} r^{\top}(\tau) r(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Windowed residual energy for FDI | Joules-equivalent |
| 12 | $M\!A\!X = \max_{i} \dfrac{\vert r_i(t) \vert}{\text{thresh}_i}$ | Multi-fault isolation index | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Robotic Rehabilitation

> [!TIP]
> **Why FTC matters in rehab robots specifically:**
>
> - **Patient safety is non-negotiable.** A runaway actuator in a stroke-patient exoskeleton can cause joint dislocation or fractures. FTC must guarantee **fault-safe** operation, not just fault-tolerant performance.
> - **Long-duration therapy sessions** (30–60 minutes) statistically *guarantee* some sensor drift or intermittent encoder glitches. Without FTC, sessions get aborted.
> - **FDA / IEC 60601 medical-device compliance** for rehab robots requires documented fault-handling — FTC provides the formal framework.
> - **Cascaded failure prevention** — one failed knee motor must not propagate to hip or ankle controllers.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Dynamics of a Healthy vs Faulty System

We begin with a nominal (fault-free) LTI plant:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)
$$

with state feedback $u(t) = -K\,x(t)$. The closed-loop dynamics are:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = (A - B K)\,x(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Introduce the fault.** Suppose the $i$-th actuator loses a fraction $\alpha \in [0,1]$ of its effectiveness (a typical rehab-robot scenario where a motor partially seizes). The input matrix $B$ is replaced by $B_f = B(I - \alpha\, E_i)$, where $E_i$ is a diagonal selector with 1 at position $(i,i)$ and 0 elsewhere. The faulty plant becomes:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B_f u(t) = A x + B(I - \alpha E_i)(-K x) = (A - BK + \alpha B E_i K) x
$$

**Step 2 — Define the fault-induced perturbation matrix.**

$$
\Delta A_f \;\triangleq\; \alpha\, B\, E_i\, K
$$

**Step 3 — Write the closed-loop dynamics under fault.**

$$
\dot{x}(t) = \left(A_{cl} + \Delta A_f\right) x(t), \quad A_{cl} = A - BK
$$

**Step 4 — Stability check via Lyapunov.** Let $V(x) = x^{\top} P x$ with $P = P^{\top} > 0$. Then:

$$
\dot{V} = x^{\top}\!\left[(A_{cl} + \Delta A_f)^{\top} P + P (A_{cl} + \Delta A_f)\right] x
$$

**Step 5 — For PFTC, we require $\dot{V} < 0$ for all admissible $\alpha$.** This leads to the Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI):

$$
(A_{cl} + \Delta A_f)^{\top} P + P (A_{cl} + \Delta A_f) < 0 \quad \forall \alpha \in [0, \alpha_{max}]
$$

Solving this LMI jointly over the polytope of $\alpha$ values yields a *single* robust $K$ — this is the essence of PFTC design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic explained:** The LMI above is **not** a guess. It is the direct algebraic translation of "energy $V$ must always decrease" when the system is hit by the worst-case fault magnitude $\alpha_{max}$. The polytopic LMI exploits the fact that the LHS is *linear* in $\alpha$, so it suffices to check the inequality at the vertices of the polytope — this is the **vertex property of convex LMIs**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kalman Filter–Based Residual Generator

Consider the discrete-time faulty plant:

$$
x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k + F_d f_k + w_k
$$

$$
y_k = C x_k + v_k
$$

with $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,Q)$ and $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,R)$.

**Step 1 — Prediction.**

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A_d \hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B_d u_{k-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation (residual).**

$$
r_k = y_k - C \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance.**

$$
\Sigma_k = C P_{k\mid k-1} C^{\top} + R
$$

**Step 4 — Fault decision.** A fault is flagged at time $k$ if:

$$
d_k = r_k^{\top} \Sigma_k^{-1} r_k > \chi^2_{n_y,\, \alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Fault isolation.** When multiple actuators can fail, run a bank of *dedicated* Kalman filters — each tuned as if a specific actuator $i$ has failed — and choose the filter with the *smallest* innovation energy. The index:

$$
i^* = \arg\min_{i} \sum_{j=k-W+1}^{k} r^{(i)\top}_j \, (\Sigma^{(i)}_j)^{-1} \, r^{(i)}_j
$$

identifies the most likely faulty actuator over a sliding window of $W$ samples.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, the examiner will give you $A_d$, $C$, $Q$, $R$, and $y_k$ for a few steps, and ask you to compute $r_k$, $\Sigma_k$, and $d_k$. The decision rule is then a direct numerical comparison against the tabulated $\chi^2$ value.

## 3.3 Derivation: Adaptive Fault Estimator Update Law

The reconfiguration layer in AFTC-CA needs an on-line estimate $\hat{f}(t)$ of the fault. The standard MRAC-style update law is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the augmented state.** Let $z = [x^{\top}, \tilde{f}^{\top}]^{\top}$ with $\tilde{f} = f - \hat{f}$.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop with reconfigured controller.** Using $u = -K_1 \hat{x} - K_2 \hat{f}$:

$$
\dot{x} = A x + B(-K_1 \hat{x} - K_2 \hat{f}) + F f
$$

**Step 3 — Error dynamics.** Define $\tilde{x} = x - \hat{x}$. Subtracting the observer equation gives:

$$
\dot{\tilde{x}} = (A - LC)\tilde{x} + F \tilde{f}
$$

**Step 4 — Lyapunov candidate.**

$$
V = \tilde{x}^{\top} P_1 \tilde{x} + \tilde{f}^{\top} \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{f}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\dot{V}$.**

$$
\dot{V} = \tilde{x}^{\top}\!\left[(A-LC)^{\top} P_1 + P_1 (A-LC)\right]\tilde{x} + 2\,\tilde{x}^{\top} P_1 F \tilde{f} - 2\,\tilde{f}^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\dot{\hat{f}}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel the cross term** by choosing:

$$
\dot{\hat{f}} = \Gamma F^{\top} P_1 \tilde{x}
$$

This is the **standard adaptive fault update law**. Substituting back:

$$
\dot{V} = \tilde{x}^{\top}\!\left[(A-LC)^{\top} P_1 + P_1 (A-LC)\right]\tilde{x} < 0
$$

which proves asymptotic convergence of $\tilde{x} \to 0$ and boundedness of $\tilde{f}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic explained:** Notice how the term $2\,\tilde{f}^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\dot{\hat{f}}$ from the derivative of the second part of $V$ is deliberately chosen to *cancel* the cross term $2\,\tilde{x}^{\top} P_1 F \tilde{f}$. This is the standard **Lyapunov redesign** trick — choose the update law to annihilate the coupling.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Guide for Rehab Robotics

| Fault Scenario in Rehab Robot | Recommended FTC Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoder drift / signal loss** | AFTC with Kalman FDI + sensor reconfiguration | Sensor noise model is well known; KF optimal. |
| **Partial actuator loss (motor wear)** | AFTC-CA with adaptive $\hat{f}$ | Fault magnitude varies smoothly; adaption handles it. |
| **Complete actuator seizure** | AFTC-CR (switch to safe-mode trajectory) | Discrete event; controller swap is clean. |
| **Patient muscle spasm (high disturbance)** | PFTC with $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ | Disturbance is bounded; pre-designed robust gain suffices. |
| **EMG sensor disconnection** | PFTC with graceful sensor-fusion degradation | Pre-compute multiple fusion weights. |
| **Communication link drop (tele-rehab)** | AFTC-CR switching to local autonomy | Discrete event triggered by watchdog timer. |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Active FTC System for a Rehabilitation Exoskeleton

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Intent] --> B[Reference Generator r_ref]
    B --> C[Reconfigurable Controller K_hat]
    C --> D[Actuator Bank with Series-Elastic Units]
    D --> E[Exoskeleton Joint]
    E --> F[Patient Limb]
    F --> G[Sensor Bank: Encoders IMU F-T EMG]
    G --> H[Observer and FDI Module]
    H --> I[Residual r of t and Chi-Square Test]
    I --> J{Fault Detected?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Fault Isolation: i star]
    K --> L[Fault Estimator f_hat update law]
    L --> M[Controller Reconfiguration Block]
    M --> N[New Gain K_new from Bank]
    N --> C
    H --> O[Alarm and Safe-Mode Halt Signal]
    O --> P[Therapist Dashboard]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
    style J fill:#e1ffe1
    style L fill:#f0e1ff
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **left branch** (A → B → C → D → E → F) is the *forward control path* delivering therapy.
- The **bottom branch** (G → H → I → J) is the *FDI/feedback diagnosis path*.
- The **right branch** (K → L → M → N → C) is the *reconfiguration path* — the *only* part that differs between PFTC and AFTC.
- In a **PFTC** system, the J → K → L → M → N loop is *absent*; the controller $C$ is fixed.

## 4.2 Layered Functional Block Diagram (Information-Flow Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Layer1 [Layer 1 Physical]
        A1[Actuators]
        A2[Sensors]
        A3[Patient Robot Interface]
    end
    subgraph Layer2 [Layer 2 FDI]
        B1[Residual Generator]
        B2[Decision Logic: Threshold or Chi-Square]
        B3[Fault Isolator]
    end
    subgraph Layer3 [Layer 3 Reconfiguration]
        C1[Controller Bank: K1 K2 K3]
        C2[Adaptive Law Update]
        C3[Safety Supervisor]
    end
    A1 --> A3
    A3 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> A1
    C2 --> A1
    B3 --> C3
    C3 --> A1
    style Layer1 fill:#e3f2fd
    style Layer2 fill:#fff3e0
    style Layer3 fill:#f3e5f5
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this diagram in a KTU answer:**
> 1. Draw the three subgraphs (or hand-draw three dashed boxes).
> 2. Label each block with its **mathematical role** (e.g., "B1: $r = y - C\hat{x}$").
> 3. Use arrows to *explicitly mark* fault information flow vs control signal flow — examiners reward clarity.

## 4.3 State Machine for FTC Mode Switching

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Nominal
    Nominal --> FaultDetected : r exceeds threshold
    FaultDetected --> Isolating : run bank of observers
    Isolating --> Reconfiguring : i star identified
    Isolating --> SafeStop : isolation failed after T_max
    Reconfiguring --> Nominal : K_new stabilizes system
    Reconfiguring --> Degraded : partial recovery only
    Degraded --> SafeStop : persistent instability
    SafeStop --> [*]
    Degraded --> Nominal : manual therapist reset
    Nominal --> SafeStop : watchdog timeout
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state machine is **directly traceable** to the **IEC 61508 SIL** philosophy used in medical-device certification. Naming the states in your answer signals to a KTU examiner that you understand *both* the control theory and the **regulatory context** of rehab robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
> **Differentiate between Passive and Active Fault-Tolerant Control. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **PFTC (Passive)** | **AFTC (Active)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Controller** | Single fixed robust $K$ | On-line reconfigurable / adaptive |
| **FDI module** | Not required | Mandatory |
| **Faults handled** | Predefined bounded set | Unknown / time-varying |
| **Advantage** | Simple, computationally light, no FDI delay | Better post-fault performance, handles wider fault range |
| **Disadvantage** | Conservative; poor performance under large faults | Complexity, FDI false alarms, transient during reconfiguration |

*(Examiner: 1 mark for clean definition, 1 mark for advantage/disadvantage pair, 1 mark for crisp comparison table.)*

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
> **Define the residual signal $r(t)$ used in observer-based FDI. Why is the chi-square test on $r(t)$ preferred over a simple threshold test on individual components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- $r(t) = y(t) - C\hat{x}(t)$ — the difference between the measured sensor output and the observer's prediction. Nominally zero-mean Gaussian under no fault (1 mark).
- The **chi-square test** aggregates information across **all** $n_y$ sensor channels into a single scalar $d_k = r^{\top} \Sigma^{-1} r$, providing **direction-independent** statistical confidence (1 mark).
- It accounts for **sensor cross-correlation** via $\Sigma^{-1}$ — a simple per-channel threshold ignores this and produces many false alarms (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** answer only the mathematics of FTC without mentioning the *rehab robot context* — OEBRT722 is a domain-specific course, and examiners **deduct 2–3 marks** for generic answers.
> 2. **Always** state the assumption set (e.g., "assuming bounded fault with known distribution matrix $F$") before writing a single equation.
> 3. In the 14-mark question, if the sub-part asks for a diagram, you **must** draw a block diagram — even a hand-drawn one. A textual description alone loses 2 marks.
> 4. When deriving an adaptive law, **show all cancellation steps** in the Lyapunov function. Skipping the cancellation algebra loses 3 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With suitable state-space equations, explain the architecture of an Active Fault-Tolerant Control (AFTC) system for a lower-limb rehabilitation exoskeleton. Clearly identify the FDI module, the fault estimator, and the reconfiguration block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same exoskeleton, derive the Kalman-filter based residual generator and the chi-square fault decision rule. Show that under the no-fault hypothesis, the test statistic $d_k = r_k^{\top} \Sigma_k^{-1} r_k$ follows a chi-square distribution with $n_y$ degrees of freedom. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AFTC Architecture for a Lower-Limb Rehab Exoskeleton — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — State-space model of the faulty exoskeleton.** Let $x = [\theta_{hip}, \dot{\theta}_{hip}, \theta_{knee}, \dot{\theta}_{knee}]^{\top}$ be the joint angle/velocity state, $u = [\tau_{hip}, \tau_{knee}]^{\top}$ the actuator torques, and $f = [f_1, f_2]^{\top}$ actuator fault magnitudes (e.g., torque-loss factor).

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) + F f(t), \quad y(t) = C x(t)
$$

The matrices $A, B$ capture the **inertia–Coriolis–gravity** dynamics of the exoskeleton + patient limb segment; $F$ is the fault signature matrix linking each actuator to its effect on the state.

**Step 2 — FDI module (Observer).** A Luenberger observer runs in parallel:

$$
\dot{\hat{x}} = A \hat{x} + B u + L (y - C\hat{x})
$$

The innovation $r(t) = y - C\hat{x}$ is monitored.

**Step 3 — Chi-square decision block.** A test statistic $d_k$ is computed at each sample $k$ and compared to a threshold.

**Step 4 — Fault isolation.** A bank of $n_u$ Kalman filters (one per assumed faulty actuator) is run; the one with the lowest innovation energy identifies the failed actuator.

**Step 5 — Fault estimator and reconfiguration.** The adaptive law $\dot{\hat{f}} = \Gamma F^{\top} P \tilde{x}$ updates the fault estimate. The control law is reconfigured to $u = -K_1 \hat{x} - K_2 \hat{f}$, where $K_2$ compensates the estimated fault.

**Step 6 — Block diagram** (must be drawn; see Section 4.1 of these notes for a clean reference).

*(Mark split: 1 for state model, 1.5 for FDI explanation, 1.5 for reconfiguration, 1.5 for diagram, 1.5 for rehab context.)*

**(b) Kalman Filter Residual and Chi-Square Derivation — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Discrete-time model.**

$$
x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k + F_d f_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)
$$

$$
y_k = C x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)
$$

**Step 2 — Prediction step.**

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A_d \hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B_d u_{k-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation (residual).**

$$
r_k = y_k - C \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}
$$

Substituting $y_k$ and expanding:

$$
r_k = C (x_k - \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}) + v_k = C \epsilon_{k\mid k-1} + v_k
$$

where $\epsilon_{k\mid k-1} = x_k - \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}$ is the prediction error. Under the no-fault assumption, $\epsilon_{k\mid k-1}$ is zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $P_{k\mid k-1}$.

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance.**

$$
\Sigma_k = E[r_k r_k^{\top}] = C P_{k\mid k-1} C^{\top} + R
$$

**Step 5 — Distribution of $r_k$.** Both $\epsilon_{k\mid k-1}$ and $v_k$ are zero-mean Gaussian and independent. Therefore $r_k$ is zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $\Sigma_k$:

$$
r_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_k)
$$

**Step 6 — Mahalanobis distance.** Define:

$$
d_k = r_k^{\top} \Sigma_k^{-1} r_k
$$

This is the squared Mahalanobis distance. For a zero-mean multivariate normal $r_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_k)$, the variable $r_k^{\top} \Sigma_k^{-1} r_k$ is the sum of squares of $n_y$ independent standard normal variables (after the whitening transformation $z = \Sigma_k^{-1/2} r_k$, $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$). Hence:

$$
d_k \sim \chi^2_{n_y}
$$

**Step 7 — Decision rule.** Declare a fault at time $k$ if:

$$
d_k > \chi^2_{n_y, \alpha}
$$

where $\chi^2_{n_y, \alpha}$ is the upper $\alpha$ critical value (e.g., for $n_y = 4, \alpha = 0.01$, threshold $\approx 13.28$).

*(Mark split: 1 for model, 1 for prediction, 1 for innovation covariance, 2 for distribution proof, 1 for whitening argument, 1 for final decision rule and numerical example.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Passive Fault-Tolerant Control (PFTC) design philosophy. Formulate the $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ robust performance condition for a faulty plant and explain how a polytopic LMI can be used to design a single robust controller that tolerates a *range* of actuator effectiveness losses $\alpha \in [0, \alpha_{max}]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1-DOF rehab robot has the state-space model $\dot{x} = a x + b u + f$, with nominal $a = -2, b = 1$. The actuator may lose up to 40% effectiveness, so $b_f = b(1 - \alpha)$ with $\alpha \in [0, 0.4]$. Design a state-feedback gain $K$ such that the closed-loop poles remain in the left half-plane for the worst-case $\alpha$. Verify using the closed-loop eigenvalue $a - b_f K$ at $\alpha = 0$ and $\alpha = 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PFTC and Polytopic LMI — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — PFTC philosophy.** PFTC designs *one* fixed controller that is *passively* tolerant of a pre-specified fault set, without any on-line reconfiguration or FDI. The controller is typically synthesized via $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ or $\mu$-synthesis to bound the worst-case gain from disturbances and faults to a regulated output.

**Step 2 — Faulty plant in standard $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ form.** Augment the plant with weighting functions $W_d, W_f$ on disturbances and faults, and $W_z$ on the regulated output. The generalized plant $P(s)$ maps exogenous inputs $w = [d^{\top}, f^{\top}]^{\top}$ to regulated output $z$.

**Step 3 — Closed-loop transfer function $T_{z\leftarrow w}$.** With the controller $K$ closing the loop, the **robust performance condition** is:

$$
\sup_{\omega} \sigma_{\max}\!\left[T_{z\leftarrow w}(j\omega)\right] < \gamma
$$

**Step 4 — Polytopic LMI formulation.** The matrix $A_{cl}(\alpha) = A - b K (1 - \alpha)$ depends *affinely* on $\alpha$. Therefore:

$$
A_{cl}(\alpha) = \alpha_0 A_0 + \alpha_1 A_1, \quad \alpha_0 = 1 - \alpha, \, \alpha_1 = \alpha
$$

with $A_0 = A - bK$, $A_1 = A - b(1-\alpha_{max})K$? No — properly: $A_{cl}(\alpha) = A - b(1-\alpha)K = A - bK + \alpha bK$. The two *vertex* matrices are:

- Vertex $\alpha = 0$: $A_{cl,0} = A - bK$
- Vertex $\alpha = \alpha_{max}$: $A_{cl,1} = A - b(1-\alpha_{max})K$

The LMI $(A_{cl,i})^{\top} P + P (A_{cl,i}) < 0$ for $i = 0, 1$ with a common $P = P^{\top} > 0$ guarantees $\dot{V} < 0$ for all $\alpha$ in the convex hull — by convexity of the LMI.

**Step 5 — Solve** the LMI numerically (e.g., via YALMIP/SeDuMi) to obtain $K$ and $P$.

*(Mark split: 1 for PFTC philosophy, 1 for $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ condition, 2 for polytopic formulation, 1.5 for vertex argument, 1.5 for common $P$ and solution procedure.)*

**(b) Numerical Design — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Define closed-loop eigenvalue.**

$$
\lambda(\alpha) = a - b(1 - \alpha) K = -2 - (1 - \alpha) K
$$

**Step 2 — Stability requires $\lambda(\alpha) < 0$ for all $\alpha \in [0, 0.4]$.**

- At $\alpha = 0$: $\lambda(0) = -2 - K < 0 \Rightarrow K > -2$ (i.e., $K > -2$).
- At $\alpha = 0.4$: $\lambda(0.4) = -2 - 0.6 K < 0 \Rightarrow 0.6K > -2 \Rightarrow K > -3.33$.

**Step 3 — Worst-case check.** The most stringent condition is the one that *most easily* drives $\lambda$ to zero or positive. To maximize actuator authority under fault, we want the *most negative* $\lambda$ — but stability is guaranteed as long as $\lambda < 0$. The *least negative* value of $\lambda$ occurs at $\alpha = 0.4$ if $K > 0$, and at $\alpha = 0$ if $K < 0$. Let's pick $K = 1$ (a sensible positive gain for a rehab robot needing tracking).

**Step 4 — Verify.**

$$
\lambda(0) = -2 - 1(1-0) = -3 < 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\lambda(0.4) = -2 - 1(1 - 0.4) = -2 - 0.6 = -2.6 < 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** $K = 1$ stabilizes the system for all $\alpha \in [0, 0.4]$. The closed-loop time constant at worst case is $\tau = 1 / 2.6 \approx 0.385$ s — sufficiently fast for a rehab robot tracking slow patient movements.

*(Mark split: 1 for eigenvalue expression, 1 for stability inequalities, 1.5 for worst-case identification, 1.5 for numerical evaluation, 1 for conclusion and rehab context, 1 for units/physical interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Examination Gold):**
>
> - **FTC = ability to maintain stability & acceptable performance under faults.** Always define it in this two-clause form.
> - **PFTC** = one *fixed* robust controller, no FDI, conservative but simple. **AFTC** = FDI + reconfiguration, more powerful but more complex.
> - The **faulty state-space equation** is $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + Ed + Ff$. Know the role of every term: $A$ = nominal dynamics, $B$ = nominal input, $E$ = disturbance channel, $F$ = fault channel.
> - The **residual** $r(t) = y(t) - C\hat{x}(t)$ is the *fingerprint* of a fault. Nominally Gaussian zero-mean.
> - The **chi-square test** $d_k = r_k^{\top} \Sigma_k^{-1} r_k > \chi^2_{n_y, \alpha}$ is the **gold-standard** FDI decision rule.
> - **AFTC-CR** = controller swap from a bank. **AFTC-CA** = continuous adaptation via $\dot{\hat{f}} = \Gamma F^{\top} P \tilde{x}$.
> - **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$ PFTC** condition: $\sup_\omega \sigma_{\max}[T_{z\leftarrow w}(j\omega)] < \gamma$. Smaller $\gamma$ = better fault rejection, more conservative controller.
> - **Polytopic LMI**: if $A_{cl}$ is affine in the fault parameter $\alpha$, check the LMI at the **vertices** of the polytope and use a *common* $P$.
> - **Lyapunov redesign** is the *standard* trick to derive the adaptive fault update law — choose $\dot{\hat{f}}$ to cancel the cross term in $\dot{V}$.
> - **Three layers**: Physical (sensors/actuators) → FDI (residual + decision) → Reconfiguration (controller swap/adaption). Always mention all three.
> - **Rehab-specific concerns**: patient safety > performance, IEC 60601 compliance, fault-safe (not just fault-tolerant) behaviour, graceful degradation.
> - **Graceful degradation index** $\eta = J_{pf} / J_{nom}$ quantifies *how well* the system performs post-fault. Cite the value range $0 < \eta \leq 1$.
> - **Fault isolation time $T_{iso}$**: typical target < 50 ms for safe rehab robot operation.
> - **Examiner's pet phrases** to use in answers: "fault signature matrix $F$", "analytical redundancy", "vertex property of convex LMIs", "Lyapunov redesign", "innovation sequence", "Mahalanobis distance".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrated Controllers, Electromagnetic Compatibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integrated Controllers, Electromagnetic Compatibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Integrated Controllers in Robotic Rehabilitation

### Formal Definition
An **Integrated Controller** in a robotic rehabilitation system is a unified hardware-software architecture that consolidates multiple control loops (position, velocity, torque, impedance, admittance, bio-signal) and communication interfaces (sensors, actuators, haptic devices, patient I/O) into a single coherent real-time computational framework. It coordinates the trajectory planner, low-level joint servos, safety supervisor, and the human–machine interaction layer (exoskeleton, end-effector, or treadmill-based gait trainer) under deterministic timing constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Under the 2024 Scheme, the term *Integrated* explicitly means the **co-design of control law, embedded hardware, and communication bus**, not merely the use of a single microcontroller. The examiner expects the student to discuss **architecture, hierarchy, and real-time scheduling** together.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Conductor of an Orchestra"
Imagine a robotic exoskeleton assisting a stroke patient to walk:
- The **muscles** of the robot are the **actuators** (motors at the knee, hip, ankle).
- The **nerves** carrying signals are the **sensor buses** (encoders, IMUs, EMG electrodes on the patient's skin).
- The **brain** that decides when each muscle must fire, with what force, and in sync with the others is the **Integrated Controller**.

A conductor (integrated controller) does NOT play every instrument. Instead, the conductor:
1. **Reads** the musical score (the therapist's prescribed therapy plan),
2. **Listens** to the live performance (real-time biosignals from the patient),
3. **Issues** synchronized cues to every section (sends torque/position commands to each joint),
4. **Adjusts** tempo on the fly (re-plans trajectory when the patient fatigues).

If the conductor is slow, distracted, or out of sync, the music collapses — the patient may stumble. If the conductor is **deterministic, low-latency, and aware of all subsystems**, the patient walks smoothly. This is precisely the role of the integrated controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Determinism** is a key word in KTU 2024. A controller is *real-time* only if worst-case response time is bounded — not merely *fast*.

---

## 1.2 Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)

### Formal Definition
**Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** is the ability of an electrical/electronic system (or biomedical device) to function satisfactorily in its intended electromagnetic environment **without introducing intolerable electromagnetic disturbances** to other equipment or being adversely affected by such disturbances.

EMC has two complementary aspects:

| Aspect | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Emission** | EMI | Unintentional electromagnetic energy released by a device that may disturb others |
| **Immunity (Susceptibility)** | EMS | Ability of a device to withstand electromagnetic disturbance without performance degradation |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module explicitly couples EMC with **rehabilitation robotics** because these devices operate in close proximity to the human body (often skin-contact electrodes, implantable stimulators in FES, and sensitive EEG/EMG amplifiers). Patient safety (IEC 60601-1) and measurement integrity (low-level bio-signals) both demand strict EMC compliance.

### Conceptual Analogy — A Quiet Restaurant Conversation
Think of a hospital rehabilitation ward as a **crowded restaurant**:
- Every robotic exoskeleton, infusion pump, and ECG monitor is a **table of diners** trying to have a conversation.
- Each conversation is a low-power **electromagnetic signal** carrying vital data (EMG, joint angle, FES pulse).
- The "noise" of cutlery, music, and other tables is the **ambient EMI** (from switching power supplies, Wi-Fi routers, mobile phones, fluorescent lights).
- **EMC** is the social convention that says: *speak at the right volume (limit emissions), and use good listening skills despite the noise (maintain immunity)* — so that the patient's EMG signal is not swamped by the motor's PWM switching noise, and the exoskeleton's controller is not reset by a passing nurse's mobile phone.

### Standard Metric (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Unit:** Field strength is measured in **V/m** (volts per metre) for radiated emissions and **dBµV** for conducted emissions. Shielding effectiveness is in **decibels (dB)**.

The **International Special Committee on Radio Interference (CISPR)** and **IEC 60601-1-2** (medical EMC standard) define the permissible emission/immunity levels for rehabilitation-grade equipment.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Topics Are Coupled in Module 3

In a rehabilitation robot:
- **High-power PWM motor drives** (5–48 V, 10–100 A peak) coexist with **micro-volt-level EMG/EEG front-ends**.
- The controller's switching edges create harmonics from **DC to several hundred MHz**.
- Patient safety requires that **leakage currents**, **radiated fields**, and **ESD events** remain below medical limits.

Therefore, the *integrated controller* must be designed **with EMC as a first-class constraint**, not as an afterthought. The two topics are inseparable in the KTU 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Integrated Controller Architectures

### 2.1.1 Centralized Architecture
A **single high-performance processor** (e.g., industrial PC, DSP, or multicore ARM) handles:
- High-level trajectory planning (therapist-prescribed motion)
- Low-level joint servo loops
- Bio-signal processing
- Haptic rendering
- Safety supervision

**Advantages:** Tight synchronization, low communication latency, simple software stack.

**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure; scaling to many DOF (degrees of freedom) hits the CPU hard; long cable runs from distributed sensors increase noise pickup (an EMC problem).

### 2.1.2 Distributed Architecture
Control intelligence is pushed to **per-joint or per-subsystem microcontrollers** (e.g., dsPIC, STM32, TI C2000). A **fieldbus** (CAN, EtherCAT, RS-485, PROFINET) links them to a supervisory host.

**Advantages:** Scalability, fault isolation, parallelism, shorter analog cables (better EMC).

**Disadvantages:** Bus latency, deterministic scheduling complexity, more firmware to maintain.

### 2.1.3 Hierarchical / Hybrid Architecture (Most Common in Rehab Robots)
Three layers:

| Layer | Function | Typical Hardware | Cycle Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-level (Supervisory)** | Therapy plan, patient state machine, GUI | Industrial PC / tablet | 100 ms – 1 s |
| **Mid-level (Coordination)** | Trajectory generation, impedance/admittance law | DSP / ARM Cortex-A | 1 – 10 ms |
| **Low-level (Servo)** | Current/torque/position loop per joint | FPGA / MCU / smart servo drive | 50 – 250 µs |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Lokomat** (Hocoma) and **Armeo Power** (Hocoma) use this three-tier structure. KTU examiners often cite it as a reference implementation.

### 2.1.4 Networked / Cloud-Connected Controller
Modern rehab robots expose a **telemetry channel** (MQTT over Wi-Fi, OPC-UA) to a remote clinician. The integrated controller then includes a **gateway MCU** with secure boot, TLS stack, and OTA firmware update.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The wireless link **adds an EMC burden**: the antenna coupling can re-radiate harmonics from the motor bus into the bio-amplifier. The KTU 2024 module specifically tests this coupling awareness.

---

## 2.2 Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Layer

A KTU-level answer must mention that the integrated controller runs on an **RTOS** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RT-Linux, QNX, RTX) that guarantees:
- **Bounded worst-case interrupt latency** $L_{max}$
- **Bounded task jitter** $J_{max}$
- **Priority-based preemptive scheduling** (Rate Monotonic or Deadline Monotonic)

A common design rule for a 6-DOF upper-limb exoskeleton:

$$f_{servo} \approx 1 \text{ kHz} \quad (\text{position/torque loop})$$

$$f_{bio} \approx 500 \text{ Hz} - 2 \text{ kHz} \quad (\text{EMG/force signal})$$

$$f_{plan} \approx 100 \text{ Hz} \quad (\text{trajectory update})$$

The integrated scheduler must satisfy the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** for $n$ periodic tasks:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

where $C_i$ is worst-case execution time and $T_i$ is the period of task $i$. For $n \to \infty$, $U_{max} \approx 0.693$.

---

## 2.3 Electromagnetic Compatibility — Deeper Theory

### 2.3.1 Sources of EMI in a Rehab Robot

1. **Motor drive switching** (PWM at 10–50 kHz → harmonics up to several MHz).
2. **Solenoid valves / relay coils** (clamp diodes needed).
3. **Switched-mode power supplies** (SMPS) on the controller board.
4. **DC–DC converters** in battery packs.
5. **Wireless modules** (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee) — intentional radiators.
6. **ESD events** from patient contact or therapist handling.
7. **Mains harmonics** from the charger.

### 2.3.2 Two Coupling Paths

- **Conducted EMI:** travels along cables/power rails, measured as voltage on a **Line Impedance Stabilization Network (LISN)** in the 150 kHz – 30 MHz band.
- **Radiated EMI:** escapes as E-field/H-field, measured with antennas in the 30 MHz – 1 GHz band (and above for medical devices per IEC 60601-1-2:2014+A1:2020 which extends immunity up to 6 GHz).

### 2.3.3 Shielding Effectiveness (SE)

A continuous metallic enclosure attenuates an incident wave. The **total SE** is the sum of three loss mechanisms:

$$SE_{dB} = R_{dB} + A_{dB} + B_{dB}$$

| Term | Physical Meaning | Dominant Regime |
|---|---|---|
| $R_{dB}$ | First-surface reflection loss | Low frequency, high conductivity |
| $A_{dB}$ | Absorption loss inside the shield | High frequency (skin effect) |
| $B_{dB}$ | Re-reflection (internal) correction | Thin shields at low frequency |

**Reflection loss** for a far-field plane wave striking a thick conductor (conductivity $\sigma$, permeability $\mu_r$):

$$R_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left[\frac{(\eta_0 / 4R_s)^2 + 1}{(\eta_0 / 4R_s)}\right] \approx 168 + 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_r}{\mu_r f^3 r^2}\right)$$

where $R_s = \sqrt{\pi f \mu_0 / \sigma}$ is the **surface resistance**, $r$ is the distance from source (m), $f$ in Hz, $\sigma_r$ is conductivity relative to copper.

**Absorption loss:**

$$A_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot t = 8.686 \cdot t \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

For copper at $f = 1$ MHz, $\alpha \approx 1.5 \times 10^4$ Np/m, so a $t = 1$ mm shield gives $A_{dB} \approx 131$ dB — practically total absorption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apertures** (display cutouts, ventilation grilles, connector shells) degrade shielding drastically. The rule of thumb is the **slot antenna rule**: keep the maximum slot dimension $d < \lambda / 20$ of the highest threat frequency.

### 2.3.4 Common-Mode vs. Differential-Mode Noise

- **Differential-mode (DM):** noise current flowing in opposite directions on the signal pair — rejected by an X-capacitor / DM choke.
- **Common-mode (CM):** noise current flowing in the same direction on all conductors, returning via ground/chassis — rejected by a **Y-capacitor** and **CM choke**.

The **CM choke impedance:**

$$Z_{CM} = j 2 \pi f L_{CM} \quad \text{at low } f$$

and at high frequency the parasitic capacitance $C_p$ sets the self-resonance:

$$f_{SR} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{CM} C_p}}$$

### 2.3.5 Grounding Topologies

| Topology | Use Case | EMC Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-point ground** | Low-frequency analog (< 1 MHz) | Avoids ground loops but starves HF return paths |
| **Multi-point ground** | High-frequency digital, RF | Minimizes loop area; chassis serves as return |
| **Hybrid / Chassis-ground + signal-ground** | Mixed-signal rehab boards | Bond at one point, separate planes for analog/digital |

### 2.3.6 ESD Protection

The **Human-Body Model (HBM)** discharge is a charged 100 pF capacitor discharged through 1.5 kΩ. The peak current for an 8 kV contact discharge is:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V}{R} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right) \approx \frac{8000}{1500} \cdot 1 = 5.33 \text{ A (instantaneous)}$$

A **TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) diode** clamps this to $V_{clamp} \ll V_{max,IO}$ within picoseconds.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Rehab robotics:** Exoskeleton controllers (Ekso, ReWalk, HAL) use hierarchical architectures with EtherCAT buses; EMC compliance per **IEC 60601-1-2** is mandatory for FDA/CE clearance.
- **FES systems:** Functional Electrical Stimulators inject current pulses that must not corrupt nearby EMG recording channels — requires careful grounding and isolation barriers.
- **Hospital EMC environment:** Rehab wards host MRI, electrosurgical units, and Wi-Fi — the controller must coexist with all.
- **Automotive/industrial robotics:** the same hierarchical + distributed pattern is used in autonomous vehicles, industrial arms, and surgical robots (da Vinci).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Absorption Loss of a Conductive Shield

**Goal:** Show that absorption loss in a metal sheet is $A_{dB} = 8.686 \, t / \delta$, where $\delta$ is the skin depth.

### Step 1 — Define the attenuation constant of a good conductor

For a good conductor ($\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon$), the complex propagation constant is:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j \omega \mu (\sigma + j \omega \varepsilon)} \approx (1 + j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

The real part (attenuation constant) is:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

### Step 2 — Define the skin depth

The skin depth $\delta$ is the depth at which the field amplitude falls to $1/e$:

$$\delta \equiv \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

### Step 3 — Express the field ratio across a thickness $t$

A plane wave entering the shield at $x=0$ decays as $E(x) = E_0 e^{-\alpha x}$. The amplitude ratio at $x=t$ is:

$$\frac{E(t)}{E(0)} = e^{-\alpha t} = e^{-t/\delta}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to decibels (natural log to base-10)

$$A_{dB} = -20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E(t)}{E(0)}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(e^{\,t/\delta}) = 20 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta} \cdot \log_{10}(e)$$

Using the identity $\log_{10}(e) = 1/2.3026 \approx 0.4343$:

$$A_{dB} = 20 \cdot 0.4343 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta} = 8.686 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $A_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot t / \delta$. This is the canonical form cited in every EMC textbook and in the KTU 2024 reference material.

### Step 5 — Numerical sanity check (Copper at 1 MHz)

- $\sigma_{Cu} = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m
- $\mu_r = 1$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot 10^6 \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 5.8 \times 10^7}}$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$\pi \cdot 10^6 \approx 3.1416 \times 10^6$$

$$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.2566 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$3.1416 \times 10^6 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.9478$$

$$3.9478 \times 5.8 \times 10^7 \approx 2.2897 \times 10^8$$

$$\delta = 1 / \sqrt{2.2897 \times 10^8} \approx 1 / (1.5132 \times 10^4) \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 66.1 \; \mu\text{m}$$

For $t = 1$ mm $= 1000 \; \mu$m:

$$A_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \frac{1000}{66.1} = 8.686 \cdot 15.13 \approx 131.4 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms: a **1 mm copper sheet provides over 130 dB of absorption loss at 1 MHz** — effectively total attenuation. Real shields are thinner (e.g., 35 µm copper cladding on PCB) and rely mostly on reflection + sealing apertures.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Control Architecture vs EMC Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: "Compare centralized and distributed controllers in a rehab robot." The analytical matrix below is the examiner's gold standard.

| Criterion | Centralized | Distributed | Hierarchical (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Worst-case loop latency** | Very low (single CPU, shared memory) | Higher (bus transit) | Low for servo, moderate for coordination |
| **Cable runs from sensors** | Long → more EMI pickup | Short → better EMC | Medium |
| **Fault isolation** | Poor (single CPU) | Excellent (per-joint MCU) | Good (mid-level supervisor isolates faults) |
| **Scalability to N DOF** | Poor (CPU saturates) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **EMC design complexity** | High (long cables act as antennas) | Lower | Lowest (modular Faraday sub-enclosures) |
| **Cost (electronics)** | High (one powerful CPU) | Medium (many MCUs) | High (mixed hardware tiers) |
| **Real-time determinism** | Achievable (single scheduling) | Harder (multi-node sync) | Achievable (RTOS + bus sync) |
| **KTU board answer suitability** | "Discus architecture with diagram" | "List advantages" | "Most appropriate for 6-DOF exoskeleton" |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Common-Mode Choke Sizing for a Rehab Robot

**Problem:** A 6-DOF upper-limb exoskeleton has a 24 V / 20 A brushed-DC bus with PWM at 20 kHz. The total cable length from controller to actuators is 1.5 m. The conducted EMI measurement shows a 45 dBµV peak at 25 MHz. To meet **CISPR 11 / EN 55011 Class B**, the limit is 40 dBµV at 25 MHz. We need a common-mode choke that provides $\ge 12$ dB additional attenuation at 25 MHz.

**Step 1 — Inductive reactance needed**

For a CM choke, the dominant attenuation near self-resonance is resistive. A reasonable design targets $|Z_{CM}| \approx 100$ Ω at 25 MHz (gives ~20 dB).

$$L_{CM} = \frac{Z_{CM}}{2 \pi f} = \frac{100}{2 \pi \cdot 25 \times 10^6} \approx 6.37 \times 10^{-7} \text{ H} = 0.637 \; \mu\text{H}$$

**Step 2 — Check self-resonance**

With parasitic capacitance $C_p \approx 5$ pF (typical for a toroid wound on a ferrite core):

$$f_{SR} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C_p}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{0.637 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$\sqrt{0.637 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{3.185 \times 10^{-18}} = 1.785 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$f_{SR} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 1.785 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 8.92 \times 10^{7} \text{ Hz} = 89.2 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Self-resonance is well above 25 MHz, so the choke behaves inductively in the band of interest. The design is **valid** for this EMC problem.

**Step 3 — Choose Y-capacitor to shunt CM noise**

Place a **Y2-rated ceramic capacitor** (1 nF, 5 kV impulse withstand) from each power line to chassis:

$$Z_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 25 \times 10^6 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 6.37 \; \Omega$$

The LC divider (choke in series, cap to chassis) gives additional attenuation of:

$$A_{extra} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{Z_{CM}}{Z_C}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{100}{6.37}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(16.7) \approx 24.4 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 0.64 µH CM choke plus 1 nF Y-capacitors provides ~24 dB attenuation, more than the required 12 dB. Compliance with CISPR 11 Class B is achievable.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Define an Integrated Controller in the context of a robotic rehabilitation system. List any two advantages of a hierarchical architecture.**

**Model Answer (expected length ≈ 1 page):**

An **Integrated Controller** in a robotic rehabilitation system is a unified real-time computational framework that consolidates the high-level therapy planner, mid-level trajectory / impedance coordinator, low-level joint servo loops, bio-signal acquisition, and the safety supervisor into a single coordinated architecture.

Two advantages of a **hierarchical** (3-tier) architecture:
1. **Latency scalability** — the high-level layer runs at 100 ms (plan), the mid-level at 1–10 ms (impedance), and the low-level servo at 50–250 µs (current/torque). Each tier meets its real-time deadline without burdening higher tiers.
2. **Fault isolation & modular EMC design** — each joint MCU is a small Faraday sub-enclosure with short cable runs, reducing both single-point-of-failure risk and radiated/conducted EMI pickup from long sensor wires.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "the controller is a single chip" — this is **not** the KTU 2024 definition. The word *integrated* refers to *architectural consolidation of control law, hardware, and communication*, not to a single SoC.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)? Distinguish between conducted and radiated EMI.**

**Model Answer:**

**EMC** is the ability of an electrical/electronic system to operate satisfactorily in its intended electromagnetic environment **without causing or suffering intolerable electromagnetic disturbance**. It is the dual property of **low emission** and **high immunity**.

| Aspect | Conducted EMI | Radiated EMI |
|---|---|---|
| **Path** | Wires, cables, PCB traces, power rails | Free space as E-field / H-field |
| **Frequency band (typical)** | 150 kHz – 30 MHz | 30 MHz – 6 GHz (medical) |
| **Measurement** | LISN + spectrum analyser (dBµV) | Antenna + EMI receiver (dBµV/m) |
| **Typical source in rehab robot** | PWM motor current ripple, SMPS noise | Cable radiation, antenna harmonics |
| **Mitigation** | Filters (CM choke, X/Y caps), ferrite beads | Shielding, gaskets, short cables, layout |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT state EMC means "no EMI" — it means EMI is *managed within tolerable limits*. A system can have EMI yet be EMC-compliant.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Integrated Controller Architecture

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier hierarchical architecture** of an integrated controller used in a 6-DOF upper-limb exoskeleton. Discuss the cycle time, hardware choice, and main function of each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **how distributed vs centralized architectures affect EMC performance** in a rehabilitation robot. What cable-management practices mitigate radiated emissions? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three-Tier Architecture — 7 marks**

*Tier 1 — High-Level Supervisory (cycle 100 ms – 1 s)*:
- **Hardware:** Industrial PC / tablet / ARM Cortex-A running Linux + RT-Preempt or Windows IoT.
- **Function:** Hosts the therapist GUI, stores the patient record, downloads the prescribed therapy protocol, and issues a coarse *task descriptor* (target trajectory waypoints, impedance setpoints) to the mid-level.
- **Why 100 ms?** Human reaction time is ~200 ms, so 100 ms is sufficient to update the plan.

*Tier 2 — Mid-Level Coordination (cycle 1–10 ms)*:
- **Hardware:** DSP (e.g., TI C6000) or ARM Cortex-M4/M7 running an RTOS (FreeRTOS, RTX).
- **Function:** Generates the *smooth reference trajectory* from the waypoints using a 5th-order polynomial or minimum-jerk profile; implements the **impedance / admittance law** (e.g., $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{human}$); runs the bio-signal pipeline (EMG rectification + envelope at 500 Hz – 2 kHz).
- **Why 1–10 ms?** Impedance control requires stability against the human's voluntary motion, which has significant content up to ~50 Hz.

*Tier 3 — Low-Level Servo (cycle 50–250 µs)*:
- **Hardware:** Per-joint FPGA or MCU (TI C2000, dsPIC, STM32) with hardware PWM and ADC.
- **Function:** Closes the **current / torque loop** at 10–20 kHz and the **position loop** at 1–4 kHz; reads encoder / resolver; applies field-oriented control (FOC) for brushless motors.
- **Why 50–250 µs?** PWM ripple must be rejected; current loop bandwidth is typically $\ge 1$ kHz for a stiff haptic feel.

Communication buses:
- Supervisory ↔ Mid-level: **Ethernet / Wi-Fi** (TCP-IP or UDP).
- Mid-level ↔ Servo: **EtherCAT, CAN-FD, or SPI** (deterministic, low-jitter).

**Block diagram (already given in Section 4.1 — reproduce in the answer sheet).**

**(b) Distributed vs Centralized and EMC — 7 marks**

*Centralized architecture* places the controller CPU near the host PC, with **long cables** to motors and sensors. Long cables:
- Act as efficient **antennas** (length $\sim \lambda/4$ at relevant harmonics) — radiating PWM edges into the EMG/EEG front-end.
- Provide a large **common-mode loop area** — picking up ambient fields from mains, fluorescent lamps, and Wi-Fi routers.
- Force the **single ground reference** to carry noisy return currents over long distances, leading to ground-bounce that injects into low-level analog channels.

*Distributed architecture* places a small MCU next to each actuator. Cables are short (≤ 30 cm) and the noise source and victim are physically separated. Advantages:
- **Loop areas are minimized** → less magnetic pickup.
- **Local bulk capacitors** near each drive handle the high di/dt of PWM switching.
- Each sub-assembly can be **individually shielded** as a Faraday module.
- A **galvanic isolator** (ADuM1411, ISO1050) between the bus and the joint breaks the common-mode return path.

*Cable-management practices to mitigate radiated emissions:*
1. Use **shielded twisted-pair (STP)** for encoder and EMG cables; ground the shield **at one end only** to avoid ground loops, or at both ends with a low-impedance bond if the equipment is in a bonded ward.
2. Keep power and signal cables in **separate trays** with at least 20 cm spacing; cross at 90°.
3. Use **ferrite clamps** on cables leaving the enclosure.
4. Add **RC snubbers** across relay/solenoid coils.
5. Maintain **chassis integrity** — every cable entry uses a **shielded backshell**; cable glands provide 360° shield termination.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (7-mark):** Students often list advantages of distributed architecture without quantifying the EMC benefit. The examiner awards full marks only if you mention **loop area, common-mode paths, and shield termination** explicitly.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Electromagnetic Compatibility

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Define **Shielding Effectiveness (SE)**. Derive the expression for **absorption loss** in a conductive enclosure, stating the role of the skin depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **IEC 60601-1-2**, explain the EMC requirements applicable to a **rehabilitation robot used in a clinical environment**. Discuss the role of **ESD protection and CM filtering** on the power input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Shielding Effectiveness and Absorption Loss — 7 marks**

**Definition:** Shielding Effectiveness (SE) is the ratio (in dB) of the magnitude of the incident field to the magnitude of the field that penetrates the shield:

$$SE_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\vert E_i \vert}{\vert E_t \vert}\right) = R_{dB} + A_{dB} + B_{dB}$$

where $R_{dB}$ is the **first-surface reflection loss**, $A_{dB}$ is the **absorption loss** (energy dissipated as heat inside the conductive medium), and $B_{dB}$ is a **re-reflection correction** that becomes significant for thin shields at low frequency.

*Derivation of $A_{dB}$:*
For a good conductor, the propagation constant is

$$\gamma = (1+j)\alpha \quad \text{with} \quad \alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

The **skin depth** $\delta \equiv 1/\alpha$ is the distance over which the field amplitude falls to $1/e$:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

A plane wave traversing a thickness $t$ of shield is attenuated as $E(t) = E_0 e^{-\alpha t} = E_0 e^{-t/\delta}$. Converting to decibels:

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(e^{\,t/\delta}) = 20 \cdot (t/\delta) \cdot \log_{10}(e) = 8.686 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta}$$

**Role of $\delta$:** A higher frequency (smaller $\delta$) gives a *larger* $A_{dB}$ for the same thickness. A thicker shield or a higher-conductivity material also increases $A_{dB}$. For a 1 mm copper shield at 1 MHz, $\delta \approx 66 \; \mu$m, giving $A_{dB} \approx 131$ dB.

**(b) IEC 60601-1-2 & Rehabilitation Robots — 7 marks**

**IEC 60601-1-2** is the international standard for **electromagnetic disturbances — requirements and tests** for medical electrical equipment. The 4th edition (2014) with Amendment 1 (2020) extends the immunity test range to 6 GHz and tightens ESD requirements.

Requirements applicable to a rehab robot:
- **Emission limits:** Conducted emissions must meet **CISPR 11 Group 1 Class B** (≤ 40 dBµV quasi-peak at 25 m at 0.15–30 MHz); radiated emissions must meet similar Class B limits at 30 MHz – 1 GHz.
- **Immunity tests:** ±8 kV contact / ±15 kV air ESD; 10 V/m RF immunity from 80 MHz to 6 GHz; 3 V/m at 1.8 GHz (DECT band, common in hospitals); surge, burst (EFT), and voltage dip immunity on AC mains.
- **Patient-coupled currents:** The standard limits **patient auxiliary current** and **leakage current** under both normal and single-fault conditions.

**Role of ESD protection:**
A patient or therapist can carry several kV of electrostatic charge and discharge it into the exoskeleton frame. The **HBM** discharge (150 pF + 1.5 kΩ → 150 ns rise time) can latch-up or destroy the controller's MCU. A **TVS diode** array (e.g., PESD5V0L1BA) clamps the ESD pulse to $V_{clamp} \approx 5$ V within picoseconds, and a series **resistor** (33 Ω) limits the residual current into the protected pin.

**Role of CM filtering on the power input:**
The AC-DC adapter that charges the rehab robot is the largest conducted-emission source. A **CM choke** (typically 1–10 mH) in series with the L/N lines, plus **Y2-class capacitors** (2.2 nF, 5 kV) from each line to chassis, presents a high impedance to common-mode noise currents and shunts them to chassis ground. The X-capacitor (100 nF) across L–N handles differential-mode noise. Combined, the input filter gives 30–60 dB of attenuation in the 150 kHz – 30 MHz range, ensuring compliance with CISPR 11.

Additional measures:
- **Galvanic isolation** (medical-grade, 4 kV withstand) between the AC mains and the patient-coupled circuits to limit leakage current.
- **Reinforced insulation** on the patient-applied parts (per IEC 60601-1) to withstand defibrillator pulses if used near an AED.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often cite "FCC Part 15" instead of **CISPR 11 / EN 55011** for medical devices. The correct standard for **rehabilitation robots in clinical use is CISPR 11 Class B**, and immunity is per IEC 60601-1-2. Mixing up the standard will cost at least 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

### Integrated Controllers
- ☐ **Definition:** unified real-time framework combining trajectory planner + servo loops + bio-signals + safety.
- ☐ **Three tiers:** Supervisory (100 ms), Coordination (1–10 ms), Servo (50–250 µs).
- ☐ **Communication buses:** EtherCAT (deterministic, <100 µs jitter), CAN-FD, RS-485, SPI, Ethernet.
- ☐ **RTOS requirement:** schedulability bound $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; Liu–Layland.
- ☐ **Hierarchical architecture** is the *default* answer for multi-DOF rehab exoskeletons (Lokomat, Armeo, Ekso).
- ☐ **Distributed vs Centralized:** distributed wins on EMC (short cables, modular shields) and fault isolation.

### Electromagnetic Compatibility
- ☐ **EMC** = low emission + high immunity; governed by **CISPR 11** (emission) and **IEC 60601-1-2** (immunity for medical).
- ☐ **SE formula:** $SE = R + A + B$ (all in dB).
- ☐ **Absorption loss:** $A_{dB} = 8.686 \, t / \delta$ with $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$.
- ☐ **Reflection loss:** $R_{dB} = 168 + 10 \log_{10}(\sigma_r/(\mu_r f^3 r^2))$ (approximate).
- ☐ **Slot rule:** maximum aperture $d_{slot} < \lambda/20$ of the highest threat frequency.
- ☐ **CM vs DM noise:** X-cap for DM, Y-cap + CM choke for CM.
- ☐ **Choke self-resonance:** $f_{SR} = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{L C_p})$ — must be *above* the interference band.
- ☐ **Grounding:** single-point for low-f analog, multi-point for HF digital; hybrid for mixed-signal.
- ☐ **ESD HBM:** 100 pF + 1.5 kΩ, ~150 ns time constant; protect with TVS + series resistor.
- ☐ **Cable management:** shielded twisted-pair, separate trays, 90° crossings, ferrite clamps, 360° backshells.

### Common Coupling Sources in Rehab Robots
- ☐ PWM motor drives → conducted CM noise + radiated E-field from cables.
- ☐ SMPS / DC-DC converters → high-frequency harmonics on power rails.
- ☐ Wireless modules (Wi-Fi / Bluetooth) → intentional radiators that may couple into bio-amplifiers.
- ☐ ESD from patient / therapist contact → high-voltage transient on I/O pins.

### High-Yield Exam Vocabulary (Use These Words!)
- *deterministic*, *bounded latency*, *hierarchical*, *RTOS*, *EtherCAT*, *haptic loop*, *impedance / admittance control*
- *CISPR 11 Class B*, *IEC 60601-1-2*, *Faraday enclosure*, *skin depth*, *common-mode choke*, *Y-capacitor*, *TVS diode*, *slot antenna rule*

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning:** In a 14-mark question, *always* draw a **neat block diagram** (Mermaid-style sketch is acceptable; box-and-arrow on paper is better). Students who skip the diagram are capped at ~10/14 even if the prose is perfect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Batteries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Battery (Electrochemical Cell):** A battery is a transducer that converts **stored chemical energy** into **electrical energy** through controlled redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions, providing a portable, self-contained DC power source for robotic rehabilitation devices such as exoskeletons, powered wheelchairs, prosthetic limbs, and therapeutic orthoses.

In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, the battery is not a peripheral component — it is a **mission-critical subsystem** that directly determines:
- The **mobility** of the patient
- The **therapy session duration**
- The **weight** carried by the user
- The **safety** in clinical/home environments

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus places batteries under *Module 3: Power Systems & Actuators for Rehabilitation Robots*, highlighting their role as the **primary energy source** for untethered (wearable) rehabilitation platforms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Lunchbox"

Imagine you are a marathon runner. You cannot run while being plugged into a wall socket — you need a **packed lunch (energy reserve)** that you carry with you. 

- A **battery** is exactly that "lunchbox" for a rehabilitation robot.
- The **sandwich** inside is the **chemical reactants** (active materials).
- As the robot "eats" (discharges), the chemical reactants are converted into electrical current that powers the motors, sensors, and controllers.
