Robotic systems are uniquely suited to deliver the Three Rs consistently, making them a clinical multiplier rather than a replacement. ## 1.5 Key Standardized Clinical Metrics (Bold Constants for the KTU Exam) These metrics will appear repeatedly in the module — memorize their names and ranges: - **Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA)** — upper extremity maximum score: **66 points**; lower extremity maximum: **34 points**. Higher = better motor recovery. - **Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS)** — measures spasticity on a **0 to 4** ordinal scale (**0 = no resistance**, **4 = rigid joint**). - **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** — total score ranges from **18 (total dependence) to 126 (complete independence)**. - **Range of Motion (ROM)** — measured in **degrees (°)**, full elbow flexion ≈ **150°**, full knee flexion ≈ **135°**. - **Barthel Index** — **0 to 100** scale of activities of daily living. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Rehabilitation Continuum — Where Robots Fit A patient does not enter and leave rehabilitation in a single stage. Clinical practice divides the journey into distinct **phases**, and different robotic technologies serve different phases. Understanding this continuum is essential for KTU long-answer questions. ### Phase 1 — Acute / Subacute (Days to Weeks Post-Injury) - **Patient state:** Flaccid or severely weakened; minimal voluntary movement; often bed-bound. - **Clinical goal:** Prevent contractures, stimulate neural pathways, begin passive mobilization. - **Robot type deployed:** **Continuous Passive Motion (CPM)** devices, early end-effector exoskeletons (e.g., Armeo® Power). - **Mechanism:** Robot moves the limb on a pre-programmed trajectory; patient is mostly passive. ### Phase 2 — Subacute to Early Chronic (Weeks to Months) - **Patient state:** Some voluntary movement returning; spasticity may be present. - **Clinical goal:** Re-educate motor patterns, increase strength, restore basic ADLs. - **Robot type deployed:** **Assist-as-needed (AAN)** controllers, bilateral training devices (e.g., Bi-Manu-Track). - **Mechanism:** Robot provides force only when the patient cannot complete the movement. ### Phase 3 — Chronic / Community Reintegration (Months to Years) - **Patient state:** Residual deficits; some functional independence. - **Clinical goal:** Restore complex, coordinated, real-world tasks. - **Robot type deployed:** **Task-specific exoskeletons**, gait trainers (e.g., Lokomat®, Ekso GT®), home-use devices. - **Mechanism:** High-level task practice with progressively less robotic assistance. ## 2.2 Target Patient Populations for Robotic Rehabilitation The KTU syllabus emphasizes four primary diagnostic groups. Each presents a distinct mechanical and neurological challenge: ### A. Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident — CVA) - **Prevalence:** Affects **~15 million people per year globally** (WHO statistic). - **Deficit pattern:** **Contralateral hemiparesis** — one side of the body is weak, opposite the brain lesion. - **Recovery window:** Most rapid neuroplastic change occurs in the **first 3–6 months**. - **Why robots help:** Deliver high-dose repetitive training within this narrow recovery window. ### B. Spinal Cord Injury (SCI) - **Classification:** **ASIA A, B, C, D, E** — A = complete (no motor/sensory below lesion), E = normal. - **Deficit pattern:** Loss of motor and/or sensory function below the level of injury. - **Why robots help:** Enable upright stepping in patients who cannot stand, providing weight-bearing and rhythmic sensory input to the spinal central pattern generators. ### C. Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) - **Deficit pattern:** Mixed motor, cognitive, and behavioral impairment. - **Why robots help:** The physical component can be trained with robots while cognitive load is gradually increased. ### D. Progressive Neurological Diseases - **Examples:** Multiple Sclerosis (MS), Parkinson's Disease (PD), Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). - **Why robots help:** Compensate for progressive loss of strength, maintain ROM, delay secondary complications. > [!NOTE] > In KTU answers, always **name the specific patient population** when discussing a robot — saying "rehabilitation robot" without context is too vague for full marks. ## 2.3 Classification of Rehabilitation Robots by Mechanical Architecture This classification table is the most exam-relevant taxonomy in the module: | Architecture | Description | Example Device | Body Region | |---|---|---|---| | **End-Effector** | Robot holds the patient's hand/foot at the distal segment; proximal joints move freely | InMotion ARM® | Upper limb | | **Exoskeleton** | Robot's joints are aligned one-to-one with the patient's anatomical joints | Armeo® Power, Lokomat® | Upper or lower limb | | **Wearable / Powered Orthosis** | Lightweight, joint-mounted actuators worn during daily activity | ReWalk®, Ekso GT® | Lower limb (gait) | | **Treadmill-Based Gait Trainer** | Combination of bodyweight support and robotic leg guidance | Lokomat® | Lower limb | | **Cable-Driven / Planar** | Cables or a 2-D plane guide the limb in a workspace | MARIONET, MIT-Manus | Upper limb | | **Soft / Pneumatic** | Compliant fabric-based actuators | Pneu-Wrex | Hand, ankle | ## 2.4 The Rehabilitation Team — A Multi-Disciplinary Reality Robotic rehabilitation is **never** a standalone intervention. The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to recognize the team structure: - **Physiatrist** (Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation doctor) — medical lead, prescribes therapy. - **Physical Therapist (PT)** — designs and supervises movement-based therapy. - **Occupational Therapist (OT)** — focuses on activities of daily living (ADLs). - **Rehabilitation Engineer** — sets up, calibrates, and troubleshoots the robot. - **Speech-Language Pathologist** — addresses communication/swallowing (not directly robot-related in this course). - **Patient and Family** — the central stakeholders whose goals drive the plan. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table) | Concept | Key Value / Range | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | FMA-UE max | **66** | Universal motor recovery benchmark | | FMA-LE max | **34** | Lower limb recovery benchmark | | MAS scale | **0–4** | Spasticity measure (0 = normal tone) | | FIM total | **18–126** | Functional independence measure | | Stroke recovery window | **3–6 months** | Peak neuroplastic period | | ASIA impairment scale | **A–E** | SCI classification | | ICF domains | **Body, Activity, Participation** | WHO framework | | Three Rs | **Repetition, Real-time feedback, Rehabilitation intensity** | Justification for robots | | Robot architecture types | **6 main types** (see Section 2.3) | Classification question | | Standard elbow flexion ROM | **≈ 150°** | Anatomical reference | | Standard knee flexion ROM | **≈ 135°** | Anatomical reference | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently award a 2-mark "list the benefits of robotic rehabilitation" sub-question. The **Three Rs** is the cleanest memorized answer. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples Because the topic is **introductory**, the analytical content here is not about deriving differential equations (those belong to Module 3). Instead, we work through the **clinical decision logic** and **quantitative scoring systems** that a robotic rehabilitation engineer must apply on the floor. Showing this reasoning explicitly will earn full marks on a KTU 14-mark question. ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Classifying a Patient for Robotic Therapy A 58-year-old male, 6 weeks post-stroke, presents with right-sided weakness. He can initiate shoulder flexion to about 30° against gravity but cannot extend his elbow. Modified Ashworth Scale is 1+ at the elbow. FMA-UE score is 22/66. **Step 1 — Identify the impairment phase.** Six weeks post-stroke places the patient in the **subacute / early chronic** phase (Phase 2 in Section 2.1). The FMA score of 22/66 indicates **moderate-severe impairment** (a score below 30 is generally considered severe). **Step 2 — Identify the dominant ICF domain.** The patient has impairment (cannot extend elbow) and activity limitation (cannot reach/grasp). Participation is affected but the immediate goal is at the body/activity level. **Step 3 — Quantify the spasticity.** MAS = 1+ means a **slight increase in muscle tone**, with a catch and release, or minimal resistance at the end of range. This is **below the threshold** (MAS ≥ 2) that would contraindicate active robotic therapy. **Step 4 — Quantify the voluntary movement.** Active shoulder flexion = 30° (against gravity) means the patient has **partial volitional control** — sufficient to engage with an **Assist-as-Needed (AAN)** controller. **Step 5 — Select the robot class.** From the Section 2.3 table, an **end-effector or exoskeleton upper-limb device** is appropriate. The Lokomat (gait) is not applicable. The MIT-Manus-style planar robot would suit shoulder/elbow planar training. **Conclusion (model answer sentence for KTU):** *This 58-year-old subacute stroke patient with moderate-severe upper limb impairment (FMA-UE 22/66), minimal spasticity (MAS 1+), and partial volitional shoulder movement is an appropriate candidate for an assist-as-needed upper limb exoskeleton or planar end-effector robot to deliver high-dose repetitive task-specific training during the peak neuroplastic window.* ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Calculating Repetition Dose A therapeutic principle often quoted in KTU answers: **"High-dose practice drives recovery."** A clinician wants to know how many robot-assisted repetitions match a typical manual therapy session. **Given information:** - Manual therapy session length: **45 minutes**. - Manual therapist achieves approximately **1 active-assisted repetition every 30 seconds** during a focused upper limb session (a standard clinical observation cited in rehabilitation literature). - Robotic session length: **45 minutes**. - Robotic device can deliver **1 guided repetition every 6 seconds** (typical for an end-effector device). **Step 1 — Calculate manual repetitions per session.** $$ N_{\text{manual}} \;=\; \frac{45 \,\text{min} \times 60 \,\text{s/min}}{30 \,\text{s/rep}} \;=\; 90 \,\text{repetitions} $$ The unit conversion is minutes × (seconds per minute) divided by (seconds per repetition), yielding repetitions. **Step 2 — Calculate robotic repetitions per session.** $$ N_{\text{robot}} \;=\; \frac{45 \,\text{min} \times 60 \,\text{s/min}}{6 \,\text{s/rep}} \;=\; 450 \,\text{repetitions} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the dose multiplier.** $$ \text{Multiplier} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{robot}}}{N_{\text{manual}}} \;=\; \frac{450}{90} \;=\; 5 $$ **Interpretation (model answer sentence for KTU):** *The robotic device delivers a 5-fold increase in the number of task-specific repetitions within the same 45-minute session, directly supporting the "Three Rs" rationale and increasing the therapy dose within the critical subacute recovery window.* ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Reading an FMA Score and Planning Progression The Fugl-Meyer Assessment is a 3-point ordinal scale per item: **0 = cannot perform**, **1 = performs partially**, **2 = performs fully**. The total FMA-UE is the sum of 33 items × 2 = **66**. **Given:** A patient scores 12/66 on FMA-UE at week 2 post-stroke. After 4 weeks of robot-assisted therapy, the score rises to 28/66. **Step 1 — Compute absolute change.** $$ \Delta \text{FMA} \;=\; 28 \;-\; 12 \;=\; 16 \,\text{points} $$ **Step 2 — Compute percentage of maximum possible recovery used.** $$ \% \text{ FMA improvement} \;=\; \frac{16}{66} \times 100 \;\approx\; 24.2 \,\% $$ **Step 3 — Compute percentage of remaining deficit recovered.** The deficit at week 2 was 66 − 12 = 54 points. The new deficit is 66 − 28 = 38 points. The reduction in deficit is 54 − 38 = 16 points. $$ \% \text{ deficit recovered} \;=\; \frac{16}{54} \times 100 \;\approx\; 29.6 \,\% $$ **Step 4 — Classify the recovery stage.** A score of 28/66 still falls in the **severe impairment** band, justifying continued robot-assisted therapy with a focus on active movement components (the proximal arm, then the wrist/hand). **Conclusion (model answer sentence for KTU):** *The patient gained 16 FMA-UE points (≈ 24% of maximum score, ≈ 30% of remaining deficit recovered) over 4 weeks of robot-assisted therapy, indicating a clinically meaningful improvement and supporting continued high-dose task-specific training.* ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Robot Class When a KTU question asks "Which type of robot would you use for X?", use this 4-step decision matrix: | Decision Step | Question to Ask | Possible Answers | |---|---|---| | **1. Body region** | Upper limb or lower limb? | Determines arm vs leg device family | | **2. Patient phase** | Acute, subacute, or chronic? | Determines passive vs active-assist controller | | **3. Volitional control** | Can the patient initiate movement? | Yes → AAN; No → CPM or triggered assistance | | **4. Functional goal** | Reach/grasp, gait, or ADL? | Determines task-specific protocol selection | ## 3.5 Summary of the Worked Examples | Example | Clinical Concept Tested | Key Numerical Result | |---|---|---| | 3.1 | Patient classification | FMA 22/66 → AAN upper-limb exoskeleton indicated | | 3.2 | Dose calculation | 5× more repetitions with robot in 45 min | | 3.3 | Outcome score interpretation | 16-point FMA gain = clinically meaningful | | 3.4 | Robot class selection | 4-step decision matrix | # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics This section visualizes the clinical ecosystem, the patient journey, and the engineering-clinical interaction in Mermaid diagrams that comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 mermaid safeguards. ## 4.1 The Clinical Robotic Rehabilitation Ecosystem ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PATIENT
with impairment] --> B[PHYSIA
PM R Doctor] B --> C[Therapy
Prescription] C --> D[PT and OT
Therapists] D --> E[Rehab
Engineer] E --> F[Robot
Device] F --> G[Therapy
Session] G --> A E --> H[Data
Logs] H --> I[Outcome
Scores] I --> B D --> J[Manual
Therapy] J --> A classDef patientNode fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef clinicalNode fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef engNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef dataNode fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 class A patientNode class B,C,D clinicalNode class E,F engNode class H,I,J dataNode ``` **How to read this diagram (model answer phrasing):** The patient is at the center of a multi-disciplinary loop. The physiatrist prescribes therapy, the therapists design the protocol, the rehabilitation engineer prepares the robot, the session generates performance data, and outcome scores loop back to inform the next prescription. The robot supplements — not replaces — manual therapy. ## 4.2 The Rehabilitation Continuum and Matching Robot Class ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[ACUTE
0 to 2 weeks
Flaccid phase] --> P2[SUBACUTE
2 to 12 weeks
Motor return begins] P2 --> P3[EARLY CHRONIC
3 to 6 months
Peak neuroplasticity] P3 --> P4[CHRONIC
6 months plus
Residual deficit] P1 --> R1[CPM Device
Passive motion] P2 --> R2[End Effector Robot
AAN controller] P3 --> R3[Exoskeleton
Task specific] P4 --> R4[Wearable Orthosis
Community use] R1 -.->|Drives| P1 R2 -.->|Drives| P2 R3 -.->|Drives| P3 R4 -.->|Drives| P4 classDef phaseNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef robotNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 class P1,P2,P3,P4 phaseNode class R1,R2,R3,R4 robotNode ``` **Reading guide:** As the patient progresses along the horizontal phase axis, the appropriate robot class changes from passive (CPM) to active-assist (AAN) to task-specific (exoskeleton) to community-wearable (powered orthosis). This is a KTU-favorite concept question. ## 4.3 The Three Rs Justification Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NEUROPLASTICITY
Driver of motor recovery] --> B[REPETITION
Hundreds to thousands per session] A --> C[REAL TIME FEEDBACK
Visual auditory haptic] A --> D[REHABILITATION INTENSITY
High dose per unit time] B --> E[ROBOT DELIVERS
Consistent precise motion] C --> F[ROBOT DELIVERS
Sensor based feedback] D --> G[ROBOT DELIVERS
Sustained dose without fatigue] E --> H[CLINICAL OUTCOME
Improved FMA and FIM] F --> H G --> H classDef baseNode fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 classDef pillarNode fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef actionNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef outNode fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 class A baseNode class B,C,D pillarNode class E,F,G actionNode class H outNode ``` **Reading guide:** Neuroplasticity is the **base of the pyramid** — the underlying physiological phenomenon the therapy must exploit. The three Rs (repetition, real-time feedback, rehabilitation intensity) are the **three pillars** that exploit it. The robot's engineering capabilities (consistent motion, sensor feedback, sustained dose) **act on the pillars**, and the result is the **clinical outcome** (improved FMA, improved FIM, improved ROM). ## 4.4 Patient Flow Through a Robotic Rehabilitation Program ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Screening
Diagnosis and impairment profile] --> S2[Assessment
FMA MAS FIM ROM] S2 --> S3[Goal Setting
ICF based SMART goals] S3 --> S4[Robot Selection
Class and protocol] S4 --> S5[Therapy Sessions
3 to 5 times per week] S5 --> S6[Reassessment
Every 2 to 4 weeks] S6 --> S7{Progress
against goals?} S7 -->|Yes| S8[Upgrade Protocol
Increase difficulty] S8 --> S5 S7 -->|No| S9[Re-evaluate
Adjust robot or strategy] S9 --> S4 S6 --> S10[Discharge
when goals met] classDef stepNode fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000 classDef decNode fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000 classDef exitNode fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S8,S9 stepNode class S7 decNode class S10 exitNode ``` **Reading guide:** This is a closed-loop clinical workflow. After every 2–4 weeks the patient is reassessed, goals are compared to actual progress, and the protocol is either upgraded (advance the difficulty) or revised (re-evaluate robot class). KTU loves this type of flowchart in 14-mark answers because it shows the iterative nature of rehabilitation. # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Q1]** > **Q1.** Define the term *robotic rehabilitation* as applied in a clinical setting. List any four target patient populations. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Robotic rehabilitation is the use of sensor-equipped, computer-controlled mechatronic devices to deliver repetitive, measurable, task-specific physical therapy to patients recovering from neurological or musculoskeletal impairment, with outcomes measured against standardized clinical scales such as FMA, FIM, and MAS. *Four target patient populations:* (i) stroke (CVA), (ii) spinal cord injury (SCI), (iii) traumatic brain injury (TBI), (iv) progressive neurological diseases such as multiple sclerosis, Parkinson's disease, or ALS. *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 0.5 mark per population, max 2 marks for the four populations.)* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Q2]** > **Q2.** State the *Three Rs* of motor recovery and explain how a rehabilitation robot satisfies each one. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Three Rs are **Repetition**, **Real-time feedback**, and **Rehabilitation intensity** (1 mark for the list). A robot satisfies *Repetition* by executing hundreds of guided movements per session (1 mark). It satisfies *Real-time feedback* by using position, force, and EMG sensors to display motion and effort to the patient and therapist (0.5 mark). It satisfies *Rehabilitation intensity* by sustaining the dose over the full session without therapist fatigue, multiplying the manual dose (0.5 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 4, Q7]** > > ### Question A (14 marks) > > **(a)** Explain, with a neat flowchart, the clinical workflow of a robotic rehabilitation program from patient screening to discharge. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Classify rehabilitation robots by mechanical architecture. Give one clinical example for each class and the body region it serves. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks** The clinical workflow proceeds in seven sequential, iterative stages: 1. **Screening** (1 mark): Confirm the diagnosis (e.g., stroke, SCI), confirm no contraindications (severe spasticity MAS ≥ 3, severe cognitive impairment, fixed contractures), and confirm the patient is medically stable. 2. **Assessment** (1 mark): Apply standardized scales — FMA for motor impairment, MAS for spasticity, FIM for functional independence, ROM measurement for joint range. Document baseline values. 3. **Goal Setting** (1 mark): Use the **ICF framework** to set SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) goals at the impairment, activity, and participation levels. 4. **Robot Selection** (1 mark): Choose a device class (end-effector, exoskeleton, etc.) and protocol based on the decision matrix in Section 3.4 (body region, phase, volitional control, functional goal). 5. **Therapy Sessions** (1 mark): Deliver 3–5 sessions per week, typically 30–60 minutes each, with robot-guided task-specific practice. 6. **Reassessment** (1 mark): Repeat FMA, MAS, FIM, ROM every 2–4 weeks and compare to baseline. 7. **Discharge or Protocol Adjustment** (1 mark): If goals are met → discharge with home program. If not → upgrade protocol (increase difficulty, reduce assistance) or re-evaluate robot choice. The workflow is **iterative**: stages 4–6 loop back as the patient improves. Refer to the patient-flow diagram in Section 4.4 for a visual representation. **Part (b) — 7 marks** Classification of rehabilitation robots by mechanical architecture (1 mark for the classification list, 1 mark per correct example, 1 mark for the body region mapping): | Class | Description (brief) | Example Device | Body Region | |---|---|---|---| | **End-Effector** | Distal segment held by robot, proximal free | InMotion ARM® | Upper limb | | **Exoskeleton** | Robot joints aligned 1:1 with anatomical joints | Armeo® Power, Lokomat® | Upper or lower limb | | **Wearable Powered Orthosis** | Joint-mounted actuators for over-ground use | ReWalk®, Ekso GT® | Lower limb gait | | **Treadmill-Based Gait Trainer** | BWS + robotic leg guidance on a treadmill | Lokomat® | Lower limb gait | | **Cable-Driven / Planar** | Cables or 2-D workspace guide the limb | MIT-Manus, MARIONET | Upper limb | | **Soft / Pneumatic** | Compliant fabric actuators | Pneu-Wrex | Hand / ankle | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > Students often confuse the **Lokomat** (treadmill-based gait trainer with exoskeletal legs) and the **ReWalk/Ekso** (wearable powered orthosis for over-ground walking). The Lokomat is a *tethered clinic device*; ReWalk/Ekso are *mobile community devices*. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks. Also, do not write "MIT-Manus is an exoskeleton" — it is a **planar end-effector** device, a common error. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 4, Q8]** > > ### Question B (14 marks) — *Alternative to Question A* > > **(a)** Discuss the *Three Rs* of motor recovery. With a labeled diagram, explain how a rehabilitation robot exploits each R to drive neuroplasticity. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 60-year-old patient is 4 weeks post-stroke with right upper limb weakness. FMA-UE score is 18/66, MAS at the elbow is 1+, and active shoulder flexion is 40° against gravity. Recommend the appropriate class of rehabilitation robot, justify your choice using the ICF framework and the patient-phase classification, and estimate the repetition dose advantage of a robotic session over a 45-minute manual session. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — 7 marks** The Three Rs are derived from the neuroscience of motor learning and neuroplasticity (1 mark): - **Repetition** (1 mark): Neuroplastic changes in the motor cortex require hundreds to thousands of task-specific movement attempts. Manual therapy delivers ~90 reps in 45 minutes; robots deliver ~450–900. - **Real-time Feedback** (1 mark): Visual, auditory, and haptic feedback during movement reinforces correct motor patterns and enables error-based learning. - **Rehabilitation Intensity** (1 mark): Higher therapy dose per unit time correlates with better functional outcomes; robots sustain the dose without therapist fatigue. How a robot exploits each R (3 marks — 1 per R, with diagram reference): - A typical end-effector robot (MIT-Manus) repeats planar reaching trajectories on a **pre-programmed or patient-triggered schedule** → satisfies Repetition. - Position, velocity, and force sensors feed a **display screen showing a target cursor and the patient's hand position** → satisfies Real-time Feedback. - The robot can run a **45-minute session delivering 5–10× more repetitions** than manual therapy → satisfies Rehabilitation Intensity. (Refer to the Three Rs pyramid in Section 4.3 for the labeled diagram — 1 mark is reserved for a clean, labeled pyramid or block diagram.) **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Patient classification (2 marks):** - Time post-stroke: 4 weeks → **subacute / early chronic phase** (within the 3–6 month peak neuroplastic window). - FMA-UE 18/66 → **severe upper limb impairment**. - MAS 1+ → **mild spasticity, not a contraindication** to active robotic therapy. - Active shoulder flexion 40° against gravity → **partial volitional control present**. **Step 2 — ICF-based goal setting (1 mark):** - **Impairment goal:** Increase active shoulder/elbow ROM and reduce motor deficit. - **Activity goal:** Restore reach-to-grasp for eating and dressing. - **Participation goal:** Return to independent self-care. **Step 3 — Robot recommendation (2 marks):** Given the partial volitional control, mild spasticity, and severe upper limb impairment, the appropriate device is an **end-effector or upper-limb exoskeleton with an Assist-as-Needed (AAN) controller** (e.g., InMotion ARM® or Armeo® Power). AAN is preferred over pure passive CPM because the patient can volitionally initiate movement, so effort should be encouraged and only the missing component should be robot-supplied. **Step 4 — Repetition dose advantage (2 marks):** Using the methodology of Section 3.2: $$ N_{\text{manual}} \;=\; \frac{45 \,\text{min} \times 60 \,\text{s/min}}{30 \,\text{s/rep}} \;=\; 90 \,\text{repetitions} $$ $$ N_{\text{robot}} \;=\; \frac{45 \,\text{min} \times 60 \,\text{s/min}}{6 \,\text{s/rep}} \;=\; 450 \,\text{repetitions} $$ $$ \text{Dose advantage} \;=\; \frac{450}{90} \;=\; 5\times $$ **Conclusion (model answer sentence):** *The patient is best served by an AAN-controlled end-effector or exoskeleton upper-limb robot delivering approximately 450 task-specific repetitions per 45-minute session — a 5-fold dose advantage over manual therapy — within the subacute peak neuroplastic window, with ICF-aligned goals spanning impairment, activity, and participation.* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > (i) Do not recommend a **CPM device** for this patient — CPM is passive and the patient has partial volitional control; using CPM would waste recovery potential. (ii) Do not recommend a **gait robot (Lokomat)** — the deficit is upper limb. (iii) Always show the **ICF alignment** explicitly; missing "Participation" loses 1 mark. (iv) In numerical answers, always include **units** in every step (s, min, reps) — a frequent 0.5-mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4, Introduction** - **Robotic rehabilitation definition:** Mechatronic, sensor-equipped devices delivering repetitive, measurable, task-specific therapy aligned to the **WHO ICF framework**. - **ICF three pillars:** Impairment → Activity → Participation. All clinical goal-setting must touch all three. - **Three Rs of motor recovery:** **Repetition**, **Real-time feedback**, **Rehabilitation intensity** — the standard justification for using robots. - **Patient populations (4):** Stroke (CVA), Spinal Cord Injury (SCI), Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI), Progressive neurological diseases (MS, PD, ALS). - **Stroke recovery window:** Peak neuroplasticity in the **first 3–6 months**. - **Rehabilitation phases:** Acute (CPM) → Subacute (AAN end-effector) → Early chronic (exoskeleton, task-specific) → Chronic (wearable orthosis, community use). - **Six robot classes by architecture:** End-effector, Exoskeleton, Wearable powered orthosis, Treadmill-based gait trainer, Cable-driven / planar, Soft / pneumatic. - **Standard scales (memorize ranges):** FMA-UE max **66**, FMA-LE max **34**, MAS **0–4**, FIM total **18–126**, Barthel Index **0–100**. - **Standard anatomical ROM references:** Elbow flexion ≈ **150°**, knee flexion ≈ **135°**. - **Rehabilitation team:** Physiatrist, PT, OT, Rehabilitation Engineer, Patient & Family. - **Typical dose advantage of a robot:** ~**5×** the repetitions of a manual session in the same time window. - **Workflow is iterative:** Screening → Assessment → Goal setting (ICF) → Robot selection → Sessions → Reassessment → (Upgrade or Re-evaluate) → Discharge. - **Common exam traps:** (i) Confusing Lokomat with ReWalk/Ekso; (ii) Calling MIT-Manus an exoskeleton; (iii) Recommending CPM for a patient with volitional control; (iv) Forgetting units in numerical answers; (v) Discussing the robot without naming the patient population.

topics.title: Components and Configurations of Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components and Configurations of Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Robot? (KTU 2024 Definition)

A **Robot** is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, robots are used to assist, augment, or replace the lost motor functions of patients suffering from neurological or musculoskeletal disorders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (ISO 8373:2021 Aligned):** An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications or therapeutic/rehabilitation interventions.

### 1.2 Core Components of a Rehabilitation Robot — The "Anatomy"

Just as the human body has a skeleton, muscles, nerves, and a brain, a robot has six fundamental subsystems that work in concert:

| # | Subsystem | Human Body Analogy | Rehabilitation Robot Example |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator)** | Skeleton & Joints | InMotion ARM (shoulder/elbow linkages) |
| 2 | **Actuators** | Muscles | DC servomotors, Pneumatic muscles (PAMs) |
| 3 | **End-Effector** | Hand / Tool | Gripper, Hand exoskeleton, Haptic stylus |
| 4 | **Sensors** | Nerves (Touch, Proprioception) | Force/torque sensors, EMG electrodes, Encoders |
| 5 | **Controller** | Brain & Spinal Cord | Industrial PC, FPGA-based real-time controller |
| 6 | **Power Supply & Drive Electronics** | Heart & Circulatory System | 48 V DC bus, Battery packs, Motor drivers |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Robot as a Patient's "External Limbs"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a stroke survivor trying to lift a cup of water. Their *intent* is generated in the brain, but the *muscle signal* cannot reach the arm reliably. A **rehabilitation robot** acts like a powered exoskeleton "second skin." The patient's brain sends a faint EMG signal (the *command*), the sensor detects it (the *nerve*), the controller interprets it (the *spinal cord*), the actuator produces the torque (the *muscle*), and the rigid linkage moves the cup (the *skeleton*). In this sense, the robot is not replacing the patient — it is **augmenting** the patient's lost biomechanical function.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following standardized metrics are routinely asked in KTU exams:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** The number of independent joint motions, denoted as **n**.
- **Payload Capacity:** Maximum mass the robot can carry, typically **2 kg to 150 kg** for rehabilitation systems.
- **Reach (Working Envelope):** Maximum distance from the robot's base to the end-effector, in **mm or m**.
- **Repeatability:** The ability to return to a programmed position, typically **± 0.05 mm** for medical-grade robots.
- **Positional Accuracy:** The deviation between commanded and actual position, often **± 0.1 mm**.
- **Stiffness:** Resistance to deflection under load, measured in **N/m**, critical for safety in patient interaction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Subsystems — Analytical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Mechanical Structure (Manipulator)
- Composed of **rigid links** connected by **joints**.
- Links: usually aluminum alloy, carbon-fiber composite, or stainless steel (for MRI compatibility).
- Joints classified as:
  - **Revolute (R):** Rotary motion about an axis (e.g., shoulder flexion).
  - **Prismatic (P):** Linear sliding motion along an axis (e.g., forearm extension).
  - **Cylindrical (C):** Combined rotation and translation.
  - **Spherical (S):** Two rotations with a common center.
  - **Helical:** Rotation plus translation along the same axis.

#### 2.1.2 Actuators — The "Muscles"
| Type | Principle | Used In |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Electric (DC/Brushless/Stepper)** | Electromagnetic torque | Lokomat, InMotion ARM |
| **Pneumatic (PAM, McKibben)** | Compressed air in rubber bladder | BLEEX exoskeleton, soft robots |
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized fluid | Heavy exoskeletons (e.g., Ekso GT) |
| **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Motor + spring in series | MIT Anklebot, compliant rehab |

#### 2.1.3 End-Effectors
- **Grippers** (2-finger, 3-finger adaptive), **Haptic interfaces**, **Therapeutic cuffs**, **Wrist-hand orthoses**.

#### 2.1.4 Sensors (Three Functional Families)
1. **Proprioceptive Sensors:** Encoders, resolvers, IMUs, potentiometers — measure *internal* state.
2. **Exteroceptive Sensors:** Cameras, depth sensors (Kinect, Intel RealSense), LiDAR — measure *environment*.
3. **Physiological Sensors (HMI):** EMG, EEG, ECG, fNIRS — measure *patient intent*.

#### 2.1.5 Controller
- **Open-loop:** No feedback (rare in rehab; unsafe for patient contact).
- **Closed-loop (PID, Impedance, Admittance):** Uses feedback to minimize error.
- **Architecture:** Centralized vs. Distributed (CAN bus, EtherCAT).

#### 2.1.6 Power & Drive Electronics
- Transformerless DC-DC converters, motor drivers (PWM at **20 kHz** carrier), regenerative braking.

### 2.2 Robot Configurations (The Core of This Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Examiners frequently ask to "compare the workspace and joint arrangement of any THREE configurations" or "draw and explain the SCARA configuration."

A robot's **configuration** is defined by the **sequence of joint types** connecting the base to the end-effector. This sequence is called the **Kinematic Chain Notation** (e.g., RRP, RRR, PPP).

#### (A) Cartesian (Gantry) — PPP
- All three joints are **prismatic**, mutually perpendicular.
- **DoF = 3**, plus optional wrist.
- **Workspace:** Rectangular box.
- **Application in Rehab:** Large workspace gait trainers (e.g., LokoHelp, G-EO).

#### (B) Cylindrical — RPP
- One revolute (base rotation) + two prismatic (vertical & radial).
- **Workspace:** Hollow cylinder.
- **Application:** Limited in rehab; mostly used for patient transfer systems.

#### (C) Spherical (Polar) — RRP
- One revolute (base) + one revolute (shoulder) + one prismatic (extension).
- **Workspace:** Portion of a sphere.
- **Application:** Early prosthetics; rarely used in modern rehab.

#### (D) Articulated (Anthropomorphic / Revolute) — RRR (+ Spherical Wrist)
- All three primary joints are **revolute**, mimicking the human shoulder/elbow.
- **DoF = 3 to 6** (with wrist).
- **Workspace:** Spherical shell (complex).
- **Application in Rehab:** **InMotion ARM**, **Hocoma Armeo Power**, **MIT Manus** — the dominant configuration for upper-limb therapy.

#### (E) SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) — RRP
- Two parallel revolute joints + one prismatic vertical.
- **Workspace:** Horizontal disc, vertically rigid.
- **Application:** Horizontal-plane pick-and-place; tablet-based rehab drawing tasks.

#### (F) Delta Robot
- Three revolute joints at the base connected via parallelogram linkages to a triangular end-effector.
- **Workspace:** Truncated octahedron.
- **Application:** High-speed light payload; used in some haptic rehab devices.

#### (G) Parallel Manipulators (Stewart Platform)
- **6 DoF**, six linear actuators connecting a fixed base to a moving platform.
- **Application in Rehab:** **Motion base** for balance and vestibular training, post-stroke posture rehabilitation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** For the joint table, `\vert` is used instead of the pipe symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics (Position) | $^0T_n \;=\; ^0T_1 \cdot ^1T_2 \cdots ^{n-1}T_n$ | $^iT_{i+1}$ is the $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transform |
| 2 | DH Parameter Transform | $^{i-1}T_i \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i$ |
| 3 | Workspace Volume — Cartesian | $V_{\text{rect}} \;=\; X_{\max} \cdot Y_{\max} \cdot Z_{\max}$ | Each in **m**, $V$ in **m³** |
| 4 | Workspace Volume — Cylindrical | $V_{\text{cyl}} \;=\; \pi (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2) \cdot h$ | $r$ radial, $h$ vertical, **m³** |
| 5 | Workspace Volume — Spherical Shell | $V_{\text{shell}} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3)$ | $r$ in **m**, $V$ in **m³** |
| 6 | Motor Torque Required | $\tau_i \;=\; \sum_{j=i}^{n} \left[ m_j \, g \, \dfrac{\partial z_j}{\partial q_i} \right]$ | $\tau_i$ in **N·m**, $g = 9.81 \;\mathrm{m/s^2}$ |
| 7 | Gravity-Compensation Torque | $\tau_g(q) \;=\; G(q)$ | Static gravity vector, **N·m** |
| 8 | Number of Joints vs DoF | $n_{\text{DoF}} \;=\; n_{\text{rigid}} - 6 \;=\; \sum f_i - 6$ | For spatial mechanism, $f_i$ link DoF |
| 9 | Static Payload Torque (Single Link) | $\tau \;=\; m \, g \, L \, \cos\theta$ | $L$ in **m**, $\theta$ angle in **rad** |
| 10 | Impedance Controller Law | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx \;=\; F_{\text{ext}}$ | $M$ inertia, $B$ damping, $K$ stiffness, all in **SI** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manipulator design** determines the reachable **dexterous workspace** — critical for upper-limb therapy where the patient must reach a wide envelope.
- **Configuration selection** directly governs **inverse-kinematic complexity**: Cartesian has 1 solution, articulated can have **up to 8 solutions** for a 6-DoF arm.
- **Rehabilitation-specific constraint:** All joint limits, velocities, and torques must satisfy **ISO 13482** and **ISO 14971** safety standards for medical robots.
- **Clinical relevance:** A **parallel (Stewart) platform** provides a high natural stiffness — ideal for high-precision balance rehabilitation under perturbation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Workspace Volume of a Spherical (Polar) Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **high-yield KTU problem**. Examiner pattern: *"A spherical robot has a radial reach from 0.5 m to 1.5 m and a base rotation of 270°. Calculate the reachable workspace volume."*

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric envelope**

A polar robot's reach, denoted $r$, varies from $r_{\min}$ to $r_{\max}$, while the base rotation $\phi$ varies over $\phi_{\max}$.

**Step 2 — Express the differential volume element in spherical coordinates**

$$
dV \;=\; r^{2} \sin\theta \; dr \; d\theta \; d\phi
$$

Here $\theta$ is the elevation angle from the base axis (polar angle), $\phi$ is the azimuth (base rotation), and $r$ is the radial extension.

**Step 3 — Set integration limits**

$$
r \in [r_{\min},\; r_{\max}] \quad
\theta \in [0,\; \pi/2] \quad
\phi \in [0,\; \phi_{\max}]
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate with respect to $r$**

$$
\int_{r_{\min}}^{r_{\max}} r^{2}\, dr
\;=\; \left[ \dfrac{r^{3}}{3} \right]_{r_{\min}}^{r_{\max}}
\;=\; \dfrac{r_{\max}^{3} - r_{\min}^{3}}{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate with respect to $\theta$**

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \sin\theta\, d\theta
\;=\; \left[ -\cos\theta \right]_{0}^{\pi/2}
\;=\; 1
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate with respect to $\phi$**

$$
\int_{0}^{\phi_{\max}} d\phi \;=\; \phi_{\max}
$$

**Step 7 — Combine all three integrals**

$$
V_{\text{spherical}}
\;=\; \dfrac{\phi_{\max}}{3} \left( r_{\max}^{3} - r_{\min}^{3} \right)
$$

For a **full** spherical shell ($\phi_{\max} = 2\pi$):

$$
V_{\text{full}}
\;=\; \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \left( r_{\max}^{3} - r_{\min}^{3} \right)
$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Example (KTU style)**

Given $r_{\min} = 0.5$ m, $r_{\max} = 1.5$ m, $\phi_{\max} = 270° = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ rad:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{spherical}}
&= \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{3\pi}{2} \cdot \left( 1.5^{3} - 0.5^{3} \right) \\
&= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot (3.375 - 0.125) \\
&= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot 3.250 \\
&\approx 5.105 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Workspace volume $\approx \mathbf{5.11 \; m^3}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Static Torque on a Single Link Holding a Payload

> **Physical scenario:** A horizontal upper-limb rehab robot extends a 0.4 m forearm link with a 2 kg cuff mass. The link makes $30°$ with the horizontal. Calculate the static torque at the shoulder joint.

**Step 1 — Free-body diagram**

The weight $W = m \cdot g$ acts at the center of mass, distance $L$ from the pivot.

**Step 2 — Compute the gravitational force**

$$
W \;=\; m \cdot g \;=\; 2 \cdot 9.81 \;=\; 19.62 \;\mathrm{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the moment arm**

The perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of $W$ is $L \cos\theta$ when $\theta$ is measured from horizontal.

$$
\text{Moment arm} \;=\; L \cos\theta \;=\; 0.4 \cdot \cos 30^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the torque**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau
&= W \cdot L \cos\theta \\
&= m g L \cos\theta \\
&= 2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.4 \cdot \cos 30^{\circ} \\
&= 2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.8660 \\
&\approx 6.795 \;\mathrm{N \cdot m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Shoulder must supply $\tau \approx \mathbf{6.80 \;\mathrm{N \cdot m}}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — DH Transform for the First Two Joints of an Articulated Rehab Arm

**Given:** Joint 1 — Revolute about $z_0$ with $d_1 = h$ vertical offset. Joint 2 — Revolute about $z_1$ with link length $a_2$.

**Step 1 — Build $^{0}T_{1}$**

For a pure revolute with vertical offset and no twist:
$\theta_1$ variable, $d_1 = h$, $a_1 = 0$, $\alpha_1 = 90°$.

$$
^{0}T_{1}
\;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta_1 & 0 & s\theta_1 & 0 \\
s\theta_1 & 0 & -c\theta_1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & h \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Build $^{1}T_{2}$**

For revolute with horizontal link:
$\theta_2$ variable, $d_2 = 0$, $a_2 = L$, $\alpha_2 = 0$.

$$
^{1}T_{2}
\;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta_2 & -s\theta_2 & 0 & L\,c\theta_2 \\
s\theta_2 & c\theta_2 & 0 & L\,s\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compose $^{0}T_{2}$**

$$
^{0}T_{2} \;=\; ^{0}T_{1} \cdot ^{1}T_{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply and read out the position vector**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= c\theta_1 \, L\,c\theta_2 + s\theta_1 \cdot 0 = L \, c\theta_1 \, c\theta_2 \\
y_2 &= s\theta_1 \, L\,c\theta_2 - c\theta_1 \cdot 0 = L \, s\theta_1 \, c\theta_2 \\
z_2 &= h + 0 = h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to radial coordinates**

$$
r = \sqrt{x_2^2 + y_2^2} \;=\; L \, \vert \cos\theta_2 \vert
$$

This result **matches the spherical configuration's radial reach formula**, confirming internal consistency between DH and direct geometry.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Configuration Comparison (For Exam Answers)

| Criterion | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) | SCARA (RRP) | Parallel (Stewart) |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|--------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Kinematic Notation** | PPP | RPP | RRP | RRR (+ spherical wrist) | RRP | 6 × P with universal joints |
| **DoF (typical)** | 3 | 3 | 3 | 6 | 4 | 6 |
| **Workspace Shape** | Rectangular | Hollow cylinder | Spherical shell | Irregular sphere | Flat disc | Hexagonal column |
| **Reach Formula** | $V = XYZ$ | $V = \pi (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2) h$ | $V = \tfrac{4\pi}{3}(r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3)$ | Numerical IK only | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $V \approx 6 L^3$ |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | Trivial (decoupled) | Easy | Moderate | Hard, multiple solutions | Easy | Hard, non-unique |
| **Stiffness** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate | High (vertical) | Very High |
| **Rehab Example** | LokoHelp gait trainer | Patient lift | Early prosthetics | Armeo Power, InMotion | Tablet drawing rehab | Balance/vestibular trainer |
| **Patient Safety** | Good (low inertia) | Moderate | Moderate | Requires impedance control | Good | Excellent (intrinsic stiffness) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Rehabilitation Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Intent Source] --> A1[EEG or EMG Electrodes]
    A1 --> B[Physiological Signal Acquisition]
    B --> C[Controller - DSP or FPGA]
    C --> C1[Impedance or Admittance Algorithm]
    C1 --> D[Motor Drive Power Stage]
    D --> D1[PWM at 20 kHz]
    D1 --> E[Actuator - Servomotor or PAM]
    E --> F[Mechanical Linkage - Manipulator]
    F --> G[End Effector - Hand or Exoskeleton]
    G --> H1[Force Torque Sensor]
    H1 --> C
    G --> H2[Position Encoder]
    H2 --> C
    C --> I[Haptic Feedback to Patient]
    C --> J[Therapist GUI Display]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Articulated Robot (RRR Configuration) with Wrist

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BaseAssembly
        B[Base] --> J1[Revolute Joint 1 - Waist]
    end
    J1 --> L1[Link 1 - Upper Arm]
    L1 --> J2[Revolute Joint 2 - Shoulder]
    J2 --> L2[Link 2 - Forearm]
    L2 --> J3[Revolute Joint 3 - Elbow]
    J3 --> L3[Link 3 - Wrist Linkage]
    L3 --> J4[Revolute Joint 4]
    J4 --> J5[Revolute Joint 5]
    J5 --> J6[Revolute Joint 6]
    J6 --> E[End Effector - Rehab Handle]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Workspace Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WorkspaceShapes
        W1[Cartesian - Rectangular Box] 
        W2[Cylindrical - Hollow Cylinder]
        W3[Spherical - Spherical Shell]
        W4[Articulated - Near Spherical]
        W5[SCARA - Flat Disc Volume]
        W6[Parallel - Small Hexagon]
    end
    W4 --> R1[Used in Armeo Power]
    W1 --> R2[Used in LokoHelp]
    W6 --> R3[Used in Balance Rehab]
```

### 4.4 Component Interaction Flow (Sensor → Decision → Action)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Encoder Measures Joint Angle] --> C[Controller - 1 kHz Loop]
    S2[Force Sensor Measures Patient Effort] --> C
    S3[EMG Measures Muscle Activation] --> C
    C --> DEC{Assist Level Decision}
    DEC -- Low Effort --> M1[Motor Provides Full Assistance]
    DEC -- Moderate Effort --> M2[Motor Provides Partial Assistance]
    DEC -- High Effort --> M3[Motor Stays in Transparent Mode]
    M1 --> FB[Feedback to Patient Haptic Display]
    M2 --> FB
    M3 --> FB
```
