# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Define impedance control. State the canonical impedance equation and explain the physical meaning of each term in the context of a rehabilitation robot.**

**Model Answer:**
Impedance control, formalized by Neville Hogan (1985), regulates the dynamic relationship between the force exerted on a robot and its resulting motion, rather than tracking position or force independently. The robot is commanded to behave as a programmable mass–spring–damper in contact with the patient. The canonical task-space impedance equation is:

$$M_d\ddot{\tilde{x}} + B_d\dot{\tilde{x}} + K_d \tilde{x} = F_{ext} - F_{ref}$$

where $\tilde{x} = x_{ref} - x$ is the deviation of the actual motion from the reference, $M_d, B_d, K_d$ are the desired (programmable) inertia, damping, and stiffness matrices (positive-definite for passivity), $F_{ext}$ is the external force from the patient on the robot, and $F_{ref}$ is an optional feed-forward reference force. Physically: $K_d$ represents the "spring-like" restorative tendency of the robot toward the reference trajectory; $B_d$ represents the "viscous damper" dissipating relative motion; $M_d$ represents the apparent inertia the patient feels when accelerating the robot. In rehabilitation, $K_d$ is typically lowered to encourage active patient effort (AAN) and $B_d$ is tuned to suppress oscillations from spasticity or tremor.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]
**What is meant by "Assist-as-Needed" (AAN) control? List any two performance signals commonly used to modulate the assistance level.**

**Model Answer:**
Assist-as-Needed (AAN) control is a rehabilitation-specific adaptive control strategy in which the robot provides only the *minimum* assistance required for the patient to complete a prescribed movement, thereby promoting active motor relearning and preventing patient slacking. The assistance gain (typically the stiffness $K_d$ in an impedance controller) is modulated in real time as a function of one or more patient-performance signals, rather than held constant. Two commonly used performance signals are:

1. **Position-tracking error** $\vert e(t) \vert = \vert x_{ref}(t) - x(t) \vert$ — large error indicates the patient is struggling and more assistance is warranted.
2. **Surface electromyogram (sEMG) amplitude** from the prime movers — high voluntary EMG activity indicates the patient is contributing effort, so assistance may be reduced.

Other valid signals include the *trial success rate*, *movement smoothness* (jerk-based), and *joint torque contribution*. The law is typically of the form $K_d(t) = K_{max}[1 - \beta P_{norm}(t)]$, where $P_{norm}$ is a normalized performance index in $[0,1]$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common pitfalls students fall into:
> 1. Writing impedance equation without defining $\tilde{x}$ and $F_{ext}$ — loses 2 marks.
> 2. Confusing admittance and impedance (they are *duals* — impedance = force-in/velocity-out; admittance = velocity-in/force-out). Examiners *will* test this.
> 3. Forgetting to mention positive-definiteness of $M_d, B_d, K_d$ for passivity/safety — loses 1 mark.
> 4. Drawing AAN block diagram without labeling feedback signals — loses 2 marks.
> 5. Mixing up Jacobian $J$ and its inverse in the control law.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the impedance control law for a 1-DOF rehabilitation robot interacting with a patient. Define all variables. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare impedance control, admittance control, and hybrid force/position control. State one clinical scenario where each is most appropriate. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation of the impedance control law (1-DOF, with block diagram):**

For a 1-DOF rotational joint interacting with a patient, the dynamic equation is:

$$J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + K_g\theta = \tau_m + \tau_h$$

where $J$ is link inertia, $B$ is viscous friction, $K_g$ is gravitational stiffness, $\tau_m$ is the motor torque, and $\tau_h$ is the torque exerted by the human patient on the robot (positive when patient drives the joint in the reference direction).

**Step 1 — Define the desired impedance behavior** between motor torque and angular deviation $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{ref} - \theta$:

$$J_d\ddot{\tilde{\theta}} + B_d\dot{\tilde{\theta}} + K_d \tilde{\theta} = \tau_h - \tau_{ref}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the required acceleration:**

$$\ddot{\theta} = \ddot{\theta}_{ref} + J_d^{-1}\left(B_d\dot{\tilde{\theta}} + K_d \tilde{\theta} - \tau_h + \tau_{ref}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Inverse dynamics:** Substitute into the plant dynamics and solve for $\tau_m$:

$$\tau_m = J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + K_g\theta - \tau_h$$

Substituting $\ddot{\theta}$:

$$\tau_m = J\left[\ddot{\theta}_{ref} + J_d^{-1}(B_d\dot{\tilde{\theta}} + K_d \tilde{\theta} - \tau_h + \tau_{ref})\right] + B\dot{\theta} + K_g\theta - \tau_h$$

Collecting terms:

$$\boxed{\tau_m = J\ddot{\theta}_{ref} + B\dot{\theta} + K_g\theta + \frac{J}{J_d}\left(B_d\dot{\tilde{\theta}} + K_d \tilde{\theta}\right) + \left(\frac{J}{J_d} - 1\right)(-\tau_h) + \frac{J}{J_d}\tau_{ref}}$$

The block diagram:

```
[θ_ref] → (Σ) → [1/Jd] → [Bd s + Kd] → (Σ) → [Inverse Dynamics] → [τ_m] → [Plant J,B,Kg] → [θ]
                       ↑                                          ↑
                    [B d ̇θ̃ + K d θ̃]                         [-τ_h + τ_ref]
```

**Variable definitions:**
- $J, B, K_g$ — actual plant inertia, damping, gravitational stiffness.
- $J_d, B_d, K_d$ — desired (programmable) impedance parameters.
- $\theta_{ref}, \dot{\theta}_{ref}, \ddot{\theta}_{ref}$ — reference trajectory derivatives.
- $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{ref} - \theta$ — position deviation.
- $\tau_h$ — patient-applied torque; $\tau_{ref}$ — optional feed-forward reference torque.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Impedance Control | Admittance Control | Hybrid Force/Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input measured** | Position / velocity | Force | Both (decoupled DOFs) |
| **Output regulated** | Force response to motion | Motion response to force | Force in some DOFs, position in others |
| **Best for** | Low-impedance (backdrivable) robots | High-impedance (geared) robots | Tasks with mixed constraints |
| **Implementation** | Inner position loop, outer impedance | Outer position loop, inner force loop | Selection matrix $S$ |
| **Stability** | Passive if $M_d,B_d,K_d \succ 0$ | Requires care (admittance zeros) | Constrained DOF can be unstable |
| **Clinical scenario** | Upper-limb therapy with backdrivable Armeo | Lower-limb exoskeleton with high gear ratio (Ekso) | Gait training over uneven terrain with obstacle-avoidance DOFs |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With necessary equations, explain the PID-based joint position control architecture for a 2-DOF rehabilitation robot. Show how the control law can be extended to an adaptive impedance controller using the *Assist-as-Needed* principle. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define passivity in the context of physical human–robot interaction (pHRI). Show that a well-tuned impedance controller is inherently passive. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) PID + AAN Adaptive Extension:**

For a 2-DOF manipulator, the joint-space dynamics are:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

A baseline **PID** controller per joint:

$$\tau_i = K_{p,i} e_i + K_{i,i} \int e_i\,dt + K_{d,i} \dot{e}_i, \quad e_i = q_{ref,i} - q_i$$

**Extension to Adaptive AAN Impedance:** The PID gains are *not* fixed; instead, the controller is recast as a stiffness-regulating impedance controller. The torque command becomes:

$$\tau = J^T(q)\left[K_d(t)\tilde{x} + B_d\dot{\tilde{x}} + K_p e_f\right]$$

where $K_d(t)$ is the *time-varying* stiffness updated by:

$$K_d(t) = K_{max} - \alpha \cdot P_{norm}(t)$$

with $\alpha \in [0, K_{max}]$ tuning the AAN aggressiveness, and the performance index:

$$P_{norm}(t) = w_1 \left(1 - \frac{\vert e(t) \vert}{e_{thresh}}\right) + w_2 \frac{EMG_{rms}(t)}{EMG_{max}} + w_3 \frac{N_{success}(t)}{N_{total}(t)}$$

**Logic:** As $P_{norm} \to 1$ (patient is performing well), $K_d \to K_{max} - \alpha$, i.e., assistance drops to a minimum (a small "safety spring" to guide the patient back to the trajectory). As $P_{norm} \to 0$ (patient struggling), $K_d \to K_{max}$ (full assistance).

**(b) Passivity and Safety:**

A two-port system (robot ↔ patient) is **passive** if it cannot generate energy; mathematically, the energy stored in the system is non-increasing:

$$\int_0^T \left[F_{ext}(t)\cdot v(t)\right]dt \ge -E(0) \quad \forall T \ge 0$$

For the impedance controller:

$$F_{ext} = M_d\ddot{\tilde{x}} + B_d\dot{\tilde{x}} + K_d \tilde{x}$$

Define the *energy storage function*:

$$E(t) = \frac{1}{2}\tilde{x}^T M_d \tilde{x} + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{x}^T K_d \tilde{x} \ge 0$$

Differentiating and using the impedance law:

$$\dot{E} = \tilde{x}^T M_d \ddot{\tilde{x}} + \tilde{x}^T K_d \dot{\tilde{x}}$$

Substituting $M_d\ddot{\tilde{x}} = F_{ext} - B_d\dot{\tilde{x}} - K_d\tilde{x}$:

$$\dot{E} = \tilde{x}^T (F_{ext} - B_d\dot{\tilde{x}} - K_d\tilde{x}) + \tilde{x}^T K_d \dot{\tilde{x}}$$

$$\dot{E} = \tilde{x}^T F_{ext} - \tilde{x}^T B_d \dot{\tilde{x}}$$

Using $\tilde{x}^T \dot{\tilde{x}} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dt}\vert \tilde{x} \vert^2$ and $B_d \succ 0$:

$$\dot{E} = F_{ext}\cdot v_{rel} - \dot{x}^T B_d \dot{x} \le F_{ext}\cdot v_{rel}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $T$:

$$E(T) - E(0) \le \int_0^T F_{ext}\cdot v\,dt$$

Hence:

$$\int_0^T F_{ext}\cdot v\,dt \ge E(0) - E(T) \ge -E(0)$$

which is the **passivity inequality**. Thus any controller with $M_d, B_d, K_d$ positive-definite is *passive* and therefore inherently safe for physical human–robot interaction.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Robot Control**

- **Hierarchical layers:** Task planner → Supervisory/AAN → Low-level servo → Hardware safety.
- **Position control:** Best for *free-space* motion; rigid, unsafe in contact.
- **Force control:** Best when the robot must *apply* a specific force.
- **Impedance control:** Robot behaves as programmable **mass–spring–damper**; canonical equation $M_d\ddot{\tilde{x}} + B_d\dot{\tilde{x}} + K_d \tilde{x} = F_{ext}$.
- **Admittance control:** Dual of impedance; $V = Y(s) F_{ext}$; preferred for geared, hard-to-backdrive exoskeletons.
- **Hybrid force/position:** Use a selection matrix $S$ to switch in task space.
- **AAN (Assist-as-Needed):** Stiffness $K_d$ is *modulated by patient performance*; goal is minimum assistance for active recovery.
- **Passivity:** Positive-definite $M_d, B_d, K_d$ ⇒ safe for pHRI (Hogan's theorem).
- **Jacobian transpose mapping:** $\tau = J^T F$ for converting task-space force to joint torques.
- **Computed-torque law:** $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q}_{ref} + C\dot{q}_{ref} + G + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$.
- **Sampling:** Low-level loops run at **1 kHz**; high-level at **10–100 Hz**.
- **EMG-driven control:** Decode voluntary intent from sEMG to drive an admittance/admittance-impedance controller.
- **Adaptive & intelligent methods:** MRAC, ILC, SMC, RL, neural, fuzzy — each addresses a specific patient variability issue.
- **Clinical mapping:** Upper-limb therapy → admittance; lower-limb exoskeleton → finite-state impedance + AAN; prosthetics → direct-force / EMG impedance.
- **Safety mandate:** In all rehab controllers, *passivity + current limits + software watchdogs* are non-negotiable for IEC 80601-2-78 compliance.

# Robot Sensors — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, a **Robot Sensor** is defined as a transducer-based electro-mechanical, optical, or bio-electrical device that detects physical, chemical, or biological stimuli from the patient–robot interaction environment and converts them into equivalent, processable electrical signals for the real-time closed-loop control of the rehabilitation manipulator.

Sensors form the **perceptual front-end** of any rehabilitation robot, enabling **sensory feedback**, **patient intent recognition**, **impedance/admittance control**, **safety monitoring**, and **quantitative outcome assessment (QoM — Quality of Movement metrics)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Verbatim):**
> *"Robot sensors are devices that measure robot status (proprioceptive) and robot environment (exteroceptive) including patient interaction parameters necessary for adaptive control in rehabilitation."*

### 1.2 Formal Classification (KTU-Aligned)

| Class | Sub-class | Function in Rehabilitation | Example Devices |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive (Internal State)** | Position | Measure joint angles/link displacements | Optical encoders, resolvers, Hall-effect |
| | Velocity | Measure rate of joint motion | Tachogenerators, incremental encoders (derived) |
| | Acceleration / Inertia | Measure link dynamics & tremor | MEMS accelerometers, gyroscopes |
| **Exteroceptive (External / Patient)** | Force / Torque | Measure interaction force between patient limb & robot | Strain-gauge F/T sensors, series elastic |
| | Tactile / Pressure | Map contact pressure on skin/prosthetic socket | FSR (Force-Sensitive Resistor), capacitive arrays |
| | Position (external) | Track limb trajectory in workspace | Magnetic trackers, IR cameras, vision |
| | Bio-electric | Measure muscle/nerve electrical activity | Surface EMG, EEG, EOG |
| | Proximity | Pre-contact safety zones | Infrared, ultrasonic, capacitive range finders |
| | Temperature | Tissue health & socket comfort | Thermistors, RTD |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Robot as a Skilled Physiotherapist":**
> Imagine a physiotherapist treating a stroke patient's paralyzed hand. Before moving the limb, the therapist:
> 1. **Asks** the patient how much force they can exert (→ bio-electric / EMG sensor).
> 2. **Touches** the skin to feel muscle stiffness (→ tactile / pressure sensor).
> 3. **Watches** the arm trajectory to avoid injury (→ vision / position sensor).
> 4. **Senses** the resistance offered by the spastic muscle (→ force/torque sensor).
> 5. **Hears/feels** trembling (→ inertial / accelerometer sensor).
>
> A rehabilitation robot performs **exactly** these five perception tasks — but with **mathematical precision, repeatability, and millisecond response times** — through its array of **Robot Sensors**.

## 1.4 Universal Performance Metrics of Sensors (Standardized)

Every sensor used in a KTU 2024 rehabilitation robotics design is evaluated using these **bolded standard metrics**:

- **Accuracy** — closeness of measured value to true value (expressed in **% Full Scale, %FS**)
- **Precision / Repeatability** — ability to reproduce the same reading under identical conditions
- **Resolution** — smallest detectable increment (e.g., **0.1 mm** for an encoder)
- **Sensitivity** — output change per unit input (slope of the transfer curve, in **V/N** or **V/rad**)
- **Linearity** — deviation from a best-fit straight line (in **% Nonlinearity**)
- **Range / Span** — minimum to maximum measurable input
- **Bandwidth** — frequency range of reliable operation (in **Hz**)
- **Hysteresis** — output difference between ascending and descending inputs
- **Drift** — slow change in output with time or temperature
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — in **dB**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **unit** of every sensor metric in the answer. Marks are deducted when units are missing.

# Robot Sensors — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Rehabilitation Sensor Chain

The signal pathway from a rehabilitation interaction to a control command follows this **mandatory six-stage flow**:

1. **Physical Stimulus (Input)** — Patient exerts force on the robot's end-effector, joint moves, or muscle fires.
2. **Sensing Element (Transduction)** — The active element (e.g., strain gauge, photodiode, Ag/AgCl electrode) converts the stimulus into a small electrical signal.
3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification (e.g., **instrumentation amplifier** with gain **G = 100–10 000**), filtering (**low-pass filter** with cut-off **f_c**), and **linearization**.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — Continuous analog voltage is sampled at **f_s (Hz)** and quantized to **N bits** with resolution $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$.
5. **Digital Processing (DSP/Microcontroller)** — Filtering, feature extraction (e.g., **RMS, MAV, MDF** for EMG), sensor fusion (e.g., **Kalman Filter**).
6. **Control Command Output** — The processed signal drives the actuator (motor) or is logged for therapy assessment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this chain matters in KTU exams:** Question paper often asks *"Explain the working of an F/T sensor in a rehabilitation robot."* — Answer by tracing the **stimulus → transduction → conditioning → control** pathway.

## 2.2 Detailed Sensor-Specific Analysis

### 2.2.1 Position Sensors — **Optical Encoder (Incremental)**

- A rotating **code disc** with **N lines** modulates light falling on a photodiode.
- Each line crossing generates one electrical pulse; two channels (A, B) in **quadrature** give direction.
- **Angular resolution:**

$$\theta_{res} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \times 4}$$

where the factor **×4** arises from quadrature decoding (rising + falling edges of both A and B).

- **Linear resolution** at a wheel/pulley of radius **r (m):**

$$x_{res} \;=\; r \,\theta_{res} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi r}{N \times 4}$$

- **Linear velocity** (derived by differentiation):

$$v(t) \;=\; \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{r \,(N_{pulses})}{4 \, \Delta t}$$

> **Rehabilitation application:** Used at every joint of the **ARMEO Power** (Hocoma) and **Lokomat** (Hocoma) to track limb position during therapy.

### 2.2.2 Force / Torque Sensors — **Strain-Gauge Based**

- A **Wheatstone bridge** of four strain gauges (gauge factor **G_f ≈ 2.0**) is bonded to an elastic element.
- Force **F** → strain **ε** → resistance change **ΔR** → bridge output **V_out**:

$$V_{out} \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot \frac{G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4} \cdot \left(1 + \eta\right)^{-1}$$

where **V_ex** is the excitation voltage (typically **5 V** or **10 V**) and **η** is the Poisson correction term.

- For a 6-axis F/T sensor (like the **ATI Mini45** or **JR3**), the **complete wrench** is reported as:

$$\mathbf{W} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \\ \tau_x \\ \tau_y \\ \tau_z \end{bmatrix}_{6\times 1} \;=\; \mathbf{C}_{cal} \cdot \mathbf{V}_{6\times 1}$$

where $\mathbf{C}_{cal}$ is the **6×6 calibration matrix** determined at the factory.

> **Rehabilitation application:** F/T sensors are the *backbone* of **assist-as-needed (AAN)** control strategies used in devices like the **InMotion ARM** (Interactive Motion Technologies) and the **MIT-Manus**.

### 2.2.3 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

- A 6-DOF IMU combines a **3-axis accelerometer** and a **3-axis gyroscope** (magnetometer optional → 9-DOF).
- Specific force relation (accelerometer):

$$a_{measured} \;=\; a_{true} - g \quad (\text{in body frame})$$

- Angular rate (gyroscope):

$$\omega_{measured} \;=\; \omega_{true} + b_{bias} + n_{noise}$$

- Orientation **R(t)** is updated via integration of angular velocity (subject to **drift**).
- **Drift-compensated orientation** uses a complementary or **Kalman filter**:

$$\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;=\; \alpha \,(\hat{\theta}_{gyro} + \omega \Delta t) + (1 - \alpha)\,\hat{\theta}_{accel}$$

where **α** is the filter weight (typically **0.95–0.98**).

> **Rehabilitation application:** Tremor quantification in **Parkinson's disease** patients and gait-phase detection in **exoskeleton robots** (e.g., **ReWalk, Ekso GT**).

### 2.2.4 Surface Electromyography (sEMG) — Bio-electric Sensor

- Ag/AgCl **bipolar electrodes** placed on skin over the muscle belly.
- Raw sEMG is a bipolar, stochastic, zero-mean voltage signal with dominant energy in the **20–500 Hz** band.
- After band-pass filtering and full-wave rectification, useful features are extracted:

| Feature | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **MAV** (Mean Absolute Value) | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$ | Overall muscle activation level |
| **RMS** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{2}}$ | Activation magnitude (proportional to force) |
| **VAR** (Variance) | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ | Power of the signal |
| **MDF** (Median Frequency) | $f_{m}\!: \int_{0}^{f_{m}} P(f)\,df = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} P(f)\,df$ | Fatigue index |

> **Rehabilitation application:** Patient intent decoding for **prosthetic limb control** and **myoelectric exoskeletons**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{res} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \times 4}$ | Encoder resolution | degrees, arc-sec | Position sensors |
| 2 | $x_{res} = \frac{2\pi r}{4N}$ | Linear resolution | m, mm | Wheel-driven joints |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{ex}\frac{G_f \varepsilon}{4}$ | Wheatstone bridge | Volts | Strain-gauge sensors |
| 4 | $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{C}_{cal}\mathbf{V}$ | F/T calibration | N, N·m | 6-axis F/T sensors |
| 5 | $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal quality | dB | All analog sensors |
| 6 | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N}}$ | ADC quantization step | Volts | ADC front-end |
| 7 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ | Sampling theorem | Hz | sEMG, vibration sensors |
| 8 | $\hat{\theta}_{fused} = \alpha(\hat{\theta}_{gyro}+\omega\Delta t)+(1-\alpha)\hat{\theta}_{accel}$ | Complementary filter | radians | IMU orientation |
| 9 | $MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum\vert x_i \vert$ | EMG feature | Volts | Myoelectric control |
| 10 | $F = kx$ (Hooke's Law for SE sensor) | Series elastic element | N, m | Series elastic actuators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — Table-Safe Notation:** Wherever an absolute value or vertical separator occurs in any math cell, use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown pipe syntax. For example, write `$MAV = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i \vert$` — never `$MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i \vert$`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical decision support:** Sensors enable **objective, quantitative rehabilitation metrics** (e.g., smoothness, accuracy, speed) replacing subjective therapist scoring.
- **Safety:** Force/collision sensors enforce **ISO 13482 / ISO 13849** safety standards for medical robots.
- **Closed-loop control:** Enables **impedance, admittance, AAN, and EMG-driven** control architectures.
- **Tele-rehabilitation:** Real-time sensor data is streamed over the network for **remote therapy sessions**.

# Robot Sensors — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Quadrature Encoder Resolution

**Given:**
- An incremental optical encoder disc with **N = 5000 lines** is mounted on a motor shaft.
- The output is read by a **quadrature decoder** (×4 mode).
- The driving pulley has radius **r = 25 mm**.

**Find:** (a) Angular resolution in degrees and arc-seconds, (b) Linear resolution in mm.

### Step 1 — Identify the formula

The angular resolution of a quadrature-decoded incremental encoder is:

$$\theta_{res} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \times 4}$$

**Logic:** The factor **4** emerges because quadrature decoding counts **four** state transitions per grating line: rising-edge of channel A, rising-edge of channel B, falling-edge of A, falling-edge of B.

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$\theta_{res} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{5000 \times 4} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{20000} \;=\; 0.018^{\circ}$$

**Logic:** Simple arithmetic division of numerator by denominator.

### Step 3 — Convert to arc-seconds

$$1^{\circ} \;=\; 3600 \text{ arc-seconds} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_{res} \;=\; 0.018 \times 3600 \;=\; 64.8 \text{ arc-seconds}$$

**Logic:** One full circle is 360°, which equals 3600″ (60 arc-minutes × 60 arc-seconds).

### Step 4 — Compute linear resolution

$$x_{res} \;=\; r \cdot \theta_{res} \;(\text{in radians})$$

First, convert degrees to radians:

$$\theta_{res, rad} \;=\; 0.018^{\circ} \times \frac{\pi}{180^{\circ}} \;=\; 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

Then:

$$x_{res} \;=\; 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \;=\; 7.854 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Logic:** Multiply the radius (in meters) by the angular resolution (in radians) to obtain the arc length traversed per count. This arc length equals the linear resolution at the rim.

### Step 5 — Final answers

$$\boxed{\theta_{res} = 0.018^{\circ} = 64.8 \text{ arc-seconds}, \quad x_{res} \approx 7.85 \,\mu\text{m}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Strain-Gauge Force Sensor Output

**Given:**
- A cantilever beam force sensor with **four** strain gauges in a full Wheatstone bridge.
- Gauge factor **G_f = 2.10**.
- Excitation voltage **V_ex = 5 V**.
- Applied force **F = 10 N** produces strain **ε = 500 μ-strain = 5 × 10⁻⁴**.

**Find:** Bridge output voltage **V_out**.

### Step 1 — Write the standard Wheatstone bridge output

For a **full bridge** with one active gauge in tension and one in compression (Poisson effect), the standard small-signal approximation is:

$$V_{out} \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot \frac{G_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (1 + \nu)}{4}$$

For most metallic gauges, the Poisson ratio **ν ≈ 0.3**, so **(1 + ν) ≈ 1.3**. In the simplified form used in KTU textbooks:

$$V_{out} \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot \frac{G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$V_{out} \;=\; 5 \cdot \frac{2.10 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate numerator

$$2.10 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 10.5 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 4 — Divide and multiply

$$V_{out} \;=\; 5 \times \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-3}}{4} \;=\; 5 \times 2.625 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 1.3125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to mV

$$\boxed{V_{out} \;=\; 1.31 \text{ mV}}$$

**Logic check:** Strain-gauge bridges give outputs in the **mV range**, which is why an **instrumentation amplifier (INA128, AD620)** with gain **G = 1000** is mandatory. The conditioned output would be **1.31 V**, well within the **0–5 V** ADC input range.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Complementary Filter for IMU Pitch Angle

**Given:**
- Accelerometer-derived pitch: $\hat{\theta}_{acc} = 5.0^{\circ}$ (low noise, slow response).
- Gyroscope-derived pitch integrated over **Δt = 0.01 s**: $\hat{\theta}_{gyro,new} = 4.8^{\circ}$.
- Filter weight: **α = 0.95**.

**Find:** Fused pitch estimate $\hat{\theta}_{fused}$.

### Step 1 — Recall the filter equation

$$\hat{\theta}_{fused}(k) \;=\; \alpha \left[\hat{\theta}_{gyro}(k-1) + \omega(k)\,\Delta t\right] + (1 - \alpha)\,\hat{\theta}_{acc}(k)$$

**Logic:** The high-pass term (gyro) provides short-term accuracy without drift. The low-pass term (accelerometer) anchors long-term accuracy.

### Step 2 — Substitute the integrated gyro term

Since the integrated gyroscope output is already given as $\hat{\theta}_{gyro,new} = 4.8^{\circ}$:

$$\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;=\; 0.95 \times 4.8^{\circ} + 0.05 \times 5.0^{\circ}$$

### Step 3 — Compute each term

$$0.95 \times 4.8 \;=\; 4.56^{\circ}$$

$$0.05 \times 5.0 \;=\; 0.25^{\circ}$$

### Step 4 — Sum

$$\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;=\; 4.56^{\circ} + 0.25^{\circ} \;=\; 4.81^{\circ}$$

### Step 5 — Final answer

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;=\; 4.81^{\circ}}$$

**Logic check:** The result lies between the gyro (4.8°) and accelerometer (5.0°), heavily weighted toward the gyro (95%), exactly as expected.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Selection for Rehabilitation

| Selection Criterion | Optical Encoder | Strain-Gauge F/T | IMU | sEMG | Vision (Camera) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | Position | Force / Torque | Inertia / Orientation | Muscle activation | Limb trajectory |
| **Bandwidth (Hz)** | 10 k–100 k | 100–1000 | 100–500 | 20–500 | 30–120 |
| **Resolution** | μm range | mN range | mg range | μV range | mm range |
| **Drift** | None | Very low | High (gyro) | Low | Low |
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Non-invasive | Non-invasive | Non-invasive | Non-invasive |
| **Best Application** | Joint angle | AAN control | Tremor, gait | Intent decoding | Workspace tracking |
| **Limitation** | Shaft mount needed | Costly calibration | Drift compensation required | Skin prep, sweat | Lighting, occlusion |
| **Cost (Relative)** | Low | High | Medium | Low | Medium–High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters:** KTU 2024 questions of the form *"Justify the choice of sensor for a gait rehabilitation exoskeleton"* require a **tabulated justification** like the above, not a vague one-line answer.

# Robot Sensors — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Generic Sensor Signal Chain in a Rehabilitation Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Physical Stimulus] --> B[Sensor Transduction Stage]
    B --> C[Analog Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Conversion ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processing DSP]
    E --> F[Sensor Fusion and Feature Extraction]
    F --> G[Closed Loop Controller]
    G --> H[Rehabilitation Robot Actuator]
    H --> I[Therapeutic Motion on Patient Limb]
    I --> A
    J[Safety Watchdog from ADC] --> G
    K[Clinician Display and Data Logger] --> E
    E --> K
```

**Reading the diagram:** The outer loop is the **patient-in-the-loop bio-feedback cycle**. The `J` block represents a **real-time safety interrupt** that halts motion if force or position exceed clinical limits. The `K` block is the **QoM (Quality of Movement) data path** for clinical reporting.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Multi-Sensor Architecture of a Rehabilitation Exoskeleton

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph proprioceptive[Proprioceptive Sensor Cluster]
        enc1[Hip Joint Optical Encoder]
        enc2[Knee Joint Optical Encoder]
        enc3[Ankle Joint Optical Encoder]
        imu1[Thigh IMU 6 DOF]
        imu2[Shank IMU 6 DOF]
    end

    subgraph exteroceptive[Exteroceptive and Patient Sensor Cluster]
        ft1[Footplate Force Torque Sensor]
        ft2[Knee Orthosis F T Sensor]
        emg1[Quadriceps sEMG Electrode Pair]
        emg2[Hamstring sEMG Electrode Pair]
        fsr1[Insole FSR Pressure Array]
        cam1[Workspace RGB Depth Camera]
    end

    fusion[Central Sensor Fusion and Kalman Filter Unit]
    ctrl[Adaptive Impedance Controller]
    act[Exoskeleton Series Elastic Actuators]

    enc1 --> fusion
    enc2 --> fusion
    enc3 --> fusion
    imu1 --> fusion
    imu2 --> fusion
    ft1 --> fusion
    ft2 --> fusion
    emg1 --> fusion
    emg2 --> fusion
    fsr1 --> fusion
    cam1 --> fusion

    fusion --> ctrl
    ctrl --> act
    act --> fusion
```

**Reading the diagram:** Sensors are clustered into two sub-graphs — **proprioceptive (robot's own state)** and **exteroceptive (patient and environment state)**. All data converge into the **Central Sensor Fusion Unit**, which produces an **unified state estimate** that the **Adaptive Impedance Controller** uses to command the **Series Elastic Actuators**. The loopback from `act` to `fusion` represents **force feedback** from the SE actuator's built-in spring deflection sensor.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Flow for Sensor Selection (KTU Exam Heuristic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Rehabilitation Task Identified] --> q1{What needs to be measured?}
    q1 -->|Joint angle or position| pos[Use Optical Encoder or Resolver]
    q1 -->|Force or torque interaction| ft[Use Strain Gauge F T Sensor]
    q1 -->|Muscle activation| emg[Use Surface EMG Electrodes]
    q1 -->|Limb trajectory in 3D| vis[Use RGB D Camera or Motion Capture]
    q1 -->|Tremor or gait phase| imu[Use IMU 6 or 9 DOF]
    q1 -->|Contact pressure mapping| pr[Use FSR Array or Capacitive Tactile]

    pos --> safety{Medical Grade ISO 13482 Compliance}
    ft --> safety
    emg --> safety
    vis --> safety
    imu --> safety
    pr --> safety

    safety -->|Yes| deploy[Deploy with Closed Loop Control and Logging]
    safety -->|No| redesign[Add Safety Watchdog and Re calibrate]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **top-down design heuristic** frequently asked in KTU viva voce. Starting from the **clinical need**, traverse the decision tree to reach an **appropriate sensor** and then verify **ISO medical-grade compliance** before deployment.
