# Robot Sensors — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]
**Q: Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors used in rehabilitation robotics. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate as 1+1+1):**

**Proprioceptive sensors** measure the **internal state** of the robot — its joint angles, velocities, accelerations, and link positions. They answer the question *"Where is my own limb in space?"*

- Example 1: **Optical incremental encoder** at the elbow joint of an upper-limb exoskeleton.
- Example 2: **MEMS gyroscope** inside an IMU mounted on the thigh of a gait exoskeleton.

**Exteroceptive sensors** measure the **external environment and patient interaction** — forces, pressures, muscle activity, limb trajectory in workspace. They answer the question *"What is the patient/environment doing to me?"*

- Example 1: **Strain-gauge 6-axis F/T sensor** at the end-effector of the MIT-Manus.
- Example 2: **Surface EMG electrode pair** on the biceps for intent decoding.

> **Distribution:** 1 mark for the correct definition, 1 mark for two examples of proprioceptive, 1 mark for two examples of exteroceptive.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]
**Q: Define sensitivity and resolution of a sensor. What is the typical resolution of an optical encoder with 2500 lines in ×4 quadrature mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate as 1+1+1):**

**Sensitivity** of a sensor is the **ratio of the change in output signal to the corresponding change in input stimulus**. It is the slope of the transfer characteristic and is expressed in units like **V/N**, **V/rad**, or **mV/°C**.

**Resolution** of a sensor is the **smallest change in the input that produces a detectable change in the output**. For digital sensors, it equals one count; for analog sensors, it is the smallest readable increment above the noise floor.

**Calculation:**

$$\theta_{res} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \times 4} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{2500 \times 4} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{10000} \;=\; 0.036^{\circ} \;=\; 129.6 \text{ arc-seconds}$$

**Final answer:** Resolution ≈ **0.036°** or **129.6 arc-seconds**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Wheatstone-bridge-based strain-gauge force sensor** used at the end-effector of a rehabilitation robot. Derive the output voltage expression. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A **quadrature optical encoder** with **N = 2000 lines** is fitted to the knee joint of a gait orthosis. The pulley radius is **30 mm**. Compute the (i) angular resolution in degrees and arc-seconds, (ii) linear resolution in micrometres, and (iii) number of pulses generated for a knee flexion of **45°**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Force F applied] → [Elastic Element / Beam] → [Strain Gauges bonded on beam]
       → [Wheatstone Bridge] → [Instrumentation Amplifier INA128]
       → [Low-Pass Filter] → [ADC] → [Microcontroller / DSP]
       → [Controller of Rehabilitation Robot]
```

**Step 2 — Working principle (3 marks):**

- A **metallic elastic element** (cantilever, cylindrical post, or cross) deforms elastically under the applied force **F**.
- **Four strain gauges** (R1, R2, R3, R4) are bonded onto the elastic element — two on the tension side, two on the compression side — and connected as a **Wheatstone bridge**.
- The bridge is excited by a **stable DC voltage V_ex** (typically 5 V or 10 V).
- Under zero force, the bridge is balanced, V_out = 0.
- Under force **F**, gauges change resistance by **ΔR**, unbalancing the bridge and producing **V_out ∝ F**.

**Step 3 — Derivation of V_out (2 marks):**

For one active gauge with resistance change ΔR:

$$V_{out} \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} \;=\; V_{ex} \cdot \frac{G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$$

For a full bridge with two active gauges (tension and compression) and Poisson correction:

$$\boxed{V_{out} \;=\; \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (1 + \nu)}{4}}$$

This voltage (typically a few mV) is then amplified by an INA128 with gain G = 1000 to a 0–5 V range suitable for ADC.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** N = 2000 lines, ×4 quadrature, r = 30 mm, knee flexion θ = 45°.

**(i) Angular resolution (3 marks):**

$$\theta_{res} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \times 4} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{8000} \;=\; 0.045^{\circ}$$

In arc-seconds:

$$\theta_{res} \;=\; 0.045^{\circ} \times 3600 \;=\; 162 \text{ arc-seconds}$$

**(ii) Linear resolution (2 marks):**

Convert to radians:

$$\theta_{res, rad} \;=\; 0.045 \times \frac{\pi}{180} \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

Linear resolution:

$$x_{res} \;=\; r \cdot \theta_{res, rad} \;=\; 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 23.56 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Number of pulses for 45° flexion (2 marks):**

Number of quadrature counts:

$$N_{counts} \;=\; \frac{\theta}{\theta_{res}} \;=\; \frac{45^{\circ}}{0.045^{\circ}} \;=\; 1000 \text{ counts}$$

Each count corresponds to **one** quadrature transition, so **1000 pulses** are generated.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **working of a Surface EMG (sEMG) sensor system** for decoding patient intent in a myoelectric prosthetic arm. List four time-domain EMG features used in control. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain the **complementary filter** used in a **6-DOF IMU** for orientation estimation in a tremor-rehabilitation robot. An accelerometer measures pitch **θ_acc = 8.2°**, and a gyroscope integrated over **Δt = 0.02 s** gives **θ_gyro = 7.9°**. Using **α = 0.96**, find the fused pitch estimate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Muscle Generates Action Potential] → [Ag-AgCl Bipolar Electrodes on Skin]
   → [Differential Amplifier AD8221] → [Band-Pass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
   → [50 Hz Notch Filter for Power-Line Interference]
   → [Full-Wave Rectifier] → [Envelope Detector / RMS]
   → [Feature Extraction MAV, RMS, VAR, MDF]
   → [Classifier LDA or SVM or Neural Network]
   → [Prosthetic Hand Motor Command]
```

**Step 2 — Working principle (3 marks):**

- During voluntary muscle contraction, **motor-unit action potentials** generate electrical potentials on the skin surface of amplitude **50 μV – 5 mV** and bandwidth **20–500 Hz**.
- **Bipolar Ag/AgCl electrodes** (placed 2 cm apart along the muscle fibre) capture the differential signal.
- A high-CMRR differential amplifier (AD8221, CMRR > 100 dB) rejects common-mode noise.
- Band-pass filter removes motion artefact (< 20 Hz) and high-frequency noise (> 500 Hz).
- A **50 Hz notch filter** removes Indian/European power-line interference.
- After rectification, the **envelope** correlates with the level of muscle activation.
- Features (MAV, RMS, etc.) are fed to a **classifier** which maps the activation pattern to a specific prosthetic hand grasp (e.g., lateral, cylindrical, pinch).

**Step 3 — Four time-domain features (2 marks):**

1. **MAV** — Mean Absolute Value: $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i \vert$
2. **RMS** — Root Mean Square: $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i^2}$
3. **VAR** — Variance: $\tfrac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$
4. **WL** — Waveform Length: $\sum \vert x_{i+1} - x_i \vert$

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Why a complementary filter is needed (2 marks):**

- The **accelerometer** gives a stable, drift-free orientation in the long term but is corrupted by **high-frequency vibration noise** (especially in a tremor-rehabilitation robot).
- The **gyroscope** gives smooth, high-bandwidth angular rate but suffers from **integration drift** over time.
- A **complementary filter** fuses both in the **frequency domain** — trusting the gyro at high frequencies and the accelerometer at low frequencies.

**Step 2 — Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Gyroscope ω] → [Integrator ∫ω dt] → [High-Pass Weight α] → [Sum Node] → θ_fused
[Accelerometer θ_acc] → [Low-Pass Weight 1-α] ────────────→ [Sum Node]
```

**Step 3 — Equation and substitution (3 marks):**

$$\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;=\; \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{acc}$$

$$\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;=\; 0.96 \times 7.9^{\circ} + 0.04 \times 8.2^{\circ}$$

$$\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;=\; 7.584^{\circ} + 0.328^{\circ} \;=\; 7.912^{\circ}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{fused} \;\approx\; 7.91^{\circ}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the (1+ν) factor** in the strain-gauge derivation — deduct **0.5 mark** if omitted.
> 2. **Forgetting the ×4 quadrature factor** in encoder resolution — this is the **most common error**, deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing "filter" instead of "complementary filter"** in IMU questions — a complementary filter is **frequency-domain complementary**, not a simple average.
> 4. **Unit omissions** in final numerical answers — always write **degrees, μm, mV, Hz**.
> 5. **Confusing MAV with RMS** in EMG feature definitions — note that MAV is L1-norm based, RMS is L2-norm based.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Sensors are transducers** converting physical, chemical, or biological stimuli into electrical signals.
- [x] **Proprioceptive = robot state** (encoders, IMU); **Exteroceptive = patient/environment** (F/T, EMG, vision).
- [x] **Encoder resolution formula** has a **mandatory ×4 factor** for quadrature decoding.
- [x] **Strain-gauge F/T sensor output** is in **mV range** — always needs an **instrumentation amplifier**.
- [x] **6-axis F/T wrench** is computed via a **factory-calibrated 6×6 matrix**.
- [x] **IMU drift** is corrected by **complementary filtering** (α typically 0.95–0.98) or **Kalman filtering**.
- [x] **sEMG bandwidth** is **20–500 Hz**; **notch filter at 50 Hz** removes power-line interference.
- [x] **EMG features**: MAV, RMS, VAR, WL, ZC, SSC, MDF, MNF.
- [x] **ADC quantization step** $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^{N}$ — **higher N = finer resolution**.
- [x] **Nyquist criterion**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- [x] **Safety standards**: ISO 13482 (medical robots) and ISO 13849 (safety-related control).
- [x] **Rehabilitation robots using these sensors**: Lokomat, Armeo Power, MIT-Manus, InMotion ARM, ReWalk, Ekso GT, MyoPro.
- [x] **Always state units** in the final numerical answer to avoid losing **0.5–1 mark**.
- [x] **Use `\vert` / `\mid` instead of `|`** in math tables to preserve markdown table syntax.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Interfaces to Robotic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human Interfaces to Robotic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Human Interface to a Robotic System** (also termed **Human-Robot Interface / HRI** or **Human-Machine Interface / HMI** in rehabilitation engineering) is defined as the dedicated hardware–software communication channel that permits bidirectional exchange of information — **intent signals, sensory feedback, and control commands** — between a human operator (typically a patient with neuromotor impairment) and a rehabilitation robot.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this interface is classified as the **perception–cognition–action loop** in which:

$$
\text{Human} \;\;\xleftrightarrow{\;\text{Input Channel (Bio-signal)}\;}\;\; \text{Interface} \;\;\xleftrightarrow{\;\text{Output Channel (Actuation)}\;}\;\; \text{Robot}
$$

The interface must satisfy three cardinal design constraints — **safety, transparency, and intuitiveness** — as mandated by ISO 13482 (Personal Care Robots) and IEC 80601-2-78 (Rehabilitation Robots).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** A Human Interface in robotic rehabilitation is the bidirectional conduit that translates human physiological intent (EMG, EEG, EOG, motion) into robotic actuation commands, while relaying synthetic sensory feedback (force, visual, haptic) back to the human, in real time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are learning to drive a car. The **steering wheel, pedals, gear-shift, and rear-view mirror** together form the *human-vehicle interface*. Your brain sends a command (intent) → your hands/feet actuate the controls (input channel) → the car moves and you *feel* the road through the steering wheel vibrations and seat (feedback channel) → you adjust accordingly.

In **robotic rehabilitation**, the patient is the "driver" and the rehabilitation robot is the "car," but the interface is far more sophisticated:

- The **patient's muscles or brain** are too weak to give strong commands, so we read faint **electrical signals (EMG/EEG)** from the skin or scalp.
- The **robot** amplifies these signals into powerful, precise movement.
- The robot must also tell the patient what it is doing, through **haptic (touch) cues, visual displays, or audio tones** — this is the *feedback* leg of the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a rehabilitation robot as a *translator* standing between a person who whispers (weak neural signal) and a giant who can do heavy lifting (powerful robot actuators). The translator's job is the **interface** — listening to the whisper, speaking loudly to the giant, and describing the giant's actions back to the person in a language they understand.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants & Standards)

The following quantitative metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU examinations and must be memorized:

- **Sampling Rate of Bio-signals:** **≥ 1 kHz** for EMG, **≥ 256 Hz** for EEG.
- **Haptic Rendering Latency:** **≤ 20 ms** (below human tactile perception threshold).
- **Visual Feedback Latency (VR):** **≤ 90 ms** to avoid cybersickness.
- **Shared Control Bandwidth:** **0.5 Hz – 10 Hz** for upper-limb rehabilitation.
- **EMG Signal Amplitude Range:** **50 µV – 5 mV** (surface EMG).
- **EEG Signal Amplitude Range:** **5 µV – 200 µV**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly emphasizes **bioelectric interfaces (EMG, EEG, EOG)**, **haptic interfaces**, **wearable/exoskeleton interfaces**, and **shared-control architectures**. Master these four pillars for full marks.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (Hierarchy)

Human Interfaces to Robotic Systems can be broadly classified into **three primary categories** based on the signal source:

| Category | Signal Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Bioelectric Interfaces** | EMG, EEG, EOG, ECG | Intent decoding, BCI-driven robots |
| **Haptic / Kinematic Interfaces** | Force, position, torque sensors | Telerobotics, exoskeletons |
| **Multimodal Interfaces** | Fusion of above + vision/VR | Advanced neurorehabilitation |

This triadic classification forms the spine of all further theoretical analysis in the upcoming sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Human-Robot Interface

A rehabilitation HRI is engineered as a **four-layer architecture**. Understanding each layer is critical for KTU long-answer (14-mark) questions.

### Layer 1 — Signal Acquisition Layer
- **Function:** Capture raw bioelectric/kinematic signals from the human.
- **Hardware:** Surface electrodes (Ag/AgCl), inertial measurement units (IMUs), encoders, force-sensitive resistors (FSRs).
- **Key consideration:** Common-mode rejection ratio (**CMRR ≥ 110 dB**) for EMG/EEG front-end amplifiers.

### Layer 2 — Signal Processing & Feature Extraction Layer
- **Function:** Clean the noisy raw signal and extract discriminative features.
- **Standard pipeline:** Band-pass filtering → Notch filtering (50 Hz) → Rectification → Smoothing → Feature extraction.
- **Features for EMG:** Root Mean Square (RMS), Mean Absolute Value (MAV), Waveform Length, Zero Crossings.
- **Features for EEG:** Power Spectral Density (PSD), Common Spatial Patterns (CSP), wavelet coefficients.

### Layer 3 — Intent Decoding / Classification Layer
- **Function:** Map features to control commands.
- **Algorithms:** Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Support Vector Machines (SVM), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS).
- **Output:** Discrete class label (e.g., "elbow flex") or continuous regression variable (joint angle, force level).

### Layer 4 — Robot Control & Feedback Rendering Layer
- **Function:** Execute the decoded intent on the robot and relay feedback.
- **Controllers:** PID, impedance/admittance control, shared control, myoelectric proportional control.
- **Feedback modalities:** Haptic force, vibrotactile, visual (VR/AR), auditory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This four-layer architecture is the industry standard used in devices such as the **InMotion ARM (Bionik Labs)**, **Lokomat (Hocoma)**, and **Armeo Power (Hocoma)**. The KTU examiner often asks students to "draw and explain the HRI architecture for a rehabilitation robot" — this four-layer model is the textbook answer.

## 2.2 EMG-Based Interface (Myoelectric Control)

The **electromyogram (EMG)** is the electrical manifestation of muscle contraction, acquired via surface or intramuscular electrodes. It is the most clinically mature bioelectric interface.

### Operating Principle
1. Motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) summate on the skin surface.
2. Surface electrodes transduce ionic current into electronic voltage.
3. The signal is amplified, filtered, and rectified.
4. Features (RMS, MAV) are computed within sliding windows of **100–250 ms**.
5. A control law maps the feature to actuator command (velocity, force, or position).

### Proportional Myoelectric Control Law
The simplest mapping is linear proportional control:

$$
u(t) = K \cdot \text{RMS}\{x(t)\}
$$

where $u(t)$ is the robot command, $K$ is the controller gain, and $x(t)$ is the raw EMG signal.

### Classification-Based Myoelectric Control
For multi-class (multi-DOF) control, a classifier is used:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{i \in \{1,\ldots,C\}} \;\mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{f}(t) + b_i
$$

where $\mathbf{f}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the feature vector, $\mathbf{w}_i$ is the weight vector of class $i$, and $b_i$ is the bias.

## 2.3 EEG-Based Interface (Brain-Computer Interface)

The **electroencephalogram (EEG)** measures cortical electrical activity through scalp electrodes following the international **10-20 system**. EEG is indispensable for patients with **locked-in syndrome** or severe ALS.

### Dominant Rhythms

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | Cognitive State |
|---|---|---|
| Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep |
| Theta ($\theta$) | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, memory |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, motor planning |
| Mu ($\mu$) | 8 – 12 Hz (sensorimotor) | Motor imagery |

### Motor Imagery (MI) BCI
The most clinically relevant paradigm. The patient *imagines* moving a limb, and the corresponding **event-related desynchronization (ERD)** in the mu/beta band over the contralateral sensorimotor cortex is detected and translated into robot motion.

$$
\text{ERD}_{\text{ratio}}(t) = \frac{P_{\text{active}}(f,t) - P_{\text{reference}}(f,t)}{P_{\text{reference}}(f,t)} \times 100\%
$$

where $P_{\text{active}}$ and $P_{\text{reference}}$ are the band powers during motor imagery and rest, respectively.

## 2.4 EOG-Based Interface (Eye Tracking)

The **electrooculogram (EOG)** captures the corneo-retinal standing potential (≈ **0.4 – 1.0 mV**) that rotates with eye motion. It is used when limb signals are unavailable.

$$
V_{\text{EOG}} = k_{\text{h}} \cdot \theta_{\text{h}} + k_{\text{v}} \cdot \theta_{\text{v}}
$$

where $\theta_{\text{h}}$ and $\theta_{\text{v}}$ are horizontal and vertical gaze angles, and $k_{\text{h}}, k_{\text{v}}$ are electrode placement constants (typically 20 µV/degree).

## 2.5 Haptic Interface

A **haptic interface** is a mechatronic device that provides force/tactile feedback to the user, enabling them to "feel" the robotic environment. In rehabilitation, haptics closes the sensorimotor loop for motor learning.

### Impedance Control Law (Core Haptic Equation)

$$
M \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K x = F_{\text{human}} - F_{\text{environment}}
$$

- $M$, $B$, $K$ are the virtual inertia, damping, and stiffness programmed by the therapist.
- $F_{\text{human}}$ is the measured user force; $F_{\text{environment}}$ is the virtual contact force.

### Admittance Control Law (Dual Form)

$$
F_{\text{robot}} = M \ddot{x}_{\text{desired}} + B \dot{x}_{\text{desired}} + K x_{\text{desired}}
$$

The choice between impedance and admittance depends on whether the robot is backdriveable and on the environment stiffness.

## 2.6 Shared Control Architecture

In clinical practice, **fully autonomous** or **fully manual** control is suboptimal. The **shared control** paradigm blends the patient's intent with the robot's autonomous intelligence.

$$
u_{\text{shared}}(t) = \alpha(t) \cdot u_{\text{patient}}(t) + \big(1 - \alpha(t)\big) \cdot u_{\text{robot}}(t)
$$

where $\alpha(t) \in [0,1]$ is the **assistance level**, often adaptively tuned by an error-based law:

$$
\alpha(t+1) = \alpha(t) + \eta \cdot \big(e_{\text{tracking}}(t) - e_{\text{threshold}}\big)
$$

Here, $\eta$ is the adaptation rate, and $e_{\text{tracking}}$ is the deviation between desired and actual trajectory.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Insight:** Always mention the **Asymmetric Cost Model** for shared control. The penalty for *over-assisting* (stifling patient effort) is typically weighted more heavily than the penalty for *under-assisting* to encourage active participation — a principle called "**challenge point regulation**" (Guadagnoli & Lee, 2004).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All mathematical notation below uses `\vert` for absolute value (NOT the pipe `|` character) to preserve the KTU markdown table layout. This is a strict KTU 2024 typing convention.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMG RMS Feature | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$ | $x_i$ in µV, $N$ = window length | Proportional myo-control |
| 2 | EMG MAV Feature | $\text{MAV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$ | $x_i$ in µV, $N$ = window length | Low-complexity intent detection |
| 3 | Proportional Myo-control | $u(t) = K \cdot \text{RMS}\{x(t)\}$ | $K$ in N/mV | Single-DOF gripper |
| 4 | EOG Gaze Mapping | $V_{\text{EOG}} = k_{\text{h}}\theta_{\text{h}} + k_{\text{v}}\theta_{\text{v}}$ | $V$ in µV, $\theta$ in degrees | Wheelchair / cursor control |
| 5 | EEG ERD Ratio | $\text{ERD}\% = \frac{P_{\text{act}} - P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{ref}}} \cdot 100$ | $P$ in µV²/Hz | MI-BCI classifier input |
| 6 | Impedance Control | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_h - F_e$ | $M$ kg, $B$ Ns/m, $K$ N/m | Free-space haptic rendering |
| 7 | Admittance Control | $F_r = M\ddot{x}_d + B\dot{x}_d + Kx_d$ | $F$ in N, $x$ in m | High-stiffness contact tasks |
| 8 | Shared Control | $u_s = \alpha u_p + (1-\alpha)u_r$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | Adaptive rehab therapy |
| 9 | Adaptation of $\alpha$ | $\alpha_{t+1} = \alpha_t + \eta(e_t - e_{\text{th}})$ | $\eta$ learning rate | Challenge point regulator |
| 10 | Stability (Z-width) | $Z_w = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+\cos\phi}{1-\cos\phi}\right)$ | $\phi$ = phase margin | Haptic stability criterion |
| 11 | Nyquist (Sampling) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | $f_s$ in Hz | Aliasing prevention |
| 12 | Closed-loop BW | $BW = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}$ | rad/s → Hz | Haptic transparency |
| 13 | Power Spectral Density | $P(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty}\frac{1}{T}\vert X(f)\vert^2$ | W/Hz | EEG rhythm extraction |
| 14 | Common Spatial Patterns | $\mathbf{J} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{W}} \frac{\mathbf{W}^T \Sigma_1 \mathbf{W}}{\mathbf{W}^T \Sigma_2 \mathbf{W}}$ | $\Sigma_i$ class covariance | EEG feature projection |
| 15 | SNR (EMG) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{\text{signal}}}{A_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB | Signal quality metric |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Haptic Interface

**Problem Setup.** Consider a single-DOF haptic interface modelled as a mass-spring-damper system driven by the patient's hand force $F_h$ and subjected to an environment contact force $F_e$. We aim to derive the relationship between the position $x$ and the input force.

### Step 1 — Write the Equation of Motion
By Newton's second law, the net force on the haptic handle equals mass times acceleration.

$$
M \ddot{x}(t) + B \dot{x}(t) + K x(t) = F_h(t) - F_e(t)
$$

**Explanation:** The mass $M$ resists acceleration via $M\ddot{x}$, the damper $B$ resists velocity via $B\dot{x}$, and the spring $K$ resists displacement via $Kx$. The right side is the net external force.

### Step 2 — Take the Laplace Transform
Assuming zero initial conditions $x(0) = \dot{x}(0) = 0$:

$$
M s^2 X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s) = F_h(s) - F_e(s)
$$

Factor out $X(s)$:

$$
X(s) \big(M s^2 + B s + K\big) = F_h(s) - F_e(s)
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Transfer Function
Define the **mechanical admittance** $Y(s)$ as the ratio of displacement to net force:

$$
Y(s) = \frac{X(s)}{F_h(s) - F_e(s)} = \frac{1}{M s^2 + B s + K}
$$

The corresponding **mechanical impedance** is:

$$
Z(s) = \frac{1}{Y(s)} = M s^2 + B s + K
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Standard Second-Order Form
Divide numerator and denominator by $M$:

$$
Y(s) = \frac{\frac{1}{K}}{\frac{M}{K} s^2 + \frac{B}{K} s + 1}
$$

Define natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}
$$

The final canonical form is:

$$
Y(s) = \frac{1/K}{\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_n} + 1}
$$

### Step 5 — Stability Discussion
For a passive environment, stability requires $\zeta \geq 0$ and $K \geq 0$, $M \geq 0$, $B \geq 0$. The **passivity condition** (Colgate & Schenkel, 1994) further demands:

$$
B \geq \frac{(K T_d)^2}{2 \pi / T_s}
$$

where $T_d$ is the digital loop delay and $T_s$ is the sampling period. This is the famous **"Z-width" criterion** that limits the maximum renderable stiffness.

$$
Z_{\text{width}} = \frac{\pi}{T_s} - \frac{(K_{\max})^2 T_s}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Z-width derivation tells designers exactly how stiff a virtual wall they can render without causing instabilities. For a 1 kHz sampling rate ($T_s = 1\,\text{ms}$), the maximum stable stiffness is approximately $K_{\max} \approx 2500$ N/m — a classic KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS Feature Extraction for EMG

**Problem.** Given a windowed EMG signal $x_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$, derive the RMS-based proportional control command.

### Step 1 — Define the Feature
The RMS in a window of $N$ samples is:

$$
\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}
$$

**Explanation:** Squaring removes the sign of the bipolar EMG, averaging yields the signal's power, and the square root restores units to voltage.

### Step 2 — Apply a Linear Mapping to Force
The proportional control law states:

$$
u = K \cdot \text{RMS} = K \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
Let the sampled window be $x = \{120, -85, 60, -45, 200, -160, 90, -30\}\,\mu\text{V}$ (so $N = 8$). Take $K = 0.1$ N/µV.

Compute squares:

$$
120^2 = 14400, \quad (-85)^2 = 7225, \quad 60^2 = 3600, \quad (-45)^2 = 2025
$$

$$
200^2 = 40000, \quad (-160)^2 = 25600, \quad 90^2 = 8100, \quad (-30)^2 = 900
$$

Sum:

$$
\sum x_i^2 = 14400 + 7225 + 3600 + 2025 + 40000 + 25600 + 8100 + 900 = 101850
$$

Compute mean:

$$
\frac{1}{8} \times 101850 = 12731.25
$$

Compute RMS:

$$
\text{RMS} = \sqrt{12731.25} = 112.83\,\mu\text{V}
$$

Compute control command:

$$
u = 0.1 \times 112.83 = 11.28\,\text{N}
$$

Thus, the robot applies approximately **11.3 N** of grip force. This numerical is a frequent KTU short-question template.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ERD Calculation for Motor Imagery BCI

**Problem.** Compute the ERD ratio for a subject where the mu-band power during motor imagery is $P_{\text{act}} = 4.2\,\mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ and the reference (rest) power is $P_{\text{ref}} = 8.6\,\mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$.

### Step 1 — Substitute into the ERD Equation

$$
\text{ERD}\% = \frac{P_{\text{act}} - P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{ref}}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2 — Numerator Calculation

$$
P_{\text{act}} - P_{\text{ref}} = 4.2 - 8.6 = -4.4
$$

### Step 3 — Divide and Scale

$$
\text{ERD}\% = \frac{-4.4}{8.6} \times 100 = -51.16\%
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The negative sign indicates **desynchronization** (power decrease during motor imagery). A magnitude **> 40 %** is considered a **strong BCI response** and is typically used to trigger a robot-assisted movement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Threshold (KTU high-yield):** ERD magnitude **> 40 %** ⇒ strong MI-BCI command. ERD between 20–40 % ⇒ moderate. ERD < 20 % ⇒ insufficient — therapy should be re-calibrated.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Shared-Controller Stability Condition

**Problem.** Show that the shared-control law $u_s = \alpha u_p + (1-\alpha) u_r$ is stable provided $\alpha \in [0,1]$ and both sub-controllers are individually stable.

### Step 1 — Assume Bounded Inputs
Since $u_p$ and $u_r$ are outputs of stable controllers, there exist finite bounds $M_p$ and $M_r$ such that $\vert u_p \vert \leq M_p$ and $\vert u_r \vert \leq M_r$.

### Step 2 — Apply Triangle Inequality
The shared command magnitude is bounded by:

$$
\vert u_s \vert = \vert \alpha u_p + (1-\alpha) u_r \vert
$$

$$
\leq \alpha \vert u_p \vert + (1-\alpha) \vert u_r \vert
$$

$$
\leq \alpha M_p + (1-\alpha) M_r
$$

### Step 3 — Convexity Argument
Since $\alpha \in [0,1]$, the bound $\alpha M_p + (1-\alpha) M_r$ is a **convex combination** of $M_p$ and $M_r$, which lies between them:

$$
\min(M_p, M_r) \leq \alpha M_p + (1-\alpha) M_r \leq \max(M_p, M_r)
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion
Therefore, $\vert u_s \vert$ is bounded, proving **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stability** of the shared controller. The mixing parameter $\alpha$ does not destabilize the system, only redistributes authority — a key reason shared control is preferred over switching controllers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix (Performance Trade-Offs Across Interface Types)

| **Interface** | **Information Rate (bits/min)** | **Setup Time (min)** | **Patient Burden** | **Calibration Drift** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EMG (Surface)** | 30 – 60 | 5 – 10 | Low | Moderate (sweat, electrode shift) | Prosthetic arms, grasp rehab |
| **EMG (Intramuscular)** | 60 – 90 | 15 – 30 | High | Low | High-DOF prosthetic hands |
| **EEG (Motor Imagery)** | 15 – 35 | 30 – 60 | Moderate | High (fatigue) | Locked-in patients, stroke |
| **EOG** | 40 – 70 | 5 – 10 | Low | Low | Communication, wheelchair |
| **Haptic (Impedance)** | High (force feedback) | 10 – 20 | Low | Low | Telerobotic surgery, exoskeletons |
| **VR-based Visual** | High (visual feedback) | 5 – 15 | Low | None | Pediatric neurorehab |
| **Hybrid (EMG+EEG)** | 50 – 100 | 30 – 60 | Moderate | Moderate | Advanced BCI-actuated exoskeletons |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Four-Layer HRI Architecture (Functional Block Flow)

The Mermaid diagram below depicts the canonical four-layer architecture introduced in Section 2.1. It is the most frequently asked 7-mark illustration in KTU Module 4 examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Neural Intent] --> B[Layer 1 Signal Acquisition]
    B --> C[Layer 2 Signal Processing and Feature Extraction]
    C --> D[Layer 3 Intent Decoding and Classification]
    D --> E[Layer 4 Robot Control and Actuation]
    E --> F[Rehabilitation Robot]
    F --> G[Patient Sensory Feedback Loop]
    G --> A
    B1[EMG Electrodes] -.-> B
    B2[EEG Scalp Electrodes] -.-> B
    B3[EOG Around Eye Electrodes] -.-> B
    B4[IMU and Force Sensors] -.-> B
    E1[PID Controller] -.-> E
    E2[Impedance Admittance Controller] -.-> E
    E3[Shared Controller] -.-> E
    F1[Upper Limb Exoskeleton] -.-> F
    F2[End Effector Robot Arm] -.-> F
    F3[Gait Trainer Lokomat] -.-> F
    G1[Haptic Force Feedback] -.-> G
    G2[Visual VR Display] -.-> G
    G3[Audio Cue] -.-> G
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **central flow** (nodes A → B → C → D → E → F → G → A) is the closed-loop patient–robot interaction cycle.
- The **dotted feeders** (B1–B4) represent alternative input modalities entering Layer 1.
- The **dotted feeders** (E1–E3) represent selectable control algorithms in Layer 4.
- The **dotted feeders** (F1–F3, G1–G3) represent the family of robots and feedback displays commonly encountered in clinical rehab.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — EMG-Driven Proportional Control Signal Flow

This diagram shows how a single-channel EMG signal is converted to a gripper velocity command, with all intermediate processing stages explicit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Muscle Fiber MUAP Generation] --> S2[Surface Electrode Pickup Ag or AgCl]
    S2 --> S3[Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR greater than 110 dB]
    S3 --> S4[Band Pass Filter 20 to 450 Hz]
    S4 --> S5[Notch Filter 50 Hz Powerline]
    S5 --> S6[Full Wave Rectification]
    S6 --> S7[Sliding RMS Window 100 to 250 ms]
    S7 --> S8{Threshold Check}
    S8 -- Above Threshold --> S9[Gain K Multiplication]
    S8 -- Below Threshold --> S10[Output Zero No Command]
    S9 --> S11[Saturator Upper and Lower Limits]
    S10 --> S11
    S11 --> S12[Robot Gripper Velocity Command]
    S12 --> S13[Actuator PWM Signal]
    S13 --> S14[Force or Position Feedback to Patient]
```

**Block-Level Annotation:**
- **MUAP** = Motor Unit Action Potential (the cellular-level origin of EMG).
- The threshold check at **S8** prevents noise from triggering spurious motion.
- The saturator at **S11** enforces safety envelope (clinically: **< 50 N grip force** for fragile patients).
- The loop closes at **S14**, where proprioceptive and tactile feedback from the gripper informs the patient.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Shared Control Architecture with Patient–Robot Authority Blending

This block diagram models the hybrid assistance paradigm used in devices like the **Armeo Spring** and **Lokomat**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient EMG or EEG Intent] --> P2[Patient Controller Block]
    P2 --> P3[Patient Command u_p]
    R1[Robot Trajectory Planner] --> R2[Robot Controller Block]
    R2 --> R3[Robot Command u_r]
    P3 --> M[Mixing Block with Gain alpha t]
    R3 --> M
    M --> SC[Shared Control Output u_s]
    SC --> ACT[Robot Actuator]
    ACT --> ENV[Patient Environment Interaction]
    ENV --> FB[Error Tracker e_t]
    FB --> AD[Adaptation Law alpha t plus 1]
    AD --> M
```

**Adaptive Behaviour:**
- When the patient's tracking error $e_t$ exceeds the threshold $e_{\text{th}}$, the adaptation law **decreases $\alpha$** — the robot takes over more authority.
- When the patient performs well, $\alpha$ **increases** — the robot recedes, encouraging active participation.
- This embodies the **challenge point principle** central to motor learning theory.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Multimodal Hybrid BCI (EMG + EEG) Fusion

For severely impaired patients, neither EMG nor EEG alone is sufficient. The hybrid interface fuses both signal streams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[EMG Signal Channel 1 to 8] --> F1[EMG Feature Bank RMS MAV WL]
    E1[EEG Signal Channel 1 to 32] --> F2[EEG Feature Bank PSD CSP ERD]
    F1 --> CN[Fused Feature Vector Concatenation]
    F2 --> CN
    CN --> CL[Classifier LDA or SVM or CNN]
    CL --> DC[Decision Confidence Score]
    DC --> D1{Confidence greater than Threshold}
    D1 -- Yes --> OUT1[Trigger Robot Motion]
    D1 -- No --> OUT2[Hold Position Safety Hold]
    OUT1 --> FB[Visual and Haptic Feedback]
    OUT2 --> FB
    FB --> M1
    FB --> E1
```

**Clinical Rationale:**
- **EMG** captures residual motor intent (high specificity, low fatigue).
- **EEG** captures cortical planning (works even with no muscle activity).
- **Fusion** increases the **information transfer rate (ITR)** to 50–100 bits/min, a 2–3× improvement over EEG alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric (e.g., `M1`, `F1`, `CL`, `D1`) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italic formatting, complying with the Mermaid safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Compliance:**
> - Part A: 3 marks, no choice, answer in **6–8 lines**.
> - Part B: 14 marks, **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.
> - Total module weightage is typically **20 %** of the course, with Module 4 carrying roughly **15 marks** in the ESE.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Human-Robot Interface (HRI) in the context of rehabilitation robotics. List any four design constraints.**

**Model Answer:**
A Human-Robot Interface (HRI) in rehabilitation robotics is the bidirectional hardware–software communication channel that translates human physiological intent — such as EMG, EEG, or kinematic signals — into robotic actuation commands, while delivering appropriate sensory feedback (haptic, visual, auditory) to the patient.

The four cardinal design constraints are:

1. **Safety** — must comply with ISO 13482; force limits, dead-man switches, and software e-stops.
2. **Transparency** — the patient should "feel" as if directly interacting with the environment (backdriveability, low inertia).
3. **Intuitiveness** — minimal cognitive load; the mapping from intent to motion must be learnable in ≤ 3 sessions.
4. **Reliability** — robust against sweat, electrode shift, and electromagnetic interference.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the difference between impedance control and admittance control in haptic interfaces. State one clinical scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Impedance Control | Admittance Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Input measured** | Force | Position |
| **Output produced** | Position | Force |
| **Equation** | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_h - F_e$ | $F_r = M\ddot{x}_d + B\dot{x}_d + Kx_d$ |
| **Best for** | Light, backdriveable robots (cables) | Heavy, geared robots (rigid arms) |

**Clinical scenario for impedance control:** A cable-driven wrist exoskeleton for stroke rehabilitation, where the patient pulls gently and the robot responds by moving — preferred because cables have negligible friction and the robot is inherently backdriveable.

**Clinical scenario for admittance control:** A rigid end-effector arm like the **InMotion ARM**, where the user cannot physically push the heavy motor; the robot measures position and applies the correct force.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern mandates that within each Module-level 14-mark question, students must attempt **exactly one** of the two given alternatives. Each alternative is internally split into **(a)** 7 marks and **(b)** 7 marks.

---

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the four-layer architecture of a Human-Robot Interface (HRI) used in upper-limb rehabilitation. Explain the function of each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a haptic impedance controller and explain the **Z-width stability criterion**. A haptic device operates with sampling period $T_s = 1$ ms and renders a virtual spring of stiffness $K = 2000$ N/m. Determine whether the rendered environment is stable. Take the human hand damping $B = 0.5$ Ns/m.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Four-Layer HRI Architecture**

The four-layer architecture, drawn in the diagram of Section 4.1, comprises:

**Layer 1 — Signal Acquisition:**
Surface EMG electrodes (Ag/AgCl) capture the patient's residual muscle activity. EEG electrodes (10-20 system) acquire cortical signals for severely impaired patients. The signals are conditioned using an **instrumentation amplifier with CMRR ≥ 110 dB** to reject common-mode noise. A typical 8-channel EMG system uses bipolar electrode pairs placed on the biceps, triceps, forearm flexors, and extensors.

**Layer 2 — Signal Processing & Feature Extraction:**
Raw bio-signals are contaminated with motion artefact, powerline interference (50 Hz), and thermal noise. The processing pipeline is: **band-pass filtering (20–450 Hz for EMG, 0.5–50 Hz for EEG) → 50 Hz notch filter → full-wave rectification → RMS or MAV computation over 100–250 ms windows**. These time-domain features compactly represent muscle activation intensity.

**Layer 3 — Intent Decoding & Classification:**
The extracted feature vector is fed into a classifier — typically **LDA** for low-complexity embedded systems or **CNN/LSTM** for higher-accuracy desktop applications. The output is a discrete class label (e.g., "grasp cup") or a continuous variable (joint angle, force level). Classification accuracy must exceed **90 %** for clinical viability.

**Layer 4 — Robot Control & Feedback Rendering:**
The decoded intent is mapped to robot commands via **PID, impedance, or shared control**. Concurrently, the robot's sensors (encoders, FSRs) drive the **feedback rendering** — haptic force, visual VR display, or auditory cues — to inform the patient of the outcome and close the sensorimotor loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Hint (KTU pattern):** 2 marks for the block diagram, 2 marks for Layer 1 + Layer 2 explanation, 1.5 marks for Layer 3, and 1.5 marks for Layer 4. Mention of real devices (InMotion, Armeo) earns extra credit.

#### **Part (b) — Haptic Transfer Function & Z-Width Stability**

**Step 1 — Write the impedance equation:**

$$
M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_h - F_e
$$

**Step 2 — Laplace transform** (with $M = 0$ for a direct-drive device, since the motor inertia is small):

$$
B s X(s) + K X(s) = F_h(s) - F_e(s)
$$

$$
X(s) = \frac{F_h(s) - F_e(s)}{B s + K}
$$

**Step 3 — State the passivity / Z-width condition:**

For a sampled-data haptic system, the passivity condition (Colgate & Schenkel, 1994) requires:

$$
B \geq \frac{K^2 T_s}{2}
$$

where $T_s$ is the digital loop delay.

**Step 4 — Plug in the numerical values:**

$$
B_{\text{required}} = \frac{(2000)^2 \times 0.001}{2} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000\,\text{Ns/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with available damping:**

Available: $B = 0.5$ Ns/m. Required: $B = 2000$ Ns/m. Since $0.5 \ll 2000$, the rendered stiffness $K = 2000$ N/m **violates** the passivity condition.

**Conclusion:** The virtual environment is **UNSTABLE** at $T_s = 1$ ms with $K = 2000$ N/m. To stabilize, the therapist must either:
- Reduce the rendered stiffness to $K \leq \sqrt{2 B / T_s} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.5 / 0.001} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$ N/m, **or**
- Decrease the sampling period to $T_s \leq 2B / K^2 = 2 \times 0.5 / 4 \times 10^6 = 0.25\,\mu$s (impractical), **or**
- Inject **virtual damping** algorithmically (the so-called "passivity observer–passivity controller" or PO-PC architecture).

---

### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4] **(ALTERNATIVE TO Question A)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **shared control** in rehabilitation robotics? Derive the BIBO stability condition for a shared controller of the form $u_s(t) = \alpha(t) u_p(t) + (1 - \alpha(t)) u_r(t)$, where $u_p$ and $u_r$ are the patient and robot commands respectively.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a motor-imagery BCI-driven rehabilitation robot, the mu-band power during imagery is $P_{\text{act}} = 3.8\,\mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ and the reference power is $P_{\text{ref}} = 9.1\,\mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$. Compute the ERD percentage and interpret whether the BCI command should be triggered. If the noise floor is $N_0 = 0.5\,\mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$, calculate the SNR of the system.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Shared Control Definition & Stability Proof**

**Definition (3 marks):** Shared control in rehabilitation robotics is a control architecture in which authority over the robot's motion is dynamically distributed between the **patient's voluntary intent** (decoded from bio-signals) and the **robot's autonomous intelligence** (trajectory planner, obstacle avoider, or assistive force). This blending is governed by a **time-varying assistance level** $\alpha(t) \in [0,1]$, where $\alpha = 1$ means full patient control and $\alpha = 0$ means full robot autonomy.

The goal is to optimize the **challenge point**: provide just enough assistance to keep the patient engaged but challenged, in accordance with motor learning theory (Guadagnoli & Lee, 2004).

**Stability Derivation (4 marks):** Assume the patient sub-controller and robot sub-controller are individually BIBO stable. Therefore, there exist finite bounds $M_p$ and $M_r$ such that $\vert u_p(t) \vert \leq M_p$ and $\vert u_r(t) \vert \leq M_r$ for all $t \geq 0$.

Take the magnitude of the shared output:

$$
\vert u_s(t) \vert = \vert \alpha u_p(t) + (1 - \alpha) u_r(t) \vert
$$

Apply the triangle inequality:

$$
\leq \alpha \vert u_p(t) \vert + (1 - \alpha) \vert u_r(t) \vert
$$

Substitute the bounds:

$$
\leq \alpha M_p + (1 - \alpha) M_r
$$

Since $\alpha \in [0,1]$, the expression $\alpha M_p + (1 - \alpha) M_r$ is a **convex combination** of $M_p$ and $M_r$, so it lies within $[\min(M_p, M_r), \max(M_p, M_r)]$. Therefore, $\vert u_s(t) \vert$ is uniformly bounded, proving **BIBO stability**. The mixing parameter $\alpha$ does not destabilize the closed loop — it merely redistributes control authority.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A & B:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "impedance" with "admittance" — they are duals. Students often swap the input/output. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Always state units** in numerical problems. Writing $K = 2000$ without N/m loses **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the triangle inequality justification** in stability proofs is a common 14-mark answer error — it costs **1.5 marks**.
> 4. In the ERD calculation, **students often forget the % scaling** at the end (multiplying by 100). This costs **0.5 mark**.
> 5. In the SNR calculation, **use $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios and $20 \log_{10}$ for amplitude ratios** — confusing the two costs **0.5 mark**.

#### **Part (b) — ERD Calculation & SNR**

**Step 1 — Apply the ERD formula:**

$$
\text{ERD}\% = \frac{P_{\text{act}} - P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{ref}}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{3.8 - 9.1}{9.1} \times 100 = \frac{-5.3}{9.1} \times 100 = -58.24\%
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the result:**
The negative sign indicates **event-related desynchronization** (power decrease during motor imagery). The magnitude **58.24 % > 40 %** threshold, so the BCI command **SHOULD be triggered** — the patient has a strong, clinically viable motor-imagery response.

**Step 3 — Compute the SNR:**
The signal power is the change in mu-band power: $\Delta P = P_{\text{ref}} - P_{\text{act}} = 9.1 - 3.8 = 5.3\,\mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$.

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta P}{N_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5.3}{0.5}\right)
$$

$$
= 10 \log_{10}(10.6) = 10 \times 1.0253 = 10.25\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
An SNR of **10.25 dB** is considered **good for clinical BCI** (typical acceptable range: 5–20 dB). Combined with the strong ERD, this patient is an excellent candidate for EEG-driven robot-assisted therapy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets Before the Exam**

- [x] **HRI Definition:** Bidirectional communication channel between patient bio-signals and robot actuation, plus feedback rendering.
- [x] **Four Layers:** Acquisition → Processing → Decoding → Control/Feedback.
- [x] **EMG Bandwidth:** 20–450 Hz, sampling ≥ 1 kHz, amplitude 50 µV – 5 mV.
- [x] **EEG Bands:** Delta < 4 Hz, Theta 4–8 Hz, Alpha 8–13 Hz, Mu 8–12 Hz, Beta 13–30 Hz.
- [x] **EOG Constant:** Corneo-retinal potential 0.4 – 1.0 mV; sensitivity ≈ 20 µV/degree.
- [x] **Impedance Equation:** $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_h - F_e$.
- [x] **Admittance Equation:** $F_r = M\ddot{x}_d + B\dot{x}_d + Kx_d$.
- [x] **Shared Control Law:** $u_s = \alpha u_p + (1-\alpha) u_r$, BIBO stable for $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
- [x] **Z-width Criterion:** $B \geq K^2 T_s / 2$ for passivity.
- [x] **ERD Threshold:** Magnitude **> 40 %** ⇒ strong MI response, trigger robot.
- [x] **Haptic Latency:** ≤ 20 ms (touch), ≤ 90 ms (VR visual).
- [x] **Standards:** ISO 13482 (personal care robots), IEC 80601-2-78 (rehab robots), 10-20 EEG system.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert µV²/Hz to dB using $10 \log_{10}$ (power) vs $20 \log_{10}$ (amplitude).
> 2. Mixing up impedance (force → position) and admittance (position → force).
> 3. Not stating the $\alpha \in [0,1]$ constraint in shared-control proofs.
> 4. Omitting the negative sign convention in ERD interpretation.
> 5. Using raw `|` in LaTeX tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 6. Drawing Mermaid diagrams with reserved words (`end`, `graph`) as node IDs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recreational Devices and Vehicles, Racing Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Recreational Devices and Vehicles in Rehabilitation Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Recreational devices and vehicles* in rehabilitation engineering refer to **mobility-assistive, sport-specific, and leisure-oriented assistive technologies** engineered to enable individuals with physical disabilities, chronic impairments, or age-related functional limitations to participate in sports, recreation, competitive athletics, and outdoor activities. These devices extend the user's functional capacity beyond Activities of Daily Living (ADL) into the domain of **Active Leisure, Therapeutic Recreation, and Adaptive Sports**.

These devices sit at the intersection of **Assistive Technology (AT)**, **Sports Biomechanics**, and **Human Factors Engineering**. Unlike clinical rehabilitation equipment (e.g., gait trainers, therapy robots), recreational devices prioritize **performance, speed, agility, and user agency**.

### 1.2 Taxonomy of Recreational Devices and Vehicles

| Category | Sub-Category | Example Devices | Primary User Group |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheeled Mobility** | Manual sport chairs | Racing wheelchairs, basketball chairs | Lower-limb impaired |
| **Cycling Devices** | Hand-cycles, adaptive tricycles | Recumbent hand-cycles, upright hand-cycles | Lower-limb impaired, balance disorders |
| **Water Sports** | Adaptive aquatic devices | Sit-skis, adaptive kayaks | Spinal cord injury (SCI), amputees |
| **Winter Sports** | Sit-skis, mono-skis | Para-alpine skiing equipment | Lower-limb impaired |
| **Aerial / Adventure** | Adaptive harnesses, off-road chairs | All-terrain wheelchairs (e.g., GRIT Freedom Chair) | Rural terrain users |
| **Powered Recreation** | Off-road power chairs | Action Trackmaster, electric sport chairs | Severe mobility limitations |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course module explicitly emphasizes **Racing Wheelchairs** as the focal case study, treating it as a *paradigm example* of high-performance recreational assistive device design.

---

## 1.3 Racing Wheelchairs — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Racing Wheelchair):**
> A *racing wheelchair* is a **specialized, high-performance, three-wheeled or multi-wheeled mobility device** specifically engineered for competitive track, road, and court sports. It is characterized by a **rigid frame geometry, fixed camber angles, low rolling resistance tires, an ultra-lightweight composite chassis, and an ergonomically optimized seating position** designed to maximize propulsive efficiency, lateral stability, and aerodynamic drag reduction for users with permanent lower-limb disabilities.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "F1 of Wheelchairs" Analogy:**
> Think of a racing wheelchair the way an F1 race car relates to a Toyota Camry. A standard manual wheelchair (the Camry) is built for **safety, comfort, and daily commuting** — wide wheelbase, foldable, cushioned. A racing wheelchair (the F1 car) is built for **one single goal: speed and competitive performance** — narrow, rigid, lightweight (often under **7 kg**), with steep cambered wheels that hug the track like a race car's slick tires. Every gram, every degree of camber, every millimetre of frame tube diameter has been engineered using the same **vehicle dynamics principles** taught in automotive engineering, but applied to human-powered propulsion.

The three cambered wheels of a racing chair work like a **three-point contact patch** in a vehicle — providing inherent static stability, allowing for **banked turning**, and creating a wide lateral base for cutting corners at high speed. The rider essentially becomes the "engine," and the chair's geometry is the **transmission**.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Racing Wheelchairs (KTU Board Exam Favorite)

| Type | Sport | Wheels | Camber | Top Speed Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Track Racing Chair** | T51/T53/T54 Paralympic track | 3 (1 front, 2 rear) | 0°–8° | 30+ km/h |
| **Road Racing Chair** | Marathon, road races | 3 (2 front, 1 rear) | 0°–3° | 35+ km/h |
| **Court Chair (Basketball)** | Wheelchair basketball | 4 (2 large, 2 small anti-tip) | 16°–20° | High agility |
| **Tennis Chair** | Wheelchair tennis | 3 (2 large cambered, 1 front caster) | 16°–22° | Quick lateral |
| **Rugby Chair** | Wheelchair rugby | 4 (omni-directional casters) | 0° | Aggressive contact |
| **Handcycle** | Para-cycling | 3 (2 front, 1 rear) | 0° | 60+ km/h (downhill) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Disability Class Mapping (T51–T54):**
> - **T51**: Tetraplegic (limited shoulder/arm function)
> - **T52**: Reduced upper limb function
> - **T53**: Good upper limb, no trunk control
> - **T54**: Good upper limb, partial trunk control

---

## 1.6 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following engineering parameters are **mandatory KTU exam knowledge**:

- **Frame mass**: typically **5–9 kg** (carbon fiber layup)
- **Camber angle (γ)**: **0° to 22°** depending on sport
- **Wheelbase**: 1.0–1.4 m
- **Toe-in/toe-out**: typically **0°** (zero toe)
- **Rolling resistance coefficient (Crr)**: 0.002–0.005 (high-pressure tires)
- **Aerodynamic drag coefficient (CdA)**: 0.15–0.30 m²
- **Maximum recorded speed**: **32.94 km/h** (T54, Paralympic track)
- **Rider's center of mass (CoM)**: positioned low and forward

---

## 1.7 Intuitive Engineering Insight: Why a 3-Wheel Configuration?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Triangle of Stability:**
> Three wheels form a **tripod contact patch** — the simplest mechanically stable polygon. Unlike a 4-wheel chair (which can tip laterally when one wheel lifts), a 3-wheel racing chair **cannot tip without first lifting its entire chassis**, because the contact triangle always contains the projection of the center of mass. This is the same principle used in **tripod camera stands** and **landing gear of tricycle aircraft**.

This makes the 3-wheel layout the *golden standard* for pure racing wheelchairs. However, for contact sports (rugby, basketball), 4-wheel configurations are used for **multi-directional impact absorption**.

---

## 1.8 Real-World Engineering Significance

Racing wheelchair design is one of the most **innovation-rich** niches in rehabilitation engineering because:

1. It borrows directly from **automotive chassis design** (FEA, composite layups, suspension).
2. It demands **biomechanical optimization** of human propulsion (catch angle, push frequency, power output).
3. It has produced **direct technology transfer** to everyday wheelchairs (lighter aluminum frames, quick-release wheels).
4. It serves as a **clinical outcome measure** — improved recreational capacity is correlated with better mental health, cardiovascular fitness, and community reintegration in SCI patients.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Design Objectives of a Racing Wheelchair (Hierarchical Priority)

1. **Minimize rolling resistance** (tire hysteresis + bearing friction)
2. **Minimize aerodynamic drag** (CdA reduction)
3. **Maximize propulsive efficiency** (effective gear ratio of human stroke)
4. **Ensure lateral stability** (camber-induced overturning moment)
5. **Minimize mass** (Newton's second law: F = ma)
6. **Maximize user comfort and energy storage** (vibration damping)

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance (Crr)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Rolling resistance is the energy lost per unit distance traveled due to **tire deformation hysteresis** at the contact patch.

When a pneumatic tire rolls, the leading edge of the contact patch is compressed while the trailing edge recovers. Because rubber is **viscoelastic**, energy is lost as heat during this cycle. The wider and softer the tire, the greater the deformation, and the higher the Crr.

**Factors that minimize Crr in racing wheelchairs:**
- **High tire pressure** (8–10 bar / 115–145 psi)
- **Narrow tire width** (22–28 mm)
- **Smooth tread pattern** (no knobs, no grooves)
- **Stiff sidewall** (latex or butyl inner tubes, or tubeless)
- **Hard wearing surface** (track > road > grass)

### 2.2.2 Aerodynamic Drag

Drag force is given by:

$$ F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_d \cdot A $$

Where:
- $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, **15°C**)
- $v$ = velocity in m/s
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient
- $A$ = frontal area in m²

**KTU Key Insight:** Drag force scales with the **square of velocity**. Doubling speed quadruples drag force. This is why **kneeling / tuck position** (reducing frontal area) is critical — at 30 km/h, ~80% of total resistive force is aerodynamic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Streamlining Principle:** The rider's posture is the **single largest contributor to drag**, not the chair. A racer in full tuck can reduce CdA from 0.35 m² (upright) to 0.20 m² (tucked), yielding a **~43% drag reduction** at constant speed.

### 2.2.3 Camber Angle (γ) — The Geometry of Speed

> [!NOTE]
> **Camber angle** is the tilt of the rear wheels from vertical, measured at the top of the wheel. Positive camber (wheels leaning outward at top) is used in racing wheelchairs.

**Effects of camber:**

| Effect | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Lateral stability** | Wider effective wheelbase at ground contact | Increased rolling resistance |
| **Push angle optimization** | Shoulders align with wheel contact point | Reduced straight-line tracking |
| **Shock absorption** | Wheel can flex sideways under lateral load | Asymmetric wear |
| **Aerodynamic margin** | Lower body fits between wheels in tuck | Reduced indoor maneuverability |

The **effective lateral stability** due to camber is:

$$ W_{eff} = 2 \cdot L \cdot \sin(\gamma) + W_{track} $$

Where:
- $L$ = effective wheel length from axle to ground contact
- $\gamma$ = camber angle
- $W_{track}$ = nominal wheel-to-wheel distance at axle

### 2.2.4 Propulsion Biomechanics

The rider generates force through **asymmetric arm stroke cycles**. The stroke has four phases:

1. **Catch** — hand contacts the pushrim
2. **Push (propulsive phase)** — ~30°–120° of arc
3. **Recovery** — hand returns to start
4. **Float / rest** — brief relaxation

**KTU High-Yield:** The pushrim acts as a **variable mechanical advantage lever**. For every push, the velocity ratio between hand and wheel is governed by the radius ratio:

$$ v_{hand} = v_{wheel} \cdot \frac{R_{wheel}}{R_{pushrim}} $$

Smaller pushrims (used in basketball) = higher mechanical advantage, faster acceleration. Larger pushrims (used in track) = longer lever arm, higher top speed.

### 2.2.5 Frame Materials Engineering

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aluminum 7075-T6** | **2810** | 71.7 | Entry-level racing |
| **Titanium Grade 9** | **4480** | 105 | Mid-range, vibration damping |
| **Carbon Fiber T800** | **1600** | 150–294 | Elite racing |
| **Magnesium AZ31** | **1740** | 45 | Ultra-lightweight prototypes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Fiber Dominance:** Modern elite racing wheelchairs use **monocoque carbon fiber layups** with layup sequences like $[0/±45/90]_s$ to balance axial stiffness, torsional rigidity, and impact absorption. Mass savings of **40–60%** over aluminum are typical.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_d = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 C_d A$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N | Air resistance on rider |
| 2 | $P_{drag} = F_d \cdot v = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^3 C_d A$ | Power to overcome drag | W | Energy at race speed |
| 3 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force | N | Tire deformation losses |
| 4 | $P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v$ | Power loss to rolling resistance | W | Sustained-speed cost |
| 5 | $v_{hand} = v_{wheel} \cdot \frac{R_{wheel}}{R_{pushrim}}$ | Hand-to-wheel velocity ratio | m/s | Pushrim sizing |
| 6 | $W_{eff} = 2 L \sin(\gamma) + W_{track}$ | Effective lateral wheelbase | m | Camber stability |
| 7 | $\omega = \frac{v}{R_{wheel}}$ | Wheel angular velocity | rad/s | Drive train design |
| 8 | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of system | J | Acceleration energy |
| 9 | $P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in}$ | Propulsion efficiency | W | Human-to-wheel power |
| 10 | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha$ | Rotational inertia torque | N·m | Acceleration torque |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination Note:** $C_{rr}$ values for racing wheelchairs are extremely low because (a) wheels do not power the chair (no drivetrain friction), and (b) high-pressure tires minimize hysteresis. Standard bicycle on road is ~0.004; elite racing wheelchair on track is **0.0025**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The theoretical framework above is directly used in:

- **Automotive chassis design** — camber principles transferred from wheelchairs to motorsport
- **Robotic prosthetics** — pushrim dynamics inform upper-limb prosthesis control
- **Biomechanics research labs** — sports wheelchair data informs SCI rehabilitation
- **Materials engineering** — carbon layup techniques originated in wheelchair racing
- **Inclusive product design** — features like quick-release wheels migrated from sports to daily chairs

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Problem Setup: Power Required to Maintain Race Speed

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (KTU Board Exam Standard):** A T54 track racer with combined mass (rider + chair) $m = 80$ kg wishes to maintain a constant velocity $v = 32$ km/h on a flat indoor track. Given: $C_d = 1.0$ (cylindrical approximation), $A = 0.30$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.0025$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Compute the total propulsive power required.

### Step 1 — Unit Conversion

The first step is to convert the velocity from km/h to m/s, as all SI formulas require m/s:

$$ v = 32 \ \text{km/h} \times \frac{1000 \ \text{m}}{3600 \ \text{s}} = \frac{32 \times 1000}{3600} \ \text{m/s} $$

$$ v = \frac{32000}{3600} = 8.889 \ \text{m/s} $$

### Step 2 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag Force

Substituting into the drag equation:

$$ F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_d \cdot A $$

$$ F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (8.889)^2 \times 1.0 \times 0.30 $$

$$ F_d = 0.6125 \times 79.01 \times 0.30 $$

$$ F_d = 14.51 \ \text{N} $$

### Step 3 — Compute Rolling Resistance Force

Using $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$:

$$ F_{rr} = 0.0025 \times 80 \times 9.81 $$

$$ F_{rr = 0.0025 \times 784.8} $$

$$ F_{rr} = 1.962 \ \text{N} $$

### Step 4 — Total Resistive Force (Neglecting Incline)

Since the track is flat, gravitational component along motion is zero:

$$ F_{total} = F_d + F_{rr} $$

$$ F_{total} = 14.51 + 1.962 $$

$$ F_{total} = 16.47 \ \text{N} $$

### Step 5 — Total Power Required

Power is force times velocity at constant speed:

$$ P_{total} = F_{total} \times v $$

$$ P_{total} = 16.47 \times 8.889 $$

$$ P_{total} = 146.4 \ \text{W} $$

### Step 6 — Result Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** At 32 km/h, the rider must sustain **~146 W** of mechanical power output. This is consistent with elite T54 racer data (typical sustained output 150–250 W during a 1500 m race). The dominant resistive force is **aerodynamic drag (~88%)**, not rolling resistance. This is why tuck posture, helmet design, and body coverall suits are critical — they directly attack the dominant loss mechanism.

### Step 3.1.1 — Sensitivity Analysis (Bonus Derivative)

> [!NOTE]
> **How does power scale with speed?** Differentiate $P_{total}$ with respect to $v$:

$$ P_{total}(v) = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^3 C_d A + C_{rr} m g v $$

$$ \frac{dP}{dv} = \frac{3}{2}\rho v^2 C_d A + C_{rr} m g $$

At $v = 8.889$ m/s:

$$ \frac{dP}{dv} = 1.5 \times 1.225 \times 79.01 \times 0.30 + 1.962 $$

$$ \frac{dP}{dv} = 43.54 + 1.962 = 45.50 \ \text{W per (m/s)} $$

Or equivalently, **~163.8 W per (km/h)**. This means going from 32 km/h to 35 km/h costs an **additional ~490 W** of metabolic power, which is why top speeds are so hard to sustain.

---

## 3.2 Derivative: Effective Lateral Stability from Camber

**Setup:** A rear wheel has effective rolling radius $R = 0.34$ m, camber angle $\gamma = 8°$, and nominal axle-to-axle track width $W_{track} = 0.45$ m. Compute effective ground-contact wheelbase $W_{eff}$.

### Step 1 — Geometry Setup

The wheel leans outward at the top by angle $\gamma$. The contact patch shifts laterally from directly below the axle by an amount $\Delta x$:

$$ \Delta x = R \cdot \sin(\gamma) $$

$$ \Delta x = 0.34 \times \sin(8°) $$

$$ \Delta x = 0.34 \times 0.1392 $$

$$ \Delta x = 0.0473 \ \text{m} = 47.3 \ \text{mm} $$

### Step 2 — Effective Wheelbase

The ground contact points are now farther apart than the axles:

$$ W_{eff} = W_{track} + 2 \cdot \Delta x $$

$$ W_{eff} = 0.45 + 2 \times 0.0473 $$

$$ W_{eff} = 0.5446 \ \text{m} $$

### Step 3 — Stability Margin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lateral tip-over prevention:** A camber increase from 0° to 8° has increased the effective wheelbase by **21%**, providing substantial resistance to lateral tip-over during cornering — at the cost of slightly increased rolling resistance due to the wheel's lateral scrub component.

---

## 3.3 Propulsion Force and Pushrim Mechanics

**Setup:** A racing wheelchair wheel has rolling radius $R = 0.34$ m and pushrim radius $R_p = 0.28$ m. The rider applies tangential force $F_{hand} = 120$ N on the pushrim during a push phase.

### Step 1 — Force at Tire-Ground Contact (Neglecting Drivetrain Losses)

By static equilibrium of moments about the wheel axle:

$$ F_{tire} \cdot R = F_{hand} \cdot R_p $$

$$ F_{tire} = F_{hand} \cdot \frac{R_p}{R} $$

$$ F_{tire} = 120 \times \frac{0.28}{0.34} $$

$$ F_{tire} = 98.8 \ \text{N} $$

### Step 2 — Mechanical Advantage (MA)

$$ MA = \frac{F_{tire}}{F_{hand}} = \frac{R_p}{R} = 0.824 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pushrim acts as a **force amplifier** — every Newton of hand force yields 0.824 N at the ground. The trade-off: the hand must travel **1/0.824 = 1.21 times** the distance the wheel rolls. This is the classic **force-velocity trade-off** in lever systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Trade-Off Summary

| Design Parameter | Increase → | Decrease → | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Camber angle | Lateral stability ↑ | Straight-line rolling ease ↓ | Athlete preference |
| Tire pressure | $C_{rr}$ ↓ | Ride comfort ↓, vibration ↑ | UCI/World Para rules |
| Frame stiffness | Energy transfer ↑ | Vibration transmission ↑ | Material limits |
| Pushrim radius | Mechanical advantage ↑ | Push velocity ↓ | Athlete's arm length |
| Seating recline | Aerodynamics ↑ | Forward visibility ↓ | Sport regulations |
| Wheel mass (rotational) | $I$ ↑, gyroscopic stability ↑ | Acceleration ↓ | Track design rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Board Exam):** Reducing **rotational inertia** of the wheels is more important than reducing translational mass for sprint performance, because $KE_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ — high-$\omega$ components dominate sprint acceleration. This is why elite racers use **ultra-light carbon disc wheels** even though they are stiffer and transmit more road buzz.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Define a racing wheelchair. List any four sport-specific design features that distinguish it from a standard manual wheelchair.**

**Model Answer:**

A *racing wheelchair* is a high-performance, three-wheeled assistive device engineered for competitive track, road, and court sports, designed to maximize propulsive efficiency, lateral stability, and aerodynamic performance for athletes with lower-limb disabilities.

**Four distinguishing design features:**

1. **Three-wheel configuration** with one front and two rear wheels (or 2F/1R for road) providing tripod static stability.
2. **Fixed camber angle (typically 6°–22°)** on rear wheels for lateral stability during cornering and shoulder alignment during push phase.
3. **Ultra-lightweight carbon fiber monocoque frame** (mass 5–9 kg) for reduced inertia and increased stiffness.
4. **Direct-drive pushrim propulsion system** (no gears, no chains) with variable pushrim diameter for mechanical advantage tuning.
5. **Aerodynamic tuck seating position** with seat recline of 6°–12° and integrated knee positioning to minimize frontal area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Hint:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any four features (0.5 each). Total 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**Q: Explain the role of camber angle in racing wheelchairs. State the typical range of camber used in track racing versus wheelchair basketball.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of camber angle:**

Camber angle is the inclination of the rear wheel from the vertical, measured at the top. In racing wheelchairs, positive camber (wheels leaning outward at top) serves four key functions:

1. **Lateral stability** — Effective wheelbase at ground contact increases, reducing tip-over risk.
2. **Push phase alignment** — The pushrim's contact point aligns with the shoulder joint, reducing shoulder impingement and torque strain.
3. **Shock absorption** — Wheel can flex laterally under impact loads.
4. **Stability during quick turns** — Provides a wider footprint for high-speed cornering.

**Typical camber ranges:**

| Sport | Camber Range |
|---|---|
| Track racing (T54) | **0° to 8°** |
| Road racing | **0° to 3°** |
| Wheelchair basketball | **16° to 20°** |
| Wheelchair tennis | **16° to 22°** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Hint:** 1 mark for each role (any 2–3 roles) + 1 mark for typical values. Total 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern (ESE):** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: Discuss in detail the engineering design of a track racing wheelchair, covering:**
**(a) Frame geometry and material selection (7 marks)**
**(b) Propulsion biomechanics and power analysis (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Frame Geometry and Material Selection — 7 Marks**

The frame of a track racing wheelchair is the **structural backbone** that determines stiffness, mass, energy transfer efficiency, and rider positioning.

**Geometric parameters:**

1. **Wheelbase:** 1.0–1.4 m. Shorter wheelbase improves maneuverability; longer improves straight-line stability.
2. **Camber angle:** 0°–8° (track), positive camber preferred for stability.
3. **Toe-in/toe-out:** 0° (neutral) to prevent scrubbing.
4. **Seat angle (recline):** 6°–12° from horizontal, balancing aerodynamics and visibility.
5. **Seat height:** Positioned as low as possible to lower the center of mass and frontal area.
6. **Front wheel diameter:** Smaller (40–50 cm) to reduce frontal area; positioned to support knee tuck.

**Material selection — comparative analysis:**

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Stiffness-to-Weight | Vibration Damping | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminum 7075-T6 | 2810 | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Titanium Grade 9 | 4480 | High | **High** | Very High |
| **Carbon Fiber T800** | **1600** | **Very High** | Moderate | High |
| Magnesium AZ31 | 1740 | Low-Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Justification:** Carbon fiber is preferred for elite racing because its **specific stiffness (E/ρ)** is the highest of all structural metals. The 7075-T6 aluminum is used in budget frames. The frame is typically a **monocoque** (single-shell) construction with $[0/\pm 45/90]_s$ layup sequence to balance axial, torsional, and shear stiffness.

**Frame mass target:** **5–9 kg** for the complete chassis (excluding wheels).

**Safety factor:** Designed to withstand **5×** the rider's body weight in static load (per ISO 7173 standards).

---

**(b) Propulsion Biomechanics and Power Analysis — 7 Marks**

**Biomechanics of the push stroke:**

The rider's stroke is divided into four phases:

1. **Catch:** Hand contacts pushrim; deceleration phase starts.
2. **Propulsive phase:** 30°–120° of arc, hand applies tangential force.
3. **Recovery:** Hand lifts off; arm returns to start.
4. **Float:** Brief rest period between strokes.

**Push frequency** for elite T54 racers: **1.5–2.2 Hz** (90–130 strokes per minute).

**Power analysis — quantitative derivation:**

Consider a racer of total mass (rider + chair) $m = 85$ kg maintaining $v = 30$ km/h = 8.33 m/s.

Rolling resistance:

$$ F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g = 0.0025 \times 85 \times 9.81 = 2.085 \ \text{N} $$

Aerodynamic drag (with $C_dA = 0.25$ m²):

$$ F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (8.33)^2 \times 0.25 = 10.62 \ \text{N} $$

Total resistive force:

$$ F_{total} = 2.085 + 10.62 = 12.71 \ \text{N} $$

Required mechanical power:

$$ P_{mech} = F_{total} \times v = 12.71 \times 8.33 = 105.9 \ \text{W} $$

Accounting for **propulsion efficiency η = 0.25** (typical human muscular efficiency):

$$ P_{metabolic} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta} = \frac{105.9}{0.25} = 423.6 \ \text{W} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The racer must expend ~424 W of metabolic power to sustain 30 km/h — approximately **80% of VO₂ max** for an elite T54 athlete. This is why even marginal aerodynamic or rolling resistance reductions translate to significant performance gains.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Alternative]

**Q: (a) Compare the design of track racing wheelchairs with road racing wheelchairs. Highlight seven key engineering differences. (7 marks)**
**(b) An athlete on a 3-wheel racing chair weighing 90 kg (combined) races at 36 km/h. The aerodynamic coefficient of drag times frontal area is 0.28 m², rolling resistance coefficient is 0.003, and air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the drag force, (ii) the rolling resistance force, and (iii) the total propulsive power required. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison: Track vs Road Racing Wheelchairs — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | Track Racing Chair | Road Racing Chair |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Wheel Configuration** | 1 front + 2 rear (stability on banked ovals) | 2 front + 1 rear (maneuvers around turns) |
| **2. Camber Angle** | 6°–8° (banked track turns) | 0°–3° (long straights) |
| **3. Frame Geometry** | Compact, low frontal area (CdA 0.15–0.20) | Longer wheelbase, slightly higher seat (CdA 0.20–0.25) |
| **4. Tire Type** | Smooth, narrow, high pressure (10 bar) | Slight tread, wider (28 mm), lower pressure (7–8 bar) |
| **5. Frame Material** | Carbon fiber monocoque (≤ 6 kg) | Carbon or titanium hybrid (7–9 kg) |
| **6. Primary Resistance** | Aerodynamic drag (~80%) | Mixed (rolling + aerodynamic) |
| **7. Athlete Position** | Full tuck, knees together | Semi-tuck, knees apart for ventilation |
| **8. Steering** | Rigid fork, single front wheel | Sometimes front wheel offset for stability |
| **9. Anti-tip** | Lateral support struts | Often none (relies on rider balance) |

**Justification:** Track racing is conducted on banked ovals where lateral stability is paramount, hence high camber. Road racing involves long straight-line travel where rolling resistance and rider fatigue dominate; hence lower camber and slightly wider tires for comfort.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $m = 90$ kg
- $v = 36$ km/h
- $C_dA = 0.28$ m²
- $C_{rr} = 0.003$
- $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step (i): Compute velocity in SI units**

$$ v = 36 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 10.0 \ \text{m/s} $$

**Step (ii): Aerodynamic Drag Force**

$$ F_d = 0.5 \times \rho \times v^2 \times C_dA $$

$$ F_d = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times (10)^2 \times 0.28 $$

$$ F_d = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 100 \times 0.28 $$

$$ F_d = 16.8 \ \text{N} $$

**Step (iii): Rolling Resistance Force**

$$ F_{rr} = C_{rr} \times m \times g $$

$$ F_{rr} = 0.003 \times 90 \times 9.81 $$

$$ F_{rr} = 2.649 \ \text{N} $$

**Step (iv): Total Propulsive Power**

$$ F_{total} = F_d + F_{rr} = 16.8 + 2.649 = 19.449 \ \text{N} $$

$$ P_{total} = F_{total} \times v $$

$$ P_{total} = 19.449 \times 10.0 = 194.49 \ \text{W} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** (i) Drag Force = **16.8 N** (ii) Rolling Resistance = **2.65 N** (iii) Total Power = **194.5 W**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s. *(Loses 1 mark immediately)*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $C_d$ with $C_dA$. The product $C_dA$ is the *drag area* in m². If the problem gives $C_dA = 0.28$ m², do not multiply by frontal area again.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Using the rider's mass alone for rolling resistance — must use **rider + chair combined mass**.
