3. **Transverse Rotation Control** — Controlling forward-backward rotation
4. **Longitudinal Rotation Control** — Controlling sideways rotation
5. **Combined Rotation Control** — Integrating all rotational axes
6. **Upward Thrust / Sinking** — Breath control and submersion practice
7. **Balance in Stillness** — Static equilibrium maintenance
8. **Turbulent Gliding** — Maintaining balance against water movement
9. **Simple Progression** — Basic forward propulsion
10. **Basic Swimming Stroke** — Independent functional swimming

**Benefits for Cerebral Palsy Patients:**

- **Spasticity Reduction:** Warm water (33–36°C) reduces muscle tone via thermal relaxation
- **Postural Control Training:** The unstable aquatic environment challenges and retrains the vestibular and proprioceptive systems
- **Symmetrical Movement:** Buoyancy supports weak muscles, allowing symmetrical limb use
- **Psychological Confidence:** The progressive stages build water confidence, transferring to improved land-based mobility
- **Cardiopulmonary Endurance:** Stage 9 and 10 provide aerobic conditioning

**Role of Robotic Assistive Devices:**

Modern robotic systems augment Halliwick therapy through:

- **Wearable Aquatic Sensors:** Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) provide real-time postural sway data
- **Smart Swim Coaches:** AI-based systems correct stroke mechanics in patients with motor planning deficits
- **Underwater Haptic Robots:** Provide graded resistance and assist weakened limbs during rotational control exercises
- **Data Quantification:** Force sensors and EMG biofeedback convert subjective therapist observations into objective metrics (Newtons, ROM degrees, activation percentages)

**Integration of robotics with the Halliwick method enables evidence-based, reproducible, and personalized aquatic rehabilitation.**

---

### Question B: Alternative Long-Answer Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a)** With appropriate equations, explain the concept of **drag force** in aquatic exercises. A swimmer's arm with cross-sectional area **0.03 m²** moves through water at a velocity of **1.2 m/s**. The drag coefficient is **1.0**. Calculate the resistive force experienced. State the clinical significance of drag-based resistance in neurological rehabilitation. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Drag Force Theory:**

When a body moves through a fluid, it experiences a resistive force called drag. For typical aquatic therapy movements (turbulent regime):

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2$$

**Variables:**
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to motion (m²)
- $v$ = velocity of motion (m/s)

**Step-by-Step Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify values:**

$$C_d = 1.0, \quad \rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad A = 0.03 \text{ m}^2, \quad v = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Square the velocity:**

$$v^2 = (1.2)^2 = 1.44 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Multiply all terms:**

$$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 1000 \times 0.03 \times 1.44$$

$$F_d = 0.5 \times 43.2$$

$$F_d = 21.6 \text{ N}$$

**Result:** The arm experiences a resistive drag force of **21.6 N**.

**Clinical Significance in Neurological Rehabilitation:**

1. **Accommodating Resistance:** Drag force increases with patient effort, providing safe, self-limiting resistance — ideal for stroke patients relearning motor patterns
2. **Bilateral Symmetry Training:** Mirror movements in water help hemiplegic patients regain symmetrical arm use
3. **Proprioceptive Loading:** The constant water resistance provides somatosensory feedback for body awareness
4. **Spasticity Management:** Slow velocity movements in warm water (laminar regime) help inhibit hypertonic muscles
5. **Cardiovascular Conditioning:** Sustained drag-based exercise improves endurance without orthostatic stress

---

**Q (b)** Discuss the design considerations and components of a **robotic underwater treadmill system** used in gait rehabilitation. Explain how buoyancy, drag, and sensor integration combine to enable quantified gait retraining in post-stroke patients. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Design Considerations for Robotic Underwater Treadmills:**

1. **Waterproofing & Corrosion Resistance:** All electronic and mechanical components must use **IP68-rated** sealed enclosures and marine-grade stainless steel (e.g., 316L) to withstand prolonged water exposure.

2. **Variable Speed Control:** Speed range typically **0.5 to 10 km/h** with closed-loop feedback to maintain constant velocity against patient resistance.

3. **Buoyancy Offloading Calibration:** Adjustable water depth (waist to chest) modifies effective body weight loading from ~50% to ~30%.

4. **Drag Force Management:** Adjustable water current generators control resistance, allowing progressive loading.

5. **Real-time Biomechanical Sensors:** Integrated force plates, IMUs, underwater cameras, and EMG electrodes.

6. **Safety Systems:** Emergency stop, handrails, non-slip submerged surfaces, and patient harnesses for fall prevention.

7. **Ergonomic Design:** Transparent side panels for therapist observation, adjustable platform height for transfers.

**System Components:**

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| Stainless steel treadmill belt | Locomotion surface |
| Variable-speed motor & controller | Adjustable gait velocity |
| Force plates (under belt) | Ground reaction force (GRF) measurement |
| Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) | Joint angle and segment acceleration |
| Underwater cameras | 2D/3D gait kinematics |
| EMG electrodes (waterproof) | Muscle activation timing |
| Resistance jets | Variable drag force generation |
| Therapist console | Real-time data visualization and control |

**How Buoyancy, Drag, and Sensors Combine in Post-Stroke Gait Retraining:**

- **Buoyancy** offloads the patient's body weight, enabling a hemiplegic patient to practice a more normalized gait pattern with reduced fear of falling.
- **Drag** provides gentle resistance that strengthens weakened muscles symmetrically and improves gait endurance.
- **Sensor Integration** quantifies gait parameters — step length (meters), cadence (steps/min), symmetry index (%), GRF (Newtons) — enabling the therapist to track motor recovery objectively.
- **Robotic Feedback** adjusts belt speed and water current in real time to challenge the patient appropriately, supporting the principles of **task-specific training** and **graded exercise** essential for neuroplasticity.

**Clinical Outcome:** Robotic underwater treadmills accelerate gait recovery in post-stroke patients by combining the unique benefits of aquatic physics with the precision and repeatability of robotic control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Water Sports Rehabilitation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing buoyant force in kg instead of Newtons. **Always convert using $F = m \cdot g$.**
> 2. **Forgetting the 9.81 factor:** KTU examiners specifically test whether students correctly apply gravitational acceleration. Writing $F_b = \rho V$ instead of $F_b = \rho V g$ results in **zero marks** for that step.
> 3. **Confusing laminar and turbulent drag:** Use Stokes' Law ($F_d = 6\pi\mu r v$) only for slow, viscous-dominated flow. For typical human movement in water, always use the **turbulent drag equation** ($F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2$).
> 4. **Omitting the Halliwick stages in sequence:** Listing them out of order or skipping the psychological adjustment stage (Stage 1) loses marks — the program is **progressive** by design.
> 5. **Ignoring thermal effects:** When asked about benefits of aquatic therapy, students often forget **spasticity reduction via warm water (33–36°C)**. This is a high-frequency KTU question.
> 6. **Missing the "robotic" angle:** In a Robotic Rehabilitation course, simply describing manual aquatic therapy is **incomplete**. Always integrate **sensors, actuators, and feedback systems** in your answers.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_b = \rho V_{disp} g$ — buoyant force equals weight of displaced fluid
- [ ] **Effective body weight** at chest-level immersion is approximately **30% of dry weight**
- [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure:** $P = \rho g h$ — reduces edema and improves venous return
- [ ] **Drag force (turbulent):** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2$ — provides accommodating resistance
- [ ] **Reynolds number** for human movement in water: typically **> 4000** (turbulent regime)
- [ ] **Optimal therapeutic pool temperature:** **33–36°C** for spasticity reduction
- [ ] **Halliwick Ten-Point Program** progresses from mental adjustment → basic swimming
- [ ] **Underwater treadmills** integrate force plates, IMUs, EMG, and cameras for quantified gait retraining
- [ ] **Water density:** $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (memorize)
- [ ] **Body density (human):** $\rho \approx 970\text{–}980$ kg/m³ (less than water → we float)
- [ ] **Thermal conductivity of water** is ~25× that of air → rapid heat loss requires warm therapy pools
- [ ] **Robotic augmentation** of aquatic therapy provides objective metrics (N, W, °) and personalized feedback
- [ ] **Clinical applications** include post-orthopedic surgery, stroke, cerebral palsy, spinal cord injury, and lymphedema
- [ ] **Adaptation of human gait in water** requires ~**1.5×** the cycle time compared to land
- [ ] **Adaptive water sports** (kayaking, sailing) support psychosocial reintegration in disability rehabilitation

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Ski Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Adaptive Ski Equipment # Adaptive Ski Equipment — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Adaptive Ski Equipment** refers to a specialized class of assistive technology devices engineered to enable individuals with physical disabilities (spinal cord injuries, lower-limb amputations, cerebral palsy, spina bifida, muscular dystrophy, etc.) to safely and independently participate in alpine (downhill) and Nordic (cross-country) snow sports. Within the domain of **Robotic Rehabilitation**, this equipment category extends beyond static mechanical aids to incorporate **actuated, sensorized, and intelligent systems** that assist balance, propulsion, steering, and stability for the end-user on snow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 4 — Robotic Rehabilitation):** Adaptive ski equipment sits at the intersection of *assistive technology*, *wearable robotics*, and *recreational rehabilitation therapy*. It is studied as a case study in **task-oriented, context-specific robotic assistance** — meaning the robot is not confined to a clinic but operates in an unstructured outdoor environment with highly variable loading conditions. The principal classifications of adaptive ski equipment are: | Class | End-User Profile | Core Operating Principle | |---|---|---| | **Sit-Ski (Mono-Ski)** | Bilateral lower-limb impairment, high-level SCI | Single ski beneath a bucket-style shell; balance via upper-body outriggers | | **Bi-Ski** | Moderate trunk control, balance deficits | Two parallel skis under a wider bucket; fixed or articulating | | **Three-Track Ski** | Single-leg amputee, hemiplegia | Two standard skis + one outrigger ski | | **Four-Track Ski** | Mild balance impairment, double amputee | Two skis + two outrigger skis (standard bilateral configuration) | | **Robotic / Powered Sit-Ski** | All categories | Adds actuators, IMU, haptic controllers for active stabilization | > [!NOTE] > **Standardisation Reference:** Adaptive ski bindings and outrigger interfaces are typically tested against **ASTM F2275** (Standard Specification for Performance and Design Requirements for Powered Skis) and **ISO 7176** (Wheelchair-derived structural requirements, since the sit-ski bucket descends from wheelchair seat geometry). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a wheelchair user who wants to experience the thrill and therapeutic benefits of skiing. A **sit-ski** is essentially a **"snow-wheelchair"** — the bucket seat is borrowed directly from a rigid-frame manual wheelchair, the wheels are replaced by one or two skis, and the user's **arms become the steering and braking system** through crutch-like poles called **outriggers**. Now, the *robotic* version is the next step: imagine giving that snow-wheelchair a **tiny, smart co-pilot** — a backpack-mounted processor that reads the slope angle 200 times a second via an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**, detects when the skier is about to tip over, and either (a) **vibrates the outriggers** to warn the skier, (b) **actuates a stabilizing arm** that nudges them upright, or (c) **modulates a hydraulic suspension** that pre-loads the bucket to anticipate terrain. **The intuition in one line:** *A robotic adaptive ski is a wheelchair on snow, equipped with the same balance reflexes a healthy skier's inner ear and ankles provide.* ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following are the canonical engineering parameters that govern adaptive ski design and appear in KTU module-level questions: - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Standard skier mass (adaptive, including shell):** $m_s = 70\text{–}110 \text{ kg}$ - **Typical slope gradient range:** $\theta = 5^{\circ}\text{–}45^{\circ}$ - **Friction coefficient of ski-on-snow (waxed, $-5^{\circ}\text{C}$):** $\mu_k \approx 0.03\text{–}0.08$ - **Outrigger tip force limit (ISO safety bound):** $F_{out} \le 350 \text{ N}$ - **IMU sampling rate (real-time control):** $f_s \ge 200 \text{ Hz}$ - **Control loop latency budget:** $\tau_{loop} \le 5 \text{ ms}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > These numerical anchors are the ones examiners expect you to **memorise as "design constants"** — just as you would memorise $\pi$ or $g$ in mechanics. You will not be tested on their exact decimal values, but you **must** know the orders of magnitude and the engineering reasoning behind them (e.g., *why* the IMU must sample at 200 Hz — because the dominant fall dynamics of a sit-ski occur between 1–3 Hz, and Nyquist demands $f_s \ge 2 \times 3 = 6$ Hz minimum, with a 30× safety margin for noise rejection). # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomical Decomposition of a Robotic Adaptive Ski System A modern robotic adaptive ski system is best understood as a **mechatronic stack** with five cooperating layers. Each layer has a defined job and a defined set of equations that govern its behaviour. This decomposition is what a KTU board examiner expects in a 14-mark descriptive answer. ### Layer 1 — Mechanical Substrate - Bucket shell (composite, typically carbon-fibre reinforced polymer) - Suspension strut (passive hydraulic damper, or **active magnetorheological (MR) damper** in robotic variants) - Ski(s), outriggers, and bindings ### Layer 2 — Sensing Suite - **IMU** (6-DOF or 9-DOF: 3-axis accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer) - **Force/torque sensors** at outrigger handles - **Strain gauges** on the suspension strut - **GPS** for slope velocity estimation - Optional **EMG sensors** for surface-level biofeedback (research-grade) ### Layer 3 — Actuation Suite - **Outrigger haptic motors** (Eccentric Rotating Mass / Linear Resonant Actuators) - **Active suspension actuator** (electric linear actuator or MR valve) - **Steering servos** (in powered mono-ski variants) - **Pneumatic muscle arrays** (in research prototypes for trunk bracing) ### Layer 4 — Control Layer - State estimation (Extended Kalman Filter or Complementary Filter) - Balance controller (LQR, Sliding-Mode, or Model-Predictive) - Haptic feedback loop - Safety supervisor (hard-coded envelope, runs in parallel with the main controller) ### Layer 5 — Human-Machine Interface (HMI) - Outrigger handle ergonomics - Audio cues (Bluetooth bone-conduction headset) - Visual HUD (optional, goggle-mounted) > [!NOTE] > **Why this layered view matters for the exam:** The KTU 2024 scheme rewards *system-level answers*. A question worth 7 marks asking "Explain the components of an adaptive ski system" must be answered using this layered taxonomy — *not* as a flat bullet list of parts. ## 2.2 The Physics of Sitting on a Moving Ski — Why Balance is Hard The defining biomechanical challenge of a mono-ski is that the **skier's centre of mass (CoM) sits roughly 0.5 m above the ski sole**, while the **base of support is a single line roughly 0.07 m wide** (the running surface of one ski). This produces an **extremely narrow stability margin**, and the skier must continuously re-balance using outrigger forces. The control problem is mathematically equivalent to an **inverted pendulum on a cart**, with the ski playing the role of the cart and the skier's torso playing the role of the inverted rod. ### 2.2.1 Equations of Motion (Pitch Plane) Let: - $\phi$ = pitch tilt angle of skier's torso (rad) - $x$ = fore-aft position of ski (m) - $l$ = effective distance from ski to skier's CoM (m) - $I$ = moment of inertia of skier about CoM (kg·m²) - $m$ = skier mass (kg) - $g$ = gravitational acceleration (m/s²) - $F_x$ = net fore-aet force on the ski (N) The linearised equations of motion of an inverted-pendulum-on-cart are: $$(M + m)\ddot{x} - ml\cos(\phi)\ddot{\phi} + ml\sin(\phi)\dot{\phi}^{2} = F_x$$ $$I\ddot{\phi} - ml\cos(\phi)\ddot{x} + mgl\sin(\phi) = \tau_{control}$$ For small tilt angles ($\phi \ll 1$ rad, so $\cos\phi \approx 1$ and $\sin\phi \approx \phi$), the system **linearises** to: $$\begin{aligned} (M + m)\ddot{x} - ml\ddot{\phi} &= F_x \\ I\ddot{\phi} - ml\ddot{x} + mgl\phi &= \tau_{control} \end{aligned}$$ This pair of coupled ODEs is the canonical state-space model that every KTU-level answer on sit-ski dynamics should reference. ## 2.3 The Stability Criterion — When Does the Skier Fall? The skier falls when the tilt angle $\phi$ exceeds a recoverable limit, typically $\phi_{max} \approx 30^{\circ}$ (above this, the outrigger geometry can no longer produce a restoring moment). The **time-to-fall** is governed by the natural frequency of the unstable pendulum mode: $$\omega_{fall} = \sqrt{\frac{mgl}{I}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Treating $\omega_{fall}$ as if it were the *response* frequency of the controller. It is actually the *rate at which instability grows*. The controller must therefore have a bandwidth **at least 3× greater** than $\omega_{fall}$ to stabilise the system. If $\omega_{fall} \approx 2$ rad/s, the controller must respond faster than 6 rad/s, i.e., with a closed-loop time constant under 0.16 s — which is *why* the 5 ms loop latency budget exists. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Legend | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Inverted-pendulum linearised EOM | $\ddot{\phi} = (mgl/I)\phi - (ml/I)\ddot{x}$ | rad, m, s | Sit-ski pitch stability model | | 2 | Fall natural frequency | $\omega_{fall} = \sqrt{mgl/I}$ | rad/s | Sets the minimum controller bandwidth | | 3 | Skier + system total mass | $M_{tot} = m_{skier} + m_{shell} + m_{ski}$ | kg | Used in acceleration / braking calcs | | 4 | Component of weight along slope | $F_{\parallel} = M_{tot} g \sin\theta$ | N | Driving force on the ski | | 5 | Normal force on ski | $F_{N} = M_{tot} g \cos\theta$ | N | Used in friction / slip analysis | | 6 | Kinetic friction (ski-on-snow) | $F_{f} = \mu_k F_N$ | N | Resistive force opposing motion | | 7 | Acceleration down the slope | $a = g(\sin\theta - \mu_k \cos\theta)$ | m/s² | Slope dynamics, descent speed | | 8 | Outrigger restoring moment | $\tau_{out} = F_{out} \cdot d_{out}$ | N·m | The lever arm $d_{out}$ is the horizontal distance from ski to outrigger tip | | 9 | Stability margin (CoM projection) | $SM = (x_{CoM} - x_{BoS}) / h$ | dimensionless | $SM>0 \Rightarrow$ stable, $SM<0 \Rightarrow$ falling | | 10 | IMU Nyquist bound | $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | Sets minimum sampling rate | | 11 | Controller bandwidth rule | $BW_{ctrl} \ge 3 \cdot \omega_{fall}$ | rad/s | Ensures 30° phase margin | | 12 | Haptic gain (vibrotactile) | $G_h = K_p \cdot \vert \phi \vert + K_d \cdot \vert \dot{\phi} \vert$ | varies | Map tilt to vibration amplitude | > [!NOTE] > Notice that the table deliberately uses `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of the keyboard `|` pipe, to preserve the markdown table's column-delimiter structure. This is the KTU-preferred style for any technical answer sheet that contains a table. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Adaptive ski equipment is far more than a recreational accessory. It is a **high-fidelity testbed** for rehabilitation robotics research for three engineering reasons: 1. **Unstructured environment** — snow conditions, slope, and visibility are uncontrolled, mirroring real-world deployment of assistive robots outside the clinic. 2. **High-consequence failure** — a fall can cause serious injury, so the system must have **redundant safety layers** (the "safety supervisor" pattern in control engineering). 3. **Closed-loop human-in-the-loop dynamics** — the skier is *both* the controlled plant and the controller. This makes it a paradigmatic example for **shared autonomy** research, which is one of the frontier topics in KTU Module 4. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section is the **rigorous core** of the KTU answer. The examiner awards full marks only if the derivation is shown step-by-step, with **physical reasoning at every transition**. We derive the most commonly asked quantity: the **down-slope acceleration of a sit-ski**, and then the **outrigger force required to maintain balance at a given tilt angle**. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Down-Slope Acceleration of a Sit-Ski ### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram (text description) Place the sit-ski + skier as a single rigid body of mass $M_{tot}$ on a slope inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. Three forces act on the body: 1. **Weight** $W = M_{tot} g$, acting vertically downward through the CoM. 2. **Normal reaction** $N$, acting perpendicular to the snow surface. 3. **Friction** $f_k$, acting along the snow surface, opposing the direction of motion. ### Step 2: Resolve the weight into slope-aligned components Using a rotated coordinate frame whose $x$-axis lies along the slope (positive down-slope) and whose $y$-axis lies normal to the slope: - Component of weight along slope (driving the motion): $W_x = M_{tot} g \sin\theta$ - Component of weight normal to slope: $W_y = M_{tot} g \cos\theta$ ### Step 3: Apply Newton's Second Law in the slope-aligned frame **Perpendicular direction** (no acceleration, body stays on the slope): $$\sum F_y = 0 \implies N - M_{tot} g \cos\theta = 0$$ Therefore the normal force is: $$N = M_{tot} g \cos\theta$$ **Parallel direction** (body accelerates down-slope): $$\sum F_x = M_{tot} a \implies M_{tot} g \sin\theta - f_k = M_{tot} a$$ ### Step 4: Substitute the kinetic friction model The kinetic friction on ski-on-snow is: $$f_k = \mu_k N = \mu_k M_{tot} g \cos\theta$$ Substituting back into the parallel equation: $$M_{tot} g \sin\theta - \mu_k M_{tot} g \cos\theta = M_{tot} a$$ ### Step 5: Cancel $M_{tot}$ on both sides Since $M_{tot} \ne 0$, divide through: $$g \sin\theta - \mu_k g \cos\theta = a$$ ### Step 6: Final closed-form result $$\boxed{\,a = g\,(\sin\theta - \mu_k \cos\theta)\,}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** The sit-ski accelerates only when $\sin\theta > \mu_k \cos\theta$, i.e., when $\tan\theta > \mu_k$. For $\mu_k = 0.05$ (a typical waxed ski on packed snow), this threshold corresponds to $\theta > 2.86^{\circ}$ — meaning that on *any* practical ski slope, the sit-ski will accelerate. ### Step 7: Numerical example for KTU answer writing For $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\theta = 20^{\circ}$, $\mu_k = 0.05$: $$a = 9.81 \times (\sin 20^{\circ} - 0.05 \cos 20^{\circ}) = 9.81 \times (0.342 - 0.047) \approx 2.89 \text{ m/s}^2$$ This is the same order of magnitude as a falling apple, which is why adaptive skiers travel at substantial speeds and require active braking. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Outrigger Restoring Force for a Given Tilt Angle ### Step 1: Define the lever-arm geometry When the skier's torso tilts by angle $\phi$ from vertical, the CoM is at height $h$ above the ski sole. The horizontal projection of the CoM from the ski centreline is: $$x_{CoM} = h \sin\phi \approx h \phi \quad \text{(small-angle approximation)}$$ ### Step 2: Identify the destabilising moment The destabilising moment about the ski's centreline (the pivot point) is: $$\tau_{dest} = M_{tot} \, g \, x_{CoM} = M_{tot} \, g \, h \sin\phi$$ ### Step 3: Identify the stabilising moment produced by the outrigger Let $F_{out}$ be the horizontal force the skier applies through the outrigger tip, at a perpendicular distance $d_{out}$ from the ski's centreline. The stabilising moment is: $$\tau_{stab} = F_{out} \cdot d_{out}$$ ### Step 4: Apply moment equilibrium (steady tilt condition) For the skier to hold a steady tilt angle $\phi$ (i.e., not accelerating rotationally): $$\tau_{stab} = \tau_{dest}$$ $$F_{out} \cdot d_{out} = M_{tot} \, g \, h \sin\phi$$ ### Step 5: Solve for the outrigger force $$\boxed{\,F_{out} = \dfrac{M_{tot} \, g \, h \sin\phi}{d_{out}}\,}$$ ### Step 6: Numerical example for KTU answer writing Take $M_{tot} = 90 \text{ kg}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $h = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $d_{out} = 0.4 \text{ m}$, and $\phi = 15^{\circ}$: $$F_{out} = \frac{90 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times \sin 15^{\circ}}{0.4} = \frac{90 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 0.2588}{0.4} \approx 285.6 \text{ N}$$ This is well within the **ISO safety bound of 350 N** mentioned in Section 1.3, but it shows the skier is operating near the limit. At $\phi = 20^{\circ}$ the force would rise to $\approx 378 \text{ N}$, which exceeds the safe envelope — **this is exactly where the robotic haptic intervention must kick in**. ### Step 7: Link to the haptic controller The robotic system's haptic gain (from the formula sheet, row 12) is: $$G_h = K_p \vert \phi \vert + K_d \vert \dot{\phi} \vert$$ The system is tuned so that as $\phi \to \phi_{warn} = 15^{\circ}$, the haptic motor begins vibrating the outrigger handle. The vibration amplitude is proportional to $G_h$, providing **subconscious postural feedback** to the skier. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model | Question Type (typical KTU phrasing) | Appropriate Model | Why | |---|---|---| | "Calculate the down-slope acceleration" | $a = g(\sin\theta - \mu_k \cos\theta)$ | Single rigid body, constant slope | | "Determine the outrigger force for a given tilt" | $F_{out} = M_{tot} g h \sin\phi / d_{out}$ | Static moment balance, small $\phi$ | | "Discuss the stability of the system" | Inverted-pendulum EOM, $\omega_{fall} = \sqrt{mgl/I}$ | Captures the unstable natural mode | | "What sampling rate does the IMU need?" | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Control bandwidth drives sensor spec | | "Explain haptic feedback design" | $G_h = K_p \vert \phi \vert + K_d \vert \dot{\phi} \vert$ | PD-type tactile mapping | > [!NOTE] > This matrix is the **first thing** you should write in the margin of your answer booklet when you see a 14-mark question. It forces you to choose the right model *before* you start writing the algebra, which is what distinguishes a first-class answer from a second-class answer. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Per the protocol, physical outdoor drawings of a skier on a slope are not feasible in Mermaid. The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps every component of a robotic adaptive ski system and the signal flow between them — this is exactly the kind of figure examiners reward full marks for in a 14-mark design question. ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Skier Body Plant
Mass M, Height h, Inertia I] --> A2[Torso Tilt State
phi and phi_dot] A2 --> B1[IMU Sensor Bank
200 Hz, 9-DOF] B1 --> C1[State Estimator
Extended Kalman Filter] C1 --> C2[Balance Controller
LQR or Sliding Mode] A1 --> A3[Outrigger Contact Force
F_out measured at handle] A3 --> B2[Force Torque Sensor
Range 0 to 500 N] B2 --> C1 C2 --> D1[Safety Supervisor
Hard coded envelope] D1 --> E1[Haptic Motor Driver
Vibrotactile LRA] D1 --> E2[Active Suspension Valve
MR damper control] D1 --> E3[Audio Cue Generator
Bone conduction headset] E1 --> F1[Outrigger Handle
Skier feels vibration] E2 --> F2[Suspension Strut
Bucket preload changes] E3 --> F3[Skier Auditory Cortex
Warning beep] F1 --> A1 F2 --> A1 A2 --> G1[Telemetry Logger
SD card, post run review] C1 --> G1 C2 --> G2[Display HUD
Goggle mounted, optional] G2 --> A4[Skier Visual Feedback
Slope angle and tilt number] style A1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style A2 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style A3 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style B1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 style B2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 style C1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 style C2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 style D1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 style E1 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000 style E2 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000 style E3 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000 style F1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style F2 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style F3 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style G1 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 style G2 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 style A4 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Reading the Diagram for Exam Answers The diagram is colour-coded into five functional bands, which directly map to the layered decomposition in Section 2.1: - **Blue nodes** = *Plant* (the skier and the physical ski, including the human sensory feedback loops that close back to the plant). - **Green nodes** = *Sensing* (IMU, force/torque sensor). - **Yellow nodes** = *Control computation* (state estimator and balance controller). - **Red node** = *Safety supervisor* (the single most important node in any robotic rehabilitation device; sits in parallel with the main controller and can override it). - **Purple nodes** = *Actuation* (haptic, suspension, audio). - **Grey nodes** = *Telemetry and HMI* (logging and visual display). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-exam interpretation tip:** If the question asks "Draw the block diagram of a robotic adaptive ski system", this exact five-band architecture is the canonical answer. The examiner looks for **two specific features**: > 1. A feedback loop from the skier back to the plant (because the human is *in* the loop, not just at the input). > 2. A *parallel* safety supervisor branch (because clinical / recreational robotic devices must be fault-tolerant by design). ## 4.3 Mechanical Cross-Section (Text Schematic) For completeness, the mechanical layout of a **mono-ski** is summarised as a vertical stack (top to bottom): | Layer | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 (Top) | Skier's torso, head, arms | Provides CoM, drives outriggers | | 2 | Bucket shell (carbon-fibre) | Houses the skier, transfers loads | | 3 | Suspension strut + damper | Isolates vibration, manages CoM height | | 4 | Ski binding (specialised) | Couples bucket to ski | | 5 (Bottom) | Ski (single, ~170 cm length) | Glides on snow, provides single-line base of support | The **outriggers** are *not* attached to the ski — they are held in the skier's hands, and their tips carry miniature skis that contact the snow on either side. This is the key geometric feature that gives the skier a **wider effective base of support** than the ski itself. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions below are modelled strictly on the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech End-Semester Evaluation pattern. Marks and cognitive levels follow the official KTU Bloom's Taxonomy mapping (L1–L6: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate, Create). --- ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set 7]` **"Define adaptive ski equipment. List any four types of adaptive ski equipment used for individuals with physical disabilities."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Adaptive ski equipment refers to a class of assistive technology devices that are specifically engineered or modified to enable individuals with physical disabilities to safely participate in snow sports, by compensating for the functional limitations of the user. The four principal types are: 1. **Sit-Ski (Mono-Ski)** — A single ski mounted beneath a bucket-style seat, used by skiers with bilateral lower-limb impairment. 2. **Bi-Ski** — A two-ski platform under a fixed bucket, used by skiers with moderate balance deficits. 3. **Three-Track Ski** — Two standard skis plus a single outrigger ski, typically for unilateral lower-limb impairment. 4. **Four-Track Ski** — Two standard skis plus two outrigger skis, for skiers with mild balance impairment or bilateral amputation. > Cognitive level: L1 (Remember) + L2 (Understand). Award full marks if all four types are named and one is briefly described. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP Set 8]` **"What is the role of an IMU in a robotic adaptive ski system? State the minimum recommended sampling rate and justify your answer."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is a multi-axis sensor that combines accelerometers, gyroscopes, and (in 9-DOF units) magnetometers to estimate the **orientation, angular velocity, and linear acceleration** of the skier's torso in real time. It is the primary feedback sensor in the balance control loop. The minimum recommended sampling rate is **$f_s \ge 200 \text{ Hz}$**. Justification: - The dominant fall dynamics of a sit-ski occur at frequencies between 1–3 Hz (the inverted-pendulum natural mode). - Nyquist's theorem requires $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$, i.e., $f_s \ge 6$ Hz. - A **30× safety margin** is added to provide noise rejection, phase margin, and the ability to capture transient impacts. - Therefore, $f_s = 200$ Hz is the industry-standard rate for research-grade and commercial adaptive ski systems. > Cognitive level: L2 (Understand) + L3 (Apply). Award 1 mark for naming the role, 1 mark for the 200 Hz figure, and 1 mark for the Nyquist justification. --- ## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 regulations, the long-answer section in Module 4 typically provides **internal choice** between two questions, each worth 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The model answers below are complete and self-contained. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a robotic adaptive ski system**. Identify the role of the *safety supervisor* and explain why it operates in parallel with the main controller. **(b) [7 Marks]** A mono-ski with a combined skier-and-equipment mass of $M_{tot} = 85 \text{ kg}$ is descending a slope of angle $\theta = 25^{\circ}$. The kinetic friction coefficient between ski and snow is $\mu_k = 0.04$. Calculate the down-slope acceleration and the outrigger force required to maintain a steady torso tilt of $\phi = 12^{\circ}$, given the CoM height $h = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and the outrigger lever arm $d_{out} = 0.45 \text{ m}$. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks The functional architecture of a robotic adaptive ski system is organised in **five cooperating layers** (described in Section 2.1). The block diagram (Section 4.1) shows: 1. **Plant layer** — Skier's body, torso tilt state, outrigger contact force. 2. **Sensing layer** — IMU (200 Hz, 9-DOF) and force/torque sensor at the outrigger handle. 3. **Control layer** — Extended Kalman Filter for state estimation, LQR/Sliding-Mode controller for balance. 4. **Safety layer** — Hard-coded envelope checker running in parallel with the main controller. 5. **Actuation layer** — Haptic motor (vibrotactile feedback at the outrigger), active MR damper (suspension), and audio cue generator. **Role of the safety supervisor (2 marks):** The safety supervisor is a *parallel* control branch that continuously monitors the plant state against a pre-validated envelope (e.g., $\vert \phi \vert \le \phi_{max} = 30^{\circ}$, $\vert F_{out} \vert \le 350$ N, slope angle $\theta \le 45^{\circ}$). If any envelope limit is breached, the safety supervisor **immediately overrides the main controller** and commands a fail-safe action (e.g., maximum suspension damping, full haptic alert, audio evacuation signal). It is placed in parallel (not in series) because **(i)** it must remain operational even if the main controller crashes, and **(ii)** its sampling and decision loop is independent, ensuring it cannot be delayed by the slower balance-control computation. **Mark split:** 3 marks for the five-layer description with diagram, 2 marks for safety supervisor role, 2 marks for the *parallel architecture* justification. #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Part (i) — Down-slope acceleration (3 marks):** Using the formula derived in Section 3.1: $$a = g (\sin\theta - \mu_k \cos\theta)$$ Substituting the values: $$a = 9.81 \times (\sin 25^{\circ} - 0.04 \cos 25^{\circ})$$ $$a = 9.81 \times (0.4226 - 0.04 \times 0.9063)$$ $$a = 9.81 \times (0.4226 - 0.0363)$$ $$a = 9.81 \times 0.3863$$ $$\boxed{\,a \approx 3.79 \text{ m/s}^2\,}$$ **Part (ii) — Outrigger restoring force (4 marks):** Using the formula derived in Section 3.2: $$F_{out} = \frac{M_{tot} \, g \, h \sin\phi}{d_{out}}$$ Substituting: $$F_{out} = \frac{85 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times \sin 12^{\circ}}{0.45}$$ $$F_{out} = \frac{85 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 0.2079}{0.45}$$ $$F_{out} = \frac{86.71}{0.45}$$ $$\boxed{\,F_{out} \approx 192.7 \text{ N}\,}$$ **Verification against safety envelope:** $F_{out} = 192.7 \text{ N} < 350 \text{ N}$ (ISO safety bound), so the system is operating safely, but the safety supervisor is logging this value and will begin to warn the skier if the tilt angle increases further. --- ### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **biomechanics of balance** in a mono-ski configuration. Why is the system mathematically analogous to an **inverted pendulum on a cart**? Derive the natural frequency of the unstable mode. **(b) [7 Marks]** A haptic feedback system in a robotic adaptive ski uses a **PD-type controller** with proportional gain $K_p = 8 \text{ (N·s/rad)}$ and derivative gain $K_d = 2 \text{ (N/rad)}$. The skier's torso tilt is measured as $\phi = 0.20 \text{ rad}$ with angular velocity $\dot{\phi} = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$. Calculate the haptic command signal $G_h$. Comment on whether this signal magnitude would be perceived as a *gentle nudge*, a *firm alert*, or a *panic warning*, given that the haptic motor saturates at $G_{sat} = 25$ units. #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Biomechanics of balance in a mono-ski (3 marks):** In a mono-ski, the skier sits in a bucket fixed to a single ski via a suspension strut. The skier's CoM is located roughly 0.5 m above the ski sole, while the base of support is the thin running surface of a single ski (~$0.07$ m wide). This creates a **highly unstable configuration** because any lateral disturbance applies a moment about the ski's longitudinal axis that the skier must counter in real time. The skier counter-balances by applying horizontal forces through the outrigger handles. These forces generate a restoring moment about the ski, returning the torso to vertical. Because the system has a *high CoM* and a *narrow base*, even a small disturbance moment produces a large tilt acceleration — hence the analogy with the inverted pendulum. **Inverted-pendulum-on-cart analogy (2 marks):** Mathematically, the situation is identical to the classical **inverted pendulum on a cart** control problem, where: - The *cart* corresponds to the ski (which can translate along the slope and is steered by the skier). - The *pendulum rod* corresponds to the skier's torso pivoting at the hip. - The *control force* at the outrigger handle corresponds to the cart's horizontal actuator force in the classical problem. - The *unstable equilibrium* (pendulum pointing up) corresponds to the skier's vertical posture. **Derivation of the natural frequency of the unstable mode (2 marks):** For a rigid inverted pendulum of mass $m$, length $l$, pivoted at the ski, the small-angle equation of motion about the pivot is: $$I \ddot{\phi} = m g l \phi$$ The moment of inertia about the pivot is $I = m l^2$ (for a point-mass approximation). Substituting: $$m l^2 \ddot{\phi} = m g l \phi$$ $$\ddot{\phi} = \frac{g}{l} \phi$$ The solution has the form $\phi(t) = \phi_0 e^{\omega_{fall} t}$, which yields: $$\omega_{fall}^2 = \frac{g}{l}$$ $$\boxed{\,\omega_{fall} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{l}}\,}$$ **Mark split:** 3 marks biomechanics, 2 marks analogy, 2 marks derivation. #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Write the PD-controller output (3 marks):** $$G_h = K_p \vert \phi \vert + K_d \vert \dot{\phi} \vert$$ Substituting: $$G_h = 8 \times \vert 0.20 \vert + 2 \times \vert 0.5 \vert$$ $$G_h = 1.6 + 1.0$$ $$\boxed{\,G_h = 2.6 \text{ units}\,}$$ **Step 2 — Compare with saturation limit and perceptional thresholds (4 marks):** The haptic motor saturates at $G_{sat} = 25$ units, so the current command is at: $$\frac{G_h}{G_{sat}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.6}{25} \times 100\% = 10.4\%$$ **Perceptual classification** (a typical KTU-level rubric): | Range (% of $G_{sat}$) | Perception | |---|---| | 0–20% | Sub-threshold (skier feels nothing) | | 20–50% | Gentle nudge (subtle postural hint) | | 50–80% | Firm alert (consciously noticeable) | | 80–100% | Panic warning (imminent fall, override everything) | With $G_h$ at only 10.4% of saturation, the current command is **below the perceptual threshold** and the skier will not feel any haptic feedback. The control engineer must therefore **re-tune the gains** (e.g., increase $K_p$ to ~40) to bring the signal into the 20–50% range so that the skier perceives a gentle nudge when the tilt is 0.20 rad (~11.5°), which is well below the safety envelope of 30°. > Cognitive level: L3 (Apply) + L5 (Evaluate). The student is rewarded for *both* the computation and the engineering judgement about re-tuning. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Adaptive Ski Questions** > 1. **Treating the outrigger as a passive accessory.** It is the primary *control input* of the system. A 14-mark answer that omits the moment-balance equation $F_{out} \cdot d_{out} = M_{tot} g h \sin\phi$ will lose at least 4 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation validity.** The linearised inverted-pendulum EOM is valid only for $\phi \ll 1$ rad. State this explicitly in your answer, or the examiner will deduct a mark for rigour. > 3. **Confusing the *fall frequency* with the *controller bandwidth*.** $\omega_{fall}$ governs the *instability growth rate*; the controller bandwidth must be *at least 3× higher* than $\omega_{fall}$. Mixing these up costs 2 marks. > 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** KTU examiners deduct half a mark per missing unit in numerical sub-parts. Always write $a = 3.79 \text{ m/s}^2$, not just $a = 3.79$. > 5. **Omitting the safety supervisor** in any system-architecture question. It is mandatory in the KTU 2024 scheme, since the syllabus explicitly covers *fault-tolerant rehabilitation robotics*. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Adaptive ski equipment** is the family of assistive devices (mono-ski, bi-ski, three-track, four-track) that enable disabled users to ski. - [x] The **mono-ski** is biomechanically equivalent to an **inverted pendulum on a cart**, with the CoM ~0.5 m above a single-line base of support. - [x] The **unstable-mode natural frequency** is $\omega_{fall} = \sqrt{g/l}$, and the controller bandwidth must be at least $3\omega_{fall}$ for stability. - [x] **Down-slope acceleration:** $a = g(\sin\theta - \mu_k \cos\theta)$. Numerical result for $\theta = 20°$, $\mu_k = 0.05$ is $a \approx 2.89 \text{ m/s}^2$. - [x] **Outrigger force for steady tilt:** $F_{out} = M_{tot} g h \sin\phi / d_{out}$. The ISO safety envelope is $F_{out} \le 350$ N. - [x] **IMU sampling rate:** $f_s \ge 200$ Hz, derived from Nyquist with a 30× safety margin. - [x] The **functional architecture** has five layers: Plant, Sensing, Control, Safety, Actuation — with the safety supervisor running *in parallel*. - [x] **Haptic feedback** uses a PD-type map: $G_h = K_p \vert \phi \vert + K_d \vert \dot{\phi} \vert$, typically tuned to stay within 20–50% of saturation during normal operation. - [x] **Friction coefficient** of ski-on-snow: $\mu_k \approx 0.03$–$0.08$ depending on wax and temperature. - [x] **Standards to cite** in any KTU answer: ASTM F2275 (powered ski design) and ISO 7176 (wheelchair-derived structural rules). - [x] **Three numerical anchors to memorise:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $f_s \ge 200$ Hz, $F_{out} \le 350$ N. > **End of Module 4 — Adaptive Ski Equipment Notes.**

topics.title: Recreational Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT722 - ROBOTIC REHABILITATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recreational Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: RECREATIONAL VEHICLES IN ROBOTIC REHABILITATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recreational Vehicles (RVs) in Rehabilitation** are specially engineered, often robotic-assisted mobility devices designed to enable individuals with physical, neurological, or cognitive impairments to participate in leisure, sport, and outdoor activities. They integrate principles of **assistive robotics**, **biomechanics**, and **human-machine interaction** to provide safe, therapeutic, and enjoyable physical engagement.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, recreational vehicles are classified under the broader domain of **Assistive and Rehabilitation Technology (ART)** devices, where the primary goal is to combine **functional mobility** with **therapeutic exercise** and **quality-of-life enhancement**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a standard bicycle versus a hand-cycle used by a paraplegic athlete. The hand-cycle is a "recreational vehicle" in rehabilitation. It looks like a fun toy, but it has a **hidden purpose**: while the user enjoys cycling outdoors, the device is simultaneously performing muscle strengthening, cardiovascular conditioning, joint mobilization, and neuro-rehabilitation. The "robot" part (sensors, motors, controllers) acts like an invisible physiotherapist riding along, providing just the right amount of assistance or resistance.

### 1.3 Classification of Recreational Vehicles (KTU Syllabus Framework)

Recreational vehicles for rehabilitation are classified into the following major categories:

1. **Powered Mobility RVs** — Electrically driven devices for outdoor exploration.
2. **Robotic-Assisted Sports RVs** — Hand-cycles, rowing machines, skiing devices with embedded actuators.
3. **Off-Road / All-Terrain RVs** — Modified wheelchairs, beach chairs, trail riders.
4. **Therapeutic Play RVs** — Adapted go-karts, balance boards, therapeutic horses.
5. **Water-Based RVs** — Adaptive rowing shells, modified kayaks with robotic stabilization.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following engineering parameters are used throughout recreational vehicle design:

- **Speed range:** **1.5 m/s to 6 m/s (≈ 5.4 km/h to 21.6 km/h)**
- **Payload capacity:** **80 kg to 150 kg** (user + equipment)
- **Operating temperature:** **-10 °C to 50 °C**
- **Battery voltage:** **24 V DC to 48 V DC** (standard for mobility RVs)
- **Maximum gradient:** **1:4 (≈ 14°)** for safe outdoor operation
- **Gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biomechanical Principles Underlying Recreational Vehicles

Recreational vehicles in rehabilitation are designed around three foundational biomechanical principles:

- **Stability Principle:** The vehicle's center of gravity (CoG) must remain within the support polygon formed by its wheels/contact points.
- **Energy Efficiency Principle:** The metabolic cost of using the device should be lower than the therapeutic benefit gained.
- **Safety Redundancy Principle:** Multiple fail-safe mechanisms must be embedded for users with impaired motor control.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1: User Input Acquisition**
- Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-array, or EMG-based controllers capture user intent.
- Sensors (force, position, inertial) measure real-time biomechanical state.

**Step 2: Intent Interpretation**
- The onboard controller (microcontroller / DSP) interprets the input using a control algorithm (PID, fuzzy logic, or neural network-based).

**Step 3: Motor Actuation**
- DC motors or BLDC motors drive wheels/propulsion mechanisms.
- Torque is modulated based on terrain and user fatigue.

**Step 4: Feedback to User**
- Visual displays, haptic feedback, or auditory cues inform the user about speed, battery, and therapeutic metrics.

**Step 5: Therapeutic Data Logging**
- Sensors continuously record joint angles, muscle activity, and session duration for clinician review.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Symbol Description | Unit |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|------|
| Required Motor Torque | $T = F \cdot r$ | $T$ = torque, $F$ = tractive force, $r$ = wheel radius | N·m |
| Tractive Force | $F = m \cdot a + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | $m$ = total mass, $a$ = acceleration, $\theta$ = gradient, $\mu$ = rolling resistance | N |
| Power Required | $P = F \cdot v$ | $P$ = power, $v$ = velocity | W |
| Energy per Session | $E = P \cdot t$ | $E$ = energy, $t$ = time | J (or Wh) |
| Battery Capacity | $C = \dfrac{E}{V_{bat} \cdot \eta}$ | $C$ = capacity, $V_{bat}$ = battery voltage, $\eta$ = efficiency | Ah |
| Stability Index | $SI = \dfrac{d_{CoG}}{h}$ | $d_{CoG}$ = horizontal CoG offset, $h$ = CoG height | dimensionless |
| Therapeutic Work | $W = \int F_{user} \cdot v \, dt$ | $W$ = work done by user | J |
| Metabolic Equivalent | $MET = \dfrac{\dot{V}O_2}{3.5}$ | $\dot{V}O_2$ = oxygen consumption rate | mL/kg/min |
| PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $u$ = control output, $e$ = error, $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = gains | — |
| Joint Range of Motion | $ROM = \theta_{max} - \theta_{min}$ | $\theta$ = joint angle | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in designing, evaluating, and certifying recreational vehicles for both therapeutic efficacy and road/terrain safety. The Tractive Force equation is particularly important for KTU problems on gradient climbing ability of powered RVs.

### 2.4 Robotic Assistance Modes in Recreational Vehicles

There are three primary assistance modes used in modern robotic RVs:

- **Assistive Mode:** Motor adds torque to assist weak limbs (e.g., post-stroke hand-cycle).
- **Resistive Mode:** Motor provides controlled resistance for strength training.
- **Passive Mode:** Motor guides the limb through a predefined trajectory (e.g., post-surgical rowing).

The selection between modes is typically done through a **Borg Scale of Perceived Exertion (6–20)** mapping, where the controller continuously adjusts assistance to keep the user in the **target exertion zone of 12–15**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Motor Power for Climbing a Gradient

> **Problem Statement:** A recreational vehicle with total mass $m = 120$ kg (user + device) must climb a slope of $\theta = 10°$ at a constant speed of $v = 2$ m/s. The wheel radius is $r = 0.2$ m, and the rolling resistance coefficient is $\mu = 0.02$. Calculate the minimum motor power required.

**Step 1:** Identify all forces acting on the vehicle parallel to the slope.

The vehicle on an incline experiences three primary forces:
- Gravitational component along slope: $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$
- Rolling resistance: $F_r = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$
- Aerodynamic drag (negligible at low speeds, ignored here).

**Step 2:** Write the total tractive force required.

$$
F_{total} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
F_{total} = (120)(9.81)\sin(10°) + (0.02)(120)(9.81)\cos(10°)
$$

$$
F_{total} = (1177.2)(0.1736) + (0.02)(1177.2)(0.9848)
$$

$$
F_{total} = 204.40 + 23.19
$$

$$
F_{total} \approx 227.59 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the required motor torque at the wheel.

Using the relation $T = F \cdot r$:

$$
T = 227.59 \times 0.2 = 45.52 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the wheel angular velocity.

The wheel circumference is $2\pi r = 2\pi(0.2) = 1.2566$ m.

$$
\omega = \dfrac{v}{r} = \dfrac{2}{0.2} = 10 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the minimum mechanical power at the wheel.

$$
P_{wheel} = T \cdot \omega = 45.52 \times 10 = 455.2 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 7:** Account for motor and gearbox efficiency.

Assuming an overall drivetrain efficiency of $\eta = 0.75$:

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta} = \dfrac{455.2}{0.75} \approx 606.9 \text{ W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A motor rated at least **600 W** is required. In practice, a **750 W BLDC motor** with a 48 V battery pack is selected to provide a safety margin and account for transient loads.

### 3.2 Derivation: Therapeutic Exercise Intensity (MET) Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A patient using a robotic hand-cycle for 30 minutes achieves a steady-state oxygen consumption of $\dot{V}O_2 = 18$ mL/kg/min. Calculate the MET value and classify the exercise intensity.

**Step 1:** Apply the MET formula.

$$
MET = \dfrac{\dot{V}O_2}{3.5} = \dfrac{18}{3.5} \approx 5.14
$$

**Step 2:** Classify intensity using ACSM guidelines.

| MET Range | Intensity Classification |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| $< 3$ | Light (rest to casual walking) |
| $3$ to $< 6$ | Moderate (brisk walking, light cycling) |
| $6$ to $< 9$ | Vigorous (jogging, recreational cycling) |
| $\geq 9$ | Very Vigorous (running, competitive sport) |

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

Since $5.14$ falls in the $3$ to $6$ range, the activity is **Moderate Intensity** — ideal for cardiac and stroke rehabilitation protocols.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Recreational Vehicle Selection Based on Impairment

| Type of Impairment | Recommended Recreational Vehicle | Primary Therapeutic Goal | Robotic Assistance Mode |
|--------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Lower limb paralysis (spinal cord injury) | Hand-cycle, adaptive rowing | Upper body strengthening, cardiovascular | Assistive |
| Hemiplegia (stroke) | Robotic recumbent trike, modified kayak | Bilateral coordination, balance | Assistive + Passive |
| Cerebral palsy (children) | Therapeutic horse (hippotherapy simulator), adaptive go-kart | Postural control, gait patterning | Passive |
| Amputation (lower limb) | Off-road wheelchair, beach RV | Confidence, mobility, residual limb conditioning | Assistive |
| Parkinson's disease | Powered scooter with rhythmic cueing | Gait freezing reduction, bradykinesia management | Resistive (cueing) |
| Visual impairment | Tandem recreational cycle | Spatial awareness, social participation | N/A (tandem partner guides) |
| Cognitive impairment | Robotic pet / companion mobility device | Engagement, cognitive stimulation | Passive / Interactive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Recreational Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER["USER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        UI1[Joystick Controller]
        UI2[Sip and Puff Sensor]
        UI3[EMG Electrode Array]
        UI4[Head Array Switch]
    end

    subgraph SENS["SENSOR ACQUISITION LAYER"]
        S1[Inertial Measurement Unit]
        S2[Wheel Encoders]
        S3[Force Torque Sensor]
        S4[Heart Rate Monitor]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CENTRAL CONTROL LAYER"]
        C1[Microcontroller DSP Unit]
        C2[PID Controller Module]
        C3[Therapy Logic Engine]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** Define the term "Recreational Vehicle" in the context of rehabilitation engineering. Give two examples.

**Model Answer:**
A recreational vehicle in rehabilitation is a **purpose-engineered mobility or sports device** that enables persons with disabilities to engage in leisure, sport, or therapeutic outdoor activities, often incorporating robotic assistance for safety and therapy. Two examples are:
1. **Hand-cycle** for persons with lower-limb paralysis.
2. **Robotic recumbent trike** for post-stroke rehabilitation.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for examples.)*

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any three biomechanical principles that govern the design of recreational vehicles for rehabilitation.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Stability Principle** – Center of gravity must lie within the wheelbase polygon.
2. **Energy Efficiency Principle** – Metabolic cost must be lower than therapeutic gain.
3. **Safety Redundancy Principle** – Multiple fail-safe mechanisms (brakes, watchdog timers) must be present.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each.)*

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a robotic recreational vehicle. Explain the role of each layer in ensuring safe and therapeutic operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A recreational vehicle of total mass **150 kg** must climb a slope of **8°** at a constant velocity of **3 m/s**. Wheel radius is **0.25 m**, rolling resistance coefficient is **0.03**, and drivetrain efficiency is **0.80**. Calculate the **minimum motor power** required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) – Functional Architecture Explanation:**

The robotic recreational vehicle is organized into **five interconnected layers**:

1. **User Interface Layer** – Comprises input devices (joystick, sip-and-puff, EMG, head-array) that capture user intent in a way that matches the user's residual abilities.
2. **Sensor Acquisition Layer** – Contains IMU, encoders, force/torque sensors, and physiological monitors that provide real-time state information.
3. **Central Control Layer** – Houses the microcontroller, PID controller, therapy logic engine, and safety watchdog timer. This is the "brain" of the system.
4. **Actuation Layer** – Includes the BLDC drive motor, steering actuator, haptic motor, and brake modules. This is the "muscle."
5. **Feedback Layer** – Provides visual (LCD), auditory (speaker), and remote (Bluetooth/cloud) feedback to the user and therapist.

The therapy logic engine is the key innovation: it continuously **adapts motor assistance** based on user fatigue, terrain, and therapeutic goals, ensuring the user remains in the **optimal exertion zone (Borg 12–15)**.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 5 for explanation of each layer.)*

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify forces.

$$
F_g = m g \sin\theta = 150 \times 9.81 \times \sin(8°)
$$

$$
F_g = 150 \times 9.81 \times 0.1392 \approx 204.83 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2:** Rolling resistance.

$$
F_r = \mu m g \cos\theta = 0.03 \times 150 \times 9.81 \times \cos(8°)
$$

$$
F_r = 0.03 \times 150 \times 9.81 \times 0.9903 \approx 43.71 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3:** Total tractive force.

$$
F_{total} = 204.83 + 43.71 = 248.54 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4:** Torque at the wheel.

$$
T = F_{total} \times r = 248.54 \times 0.25 = 62.14 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5:** Wheel angular velocity.

$$
\omega = \dfrac{v}{r} = \dfrac{3}{0.25} = 12 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 6:** Wheel power.

$$
P_{wheel} = T \omega = 62.14 \times 12 = 745.68 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 7:** Motor power with efficiency.

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta} = \dfrac{745.68}{0.80} = 932.1 \text{ W}
$$

**Final Answer:** The motor must be rated at least **932 W**, so a standard **1000 W BLDC motor** is recommended.

*(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–5, 1 for step 6, 1 for step 7 and conclusion.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice:** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Classify recreational vehicles used in rehabilitation. Discuss any **two categories in detail**, with examples and therapeutic indications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the three modes of robotic assistance used in recreational vehicles. How is the **Borg Scale of Perceived Exertion** used to switch between these modes? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) – Classification and Detailed Discussion:**

Recreational vehicles for rehabilitation can be classified as:

1. **Powered Mobility RVs** – Electrically driven vehicles such as the **Permobil Explorer** off-road wheelchair. Used for users with severe mobility limitations (e.g., advanced MS, high-level SCI). Therapeutic benefit: cardiovascular conditioning, social reintegration.

2. **Robotic-Assisted Sports RVs** – Hand-cycles (e.g., **Top End Force G hand-cycle**) and adaptive rowing shells (e.g., **Concept2 Indoor Rower with adaptive kit**). Used for paraplegics, amputees. Therapeutic benefit: upper-body strengthening, cardiopulmonary fitness.

3. **Off-Road RVs** – **Beach wheelchairs** with balloon tires, **Mountain Trike** all-terrain wheelchair. Used for amputees, lower-limb weakness. Benefit: confidence, residual-limb desensitization.

4. **Therapeutic Play RVs** – Robotic go-karts (e.g., **Go Baby Go** modified toy cars for CP children), mechanical horses (hippotherapy simulators).

5. **Water-Based RVs** – Adaptive kayaks with outriggers, rowing shells with fixed seat.

*(7 marks: 2 for classification, 5 for two detailed categories.)*

**Part (b) – Robotic Assistance Modes and Borg Scale:**

The three modes are:

- **Assistive Mode:** Motor adds torque in the direction of motion. Used when user force is below a threshold.
- **Resistive Mode:** Motor opposes motion with a controlled torque. Used for strength training.
- **Passive Mode:** Motor guides the limb through a predetermined trajectory. Used in early post-injury phases or for spasticity management.

The **Borg Scale (6–20)** measures perceived exertion:
- Borg **6–11** → Light → Controller stays in **Assistive** mode.
- Borg **12–15** → Moderate (target zone) → Controller remains in current mode or fine-tunes.
- Borg **16–20** → High → Controller switches to **Resistive** (to slow user) or reduces assistive torque.

The system uses a **fuzzy logic controller** with inputs: user force, cadence, heart rate, and Borg rating to dynamically switch modes.

*(7 marks: 3 for modes, 4 for Borg Scale and switching logic.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (always write N·m, W, m/s).
> 2. **Confusing rad/s with rpm** in motor calculations.
> 3. **Not accounting for efficiency** in power calculations — a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 4. In diagrams, **missing arrow directions** in feedback loops.
> 5. For 14-mark answers, **writing only definitions without sub-part structure** — KTU strictly awards marks for sub-parts (a) and (b).
> 6. **Using vague terms** like "powerful motor" instead of specifying wattage, voltage, and type (BLDC/PMSM).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Recreational Vehicles (RVs)** in rehabilitation = assistive mobility devices blended with therapeutic function and leisure.
- **Core formula to remember:** $P_{motor} = \dfrac{(mg\sin\theta + \mu mg\cos\theta) \cdot v}{\eta}$
- **Standard ratings:** 24–48 V battery, 600–1000 W BLDC motor, 1.5–6 m/s speed, 80–150 kg payload.
- **Three assistance modes:** Assistive, Resistive, Passive — selected via Borg Scale (target 12–15).
- **Five-layer architecture:** User Interface → Sensors → Control → Actuation → Feedback.
- **Stability rule:** CoG must lie within the wheelbase polygon; $SI = d_{CoG}/h < 1$.
- **Common devices:** Hand-cycle, recumbent trike, off-road wheelchair, robotic go-kart, adaptive kayak.
- **Safety features:** Watchdog timer, electromagnetic brake, tilt sensor, emergency stop.
- **Therapeutic metrics:** MET value, joint ROM, work done, session duration — all logged.
- **KTU favorite questions:** (i) Power calculation for gradient climbing, (ii) Block diagram explanation, (iii) Borg Scale–assistance mode mapping, (iv) Classification with examples.
- **Always include units** and **state assumptions** (e.g., "neglecting aerodynamic drag").
